Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are conducted on small to moderate N (up to 1000 variables), well below scales relevant to practical discrete generation tasks; whether the theoretical asymptotic predictions hold at larger N is assumed but not verified for all settings. - The reversed-leaf ordering applies cleanly only to XORSAT; the analogous result for k-SAT remains an open problem, with only a heuristic generalization (dynamic reverse min-degree) explored empirically. - Approximate uniformity is verified only with an indirect proxy (log-probability concentration), not with rigorous total-variation bounds; the paper acknowledges that achieving provably uniform generation remains open.
Search Results 2603.20589 PDF
#14 — Highly Innovative (79)

RoboAlign: Learning Test-Time Reasoning for Language-Action Alignment in Vision-Language-Action Models (21 ▲ )

Dongyoung Kim, Sumin Park, Woomin Song, Seungku Kim, Taeyoung Kim, Huiwon Jang, Jinwoo Shin, Jaehyung Kim, Younggyo Seo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Mar 2026
Why this rank: RoboAlign scores 79 for its surprising and well-evidenced diagnosis that higher embodied-reasoning benchmark scores can actually degrade VLA task performance — a counterintuitive finding that reframes the field's assumptions about language-action alignment. The solution of using FAST token prefix accuracy as an RL reward signal is creative and remarkably data-efficient (12.8K samples, ~1 GPU-hour outperforming 30-hour SFT). Generalization across three benchmarks, two backbones, and sim-to-real transfer (doubling real-robot success) lends strong credibility, though the brittleness of prefix-match rewards and restriction to Franka tabletop manipulation temper the breadth of the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) built on top of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) struggle to translate improved language-side embodied reasoning into actual low-level action gains. Prior work fine-tunes MLLMs on vision-QA tasks (spatial reasoning, trajectory prediction, high-level action prediction) to boost embodied reasoning, but a systematic study (VLM4VLA) and the authors' own experiments show the correlation between reasoning benchmark scores and VLA task success is inconsistent and sometimes negative. Strikingly, RoboBrain 2.0—which scores highest on embodied reasoning benchmarks and outperforms GPT-4o—yields the lowest VLA performance when used as a backbone. The root cause identified is a modality gap: language-supervision alone does not align MLLM representations with the low-level continuous action space. Methodology: RoboAlign is a two-stage MLLM training framework that closes the language-action modality gap by treating low-level action token accuracy as the direct training signal. Stage 1 is a robotics-aware SFT that trains Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct on a 2.28M-sample mixture comprising: (i) general MLLM data (LLaVA-OneVision), (ii) embodied reasoning VQA (RefSpatial, RoboPoint, ShareRobot, RoboVQA), (iii) a custom RoboAlign VQA dataset generated by prompting Gemini-2.5-Pro with robot images and metadata to produce grounding/captioning QA pairs, (iv) a CoT reasoning distillation dataset, and (v) a FAST-tokenized action prediction dataset from BridgeV2. The FAST tokenizer compresses action chunks via DCT followed by BPE into discrete tokens added to the MLLM vocabulary. Stage 2 applies GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) on a tiny 12.8K subset of BridgeV2 FAST-token data. The reward is the average of a format reward (binary: correct think/answer tag structure) and an accuracy reward computed as the longest correct prefix of predicted FAST tokens divided by target length. The RL stage explicitly encourages the model to reason in natural language before emitting action tokens, then optimizes the full reasoning trace toward token-level action accuracy. The resulting MLLM backbone is then frozen, and a diffusion-based action head (following GR00T-N1.5) is trained on benchmark-specific robot data to produce the final VLA. Key Results: Evaluated on LIBERO, CALVIN ABC→D zero-shot transfer, and a real Franka Research 3 robot, RoboAlign consistently outperforms all baselines. On LIBERO (500 trials/category), average success improves from 73.9% (base Qwen2.5VL-7B) to 86.8% with RoboAlign, a 17.5% relative gain over the SFT-only baseline (81.5%). The Goal category jumps from 42.4% to 87.2%, and the Long-horizon category from 63.2% to 70.0%. On CALVIN, average task completion length improves from 2.16 to 2.57 (18.9% relative), with length-5 success rising from 18.1% to 22.2%—the only method that does not degrade on longer chains. In the real-robot pick-and-place setting, average success doubles from 32.3% to 66.7% (+106.6%). The RL stage consumes less than 1% of the SFT data (12.8K vs. 2.28M samples) yet drives most of the gain. Action-based RL outperforms language-based RL (83.6% avg LIBERO) and visual trajectory-based RL (85.1%) which both plateau on long-horizon tasks. SFT-based alignment with ECoT degrades performance to 67.7%, confirming RL is essential. KNN classification accuracy on MLLM hidden states improves from 39.06% to 69.79%, showing RL sharpens state-discriminative representations. On MLLM benchmarks (Table 9), RoboAlign (SFT+RL) scores 1.38 on Robot-R1 Bench (vs. 1.02 for base Qwen, 1.55 for GPT-4o) and 87.90 on BLINK depth, outperforming RoboBrain 2.0 and CosmosReason1 while preserving MMStar general VQA performance (62.80 vs. 60.30 base). Strengths: - Precisely diagnoses the modality gap problem with controlled ablations showing that even the best embodied-reasoning MLLM (RoboBrain 2.0) degrades VLA performance, motivating the design choice of using action tokens as RL reward signal rather than language proxies. - Data efficiency of the RL stage is remarkable: 12.8K samples and ~1 GPU-hour improve all benchmarks substantially over 30-hour SFT, making the approach practically attractive. - Generalization is demonstrated across two MLLM backbones (Qwen2.5-VL-7B and Qwen3-VL-8B), three benchmarks of different character (LIBERO in-domain, CALVIN zero-shot transfer, real robot), and short vs. long-horizon tasks, lending credibility to the claims. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The reward signal—prefix accuracy of FAST tokens against a single ground-truth trajectory—is a brittle proxy: many action sequences are physically equivalent but differ in token prefix, so the reward may penalize valid behaviors and create reward hacking incentives that are not analyzed. - The RL training data (BridgeV2) and the VLA evaluation environments (LIBERO, CALVIN, real robot) share the same robot morphology (Franka arm) and similar tabletop manipulation domains; it is unclear whether the alignment generalizes to different embodiments, action spaces (e.g., humanoid), or out-of-distribution tasks.
Recommended 2603.21341 PDF
#15 — Highly Innovative (79)

Repurposing Geometric Foundation Models for Multi-view Diffusion (20 ▲ )

Wooseok Jang, Seonghu Jeon, Jisang Han, Jinhyeok Choi, Minkyung Kwon, Seungryong Kim, Saining Xie, Sainan Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Mar 2026
Why this rank: GLD introduces a genuinely novel paradigm by repurposing geometric foundation model features as the latent space for diffusion, replacing the standard VAE latent space with geometry-aware representations. The insight that DA3's internal features encode cross-view geometric structure — and that this can substitute for massive T2I pretraining — is both principled and surprising. The boundary layer analysis identifying level 1 as the optimal synthesis point is a non-obvious finding with theoretical grounding. The 2.8× lower ATE and 2.6× lower RPE over VAE baselines demonstrate real geometric fidelity gains, not just perceptual improvements, and training from scratch while outperforming fine-tuned T2I models is compelling.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Novel view synthesis (NVS) requires generating geometrically consistent images across viewpoints, yet existing latent diffusion approaches operate in VAE latent spaces designed for single-image synthesis — spaces that encode no cross-view geometric structure. Models must then implicitly discover geometric correspondences from scratch, requiring large-scale text-to-image pretraining to compensate. The paper asks whether a latent space with geometry already baked in can replace these generic representations, improving both efficiency and 3D consistency without relying on massive generative priors. Methodology: The paper proposes Geometric Latent Diffusion (GLD), which repurposes the internal feature space of a geometric foundation model — specifically Depth Anything 3 (DA3) — as the latent space for a multi-view diffusion model. DA3 extracts four levels of multi-view features via a ViT encoder with 3D attention; GLD trains a diffusion model to synthesize in this geometry-aware space rather than in a VAE or DINO space. Three design contributions address practical challenges: (1) A ViT-based RGB decoder Drgb is trained to map DA3 multi-level features back to pixel space with level-wise dropout for robustness. (2) An optimal synthesis boundary layer is identified (level 1 out of 4) — features at deeper levels are deterministically derived by propagating through the frozen backbone, avoiding the cost of diffusing all levels. (3) A cascaded generation scheme synthesizes level 0 features conditioned on generated level 1 features to enforce cross-level alignment. The diffusion model uses a DiT architecture with flow-matching, 3D self-attention with PRoPE, and Plücker ray embeddings for camera conditioning, trained from scratch on Re10K, DL3DV, HyperSim, and TartanAir. Key Results: GLD outperforms VAE and DINO latent space baselines consistently across all in-domain and zero-shot benchmarks on both 2D metrics (PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS) and 3D metrics (ATE, RPEr, RPEt, reprojection error, MEt3R). On Re10K, GLD achieves PSNR 16.36 vs. VAE 15.66 and DINO 15.64; on DL3DV, 15.50 vs. VAE 14.73. The 3D consistency gains are particularly large: up to 2.8× lower ATE and 2.6× lower RPE vs. VAE. GLD also surpasses all state-of-the-art methods fine-tuned from large-scale T2I models (MVGenMaster, Matrix3D, CAMEO, NVComposer, CAT3D) on in-domain 2D metrics, despite being trained from scratch. Training convergence is 4.4× faster vs. the VAE latent space. The DA3 decoder alone reconstructs Re10K images at PSNR 35.41 / SSIM 0.960 / LPIPS 0.019, outperforming pretrained SD VAE and SDXL VAE. Zero-shot depth maps and 3D point clouds decoded directly from synthesized latents exhibit strong global consistency without any additional training. Strengths: - Principled insight: replacing generic VAE latents with geometry-aware foundation model features provides a clean inductive bias for NVS that reduces the need for massive pretraining — validated with rigorous ablations. - Strong empirical gains on 3D consistency metrics (ATE, RPE) where prior methods are notoriously weak, demonstrating real geometric fidelity rather than just perceptual sharpness. - The boundary layer analysis is thorough and theoretically motivated, showing that level 1 uniquely balances geometric correspondence (high PCK) and photometric fidelity — a non-obvious sweet spot. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The 4.4× training speedup claim and from-scratch comparisons do not account for the pretraining cost of the DA3 backbone itself; total compute including backbone pretraining likely exceeds T2I fine-tuned baselines. - Out-of-domain generalization to object-centric scenes (Mip-NeRF 360) is competitive but not dominant, partly because GLD trains only on scene-level data — the gap vs. methods trained on object-centric data is not fully addressed. - The cascaded generation adds a second inference pass for level 0, and the interaction between cascaded noise conditioning and imperfect level-1 latents at scale is not deeply analyzed.
Recommended 2603.22275 PDF
#16 — Highly Innovative (79)

TerraScope: Pixel-Grounded Visual Reasoning for Earth Observation (42 ▲ )

Yan Shu, Bin Ren, Zhitong Xiong, Xiao Xiang Zhu, Begüm Demir, Nicu Sebe, Paolo Rota · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Mar 2026
Why this rank: TerraScope introduces a genuinely novel pixel-grounded chain-of-thought mechanism where segmentation masks are generated inline during reasoning and their visual tokens are re-injected into the LLM's KV cache at each step — a tight perception-reasoning loop that is architecturally distinct from prior grounding approaches. The adaptive optical-SAR modality selection via cross-attention relevance scoring is a creative solution to a real multi-sensor challenge in earth observation. The bootstrapped Terra-CoT data curation pipeline and the dual-metric benchmark (answer accuracy + segmentation IoU) fill legitimate gaps and demonstrate strong empirical validation, with the IoU-accuracy correlation providing causal evidence for the grounding mechanism's utility. The domain is narrower than general vision-language research, but the technical contributions — particularly the interleaved mask-reasoning architecture — have clear transferability potential.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing vision-language models (VLMs), including state-of-the-art general-purpose models (GPT-4o, Qwen3-VL) and earth observation (EO)-specific variants (EarthDial), fail at fine-grained geospatial reasoning tasks that require pixel-accurate spatial analysis — e.g., computing land-cover coverage percentages, measuring inter-class distances, or estimating disaster damage. Two structural gaps explain this failure: (1) EO imagery depicts continuous spatial distributions rather than discrete objects, making coarse-grained grounding (bounding boxes, crops) insufficient; and (2) EO analysis inherently involves multi-sensor (optical + SAR) and multi-temporal data that existing VLMs cannot jointly reason over. No benchmark existed to evaluate pixel-level geospatial reasoning, and no training dataset paired segmentation masks with reasoning chains at scale. Methodology: TerraScope introduces pixel-grounded visual reasoning, where segmentation masks are generated inline during chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning and the resulting masked visual tokens are injected back into the reasoning sequence at each step. The architecture builds on InternVL3 (8B) augmented with a SAM-2-initialized mask decoder (+228M parameters, ~2.8% overhead). A special [SEG] token triggers mask generation; the mask is aligned to the vision encoder's token grid, and visual tokens with >50% spatial coverage are selected (capped at λ=128 via spatial uniform sampling) and inserted into the LLM's KV cache before the next reasoning step. For optical-SAR pairs, text-guided token-level modality selection computes cross-attention relevance scores between question embeddings and each modality's visual tokens, then picks the higher-relevance modality per spatial position — adaptively favoring optical in cloud-free regions and SAR under cloud cover. For multi-temporal inputs, explicit temporal indicators route each [SEG] token to the correct time-point image. Training is two-stage: (1) grounded pretraining on 2M referring expression segmentation pairs (only mask decoder trained); (2) instruction tuning on Terra-CoT (1M samples) with LoRA on the LLM and full fine-tuning of the projector and mask decoder. The loss combines cross-entropy on text/[SEG] tokens and Dice + pixel-wise cross-entropy on masks (λ=0.5). Terra-CoT is curated via a bootstrapped pipeline: an intermediate captioner (TerraScope-Cap) trained on 250K Cap-CoT samples annotates unlabeled imagery with masks and captions, which then feed hierarchical VQA synthesis at two levels — L1 (basic spatial grounding: existence, counting, localization, area, boundary, distance) and L2 (complex multi-step: spatial relationship inference, semantic land suitability). TerraScope-Bench, the first pixel-grounded EO reasoning benchmark, comprises 3,837 expert-verified multiple-choice questions across six tasks with dual evaluation metrics: answer accuracy and segmentation IoU against ground-truth masks. Key Results: On TerraScope-Bench, TerraScope (68.9% avg accuracy) substantially outperforms all baselines: Qwen3-VL-Think (43.3%), GPT-4o (38.7%), and the best EO-specific model EarthMind (42.1%). Fine-tuning InternVL3 and GLM-4.1V-Think on Terra-CoT alone (without the pixel-grounding architecture) achieves 54.9% and 59.6% respectively, confirming that both the data and the architecture contribute independently. In grounding IoU, TerraScope also leads across all six task categories. On zero-shot generalization, TerraScope achieves 73.9% on Landsat30-AU (vs. 65.0% for Qwen3-VL-Think) and 46.5% on DisasterM3 (vs. 32.5% for Qwen3-VL-Think), and 79.36% overall on Landsat30-AU VQA tasks vs. 75.55% for MiMo. Ablations confirm that precise pixel masks are critical: replacing masks with random token selection degrades to 43.2%, and bounding-box-based selection reaches only 62.8% vs. TerraScope's 68.9%. There is a strong Pearson correlation (r=0.607, p<0.001) between intermediate segmentation quality (mean IoU 0.628 for correct vs. 0.443 for incorrect predictions) and final answer accuracy. For optical-SAR multi-modal reasoning on DisasterM3, TerraScope (41.5%) far exceeds all baselines (best: 20.8% for LLaVA-OV), demonstrating the effectiveness of adaptive modality selection. Strengths: - The interleaved pixel-CoT mechanism is technically sound and well-motivated: grounding reasoning steps in precise segmentation masks is shown empirically (via IoU-accuracy correlation) to be causal, not merely correlated, to correct spatial reasoning. - The Terra-CoT dataset curation pipeline is elegant — bootstrapping from a self-trained captioner enables large-scale annotation without human labeling, and the hierarchical L1/L2 synthesis produces structured diversity in reasoning complexity. - TerraScope-Bench fills a genuine gap: the dual evaluation metric (answer accuracy + segmentation IoU) is the right design choice to prevent models from achieving correct answers through hallucinated or shortcut reasoning paths. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The model processes only RGB bands, discarding the rich spectral information (NIR, SWIR, red-edge) available in multispectral sensors like Sentinel-2 that are routinely used in EO for land cover discrimination; this directly causes failure on spectrally similar classes. - Temporal reasoning is limited to bi-temporal (two time-point) comparison, which is insufficient for the long time-series analysis required in applications like urban growth monitoring, deforestation tracking, and climate trend analysis — a significant gap for real-world EO deployment.
Recommended 2603.19039 PDF
#17 — Highly Innovative (78)

Revealing Behavioral Plasticity in Large Language Models: A Token-Conditional Perspective

Liyuan Mao, Le Yu, Jing Zhou, Chujie Zheng, Bowen Yu, Chang Gao, Shixuan Liu, An Yang, Weinan Zhang, JunYang Lin · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper scores 78 for its creative insight that LLMs possess latent behavioral plasticity activatable via token-prefix conditioning at inference time, without parameter modification. The token-conditional RL framework is theoretically grounded and achieves the practically significant result of simultaneously improving both factual QA (+9.4 pp on SimpleQA) and mathematical reasoning, resolving a long-standing capability trade-off. The emergent recalibrative reasoning behavior—absent from training data—is genuinely surprising and suggests deeper untapped structure in pretrained models. However, validation on a single model family (Qwen3-30B) and reliance on LLM-as-judge rewards temper the generalizability claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) such as Qwen3-30B-A3B-2507-Thinking excel at complex mathematical reasoning via step-by-step chain-of-thought but perform poorly on straightforward factual question answering (18.9% on SimpleQA vs. 22.1% for the Instruct variant). Conversely, Instruct models answer factual queries well but underperform on hard math. This capability divergence is conventionally attributed to different training distributions, and closing it has required separate specialized models or costly parameter updates. The paper challenges this view by demonstrating that LLMs possess intrinsic "behavioral plasticity"—latent behavioral modes that can be activated at inference time without any parameter modification, analogous to chameleons adapting their coloration to environmental cues. Methodology: The paper introduces two contributions. First, token-conditional generation (ToCo Gen): given a factual query, the authors extract the first k≈6 tokens from a direct-answer response generated by an Instruct model, prepend these as a forced prefix inside the `<think>` block, and let the Thinking model continue generation. This inference-time trick shifts the model from exploratory chain-of-thought into concise direct-answer mode, immediately improving SimpleQA accuracy from 18.9% to 20.7% with no training. Second, Token-Conditional Reinforcement Learning (ToCoRL) internalizes this transient adaptation. The RL objective combines a standard REINFORCE policy gradient with a KL divergence toward the token-conditional policy π_TC, which is defined as the original policy π augmented by an advantage-weighted correction term. In practice, each rollout batch mixes normal generation and token-conditional generation sampled from the composite policy π_mix (parameterized by a mixing weight λ). This mixed rollout design reduces advantage-estimation variance and provides a provable lower bound on the true objective. Rewards are binary (0/1) via LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o for SimpleQA), and a format penalty is applied for malformed outputs. Training uses 10K factual Q&A pairs jointly with 10K mathematical problem pairs, starting from Qwen3-30B-A3B-2507-Thinking. The algorithm is grounded in formal proofs showing π_TC is well-defined and that the mixed-policy surrogate gradient is equivalent to the primary objective's gradient. Key Results: ToCoRL improves Qwen3-30B-A3B-2507-Thinking on SimpleQA from 18.9 to 28.3 (+9.4 absolute), on AA-Omniscience from 16.1 to 18.6, while simultaneously improving AIME'24 from 84.8 to 89.0 and AIME'25 from 80.5 to 81.5—demonstrating that factual and mathematical capabilities coexist without interference. Baseline comparisons are decisive: GRPO achieves 23.6/16.7 on SimpleQA/Omniscience and degrades AIME'25 slightly; Adaptive-Thinking reaches 23.9/13.8 with noticeable AIME regression; Instruct-Tuning GRPO achieves 21.1/14.8 with larger math degradation. The emergent behavior is qualitatively novel: the ToCoRL-trained model begins responses with a direct-answer attempt and then performs "recalibrative reasoning," iteratively enumerating candidate answers and self-verifying until sufficiently confident—a pattern absent from the SFT data or the base Thinking model. Ablations show robustness across token prefix lengths k∈{6,7,8}, KL coefficients λ∈{0.5,1,2}, and alternative prefix providers including smaller models (Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct). The discovered behavior also transfers effectively via SFT distillation: regenerating factual SFT data using the ToCoRL-trained model and fine-tuning a fresh base model yields the largest accuracy gains across all SFT variants. Strengths: - The core insight—that behavioral plasticity can be exposed via token-prefix conditioning and stabilized via RL—is conceptually clean, well-motivated, and supported by formal theoretical guarantees (proofs for well-definedness of π_TC and gradient equivalence of the surrogate objective). - Achieves simultaneous improvement on both factual QA and complex math, resolving a long-standing capability trade-off without a separate model, which is a practically significant result. - The emergent "recalibrative reasoning" behavior is genuinely novel and not simply a retrieval of SFT patterns, evidenced by its appearance even when starting from a base model with deliberately different SFT behavior. - Strong robustness analysis across hyperparameters and prefix providers substantiates that the method's efficacy is not tied to specific implementation choices. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The experimental validation is limited to a single application domain (factual QA + math reasoning on Qwen3-30B). The authors acknowledge computational constraints preclude broader evaluation across task types and model families, limiting generalizability claims. - The approach depends on LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Flash) for reward signals, introducing noise and potential reward hacking risk. Less capable judge models are noted to potentially misclassify long incorrect responses as correct, a vulnerability not fully addressed. - The recalibrative reasoning behavior involves potentially long candidate enumeration sequences, which may be computationally wasteful at inference time even when ultimately correct—a latency cost not characterized in the paper. - The theoretical analysis operates in the energy-function perspective of discrete distributions, which, while elegant, does not directly translate to gradient magnitudes or convergence rates for billion-parameter models in practice.
Recommended 2603.08398 PDF
#18 — Highly Innovative (78)

SIMART: Decomposing Monolithic Meshes into Sim-ready Articulated Assets via MLLM (33 ▲ )

Chuanrui Zhang, Minghan Qin, Yuang Wang, Baifeng Xie, Hang Li, Ziwei Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Mar 2026
Why this rank: SIMART scores 78 for its elegant sparse zero-token VQ-VAE design that resolves a concrete scalability bottleneck in 3D asset articulation, and for its unified single-stage architecture that jointly predicts part decomposition, kinematic structure, and physical properties — avoiding error accumulation of prior multi-stage pipelines. The strong out-of-distribution generalization to AI-generated meshes (IoU 0.777 vs. 0.618 for the next best method) suggests genuine structural understanding rather than dataset memorization. The approach opens a practical path toward automated sim-ready asset creation for embodied AI, a high-impact bottleneck in the field.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The vast majority of 3D assets available today are static, monolithic meshes lacking kinematic structure or physical metadata needed for simulation and embodied AI. While recent 3D generative models can synthesize high-quality static objects, converting them into simulation-ready articulated assets (with URDF specifications, joint axes, limits, and part decompositions) remains unsolved. Prior multi-stage pipelines decouple part segmentation from joint estimation, accumulating errors across modules; direct MLLM-based approaches suffer from O(N³) dense voxel tokenization that causes memory exhaustion and limits scalability to complex multi-part objects. Methodology: SIMART is a unified MLLM framework built on Qwen3-VL-8B that jointly performs part-level mesh decomposition and kinematic parameter prediction from a single forward pass. The central technical contribution is a Sparse 3D VQ-VAE: a 3D-UNet encoder maps the input 64³ voxel grid to a 16³ latent grid, which is then aggregated to an 8×8×8 grid with 64-dimensional features per token. Critically, unoccupied voxels are assigned a reserved zero token (index 0) rather than passing through full vector quantization, reducing the number of active tokens by ~70%. Each occupied voxel is serialized as a triplet ⟨voxel⟩ [xyz] [K] with explicit coordinate encoding (xyz = 64x + 8y + z), enabling spatial reasoning over variable-length sequences. These sparse geometry tokens are concatenated with ViT-extracted visual tokens and text instruction tokens and processed jointly by the MLLM. Outputs include per-part voxel token sequences (decoded back to point clouds via the VQ-VAE decoder, then mapped to the original mesh surface via graph-based Gaussian-kernel smoothing) and a structured URDF JSON specifying joint types, axes, origins, limits, material density, and friction. Training uses 39,600 objects (5,600 articulated + 34,000 static), augmented to 960K QA pairs each for URDF generation and part grounding tasks, with two-stage VQ-VAE pretraining (60K steps/stage on 8×A100) followed by MLLM fine-tuning (30K steps on 32×A100). Key Results: On SIMART-Bench (in-domain PartNet-Mobility items), SIMART achieves joint type accuracy of 0.928, axis error 0.080, origin error 0.111, IoU 0.690, and Chamfer Distance 0.087—outperforming the strongest competitor, Particulate (0.822 / 0.208 / 0.204 / 0.643 / 0.140). On out-of-distribution AI-generated objects, the gap widens: SIMART attains IoU 0.777 vs. Particulate's 0.618 and CD 0.079 vs. 0.106. For the 3D part grounding task on AI-generated items, SIMART achieves IoU 0.807 and CD 0.018, versus PhysX-Anything (0.067 / 0.347) and P3SAM + Qwen3-VL-235B (0.507 / 0.234)—a >12× reduction in Chamfer Distance over PhysX-Anything. Ablations confirm that the zero-token mechanism is critical: replacing it with force-sparse (no zero token) raises token count from 516 to 862 and degrades IoU from 0.832 to 0.678, while dense tokenization (4,138 tokens) causes OOM entirely. Adding visual features on top of sparse geometry tokens yields the final boost (IoU 0.745→0.832, CD 0.074→0.055). Strengths: - The sparse zero-token VQ-VAE is an elegant, well-motivated design that resolves a concrete scalability bottleneck; the emergent behavior (VQ-VAEs naturally learn empty-space tokens) provides principled justification. - Truly unified single-stage architecture: part decomposition, kinematic structure, physical properties, and real-world scale are all predicted jointly, avoiding the error accumulation of multi-stage pipelines. - Strong OOD generalization to AI-generated meshes with diverse topologies—IoU 0.777 on novel objects is substantially better than all baselines, suggesting genuine structural understanding rather than dataset memorization. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark (SIMART-Bench) is small: only 36 AI-generated assets across 10+ categories, with ground-truth labels requiring significant manual annotation effort. Conclusions on OOD generalization rest on a thin evaluation set. - The 8×8×8 voxel resolution is coarse; Table 4 shows that a 16×8×8 grid yields meaningfully better reconstruction (CD 2.27 vs. 4.19 ×10⁻⁵), but was sacrificed for memory budget. This fundamental resolution ceiling may limit accuracy on fine-grained parts (thin hinges, small knobs) and complex assemblies with more than ~4 parts. - The method is constrained to objects with simple kinematic topologies (revolute, prismatic, fixed joints); complex mechanisms with multiple degrees of freedom per link, closed-loop kinematics, or non-rigid deformations are not addressed, limiting applicability to more intricate robotic manipulation scenarios.
Recommended 2603.23386 PDF
#19 — Highly Innovative (78)

ProactiveBench: Benchmarking Proactiveness in Multimodal Large Language Models (26 ▲ )

Thomas De Min, Subhankar Roy, Stéphane Lathuilière, Elisa Ricci, Massimiliano Mancini · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Mar 2026
Why this rank: ProactiveBench defines a genuinely new capability axis — proactiveness — that no prior benchmark has measured, shifting the evaluation paradigm from reactive accuracy to interactive information-seeking behavior. The finding that proactiveness does not scale with model size (InternVL3-1B outperforms InternVL3-78B) is surprising and challenges conventional scaling assumptions. The invalid-proactive-option ablation is a particularly sharp diagnostic that reveals apparent proactiveness in many models is merely abstention avoidance, not genuine understanding. The RL post-training result generalizing to unseen scenarios provides an actionable path forward for the community.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) operate in a purely reactive mode: given an ambiguous or unanswerable visual query, they either hallucinate an answer or abstain. A more human-like behavior would be proactive — recognizing when a query cannot be answered from the current visual input and requesting a specific user intervention (e.g., move an occluding object, deblur the image, rotate the camera) to gather the additional information needed. No benchmark existed to measure this proactive capability, leaving the field without a systematic understanding of where current MLLMs stand or how to improve them. Methodology: The authors introduce ProactiveBench, a benchmark constructed by repurposing seven existing datasets (ROD, VSOD, MVP-N, ImageNet-C, QuickDraw, ChangeIt, MS-COCO) into seven proactive scenarios: occluded object removal, temporal occlusion resolution, uninformative viewpoint correction, image quality enhancement, sketch detail augmentation, temporal ambiguity resolution, and camera movement. Each sample is a sequence starting from a minimally informative frame and progressing to a fully informative reference frame; the model must either answer correctly or request the appropriate user intervention. Evaluation is framed as a Markov decision process in the multiple-choice question answering (MCQA) setting and also as open-ended generation (OEG) scored with an LLM-as-judge (Qwen3-8B). A filtering step removes samples solvable without proactiveness (those with ≥25% first-turn accuracy across models), reducing the benchmark from 17,909 to 7,557 samples. 22 MLLMs are evaluated, including LLaVA variants, Qwen2.5-VL, InternVL3, GPT-4.1, GPT-5.2, and o4-mini. To investigate whether proactiveness can be learned, GRPO-based RL post-training is applied to two models (LLaVA-NeXT-Mistral-7B and Qwen2.5-VL-3B) using ~27k single-turn training samples from QuickDraw and MS-COCO, with a reward function that rewards correct predictions (rc=1), valid proactive suggestions (rp∈{0.5,0.75,1.0}), and penalizes wrong answers (rw=0). Key Results: Across 22 models, average MCQA accuracy is only 17.5% versus 79.8% on reference (unoccluded) frames — a >60% gap. Proactiveness does not scale with model size: InternVL3-1B (27.1%) outperforms InternVL3-8B (12.7%) and InternVL3-78B (15.6%). Older LLaVA-1.5-7B (24.8%) beats the much larger LLaVA-OV-72B (13.0%). Closed-source models show best raw accuracy (GPT-4.1: 30.9%, o4-mini: 34.0%) but achieve implausibly high COCO accuracy (~94%), suggesting data contamination. Replacing valid proactive actions with invalid ones collapses proactiveness in apparently-proactive models by 60–90%, revealing that elevated proactive suggestion rates often reflect an anti-abstention bias rather than genuine understanding. Hint-conditioned prompting increases the proactive suggestion rate by 1.9 on average but causes 16% of evaluations to blind-loop through proactive actions, actually reducing net accuracy. Conversation history conditioning similarly increases proactive suggestions but drops accuracy by 7%. RL post-training with rp=0.5 yields LLaVA-NeXT-Mistral-7B at 40.4% avg accuracy (vs. 4.5% original), surpassing all zero-shot models including o4-mini (34.0%), and generalizes to unseen scenarios (e.g., CIT improves from 17.2% to 47.2%). Setting rp=rc causes excessive proactive looping and accuracy collapse, confirming that reward calibration is critical. Strengths: - Rigorous benchmark construction with automated annotation, a principled filtering pipeline, and multi-turn MDP framing that cleanly separates recognition ability from proactiveness. - Comprehensive evaluation across 22 diverse MLLMs with both MCQA and OEG protocols; the invalid-proactive-option ablation is a particularly sharp diagnostic for distinguishing genuine proactiveness from abstention avoidance. - The RL post-training result is actionable and generalizes across unseen scenarios, providing a concrete baseline for future proactive MLLM development. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is constructed entirely from classification tasks (object/person/action recognition); it is unclear whether findings generalize to more open-ended or compositional visual queries where proactiveness is harder to operationalize. - The filtering threshold (≥25% first-turn accuracy) is applied across all models collectively, so residual easy samples for individual strong models remain, potentially underestimating the proactiveness gap for the best models. - RL training uses only single-turn interactions and two domains (QuickDraw, COCO), limiting the richness of learned proactive behaviors; the remaining large gap to reference accuracy (40.7% vs. 75.1%) indicates the approach is a proof of concept rather than a solved problem. - Potential MS-COCO training contamination in closed-source models (GPT-4.1/o4-mini achieve ~94% on COCO) is acknowledged but unverifiable, casting doubt on closed-model results for that subset.
Recommended 2603.19466 PDF
#20 — Highly Innovative (77)

Pseudo-Likelihood Training for Reasoning Diffusion Language Models

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 77 for its principled solution to a genuine open problem — how to do RL alignment for diffusion language models without biased likelihood approximations. The pseudo-KL objective grounded in the Hammersley-Clifford theorem is theoretically sound, and the augmented-sequence trick for efficient multi-conditional evaluation is a clever engineering contribution. Consistent empirical gains over all dLLM alignment baselines across math and coding tasks validate the approach. However, evaluation on only LLaDA-8B limits generalizability claims, and the practical conditional approximation reintroduces some of the bias the theory aims to eliminate.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Policy-gradient reinforcement learning (PGRL) methods like GRPO have become the dominant approach for post-training LLMs on reasoning tasks, but they fundamentally require efficient computation of token-level log-likelihoods. Diffusion-based language models (dLLMs), such as LLaDA, generate text through iterative masked denoising rather than autoregressive factorization, making exact likelihood computation intractable. Existing attempts to adapt PGRL to dLLMs (e.g., diffu-GRPO/d1) rely on biased Monte-Carlo ELBO approximations or mean-field approximations of the joint density, leading to gradient bias and training instability. The gap is the lack of a principled, scalable RL alignment objective native to the dLLM parameterization. Methodology: PADRE reframes KL-regularized RL as a distribution-matching problem and replaces the standard joint KL divergence with a pseudo-KL (PKL) objective over per-token conditional distributions. The key theoretical insight is that for strictly positive distributions, matching all single-token conditionals p(y^i | y^{-i}) is sufficient to match the full joint distribution (Hammersley-Clifford). The resulting tractable objective is J_PKL = E_{y~π_θ} Σ_i [log π_θ(y^i | y^{-i}) - log q̃(y^i | y^{-i})], where q̃ is the Boltzmann target distribution. This avoids computing the partition function and requires only conditional token probabilities, which dLLMs can approximate efficiently by masking one position at a time and reading the denoiser output. To compute all L conditionals in roughly one forward pass, PADRE constructs an augmented sequence x ⊕ y_{1:L} ⊕ m_{L+1:2L} with shared positional embeddings and a custom attention mask, so each mask row L+i attends to y_{-i} without cross-contamination. Two efficient approximation variants are proposed: PKL-U (block mean-field, interpolates between full PKL and d1) and PKL-T (trajectory-based, uses the denoising unmasking order to restrict attention). Training follows the GRPO recipe with group-normalized advantages, clipped importance weights, KL regularization, and optional prompt token perturbation as dropout regularization. Key Results: On math reasoning benchmarks with LLaDA-8B as the base model, PKL+ (with prompt perturbation) achieves 87.3% on GSM8K (+5.2% over base, +5.2% over d1-LLaDA), 42.4% on MATH500 (+6.2% over base, +2.2% over d1-LLaDA), and 44.3% on MATH (+5.4% over base). The full PKL variant achieves 85.6/40.9/41.6 on the three benchmarks, consistently outperforming diffu-GRPO, d1, VRPO, and wd1. On coding benchmarks (HumanEval, MBPP), PKL achieves the best results at all tested sequence lengths (128/256/512 tokens), e.g., 36.2% on HumanEval-256 vs. 32.9% for d1 and 47.4% on MBPP-256 vs. 44.7% for d1. A KL divergence diagnostic confirms that PKL's conditional approximation error (avg KL < 1) is substantially lower than d1's mean-field approximation error (KL = 2.87–3.15 on GSM8K/MATH500). FLOP-equalized comparisons show that at sufficient compute budgets, PKL outperforms d1 on GSM8K, though d1 has an edge at very low FLOP counts. Strengths: - Theoretically principled: the pseudo-KL objective shares the same global optima as KL-regularized PGRL, unlike the biased approximations used in competing dLLM methods, and this is backed by a formal theorem (Hammersley-Clifford) and derivation. - Practical efficiency: the augmented-sequence trick with shared positional embeddings and custom attention masks enables approximate computation of all L conditionals in near-constant forward passes, making the method scalable. - Consistent empirical gains: PADRE outperforms all dLLM alignment baselines across math and coding benchmarks by meaningful margins, with the PKL-U and PKL-T variants providing a compute-accuracy tradeoff spectrum. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The conditional likelihood approximation used in practice (masking one token, reading denoiser output) involves the same Markovian approximation as prior work; the paper acknowledges that PKL's per-token conditional KL is still non-zero (avg < 1 but not 0), so the theoretical guarantee of bias-free optimization does not fully carry through to the implemented algorithm. - Experimental scope is narrow: only LLaDA-8B is evaluated, no larger model scales are tested, and tasks like Sudoku (where dLLMs already struggle) are excluded—making it unclear how well the method generalizes across model families, scales, or longer-horizon reasoning tasks where the conditioning approximations may degrade. - The efficient multi-conditional evaluation via attention mask sharing is only exact for a single transformer layer; across multiple layers the attended context for y^j bleeds through to y^i, requiring the PKL-U or PKL-T heuristics whose theoretical properties revert to those of prior approximations.
Recommended
#21 — Highly Innovative (76)

Safeguarding Language Models via Self-Destruct Trapdoor

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 76 for a genuinely creative exploitation of an overlooked hardware-level property — floating-point overflow — as an in-weights defense mechanism with zero inference overhead. The null-space SVD construction that cleanly separates harmful from benign activation subspaces is technically elegant, and the emergent resistance to fine-tuning (NaN gradients from iteration one) is a surprising and non-obvious side effect that strengthens the defense beyond its design intent. The extension to fingerprinting and bias mitigation demonstrates generality. However, the fundamental vulnerability to trivial weight-magnitude thresholding (injected weights at 10^17 vs. typical two-digit values) severely limits real-world deployment against any adversary with model access, capping the practical impact of an otherwise inventive mechanism.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Language models (LMs) remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks that bypass alignment training, and existing defenses fall into unsatisfying trade-offs: alignment training is brittle and computationally expensive, external input/output filters add inference overhead and rely on auxiliary models, and interpretability-based monitors are limited in maturity and scope. A critical hardware-level property of modern LM frameworks—that limited-precision floating-point formats (BF16, FP16, FP32) trigger deterministic overflow errors when matrix products exceed representable range—has been entirely overlooked as a potential defense mechanism. This work exploits that gap. Methodology: Self-Destruct embeds a protective trapdoor directly into a single attention head's query and key weight matrices using a three-step, training-free calibration procedure. Step 1 (Null-space Isolation): compute the SVD of benign activations H and extract V₀, the right null space, which spans directions invisible to benign inputs. Step 2 (Targeted Subspace Extraction): project harmful activations H′ into this null space and extract the top-δ right singular vectors via a second SVD, identifying directions that maximally activate on H′ while remaining suppressed on H. Step 3 (Head Injection): map these directions back to model space, scale them by a destruction factor D_factor such that harmful activations produce query-key dot products approaching the BF16/FP32 maximum (≈3.39×10³⁸), and add the result to one attention head's Ŵ_q and Ŵ_k. For harmful inputs, the injected component dominates and causes ‖(q+q′)(k+k′)ᵀ‖ ≈ Ω → overflow; for benign inputs, the null-space construction ensures the contribution is suppressed (≈0). K-means clustering of harmful activations improves coverage across diverse harmful prompt distributions. The destruction factor is selected via binary search on a validation set, and all hyperparameters (layer, head index, SVD width δ, cluster count n) are chosen by grid search maximizing Δ = RefusalRate(harmful) − RefusalRate(harmless). Key Results: Evaluated across 10 models from 5 families (Falcon3, Llama-3.2, Mistral-0.3, Qwen2.5, Gemma2; 1B–14B parameters) on four jailbreak benchmarks (MalwareGen, PromptInject, PAIR, AutoDAN) and four QA capability benchmarks (PIQA, OpenBookQA, SIQA, ARC). Self-Destruct achieves the lowest average ASR in 9 of 10 models. Representative reductions: MalwareGen ASR Qwen2.5-7B 39→1%, Mistral-7B 67→4%, Gemma-2-2B 10→1%; AutoDAN ASR drops to 0 on Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B; PAIR ASR Qwen2.5-14B 33→15%, Gemma-2-9B 22→1%. Calibration achieves Δ ≥ 0.97 on both train and test splits across all models, with 7B+ models typically reaching Δ = 0.99. On capability benchmarks, the strongest models show near-zero accuracy degradation (Qwen2.5-14B avg Δ ≈ 0, Gemma-2-2B avg +1.2), while mid-size models incur moderate drops (Mistral-7B avg −3.2, Qwen2.5-7B avg −5.5). Crucially, fine-tuning the protected model fails entirely—backpropagation yields NaN gradients from the first iteration regardless of optimizer, batch size, or dataset, making the model effectively untrainable. Gradient-based GCG attacks also diverge immediately against protected models. The mechanism extends to model fingerprinting (Δ = 1.0 on Gemma-2-2B with human-readable passcodes; Δ = 0.95 with random 12–20-character strings on Qwen2.5-3B) and bias mitigation on BBQ (Δ = 1.0 across all tested configurations). Compatibility is confirmed across NVIDIA GPU generations, Apple MPS, attention backends (EAGER, SDPA, FLEX), and vLLM. Strengths: - Genuinely novel exploitation of floating-point overflow as an in-weights defense: zero inference overhead since the mechanism fires simultaneously with generation, unlike perplexity filtering (requires full-text generation) or SmoothLLM/Self-Defense (require extra LM runs). - Strong adversarial robustness against both fine-tuning and gradient-based attacks (GCG) as an emergent consequence of high-magnitude weight injection corrupting gradient flow—a notable and non-obvious side effect. - Broad versatility: the same SVD-based calibration framework extends to model fingerprinting and bias mitigation with only a dataset swap, demonstrating generality of the underlying mechanism. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The trapdoor is easily detectable and reversible by any user with model access: injected weights have magnitudes of 10¹⁷–10¹⁸ versus typical two-digit values, making a simple threshold scan (e.g., >10⁵) sufficient to identify and zero out the affected head—a fundamental transparency limitation the authors acknowledge. - Evaluation is restricted to BF16/FP32 and standard attention implementations; behavior under quantization (INT8, FP8, INT4), FP64, or cross-precision inference pipelines is entirely uncharacterized, which is a significant gap given the prevalence of quantized deployment. - The accuracy-refusal tradeoff is model-family-dependent and unpredictable: Qwen2.5-7B loses 5.5 points average QA accuracy and shows elevated refusal on benign PIQA/SIQA prompts, while Falcon3-7B gains 17.8 points—suggesting the mechanism's selectivity is sensitive to architecture in ways that are not yet well understood or controllable.
Recommended
#22 — Highly Innovative (76)

DUET: Disaggregated Hybrid Mamba-Transformer LLMs with Prefill and Decode-Specific Packages

Alish Kanani, Sangwan Lee, Han Lyu, Jiahao Lin, Jaehyun Park, Umit Y. Ogras · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: DUET is the first accelerator to jointly address phase disaggregation, SSM-attention unification, and chiplet-based scalability for hybrid Mamba-Transformer models. The state-stationary SSM dataflow on systolic arrays is technically elegant, eliminating SRAM traffic for recurrent intermediates by exploiting parameter-sharing structure — a fusion that prior unified architectures did not achieve. The algebraic reordering of the Mamba recurrence to reduce multiplications is a clean optimization. While the comparison against a single B200 rather than matched hardware budget weakens absolute claims, the architectural contribution is substantive.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM inference is split into two fundamentally different phases: a compute-bound prefill (prompt processing) and a memory bandwidth-bound decode (token generation). Existing GPU-based systems — even software-disaggregated ones like DistServe and Mooncake — use homogeneous hardware for both phases, forcing a mismatch where prefill underutilizes memory bandwidth and decode underutilizes compute. This problem is compounded in emerging hybrid Mamba-Transformer models, which interleave SSM (state-space model) recurrences with attention layers. SSM operations involve element-wise recurrences and inter-step dependencies that map poorly onto matmul-centric accelerators (e.g., GPUs with tensor cores), creating additional kernel-diversity challenges beyond the compute/memory asymmetry. No prior hardware accelerator simultaneously handles phase disaggregation, SSM-attention unification, and scalable chiplet-based design. Methodology: DUET proposes hardware-level phase disaggregation via two specialized chiplet packages. The Prefill Package uses systolic array chiplets backed by off-package GDDR7 memory (3 TB/s, 192 GB), targeting the compute-bound regime. A key innovation is a "state-stationary" SSM dataflow mapped onto the systolic array: by unrolling embedding dimensions (ED) across rows and state dimensions (N) across columns, and keeping the recurrent state X_k local within PEs, DUET eliminates all SRAM read-write traffic for SSM intermediates and sustains one SSM update every three cycles after an O(N) pipeline fill. An algebraic reordering of the Mamba recurrence ((Δ_k·u_k)B_k instead of (Δ_k·B_k)u_k) reduces two vector-wide multiplications to one. The same systolic array executes standard GEMM via output-stationary dataflow, with SSM-specific registers clock-gated during GEMM; mode switching is controlled by a single bit. The Decode Package uses vector-unit array chiplets with in-package HBM3e (12 TB/s, 288 GB), targeting bandwidth-bound GEMV and token-wise SSM. Each W=32-wide vector unit supports vector-vector mul/add, MAC+reduce for dot products, and holds one SSM intermediate register, enabling zero-overhead SSM execution without intermediate SRAM write-backs. Dot-product reduction is O(log N) via a tree structure. Both packages are implemented as chiplet-based systems fitting within ~2700–3000 mm² interposers. Design space exploration over systolic arrays (8×8 to 256×256) and vector-unit arrays (4×4 to 32×32, W∈{8,16,32,64}) selects Pareto-optimal configurations: 64×32 systolic arrays and 32-wide 16×8 vector-unit arrays. The final DUET system has 16 Prefill chiplets (192 systolic arrays each) and 8 Decode chiplets (96 vector-unit arrays each), both synthesized in TSMC 28 nm (targeting 7 nm for full deployment). Evaluation uses a cycle-accurate in-house simulator validated against RTL, modeling three LLMs (Nemotron-H-56B, Zamba2-7B, Llama3-8B) across four workloads (ArXiv, BWB, LongWriter, Chat) covering all prefill/decode length combinations. Key Results: Against an NVIDIA B200 GPU (2.25 PFLOP/s, 8 TB/s HBM, 192 GB), DUET achieves a geometric mean 4× reduction in TTFT, 1.4× higher decode throughput, and 1.5× lower time-between-tokens (TBT). For Nemotron-H-56B on ArXiv (long prefill/short decode), DUET reduces TTFT by 5.9× and improves throughput by 1.4× over B200. Against a prefill-friendly aggregated baseline (same Prefill package geometry but with both compute types on every chiplet), DUET reduces TTFT by 1.4×, improves throughput by 3.4×, and reduces TBT by 4×. Against a decode-friendly aggregated baseline, DUET reduces TTFT by 2.7×, marginally improves throughput (1.1×), and reduces TBT by 1.2×. Notably, B200 and prefill-friendly baselines cannot serve batch sizes above 64 on ArXiv due to KV/state cache capacity limits, while DUET sustains up to batch size 128 by streaming caches from Prefill to Decode concurrently. The DUET Prefill package achieves 4.4 PFLOP/s FP16 peak (vs. 2.3 PFLOP/s for B200) and the Decode package achieves 12 TB/s memory bandwidth (vs. 8 TB/s for B200). Both arrays reach 763 MHz in TSMC 28 nm synthesis. Strengths: - The state-stationary SSM dataflow on systolic arrays is technically elegant: it eliminates SRAM traffic for recurrent intermediates by exploiting parameter-sharing structure (B_k/C_k shared across rows/columns), achieving in-situ fusion that prior unified architectures (MARCA, HCSAs) did not accomplish. - The design is the first to jointly address phase disaggregation, SSM-attention unification, and chiplet-based scalability, filling a clear gap vs. SPAD (Transformer-only, monolithic), MARCA (Mamba-only, no disaggregation), and HLX (hybrid but aggregated). - Evaluation is rigorous: cycle-accurate RTL-validated simulation, three models spanning pure Transformer and hybrid SSM architectures, four workloads with diverse prefill/decode length distributions, and three meaningful baselines including aggregated phase-asymmetric systems. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation baseline is a single B200 GPU per phase; a fair comparison would pair two B200s (one for prefill, one for decode) to match DUET's doubled hardware budget, since DUET uses two separate packages whose combined cost likely exceeds a single GPU. - The simulation operates at 700 MHz on a 28 nm process, while the B200 runs at 1.8 GHz on a much more advanced node; scaling projections to 7 nm are estimated via DeepScale rather than validated tape-out, making absolute performance claims uncertain. - Inter-package cache transfer latency (KV-cache and SSM state moving from Prefill to Decode via NVLink/UALink) is assumed to be hidden by layer-level pipelining, but this assumption breaks for short models or large batch sizes where transfer volume is high relative to compute time — the paper does not quantify this overhead.
Search Results 2603.15530 PDF
#23 — Highly Innovative (76)

OpenResearcher: A Fully Open Pipeline for Long-Horizon Deep Research Trajectory Synthesis (51 ▲ )

Zhuofeng Li, Dongfu Jiang, Xueguang Ma, Haoxiang Zhang, Ping Nie, Yuyu Zhang, Kai Zou, Jianwen Xie, Yu Zhang, Wenhu Chen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Mar 2026
Why this rank: OpenResearcher's core contribution — decoupling corpus construction from trajectory synthesis to enable offline, reproducible deep research training at near-zero marginal cost — is a practically important architectural insight that could accelerate the entire field. The counterintuitive finding that incorrect trajectories are equally valuable for SFT (RQ1) challenges rejection-sampling orthodoxy and has broad implications. The three-primitive browser abstraction with empirically validated per-level gains is well-motivated. However, the one-time bootstrapping still requires online APIs, and the SFT-only approach leaves the RL ceiling unexplored.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Training deep research agents — systems that iteratively search the web, aggregate evidence, and reason across many steps — requires long-horizon trajectories with 100+ tool calls. Existing pipelines generate these trajectories using live web APIs (e.g., Google Search), which makes synthesis expensive (up to $28,800 for 5.76M search requests via SerpAPI), non-reproducible due to a changing live web, and difficult to analyze in a controlled manner because gold-document annotations are not exposed. Short-horizon datasets like NQ or 2WikiMultiHopQA are inadequate because they require only 2–5 retrieval steps. The paper asks: how can one synthesize high-quality, long-horizon deep research trajectories in a scalable, low-cost, reproducible, and analytically useful way? Methodology: OpenResearcher decouples corpus construction from trajectory synthesis. First, 6K challenging QA pairs are sampled from MiroVerse-v0.1, a dataset requiring long-horizon multi-hop reasoning. A one-time online bootstrapping step uses answer-guided Serper API queries to collect ~10K gold documents per question. These are merged with 15M FineWeb documents (≈10 trillion tokens) to form an offline corpus indexed via Qwen3-Embedding-8B and FAISS. The teacher model (GPT-OSS-120B) then operates entirely offline using three explicit browser primitives: `search` (returns top-K snippets), `open` (fetches full document content), and `find` (locates exact string matches within an opened page). This three-level abstraction mirrors human browsing behavior from broad retrieval down to pinpoint evidence localization. Sixteen trajectories per question are generated with diverse seeds, filtered to remove malformed or inconclusive traces, yielding 97K+ trajectories (55K correct ones used for SFT). A 30B-A3B Nemotron backbone is fine-tuned on these trajectories using Megatron-LM on 8 H100 GPUs with a 256K context window for ≈8 hours. Key Results: On BrowseComp-Plus (closed-web), OpenResearcher-30B-A3B achieves 54.8%, a +34.0 point absolute improvement over the base model (20.8%) and outperforming GPT-4.1 (36.4%), Claude-4-Opus (36.8%), and Tongyi DeepResearch (44.5%). On live-web benchmarks, the model achieves 26.3% on BrowseComp, 64.1% on GAIA, and 65.0% on xbench-DeepSearch, surpassing all open-source deep research agents (e.g., WebSailor-72B at 12.0%/55.4%/55.0%) while being competitive with frontier proprietary models despite no live-web training data. Ablation studies reveal: (RQ1) final-answer correctness is not a strong filtering signal — correct-only (54.81%), incorrect-only (55.06%), and all trajectories (54.46%) yield nearly identical downstream accuracy; (RQ2) one-time bootstrapping is essential — removing gold documents collapses BrowseComp-Plus accuracy from 54.81% to 6.35%; (RQ3) performance plateaus beyond ~100 turns; (RQ4) adding `open` produces the largest jump (43.86% → 56.39%), with `find` providing additional gains (→ 62.17%); (RQ5) P(correct | open-hit) = 86.72% vs. P(correct | search-hit) = 61.84%, showing that explicit document opening is critical but not sufficient. Failed trajectories average 71.7 tool calls vs. 38.4 for successes, driven primarily by excessive search reformulation rather than insufficient document inspection. Strengths: - The offline pipeline eliminates $5,760–$28,800 in API costs for 97K trajectories while enabling perfect reproducibility and controlled analysis not possible with live-web systems. - The three-primitive browser abstraction (`search`/`open`/`find`) is well-motivated and empirically validated — each level provides measurable gains, and the design closely mirrors human research behavior. - The finding that incorrect trajectories are as valuable for SFT as correct ones (RQ1) is a practically important and counterintuitive insight that challenges conventional rejection-sampling wisdom in this setting. - The model generalizes from an offline corpus to live-web benchmarks without any live-web training, demonstrating the quality and diversity of offline synthesis. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The one-time bootstrapping step still requires the Serper API, meaning full reproducibility requires at least one online phase; a truly zero-API pipeline is not achieved. - Evaluation relies on GPT-4.1 as an LLM judge rather than human annotation, and the BrowseComp-Plus benchmark (830 examples) is relatively narrow — the 54.8% result may partly reflect in-distribution overlap between the MiroVerse training questions and the BrowseComp-Plus corpus construction. - The paper uses SFT only; no reinforcement learning is applied, leaving open whether RL on the offline environment could further close the gap against proprietary deep research systems or resolve the hard bimodal tail of questions where Pass@16 remains near 0%.

#24 — Highly Innovative (76)

Intern-S1-Pro: Scientific Multimodal Foundation Model at Trillion Scale (87 ▲ )

Yicheng Zou, Dongsheng Zhu, Lin Zhu, Tong Zhu, Yunhua Zhou, Peiheng Zhou, Xinyu Zhou, Dongzhan Zhou, Zhiwang Zhou, Yuhao Zhou, Bowen Zhou, Zhanping Zhong, Zhijie Zhong, Haiteng Zhao, Penghao Zhao, Xiaomeng Zhao, Zhiyuan Zhao, Yechen Zhang, Jin Zhang, Wenwei Zhang, Hongjie Zhang, Zhuo Zhang, Wenlong Zhang, Bo Zhang, Chao Zhang, Chen Zhang, Yuhang Zang, Fei Yuan, Jiakang Yuan, Jiashuo Yu, Jinhui Yin, Haochen Ye, Qian Yao, Bowen Yang, Danni Yang, Kaichen Yang, Ziang Yan, Jun Xu, Yicheng Xu, Wanghan Xu, Xuenan Xu, Chao Xu, Ruiliang Xu, Shuhao Xing, Long Xing, Xinchen Xie, Ling-I Wu, Zijian Wu, Zhenyu Wu, Lijun Wu, Yue Wu, Jianyu Wu, Wen Wu, Fan Wu, Xilin Wei, Qi Wei, Bingli Wang, Rui Wang, Ziyi Wang, Zun Wang, Yi Wang, Haomin Wang, Yizhou Wang, Lintao Wang, Yiheng Wang, Longjiang Wang, Bin Wang, Jian Tong, Zhongbo Tian, Huanze Tang, Chen Tang, Shixiang Tang, Yu Sun, Qiushi Sun, Xuerui Su, Qisheng Su, Chenlin Su, Demin Song, Jin Shi, Fukai Shang, Yuchen Ren, Pengli Ren, Xiaoye Qu, Yuan Qu, Jiantao Qiu, Yu Qiao, Runyu Peng, Tianshuo Peng, Jiahui Peng, Qizhi Pei, Zhuoshi Pan, Linke Ouyang, Wenchang Ning, Yichuan Ma, Zerun Ma, Ningsheng Ma, Runyuan Ma, Chengqi Lyu, Haijun Lv, Han Lv, Lindong Lu, Kuikun Liu, Jiangning Liu, Yuhong Liu, Kai Liu, Hongwei Liu, Zhoumianze Liu, Mengjie Liu, Ziyu Liu, Wenran Liu, Yang Liu, Liwei Liu, Kaiwen Liu, Junyao Lin, Junming Lin, Tianyang Lin, Dahua Lin, Jianze Liang, Linyang Li, Peiji Li, Zonglin Li, Zehao Li, Pengze Li, Guoyan Li, Lingkai Kong, Linglin Jing, Zhenjiang Jin, Feifei Jiang, Qian Jiang, Junhao Huang, Zixian Huang, Haian Huang, Zhouqi Hua, Han Hu, Linfeng Hou, Yinan He, Conghui He, Tianyao He, Xu Guo, Qipeng Guo, Aijia Guo, Yuzhe Gu, Lixin Gu, Jingyang Gong, Qiming Ge, Jiaye Ge, Songyang Gao, Jianfei Gao, Xinyu Fang, Caihua fan, Yue Fan, Yanhui Duan, Zichen Ding, Shengyuan Ding, Xuanlang Dai, Erfei Cui, Ganqu Cui, Pei Chu, Tao Chu, Guangran Cheng, Yu Cheng, Kai Chen, Yongkang Chen, Chiyu Chen, Guanzhou Chen, Qiaosheng Chen, Sitao Chen, Xin Chen, Haojiong Chen, Yicheng Chen, Weihan Cao, Yuhang Cao, Qinglong Cao, Lei Bai · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Scores 76 for its principled solutions to MoE scaling challenges — grouped routing achieving absolute load balance and the STE gradient estimator for sparse routers are genuinely novel architectural contributions that address fundamental training pathologies at trillion-parameter scale. The empirical finding that a 'specializable generalist' outperforms dedicated specialists on controlled biology tasks challenges prevailing assumptions about task-specific models. However, comparisons against non-public models (GPT-5.2, Gemini-3-Pro) undermine verifiability, and the work is ultimately an engineering-heavy scaling paper whose individual components (FoPE, time-series encoder) lack isolation ablations.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Scientific AI requires models with sufficient capacity to master highly diverse, domain-specific tasks across chemistry, biology, materials science, earth sciences, and more — each with its own specialized notation, knowledge, and reasoning patterns. Prior work relied on smaller specialized models, which lack the general reasoning capacity to fully exploit domain data. Scaling to one trillion parameters has been technically infeasible due to MoE load imbalance, router gradient starvation, RL training instability, and throughput collapse when enlarging expert pools. This paper addresses all of these jointly to produce the first open-source 1T-parameter scientific multimodal foundation model. Methodology: Intern-S1-Pro scales up from Intern-S1 via expert expansion (upcycling), tripling the number of experts while preserving strong initialization by copying Top-1/Top-2 experts into each group. Four key technical contributions enable this scale: (1) Grouped Routing — partitions experts into G device-aligned groups and selects Top-(K/G) per group, achieving absolute load balance under EP8 and eliminating OOM risk; (2) Straight-Through Estimator (STE) for routers — decouples sparse forward routing from dense backward gradient propagation so all N−K non-selected experts still receive meaningful gradient signal, improving convergence and load balancing; (3) Fourier Position Encoding (FoPE) — replaces RoPE by modeling each embedding dimension as a multi-frequency Fourier series, capturing continuous wave-like spectral structure in physical signals while zeroing out undertrained frequency components during length extrapolation; (4) Stable Mixed-Precision RL — achieves FP8 RL at 1T scale via operator-level precision alignment between XTuner training and LMDeploy rollout engines, rollout router replay (expert routing decisions transmitted via Ray object references), selective FP8 quantization of expert MLPs only (non-expert layers stay BF16, LM head FP32), and dual importance sampling ratios to handle train/rollout and on/off-policy discrepancy. Pre-training uses 6T multimodal tokens with a specialized scientific caption pipeline: MinerU2.5 extracts sub-figures from PDFs, InternVL3.5-241B captions scientific images (~1000 words avg vs. <100 for natural captions), CapRL-32B handles general images, and a 0.5B quality discriminator filters low-density outputs — yielding ~270B tokens of dense scientific image-text data. A dedicated adaptive time-series encoder with dynamic patching handles inputs from 10^0 to 10^6 timesteps. Post-training uses RL across both general and scientific tasks (the SAGE framework: Foundation → Fusion → Evolution). Key Results: On scientific benchmarks, Intern-S1-Pro achieves 55.5 on SciReasoner vs. 14.7 (Gemini-3-Pro) and 13.6 (GPT-5.2); 74.8 on SmolInstruct vs. 58.3 (Gemini-3-Pro); 72.8 on MatBench vs. 64.9 (Gemini-3-Pro); 52.5 on Biology-Instruction vs. 12.0 (Gemini-3-Pro). On general benchmarks it achieves 93.1 on AIME-2025 (vs. 96.1 for Kimi-K2.5 and 100.0 for GPT-5.2), 86.6 MMLU-Pro, 91.9 RefCOCO-avg (best), 93.6 ScreenSpot V2 (best). On time-series (SciTS), it achieves F1=99.5 on EAU01 vs. 72.5 for Gemini2.5-Flash-VL and 67.6 for GPT-5-mini. The 'specializable generalist' case study is striking: using identical underlying biology data, Intern-S1-Pro scores 52.45 avg across 22 biological tasks vs. 39.24 for the dedicated Biology-Instruction specialist, with Protein-Fluorescence improving from 2.57 to 78.14 and Protein-FunctionEC from 19.79 to 72.70. Training efficiency overhead from 4× expert expansion is only ~20% throughput reduction. FP8 mixed-precision RL matches BF16 validation accuracy throughout training. Strengths: - The grouped routing mechanism is a clean, principled solution to MoE load imbalance that achieves absolute device balance rather than approximate balance via auxiliary losses, directly solving OOM risk. - The STE gradient estimator for sparse routing is well-motivated and addresses a fundamental training pathology (gradient starvation of non-selected experts) with minimal overhead. - The 'specializable generalist beats specialist' result with controlled data is a genuinely important empirical finding — it challenges the community assumption that task-specific models are always preferable for narrow domains. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Several benchmarks compared against (GPT-5.2, Gemini-3-Pro) are non-public at time of writing, making independent verification of the comparison impossible and raising concern about cherry-picking or version mismatch. - The biology case study uses a relatively weak specialist baseline (Biology-Instruction, a supervised fine-tuned model without RL), so the comparison does not fully isolate whether the gains come from model scale, RL post-training, or the joint training strategy specifically. - FoPE and the time-series encoder are described at a high level without ablations against their respective baselines (RoPE and image-serialization of time series), making it hard to quantify their individual contribution to the overall results.
