#25 — Highly Innovative (76)

The Pulse of Motion: Measuring Physical Frame Rate from Visual Dynamics (10 ▲ )

Xiangbo Gao, Mingyang Wu, Siyuan Yang, Jiongze Yu, Pardis Taghavi, Fangzhou Lin, Zhengzhong Tu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Scores 76 for identifying and cleanly formalizing chronometric hallucination — a genuinely overlooked failure mode in video generation that no prior benchmark measures. The PhyFPS construct is a novel conceptual contribution, the physics-grounded augmentation pipeline is principled, and the comprehensive evaluation across 17 generators establishes a new diagnostic dimension for the field. The user study with Bradley-Terry analysis provides credible evidence that the metric captures perceptually meaningful differences. Held back from higher scores because the contribution is diagnostic rather than generative — it measures the problem but does not solve it at the generation level.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern generative video models achieve high spatial realism but systematically fail to ground generated motion in a consistent real-world time scale. The root cause is that training datasets mix standard-rate footage, slow-motion, and time-lapses without time-scale labels — models learn plausible frame-to-frame transitions but have no internal sense of physical speed. The authors formalize this failure mode as chronometric hallucination: generated videos exhibit ambiguous, unstable, and uncontrollable physical motion speeds (e.g., a hummingbird rendered in extreme slow-motion, a person falling at sub-gravity velocity) even when no speed-manipulation keywords are prompted. No existing benchmark measures this deficiency; evaluation suites like VBench assess perceptual smoothness, not whether the depicted physics occur at a calibrated temporal rate. Methodology: The paper proposes Visual Chronometer (VC), a regression model that predicts Physical Frames Per Second (PhyFPS) — the true frame rate implied by the motion itself, as distinct from the nominal container metadata FPS — directly from raw video frames. The backbone is VideoVAE+, a pretrained video encoder, with an attention-based query-pooling head that aggregates temporal latents into a clip-level representation, followed by an MLP outputting log(PhyFPS). Predicting in log-space stabilizes optimization over the wide dynamic range of frame rates. Training data is curated exclusively from sources where meta FPS = PhyFPS (Adobe240, BVI-VFI, UVG, autonomous driving datasets), then synthetically expanded via controlled temporal resampling from a 240 FPS base: (1) sharp capture via uniform subsampling, (2) motion blur via temporal frame averaging with variable exposure windows, and (3) rolling shutter simulation via spatially progressive frame indexing. This yields 465,535 clips across 18 target PhyFPS values. Two model variants are trained: VC-Wide (2–240 FPS) and VC-Common (12–60 FPS). Two benchmarks are introduced: PhyFPS-Bench-Real (4,000 verified clips with ground-truth PhyFPS) and PhyFPS-Bench-Gen (100 prompts × 17 generators, measuring meta-vs-PhyFPS alignment, intra-video CV, and inter-video CV). Key Results: On PhyFPS-Bench-Real, VC-Common achieves MAE = 3.46 FPS and MAPE = 9% (mean ground-truth PhyFPS ≈ 38.8), while VC-Wide reaches MAE = 7.76 / MAPE = 21%. In stark contrast, all tested VLMs fail catastrophically: best video-based VLM (Seed-1.6-Flash) achieves MAE = 20.0 / MAPE = 40%, and many collapse to predicting a fixed value (e.g., Seed-1.6-Flash always predicts 30 FPS). On PhyFPS-Bench-Gen, all 17 evaluated generators exhibit substantial PhyFPS misalignment: open-source models show percentage errors of 31–126%, with InfinityStar reaching 126% error. Closed-source models (Sora-2, Kling-o3, Seedance) achieve percentage errors of 28–58%, lower but still large. Predicted PhyFPS is consistently higher than meta FPS across all models, confirming the known "slow but smooth" generation tendency. A user study with 1,490 pairwise comparisons shows that PhyFPS-guided global re-timing (Pred, 44.2% Bradley-Terry score) and local dynamic re-timing (Pred Dyn, 36.9%) both strongly outperform untouched originals (19.5%), with global correction preferred over dynamic due to smoother perceived playback. Ablations confirm that motion blur augmentation reduces MAE from 5.12 to 4.87, and adding rolling shutter further reduces it to 3.46; optimal inference patch size is T=32–64 frames. Strengths: - Identifies and cleanly formalizes a real, overlooked failure mode (chronometric hallucination) with clear operationalization via the PhyFPS construct, filling a genuine gap in video generation evaluation. - Physics-grounded augmentation strategy (fast shutter, motion blur, rolling shutter synthesis from 240 FPS source) is principled and shown via ablation to be critical, not cosmetic. - Comprehensive benchmark covering 17 generators (open and closed source) with three complementary axes (alignment, intra-video stability, inter-video stability) provides a thorough empirical picture. - User study with Bradley-Terry analysis provides statistically grounded evidence that mathematical PhyFPS accuracy translates to perceptual improvement. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The training data synthesis relies on RIFE frame interpolation to reach 240 FPS, introducing interpolation artifacts that may confound the model's notion of "true" physical dynamics, particularly for fast motion where interpolation quality degrades. - The user study is small (15+ participants, 1,490 comparisons) and uses a limited set of generated videos; the finding that global correction outperforms dynamic correction may not generalize — it could reflect the specific video content tested rather than a fundamental perceptual principle. - VC-Common's effective range (12–60 FPS) excludes extreme cases (slow-motion at 2–10 FPS, high-speed at 90–240 FPS) that are increasingly relevant as generative models attempt cinematic effects; the paper frames this as a design choice but it limits diagnostic coverage. - The approach provides post-hoc measurement and correction but does not directly fix chronometric hallucination at the generation level; the path from this diagnostic tool to actually training temporally-grounded generators remains largely aspirational and unvalidated.
Recommended 2603.14375 PDF
#26 — Highly Innovative (76)

Hyperagents (18 ▲ )

Jenny Zhang, Bingchen Zhao, Wannan Yang, Jakob Foerster, Jeff Clune, Minqi Jiang, Sam Devlin, Tatiana Shavrina · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Mar 2026
Why this rank: HyperAgents introduces a genuinely paradigm-shifting concept by making the meta-level improvement mechanism itself editable, directly resolving the alignment bottleneck that confined prior recursive self-improvement systems like DGM to coding-adjacent domains. The cross-domain transfer experiment cleanly demonstrates that the system learns transferable meta-strategies rather than domain-specific tricks, which is a surprising and important finding. The breadth of validation across four qualitatively different domains (coding, paper review, robotics, math grading) strengthens the generality claim. However, the outer loop remains handcrafted and experiment scale is modest, tempering the strength of the empirical evidence behind this ambitious vision.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing recursive self-improvement systems, including the Darwin Gödel Machine (DGM), rely on fixed, handcrafted meta-level mechanisms that fundamentally cap how fast a system can improve itself. The DGM's instruction-generation step is not modifiable, so compounding gains depend on alignment between the evaluation task and the skills needed for self-modification — an alignment that holds for coding but breaks down in other domains (paper review, robotics, mathematics). The paper asks: can a single framework support open-ended self-improvement on any computable task without domain-specific engineering at the meta level? Methodology: The paper introduces hyperagents — self-referential programs that unify a task agent (solves the target task) and a meta agent (modifies agents) into a single editable codebase. Because the meta agent is part of the same editable program, it can rewrite itself, enabling metacognitive self-modification: improving not only task behavior but also the procedure that generates future improvements. The authors instantiate this as DGM-Hyperagents (DGM-H) by extending DGM's open-ended evolutionary archive with a fully modifiable meta level. The system alternates between (1) metacognitive self-modification, where a parent hyperagent selected probabilistically by performance and novelty generates a modified child, and (2) empirical evaluation. The initial hyperagent is deliberately minimal — a single foundation model call for the task agent and a one-line instruction ("modify any part of the codebase") for the meta agent — maximizing the search space. Experiments span four domains: Polyglot coding (pass@1), paper accept/reject prediction, robotics reward design (Genesis quadruped RL), and Olympiad-level math grading (IMO-GradingBench). Ablations remove self-improving meta agents or open-ended exploration to isolate each component's contribution. Cross-domain transfer is quantified via a new improvement@k (imp@k) metric measuring how much a fixed meta agent can improve a task agent within k iterations. Key Results: On Polyglot coding, DGM-H improves from 0.084 to 0.267 on the full benchmark (165 tasks), matching the original DGM (0.142→0.307) despite not being designed specifically for coding. On paper review, DGM-H improves test accuracy from 0.0 to 0.710, exceeding the AI-Scientist-v2 static baseline (0.630). On robotics reward design, test performance rises from 0.060 to 0.372, surpassing the default reward (0.348) and qualitatively inducing jumping rather than standing-tall local optima. Both ablations (no self-improvement, no open-ended exploration) fail to improve significantly (p<0.05 vs DGM-H). For cross-domain transfer to Olympiad math grading: hyperagents trained on paper review + robotics achieve imp@50=0.630, while DGM transfer agents achieve imp@50≈0.0 — demonstrating that DGM-H learns transferable meta-level strategies (persistent memory, performance tracking, bias detection) rather than domain-specific tricks. In compounding experiments (Section 5.3), DGM-H initialized from transfer hyperagents + ProofAutoGrader achieves test accuracy of 0.700 on the held-out IMO subset vs ProofAutoGrader's 0.670, and on the full IMO-GradingBench accuracy improves from 0.561 to 0.601 with reduced mean absolute error. Strengths: - The key conceptual contribution — making the meta-level mechanism itself editable — is clean and well-motivated, directly resolving the alignment bottleneck that limits DGM to coding domains. - Strong empirical evidence across four qualitatively different domains, including a hard cross-domain transfer experiment that cleanly separates transferable meta-improvement from domain-specific adaptation. - Rich qualitative analysis of what DGM-H autonomously discovers (performance trackers, persistent memory, bias detectors, structured evaluation pipelines, compute-aware planning) adds genuine scientific insight and supports the core claims. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The outer-loop components (parent selection, evaluation protocol) remain fixed and handcrafted; full self-referentiality is not achieved, and preliminary results (Appendix E.5) show the system does not yet match the handcrafted parent-selection heuristic. - Experiment scale is modest (5 runs, 80–200 iterations, small task subsets for staged evaluation) due to high compute cost (~88.6M tokens per 100-iteration run), making statistical power limited and confidence intervals wide; many key comparisons (e.g., DGM-H vs DGM-custom) are not statistically significant. - The task distribution is fixed throughout training; the system cannot co-evolve curricula, which the authors acknowledge as a ceiling on unbounded progress but do not empirically address.
Recommended 2603.19461 PDF
#27 — Highly Innovative (76)

Astrolabe: Steering Forward-Process Reinforcement Learning for Distilled Autoregressive Video Models (84 ▲ )

Songchun Zhang, Zeyue Xue, Siming Fu, Jie Huang, Xianghao Kong, Y Ma, Haoyang Huang, Nan Duan, Anyi Rao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Astrolabe solves a well-defined but technically challenging problem — online RL alignment for highly distilled, few-step autoregressive video models — with a principled forward-process formulation that is provably solver-agnostic and avoids expensive trajectory storage. The streaming training scheme with detached historical context elegantly achieves constant memory regardless of video length. The uncertainty-aware selective KL penalty is a concrete mechanism for distinguishing reward hacking from genuine improvement, addressing a key failure mode in RL-based generative model alignment. The multi-component design is well-ablated and each piece is individually justified.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Distilled autoregressive (AR) video models—trained via distribution matching distillation (DMD) from bidirectional diffusion teachers—enable real-time streaming video generation through KV-caching, but inherit a critical deficiency: distillation aligns the student with teacher distribution statistics, not human visual preferences. As a result, outputs exhibit artifacts, unnatural motion, and temporal inconsistencies. Existing RL alignment methods are ill-suited: reward-weighted distillation lacks exploration and cannot penalize bad samples, while reverse-process RL methods (DanceGRPO, Flow-GRPO) require log-probability estimation along denoising trajectories, coupling them to specific ODE solvers and demanding expensive intermediate state storage that negates the efficiency advantages of streaming architectures. No prior work had solved online RL alignment for highly distilled, few-step AR video models without re-distillation. Methodology: Astrolabe is an online RL post-training framework built around three interlocking components. First, it adopts a forward-process RL formulation adapted from DiffusionNFT: given a clean generated sample with normalized reward, the method defines implicit positive and negative velocity policies via linear interpolation between the current and old velocity predictors (v⁺ = (1−β)v_old + βv_θ, v⁻ = (1+β)v_old − βv_θ). The policy loss contrasts these against the forward-process target velocity, requiring only clean inference endpoints—no trajectory unrolling or solver coupling. The adaptive loss weighting from DiffusionNFT is discarded because large discretization gaps in 4-step distilled models cause gradient explosion. Second, a streaming training scheme handles long videos: a rolling KV cache with frame sinks (S permanently retained frames) plus a rolling window of L recent frames bounds memory to a constant regardless of video length. During training, RL updates apply only to a local clip window while all preceding KV states are detached from the computation graph, bounding backpropagation cost. G=24 candidate clips share a frozen context prefix, amortizing rollout cost across the group. Clip-level group-wise advantages are computed via mean-centering. Third, multi-reward optimization with uncertainty-aware regularization covers Visual Quality (HPSv3 on top-30% frames), Motion Quality (VideoAlign on grayscale inputs to isolate dynamics from texture), and Text Alignment (VideoAlign on RGB). To prevent reward hacking, rank disagreement across M reward models quantifies per-sample uncertainty: samples where the primary reward rank significantly exceeds auxiliary consensus are flagged as risky, and KL penalties apply only to these flagged samples. An EMA update maintains the behavior policy and the KL reference policy resets when drift exceeds a threshold. Training uses LoRA (rank 256) on 48 H200 GPUs with the Causal Wan 2.1 backbone. Key Results: On short-video VBench (946 prompts), Astrolabe consistently improves all three base models without changing inference speed: Self-Forcing improves from 83.74→83.79 total, HPSv3 9.36→10.72 (+1.36), MQ 1.65→1.71; LongLive from 83.22→84.93 (+1.71 total), HPSv3 9.38→11.03 (+1.65); Causal Forcing from 84.04→84.46 (+0.42), HPSv3 9.48→10.84 (+1.36). HPSv3 gains (~+1.4) are the most consistent signal of preference alignment. On VBench-Long (30s videos), Causal Forcing improves from 82.87→84.24 total and LongLive from 83.52→84.07, with HPSv3 gains of ~+1.2–1.5 preserved even in extended generation. In multi-prompt 60-second generation, CLIP scores improve across all 10-second intervals. Ablations confirm the necessity of all components: sequence-level rollout with full backpropagation causes OOM for 30s videos; clip+detach cuts memory ~2× vs. clip+full-BP while improving metrics; VQ-only training collapses into static frames (HPSv3 improves but MQ drops to 1.58); selective+EMA KL outperforms both no-KL (instability) and uniform KL (over-constraint); removing DiffusionNFT's adaptive weighting prevents gradient explosion and enables steady reward improvement. Strengths: - The forward-process RL formulation is genuinely solver-agnostic and avoids full trajectory storage, making it well-matched to the efficiency requirements of distilled streaming models—this is a principled, not ad hoc, design choice backed by theoretical proofs (Theorems 1 and 2). - The streaming training scheme with detached historical context elegantly decouples long-video inference simulation from gradient computation, achieving constant peak memory regardless of video length while still conditioning on extended temporal context. - The uncertainty-aware selective KL penalty is a concrete and well-motivated mechanism for distinguishing reward hacking from genuine quality improvements, avoiding the exploration-suppression failure mode of uniform KL regularization. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method is fundamentally bounded by the quality of available reward models: VideoAlign and HPSv3 assess short-term aesthetics and text-video correspondence but cannot reliably detect long-horizon causal consistency, complex physics, or multi-entity interactions—meaning temporal hallucinations that defeat human perception may pass unpenalized. - As a post-training RL framework, Astrolabe cannot inject capabilities absent from the distilled base model; extreme distillation may create a hard ceiling on spatial complexity or domain-specific rendering that RL cannot overcome regardless of reward signal.
Recommended 2603.17051 PDF
#28 — Highly Innovative (76)

Stepwise Contrastive Reasoning for Retrieval-Augmented Generation over Knowledge Graphs

ojs.aaai.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: SCR introduces a genuinely novel stepwise contrastive reasoning framework that elegantly addresses the 'lost in the middle' problem by iteratively narrowing KG traversal with dynamic question context updates, rather than dumping all candidate paths into the LLM. The joint GNN+Transformer training via InfoNCE contrastive loss is a clean architectural contribution, and the efficiency gains are striking — 56x faster retrieval than GNN-RAG with far fewer LLM tokens. The demonstration that even a 0.6B parameter model with SCR matches frontier standalone LLMs is a compelling result that validates the modularity claim. Limited to Freebase-based benchmarks, but the core idea of progressive graph filtering with contrastive alignment is broadly applicable.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) requires accurately retrieving multi-hop reasoning paths from large KGs to ground LLM responses in structured facts. Existing LLM-based methods represent KG facts as flat text (e.g., triples), losing graph structure and failing at multi-hop reasoning. GNN-based methods capture structure but lack semantic alignment between question text and entity descriptions. Joint GNN+LLM methods (e.g., GNN-RAG) improve recall but flood the LLM with many candidate paths, exacerbating the "lost in the middle" problem. Fine-tuning LLMs on KG-specific data is expensive and brittle to KG updates. The gap is a lightweight, no-fine-tuning framework that jointly leverages graph structure and textual semantics for interpretable, efficient KG retrieval. Methodology: SCR (Stepwise Contrastive Reasoning) trains a relational GAT-based GNN and a BERT Transformer encoder jointly via InfoNCE contrastive loss, totaling ~112M trainable parameters. A frozen pretrained BERT encodes all entity and relation texts into embeddings, which the GNN propagates via relation-aware attention message passing (incorporating both entity and relation text embeddings) to produce structurally-enriched entity representations. Reasoning is iterative: starting from a topic entity e0, a trainable BERT encodes the current question context qi; cosine similarity selects the most aligned neighbor entity as the next step; the question is updated by appending the selected entity's description and the traversed relation. This repeats until the topic entity stabilizes (convergence = answer found). The process is extended to beam search (top-K paths in parallel), producing K hypothesis answer-path pairs that are formatted into a prompt for any downstream LLM. Training supervision uses shortest paths between topic and answer entities as positive examples; the InfoNCE loss pushes the question embedding closer to the correct next-hop entity versus all candidate neighbors. Key Results: On WebQSP, SCR (ChatGPT) achieves 90.9 Hit / 73.4 F1 and SCR (GPT-4o-mini) achieves 90.8 Hit / 74.8 F1, competitive with or exceeding all baselines except SubgraphRAG (GPT-4o-mini) at 90.1 / 77.5 F1. On CWQ, SCR (ChatGPT) achieves 70.4 Hit / 60.2 F1, the best F1 across all methods. Crucially, SCR with tiny LLMs (Qwen3-0.6B: 86.5 / 51.0 on WebQSP; Qwen3-8B: 89.1 / 68.6) matches much larger standalone LLMs. For multi-hop breakdown on CWQ ≥3 hops, SCR's F1 of 64.2 far exceeds GNN-RAG (51.8) and RoG (40.0). On efficiency, SCR achieves 1.2s retrieval time on WebQSP vs. GNN-RAG's 68s and RoG's 948s, with only 240 LLM input tokens vs. 414–1229 for competitors. Ablation shows removing stepwise reasoning drops Hit/F1 by 20.7/29.4 on WebQSP; removing beam search drops 16.1/25.7; replacing the custom GNN with R-GCN drops 6.9/8.0. Strengths: - Strong efficiency-accuracy tradeoff: fastest retrieval (1.2s vs. next-best 2.5s for GCR, which performs poorly) with fewest LLM tokens, while matching or exceeding state-of-the-art performance on two standard benchmarks. - Generalizes across LLM backends without any LLM fine-tuning — even a 0.6B model with SCR matches frontier LLMs used standalone, demonstrating genuine modularity. - Stepwise reasoning with dynamic question context update elegantly addresses the "lost in the middle" problem by progressively filtering the KG rather than dumping all candidates at once. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluated only on Freebase-based benchmarks (WebQSP and CWQ); generalization to other KG schemas or open-domain KGs with heterogeneous entity descriptions is not demonstrated. - The termination criterion (topic entity stops changing) can cause premature stopping if the correct answer is not a neighbor of any visited entity, or if the graph has misleading high-similarity neighbors; the authors acknowledge filtering erroneous paths as future work. - F1 on WebQSP (74.8) is still 2.7 points below SubgraphRAG (GPT-4o-mini, 77.5), which uses more tokens via in-context learning — suggesting the retrieval precision of SCR is not uniformly dominant, especially for simpler questions where richer LLM context helps.
Citations
#29 — Innovative (74)

LoopRPT: Reinforcement Pre-Training for Looped Language Models

Guo Tang, Shixin Jiang, Heng Chang, Nuo Chen, Yuhan Li, Huiming Fan, Jia Li, Ming Liu, Bing Qin · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: LoopRPT identifies a genuinely novel problem—the structural mismatch between sparse-reward RL and latent-recurrent architectures—and provides a principled, multi-component solution with step-wise dense rewards, entropy-based token selection, and noisy latent rollouts. The difficulty-aware time penalty design is technically elegant, and Pareto dominance across difficulty levels is convincing. However, evaluation on a single architecture (Ouro) with training exclusively on competition math limits evidence of generality, and downstream gains on non-math benchmarks are marginal.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard RL alignment methods (RLVR) for LLMs operate on output tokens, creating a fundamental mismatch with Looped Language Models (LoopLMs) whose reasoning unfolds implicitly across iterative latent steps rather than explicit token sequences. LoopLMs like Ouro use parameter-shared recurrent backbones with adaptive early-exit gates, enabling multi-step latent computation without emitting intermediate tokens—a computationally efficient alternative to chain-of-thought. However, sparse reward signals tied to final output tokens fail to provide useful credit assignment signals for the intermediate latent iterations, and the dynamic early-exit mechanism introduces additional training instability under standard RLVR. The paper addresses how to apply RL meaningfully within the pre-training phase for this architecture class. Methodology: LoopRPT adapts the Reinforcement Pre-Training (RPT) paradigm—which reframes next-token prediction as a reasoning task with verifiable self-supervised rewards—specifically for LoopLMs. The framework has three interlocking components: (1) Entropy-based hard-token selection: only the top-ρ% of tokens (by teacher uncertainty) receive RL updates, concentrating learning signal on positions where latent computation is most valuable; (2) Step-wise rewards via EMA teacher: an exponential moving average teacher model provides stable baselines; the reward at each latent step k combines an accuracy gain (student log-probability minus teacher reference log-probability at the teacher's exit step tref) minus a difficulty-aware time penalty that penalizes computation beyond tref, scaled inversely with token difficulty so harder tokens incur smaller exit penalties; (3) Noisy latent rollouts for joint optimization: Gaussian noise is injected into recurrent hidden states to generate on-policy trajectory diversity (since latent reasoning has no discrete action space), enabling GRPO-style policy gradient updates over the exit distribution. Simultaneously, a step-weighted next-token objective strengthens backbone representations at early latent steps. The total loss combines a policy-gradient term (LPG), step-weighted representation loss (Lrep), exit entropy regularization (Lent), and a K3-style KL surrogate anchoring the student to the EMA teacher (LKL). The framework is instantiated on the Ouro architecture at 1.4B and 2.6B parameter scales, trained on OMNI-MATH competition mathematics problems for 3 epochs on 8×A100 GPUs. Key Results: On next-token reasoning tasks stratified by difficulty, LoopRPT-2.6B improves Hard-split Peak accuracy by +3.58 points and reduces average inference steps from 3.51 to 2.28 under adaptive exit (a 35% reduction in compute), achieving Pareto dominance in accuracy-vs-computation tradeoff curves across all difficulty levels. Explicit CoT prompting on comparable Qwen3-1.7B degrades performance severely (Hard accuracy drops from 19.19 to 7.44), while LoopRPT improves it. On downstream benchmarks, LoopRPT-2.6B achieves GSM8K 85.36 vs. 81.76 baseline (+3.60), HumanEval+ 71.95 vs. 70.13 (+1.82), and MBPP+ +2.91 at 1.4B scale—competitive with models 2-3× larger (Qwen2.5-4B, Qwen3-4B) on several tasks. Per-step accuracy analysis confirms the gains are due to improved early-step representation quality rather than merely encouraging premature exits: LoopRPT substantially improves step-1 and step-2 accuracy especially on hard tokens. Ablations confirm all components are necessary; removing Lrep+Lent causes the largest joint degradation in both accuracy and efficiency. Strengths: - Cleanly identifies and addresses a genuine architectural mismatch: standard sparse-reward RL is structurally ill-suited for latent-recurrent models, and the proposed step-wise dense reward formulation is principled and theoretically grounded (with proofs connecting entropy selection to gradient energy, EMA stability to slow reference drift, and noisy rollouts to a smoothed on-policy objective). - The difficulty-aware time penalty design is technically elegant—harder tokens receive smaller exit penalties, preventing premature forced exits on tokens that genuinely require deep computation, which is a non-obvious design choice supported by ablation. - Pareto dominance across multiple difficulty levels and model scales (1.4B and 2.6B) strengthens the claim of generality, and the downstream transfer to math and code benchmarks demonstrates practical utility beyond the next-token reasoning proxy task. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is confined to two model scales (1.4B and 2.6B) on a single looped architecture (Ouro with K=4 loops), making it unclear whether findings generalize to deeper loop counts, other LoopLM architectures (e.g., Universal Transformers), or significantly larger scales. - Training exclusively on competition mathematics (OMNI-MATH) limits the evidence for generality; while downstream improvements appear on coding benchmarks, the mechanism by which math-focused RPT training transfers to code is not explained, and gains on general language benchmarks (MMLU, BBH) are marginal (≤0.77 points), raising questions about whether the approach improves general reasoning or primarily exploits domain-specific patterns in the training data.
Recommended 2603.19714 PDF
#30 — Innovative (74)

DCTR: Dual-Constraint Subgraph Optimization for Knowledge Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: DCTR introduces a theoretically grounded dual-constraint framework combining maximum-flow subgraph retrieval with differentiable ranking supervision, addressing the underexplored problem of jointly optimizing subgraph completeness and LLM-friendly ordering. The Lipschitz error-propagation bound for MaxMin-InfoPath and the MAP Bayesian justification for MaxDensity-InfoPath elevate this beyond ad hoc engineering. The smooth surrogate for Kendall's τ is an elegant solution to a real optimization bottleneck. However, evaluation is limited to two Freebase benchmarks and cross-dataset degradation suggests some overfitting to graph structure, tempering the broader impact claim.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: KG-based RAG systems must retrieve a relevant subgraph from a knowledge graph to ground LLM reasoning in structured relational knowledge. Existing approaches fall into two flawed camps: (1) multi-hop expansion methods (e.g., BFS traversal) retrieve semantically redundant subgraphs with high overlap noise, and (2) supervised retrieval methods trained on shortest-path pseudo-labels produce structurally compact but semantically incomplete subgraphs. Neither addresses the positional bias of LLMs—the tendency to underweight facts appearing in the middle of long contexts—which degrades faithfulness of generated answers. The gap is that no prior method jointly optimizes subgraph completeness, semantic richness, and content ordering for LLM consumption. Methodology: DCTR introduces a two-component pipeline. The first component, Dual-Constrained Flux Graph Retrieval (DCFGR), generates high-quality pseudo-labels via a two-stage graph algorithm. In stage one, subgraph retrieval is formulated as a maximum flow problem on a semantically-weighted KG, where edge capacities equal LLM-scored triple relevance weights; solving this yields a globally saturated subgraph Hm capturing all viable reasoning paths. In stage two, a dual-flux path extractor selects two complementary paths from Hm: a lower-bound MaxMin-InfoPath (lexicographically maximizes the bottleneck flux, i.e., the weakest-link edge weight, then total information—justified by a Lipschitz error-propagation bound showing that maximizing the minimum hop quality suppresses compounding semantic drift) and an upper-bound MaxDensity-InfoPath (maximizes average edge weight density, then total information—justified via MAP Bayesian inference showing density maximization is the principled surrogate for posterior path probability). The union of these two paths forms the pseudo-label. The second component, Differentiable Subgraph Ranking Supervision (DSRS), trains a lightweight retriever to output triple importance scores whose ordinal structure matches the pseudo-label weights. Since Kendall's τ rank correlation is non-differentiable, DSRS replaces the sign function with a scaled tanh smooth surrogate, yielding a pairwise sigmoid ranking loss that directly optimizes ranking agreement. At inference, the retriever scores and reorders subgraph triples, mitigating LLM positional bias without any LLM fine-tuning. Key Results: On WebQSP and CWQ (Freebase-based multi-hop KGQA benchmarks, using cleaned "sub" variants), DCTR outperforms all baselines across retrieval and generation metrics. For retrieval, DCTR achieves GPT-4o triple recall of 0.866/0.839 on WebQSP/CWQ vs. SubgraphRAG's 0.815/0.807—relative gains of 5.1% and 3.9%. On multi-hop queries (≥3 hops), gains exceed 8% in GPT-4o triple recall. For end-to-end generation with Llama3.1-70B, DCTR achieves 77.29 Macro-F1 / 89.69 Hit on WebQSP-sub and 63.27 Macro-F1 / 71.30 Hit on CWQ-sub, outperforming SubgraphRAG-70B (75.37/87.22 and 60.39/68.13) and GNN-RAG (71.3/85.7 and 59.4/66.8). At 8B scale, DCTR surpasses SubgraphRAG-8B by ~2.5% in Macro-F1. DCTR is fine-tuning-free at inference, unlike GNN-RAG which requires costly LLM fine-tuning. Ablation confirms that removing either dual path (Plower or Pupper) degrades performance, and removing DSRS causes the largest drop (6.4 pp Macro-F1 on WebQSP generation), confirming the ranking supervision is the most critical component. Strengths: - The maximum-flow formulation for initial subgraph retrieval is theoretically principled and provides a global view of all reasoning paths, avoiding the narrow local greedy bias of BFS/shortest-path heuristics. - The theoretical justifications for both path types are non-trivial: error-propagation via Lipschitz bounds for MaxMin-InfoPath and MAP Bayesian inference for MaxDensity-InfoPath give the dual-path design a rigorous foundation rather than an ad hoc combination. - DSRS is an elegant and practical solution: replacing non-differentiable Kendall's τ with a sigmoid-based surrogate allows direct gradient optimization of ranking structure, directly targeting LLM positional bias without any downstream model modification. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The LLM-based triple weighting function (FLLM) used to construct the semantic graph is itself a costly step; the paper uses a greedy approximation for large KGs but does not rigorously characterize the quality loss or runtime scaling of this approximation. - Evaluation is confined to two Freebase-derived KGQA benchmarks (WebQSP, CWQ); generalization to other KG types (e.g., domain-specific KGs, heterogeneous graphs) or text-rich KGs is not explored, limiting claims about broad applicability. - The cross-dataset generalization experiments show significant degradation (e.g., DCTR+8B(↔) drops from 72.13 to 67.52 Macro-F1 on WebQSP, and more severely from 50.12 to 38.92 on CWQ), suggesting the pseudo-label generation process may overfit to dataset-specific graph structure and that out-of-distribution robustness remains a concern.
Search Results
#31 — Innovative (74)

4DGS360: 360° Gaussian Reconstruction of Dynamic Objects from a Single Video (9 ▲ )

Jae Won Jang, Yeonjin Chang, Wonsik Shin, Juhwan Cho, Nojun Kwak · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Mar 2026
Why this rank: 4DGS360 addresses a genuine blind spot in the field: existing benchmarks and methods cannot handle truly extreme viewpoint changes for dynamic reconstruction. The AnchorTAP3D tracker is a clean, training-free solution that elegantly bridges 2D tracking reliability with 3D geometric reasoning via confidence-gated anchors. The iPhone360 benchmark fills an important evaluation gap. However, the core reconstruction pipeline (HiMoR-based deformable Gaussians) is inherited rather than novel, and the ablation study lacks quantitative rigor.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Reconstructing a dynamic 3D object from a casual monocular video at truly arbitrary viewpoints—including those more than 90° away from any training camera—remains unsolved. Existing 4D Gaussian splatting methods (MoSca, HiMoR, Shape of Motion) initialize Gaussian point clouds by lifting 2D tracked points into 3D via depth maps. Because depth maps only provide valid depth for currently visible surfaces, occluded regions receive no reliable 3D initialization; the resulting geometry overfits to the visible surface at each timestep and cannot recover when the camera swings to an extreme novel view. A compounding issue is that existing monocular benchmarks (HyperNeRF, Nerfies, iPhone) place test cameras at most ~45° from training cameras, making 360° evaluation impossible. Methodology: 4DGS360 addresses the initialization gap with AnchorTAP3D, a training-free hybrid 3D tracker. The key idea is to use high-confidence 2D tracks as spatial anchors that constrain a transformer-based 3D tracker (TAPIP3D) within a sliding temporal window of length L=16. Concretely, a 2D tracker (BootsTAP) predicts per-point confidences; points above threshold τ are unprojected into reliable 3D anchor positions. These anchors are injected as geometry-consistent conditioning signals into the 3D tracking transformer during each inference window (8-frame overlap), suppressing the error drift that plagues unconstrained 3D trackers under occlusion and large rotation. The resulting trajectories cover occluded regions that pure 2D lifting cannot reach. These complete trajectories initialize a hierarchical deformable Gaussian representation (following HiMoR): canonical-space Gaussians are deformed by a tree of motion nodes whose transformations are factored into shared parent-level motion bases. With a complete initialization, the ARAP rigidity regularization—which previously degraded on corrupted occluded geometry—can now function correctly. The total loss combines RGB (D-SSIM + LPIPS), mask, depth consistency, 2D tracking, and ARAP terms. Alongside the method, the authors release iPhone360, a 6-scene real-world benchmark captured with a single handheld iPhone; synchronized test cameras are placed 70°–135° from training views, providing the first dataset designed for true 360° evaluation of monocular dynamic reconstruction. Key Results: On iPhone360 (the paper's own benchmark), 4DGS360 consistently outperforms MoSca and HiMoR across all 6 scenes on CLIP-I, CLIP-T, and LPIPS. Representative gains over the strongest prior (HiMoR): Block2 CLIP-I 0.902 vs. 0.842, LPIPS 0.257 vs. 0.312; Jacket CLIP-I 0.915 vs. 0.704, LPIPS 0.275 vs. 0.294; Jelly CLIP-I 0.836 vs. 0.684, LPIPS 0.354 vs. 0.354 (tie). On the standard iPhone dataset (5 scenes, moderate view disparity), 4DGS360 achieves a mean CLIP-I of 0.902 vs. HiMoR's 0.891 and LPIPS of 0.388 vs. 0.464—best overall mean. On DAVIS (in-the-wild, no GT depth or cameras), qualitative results show more coherent geometry and better-recovered occlusions than Shape of Motion and HiMoR. Ablations confirm both components matter: removing 3D initialization (reverting to 2D unprojection) or removing anchors (naive TAPIP3D) each substantially degrades extreme-viewpoint geometry, with naive 3D tracking failing completely on longer sequences like "jelly." Strengths: - AnchorTAP3D is a clean, training-free solution that bridges 2D reliability and 3D geometric understanding; the confidence-gated anchor mechanism is well-motivated and validated by ablation. - iPhone360 fills a genuine gap—existing benchmarks could not evaluate 360° reconstruction, and the new dataset provides ground-truth depth from LiDAR and test cameras placed up to 135° away. - The method is diffusion-free yet outperforms diffusion-augmented baselines (DIFIX3D+) at extreme viewpoints, suggesting the geometry improvement is fundamental rather than cosmetic. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The ablation study is qualitative only; no quantitative breakdown of how much CLIP-I or LPIPS improves from each component (anchor vs. 3D init vs. ARAP) is reported, making it hard to attribute credit rigorously. - The model assumes fixed per-Gaussian color, which prevents handling real-world illumination changes and limits applicability to scenes with varying lighting conditions. - Performance still depends heavily on the quality of pretrained 2D trackers and monocular depth estimators; errors in these upstream components propagate into the initialization and are not recovered during optimization.
Recommended 2603.21618 PDF
#32 — Innovative (74)

Manifold-Aware Exploration for Reinforcement Learning in Video Generation (30 ▲ )

Mingzhe Zheng, Weijie Kong, Yue Wu, Dengyang Jiang, Yue Ma, Xuanhua He, Bin Lin, Kaixiong Gong, Zhao Zhong, Liefeng Bo, Qifeng Chen, Harry Yang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Mar 2026
Why this rank: SAGE-GRPO delivers a mathematically rigorous fix to a well-identified failure mode in video generation RL: the analytically integrated SDE replaces crude first-order approximations with a principled geometric correction, and the position-velocity trust region decomposition cleanly separates two distinct instability mechanisms. The unified re-implementation of all baselines on the same infrastructure strengthens the empirical claims. However, validation on a single base model and reward function, plus dependence on the dual KL mechanism for gains under certain reward weightings, limits the demonstrated generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Applying Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to video generation models remains substantially less reliable than for language models or image generation. The core failure mode is the ODE-to-SDE conversion required for stochastic exploration: existing methods (DanceGRPO, FlowGRPO) use Euler-style, first-order approximations of the noise standard deviation, which overestimate variance in high-noise timesteps. This excess injected noise drives rollout trajectories off the underlying video data manifold, degrading rollout quality and making reward estimates unreliable. The result is training instability and the well-known stability-plasticity dilemma: tight KL constraints limit policy improvement (underfitting), while loose constraints lead to reward hacking and temporal artifacts. Methodology: SAGE-GRPO (Stable Alignment via Exploration) frames the problem geometrically: the pretrained video model defines a valid data manifold M ⊂ ℝ^D, and the goal is to constrain GRPO exploration to the vicinity of this manifold at both micro and macro levels. At the micro level, the key contribution is a Precise Manifold-Aware SDE. Instead of the first-order Euler approximation used by DanceGRPO (η√(σ_t − σ_{t+1})) or FlowGRPO (η√(σ_t/(1−σ_t)·(σ_t − σ_{t+1}))), SAGE-GRPO derives the exact integrated variance by choosing diffusion coefficient ε_t = η√(σ_t/(1−σ_t)) and integrating analytically: Σ_t = η²[−(σ_t − σ_{t+1}) + log((1−σ_{t+1})/(1−σ_t))]. The logarithmic correction term captures the geometric contraction of the signal coefficient (1−σ_t) that linear approximations miss, yielding smaller and more manifold-aligned noise. Separately, a Gradient Norm Equalizer addresses inherent signal-to-noise imbalance across timesteps: gradient norms scale as 1/√Σ_t, vanishing at high noise and exploding at low noise. A per-timestep scale proxy N_t is estimated and median-normalized to equalize optimization pressure. At the macro level, a Dual Trust Region combines two KL mechanisms: (1) a Periodical Moving Anchor that updates the reference policy π_ref every N steps, providing position control that prevents long-horizon drift while preserving plasticity, and (2) a step-wise KL constraint DKL(π_θ||π_{k−1}) that damps instantaneous update magnitudes. Together these form a position-velocity controller: L_KL = β_pos·DKL(π_θ||π_ref_N) + β_vel·DKL(π_θ||π_{k−1}). The KL coefficient λ_KL is adapted online via a warm-up schedule (10^{−7} → 10^{−5}) followed by proportional feedback control. The full loss integrates the time-equalized GRPO objective with this dual regularizer. Key Results: Experiments are conducted on HunyuanVideo 1.5 with the VideoAlign reward model (evaluating Visual Quality VQ, Motion Quality MQ, and Text Alignment TA), with all methods reimplemented in a unified framework to isolate algorithmic differences. Under the alignment-focused reward setting (Setting B, wta=1.0, wvq=wmq=0.5), SAGE-GRPO with Dual Moving KL achieves an overall VideoAlign reward of 0.8066, versus 0.3705 for CPS (best baseline), 0.2103 for FlowGRPO, and 0.1290 for DanceGRPO. SAGE-GRPO also achieves the best VQ (−0.4765), best MQ (−0.2384), and best CLIPScore (0.5484). Under the averaged reward setting, results are more mixed, with CPS achieving higher unconstrained reward, but SAGE-GRPO maintaining the best PickScore (0.7452). A user preference study with 29 evaluators on 32 prompts confirms the trends: SAGE-GRPO wins against DanceGRPO in visual quality (85.9%), motion quality (75.8%), and semantic alignment (79.2%), with similar margins against FlowGRPO and CPS. Ablations confirm that both the precise SDE and gradient equalizer are necessary — without the equalizer, reward curves are unstable or plateau; without the Dual Moving KL, the policy either drifts or underfits. Strengths: - The manifold-aware SDE derivation is mathematically rigorous — analytically integrating the diffusion coefficient rather than using first-order approximation is a principled fix with clear geometric motivation and empirical validation across three parameterization regimes. - The position-velocity decomposition of the trust region (moving anchor + step-wise KL) cleanly separates two failure modes (long-horizon drift vs. abrupt updates) and is more principled than heuristic KL schedules used in prior work. - The unified re-implementation of all baselines on HunyuanVideo 1.5 with shared infrastructure is methodologically sound and avoids confounding algorithmic improvements with engineering differences. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All experiments are conducted on a single base model (HunyuanVideo 1.5) with a single reward model (VideoAlign); it is unclear whether the benefits of the precise SDE generalize to other flow-matching architectures, DDPM-style models, or different reward functions. - The user study is small (29 evaluators, 32 prompts) and evaluates only at one training checkpoint (iter 100); robustness of human preferences across training duration is not assessed. - The method introduces several additional hyperparameters (N for anchor refresh, β_pos, β_vel, λ_KL schedule, target KL) with non-obvious interactions; the sensitivity analysis covers KL weight schedules but not the anchor refresh interval or the β ratio. - Under the averaged reward setting (Setting B), SAGE-GRPO without KL actually performs worse than baselines (overall −0.1222), suggesting the gains are dependent on the specific reward weighting and the Dual Moving KL mechanism.
Recommended 2603.21872 PDF
#33 — Innovative (74)

T-MAP: Red-Teaming LLM Agents with Trajectory-aware Evolutionary Search (31 ▲ )

Hyomin Lee, Sangwoo Park, Yumin Choi, Sohyun An, Seanie Lee, Sung Ju Hwang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Mar 2026
Why this rank: T-MAP makes a meaningful contribution by formalizing agent red-teaming at the trajectory level rather than text-output level, which is a timely and well-motivated shift given MCP-style agent deployments. The Tool Call Graph is a clever lightweight mechanism that accumulates empirical transition statistics online to guide mutation toward viable attack sequences. However, the core algorithmic framework (MAP-Elites with LLM-based mutation) is a natural extension of Rainbow Teaming rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, and reliance on a weakly-aligned attacker model (DeepSeek-V3.2) introduces confounds that are not disentangled.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing red-teaming methods for LLMs focus on eliciting harmful text outputs, but fail to capture agent-specific vulnerabilities that emerge through multi-step tool execution. As LLM agents are increasingly deployed via protocols like the Model Context Protocol (MCP), the attack surface expands dramatically: adversarial manipulation can result in real environmental actions such as sending phishing emails, executing malware, or leaking sensitive data. Prior evolutionary red-teaming methods (e.g., Rainbow Teaming) still operate at the text-output level, leaving the space of harmful multi-step tool trajectories largely unexplored. This paper addresses the gap by formalizing agent red-teaming in terms of whether harmful objectives are realized through actual tool execution, not just harmful text generation. Methodology: T-MAP adapts the MAP-Elites quality-diversity evolutionary algorithm to the agent red-teaming setting. It maintains a 2D archive of attack prompts indexed by risk category (8 types: data leakage, property loss, physical harm, etc.) and attack style (8 types: role-play, authority manipulation, hypothetical framing, etc.), yielding 64 archive cells. Each iteration: (1) Cross-Diagnosis — an LLM Analyst extracts success factors from a high-performing parent cell and failure causes from the target cell; (2) Trajectory-Guided Mutation — an LLM Mutator generates a new attack prompt incorporating the cross-diagnosis signals plus a learned Tool Call Graph (TCG), a directed graph over tool transitions annotated with empirical success/failure counts and rationales; (3) MCP Execution — the mutated prompt is executed by the target agent in the live environment; (4) Archive Update — an LLM Judge evaluates the full execution trajectory and updates the archive if the new prompt achieves equal or higher attack realization. The TCG is updated after each execution, accumulating transition-level priors that guide future mutations toward high-success tool sequences. DeepSeek-V3.2 serves as the attacker (Mutator, Analyst, Judge) and GPT-5-mini as the default target agent in a sandboxed MCP environment spanning five servers: CodeExecutor, Slack, Gmail, Playwright, and Filesystem. Key Results: T-MAP achieves an average Attack Realization Rate (ARR) of 57.8% across five MCP environments, versus 32.5% for Standard Evolution (SE), 15.6% for Iterative Refinement (IR), 10.0% for Multi-Trial, and 1.9% for Zero-Shot. Refusal rate drops to 12.5% under T-MAP versus 50.3% for IR. T-MAP discovers 21.80 distinct successful tool trajectories on average, compared to 12.80 for SE and 9.20 for IR, while achieving lower Self-BLEU (0.25 vs. 0.45 for SE) and lower cosine similarity (0.47 vs. 0.52), confirming higher lexical and semantic diversity. In multi-MCP chain settings (e.g., Gmail + CodeExecutor + Filesystem), T-MAP still leads with 46.28% cross-server trajectories versus 14–23% for baselines. Generalization experiments across 9 frontier models — including GPT-5.2, Gemini-3-Pro, Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6, Qwen3.5-397B, and GLM-5 — confirm T-MAP consistently outperforms ZS and SE, though Claude models (Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6) retain relatively higher refusal rates. Cross-model transferability (pass@5) of attacks discovered on GPT-5.2 remains high across diverse architectures. Ablation confirms both the TCG (primary contributor to ARR: 58.4% vs. 45.7% without) and Cross-Diagnosis (primary contributor to bypassing guardrails: RR 11.9% vs. 15.6% without) are necessary components. Strengths: - The formalization of agent red-teaming as trajectory-level harm realization (L0–L3 levels) is a concrete and meaningful advance over text-output-based success metrics, directly measuring whether attacks have real-world consequences. - The Tool Call Graph is a lightweight but effective mechanism: it accumulates empirical transition statistics online, enabling the mutator to steer toward viable tool sequences and away from high-failure transitions without requiring any gradient information. - The paper is thorough experimentally — five environments, nine target models, multi-MCP chains, ablations, diversity analysis, human evaluation with 0.83 Pearson correlation, and cost accounting — providing strong evidence for the claims. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All experiments are conducted in sandboxed environments with artificially permissive tool access; the paper acknowledges that real-world deployments add permission checks, user confirmation, and input validation that would reduce realized ARR, but no experiments quantify this gap. - The attacker model is DeepSeek-V3.2, chosen partly for its weak safety alignment, which inherently makes it willing to generate harmful prompts. This creates a confound: improvements in T-MAP's ARR may partly reflect the attacker model's permissiveness rather than the algorithmic contribution of TCG and Cross-Diagnosis alone. Sensitivity to attacker model choice is not evaluated.
Recommended 2603.22341 PDF
#34 — Innovative (74)

SpatialBoost: Enhancing Visual Representation through Language-Guided Reasoning (38 ▲ )

Byungwoo Jeon, Dongyoung Kim, Huiwon Jang, Insoo Kim, Jinwoo Shin · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Mar 2026
Why this rank: SpatialBoost's core insight — using language-based chain-of-thought supervision from a frozen LLM to propagate 3D spatial knowledge back into vision encoders without any ground-truth 3D annotations — is a creative inversion of the typical VLM training paradigm. The dual-channel attention mechanism is a principled and effective solution to catastrophic forgetting that demonstrably outperforms LoRA and full fine-tuning. The universality of improvements across four backbone families, six task families, and even already-spatial encoders is impressive and well-validated. However, the three-stage pipeline with a 7B LLM decoder is complex and expensive, and the individual components (MLP projector, CoT VQA, attention duplication) are each well-established — the novelty lies more in their effective composition than in fundamental methodological departure.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Pre-trained vision encoders (DINOv2, SigLIP, CLIP, etc.) are trained exclusively on 2D image data and consequently lack 3D spatial awareness. This deficit manifests as poor performance on depth estimation, 3D scene understanding, and vision-based robot control. Naïve remedies—training on multi-view data from simulation or carefully curated 3D datasets—impose severe data scalability constraints. The paper asks whether 3D spatial knowledge can be injected into existing frozen vision encoders cheaply, without multi-view requirements and without catastrophic forgetting. Methodology: SpatialBoost is a three-stage post-training framework that converts dense 3D spatial information extracted from standard 2D images into linguistic descriptions, then uses an LLM decoder to propagate that knowledge back into the vision encoder. Stage 1 trains a projector (2-layer MLP) to align vision features with a 7B LLM (Qwen-2.0) following the LLaVA-1.5 recipe. Stage 2 adds visual instruction tuning using a newly constructed multi-view VQA dataset (200K samples from ScanNet, Mip-NeRF360, MVImgNet, Ego4D) filtered by perceptual similarity (LPIPS 0.35–0.65). Stage 3 is the core: the vision encoder is fine-tuned on a multi-turn Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Visual Spatial Reasoning dataset (100K single-view + 200K multi-view) while the LLM is frozen. The CoT dataset contains 12-turn conversations structured as pixel-level (absolute/relative depth at 2D coordinates), object-level (3D bounding-cube prediction and relative position), and scene-level (inter-object distance) questions, extracted automatically using Depth-Pro and SAM2 for single-view and VGGT for multi-view. To prevent catastrophic forgetting, a dual-channel attention mechanism is introduced: each attention layer in the vision encoder is duplicated, and outputs are merged via a learnable per-dimension sigmoid scalar α initialized to 0.5. Only the new attention branch and α are updated; the original weights are frozen. The LLM autoregressive loss propagates spatial knowledge back through the projector into the new attention branch. Key Results: SpatialBoost consistently improves every tested backbone across all task families. On monocular depth estimation (NYUd linear probe), DINOv3 improves from RMSE 0.31 to 0.25 and SigLIPv2 from 0.51 to 0.39—exceeding most dedicated vision-language encoders. On semantic segmentation (ADE20K linear probe), DINOv3 improves from 55.9 to 59.7 mIoU, establishing new state-of-the-art. On Lexicon3D 3D scene understanding, DINOv3 SQA3D improves from 51.4% to 54.9% BLEU-1 and ScanRefer visual grounding from 56.2% to 61.1%. On CortexBench vision-based robot learning, DINOv3 average score improves from 72.8 to 80.8 across four domains. Importantly, ImageNet linear probe classification improves from 88.4% to 90.2% for DINOv3, demonstrating that spatial fine-tuning does not harm—and actually boosts—general visual representations. The method scales favorably: performance monotonically improves from 50K to 300K training samples. Ablations confirm: (a) forward hierarchical CoT ordering (pixel→object→scene) outperforms reversed or random orderings; (b) combining single-view and multi-view data is better than either alone; (c) dual-channel attention uniquely preserves pre-trained knowledge while full fine-tuning and LoRA degrade classification accuracy by 6.8% and 4.6% respectively; (d) LLM-based supervision outperforms pixel-level alternatives (linear depth/segmentation heads, SAM decoder, VGGT decoder) across all tasks. SpatialBoost also improves already-spatial encoders (TIPS, PE-Core, PE-Spatial) and vision encoders inside large MLLMs (InternVL3-38B BLINK score +5.2, Qwen3-VL-32B MUIRBench +3.6). Strengths: - The CoT dataset construction pipeline is fully automatic—3D information is distilled from off-the-shelf depth and segmentation models—requiring no ground-truth 3D annotation, yet bias propagation analysis shows negligible performance difference vs. GT-based data. - Dual-channel attention is a principled, low-overhead solution to catastrophic forgetting: it adds only 25–30% extra parameters, preserves the original attention path, and demonstrably outperforms LoRA and full fine-tuning at preserving prior knowledge. - Universality: the same framework uniformly improves four different backbone families (OpenCLIP, SigLIPv2, DINOv2, DINOv3) across six distinct task families (depth, segmentation, 3D scene understanding, robot control, classification, retrieval), including encoders already specialized for spatial tasks. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The data pipeline inherits errors from the vision foundation models (Depth-Pro, SAM2, VGGT) used to generate spatial annotations. While the bias propagation experiment shows negligible effect on a 100K ScanNet subset, this result only covers a clean indoor 3D dataset; generalization to unconstrained in-the-wild images with noisy depth predictions is not validated. - Training requires three sequential stages using a 7B LLM decoder (Qwen-2.0-7B) on 4× A100s, making the pipeline significantly more expensive than simple fine-tuning. The cost/benefit relative to directly training a larger vision encoder from scratch with 3D data is not analyzed. The dual-channel attention increase (25–30% more parameters) also increases inference cost permanently.
Recommended 2603.22057 PDF
#35 — Innovative (74)

When Models Judge Themselves: Unsupervised Self-Evolution for Multimodal Reasoning (12 ▲ )

Zhengxian Wu, Kai Shi, Chuanrui Zhang, Zirui Liao, Jun Yang, Ni Yang, Qiuying Peng, Luyuan Zhang, Hangrui Xu, Tianhuang Su, Zhenyu Yang, Haonan Lu, Haoqian Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Mar 2026
Why this rank: This paper scores 74 for its sharp identification that majority voting conflates consistency with correctness in unsupervised self-evolution, backed by compelling entropy trajectory analysis. The bounded calibration function and energy-based normalization (with KL-minimization interpretation) are creative, principled design choices that address a real instability problem. Competitive performance with supervised methods across six backbone models is noteworthy, though the frozen Judge ceiling and confinement to math reasoning limit the demonstrated generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Training multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for reasoning typically requires either expensive human-annotated data or distillation from stronger teacher models. Recent unsupervised self-evolution approaches use majority voting over multiple sampled responses as a pseudo-label signal, but this has a fundamental flaw: high self-consistency does not imply high reasoning quality. When a model systematically produces an incorrect answer with high frequency, majority voting reinforces that error, accelerating policy collapse toward low-entropy, near-deterministic outputs. The paper seeks a stable unsupervised post-training framework that can distinguish fine-grained quality differences among candidate trajectories without any ground-truth labels or external reward models. Methodology: The framework instantiates two roles from a single pretrained multimodal model: an Actor (trained) and a Judge (frozen copy of the Actor at initialization). For each image-question pair, the Actor samples n=8 reasoning trajectories and computes a self-consistency (SC) reward — the empirical frequency of each trajectory's answer. Rather than binarizing this into majority vote, the SC reward is kept as a soft signal preserving the full answer distribution. The frozen Judge then independently scores each trajectory along three dimensions (answer correctness 50%, reasoning quality 30%, visual grounding 20%) and outputs a raw score in [0,1]. Crucially, the Judge score is not used directly as a reward; instead it passes through a bounded, differentiable calibration function g(s) = 1 + λ+σ((s−th)/τh) − λ−σ((tl−s)/τl), which amplifies high-quality trajectories and suppresses low-quality ones within configurable bounds, preventing Judge noise from destabilizing the training loop. The final per-trajectory reward is Rk = r_SC · g(sk) minus a format penalty. Policy updates use GRPO, but rather than normalizing advantages by the group mean and standard deviation (standard GRPO), the paper applies an energy-based log-sum-exp baseline: the group-relative advantage Ak = α·Rk − log Σ exp(α·Rj). This is shown to be equivalent to minimizing KL divergence between the current policy and a soft reward-induced target distribution over the candidate set, preventing hard collapse to a single mode. Key Results: Evaluated on five math reasoning benchmarks (MathVision, MathVerse, WeMath, LogicVista, DynaMath) starting from Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct (baseline avg 34.6%). Training on Geo3K (unlabeled), the method achieves avg 37.9% (+3.3 absolute), with MathVision improving from 25.0% to 30.9% (+5.9 points) — the largest single-benchmark gain reported. This surpasses MM-UPT (majority vote, 35.4%), EvoLMM (35.2%), and VisionZero (36.7%) under comparable unsupervised settings, and is competitive with supervised RL-GRPO (37.7%) and teacher-distillation methods (Vision-R1: 38.4%). Ablation confirms all three components contribute: SC alone yields +0.2 avg, Judge alone +1.6, MV+Judge +2.9, SC+Judge +4.3, full system (SC+Judge+distributional) +4.9. The method scales across six backbone models (Qwen2-VL-2B through Qwen2.5-VL-32B, InternVL3-8B, GLM-4.1V-9B), consistently outperforming majority vote. Training cost is 1.4× relative to supervised GRPO due to online Judge scoring, cheaper than VisionZero (2.8×) and EvoLMM (2.2×). pass@10 comparison shows the method (0.54 avg) is slightly worse than the base model (0.57) but better than majority vote (0.48), indicating moderate distributional contraction. On non-math vision benchmarks (ChartQA, MMVP), the method generalizes better than MM-UPT across all three training sets. Strengths: - The core insight — that majority voting conflates consistency with correctness and triggers mode collapse — is empirically well-supported with entropy trajectory analysis and qualitative failure cases. - The bounded calibration function g(s) is a principled design choice: it prevents the unsupervised closed loop from over-fitting to Judge noise while still providing useful quality differentiation, avoiding the instability observed when raw Judge scores are used directly. - The distributional reward interpretation (KL minimization to a soft target distribution) provides rigorous theoretical grounding for why energy-based normalization prevents policy collapse better than standard GRPO mean-subtraction. - Breadth of evaluation is strong: five benchmarks, six backbone models spanning 2B–32B, three training datasets, pass@k analysis, generalization to non-math tasks, and cost comparison. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The frozen Judge is initialized from the same model being trained and never updated. As the Actor improves, the Judge's evaluation capability becomes a hard ceiling on self-evolution — the paper acknowledges this explicitly but leaves dynamic Judge updating to future work. - pass@10 degrades relative to the base model in some settings (0.54 vs 0.57), indicating the method does reduce output diversity. The paper attributes this to incorrect-consensus scenarios but does not propose a remedy. - Experiments are confined to geometry/math reasoning datasets; it is unclear whether the Actor–Judge synergy generalizes to open-ended reasoning domains where the Judge's structured scoring rubric (answer correctness, reasoning quality, visual grounding) may be less applicable or harder to calibrate. - The method introduces multiple sensitive hyperparameters (λ+, λ−, th, tl, τh, τl, α) for the calibration and distributional modeling stages; sensitivity analysis across these is limited to a few ablation entries rather than systematic sweeps.

#36 — Innovative (73)

Tiny Inference -Time Scaling with Latent Verifiers

Davide Bucciarelli, Evelyn Turri, Lorenzo Baraldi, Marcella Cornia, Lorenzo Baraldi, Rita Cucchiara · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: This paper scores 73 for its elegant architectural insight that DiT hidden states already carry rich semantic information, eliminating the redundant decode-re-encode bottleneck that prior work treated as unavoidable. The latency-aware evaluation framework — fixing wall-clock budget rather than candidate count — is a genuine methodological contribution that better reflects deployment realities. The finding that visual representation quality, not LLM capacity, is the primary bottleneck provides actionable guidance for the field.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Inference-time scaling for image generation — generating multiple candidates and selecting the best via a verifier — has emerged as a practical alternative to scaling training compute. The dominant approach uses Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) as verifiers, but these carry substantial overhead: diffusion models operate in a compressed latent space, yet MLLM verifiers require decoding latents to pixel space and re-encoding through an external visual encoder (e.g., CLIP), creating a redundant decode–re-encode bottleneck. This cost is especially acute for single-step generators (which complete generation in one forward pass), where the decoder itself is a non-trivial fraction of total inference time. Worse, prior work almost exclusively studies large candidate budgets (hundreds to thousands of samples), while real deployments typically afford only a handful. The paper addresses the gap between practical, latency-constrained deployment and the compute-oblivious assumptions of existing inference-time scaling benchmarks. Methodology: The paper proposes Verifier on Hidden States (VHS), a MLLM-based verifier that bypasses the decode–re-encode pipeline entirely by operating directly on intermediate hidden representations of a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) generator. Given a single-step DiT generator with layers {DiT_1, ..., DiT_L}, VHS taps the hidden state h_{ℓ} at a chosen intermediate layer ℓ and feeds it through a lightweight two-layer MLP connector into a compact LLM (Qwen2.5-0.5B), producing a binary yes/no score. Because VHS intercepts the forward pass at layer ℓ, it also skips the remaining L-(ℓ+1) DiT layers during verification, further reducing cost. Training is two-stage: (1) an alignment stage using LLaVA-style image–caption pairs, where captions are re-generated via Gemma-3-4B to correct generator hallucinations; (2) a verifier fine-tuning stage using 118k samples labeled by the GenEval automated pipeline, with weighted cross-entropy loss to address a 63%/37% class imbalance. Continuous scores are derived from the LLM's token-level probabilities for "yes"/"no", enabling finer-grained Best-of-N selection than binary decisions alone. The entire system is built on SANA-Sprint (a single-step DiT generator via continuous-time consistency distillation) and evaluated on GenEval under explicit wall-clock time budgets. Key Results: Against the standard MLLM w/ CLIP verifier baseline (LLaVA-style with frozen ViT-L/14@336), VHS on h7 reduces joint generation-and-verification time by 63.3% (102ms vs. 277ms per single candidate), FLOPs by 62.9% (5.6 vs. 15.1 TFLOPs), and VRAM by 14.5% (11.8 vs. 13.8 GB). An AE-based variant (MLLM w/ AE, which replaces CLIP with the autoencoder latent) achieves only 50.2% time savings and performs substantially worse on semantic tasks, confirming that autoencoder features are perceptually rich but semantically weak. Under matched wall-clock budgets of 550ms, 1100ms, and 1650ms, VHS (Best-of-4/9/15) achieves GenEval overall scores of 78.1%, 80.5%, and 80.9% respectively, versus the MLLM w/ CLIP baseline (Best-of-2/4/6) at 75.4%, 78.8%, and 80.4% — gains of +2.7%, +1.7%, and +0.5%. VHS also outperforms increasing the number of denoising steps (SANA-Sprint with 30 steps scores only 71.4% at 1650ms) and multi-step generators (SANA-1.5 at 79.4%). The gains are largest on semantically demanding categories: +5.2% on position, +3.0% on attribute binding, and +3.0% on two objects at 550ms. Ablations identify h7 (~35% depth in a 20-layer DiT) as the optimal layer, weighted cross-entropy as superior to standard cross-entropy (+4.2% overall), and token probability scoring as yielding +5.8% over binary selection. Generalization to PixArt-α-DMD shows 48% speedup vs. MLLM w/ CLIP with competitive accuracy. Robustness experiments with LoRA fine-tuning show VHS degrades more gracefully than MLLM w/ CLIP under generator weight updates. Strengths: - Elegantly eliminates the decode–re-encode bottleneck by exploiting that DiT hidden states already carry rich semantic information conditioned on the generation prompt, rather than treating the bottleneck as unavoidable infrastructure. - Latency-aware evaluation framework is a genuine methodological contribution: fixing wall-clock budget and comparing how many candidates each verifier can afford is far more deployment-relevant than comparing at identical N. - Thorough ablations over layer depth, loss function, LLM size, and training data cleanly isolate where gains come from — particularly the important finding that visual representation quality (not LLM capacity) is the primary bottleneck. Weaknesses / Limitations: - VHS is architecturally coupled to a specific generator: it must be retrained if the generator's architecture changes significantly, limiting plug-and-play applicability compared to pixel-space verifiers that work with any generator. - Evaluation is confined to GenEval, a structured compositional benchmark; it is unclear whether VHS's semantic advantage over MLLM w/ AE transfers to open-ended aesthetic quality or photorealism tasks where perceptual features (which AE captures better) matter more. - The optimal layer ℓ* (h7 for SANA-Sprint, h13 for PixArt-α-DMD) requires empirical search per generator, and the paper provides no principled criterion for selecting it beyond ablation, making adoption on new architectures non-trivial.
Search Results 2603.22492 PDF
#37 — Innovative (73)

BubbleRAG: Evidence-Driven Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Black-Box Knowledge Graphs

Duyi Pan, Tianao Lou, Xin Li, Haoze Song, Yiwen Wu, Mengyi Deng, Mingyu Yang, Wei Wang · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: BubbleRAG distinguishes itself by formalizing black-box KG retrieval as the Optimal Informative Subgraph Retrieval problem with formal NP-hardness and APX-hardness proofs, grounding its heuristic pipeline in principled theory rather than ad-hoc engineering. The anisotropic bubble expansion with anchor specialization and schema relaxation is a creative retrieval mechanism that addresses real failure modes in schema-unknown KGs. However, evaluation is limited to LLM-extracted KGs from standard QA corpora rather than truly heterogeneous real-world KGs, and the ~5x latency overhead tempers practical impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing graph-based RAG systems struggle when operating over "black-box" knowledge graphs—KGs whose schema, entity types, and relational structure are unknown at retrieval time. The paper identifies three root causes of failure: (1) semantic instantiation uncertainty (a query concept may map to heterogeneous KG surface forms, causing recall loss); (2) structural path uncertainty (the same high-level relation may appear as direct edges, multi-hop chains, or composite structures, again causing recall loss); and (3) evidential comparison uncertainty (implicit, multi-factor evidence is needed to rank candidates, causing precision loss). No prior method jointly optimizes for both recall and precision under these conditions. Methodology: BubbleRAG formalizes KG retrieval as the Optimal Informative Subgraph Retrieval (OISR) problem—a variant of Group Steiner Tree that seeks a connected subgraph maximizing average semantic relevance (information density) while covering all anchor groups derived from the query. The paper proves OISR is NP-hard and APX-hard via reduction from GST, motivating a heuristic pipeline with five stages. (1) Semantic Anchor Grouping: an LLM extracts both explicit and latent keywords, rewrites underspecified terms into query-conditioned constraints (anchor specialization), and uses retrieved text chunks to relax schema-sensitive conditions when strict matching would miss relevant nodes (schema relaxation). Anchors are clustered into weighted groups S = {S₁,...,Sₘ} with LLM-assigned importance weights. (2) CEG Discovery via Bubble Expansion: a multi-source, Dijkstra-like anisotropic search expands from all anchor nodes simultaneously using node cost = 1 − cos(z_q, z_v). Wavefronts grow through low-resistance (query-aligned) regions and fuse when frontiers from different anchor groups collide at a candidate Steiner node, producing Candidate Evidence Graphs (CEGs). A localized h-hop neighborhood construction keeps per-query complexity independent of global graph size. (3) CEG Ranking: candidates are scored by Score(T) = 1 / (Cost_sem(T) · Penalty_miss(T) + ε), where Cost_sem is the mean node semantic dissonance and Penalty_miss = e^{α·r_miss} exponentially penalizes uncovered high-weight anchor groups. The α hyperparameter enables AND/OR/comparison query semantics. (4) Reasoning-Aware Expansion: LLM-guided multi-hop expansion is applied only to the top-n ranked CEGs to discover answer entities in the neighborhood of the retrieved backbone. (5) Answer Generation: top CEGs are merged into a unified evidence graph combined with provenance text chunks for final LLM answer generation. Key Results: On three multi-hop QA benchmarks (HotpotQA, MuSiQue, 2WikiMultiHopQA) using 1,000-question validation splits, BubbleRAG achieves an average F1 of 63.02 and ACC_L of 66.63 with a 30B model, outperforming the strongest baseline (HippoRAG2) by +2.52% F1 and +2.23% accuracy on average. The gains are most pronounced on MuSiQue (3–4 hop reasoning): BubbleRAG F1 = 53.03 vs. HippoRAG2 = 45.04, a ~8-point gap. With the smaller 8B model, BubbleRAG (avg F1 = 60.88 across datasets) matches or exceeds many baselines running 30B models, suggesting retrieval quality—not model scale—is the primary bottleneck. Ablation studies confirm all components contribute: removing schema relaxation causes the largest drop (−11.35 F1 on 2Wiki), followed by removing anchor specialization (−4.52) and CEG ranking (−6.21). Latency is 21s/query, substantially faster than ToG (46s) but ~5× slower than HippoRAG2 (4.3s) due to LLM calls in anchor grouping and reasoning-aware expansion. Strengths: - Principled problem formulation as OISR with formal NP-hardness and APX-hardness proofs grounds the heuristic design and distinguishes the work from purely empirical RAG papers. - The anchor specialization + schema relaxation tandem is practically motivated and ablation-validated; it directly addresses the black-box regime where schema mismatch destroys recall before graph traversal even begins. - Anisotropic Bubble Expansion is computationally local (localized subgraph typically <10³ nodes even when the full KG has 10⁵+), making the method tractable and plug-and-play without retriever fine-tuning or KG modification. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The ~5× latency overhead over HippoRAG2 is non-trivial; the paper attributes it to LLM calls in Steps 2 and 5 but does not explore distillation, caching, or lighter alternatives that could close this gap. - Sensitivity analysis uses only 100 sampled queries from 2Wiki, making it difficult to assess whether the observed parameter sensitivities (especially the non-monotone behavior of budget B) are statistically reliable or dataset-specific artifacts. - The paper does not evaluate on truly heterogeneous or enterprise black-box KGs (e.g., Wikidata, proprietary KGs), relying exclusively on LLM-extracted KGs from the three QA corpora—leaving open how well OISR and Bubble Expansion generalize to denser, noisier, or structurally distinct real-world graphs.
Citations 2603.20309 PDF
#38 — Innovative (73)

SlopCodeBench: Benchmarking How Coding Agents Degrade Over Long-Horizon Iterative Tasks (20 ▲ )

Gabriel Orlanski, Devjeet Roy, Alexander Yun, Changho Shin, Alex Gu, Albert Ge, Dyah Adila, Frederic Sala, Aws Albarghouthi · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 Mar 2026
Why this rank: SlopCodeBench defines a genuinely new evaluation paradigm: measuring how AI coding agents accumulate architectural debt over iterative, causally-linked development checkpoints — a problem no prior benchmark addresses. The causal design forcing agents to inherit their own code without gold-standard resets is methodologically sound, and the negative result that prompt interventions cannot arrest degradation slope is a valuable and surprising finding.
