The human calibration panel and cleanly defined erosion/verbosity metrics provide interpretable baselines, though the small problem set (20 tasks) and lack of correlation between structural erosion and functional failure weaken the practical implications.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing agentic coding benchmarks evaluate agents in single-shot settings against complete, fixed specifications. This ignores the most practically important property of software: extensibility under evolving requirements. Code can pass all tests at checkpoint N while accumulating structural debt that makes checkpoint N+1 far harder to implement correctly. Prior iterative benchmarks either supply gold-standard implementations for prior turns (removing the causal chain from early decisions to later degradation), evaluate each turn independently, or use commit histories from public repositories (introducing contamination risk). No prior work quantifies how verbosity and structural complexity concentrate over iterative self-extension, or whether prompt interventions can arrest this degradation. Methodology: SlopCodeBench (SCBench) is a language-agnostic benchmark of 20 problems spanning 93 checkpoints. Each problem specifies only observable CLI or API behavior—no internal interfaces, no exposed test suites, no language prescriptions. At each checkpoint, the agent receives the new specification and its own prior workspace; conversation history is not provided, forcing the agent to reason from code state alone. Bad architectural choices at checkpoint i compound into checkpoint i+1. Two trajectory-level quality metrics are defined: (1) Structural erosion, the fraction of total complexity mass (CC × √SLOC per callable) held by functions with cyclomatic complexity > 10, measuring how branch logic collapses into god functions; and (2) Verbosity, the union of AST-grep-flagged wasteful lines (137 targeted rules) and clone lines, normalized by LOC. Eleven models (Sonnet 4.5/4.6, Opus 4.5/4.6, GPT 5.1–5.4 variants, GLM 4.7) are each evaluated in their provider's native CLI harness (Claude Code, Codex CLI) in headless mode inside fresh Docker containers per checkpoint. A calibration panel of 48 maintained Python repositories with temporal commit histories (568 checkpoints) provides a human baseline. A prompt-intervention study tests a minimal baseline ('just-solve'), an 'anti-slop' prompt forbidding verbose patterns, and a 'plan-first' prompt requiring architectural planning before coding, run on GPT 5.3 Codex and GPT 5.4. Key Results: No agent solves any problem end-to-end across all checkpoints. The best strict solve rate is 17.2% (Opus 4.6); isolated solve rates (excluding regression tests) span 7.5–23.7%, core rates 19.4–53.8%. Quality degrades monotonically: structural erosion rises in 80% of trajectories, verbosity in 89.8%. Mean high-CC function count climbs from 4.1 to 37.0 across checkpoints; mean maximum CC from 27.1 to 68.2. In a pathological case, Opus 4.6's main() on circuit_eval grows from CC=29 (84 lines) to CC=285 (1,099 lines) over 8 checkpoints. Against the 48-repository human panel, agent code averages 0.68 erosion vs. 0.31 for humans and 0.32 verbosity vs. 0.11; the gap widens over time. Human repositories show stable metrics across commits, while agent trajectories diverge monotonically. Prompt interventions (anti-slop, plan-first) reduce initial verbosity by 33–35% and lower initial erosion, but the degradation slope is statistically indistinguishable from baseline on both models (Wilcoxon p > 0.05 for all pass-rate subtypes). Cleaner code does not improve correctness and increases cost by up to 47.9% per run. Checkpoint cost grows 2.9× from the first to the final progress bin without improving pass rates. Strengths: - The core causal design—agents inherit their own code across checkpoints with no gold-standard resets—is the right experimental setup to isolate architectural debt accumulation; this is the primary gap over all prior iterative benchmarks. - The two quality metrics are cleanly defined, complementary (erosion ≠ verbosity), comparable across runs, and bounded; the human calibration panel provides interpretable absolute scale. - The prompt-intervention study is a genuine negative result: it rules out the obvious mitigation (just tell the agent to write clean code), establishes that degradation slope is parameter-invariant, and reports effect sizes with proper statistical testing. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark contains only 20 problems and 93 checkpoints, which is a small sample for drawing strong quantitative conclusions about model differences; confidence intervals on per-model metrics are very wide (e.g., Opus 4.6 cost: 3.47 ± 3.40 $/CKPT). - Python-only evaluation despite language-agnostic design limits generalizability; whether the degradation pattern holds in statically typed languages (where compilers enforce interface discipline) is untested and left as future work. - The human calibration comparison is not matched by task or difficulty—human repositories have years of refactoring history, code reviews, and test-driven discipline that agents lack by construction, making the gap partially a confound rather than a pure quality signal. - Structural erosion is uncorrelated with next-checkpoint pass rate (r ≈ -0.003), meaning the metrics measure something real about code quality but do not predict functional failure, weakening the argument that slop directly causes correctness collapse rather than merely co-occurring with it.
Recommended 2603.24755 PDF
#39 — Innovative (73)

Harmful Factuality: LLMs Correcting What They Shouldn't

aclanthology.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper earns a high score for formalizing Harmful Factuality Hallucination — a genuinely overlooked failure mode that inverts the standard hallucination framing and sits in a blind spot of prior taxonomies. The finding that larger, more capable models exhibit worse overcorrection behavior is counterintuitive and practically important for RAG pipeline design. The dual perturbation methodology (GEP + LIER) is well-conceived and reproducible. However, the mitigation contribution is thin — a single instruction prompt — and the string-matching evaluation without human validation limits confidence in the quantitative claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs are trained to be both factually accurate and faithful to source material, but these objectives fundamentally conflict when the source contains errors. While extensive prior work addresses models generating falsehoods from factual sources (harmful falsity), the opposite failure mode has been largely ignored: an LLM silently "correcting" a nonfactual source to align with its parametric knowledge, producing output that is world-factual but source-unfaithful. This paper formalizes this as Harmful Factuality Hallucination (HFH). The problem is severe in high-stakes domains — law, medicine, RAG pipelines, scientific analysis — where preserving source content verbatim, errors included, is non-negotiable. Methodology: The authors introduce a reproducible framework to elicit and measure HFH using controlled entity-level perturbations on the WikiEntities dataset (1,000 sampled texts from 3.2M Wikipedia documents). Two complementary perturbation methods are proposed: (1) Gaussian Embedding Perturbation (GEP), a soft method that adds calibrated Gaussian noise (α ∈ [0.1, 0.3]) to BERT embeddings of named entities and finds the nearest vocabulary token, producing semantically plausible but factually incorrect substitutions; and (2) LLM-Instructed Entity Replacement (LIER), a hard method that prompts GPT-4o to generate type-consistent, formally similar, but factually distinct entity replacements. Three entity selection strategies are tested: uniform (all entities), positional (head/body/tail segments), and theme-related (entities extracted from LLM-generated summaries). Models are evaluated across abstractive summarization, rephrasing, and QA (open-ended and multiple-choice) tasks on GPT-4.1/4o/4o-mini/o1/o4-mini and Llama-1B/3B/8B. Outputs are classified into four categories: Harmful Factuality (perturbed entity corrected back), Faithfulness Adherence (perturbed entity preserved), Dual Presence (both entities appear), and Entity Omission. HFH classification is fully automatic via string matching. Key Results: HFH is prevalent and worsens with model scale: GPT-4o exhibits HFH in 5.35% of summarization cases under GEP vs. 2.81% for GPT-4o-mini; Llama-8B shows roughly twice the rate of Llama-1B. Proprietary GPT models show substantially lower Faithfulness Adherence (~10-18%) than open-weight Llama models (~18-30%), indicating stronger overcorrection tendencies in larger, more knowledge-rich models. Entity position has a dramatic effect: head entities (first 25% of tokens) exhibit 11.81% HFH vs. 2.35% for body and 0.97% for tail under GEP. Theme-related entity selection produces the highest HFH rate of 20.51%, as these entities are central to summarization and thus focal points for model correction. Perturbation strength (α) has modest impact: GPT-4.1 increases from 5.88% to 6.25% as α goes from 0.1 to 0.3 (not statistically significant). Three underlying mechanisms are identified in Dual Presence outputs: Error-Correction (dominant in GPT-4.1), Coreference/Homonym Mixing, and Conflation/Fabrication. A simple instructional prompt ("Only use the context and knowledge in the given text. DO NOT use the interior knowledge.") reduces HFH rates by approximately 50% — GPT-4.1 drops from >5% to <2.5% under GEP. All scale-based comparisons are statistically significant (χ² tests, p < 0.01). Strengths: - Clearly identifies and formalizes a genuinely overlooked failure mode that sits in a blind spot of prior hallucination taxonomies, with compelling practical motivation in RAG and high-stakes domains. - Dual perturbation methodology (embedding-space GEP + symbolic LIER) is principled, reproducible, and spans complementary difficulty regimes; the full evaluation pipeline is fully automated without requiring human annotation. - Empirical coverage is comprehensive: 8 models, 3 tasks, 3 entity selection strategies, 3 perturbation strengths, with statistical significance validation via χ² tests. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is restricted to Wikipedia text via the WikiEntities dataset, which has well-known editorial conventions (key facts front-loaded) that likely amplify the head-position HFH effect; generalizability to clinical notes, legal documents, or conversational data is unvalidated. - HFH measurement relies purely on string matching for entity presence/absence, which is brittle — paraphrases, abbreviations, or partial mentions can produce false classifications in either direction, yet no human validation of the automated labels is reported. - The proposed mitigation is a single-sentence instruction prompt; no exploration of more robust or scalable defenses (RLHF from counterfactuals, decoding regularization, retrieval filtering) is provided, leaving the practical takeaway limited to a prompt hack that may degrade factual accuracy on legitimate correction tasks.
Citations
#40 — Innovative (73)

Versatile Editing of Video Content, Actions, and Dynamics without Training (11 ▲ )

Vladimir Kulikov, Roni Paiss, Andrey Voynov, Inbar Mosseri, Tali Dekel, Tomer Michaeli · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Mar 2026
Why this rank: DynaEdit addresses a genuine open problem — dynamic, physics-aware video editing without training — through principled diagnosis of two specific failure modes (low-frequency misalignment and high-frequency jitter) followed by targeted solutions (SGA and ANC). Matching a proprietary trained model (Runway Aleph) in a user study while requiring no fine-tuning is a surprising and practically significant result. The model-agnostic, training-free design ensures immediate benefit from future stronger base models.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Editing dynamic events in real-world videos—changing actions, inserting objects that interact with the scene, or modifying physics-grounded outcomes—remains an open challenge. Existing trained models (e.g., Runway Gen-4 Aleph) require difficult-to-collect paired source-target video data and still struggle with complex non-rigid edits. Training-free methods are inherently restricted to structure-preserving, structurally-aligned transformations and cannot alter motion trajectories or cause inserted objects to meaningfully interact with the scene. The paper addresses the fundamental gap of enabling arbitrary spatio-temporal modifications without any additional training. Methodology: DynaEdit builds on the inversion-free FlowEdit paradigm, which constructs a noise-free ODE path transforming the source video into an edited video using a pretrained text-to-video flow model (WAN2.1 14B I2V). The authors first diagnose two failure modes that arise when extending this approach to unconstrained editing: (1) low-frequency misalignment—when the ODE is initialized at full noise (nmax=N), the initial edit steps have no connection to the source video's coarse spatio-temporal structure, causing the edited video to diverge in camera motion, object trajectories, etc.; and (2) high-frequency jitter—independent noise samples across timesteps cause edit velocities to point in inconsistent directions, producing blurry, artifact-laden results. To address these, DynaEdit introduces two novel components. Similarity Guided Aggregation (SGA): At each edit step, multiple noise samples are drawn, each predicting a projected final edit; these are weighted by their cosine similarity to the source video via a softmax with temperature τ, so that noise directions more consistent with the source are favored. This soft selection steers coarse features toward the source without hard constraints. Annealed Noise Correlation (ANC): Rather than using fully independent noise samples across timesteps (which causes jitter) or the same sample (which harms low-frequency alignment), ANC gradually increases temporal noise correlation using a linear annealing schedule: earlier steps use diverse independent noise (for low-frequency alignment), while later steps use highly correlated noise (to suppress high-frequency jitter). The method is model-agnostic and also demonstrated with Hunyuan 1.5 I2V. Key Results: DynaEdit is evaluated on a curated dataset of 71 videos across four categories: interactive insertion, object swap with action consequences, action/motion change, and global effects. Using VLM-based scoring (Gemini 3 Pro, 1–5 scale), DynaEdit achieves the best balance among competing methods: content preservation 4.50, text adherence 4.08, and visual quality 3.83 (best configuration), substantially outperforming FlowEdit (4.07 / 3.85 / 2.87) and FlowAlign (3.40 / 3.76 / 1.95) on all axes. Against the trained Runway Aleph model, DynaEdit matches or exceeds performance (Aleph: 4.18 / 4.18 / 3.61), with DynaEdit scoring higher on content preservation and visual quality in some configurations. A user study with 32 participants (2,400+ responses) confirms preference for DynaEdit over Aleph (59.2% vs Aleph), FlowEdit (77.7%), and I2V sampling (79.9%) across all three evaluation criteria. Per-category analysis shows DynaEdit outperforms Aleph on insertion and swap categories, is comparable on action change, and trails only on global effects. Strengths: - Addresses a genuine open problem (dynamic, physics-aware video editing) with a principled analysis of failure modes in the inversion-free paradigm, providing both diagnosis and targeted solutions rather than heuristic patches. - Training-free and model-agnostic design: the method requires no fine-tuning, leverages any pretrained I2V flow model, and does not intervene in model internals—enabling immediate benefit from stronger base models. - Comprehensive evaluation on a newly curated benchmark covering four distinct edit categories, with both VLM-based automated metrics and a rigorous user study; DynaEdit is the first training-free method to match a proprietary trained model (Aleph) on this task. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method inherits the limitations of the underlying I2V model, which can struggle with physical plausibility (e.g., gravity, collisions), meaning complex physics-based edits may still fail even when the editing framework works correctly. - The evaluation benchmark is self-curated (71 videos), which risks selection bias; there is no established community benchmark for dynamic video editing, limiting objective cross-paper comparisons. - SGA requires evaluating multiple noise samples per step (nSGA=5 for the first three steps), increasing per-edit compute cost, though the fixed schedule partially mitigates this; the sensitivity to CFG scale and temperature τ hyperparameters still requires some tuning per edit type.
Recommended 2603.17989 PDF
#41 — Innovative (73)

Mixture-of-Trees: Learning to Select and Weigh Reasoning Paths for Efficient LLM Inference

ojs.aaai.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Mixture-of-Trees creatively transplants the sparse-activation principle from parameter-level MoE to reasoning-level orchestration, achieving a rare Pareto improvement: ~40% compute reduction with simultaneous accuracy gains over Forest-of-Thought. The independent sigmoid gating and early-stopping design are well-motivated departures from standard practice. However, the evaluation is limited to mid-tier models, and the gating network's supervision requirements raise reproducibility concerns.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-path LLM reasoning methods (Tree-of-Thought, Forest-of-Thought) improve accuracy over Chain-of-Thought but incur prohibitive computational costs by uniformly activating all reasoning paths regardless of problem complexity or domain. This one-size-fits-all paradigm scales poorly in practice: costs grow exponentially with path count, no specialization is applied to different problem types, and there is no mechanism for structured expert collaboration when paths conflict. The paper addresses all three gaps simultaneously. Methodology: Mixture-of-Trees (MoT) transplants the sparse-activation principle of Mixture-of-Experts from the parameter level to the reasoning level. The framework maintains a pool of N heterogeneous expert trees, each parameterized by a domain-specific role prompt (e.g., "Algebra Expert") and a model of appropriate capacity (e.g., Qwen-32B for hard tasks, Qwen-7B for easy ones). A lightweight two-layer MLP gating network takes joint embeddings of the input question and each expert role and outputs independent activation probabilities via sigmoid (not softmax, so activations are mutually independent). The top-k experts exceeding a confidence threshold τ are selected. Before running any expert, a shared baseline tree generates a quick candidate answer; activated experts then perform lightweight binary validation (accept/reject) rather than full re-solving. If weighted votes favor acceptance, the system exits early. Otherwise, each expert generates a full solution on role-enhanced input (few-shot examples + domain rephrasing), and if solutions conflict, T rounds of structured debate allow experts to revise answers by examining peers' reasoning. Final output is determined by weighted voting using normalized activation probabilities scaled by per-expert confidence scores. The gating network is trained with a loss combining cross-entropy accuracy, an expert-count sparsity penalty, and a token-cost penalty. Key Results: Evaluated on GSM8K, MATH, AIME 2024, MMLU, and HotpotQA with GPT-4o-mini and Qwen2.5-7B as base models. MoT (k=4, T=2, 12 experts) outperforms the strongest prior method FoT (n=4) by 2–7 percentage points across all benchmarks. On competition-level AIME 2024, MoT reaches 40.0% with GPT-4o-mini versus FoT's 33.3% (+6.7 pp). On MMLU with GPT-4o-mini: 93.9% vs. FoT's 91.6%. Critically, MoT reduces LLM calls by 40.6% on GSM8K and 37.0% on MMLU versus FoT while achieving higher accuracy. Efficiency Score (Accuracy/Cost) reaches 87.4 on GSM8K (+138% over FoT) and 104.3 on MMLU (+162% over FoT). Ablation on HotpotQA shows sparse activation alone cuts calls from 98.45 to 33.67 (−66%) with only 1.4% accuracy drop; adding early stopping reduces calls further to 23.91; the full system reaches 20.23 calls while recovering accuracy to 81.1% via expert trees. Scalability experiments show accuracy improves steadily from 8 to 64 experts with only slight token cost increases due to sparse activation. Strengths: - The combination of sparse gating, heterogeneous model assignment, and early stopping is practically impactful: the framework cuts compute by ~40% while simultaneously improving accuracy, a rare Pareto improvement over prior work. - The independent (non-normalized) sigmoid gating is a well-motivated departure from standard softmax MoE, correctly reflecting that expert relevance decisions are not mutually exclusive for reasoning tasks. - Ablation study is thorough and cleanly isolates the contribution of each component; the interaction between sparse activation (efficiency) and expert trees (accuracy recovery) is explicitly characterized. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The gating MLP requires training data with labeled problem-expert relevance pairs; the paper does not clearly explain how this supervision is obtained or how sensitive results are to the quality/size of this training set, raising reproducibility concerns. - Evaluation is limited to two base models (GPT-4o-mini and Qwen2.5-7B); both are mid-tier models. It is unclear whether the gains hold with frontier models (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet) where single-path reasoning is already very strong, or whether the debate mechanism adds value when baseline accuracy is already >90%. - The collaborative debate mechanism adds T×|A| additional LLM calls in the conflict branch; the paper does not report how often debate is triggered per dataset, making it hard to estimate worst-case cost. The fixed-round debate could be expensive on hard tasks where experts frequently disagree.
Search Results
#42 — Innovative (72)

Multi-stage Flow Scheduling for LLM Serving

Yijun Sun, Xudong Liao, Songrun Xie, Hao Chen, Han Tian, Wenxue Li, Yiming Zhang, Kai Chen · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: This paper introduces a genuinely novel abstraction — multi-stage flows with stage-specific deadline semantics — that correctly identifies why existing coflow schedulers fail for disaggregated LLM serving. The Defer-and-Promote principle and Reverse MLFQ are creative inversions of classical scheduling ideas, and the formal optimality proof for Smallest-RLI-First adds theoretical grounding. The 20–32% TTFT reductions and 1.2–2.4× SLO attainment improvements are substantial, though evaluation on RTX 3090 Ethernet testbeds rather than production-class NVLink/InfiniBand clusters leaves open questions about real-world applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern LLM serving systems using disaggregated prefill-decode architectures with KV-cache reuse and diverse parallelisms generate complex multi-stage communication workflows during the prefill phase: (1) KV-cache reuse transfers, (2) collective communication (all-to-all for expert/sequence parallelism), and (3) Prefill-to-Decode (P2D) transfers. These flows from dependent stages overlap on shared network links, causing both intra-request contention (different communication stages of the same request competing) and inter-request contention (concurrent requests fighting for bandwidth). Measurements on a 16-GPU cluster show contention inflates end-to-end TTFT by ~50% and nearly doubles all-to-all collective completion time (1.8×). Critically, Microsoft production data attributes ~40% of service timeout failures to delayed first tokens. Existing flow and coflow scheduling algorithms treat flows in a stage-agnostic manner, misaligning optimization objectives (minimizing flow completion time) with the application-level goal (TTFT SLO attainment). Prior stage-specific optimizations (collective algorithm synthesis, KV-cache prefetching) operate in isolation and can amplify cross-stage contention. Methodology: The paper presents MFS (Multi-stage Flow Scheduling), a holistic scheduler integrated into vLLM that jointly orchestrates all three communication stages to maximize TTFT SLO attainment. The core insight is that request-level TTFT deadlines progressively materialize into explicit flow-level deadlines as prefill execution advances—early stages have implicit, uncertain laxity, while the final P2D stage has a deterministic deadline. This motivates the Defer-and-Promote principle: defer non-urgent flows to the latest safe moment and promote priority only as laxity diminishes, approximating Least-Laxity-First (LLF) without requiring precise laxity knowledge. MFS implements this via a Reverse Multi-Level Queue (RMLQ), which inverts the classic MLFQ by initializing flows in low-priority queues and promoting upward. For Stage 3 (explicit deadline), MFS defines Minimal Link Utilization (MLU = remaining_size / (remaining_time × effective_bandwidth)) as the urgency metric, with geometrically-spaced promotion thresholds to minimize worst-case quantization error; promotions occur only at layer granularity to prevent packet reordering. For Stages 1–2 (implicit deadline), MFS uses Relative Layer Index (RLI = L_target − L_curr) to capture structural determinism: flows with RLI=0 block the GPU and must be prioritized immediately (proven optimal for minimizing prefill makespan via exchange argument). For inter-request contention, MFS employs Robust Effective Deadline (RED) to counteract the Piggyback effect (tight outliers hijacking batch priority) and performs worst-case feasibility checks with selective pruning to avoid the Black Hole effect (overloaded batches starving viable ones). Priority is enforced via hybrid software queues (host-side) combined with DSCP-tagged hardware queues on switches, implemented as ~10,000 lines of code integrating with NCCL and Mooncake. Key Results: Testbed evaluation on 8 servers / 32 NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPUs with Mixtral-8x7B shows MFS reduces mean TTFT by 20.7% (1.26×) on conversation workloads and 32.3% (1.48×) on agent workloads, with stage-2 collective completion time reduced by 31.9–43.1%. Large-scale simulations across four MoE models (Mixtral-8x22B, DBRX, Qwen3-Coder, Grok-2) and a dense Llama3-8B model show MFS improves TTFT SLO attainment by 1.2×–2.4× over all baselines (Fair Sharing, SJF, EDF, Karuna). MFS sustains 1.17×–1.46× higher request rates than the strongest baseline (Karuna) at equivalent SLO attainment. Breakdown analysis confirms two sources of gain: a 52% reduction in average collective completion time (prefill makespan reduction) and a 42% reduction in unnecessary earliness (better urgency prioritization). Gains are consistent across both conversation and agent workloads and across MoE and dense models with sequence parallelism. Strengths: - The abstraction of multi-stage flows with stage-specific semantics (blocking vs. non-blocking, explicit vs. implicit deadlines) is a clean and general formulation that correctly identifies why prior coflow and flow schedulers systematically fail for LLM serving. - The Theorem 1 proof (Smallest-RLI-First minimizes prefill makespan) provides a formal optimality guarantee for intra-request scheduling under the stated model assumptions, lending theoretical grounding to the heuristic. - The system engineering is thorough: RMLQ integrates with both NCCL and Mooncake, avoids packet reordering via layer-granularity promotion, and uses DSCP tagging for switch-level enforcement on commodity hardware without requiring programmable network devices. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The testbed uses only NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPUs connected via 100Gbps Ethernet, which is substantially slower and less capable than production H100/A100 NVLink+InfiniBand clusters; it is unclear whether the contention patterns and relative gains hold at production scale and bandwidth. - The geometric MLU promotion thresholds (E=4, U=0.5) are set empirically without rigorous sensitivity analysis; the paper acknowledges optimal threshold derivation is NP-hard but does not quantify performance degradation from suboptimal parameter choices. - MFS is evaluated only in disaggregated prefill-decode settings; its applicability to non-disaggregated deployments or emerging GPU-initiated collective communication (e.g., DeepSeek's DeepEP) is acknowledged but not empirically validated, limiting practical coverage.
Search Results 2603.17456 PDF
#43 — Innovative (72)

Why Does Self-Distillation (Sometimes) Degrade the Reasoning Capability of LLMs? (27 ▲ )

Jeonghye Kim, Xufang Luo, Minbeom Kim, Sangmook Lee, Dohyung Kim, Jiwon Jeon, Dongsheng Li, Yuqing Yang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Mar 2026
Why this rank: This paper scores 72 for identifying epistemic verbalization as the mechanism behind self-distillation failures in mathematical reasoning — a genuinely novel diagnostic insight. The information-theoretic framing via conditional mutual information provides a principled explanation for the apparent contradiction between prior positive results and math-domain degradation. The two-factor decomposition (information richness × task coverage) is elegant and well-validated across three model families. However, the work is primarily diagnostic with no actionable remedy, and the 10-token marker set is a coarse proxy for true uncertainty-aware reasoning.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Self-distillation has become a popular post-training paradigm for LLMs, where one instance of a model conditioned on ground-truth solutions acts as a teacher to guide another instance without solution access. Prior work reports that self-distillation improves performance while shortening reasoning traces in domains like chemistry, scientific Q&A, and code. However, when applied to mathematical reasoning, the same approach can sharply degrade performance even as response length decreases. This paper investigates why: what mechanism causes self-distillation to harm OOD generalization in math despite training on correct answer traces? Methodology: The authors identify epistemic verbalization — the model's explicit expression of uncertainty during reasoning (tokens like "wait", "hmm", "perhaps", "maybe") — as the key mechanism. They conduct a four-condition experiment varying conditioning richness (unguided, solution-guided with full chain-of-thought, solution-guided without think tags, regeneration-conditioned) using DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B on DAPO-Math-17k, measuring response length, accuracy, and counts of 10 epistemic marker tokens. They then run both off-policy SFT experiments (training on 800 correct unguided vs. solution-guided trajectories) and on-policy RL experiments comparing GRPO against SDPO across Qwen3-8B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B, and OLMo-3-7B-Instruct. A second key variable — task coverage — is isolated by varying training set size |D| ∈ {1, 8, 64, 128, 512} to disentangle in-domain efficiency from OOD generalization. The information-theoretic framing uses conditional mutual information I(y; c | x) to formally order the richness of conditioning contexts. Key Results: Richer conditioning monotonically suppresses epistemic tokens: unguided generation averages 182.5 epistemic tokens vs. 8.8 under full solution guidance, while accuracy jumps from 0.30 to 0.98 — showing the teacher generates trivially confident responses. Off-policy SFT on solution-guided trajectories (Dsg) catastrophically degrades all math benchmarks: AIME24 drops from 54.79 to 20.21, AMC23 from 89.06 to 57.03, MATH500 from 92.19 to 65.52 — a ~35 point drop — while SFT on unguided trajectories (Dug) causes no significant change. On-policy SDPO with full solution conditioning (c = s) causes ~40% OOD degradation on AIME24 for DeepSeek-Distill-7B (54.7 → ~33%) and consistently underperforms GRPO across all three model families. Reducing conditioning to c = s∖think partially mitigates the drop but does not recover to baseline. Task coverage experiments show SDPO outperforms GRPO when |D| ≤ 128 (small, repetitive task sets) but progressively degrades as |D| grows, while GRPO scales favorably — establishing that epistemic verbalization is redundant for narrow task sets but critical for broad generalization. A moving-target EMA teacher amplifies degradation via a feedback loop even at slow update rates (0.05). Strengths: - The information-theoretic framing via conditional mutual information provides a principled and falsifiable account of why richer teacher context suppresses uncertainty expression, going beyond empirical observation. - The two-factor decomposition (information richness × task coverage) elegantly explains the apparent contradiction between prior work (where self-distillation helps) and the authors' math setting — the key confound is low task coverage in prior benchmarks. - Results are replicated across three distinct model families (Qwen3-8B, DeepSeek-Distill-7B, OLMo-3-7B) and both off-policy and on-policy settings, substantially strengthening the generality claim. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The 10-token epistemic marker set is a coarse proxy for genuine uncertainty-aware reasoning; the paper does not verify that suppression of these surface tokens actually corresponds to loss of inferential flexibility rather than mere stylistic change. - The paper is primarily diagnostic — it characterizes when and why self-distillation fails but offers no concrete remedy, leaving practitioners without actionable guidance beyond "use less informative conditioning" or "reduce task coverage." - All experiments use 7–8B parameter models on mathematical benchmarks; it is unclear whether the epistemic verbalization mechanism generalizes to larger models (which may encode uncertainty implicitly) or to other reasoning-heavy domains beyond math.
Recommended 2603.24472 PDF
#44 — Innovative (72)

CrossLinker: Aligning Relational and Sequential Contexts for Drug-Target Interaction Prediction in Cold-Start and Few-Shot Scenarios

pubs.acs.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: CrossLinker's link-aware cross-attention mechanism is a well-motivated architectural innovation that efficiently produces context-dependent representations by using the counterpart's global feature as the query — an elegant design choice that avoids the quadratic cost of full cross-sequence attention. The +17.6 AUROC point gain on unseen-protein cold-start is a substantial and practically important improvement addressing the most clinically relevant deployment scenario. However, the warm-start performance trade-off and the incomplete main paper limit the overall impact assessment.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Predicting drug-target interactions (DTIs) is a central challenge in computational drug discovery. Existing sequence- and graph-based models learn static, entity-level representations that fail to adapt to the interaction context — a drug's binding-relevant features differ depending on which target it is paired with. This context-blindness is especially damaging in cold-start scenarios where entirely novel drugs or proteins appear at inference time, as models must extrapolate without any relational prior. CrossLinker addresses this gap by coupling biomedical knowledge graph (KG) relational structure with sequence-level encoders through a pairwise, link-conditioned representation learning framework. Methodology: CrossLinker operates through two parallel branches. The KG branch is pretrained on KG triples using a graph network and produces graph-level entity features. The sequence branch uses pretrained encoders for SMILES (drugs) and amino-acid sequences (proteins). The key innovation is a link-aware cross-attention mechanism: rather than computing self-attention independently for each entity, each entity uses its counterpart's global feature as a query to attend over its own local token/neighbor embeddings. This produces pair-conditioned local features — representations that shift based on the specific interaction context. Computational overhead is modest: O(4ds) per node pair for the graph branch (linear in sampled neighborhood size s) and O(2d(m+n)) per drug-target pair for the sequence branch (linear in sequence lengths). A link-based contrastive learning strategy then aligns these pair-conditioned local features across the KG and sequence branches via an in-batch contrastive objective, transferring relational priors from the KG into the sequence representation space. Training follows three stages — KG pretraining, alignment training, and label fine-tuning — with an optional fourth 10-shot fine-tuning stage for few-shot settings. The final classifier is an MLP. Key Results: On the BindingDB dataset with AUROC as metric, CrossLinker achieves 0.940 (warm/random split), 0.831 (cold-start, unseen protein), and 0.947 (cold-start, unseen drug). Compared to fine-grained interaction baselines: DrugBAN achieves 0.962/0.655/0.941 and PSICHIC achieves 0.949/0.754/0.927 on the same three splits. CrossLinker trades ~2% warm-start AUROC versus DrugBAN for a +17.6 point gain on unseen-protein cold-start (0.831 vs. 0.655) and +2.0 points on unseen-drug cold-start. Qualitative attention visualization confirms that CrossLinker's atom-level attention scores shift meaningfully between different target contexts for the same drug (e.g., warfarin attending differently w.r.t. VKORC1 vs. HSA), whereas baseline self-attention produces identical static heatmaps regardless of the paired target. Strengths: - The link-aware cross-attention design is mechanistically well-motivated: using the counterpart's global feature as the query is an efficient (O(1) query length) way to produce context-dependent local features without full cross-sequence attention. - The cold-start improvements are substantial and practically important — +17.6 AUROC points on unseen proteins over the best baseline — directly addressing the most clinically relevant deployment scenario. - The attention visualization provides interpretable, biochemically grounded evidence that the model learns interaction-specific binding determinants rather than generic scaffold features. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The PDF provided is the Supporting Information only; the main paper's full experimental tables, dataset statistics, ablation results, and broader baseline comparisons are not available for review, making it impossible to assess the complete empirical picture. - The warm-start AUROC (0.940) is slightly below both DrugBAN (0.962) and PSICHIC (0.949), suggesting the KG alignment may introduce a trade-off that slightly hurts interpolation performance on seen entities — a practical concern for production settings where warm-start accuracy matters.
Search Results
#45 — Innovative (72)

CarePilot: A Multi-Agent Framework for Long-Horizon Computer Task Automation in Healthcare (8 ▲ )

Akash Ghosh, Tajamul Ashraf, Rishu Kumar Singh, Numan Saeed, Sriparna Saha, Xiuying Chen, Salman Khan · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Mar 2026
Why this rank: CarePilot addresses a genuinely underexplored domain—autonomous GUI agents for clinical software—and contributes CareFlow, a well-grounded benchmark with expert annotation and κ=0.78 agreement across five real healthcare platforms. The actor-critic distillation strategy that removes the Critic at inference while preserving reflection signal is an elegant architectural contribution. The thorough ablation decomposition provides actionable insights. However, task accuracy plateauing at ~50% and collapsing beyond 20 steps reveals fundamental unsolved brittleness, and the coarse action space abstracts away the hardest grounding challenges.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Autonomous GUI agents have made progress on general-purpose desktop, mobile, and web tasks, but long-horizon automation of domain-specific healthcare software—DICOM viewers, medical image annotation tools, EHR systems, and laboratory information systems—remains almost entirely unexplored. Clinical workflows require chaining 10–24 interdependent actions under strict data integrity and compliance constraints, on highly heterogeneous interfaces that evolve with institutional customization. No public benchmark existed to measure agent performance in these settings, making it impossible to assess or improve generalization to clinical software. Methodology: The paper makes two contributions. First, CareFlow: a human-annotated benchmark of 1,100 long-horizon tasks (8–24 steps each) across five healthcare platforms (Orthanc, Weasis, 3D Slicer, OpenEMR, OpenHospital). Tasks were constructed through a four-stage pipeline—expert-designed seed tasks, diversity expansion via controlled substitutions, step-by-step GUI annotation by trained interns, and quality filtering with Cohen's κ = 0.78. The action space has six types: CLICK, SCROLL, ZOOM, TEXT, SEGMENT, COMPLETE. Second, CarePilot: a multi-agent actor–critic framework built on Qwen-VL (7B or 8B). The Actor receives the current screenshot plus tool-grounded perceptual signals (open-vocabulary object detection, zoom/crop, OCR, template/icon matching) and selects the next semantic action conditioned on dual memories. Short-term memory encodes the immediately preceding action and critic feedback; long-term memory maintains a compact trajectory embedding updated via tool-grounded features. The Critic evaluates each proposed action and applies three-level hierarchical reflection—action-level (consecutive state comparison), trajectory-level (recent window), and global-level (entire history)—issuing structured corrective feedback that updates both memories. Training distills Critic feedback into the Actor via supervised fine-tuning on Critic-verified successful trajectories only, so inference requires only the Actor with no Critic overhead. LoRA adapters (rank 2) are trained for 2 epochs on A100 GPUs. Key Results: On the CareFlow test set (315 tasks), CarePilot (Qwen 3 VL-8B) achieves 90.18% step-wise accuracy (SWA) and 51.45% task accuracy (TA), versus the best baseline GPT-5 at 85.22% SWA and 36.19% TA—a 15.26-point TA improvement. The 7B variant reaches 88.05% SWA and 48.90% TA. On the out-of-distribution OpenHospital benchmark (50 tasks), CarePilot (Qwen 3 VL-8B) achieves 79.27% SWA and 38.18% TA versus GPT-5 at 79.70% SWA and 34.80% TA. Ablations confirm tool grounding is the most critical component (removing it drops TA from 48.90% to 9.37%), followed by long-term memory (23.67% without LTM) and short-term memory (30.42% without STM). Among individual tools, template/icon matching (TM) contributes most, OCR second; zoom contributes least. Performance degrades sharply with task length: TA above 64% for <10 steps, falling below 35% for 15–20 steps, and converging to ~27% beyond 20 steps for both model variants. The distilled Actor (~90s/task) outperforms an explicit actor–critic loop at inference (~150s/task) while also being faster. Strengths: - The CareFlow benchmark is a concrete and needed contribution: expert-annotated, covering realistic clinical platforms with domain-expert validation, interface-invariant action labels, and a Cohen's κ = 0.78 inter-annotator agreement—substantially better grounded than synthetic or web-scraped benchmarks. - The reasoning distillation training strategy elegantly removes the Critic at inference, recovering both speed and accuracy versus a live actor–critic loop, while preserving the structured reflection signal. - Ablations are thorough and clearly decompose contributions: individual tools, each memory component, and the Critic itself are all ablated, yielding actionable understanding of the design. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Task accuracy plateaus at ~50% even for the best model, and collapses to ~27% for sequences exceeding 20 steps—exposing a fundamental brittleness in long-horizon reasoning that the paper characterizes but does not solve. - CareFlow covers only five platforms and the action space (six coarse action types, no pixel-level coordinates) abstracts away the hardest grounding challenge: determining exactly where to click or drag. The benchmark measures action-type prediction, not full GUI execution, which limits clinical deployment relevance. - The benchmark was constructed primarily through controlled substitutions of seed tasks rather than independently collected clinical workflows, raising questions about the true diversity of failure modes relative to real-world clinical practice.
Recommended 2603.24157 PDF
#46 — Innovative (72)

Group3D: MLLM-Driven Semantic Grouping for Open-Vocabulary 3D Object Detection (24 ▲ )

Youbin Kim, Jinho Park, Hogun Park, Eunbyung Park · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Group3D introduces a genuinely novel architectural idea: injecting MLLM-driven semantic compatibility constraints directly into the 3D fragment merging stage, addressing a real and previously under-studied failure mode in open-vocabulary 3D detection. The training-free pipeline achieves remarkably large gains (+18.3 mAP25 over Zoo3D on ScanNet20) that surpass even point-cloud-based methods using only RGB inputs, demonstrating the power of semantic reasoning at merge time. The modular design generalizes across datasets and vocabulary scales without task-specific tuning. However, the heavy reliance on a frontier proprietary MLLM (GPT-5.1) limits reproducibility and fair comparison, and the greedy merging algorithm lacks global optimization guarantees.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Open-vocabulary 3D object detection from multi-view RGB images requires fusing 2D detections across views into coherent 3D instances without being constrained to a fixed category taxonomy. Prior pipelines (e.g., Zoo3D) decouple instance construction from semantic labeling: they first merge 3D fragments purely on geometric consistency, then assign open-vocabulary labels post hoc. This geometry-first strategy is fragile when RGB-derived geometry is noisy or view-dependent — a common condition in RGB-only settings — leading to irreversible over-merging of geometrically adjacent but semantically distinct objects, or fragmentation of a single object whose views produce inconsistent category labels. No prior work addresses semantic constraints at merge time for this setting. Methodology: Group3D is a training-free, multi-view open-vocabulary 3D detection pipeline that injects semantic constraints directly into the instance construction step. It operates in three stages. First, a Scene Vocabulary Memory is built by querying an MLLM (GPT-5.1) on each sampled view (K=5 categories per view, 128 frames per scene) and aggregating, deduplicating, and canonicalizing the predicted category names into a compact scene-level vocabulary. In parallel, a 3D Fragment Memory is built by prompting SAM 3 with each vocabulary category to produce category-aware 2D segmentation masks, which are back-projected into 3D using camera poses and depth maps from a feed-forward reconstruction backbone (Depth Anything 3 or VGGT). Each fragment stores its 3D point cloud, category hypothesis, and a composite confidence score (query-level mask match × global category presence). Second, the MLLM partitions the scene vocabulary into Semantic Compatibility Groups encoding plausible cross-view category equivalences (e.g., chair–sofa, desk–table) while explicitly excluding structural, part–whole, and co-occurrence relationships. Third, Group-Gated Fragment Merging proceeds greedily by spatial extent: two fragments are merged only if (a) their categories belong to the same compatibility group and (b) they satisfy voxel-level geometric overlap via IoU ≥ 0.01 or containment ratio ≥ 0.10. The use of containment ratio alongside IoU handles the common case of asymmetric fragment sizes. Final category labels are assigned via confidence-weighted multi-view evidence accumulation using a saturation function to prevent dominance by high-fragment-count labels. The pipeline works in both pose-known (using GT camera poses) and pose-free settings (using estimated poses and depth). Key Results: On ScanNet20 (pose-known, zero-shot), Group3D achieves 51.1 mAP25 / 27.4 mAP50, compared to the prior best multi-view method Zoo3D at 32.8 / 15.5 — a gain of +18.3 / +11.9. Notably, this also surpasses point cloud-based methods such as OV-Uni3DETR (25.3 mAP25) and Zoo3D with point clouds (37.2 mAP25), despite using only RGB inputs. In the harder pose-free, zero-shot setting, Group3D achieves 41.2 mAP25 vs. 27.9 for Zoo3D, a +13.3 gain. On ScanNet60 (pose-known), Group3D scores 29.1 mAP25, up from 23.9 for Zoo3D. On the long-tail ScanNet200 setting, Group3D reaches 17.9 mAP25 (vs. 16.5 for Zoo3D). On ARKitScenes, Group3D achieves 20.5 mAP25 (pose-known) and 18.4 (pose-free), surpassing Zoo3D's 16.1 and 13.0 respectively. Ablations confirm the importance of semantic compatibility grouping: removing category constraints yields 28.2 mAP25; same-category strict matching yields 35.9; full semantic compatibility grouping yields 41.2. Component swaps show the method is robust to backbone choice, with smaller MLLMs (Qwen3-VL-8B) causing only moderate drops (38.5 vs. 41.2 mAP25). Strengths: - Addresses a genuine and previously under-studied failure mode (geometry-driven over-merging under incomplete RGB-based reconstruction) with a well-motivated, targeted fix at the fragment merging stage rather than post hoc correction. - Training-free with respect to 3D supervision: no annotated 3D boxes, no dataset-specific fine-tuning, and clean zero-shot evaluation enforced by selecting reconstruction backbones not trained on the test benchmarks. - The semantic compatibility grouping mechanism is modular and transfers across datasets (ScanNet, ARKitScenes) and vocabulary scales (20 to 200 categories), demonstrating genuine generalization rather than dataset-specific tuning. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The pipeline depends heavily on MLLM quality for both vocabulary generation and compatibility grouping; the paper uses GPT-5.1 (a frontier proprietary model) as the primary component, making fair cost and reproducibility comparisons difficult. Performance degrades with smaller open models (Qwen3-VL-8B: −2.7 mAP25), and the sensitivity to MLLM reasoning quality at scale is not fully characterized. - The greedy, extent-sorted fragment merging algorithm (Algorithm 1) is a linear scan with first-match semantics, which may be sensitive to fragment ordering and does not globally optimize the clustering objective. The low geometric overlap thresholds (τ_iou=0.01, τ_cont=0.10) suggest the method may be permissive to false merges between spatially proximate but distinct objects in the same compatibility group (e.g., two different chairs), a failure mode not explicitly analyzed.
Recommended 2603.21944 PDF
#47 — Innovative (72)

SciDesignBench: Benchmarking and Improving Language Models for Scientific Inverse Design

David van Dijk, Ivan Vrkic · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Introduces a well-structured benchmark that cleanly separates zero-shot design, feedback utilization, and constrained optimization — capabilities that existing benchmarks conflate — and the empirical finding that leaderboards reshuffle across these settings is substantive. The RLSF training recipe is practical but demonstrated on only three domains with one model, and the absence of comparisons against classical domain-specific solvers leaves the core efficiency claim unsubstantiated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing scientific LLM benchmarks test knowledge retrieval, forward reasoning, and mathematical problem-solving, but not whether a model can actually construct a design that satisfies quantitative scientific targets. Scientific inverse design — given a desired outcome, find inputs that achieve it under a known forward model — is a harder problem than forward prediction: the inverse map is often ill-posed, many-to-one, and requires simultaneous satisfaction of multiple quantitative constraints. No benchmark systematically measures whether frontier LLMs can solve inverse problems across diverse scientific domains, nor whether long-horizon simulator feedback improves performance differently than single-shot capability, nor whether constrained design modification (optimization) is the same skill as unconstrained de novo design. Methodology: The paper introduces SCIDESIGNBENCH, a benchmark of 520 simulator-grounded tasks across 14 scientific domains (drug design, biology, physics, engineering, chemical engineering), each with a frozen forward oracle. Every task is specified as a natural-language goal; the model emits a structured JSON design that is then parsed, validated, executed by the oracle, and scored. Tasks span four difficulty levels (L1–L4) defined by number of simultaneous targets and tolerance tightness, and two task types: de novo design (from scratch) and optimization (modify a provided seed). Five evaluation settings are defined: 1-turn de novo, 5-turn feedback, 20-turn long-horizon feedback, 1-turn optimization, and 20-turn long-horizon optimization. Seven frontier LLMs are evaluated. The authors then introduce RLSF (Reinforcement Learning from Simulator Feedback): a two-stage training recipe applied to Qwen3-8B using QLoRA, consisting of (1) supervised fine-tuning on oracle-verified (goal, design) pairs to establish format compliance, and (2) GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) using forward simulator scores as reward signals, with curriculum scheduling that progressively introduces harder difficulty levels. Group size K=32 was necessary for sufficient diversity in GRPO gradient signal; cosine LR decay caused training collapse and was replaced with a constant rate of 1e-5. RLSF case studies are demonstrated on ADMET optimization, PK/PD dosing design, and molecular docking (AutoDock Vina). Key Results: On the 10-domain shared-core zero-shot subset, the best frontier model (Claude Sonnet 4.5) achieves only 29.0% success despite high parse rates, confirming that format compliance does not imply scientific correctness — the gap is predominantly scientific reasoning failure, not syntactic failure. Simulator feedback roughly doubles performance: 5-turn feedback raises most models to 57–67% shared-core success. Long-horizon capability is orthogonal to one-turn competence: Sonnet 4.5 leads at 1 turn (29.0%) but Opus 4.6 leads at 20 turns (76.0%), with gains continuing beyond 5 turns only for some models. Providing a seed design for optimization reshuffles the leaderboard again (Opus 4.6 leads 1-turn optimization), and across all seven models, providing a starting design on average degrades aggregate 20-turn performance relative to unconstrained de novo search (e.g., −5.0 pp for Opus 4.6, −10.6 pp for GPT-4o), confirming that constrained modification is a distinct and harder capability. RLSF training of Qwen3-8B produces gains of +11 pp on ADMET optimization (30%→41%), +12 pp on PK/PD (24%→36% de novo, 32%→47% optimization), and +17 pp on docking (42%→59%), with ADMET narrowly exceeding the strongest frontier model on that domain (40%). Strengths: - The benchmark cleanly separates several distinct capabilities — zero-shot design, feedback utilization, and constrained optimization — that existing benchmarks collapse into a single score, and the empirical finding that leaderboards shift across these settings is substantive and actionable. - The oracle fidelity spectrum (mathematically exact through empirical/data-derived) is well-motivated and covers qualitatively different failure modes, including reward hacking risks for empirical oracles. - The RLSF training interface is practically attractive: it reuses the same oracle for both evaluation and training, enabling amortization across a goal distribution without per-goal test-time search, and the curriculum GRPO approach with anti-memorization safeguards (seed separation, goal jitter, diversity bonus) is carefully implemented. Weaknesses / Limitations: - RLSF training claims are demonstrated on only three domains with a single 8B model; it is explicitly not shown to generalize across all 14 domains, and the paper does not compare RLSF against classical per-domain solvers (Bayesian optimization, REINVENT, OptKnock, GRAPE) on any domain, leaving the efficiency advantage of amortized training unquantified against the strongest domain-specific baselines. - The benchmark uses simplified simulators (ODE-based PK/PD, CALPHAD-inspired alloy mixing rules, linear GRN for perturbation) rather than high-fidelity oracles, and the authors acknowledge results may not transfer; the docking case uses real AutoDock Vina but only five protein targets, which is a narrow evaluation of structure-based drug design. - With only K=3 attempts per task and 20 tasks per domain per difficulty level, statistical power is limited — standard errors on per-domain success rates are typically ±7–11 pp — making fine-grained comparisons between close models unreliable, and all models are evaluated at a fixed temperature (0.7) rather than per-model optimal settings.
Recommended 2603.12724 PDF
#48 — Innovative (72)

MinerU-Diffusion: Rethinking Document OCR as Inverse Rendering via Diffusion Decoding (110 ▲ )

Hejun Dong, Junbo Niu, Bin Wang, Weijun Zeng, Wentao Zhang, Conghui He · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Mar 2026
Why this rank: MinerU-Diffusion makes a genuinely paradigm-questioning contribution by reframing document OCR as inverse rendering via masked diffusion rather than autoregressive decoding, backed by a principled argument that OCR's near-deterministic image-to-text mapping better suits conditional independence than causal generation. The block-wise hybrid factorization and continuous confidence-threshold knob are practical innovations, and the Semantic Shuffle benchmark validates the core robustness claim. However, the diffusion approach still trails smaller AR baselines in fully automatic accuracy, and layout detection—the dominant error source—is not addressed.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern document OCR systems built on vision-language models (VLMs) rely on autoregressive (AR) left-to-right decoding, which introduces two fundamental problems: (1) sequential latency that scales linearly with output length, making long-document parsing slow, and (2) strong dependence on linguistic priors that causes semantic hallucinations and cumulative error propagation when visual evidence is ambiguous or semantic structure is disrupted. The authors argue that left-to-right causal generation is merely an artifact of sequence serialization, not an intrinsic requirement of OCR—since document text has a near-deterministic mapping to image content, the conditional independence assumption of masked diffusion is far more natural here than in open-ended language generation. Methodology: MinerU-Diffusion reframes document OCR as an inverse rendering problem solved via discrete masked diffusion. The core architecture is a block-wise diffusion vision-language model (dVLM) built on SDAR-1.7B-Chat-b32 (2.5B total parameters). The output token sequence is partitioned into contiguous blocks; across blocks, a coarse autoregressive structure is preserved (causal attention), while within each block, full bidirectional diffusion denoising operates in parallel. This hybrid factorization reduces attention complexity from O(L²) to O(BL'²), enables KV-cache reuse across blocks, and prevents long-range alignment drift. A dynamic confidence threshold controls decoding parallelism at inference: tokens whose predicted probability exceeds the threshold are confirmed and frozen, while uncertain tokens continue denoising—yielding a continuously tunable accuracy-efficiency tradeoff. Training uses a two-stage curriculum: Stage I trains on a large broad dataset (Dbase, ~7.5M samples from MinerU2.5 data) to establish foundational structure understanding; Stage II identifies hard samples via model uncertainty (C(x) < τ), refines their labels through AI-assisted human annotation, then fine-tunes with adaptive sample weighting that emphasizes low-confidence boundary cases. This addresses diffusion's inherent lower data utilization efficiency relative to AR models. Key Results: On OmniDocBench v1.5 without ground-truth layout (fully automatic setting), MinerU-Diffusion achieves an Overall score of 88.94, outperforming most sub-3B AR models but trailing MinerU2.5 (90.67) and PaddleOCR-VL (92.56). With oracle layout provided, it reaches 93.37 Overall, essentially matching MinerU2.5 (93.44) and PaddleOCR-VL (93.91) at competitive accuracy. In throughput at confidence threshold 0.95, it achieves ~109 TPS vs. MinerU2.5's ~51 TPS—a 2.1× speedup with 99.9% relative accuracy—and up to 3.26× speedup at 90%+ relative accuracy. On element-specific tasks, it achieves 81.18/88.66 TEDS/TEDS-S on OCRBench v2 tables and 91.6/91.6/92.0/96.8 CPE/HWE/SCE/SPE on UniMER-Test formulas, competitive with AR models in the same parameter range. On the Semantic Shuffle benchmark (where semantic token order is deliberately disrupted), MinerU-Diffusion degrades substantially less than AR baselines, confirming reduced reliance on linguistic priors. Strengths: - Theoretically principled reformulation: the near-deterministic image-to-text mapping in OCR genuinely justifies the conditional independence assumption of masked diffusion, making this not just an efficiency hack but a structurally motivated design choice. - Practically useful accuracy-throughput knob: the confidence threshold provides a continuous, predictable tradeoff with no retraining required, enabling deployment-time adaptation to latency vs. accuracy constraints. - Demonstrated robustness advantage on the Semantic Shuffle benchmark validates the core claim that AR systems are fragile to linguistic prior disruption—a real failure mode in practice. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Despite the 2.5B parameter count (roughly 2–2.8× larger than MinerU2.5 at 1.2B and PaddleOCR-VL at 0.9B), MinerU-Diffusion still trails these AR baselines in the fully automatic setting (88.94 vs. 90.67/92.56), suggesting the diffusion paradigm has not yet closed the accuracy gap with state-of-the-art AR systems at matched or smaller scale. - Layout detection remains the dominant bottleneck (the gap between w/ and w/o GT Layout settings is 4–5 Overall points), and the paper does not substantially advance layout prediction quality—meaning the largest practical source of error is not addressed by the diffusion decoding contribution. - The evaluation is restricted to Chinese and English; the authors explicitly note no low-resource language evaluation was conducted, limiting claims about generality. - The hard-sample curriculum requires an AI-assisted human annotation pipeline (Stage II), adding a labeling cost that may be non-trivial to replicate outside the authors' infrastructure.
Recommended 2603.22458 PDF
#49 — Innovative (72)

How Well Does Generative Recommendation Generalize?

Yijie Ding, Zitian Guo, Jiacheng Li, Letian Peng, Shuai Shao, Wei Shao, Xiaoqiang Luo, Luke Simon, Jingbo Shang, Julian McAuley, Yupeng Hou · arxiv.org · Julian McAuley - new articles
Why this rank: This paper introduces an elegant and principled decomposition of test instances into memorization vs. generalization categories, then convincingly explains why generative recommendation models generalize — their item-level generalization collapses to token-level prefix memorization. The two-level analysis bridging empirical observation to causal mechanism is a meaningful methodological contribution. However, the analysis is restricted to only two models (TIGER and SASRec), and the practical ensemble gains are modest.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Generative recommendation (GR) models — which tokenize items as sequences of sub-item semantic ID tokens — are widely believed to outperform conventional item ID-based models (e.g., SASRec) because they generalize better. However, this claim has only been validated through overall benchmark comparisons, not through rigorous decomposition of what types of test instances each paradigm handles well. The paper addresses this gap by asking: do GR models actually generalize better, and if so, why? Methodology: The authors propose a principled framework to categorize each test instance in sequential recommendation by the primary capability required for a correct prediction: memorization (the direct 1-hop item transition from the last history item to the target was observed during training) or generalization (the transition was not observed, but can be inferred or composed from seen transitions). Generalization is further subdivided into: Symmetry (reverse transition seen), Transitivity (bridging via intermediate item), 2nd-Order Symmetry (common cause/effect/reverse path), and Substitutability (multi-hop skip transitions). Instances satisfying none of these up to hop depth 4 are labeled uncategorized. Using this taxonomy, they partition test sets from seven real-world datasets and separately evaluate TIGER (semantic ID-based GR) and SASRec (item ID-based). To explain the mechanism, they introduce prefix n-gram memorization at the token level — checking whether the n-gram prefix of the source and target items in a transition both appear in training data, even if the exact items differ. They then analyze how item-level generalization collapses to token-level memorization for GR models. Finally, they propose a memorization-aware adaptive ensemble using maximum softmax probability (MSP) of the ID-based model as a proxy for memorization likelihood, dynamically weighting both models per instance via a sigmoid-transformed confidence score. Key Results: Across seven datasets (Sports, Beauty, Science, Music, Office, Steam, Yelp), TIGER consistently underperforms SASRec on memorization subsets (e.g., −43.6% on Yelp, −41.2% on Sports) while consistently outperforming on generalization subsets (e.g., +58.8% on Office, +56.7% on Beauty). Generalization instances dominate the test data (81–93%), meaning the majority of test cases require generalization rather than memorization. At the token level, >99% of item-level generalization instances admit at least 1-gram prefix memorization, explaining why GR models generalize well — they are effectively performing token-level memorization. Smaller codebook sizes (denser tokenization) increase token memorization ratio and improve generalization by +10.24% relative but degrade item memorization by −7.62% relative, directly validating the mechanism. The adaptive ensemble (MSP-based) consistently outperforms both individual models and fixed-weight ensembles on all seven datasets, with larger gains where the model crossover effect is stronger (e.g., NDCG@10 of 0.0476 vs. 0.0436/0.0383 for SASRec/TIGER on Beauty; Recall@10 of 0.0841 vs. 0.0566/0.0542). Strengths: - The memorization/generalization decomposition is elegant, data-driven, and avoids expensive counterfactual retraining — making it scalable to large recommendation datasets while still being theoretically grounded. - The two-level analysis (item-level taxonomy → token-level mechanism) convincingly explains why the performance gap exists, not just that it exists, bridging empirical observation with causal mechanism. - The controlled codebook ablation (Section 4.4) directly validates the hypothesized causal direction — more prefix sharing drives better generalization at the cost of memorization — providing strong experimental evidence beyond correlational analysis. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The analysis is limited to two specific models (TIGER and SASRec), and the generalizability of findings to other GR architectures (e.g., LLM-based recommenders, contrastive tokenization methods) is assumed but not demonstrated. - The memorization-aware adaptive ensemble relies on tuning hyperparameters (q, τ) on a validation set, and the gains over the fixed-weight ensemble are modest (often <1% absolute), raising questions about practical significance relative to deployment complexity.
Recommended 2603.19809 PDF
#50 — Innovative (72)

Learning to Generate Structured Meshes with In-Context: Toward Generalization in Mesh Generation

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: ICL-Mesh is the first work to apply in-context learning to structured mesh generation, enabling zero-shot and few-shot generalization to unseen geometries without parameter updates — a qualitatively new capability in this domain. The creative transfer of the ICL paradigm from language models to computational mesh generation, combined with the simplified cross-attention decoder enabling arbitrary-resolution inference, represents genuine methodological originality. However, the evaluation is limited to small 2D grids and modest geometry diversity, and the comparison against geometry-specific baselines conflates generalization advantage with quality assessment, leaving the practical tradeoff unclear.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Structured mesh generation—discretizing geometric domains into computational grids for numerical simulations (CFD, electromagnetics, aerospace)—has long required geometry-specific solutions. Existing neural approaches (PDE-based methods like MGNet, data-driven methods like MeshONet) each learn a dedicated mapping per geometry, failing to share knowledge across geometries and requiring costly retraining or recomputation whenever a new geometry arises. This paper addresses the fundamental lack of cross-geometry generalization by asking: can a single model adapt to unseen geometries at inference time using only a few examples, without any parameter updates? Methodology: ICL-Mesh frames structured mesh generation as a meta-learning problem inspired by in-context learning (ICL) in large language models. Each geometry-to-mesh mapping is treated as a distinct "task," and a single Transformer-based model is trained across many tasks to extract shared structural patterns. At inference, the model receives a small context set of geometry-mesh pairs (prompts) plus a query geometry, and directly predicts the mesh without gradient updates. The architecture consists of: (1) a Hybrid Feature Encoder that encodes geometry via uniform sampling and projection, and mesh data via CNN+flatten, concatenated with signed one-hot index encodings to disambiguate geometry/mesh pairs; (2) a Transformer encoder that processes the prompt into a task embedding; and (3) a simplified Transformer decoder (cross-attention only, no self-attention) that queries the embedding at arbitrary grid points to produce the output mesh. Training uses MSE loss with randomly sampled context sets of variable size (1 to M examples), forcing the model to operate in both few-shot and zero-shot regimes. Key Results: ICL-Mesh is evaluated on 2D datasets across three test cases against TFI (transfinite interpolation), PDE-based methods, MGNet, KANMeshNet, and MeshONet—all of which are geometry-specific and retrained per case. ICL-Mesh achieves valid meshes across all three cases while TFI, MGNet, and KANMeshNet produce invalid meshes on multiple cases. On Case-1, ICL-Mesh achieves best Max Included Angle (119.896°) and Min Included Angle (60.925°) among valid methods, surpassing all baselines. On Cases 2 and 3, it is competitive with MeshONet (e.g., Case-2: 100.742° vs. 100.002° for MeshONet). The model generalizes across resolutions—trained at 30×30 but deployed at 10×10, 60×60, and 120×120—with CFD simulation error (icoFOAM/OpenFOAM) decreasing monotonically with resolution. The model is extended to 3D with only encoder modifications. Robustness experiments across 7 prompt orderings (in-distribution and out-of-distribution) show consistent MSE (~1.5×10⁻⁵), and few-shot analysis confirms performance improves monotonically from 0 to 4 examples. Strengths: - First application of in-context learning to structured mesh generation, enabling zero-shot and few-shot generalization to unseen geometries without any parameter updates—a qualitatively different capability from all prior work. - Architecture design is well-motivated: the simplified decoder (cross-attention only) allows mesh prediction at arbitrary query locations, enabling cross-resolution transfer without retraining. - Evaluation is comprehensive: mesh validity, geometric quality metrics, CFD simulation accuracy, 3D extension, prompt order robustness, and few-shot scaling are all tested. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The comparison is inherently unfair: baselines are geometry-specific models retrained per test case (their upper bound), while ICL-Mesh is a single generalizable model. This conflates the generalization advantage with mesh quality comparison—ICL-Mesh being merely "competitive" may actually represent a degradation in peak quality for the same computational budget. - Experiments are constrained to relatively small 2D grids (up to 120×120) and limited 3D qualitative results with no quantitative 3D metrics; scalability to high-resolution, industrially relevant meshes remains undemonstrated. - The dataset construction (18–20 examples per geometry using Pointwise software) is not publicly released and the diversity of geometries tested is modest; generalization to highly irregular or topologically complex geometries (e.g., multi-block, non-simply-connected domains) is not assessed.
Search Results
#51 — Innovative (71)

Inducing Epistemological Humility in Large Language Models: A Targeted SFT Approach to Reducing Hallucination

Cem Uluoglakci, Tugba Taskaya Temizel · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 71 for the clean conceptual insight of decoupling uncertainty recognition from factual content by training on synthetic non-existent terms, sidestepping the need for ground-truth correctness labels or RL pipelines. The experimental rigor is exceptional — 800 controlled LoRA runs with statistical testing — and the mechanistic analysis revealing an orthogonal uncertainty vector is a genuine scientific contribution. However, the dramatic architecture dependence (Gemma +25% vs. Llama +1%) undermines generalizability claims, and the shared generative process between training data and evaluation benchmark raises legitimate concerns about whether gains reflect true meta-cognitive transfer or distribution-specific pattern matching.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs hallucinate partly because standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) implicitly rewards always answering, discouraging admission of ignorance. Prior abstention methods couple the skill of saying "I don't know" to specific factual content—training on questions the model historically answered incorrectly, or using ground-truth labels to shape rewards. This coupling limits generalization: models learn what they don't know rather than how to recognize when they don't know. The paper targets a deeper meta-cognitive gap: teaching models a transferable heuristic for uncertainty recognition that generalizes beyond the training distribution. Methodology: The core insight is to decouple uncertainty recognition from factual content by training exclusively on questions about "hypothetical" terms—synthetic concepts validated as absent from Google, Bing, Brave, and the Dolma pretraining corpus. Because these terms cannot be retrieved from parametric memory, the model must learn the abstract pattern of detecting knowledge absence. This produces HypoTermInstruct, an SFT dataset of 31,487 question-answer pairs across 20 topics, where golden answers (generated by Llama3.1-405B, R1-671B, and GPT-4o in round-robin fashion) acknowledge the non-existent term while providing accurate information about the valid co-occurring term. The experimental design is rigorous: 800 controlled LoRA SFT runs across Llama3.1-8B and Gemma3-4B (base and instruct variants), with 100 random hyperparameter configurations per model-checkpoint pair. The experimental condition replaces 12.9% of generic instruction data with HypoTermInstruct while holding total sample count fixed. Evaluation uses six metrics: HypoTerm Score, FactScore, MMLU, IFEval (Instruct/Prompt), and AILuminate safety. Mechanistic analysis employs Logit Lens, linear probing of residual stream activations, and SVD of LoRA adapter weights. Key Results: HypoTermInstruct consistently and significantly improves hallucination metrics across all four model-checkpoint combinations. Median HypoTerm Score gains range from +0.19% (Llama3.1-8B Base) to +25.91% (Gemma3-4B Instruct), with Gemma3-4B showing dramatically larger gains. FactScore improves modestly by +0.39% to +0.86%. MMLU decreases are small (−0.26% to −0.35%) and statistically significant only for select checkpoints. Instruction-following drops significantly for Llama3.1-8B Base but not for Gemma. Safety scores degrade significantly for base checkpoints (up to −2.29%) but show non-significant changes for instruction-tuned variants. Mechanistic analysis reveals that fine-tuning creates an orthogonal "uncertainty vector" in residual streams (cosine similarity |cos| < 0.02 with both safety and knowledge vectors), explaining why hallucination reduction does not erase factual knowledge. The two architectures implement uncertainty via distinct strategies: Gemma3-4B makes a "surgical" intervention concentrated in the output projector (o_proj), while Llama3.1-8B undergoes "systemic" rotation across all modules, consuming more adapter capacity and causing more interference with instruction-following. Strengths: - Rigorous experimental design with 800 paired runs and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests, providing statistically reliable conclusions rather than cherry-picked examples - The decoupling insight is clean and well-motivated: training on non-existent terms sidesteps the need for ground-truth correctness labels or RL pipelines, making the approach practically accessible - Mechanistic interpretability analysis goes beyond behavioral metrics to explain why trade-offs occur (late-stage intervention, LoRA capacity competition), offering genuine scientific insight rather than empirical black-box reporting Weaknesses / Limitations: - The gains are highly architecture-dependent (Gemma +25% vs. Llama +1%), and the paper does not resolve whether this reflects fundamental architectural differences or a limitation of the training methodology for Llama-style models—leaving practitioners uncertain about generalizability - Safety degradation in base models is dismissed as expected and recoverable during downstream alignment, but this is asserted rather than demonstrated; no experiment shows that subsequent RLHF or DPO restores safety without undoing the hallucination gains - The benchmark (HypoTermQA-Enhanced) and training data share the same generative process and term vocabulary (20 topics, same hypothetical terms), raising questions about whether improvements reflect genuine meta-cognitive transfer or sophisticated pattern-matching to the synthetic distribution
Recommended 2603.17504 PDF
#52 — Innovative (71)

Dynamic Sparse Attention: Access Patterns and Architecture

Noam Levy · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
