Why this rank: This paper fills a concrete and underexplored gap by rigorously characterizing dynamic sparse attention memory access patterns across four model scales, producing actionable numerical targets for hardware designers. The finding that over 50% of top-k entries are new each step and PagedAttention achieves only 35% page utilization directly challenges assumptions underlying current serving infrastructure. The LL cache reservation proposal is practical though only analytically validated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Dynamic sparse attention (DSA) limits per-token attention to a top-k subset of KV cache entries, making attention O(k) in memory bandwidth rather than O(T). This is attractive for long-context LLM inference, where standard attention is HBM bandwidth-bound (Table 1 shows 93–99% HBM BW utilization across LLaMA 70B, Llama 4 Maverick, GPT-OSS 120B, and QWEN3-480B at 100 tokens/s/user). However, DSA's token-dependent, irregular gather pattern over KV memory is poorly matched to modern GPU memory hierarchy assumptions—prefetchers expect spatial locality, PagedAttention assumes high sequential utilization, and L2 cache reuse presupposes temporal stability of the working set. Prior work on DSA (e.g., DeepSeek-V3.2) focuses on the algorithmic side without characterizing the resulting hardware inefficiencies. This paper closes that gap. Methodology: The authors implement a "lightning indexer" following the DeepSeek DSA methodology and train it into four frozen LLaMA 3 backbones (1B, 3B, 8B, 70B) via knowledge distillation. The indexer scores each context token using a lightweight 4-head, d=64 projection and selects a top-k subset; the frozen backbone's attention is monkey-patched to use only those k KV entries. Training uses SlimPajama with a compound loss (KL on logits, KL on attention scores, L1 sparsity, and entropy regularization) for 100k steps on an H200. Access patterns are then instrumented during autoregressive decoding across 50 synthetic evaluation sequences (500–1500 token contexts, 200 generated tokens, k∈{64, 128, 256}), collecting per-layer top-k indices. Four metrics are quantified: working set size over N=50-token chunks, KV persistence (how long an entry stays in top-k), lookback distance, step-to-step new lookup fraction, and inter-layer index overlap. GPU profiling via NVIDIA NCU on H200 validates the hardware impact. The authors then build a roofline architectural model of a proposed KV-granular LRU reservation in the L2/LL cache. Key Results: Profiling shows DSA reduces SM utilization from 23.5% to 10.9% and HBM bandwidth utilization from 36% to 7.3% relative to dense attention—a severe underutilization caused by irregular gather latency rather than compute saturation. The access pattern analysis reveals: (1) working set grows to ~5× top-k (P95: 7.2×) over 50-token chunks—for k=128, that is 922 KV entries per layer per tenant; (2) KV entry persistence has mean <2 steps with exponential falloff, making the previous step a poor predictor of the next; (3) over 50% of top-k entries are new at each step; (4) inter-layer index overlap is below 50%, making layer-to-layer prediction also weak; (5) average KV page utilization under PagedAttention is only 35%, causing severe internal fragmentation. The proposed fix—reserving 5–20 MB of LL cache with token-granularity LRU eviction—reduces decode slowdown from 1.87× (baseline) to 1.15× (20 MB reserved) in the roofline model for LLaMA 3.1-70B at batch size 8. Strengths: - Fills a concrete and underexplored gap: rigorous empirical characterization of DSA's memory access patterns across four model scales, yielding actionable numerical targets (working set size, persistence, new lookup rate) for hardware designers. - Hardware proposal (LL cache reservation with token-level LRU) is practical and does not increase die area—it reallocates existing L2 capacity, with eviction cost (10–20 cycles) well below HBM miss penalty (~200 ns). - The roofline model cleanly quantifies the expected benefit across reservation sizes, giving a concrete design-space curve rather than qualitative speculation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The roofline model for the proposed LL cache architecture is analytical, not measured on real or cycle-accurate simulated hardware; actual speedups may differ due to LL cache contention from other traffic, multi-tenant interference, and implementation overhead of fully-associative LRU at scale. - The DSA indexer is trained via knowledge distillation into frozen backbones that were not pretrained with DSA, diverging from the full DeepSeek approach (which integrates MLA and pretrains end-to-end); model quality degradation from this approximation is evaluated with a judge LLM but not benchmarked on standard tasks, making it unclear whether the access pattern statistics faithfully reflect a production-quality DSA model. - Evaluation is limited to sequences up to 1500 tokens with k≤256; behavior at the 64k+ context lengths relevant to modern long-context serving (shown in Table 1) is not measured, and lookback/working-set distributions may shift substantially at those scales.
Search Results 2603.13430 PDF
#53 — Innovative (71)

Trimming the Fat: Redundancy-Aware Acceleration Framework for DGNNs

ojs.aaai.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: TrimDG presents a principled decomposition of redundancy in dynamic GNNs into static and runtime types, grounded in PPR theory and the information bottleneck principle. The Temporal Personalized PageRank metric with formal connections to DGNN message passing is a meaningful theoretical contribution. The 83% average runtime reduction across seven diverse DGNN backbones without performance degradation — and the ability to handle DGraph at scale where competitors OOM — demonstrates genuine practical impact. However, the individual components (temporal decay, edge sampling, entropy-based selection, caching) draw heavily from established techniques recombined for the DGNN setting.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Dynamic Graph Neural Networks (DGNNs) face severe scalability challenges when applied to industrial-scale temporal graphs with millions of nodes and billions of timestamped edges. Two core inefficiencies drive this: the neighbor explosion problem (unbounded growth of a node's temporal neighborhood) and repeated reprocessing of full historical event traces per node. Prior static GNN acceleration techniques (DropEdge, GraphSAINT) ignore temporal ordering and introduce temporal biases when applied naively to dynamic graphs, while existing DGNN-specific methods overlook redundancy as a first-class concern. Methodology: TrimDG is a modular, model-agnostic framework that attacks redundancy at two stages. For static redundancy (superfluous information baked into the graph data), it introduces Temporal Personalized PageRank (TPP), a novel node influence metric that extends PPR with exponential temporal decay to score node importance over time-respecting paths; nodes below a pruning ratio β are discarded. It also applies temporal binning, which merges high-frequency events between the same node pair within a time window T_merge, with an adaptive variant that sets T_merge per node pair based on inter-event interval statistics. For runtime redundancy (information that becomes uninformative relative to model learning needs during training), TrimDG introduces an adaptive edge sampler trained via the Graph Information Bottleneck principle: a learned MLP assigns Bernoulli inclusion probabilities to candidate edges, optimized jointly with a contrastive loss that treats original/sampled subgraph pairs as positives and cross-node pairs as negatives, using Gumbel-softmax reparameterization for differentiability. Sampling frequency is further reduced via (1) an entropy-based batch selector that retains only the top-μ% most uncertain training nodes per mini-batch, and (2) an LRU sampling cache with a bypass probability γ to balance reuse against staleness. Theorem 4.1 formally connects TPP to influence propagation in DGNN message passing via a closed-form expression involving a time-decayed adjacency matrix. Key Results: TrimDG is evaluated on six datasets (Wikipedia, Reddit, MOOC, BitAlpha, BitOtc, DGraph) across seven DGNN backbones (JODIE, DyRep, TGAT, TGN, TCL, GraphMixer, DyGFormer), compared against DropEdge, STEP, and TASER. It achieves an average runtime reduction of 83.49% while maintaining competitive AUC. On Wikipedia with TGAT, TrimDG reduces training time from ~10,393s to ~411s (~25×) while improving AUC from 79.10% to 81.90%. On the large-scale DGraph dataset, only TrimDG and DropEdge avoid OOM/non-convergence across backbones, demonstrating scalability advantage. TrimDG ranks 1st in runtime across all seven backbones in average rank. It also achieves a 2.66% average AUC improvement on Wikipedia, suggesting that redundancy removal acts as implicit regularization. Ablation studies confirm all four components contribute: removing static redundancy removal (TrimDG-SR) costs ~33% of sampling speedup; removing runtime redundancy removal (TrimDG-RR) is comparably costly. Strengths: - Principled decomposition of redundancy into static vs. runtime types, each addressed with complementary methods grounded in information theory (IB principle) and graph theory (PPR), rather than purely heuristic approaches. - Strong backbone generality: evaluated across seven architecturally diverse DGNNs (RNN, attention, transformer, MLP-mixer paradigms) on six datasets from social, financial, and interaction domains, with consistent speedups and no performance collapse. - Industrial relevance validated: the only method to handle DGraph (3.7M nodes, 4.3M edges) without OOM failures across multiple backbones, directly addressing the stated motivation of Alipay-scale deployments. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The adaptive sampling component adds a learned MLP on top of each backbone, introducing additional parameters and a non-trivial contrastive loss; for architectures already lacking neighbor sampling (e.g., JODIE), the speedup is weaker and the overhead less justified. - Hyperparameter sensitivity is non-trivial: β (pruning ratio), μ (batch selection), γ (cache bypass), and T_merge all interact, and the paper reports a single fixed configuration across datasets with limited analysis of cross-dataset sensitivity or automated tuning. - Evaluation is limited to node classification; dynamic link prediction results are deferred to the appendix, and it is unclear whether the gains hold equally for that more commonly benchmarked task in the DGNN literature.
Citations
#54 — Innovative (71)

Can Activation Steering Generalize Across Languages? A Study on Syllogistic Reasoning in Language Models

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper earns 71 for being the first systematic study of activation steering transfer across languages for formal reasoning, introducing two principled metrics (ACE Ratio, Steering Efficiency) that cleanly decouple failure modes. The mechanistic failure analysis — distinguishing Mistral's non-linear overfitting from Llama's fundamentally unlearnable abstract-concrete mapping via Ridge-AST ablation — is a genuinely informative methodological contribution. However, the core intervention (centroid-based shifting) is geometrically simple, and the cross-lingual bottleneck (English-only probe) remains unresolved within the study.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs exhibit a well-documented "content effect" in syllogistic reasoning: they conflate semantic plausibility with formal logical validity, frequently accepting plausible-but-invalid conclusions and rejecting implausible-but-valid ones. This bias undermines reliable deductive reasoning, and is likely amplified in multilingual settings where both linguistic variability and differing world-knowledge distributions compound the interference. Prior interventions (prompting, chain-of-thought, fine-tuning) only partially mitigate this problem. Activation steering — modifying internal representations at inference time — has shown promise in English but has never been evaluated across languages. The paper asks whether English-trained steering interventions transfer to multilingual settings. Methodology: The authors construct a multilingual syllogistic reasoning benchmark covering 10 languages (English plus German, Spanish, French, Italian, Russian, Chinese, Swahili, Bengali, Telugu), translated via GPT-4 with round-trip back-translation quality control, spanning 24 logical schemes instantiated with WordNet taxonomic relations. Each instance is annotated for both formal validity and conclusion plausibility. Two families of activation steering are proposed, both gated by a linear probe (logistic regression) trained on English activations to predict validity class: (1) Additive / CEN-AST — shifts activations toward the class-specific centroid computed from correctly classified discovery-set examples; (2) Blending strategies — interpolate original activations toward a learned "content-agnostic" target activation derived by training regressors to map concrete syllogism activations to their abstract (variable-substituted) counterparts. Two blending architectures are explored: FFN-AST (two-headed MLP predicting direction and magnitude separately) and RRN-AST (rotational-residual network that learns an explicit rotation + translation in a PCA-reduced latent space). Interventions target contiguous layer blocks in the final third of each model. Six open-source models (Qwen-2.5-7B, Gemma-2-9B, Mistral-7B, Llama-3.1-8B, EuroLLM-9B, Velvet-2B) are evaluated. The primary metric is the ACE Ratio (accuracy / content effect), rewarding high accuracy while penalizing reliance on plausibility heuristics. A novel Steering Efficiency (η) metric — accuracy conditioned on correct probe gating — decouples intrinsic strategy quality from probe accuracy. Key Results: CEN-AST is the most robust strategy across all models and languages. For Qwen-2.5-7B it raises multilingual accuracy from 65.3% to 83.4% (+28%) and the ACE Ratio by +562%. For Llama-3.1-8B, CEN-AST achieves +36% multilingual accuracy gain and +687% ACE Ratio improvement. Blending strategies are competitive on models with well-structured internal geometry (Qwen, Gemma) — FFN-AST reaches +505% and +596% ACE Ratio gains respectively — but collapse on Mistral and Llama. Diagnostic analysis reveals the cause is model-specific: for Mistral, the content-to-abstract activation mapping is approximately linear (Ridge-AST achieves 76% multilingual efficiency vs. FFN/RRN at 48%), so non-linear blending overfits language-specific non-linearities. For Llama, the mapping is fundamentally unlearnable even linearly (Ridge-AST: 55% English, 48% multilingual), suggesting its representation space lacks a geometrically stable separation between concrete and abstract syllogisms. CEN-AST achieves near-perfect steering efficiency (η ≈ 97–100%) across all models and languages when the probe gates correctly, confirming that valid/invalid representations are cross-lingually consistent as simple geometric clusters. Perplexity on held-out Wikipedia text increases only marginally for CEN-AST (+1.8–2.4%) and modestly for blending strategies (+7–36%), confirming that fluency is largely preserved. Paraphrase robustness tests show ACE Ratios remain stable across surface-form variation, indicating the steering captures logical structure rather than lexical patterns. Strengths: - First systematic multilingual evaluation of activation steering for formal reasoning, with a well-controlled benchmark spanning 10 typologically diverse languages including low-resource ones. - The ACE Ratio and Steering Efficiency metrics are principled contributions: ACE jointly rewards accuracy and content-invariance, while η isolates strategy quality from probe limitations, enabling clean failure diagnosis. - The failure analysis is the paper's most valuable scientific contribution — distinguishing Mistral's generalization failure (non-linear overfitting) from Llama's learnability failure (no stable abstract-concrete mapping) via Ridge-AST ablation is methodologically rigorous and informative for the mechanistic interpretability community. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All experiments are confined to 7–9B parameter models due to hardware constraints; it is unknown whether blending strategy failures on Mistral/Llama are architectural or scale-dependent, a critical gap given that larger models often develop more structured representations. - The probe is trained exclusively on English, and its multilingual transfer accuracy (80–92%) is a hard ceiling on end-to-end performance. The paper acknowledges this but treats it as future work rather than addressing it, leaving the core cross-lingual bottleneck unresolved within the study. - The syllogism dataset uses templated, taxonomic content from WordNet, which may not represent the full distribution of real-world logical arguments. Generalization to naturalistic or domain-specific reasoning tasks is only partially evaluated via an out-of-distribution mention in the abstract but not systematically reported.
Recommended
#55 — Innovative (71)

Behavioral Fingerprints for LLM Endpoint Stability and Identity

Jonah Leshin, Manish Shah, Ian Timmis, Daniel Kang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: This paper defines a genuinely new operational problem — behavioral drift detection for LLM endpoints — that is distinct from traditional reliability monitoring and practically important for production AI systems. The methodology is technically sound, combining energy distance, permutation testing, and e-value sequential detection in a principled, nonparametric pipeline. The real-world deployment evidence (cross-provider divergence, post-hoc attribution to a hardware failure) elevates this beyond a toy demonstration. However, the contribution is a short system report with limited controlled evaluation, and the prompt set design lacks detail.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Traditional reliability metrics (uptime, latency, throughput) cannot detect behavioral drift in LLM endpoints. A model can pass all health checks while silently changing due to weight updates, tokenizer swaps, quantization, inference engine changes, caching, routing, or hardware variation—any of which can break downstream parsers, guardrails, and multi-step agentic workflows. The problem is compounded by infrastructure-level nondeterminism that persists even at temperature=0. This paper treats "stability" as a distinct operational metric: behavioral consistency of an endpoint under a fixed interface contract. Methodology: The system, called Stability Monitor, constructs behavioral fingerprints by sending a fixed model-agnostic prompt set to an endpoint and collecting multiple sampled responses per prompt. Responses are embedded as real-valued vectors, yielding a fingerprint as a set-of-sets-of-vectors (one sample set per prompt). Each fingerprint requires 800 inference requests. Pairwise fingerprint comparison uses a summed energy distance statistic across prompts—a nonparametric, parameter-free distributional distance. Statistical significance is assessed via permutation testing on the aggregate energy distance. Change detection over time uses sequential evidence accumulation via e-values (expectation-bounded test statistics), enabling continuous monitoring with optional stopping behavior: once cumulative evidence exceeds a threshold, a change event is declared and the current fingerprint becomes the new baseline. A companion web application, Stability Arena, continuously monitors multiple providers serving the same nominal model and visualizes per-provider stability histories, pairwise provider distance heatmaps, and a "provider vs the pack" divergence ratio. The approach is fully black-box—no access to weights or provider internals is required, and the prompt set is fixed and model-agnostic (no per-endpoint initialization needed). Key Results: In controlled validation across five change types (model family swap, version upgrade, inference stack change, quantization, and temperature perturbation), Stability Monitor detected four of five changes on the very next fingerprint after the intervention. The exception was a small temperature change (0.7→0.6), which required 18 subsequent fingerprints to trigger a change event. No false positives were observed—exactly one change event per intervention, with stable periods before and after. In real-world deployment, monitoring of Kimi-K2-0905-Instruct across five providers revealed large cross-provider behavioral divergence: the pairwise energy distance heatmap showed that providers like DeepInfra (distance ~1.7 from others) behaved very differently from Moonshot (the model creator), which showed 100% stability during the same period. DeepInfra triggered a change event on nearly every fingerprint generation. The system also enabled post-hoc attribution: when Stability Monitor flagged a change event for Parasail in December 2025, the Parasail team confirmed a hardware-provider switch caused by a physical node failure. Strengths: - The energy distance + permutation test + e-value sequential detection pipeline is statistically principled, nonparametric, and parameter-free, requiring no distributional assumptions about model outputs. - The fixed, model-agnostic prompt set avoids the per-endpoint initialization and post-change re-discovery problem that affects methods like B3IT, making the system robust across change events and across endpoints. - Real-world deployment data provides compelling operational evidence that "same model, different provider" is a genuine behavioral phenomenon, not merely a theoretical concern—with concrete attribution to a hardware failure confirmed by a provider. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is a short system/experience report (4 pages), not a full research paper. Controlled validation covers only 5 change types with minimal repetition and no systematic false-positive rate characterization or power analysis. - The prompt set design and embedding model choice are not described in detail, leaving open questions about sensitivity to prompt selection and embedding quality, and whether the 800-request fingerprint cost is justified relative to cheaper alternatives. - Infrastructure-driven nondeterminism (load-dependent batching, heterogeneous kernels) creates an ambiguous regime between "stable but noisy" and "changed," and the paper offers no principled method for setting the detection threshold or distinguishing these cases beyond acknowledging the limitation.
Search Results 2603.19022 PDF
#56 — Innovative (71)

CacheNotes: Task-Aware Key-Value Cache Compression for Reasoning-Intensive Knowledge Tasks

aclanthology.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: CACHENOTES introduces a clean conceptual contribution by positioning task-aware but query-agnostic KV cache compression in the gap between per-query recomputation and blind pruning. The reuse of CPT query vectors as an attention scaffold for identifying salient tokens is an elegant mechanism that avoids auxiliary networks. However, the core idea of using attention scores for importance-based compression is well-explored, and the task description sensitivity and corpus size limitations reduce the practical novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Integrating external knowledge into LLMs for reasoning-intensive tasks faces a fundamental trade-off: RAG is efficient but fails on multi-hop and broad queries that require synthesizing dispersed evidence, while full-context long-context models are computationally prohibitive due to quadratic prefill costs and GPU memory constraints. Existing KV cache compression methods are either query-aware (requiring per-query recomputation, negating efficiency gains) or query-agnostic (low quality due to lack of task guidance). The gap between query-agnostic and query-aware compression quality has remained a key open challenge. Methodology: CACHENOTES introduces task-aware, query-agnostic KV cache compression via a two-stage offline pipeline. First, given a corpus and a human-written task description, an LLM generates Compression-Planning Tokens (CPTs) — a "cheat sheet" summarizing task-relevant information from the corpus. CPTs are not used as a textual prefix; instead, their query vectors serve as an attention scaffold: layer-wise cross-attention scores between CPT query vectors and corpus key vectors (weighted by CPT value norms) identify the top-k most salient corpus tokens. These tokens' KV states are retained to form the compressed cache, with rotary positional embeddings re-applied for positional consistency. At inference, query tokens are simply appended to the precomputed compressed cache. To handle large corpora without OOM failures, the cross-attention is computed via a chunked streaming softmax algorithm (inspired by FlashAttention), reducing memory from O(mn) to O(mc). The entire offline cost (~2.5× vanilla prefill) is amortized across all downstream queries. Key Results: On LongBench (16 datasets, LLAMA-3.1-8B and QWEN3-4B), CACHENOTES outperforms RAG by +7.58 F1 (LLAMA) and +3.8 F1 (QWEN) on Single-Doc QA at a 512-token budget, and achieves 98.9% of full-context performance at 2.5× compression. On RULER, CACHENOTES surpasses the prior best query-agnostic method (Expected Attention) by 55 String Match points at 0.75 compression ratio, narrowing the gap to query-aware FINCH to just 2 SM points. On real-world enterprise Company Notes, CACHENOTES wins 30–40 more pairwise comparisons than RAG in correctness and insightfulness. On a controlled synthetic dataset, CACHENOTES tracks full-context performance on join-like multi-hop queries where RAG degrades by ~15 F1 points as inter-chunk connectivity increases. Deployment measurements on an H100 show TTFT of 0.13s vs. 0.61s for RAG and 15.88s for full prefill at 128k tokens, with memory capped at 16.95 GB vs. 31.6 GB for full context. Strengths: - Elegant reuse of the model's own attention mechanism (via CPT query vectors) for compression scoring, avoiding auxiliary networks or heuristics. - Clear efficiency advantage: flat TTFT and fixed memory across corpus lengths, with offline cost amortized after just ~2 queries. - Thorough ablation study isolating the contributions of CPTs vs. task description vs. KV compression, validating that each component is necessary. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Task description sensitivity creates a practical burden: CPTs show clear negative transfer across task types (e.g., summarization CPTs hurt passage retrieval by 12.5 points), requiring per-task-family prompt engineering similar to RAG system prompt design. - The method assumes the full corpus fits within the model's context window for the offline prefill; for corpora exceeding this limit, independent chunk compression loses cross-document attention, and no principled solution for merging compressed caches is provided. - Evaluated primarily on relatively small corpora (~10–32k tokens); scalability to millions of tokens and dynamic/streaming corpora remains unvalidated beyond a brief discussion.
Citations
#57 — Innovative (71)

Speed by Simplicity: A Single-Stream Architecture for Fast Audio-Video Generative Foundation Model (88 ▲ )

SII-GAIR, Sand. ai, :, Ethan Chern, Hansi Teng, Hanwen Sun, Hao Wang, Hong Pan, Hongyu Jia, Jiadi Su, Jin Li, Junjie Yu, Lijie Liu, Lingzhi Li, Lyumanshan Ye, Min Hu, Qiangang Wang, Quanwei Qi, Steffi Chern, Tao Bu, Taoran Wang, Teren Xu, Tianning Zhang, Tiantian Mi, Weixian Xu, Wenqiang Zhang, Wentai Zhang, Xianping Yi, Xiaojie Cai, Xiaoyang Kang, Yan Ma, Yixiu Liu, Yunbo Zhang, Yunpeng Huang, Yutong Lin, Zewei Tao, Zhaoliang Liu, Zheng Zhang, Zhiyao Cen, Zhixuan Yu, Zhongshu Wang, Zhulin Hu, Zijin Zhou, Zinan Guo, Yue Cao, Pengfei Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The core finding that a single-stream self-attention architecture matches or exceeds multi-stream designs for joint audio-video generation is non-obvious and practically significant, suggesting that explicit cross-modal fusion modules may be unnecessary overhead. The 3x WER reduction over Ovi 1.1 without synchronization modules is a strong empirical signal. Timestep-free denoising is an interesting simplification, though the lack of ablations makes it impossible to attribute gains to specific design choices versus scale and data.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Joint audio-video generation — producing synchronized speech and video from text — is a frontier challenge where closed-source models (Veo 3, Sora 2, Kling 3.0) lead but open-source alternatives remain limited. Existing open models (Ovi, LTX-2) rely on multi-stream architectures with separate pathways for video and audio, cross-attention fusion modules, and modality-specific alignment layers. While expressive, such designs introduce irregular computation patterns that complicate training, inference optimization, and community adoption. The paper asks whether a simpler, unified architecture can match or exceed these specialized designs while enabling practical inference speeds. Methodology: daVinci-MagiHuman is a 15B-parameter, 40-layer flow-matching diffusion Transformer that processes text, video, and audio tokens in a single unified sequence using only self-attention. Three architectural choices distinguish it: (1) a sandwich layout where the first and last 4 layers use modality-specific projections and RMSNorm, while the middle 32 layers share weights across modalities; (2) timestep-free denoising — no explicit timestep embeddings or AdaLN conditioning, with the model inferring denoising state directly from the noisy latents; and (3) per-head gating via a learned sigmoid scalar on each attention head output for training stability. Conditioning (text, reference image, noisy video/audio) is handled entirely within the shared token space without dedicated fusion modules. For inference efficiency, four techniques are stacked: latent-space super-resolution (base model generates at 256p, a dedicated SR checkpoint refines in 5 steps using trilinear upsampling + noise injection), a Turbo VAE decoder replacing the standard Wan2.2 decoder, full-graph compilation (MagiCompiler, ~1.2× speedup), and DMD-2 distillation reducing sampling to 8 steps without classifier-free guidance. Key Results: On automatic benchmarks (VerseBench for video, TalkVid-Bench for audio), daVinci-MagiHuman achieves the best visual quality (4.80 vs. 4.76 for LTX 2.3 and 4.73 for Ovi 1.1), best text alignment (4.18 vs. 4.12/4.10), and lowest word error rate (14.60% vs. 19.23% for LTX 2.3 and 40.45% for Ovi 1.1) — a 24% absolute WER reduction over the next-best open model. Physical consistency is slightly below LTX 2.3 (4.52 vs. 4.56). In pairwise human evaluation over 2,000 comparisons (10 raters × 200 pairs), daVinci-MagiHuman achieves 80.0% win rate against Ovi 1.1 and 60.9% against LTX 2.3. Inference: the distilled model generates a 5-second 256p video in 2.0 seconds and a 5-second 1080p video in 38.4 seconds on a single H100 GPU. Strengths: - The single-stream, self-attention-only architecture is a principled simplification over multi-stream designs, and the empirical results validate that it does not sacrifice quality — a non-obvious finding given the structural differences between video and audio modalities. - The speech intelligibility improvement is substantial: 14.60% WER vs. 40.45% for Ovi 1.1 is a nearly 3× reduction, suggesting the unified token-space representation learns strong audio-visual alignment without explicit synchronization modules. - The inference stack is well-engineered — latent-space SR avoids costly decode/encode round trips, Turbo VAE cuts decoding overhead on the critical path, and distillation reduces NFEs to 8, yielding genuinely fast wall-clock times suitable for interactive use. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is a technical report without ablations: there is no isolation of which architectural choices (sandwich layout, timestep-free denoising, per-head gating) contribute how much to final quality or stability, making it difficult to assess the necessity of each component. - Evaluation is narrow — only two open-source baselines are compared, and the automatic metrics (VideoScore2, WER) may not fully capture synchronization quality, temporal coherence at scale, or failure modes on non-human-centric content, which the model explicitly does not prioritize.
Recommended 2603.21986 PDF
#58 — Innovative (71)

Less Gaussians, Texture More: 4K Feed-Forward Textured Splatting (6 ▲ )

Yixing Lao, Xuyang Bai, Xiaoyang Wu, Nuoyuan Yan, Zixin Luo, Tian Fang, Jean-Daniel Nahmias, Yanghai Tsin, Shiwei Li, Hengshuang Zhao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 Mar 2026
Why this rank: LGTM achieves a practical breakthrough as the first feed-forward method capable of 4K novel view synthesis, solving the quadratic scaling bottleneck through a clean frequency-separation principle. The 64× pixel increase costing only 1.80× memory is an impressive engineering result. The plug-and-play design wrapping four different baselines demonstrates genuine generality. However, the texture mechanism is a relatively natural extension of existing per-scene textured Gaussian ideas to the feed-forward setting.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) methods predict pixel-aligned Gaussian primitives, causing primitive count to grow quadratically with image resolution. Scaling a 512-resolution model to 4K requires 64× more primitives, making training and inference prohibitively expensive in memory and compute—prior methods simply run out of memory at 2K or 4K. Additionally, standard 3DGS couples appearance and geometry within each primitive, requiring an excessive number of Gaussians to represent high-frequency textures on geometrically simple surfaces. While per-scene textured Gaussian methods exist, none generalize in a feed-forward manner across scenes. Methodology: LGTM (Less Gaussians, Texture More) introduces a dual-network architecture that decouples geometry prediction from appearance prediction. A primitive network (ViT encoder-decoder) processes low-resolution images (e.g., 512×288) and predicts a compact grid of 2D Gaussian Splatting (2DGS) primitives—positions, scales, rotations, and spherical harmonic colors. Crucially, it is trained with high-resolution rendering supervision to force appropriate primitive scaling for full-resolution rendering. A separate texture network takes the full high-resolution images and the primitive network's feature maps, and predicts per-primitive texture maps: an RGB color texture T^c and an alpha texture T^α (each T×T pixels), where T is tunable (2×2 to 8×8). The texture network uses two complementary feature sources: (1) image patchification with convolutional layers for local high-frequency features, and (2) projective prior textures obtained by inverse-rasterizing the high-res image onto each primitive's local UV space via the inverse homography transform M^{-1}. These projective priors provide a strong initialization of appearance. The alpha texture replaces the Gaussian falloff function, and the color texture adds detail on top of the spherical harmonic base color. Training is staged: first pre-train the primitive network with high-resolution supervision only, then jointly train both networks with a reduced learning rate on the primitive network. The framework is a plug-in that wraps existing baselines (Flash3D, DepthSplat, NoPoSplat, VGGT) without architectural redesign of their backbones. Key Results: LGTM is the first feed-forward method to achieve 4K novel view synthesis. On DL3DV-10K at 4K with NoPoSplat as backbone: LGTM achieves LPIPS=0.200, SSIM=0.803, PSNR=24.489, versus 3DGS LPIPS=0.351 and 2DGS LPIPS=0.322—a 43–75% reduction in LPIPS. With DepthSplat at 4K: LGTM reaches LPIPS=0.170 vs. 3DGS LPIPS=0.210 (19% improvement). In training memory, LGTM trains at 4K using under 30 GB GPU memory (batch size 1) while the baseline NoPoSplat runs OOM at 2K (>61.85 GB at 1K). Inference efficiency: scaling from 512×288 to 4096×2304 (64× pixels) costs only 1.80× peak GPU memory (3.06→5.51 GB) and 1.47× total time (119→175 ms) on an A100. LGTM consistently outperforms 3DGS and 2DGS baselines across all tested resolutions (1K, 2K, 4K), datasets (RealEstate10K, DL3DV), and input settings (single-view, posed two-view, unposed two-view, multi-view). Ablation confirms each component contributes: high-res retraining alone (LPIPS 0.371→0.256), adding image patchify features (→0.199), color texture (→0.189), and alpha texture (→0.176). Strengths: - Elegantly solves the quadratic scaling bottleneck of pixel-aligned 3DGS by exploiting natural frequency separation (low-freq geometry, high-freq appearance), enabling 4K feed-forward NVS that was previously impossible. - The framework is genuinely plug-and-play: LGTM wraps four different baselines (mono, posed, unposed, multi-view) without redesigning their architectures, demonstrating broad generality. - Excellent inference efficiency—the 64× pixel increase costs only 1.80× memory and 1.47× time—making the approach practically deployable for AR/VR use cases. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Reconstruction quality remains geometry-bottlenecked: gains are most pronounced in single-view (Flash3D), moderate for posed two-view, smaller for unposed two-view, and marginal for multi-view (VGGT), suggesting that when geometry is imprecise, better textures cannot fully compensate. - The texture resolution T is a fixed hyperparameter requiring manual tuning per resolution target; there is no adaptive or learned mechanism to allocate texture resolution non-uniformly across primitives based on local appearance complexity.
Recommended 2603.25745 PDF
#59 — Innovative (71)

Qworld: Question-Specific Evaluation Criteria for LLMs (7 ▲ )

Shanghua Gao, Yuchang Su, Pengwei Sui, Curtis Ginder, Marinka Zitnik · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Qworld introduces a genuinely novel framing—question-specific evaluation criteria via recursive expansion trees—that moves LLM evaluation beyond one-size-fits-all rubrics. The dual coverage/uniqueness metric is well-designed, and the demonstrated ability to reshuffle frontier model rankings by exposing dimension-level strengths (e.g., Qwen3-30B on empathy, GPT-5 on safety) is both actionable and surprising. However, the heavy reliance on GPT-4.1 throughout the pipeline raises circularity concerns, and computational costs remain unaddressed.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Evaluating LLMs on open-ended questions is fundamentally harder than closed-ended benchmarks because response quality depends on the specific context, intent, and implicit requirements of each question. Existing evaluation frameworks rely on dataset-level or task-level rubrics that assume all questions within a domain share the same evaluation needs, or they generate criteria in a single LLM pass without systematically searching the evaluation space. This conflates distinct requirements—e.g., patient-facing communication vs. safety-critical risk management in medical settings—and produces criteria that either miss subtle but important dimensions or saturate benchmarks by being too lenient. Expert-authored question-level criteria are high quality but prohibitively expensive to produce at scale. Methodology: Qworld (One-Question-One-World) generates question-specific evaluation criteria via a Recursive Expansion Tree (RET) algorithm. Given a question, RET builds a three-level tree: Level 1 (Scenarios) infers distinct contexts implied by the question; Level 2 (Perspectives) derives evaluation axes for each scenario (e.g., safety, completeness, equity); Level 3 (Criteria) instantiates binary, weighted, verifiable criteria per perspective. Tree growth uses two complementary operators at each level: hierarchical expansion (decomposing a node into finer-grained children) and horizontal expansion (identifying and adding missing siblings to improve coverage). These alternate for configurable expansion rounds (w1=3 scenarios, w2=4 perspectives). The final leaf nodes form the criteria set, each with a binary yes/no verifiability condition and an importance weight used in a HealthBench-compatible normalized scoring function. Criteria are subsequently deduplicated and consolidated via a reviewer LLM pass, and polarity-checked to distinguish positive (desirable) from negative (penalizing) criteria. A retrieval-augmented variant (Qworld_ret.) additionally fetches external web content to ground criteria in up-to-date domain knowledge. Key Results: On a 1K subset of HealthBench against physician-authored criteria, Qworld achieves Coverage 0.89 (vs. 0.83 for best prior method EvalAgent) and Uniqueness 0.79 (vs. 0.50 for EvalAgent), meaning 79% of generated criteria are novel beyond expert references. Human expert ratings show Insight 0.83 (vs. 0.42 best prior) and Granularity 0.85, with the insight gap of +0.40 over prior best being the most striking result. Ablation confirms RET outperforms CoT (Coverage 0.67/Uniqueness 0.40), Self-Reflection (0.84/0.70), and Tree Decomposition (0.77/0.65) individually. Applied to 11 frontier LLMs on HealthBench, Qworld-based scores are approximately 20% lower than official scores, reducing saturation; Qwen3-30B rises from 6th to 2nd by revealing strength in patient-facing dimensions (Empathy, Health Equity, Sustainability), while Grok-4.1-Fast drops from 4th to 6th. On Humanity's Last Exam, where the official metric measures only final-answer correctness, Qworld reshuffles rankings substantially: Claude Sonnet 4.5 rises from 6th to 3rd due to higher scores on Pedagogical Effectiveness and Notation Precision. Across expansion steps, criteria Value remains above 0.90 as count grows from 15 to 60, confirming that added criteria are non-redundant. Strengths: - The recursive expansion tree is a principled and well-ablated design choice: the combination of hierarchical (depth) and horizontal (breadth) operators is demonstrably better than either alone, and better than iterative self-reflection at matched criteria counts, confirming that gains come from genuine coverage of new evaluation axes rather than volume inflation. - The two-dimensional quality characterization—Coverage (recall against expert references) plus Uniqueness (discovery of expert-missed criteria)—is a rigorous and non-trivially gamed metric pair; high scores on both simultaneously is a strong signal. - The application to frontier LLM benchmarking yields actionable and interpretable insights: rank changes are explained by dimension-level breakdowns (e.g., GPT-5 strong on safety-critical dimensions, Qwen3-30B strong on patient-facing value dimensions), demonstrating that the criteria are discriminating in a semantically meaningful way. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The entire pipeline—criteria generation, coverage/uniqueness judging, and response evaluation—uses GPT-4.1 as the backbone, creating a potential circularity: the same model that generates criteria also evaluates whether those criteria are covered and whether responses satisfy them. Self-evaluation bias cannot be fully ruled out, and the robustness check (cross-evaluating with Qwen3-30B) covers only the judging step, not the generation step. - Computational cost is not reported. The RET algorithm involves multiple sequential LLM calls per question (scenario analysis, scenario expansion × w1 rounds, perspective analysis × scenarios, perspective expansion × w2 rounds, criteria generation × perspectives, criteria expansion, review, polarity check, score assignment), which could be prohibitively expensive for large-scale benchmark construction. - The Coverage and Uniqueness metrics themselves rely on an LLM judge to assess semantic equivalence between criteria, introducing noise and potential inconsistency; no inter-rater reliability or confidence intervals are reported for these automatic metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical significance of the differences between methods.
Recommended 2603.23522 PDF
#60 — Innovative (71)

SCoUT: A Framework for Structured Stereotype Analysis in Language Models

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: SCoUT earns 71 for its principled interdisciplinary integration of Thurstone's psychometric framework with the Stereotype Content Model from social psychology, moving LLM stereotype auditing from ad hoc benchmarks to a latent-variable model with quantifiable fit. The closed-loop design connecting modeling, probing, and intervention provides convergent mechanistic evidence, and the high cross-model consistency across five architectures suggests the recovered structure is robust. However, the restriction to two SCM dimensions and the proof-of-concept nature of the steering intervention limit immediate practical impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing methods for auditing stereotypes in Large Language Models fall into two unsatisfying categories: association-level tests (WEAT, StereoSet) that measure surface-level semantic associations without structural insight, and decision/generation-based probes (CrowS-Pairs, BiasInBios) that are highly sensitive to prompt phrasing, yielding fragmented and non-comparable results. Both families measure only external outputs, failing to answer whether stereotypes reflect a coherent internal organization or merely surface-level patterns. This gap limits auditing to descriptive detection, precluding actionable governance or mechanistic explanation. Methodology: SCoUT (Stereotype Content-oriented Utility structure via Thurstonian modeling) is a three-stage closed-loop framework grounded in the Stereotype Content Model (SCM) from social psychology. Stage 1 constructs a latent stereotype utility space: the LLM is queried with forced pairwise comparisons between 98 social groups on warmth and competence dimensions (K=10 trials per pair), and Thurstone's law of comparative judgment is used to fit latent utilities via maximum likelihood, yielding a globally normalized 2D embedding per model. Stage 2 probes internal representations by training linear ridge regression probes on mean-pooled attention head outputs to predict the modeled utilities; Spearman correlation identifies dimension-sensitive heads (top-k=20 used downstream). Word-level probing with human-annotated SCM lexicons tests whether these heads generalize beyond training groups. Stage 3 performs inference-time activation steering by shifting selected attention head activations along the learned probe direction (x → x + α·w), with effectiveness measured qualitatively via generated text and quantitatively via a communality classifier on a professor-review task across demographic subgroups. Key Results: Thurstonian modeling achieves pairwise preference prediction accuracy ≥90% on most larger models (e.g., LLaMA3.1-70B: 95.4%/95.8%, Qwen2.5-72B: 94.9%/93.7% for competence/warmth); reasoning prompts yield gains up to +15.6% on weaker models. Cross-model consistency among 70B-scale models is high (Spearman ρ̄_warm=0.923, ρ̄_comp=0.969; Kendall's W=0.948/0.979), suggesting a model-agnostic latent structure. Compared to Stereomap's direct Likert scoring, which collapses >1/3 of groups to identical warmth scores (Std=0.708, range [-2.0, 1.67]), the Thurstonian approach yields wider, more discriminative distributions (Std=1.0, range [-2.53, 2.17]). k-means clustering of the 2D utility space recovers SCM's four affective quadrants, with cluster-dominant emotions (pity: 3.26, contempt: 2.62, envy: 2.52, admiration: 3.58) matching theoretical predictions. Probing localizes stereotype utilities to specific attention heads in upper-middle layers (peak Spearman ρ=0.83 warmth, 0.90 competence; top 1% heads average 0.728/0.794). An asymmetry emerges: competence-sensitive heads are more tied to group identity, warmth-sensitive heads generalize more to lexical descriptors. Activation steering on the professor-review task yields mean communality shifts of -17.10 (α=-30) and +29.17 (α=+30) points across demographic subgroups, with strong effect sizes (Cohen's d=1.5 and 3.8). Strengths: - Strong theoretical grounding: integrates Thurstone's psychometric framework with SCM social psychology, moving beyond ad hoc benchmarks to a principled latent-variable model with quantifiable fit. - Closed-loop design: the same utility structure drives modeling, probing, and intervention, providing convergent mechanistic evidence rather than isolated observational claims. - Cross-model generalizability: high Spearman/Kendall consistency across 5 architectures spanning 7B–72B parameters suggests the recovered structure reflects shared training data biases rather than model-specific artifacts. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework restricts analysis to warmth and competence from SCM, which, while theoretically motivated, may miss other stereotype dimensions (e.g., agency, morality) relevant to LLM behavior; generalization to a richer dimensional space is unaddressed. - Intervention via activation steering is presented as a mechanistic proof-of-concept rather than a practical debiasing method; the paper acknowledges that permanent mitigation (fine-tuning, model editing) is left to future work, limiting immediate practical utility. - The evaluation uses only 98 social groups drawn from prior SCM literature and a single quantitative steering task (professor reviews); broader validation across diverse downstream tasks and group taxonomies would strengthen claims of generality.
Recommended
#61 — Innovative (69)

Iterative Multi-Granular RAG with Contextual Hierarchical Graph

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: MGranRAG makes a meaningful contribution by showing that a lightweight spaCy-based hierarchical graph (no LLM at index time) can substitute for expensive LLM-constructed knowledge graphs while retaining global structural benefits via PPR propagation. The dual local-global iterative loop is well-motivated and each component is cleanly ablated. However, the high retrieval-time token costs (up to 12M tokens) partially offset the indexing savings, and gains on single-hop tasks are modest, limiting the generality of the approach.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard RAG systems suffer from evidence fragmentation: they retrieve topically relevant but logically isolated passages, which breaks down for multi-hop questions requiring coherent reasoning chains across multiple documents. Iterative RAG methods improve flexibility but remain constrained by local information horizons, missing global associative structures. Structure-augmented approaches (GraphRAG, HippoRAG) capture global relationships but carry prohibitive construction costs from LLM-based entity/relation extraction. The gap is a method that provides global semantic coherence at low construction cost while retaining iterative adaptivity. Methodology: MGranRAG combines a lightweight Contextual Hierarchical Graph (CHG) with a dual local-global iterative retrieval loop. The CHG is built offline using spaCy (no LLM calls): documents are chunked into passages, sentences, and noun phrases/named entities, connected via hierarchical containment edges (passage→sentence→phrase). This creates a three-tier graph G=(V,E) at negligible cost compared to semantic KG construction. Online retrieval iterates T rounds. In each round, Local Evidence Refinement prompts an LLM (Qwen2.5-7B or Qwen3-8B in few-shot CoT) to extract sentence-level evidence units with associated key phrases from the current top-M candidate passages, and labels each unit as fully supported, partially supported, or irrelevant with respect to the query and its CoT-decomposed sub-queries. A confidence score ψ(ε) aggregates these labels. The evidence-augmented query then re-ranks passages via dense retrieval. Global Structure-Aware Calibration maps these evidence units onto CHG nodes — passage weights combine cosine similarity with momentum from prior iterations; sentence weights use the confidence score; phrase weights incorporate confidence, phrase-type features, and inverse node degree. Personalized PageRank (PPR) propagates these weights through the graph to produce a globally coherent re-ranking. When the LLM discovers phrases absent from the CHG, they are incrementally added at negligible overhead. The final passage set after T iterations feeds a separate reader LLM (Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct) for answer generation. Key Results: Evaluated on six benchmarks (HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, MuSiQue, NQ, PopQA, NarrativeQA) with 1,000 questions each. MGranRAG (Qwen3-8B, T=3) achieves average Recall@5 of 84.2% and average F1 of 56.2%, both best overall. Compared to the strong NV-Embed-v2 dense retriever baseline (73.4% R@5, 52.6% F1), gains are especially large on multi-hop datasets: PopQA +22.7% R@5, 2Wiki +19.4% R@5, HotpotQA +3.7% R@5. It outperforms HippoRAG 2 (78.2% R@5, 55.0% F1) on average and reaches 98.2% R@5 on HotpotQA. Even at T=1 it beats all baselines on average. QA performance is comparable to RL-trained iterative methods like R1-Searcher (54.7% F1) on multi-hop benchmarks despite using only few-shot prompting. Ablation confirms CHG alone yields +18.6%/+21.7% R@5 on 2Wiki/HotpotQA; query decomposition adds further gains; full EAR completes the picture. Token usage at retrieval time (4.5M input tokens for T=1, 12M for T=3 on 2Wiki) is higher than baselines but requires zero indexing-time LLM tokens, unlike HippoRAG 2 (5.8M index input tokens). Strengths: - The CHG construction is genuinely lightweight (spaCy only, no LLM at index time), directly addressing the cost bottleneck of GraphRAG/HippoRAG without sacrificing global structure. - The dual local-global loop is principled: local evidence extraction sharpens query signals, and PPR over the hierarchical graph propagates them globally — these two mechanisms are complementary and each is ablated to confirm independent contribution. - Plug-and-play design is validated across multiple retrievers (Contriever, NV-Embed-v2) and multiple LLM backbones (1.5B to 8B), showing the framework is not brittle to component choices. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Retrieval-time token costs are substantially higher than baselines (12M input tokens for T=3 vs. 3-4M for Search-R1/R1-Searcher), driven by fixed ~3k-token evidence extraction prompts per iteration, making real-time or large-scale deployment expensive. - The CHG lacks semantic edges between passages (no cross-document coreference or entity linking), so it can miss connections that require understanding that two passages discuss the same entity under different surface forms — a limitation the authors acknowledge but defer to future work. - Gains on simpler single-hop datasets (NQ, NarrativeQA) are modest or inconsistent, suggesting the framework's complexity is not always warranted and the overhead may not be justified for non-multi-hop use cases.
Recommended
#62 — Innovative (68)

Linear Predictability of Attention Heads in Large Language Models

Khalid Shaikh, Asmit Kumar Singh, Rebecca Christopher Dsouza, Shikhar Shiromani · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 68 for a well-executed mechanistic study that reveals systematic linear dependencies among attention head activations and traces their emergence during pretraining via OLMo-2 checkpoints. The Key/Value asymmetry discovery is both novel and actionable for KV cache compression. The theoretical lower bound via Hanson-Wright rigorously distinguishes learned from architectural structure. Compression results are model-dependent and sometimes modest, limiting immediate practical impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM inference is increasingly bottlenecked by the KV cache, which grows with sequence length and limits batch size, context length, and deployability on memory-constrained devices. Prior interpretability work studied attention head redundancy at a semantic level (which tokens heads attend to, which heads can be pruned) but left the fine-grained structure of internal Key, Value, and Query activation vectors largely unexplored. This paper asks whether pretrained LLMs develop systematic linear dependencies among these activation vectors, when such structure emerges, and whether it can be exploited for KV cache compression without architectural changes. Methodology: The core empirical approach fits Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regressors between attention head activations collected on 150 sequences (~30,000 tokens) from C4. For each target head, the top-N reference heads (by pairwise R²) are selected and a multivariate OLS model predicts the target's K/V/Q vectors from the concatenated reference vectors. To study emergence, the authors use publicly available OLMo-2 intermediate checkpoints spanning 1B to ~4T training tokens, measuring both the R² distribution over time and the intra-layer subspace overlap dimension (OD_H = sum of individual head ranks minus rank of the concatenated projection matrix). For KV cache compression, a calibration phase identifies reference and target heads via a binary-search algorithm that constructs a directed predictability graph and greedily selects up to fraction f of heads as targets; learned linear maps then reconstruct target KV states on-the-fly during inference from cached reference states only. A theoretical lower bound (Theorem 3.1, proved via the Hanson-Wright inequality) establishes that random initialization produces high MSE with probability at least 1 − 2e^{−c·m}, confirming structure must be learned. Key Results: Across Llama-3.1 8B, Falcon3-10B, OLMo-2-7B, and Qwen3-32B, Key activations achieve mean R² ≈ 0.76 on C4 with N=5 reference heads, rising to R² > 0.85 on GSM8K; over 75% of pairwise head relationships exceed R² = 0.5. At random initialization, fewer than 5% of head pairs exceed R² = 0.10. During OLMo-2 pretraining, intra-layer predictability surpasses inter-layer links by 50k–100k steps and remains dominant, correlated with monotonically growing Key subspace overlap (Keys align fastest, Queries follow, Values remain near-orthogonal). The 2× KV cache compression (predicting 50% of heads) yields average accuracy drops of 4.5 pp on Falcon3-10B and 5.5 pp on Qwen3-32B across five benchmarks, but a larger 9.9 pp drop on Llama-3.1 8B. Disentangling compression streams reveals Key-only compression costs only 1.97 pp on GSM8K math accuracy versus 4.30 pp for Value-only and 6.73 pp for joint K+V compression on Llama-3.1 8B, consistent with the asymmetric subspace overlap found during training. Cross-task transferability experiments show within-domain generalization is strong (math→math) while cross-domain transfer degrades more, especially for Values. Strengths: - The emergence analysis via OLMo-2 checkpoints is a genuine mechanistic contribution: tracing when and how inter-head linearity develops (intra-layer dominance after 50k–100k steps, correlated with Key subspace overlap growth) goes beyond simply observing the phenomenon in fully trained models. - The theoretical lower bound (Theorem 3.1) via Hanson-Wright rigorously rules out the possibility that this structure is architectural rather than learned, strengthening the empirical narrative considerably. - The Key/Value asymmetry discovery is both well-motivated by the subspace analysis and directly actionable: it suggests a practical hybrid compression strategy (aggressive Key compression, conservative Value compression) that is cleaner than prior all-or-nothing approaches. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The compression results are model-dependent and sometimes modest: Llama-3.1 8B loses 9.9 pp average accuracy at 2× compression, which is likely unacceptable in practice, and the paper does not identify what architectural features make some models more compressible than others. - The method requires a calibration phase to select reference heads and fit linear maps, introducing data and compute overhead; the inference-time reconstruction latency added by the linear maps is acknowledged but not quantified, making it unclear whether the memory savings translate to actual throughput gains. - The theoretical analysis (Theorem 3.1) applies formally only to the first layer under Gaussian inputs; the extension to deeper layers under real distributions is empirical rather than theoretical, and the paper does not provide guarantees on reconstruction quality for the compression scheme itself.
Search Results 2603.13314 PDF
#63 — Innovative (68)

FleetOpt: Analytical Fleet Provisioning for LLM Inference with Compress-and-Route as Implementation Mechanism

Huamin Chen, Xunzhuo Liu, Yuhan Liu, Junchen Jiang, Bowei He, Xue Liu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: FleetOpt makes a clean conceptual contribution by identifying and quantifying the 'cost cliff' at pool boundaries and providing a principled analytical framework (M/G/c + Erlang-C) for fleet provisioning rather than heuristic tuning. The compress-and-route mechanism is practical and lightweight, but the approach is fundamentally an optimization over a well-understood queueing model, and the most dramatic gains (82.4%) depend on a favorable workload CDF that may not generalize.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM GPU fleets are typically provisioned for worst-case context lengths (128K tokens), yet production traces show 80% of requests use fewer than 2K tokens. This wastes enormous KV-cache capacity. Prior work on pool routing (splitting fleets into short- and long-context pools) reduces cost by 16–38%, but leaves a structural inefficiency at the pool boundary: a single token above the threshold forces a request into a long-context pool slot provisioned for 64K tokens, consuming 8–42× more GPU capacity than a short-pool slot. This "cost cliff" makes it impossible to achieve the analytically optimal pool boundary in practice, since borderline requests (4.6–11.2% of all traffic in studied workloads) are wastefully assigned to expensive long-pool slots. Methodology: FleetOpt takes an analytical-first approach: given a workload prompt-length CDF and a P99 TTFT SLO, it derives the minimum-cost fleet configuration before deployment. The analytical core models each pool as an M/G/c queue where servers correspond to KV-cache slots (c = n_gpus × n_max). GPU iteration latency is modeled as t_iter = W + H·n_slots, with hardware constants calibrated to A100-80GB/Llama-3-70B. The optimal boundary B_short is derived by minimizing total GPU cost subject to the SLO constraint, yielding an equal marginal GPU cost condition across pools (Proposition 1). The cost cliff prevents achieving B_short directly, so Compress-and-Route (C&R) serves as the implementation mechanism: a gateway-layer extractive compressor trims borderline requests (those in (B_short, γB_short]) below B_short before they reach the inference engine. The compressor uses a classical NLP pipeline (TextRank + Position + TF-IDF + Novelty scoring, greedy sentence selection) requiring no LLM inference, completing in 2–7 ms. A hard OOM guarantee is enforced by construction (T_c + L_out = B_short). The FleetOpt offline planner sweeps over candidate boundary values and γ ∈ {1.0,...,2.0}, invoking Erlang-C inversion at each point, and returns the optimal (n_s, n_l, B_short, γ*) in under 1 ms. A key correctness requirement is recalibrating the long-pool service rate μ_l from the post-compression request distribution; omitting this step systematically overestimates savings. Key Results: On three production traces (Azure LLM Inference Trace 2023, LMSYS-Chat-1M multi-turn, and a synthetic Agent-heavy trace), FleetOpt achieves 6.7–82.4% GPU cost reduction versus a homogeneous baseline. Pool routing alone saves 5.5–41.7%. C&R contributes an incremental 1.2 percentage points for Agent-heavy (dispersed distribution, ρ=8×, partial code exclusion), 15.9 pp for LMSYS (42× cliff), and 43.7 pp for Azure (16× cliff). The most dramatic case is Azure at B_short=4,096 with γ*=2.0: FleetOpt reduces the fleet from 284 GPUs (homogeneous) to just 50 GPUs (48 short + 2 long), saving 82.4% in annualized cost ($5.5M → $968K). The analytical M/G/c model is validated against a discrete-event simulator with ≤3% utilization error across all pools and workloads. Compression adds ≤0.39 ms mean overhead per request against a 500 ms TTFT SLO. Compression fidelity on borderline prompts achieves BERTScore F1=0.884, ROUGE-L recall=0.856, TF-IDF cosine=0.981 at a mean 15.4% token reduction. Strengths: - The analytical formulation is principled: deriving the optimal fleet from first principles (M/G/c + Erlang-C inversion) rather than heuristic tuning, with a formal proof that co-design is never worse than retrofit (Theorem 2). - The identification and quantification of the "cost cliff" as a structural barrier is a useful conceptual contribution that cleanly explains why naive pool routing underperforms. - The compressor is LLM-free (pure classical NLP), runs in 2–7 ms, and provides a hard OOM safety guarantee by construction — making it practically deployable without additional inference infrastructure. - The planner runs in under 1 ms, enabling frequent re-provisioning as workload CDFs shift. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Gains are highly workload-dependent and the paper's most dramatic result (82.4% on Azure) depends on a concentrated-below CDF with a large cliff ratio (ρ=16×); for Agent-heavy workloads with dispersed distributions the gains are modest (6.7%), limiting generalizability claims. - The evaluation uses only one hardware configuration (Llama-3-70B on A100-80GB) with fixed hardware constants W and H; applicability to other model sizes, quantization levels, or GPU generations requires recalibration with no guidance provided. - The extractive compressor excludes code (which comprises ~25% of borderline traffic in Agent-heavy), and the fidelity study covers only prose/RAG content from a single dataset (LMSYS-Chat-1M); the approach may degrade for structured or technical content not well-represented in that corpus. - The M/G/c model assumes Poisson arrivals and operates in the many-server regime where queueing delays are negligible — in this regime the full queueing apparatus reduces to a simple utilization-cap formula, raising questions about the value added by the queueing formalism over a simpler model.
Search Results 2603.16514 PDF
#64 — Innovative (68)

Self-Interpretable Subgraph Neural Network with Deep Reinforcement Walk Exploration

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: RWE-SGNN's unification of subgraph neural network expressiveness with intrinsic interpretability via reinforcement walk exploration is technically elegant — interpretability emerges from the RL reward signal without auxiliary losses, which is rare. The walk-based MDP with a formal equivalence guarantee (Theorem 1) to connected subgraph generation provides a clean efficiency-expressiveness tradeoff. Simultaneously achieving SOTA accuracy and explainability on multiple benchmarks is a compelling result. The limitation to small binary classification benchmarks tempers the impact claim.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard message-passing GNNs are limited to 1-WL expressiveness and act as black boxes, offering no insight into which substructures drive predictions. Subgraph Neural Networks (SGNNs) improve expressiveness by aggregating over bags of subgraphs, but rely on predefined (random or algorithmic) sampling strategies that are data-agnostic, computationally inefficient (quadratic candidate explosion), and produce subgraphs with no interpretable meaning. Post-hoc GNN explainers identify important substructures after the fact but cannot feed those explanations back to improve the model. The gap is that no existing approach jointly optimizes for predictive performance and intrinsic interpretability within a single end-to-end framework. Methodology: The paper proposes RWE-SGNN, a self-interpretable SGNN that replaces static subgraph sampling with a data-driven reinforcement walk exploration (RWE) module. The architecture has two coupled sub-models. The sampling model consists of: (1) a low-level graphlet perceptron—a shallow MPNN that computes graphlet-aware node embeddings identifying nodes of interest (NOIs) exhibiting local structural patterns; and (2) a high-level substructure extractor that uses Deep Q-Networks (DQN) over a walk-exploration-based Markov Decision Process (MDP) to generate informative walk sequences. The walk-based MDP restricts the action space at each step to only the neighbors of the current node (depth-first), reducing complexity from O(N²D) to O(LD) compared to the breadth-first subgraph-generation MDP. Theorem 1 proves that walk exploration achieves equivalent node coverage to connected subgraph generation. The output model encodes the generated walk sequences via a separate shallow MPNN and MLP for the downstream task. Training alternates between the sampling model (RL reward = reduction in downstream loss) and the output model (supervised loss), without any explicit interpretability loss function. Key Results: On seven graph classification benchmarks, RWE-SGNN achieves top-2 accuracy across all datasets and ranks first on MUTAG (97.31±4.35%), PTC-MR (75.55±6.87%), BZR (93.34±3.13%), and COX2 (88.65±2.69%), outperforming strong baselines including GIN, GraphSNN, DSS-GNN, and SUGAR. On explainability (Edge AUC), RWE-SGNN achieves 0.918±0.018 on BA-2motifs and 0.895±0.054 on Mutagenicity, surpassing all post-hoc explainers including GFlowExplainer (0.854 and 0.882 respectively)—without using any explainer-specific loss. Substructure generation time is ~45–51ms for RWE-SGNN vs. ~120–136ms for the subgraph-based variant (RWE-SGNN(S)), confirming the ~3× speedup predicted by the complexity analysis. Visualization on MUTAG correctly recovers the NO₂ functional group as mutagenicity-discriminating, and on BA-2motifs correctly isolates circle and house motifs. Strengths: - The unification of SGNN expressiveness and intrinsic interpretability in a single framework is technically clean: the RL reward signal directly ties subgraph selection to downstream task performance, so interpretability emerges for free without auxiliary losses. - The walk-based MDP has a formal theoretical guarantee (Theorem 1) of equivalent node coverage to connected subgraph generation, grounding the efficiency-expressiveness tradeoff rigorously. - Empirical results are convincing on both accuracy and explainability fronts simultaneously, which is rare—most methods trade one for the other. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are limited to binary graph classification on small-to-medium TUDataset benchmarks; scalability to large graphs (e.g., OGB-scale) with millions of nodes is untested and the walk-based MDP's serial generation may not parallelize easily. - The walk-based MDP inherently produces path-like substructures; dense or highly branched motifs (e.g., cliques, star graphs with many arms) require extensive backtracking, and the paper's mitigation (shallow MPNN graphlet encoder) is acknowledged but not rigorously quantified in terms of coverage loss. - The two-stage alternating optimization (RL agent vs. output model) introduces training instability, and hyperparameter sensitivity (trajectory length L, sample number K, DQN smoothing factor β) is only partially ablated on one dataset (NCI109), with supplementary results not fully discussed in the main paper.
Search Results
#65 — Innovative (68)

Language Models and Logic Programs for Trustworthy Tax Reasoning

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The break-even price metric grounded in IRS penalty law is a genuinely novel evaluation framework that moves beyond accuracy to model real deployment economics. The empirical discovery that reasoning models underperform chat models at few-shot semantic parsing is surprising and has implications beyond tax. Limited by evaluation on only 100 SARA cases covering 9 simplified tax sections, leaving real-world generalization open.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Tax filing requires complex statutory reasoning — applying overlapping rules, numerical calculations, and eligibility tests — where errors incur real financial penalties. Pure LLM approaches are ill-suited because they lack auditability (chain-of-thought is not faithful to the actual computation) and cannot reliably refuse when uncertain. The paper targets the gap between LLM fluency and the reliability/transparency requirements of high-stakes legal-financial domains, motivated partly by the equity argument that better automated tax assistance disproportionately benefits lower-income filers who are frequently audited. Methodology: The system integrates LLMs with a SWI-Prolog symbolic solver under three increasingly structured paradigms: (1) Direct calculation — plain-text statutes and case facts fed to an LLM for end-to-end answer generation; (2) Zero-shot parsing — LLM generates a Prolog program from plain-text statutes and case facts, which the symbolic engine executes; failure to execute triggers abstention; (3) Few-shot parsing with gold statutes — LLM parses only the case facts into Prolog (statute rules are pre-translated by humans), conditioned on intelligently retrieved exemplar cases with gold Prolog translations. Exemplar retrieval uses instruction-tuned retrievers (Promptriever) reranked by OpenAI o4-mini at test time. Self-consistency checks — accepting an answer only when two independent reasoning paths agree — add a further selectivity layer. A novel economic metric, "break-even price," is introduced: the minimum per-filing charge at which a deploying organization would remain solvent, incorporating IRS §6662 understatement penalties (20% of underpaid amount), overstatement costs, and a $270 abstention fee matching the IRS-reported average human filing cost. Experiments span four model families (Qwen2.5-32B, Llama-3.3-70B, DeepSeek-V3/R1-671B, GPT-4.1/o3) plus GPT-5, evaluated on the 100 numerical cases in the SARA dataset. Key Results: Across all settings, neuro-symbolic methods outperform pure LLM baselines on the break-even metric. The best system without gold statutes is DeepSeek-R1 with "Direct + Direct" self-consistency ($94.20 break-even vs. $270 average human cost). The best system with gold statutes is GPT-5 few-shot ($15.78 break-even, >94% reduction vs. the $270 human baseline). A striking finding is the chat-vs.-reasoning model inversion: reasoning models (R1, o3) dominate at direct solving and zero-shot Prolog parsing, but chat models (GPT-4.1, DeepSeek-V3) dramatically outperform their reasoning counterparts at few-shot case parsing when gold statute Prolog is provided (e.g., GPT-4.1 Few-Shot achieves 87 correct vs. o3 Few-Shot's 81, with far fewer errors). The hypothesis is that long chain-of-thought training causes reasoning models to emulate explicit Prolog-like steps rather than accurately map surface text to symbolic form. Case length moderately correlates with error rate (R=0.4), with longer cases being systematically harder. Strengths: - Novel and practically grounded economic evaluation framework (break-even price) that goes beyond accuracy to model real deployment costs under IRS penalty law, enabling direct comparison to the human baseline. - Clean empirical discovery of the chat/reasoning model inversion, which has implications well beyond tax reasoning for any semantic parsing task where exemplars are available. - Symbolic solver provides genuine auditability — the derivation is a deterministic Prolog execution trace, unlike chain-of-thought which is demonstrably unfaithful to model internals. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The few-shot gold-statutes setting requires substantial one-time human labor to translate all statute sections into Prolog — a strong assumption that limits immediate generalizability to new tax codes or legal domains without that investment. - Evaluation is confined to SARA, which covers only 9 moderately simplified, self-contained US tax code sections; real-world tax law is far more interconnected, ambiguous, and dynamic, and no generalization experiments are conducted. - The break-even cost model excludes LLM inference costs (argued marginal) and assumes the deploying organization bears full liability; both assumptions may not hold in practice, and the liability/negligence question for AI-assisted filing is unresolved legally.
Recommended
#66 — Innovative (68)

A Subgoal-driven Framework for Improving Long-Horizon LLM Agents (13 ▲ )

Taiyi Wang, Sian Gooding, Florian Hartmann, Oriana Riva, Edward Grefenstette · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The dual-critic architecture with potential-based shaping from subgoal milestones is a well-engineered solution to the dense reward problem in web agent RL, and the rigorous automated failure analysis (92.5% human agreement) provides unusually strong empirical motivation. However, the core ideas — subgoal decomposition, potential-based shaping, process reward models — are established RL techniques recombined rather than fundamentally new, and the hard dependency on Gemini-2.5-Pro as teacher limits practical independence.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-based web agents fail systematically on long-horizon tasks due to two compounding problems: (1) during inference, agents lose track of progress and enter non-productive loops ('Get Stuck Midway' accounts for 42–49% of all failures across baselines); (2) during RL fine-tuning, sparse binary rewards make credit assignment nearly impossible across multi-step trajectories. Existing approaches—SFT on demonstrations, process reward models, and hierarchical RL—each address part of the problem but introduce brittleness, latent-space opacity, or reward over-optimization. The paper targets both failure modes simultaneously through explicit subgoal milestoning. Methodology: The framework has two complementary components. The first, Gemini-SGO, integrates inference-time planning: a teacher model (Gemini-2.5-Pro) generates 4-step subgoal decompositions from task intent and an initial screenshot, then the executing agent performs retrospective reflection at every step, answering three questions (milestones achieved, current subgoal status, next milestone). This creates an explicit belief-state vector that prevents hallucinated progress and enables mid-trajectory correction. The second component, MiRA (Milestoning your Reinforcement Learning Enhanced Agent), addresses RL training. A dual-critic architecture trains a standard goal-conditioned value critic V_φ on binary terminal outcomes and a novel potential critic P_ψ on dense progress labels derived from subgoal completion vectors. Progress labels are computed by linear interpolation between key subgoal-completion timesteps, converting discrete milestone events into a continuous [0,1] signal. The auxiliary shaping reward r^(aux) = α(P_ψ(s_{t+1}) − P_ψ(s_t)) is added to environment rewards before advantage computation. Policy updates use an MSE regression objective on log-probability ratios (rather than KL divergence), enabling off-policy learning from replay buffers with bidirectional probability adjustment.
