Advantage estimation combines 1-step TD error with Monte Carlo returns (doubly-robust estimator). Training proceeds iteratively: each phase collects rollouts, processes them through subgoal annotation and perplexity filtering (rank score [0.5, 0.9]), then updates all three networks, with failure-driven curriculum generation resampling hard tasks for the next phase. Key Results: On WebArena-Lite (165 tasks across 5 domains), Gemma3-12B + MiRA achieves 43.0% success rate, surpassing WebRL (38.4% on Llama-3.1-8B, 35.1% on Gemma3), DigiRL (33.3%), and proprietary models GPT-4-Turbo (17.6%), GPT-4o (13.9%), and Gemini-2.5-Pro (23.0%). Gemini-2.5-Pro-SGO reaches 32.1% (+9.1pp over base). Strongest per-domain gains appear on Gitlab (56.7%) and Shopping Admin (54.3%), where procedural dependencies most reward dense intermediate feedback. The automated failure analyzer confirms MiRA reduces 'Get Stuck Midway' errors from ~33% (Gemma-SFT) to ~21%, outperforming WebRL (~25%). Ablations confirm each component is necessary: removing the potential critic plateaus at ~35%; using KL instead of MSE reaches only ~33% by Phase 6; removing doubly-robust advantage estimation causes early-phase collapse to ~25%. The optimal shaping factor α=0.3 and perplexity filter band [1/0.9, 1/0.5] were validated by grid search. Subgoal AUROC of 0.84 and strict monotonicity (Kendall's τ=0.46, p<0.001) confirm the generated subgoals are reliable progress proxies. Strengths: - Rigorous empirical failure analysis motivates design choices: the automated analyzer with 92.5% human agreement provides a scientific basis for the milestoning hypothesis rather than just engineering intuition. - The dual-critic architecture cleanly separates dense auxiliary shaping from the true optimization target, avoiding reward misalignment; restricting shaping to positive traces ensures statistical correlation between subgoal completion and task success. - The MSE regression objective for policy updates is a principled improvement over KL-based methods in off-policy settings, with clear theoretical grounding and empirical validation showing ~10pp advantage over the KL variant. - Solid ablation coverage across five design dimensions (potential critic, policy loss, advantage estimator, shaping magnitude, perplexity filtering) with consistent results across 3–5 runs. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The subgoal generator (Gemini-2.5-Pro) and subgoal checker introduce a hard dependency on a powerful proprietary teacher model at both training and inference time; the cost and latency of this are not fully quantified, and the approach may not generalize to domains where Gemini lacks strong visual reasoning. - Evaluation is limited to WebArena-Lite (165 tasks), a curated subset chosen partly for evaluation convenience (~40 min vs. 6 hours for the full benchmark); generalization to the full 812-task suite, other web benchmarks (OSWorld, AndroidWorld), or tasks beyond web navigation is not demonstrated. - The fixed 4-subgoal decomposition is a simplifying assumption that may be poorly calibrated for very short or very long tasks; the paper acknowledges this but does not address non-uniform subgoal difficulty or adaptive granularity. - The increase in Wrong Termination errors (~21% → ~31%) after MiRA training, attributed partly to AutoRater limitations in verifying semantic correctness, suggests the evaluation pipeline itself may be noisy and that reported success rates conflate navigation capability with answer quality.
Recommended 2603.19685 PDF
#67 — Innovative (68)

UniGRPO: Unified Policy Optimization for Reasoning-Driven Visual Generation (29 ▲ )

Jie Liu, Zilyu Ye, Linxiao Yuan, Shenhan Zhu, Yu Gao, Jie Wu, Kunchang Li, Xionghui Wang, Xiaonan Nie, Weilin Huang, Wanli Ouyang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The unified MDP formulation treating autoregressive reasoning and flow-matching denoising as a single sequential decision process is a natural but non-obvious extension that enables end-to-end reward propagation across modalities. The velocity-MSE regularization closing the timestep-dependent loophole and CFG elimination during training are practical, well-ablated contributions. Evaluation narrowness (one internal benchmark, GenEval only) and unvalidated multi-round claims temper the impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Unified multimodal models capable of interleaved text-and-image generation are an emerging paradigm, but no principled reinforcement learning framework exists to jointly optimize both the language reasoning and visual synthesis components end-to-end. Prior work either fine-tunes diffusion models in isolation (ReFL, FlowGRPO) or trains text reasoning separately from image generation, creating a mismatch between the reasoning policy and the visual reward signal. As the community converges on autoregressive models for text paired with flow matching for images, a unified RL training recipe that spans both modalities — and scales to multi-round, multi-condition generation — is needed. Methodology: UniGRPO formulates the full "Prompt → Thinking → Image" pipeline as a single Markov Decision Process with sparse terminal rewards on the final generated image. The state space spans two sequential phases: a discrete token-prediction phase (text reasoning) and a continuous denoising phase (flow matching). Both phases are optimized jointly using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO): standard GRPO for the autoregressive reasoning tokens and FlowGRPO for the visual synthesis policy, with the combined objective J = J_Text + λJ_Flow (λ=1). The backbone is the Bagel unified model after supervised fine-tuning on an internal dataset. Two critical modifications to FlowGRPO are introduced for scalability: (1) Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) is eliminated during training to enforce linear, unbranched rollouts — essential for multi-turn and multi-condition settings where CFG would require exponentially branching computation graphs; prompt adherence is instead internalized directly into the policy weights via reward maximization. (2) The standard latent KL penalty is replaced with an unweighted MSE penalty on the predicted velocity fields (L_MSE = ||v_θ - v_ref||²), removing the timestep-dependent 1/σ² weighting that creates temporal loopholes exploitable by reward hacking. RatioNorm from GRPO-Guard is also adopted to correct the systematic left-shift in importance ratios that undermines clipping in flow models. Key Results: UniGRPO achieves state-of-the-art results among all evaluated methods. On the internal Text Alignment (TA) benchmark (150 prompts, VLM-graded), UniGRPO scores 0.8381 versus 0.8208 for FlowGRPO-with-thinking and 0.8078 for TextGRPO alone — demonstrating that jointly optimizing both policies outperforms either in isolation. On GenEval (compositional text-to-image benchmark), UniGRPO reaches 0.90 overall, compared to 0.88 for both FlowGRPO-without-thinking and TextGRPO, with particular gains in object counting (0.91) and two-object scenes (0.99). The competing UniFPO (FPO-based unified framework) fails to converge entirely, confirming the stability advantage of the GRPO formulation. Ablation studies confirm: (a) CFG-free training achieves comparable or better evaluation performance while enabling linear rollouts; (b) velocity-MSE regularization prevents reward hacking whereas removing KL leads to degraded image quality and latent KL regularization still produces grid artifacts as early as step 250. Strengths: - The unified MDP formulation is technically clean and principled: treating token prediction and denoising steps as a single sequential decision process with sparse terminal rewards is a natural extension of existing GRPO machinery. - The velocity-MSE regularization is well-motivated: removing the problematic 1/σ² timestep weighting that creates exploitable loopholes is a concrete, empirically validated improvement over prior FlowGRPO. - CFG elimination during training is both practically useful (linear compute scaling) and empirically validated to match CFG-trained performance at evaluation, challenging the assumption that CFG is necessary during RL fine-tuning. - Comprehensive baselines covering ReFL, FPO, FlowGRPO, TextGRPO, and hybrid sequential methods, with ablations cleanly isolating individual design choices. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework is validated only on single-round generation (Prompt → Think → Image); the claimed scalability to multi-round interleaved generation is entirely prospective and unvalidated in the paper. - The reward model used (InternVL fine-tuned on user preference data from RewardDance) is differentiable and task-specific; generalization to verifier-based or non-differentiable rewards — highlighted as a GRPO advantage — is not demonstrated empirically. - Evaluation relies on one internal benchmark (TA, 150 prompts) that is not publicly reproducible, and GenEval, while standard, is a relatively narrow compositional benchmark; broader aesthetic and diversity metrics are absent. - The base model (Bagel after internal SFT) is not publicly available in the exact form used, limiting reproducibility and fair comparison with concurrent methods.
Recommended 2603.23500 PDF
#68 — Innovative (68)

SpecEyes: Accelerating Agentic Multimodal LLMs via Speculative Perception and Planning (42 ▲ )

Haoyu Huang, Jinfa Huang, Zhongwei Wan, Xiawu Zheng, Rongrong Ji, Jiebo Luo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Mar 2026
Why this rank: SpecEyes makes a creative conceptual leap by lifting speculative execution from the token level to the full agentic tool-use loop, and the answer separability score is a principled, scale-invariant confidence metric grounded in risk theory. The finding that bypassing unnecessary tool invocations can simultaneously improve both speed and accuracy is genuinely surprising and practically valuable.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Agentic multimodal large language models (MLLMs) such as OpenAI o3 and Gemini Agentic Vision achieve strong visual reasoning by iteratively invoking perception tools (zoom-in, crop, OCR) in multi-step loops. However, each tool call depends causally on the prior step's output, creating a strictly serial Markovian chain the authors term "agentic depth" D. This serialization causes two compounding problems: (1) end-to-end query latency scales linearly with D, and (2) per-query stateful execution prevents GPU batching across queries, collapsing system-level concurrency. Prior acceleration work—token-level speculative decoding, multimodal token pruning, KV-cache compression—all operate within this fixed sequential loop and do not question whether the loop itself is needed for every query. The gap is a method that can speculatively bypass entire tool-use chains for queries that do not require them. Methodology: SpecEyes lifts speculative execution from the token level to the agentic level via a four-phase heterogeneous pipeline. Phase I uses the large agentic model M_L in a lightweight binary classification mode (single token, no tools) to judge whether a query requires tool assistance (screening ratio β). Queries flagged as tool-free proceed to Phase II, where a small stateless non-agentic model M_S (Qwen3-VL-2B) generates speculative answers in parallel across the batch. Phase III applies cognitive gating via a novel answer separability score S_sep: for each generated token, the top logit is z-scored against the mean and standard deviation of the top-K competing logits, giving a scale-invariant, calibration-free confidence signal. The minimum token-level separability across the answer is used as the answer-level score (motivated by a risk-theoretic union-bound argument), and answers above threshold τ are accepted immediately (acceptance rate α), bypassing the agentic pipeline entirely. Below-threshold queries fall back to Phase IV, the full stateful agentic loop. The heterogeneous parallel funnel runs Phases I–II as stateless batch-parallelizable forward passes, so the dominant serving cost becomes the (1−βα) fraction that requires agentic fallback, yielding throughput speedup ≈1/(1−βα). The threshold τ is calibrated offline in ~5–10 minutes by sampling M_S's empirical confidence distribution on a small held-out set. Key Results: On V Bench, HR-Bench, and POPE with DeepEyes as the agentic backbone and Qwen3-VL-2B as the speculative model, SpecEyes (min aggregation) achieves 1.73× average wall-clock speedup while improving average accuracy from 81.39% to 84.26%. POPE benefits most (2.13–2.19×) with accuracy gains of ~7 points on the Adversarial split (78.43%→85.13%), indicating that bypassing spurious tool invocations reduces hallucinations. V Relative Position accuracy improves from 82.89% to 89.47% at 1.90×. HR-Bench is the bottleneck (1.08–1.13×), as high-resolution queries genuinely require tool assistance, suppressing both β and α. With Thyme as the backbone, SpecEyes yields 1.42× average speedup and 82.29%→83.99% accuracy. The competing baseline SpecReason consistently slows inference (0.37–0.61× with DeepEyes) and severely degrades POPE accuracy (as low as 49.10%), because it lacks tool-calling structure and adds 414 tokens and 3.48 extra turns on average. Ablations confirm that the gating threshold is a smooth Pareto knob, larger batch sizes monotonically improve throughput (with diminishing returns as the agentic fallback dominates), and K=64 is a balanced default for separability computation. Strengths: - The conceptual elevation of speculative decoding from token-level to the full agentic loop is clean and well-motivated; the stateful bottleneck is formally characterized with latency and throughput bounds. - The answer separability score is principled: it is scale-invariant (unlike softmax probability), explicitly models the competitive margin among top candidates, and is backed by a risk-theoretic proposition showing min-aggregation is the tightest upper bound on answer-level error probability. - System design is pragmatic—no retraining of either model, no oracle labels at inference time, ~5–10 minute offline calibration, and the improvement in accuracy (not just speed preservation) on several benchmarks is a notable finding that bypassing unnecessary tool use can reduce compounding errors. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The speculative model operates at agentic depth D=0 (fully tool-free), providing no benefit on benchmarks like HR-Bench 8K where most queries genuinely require tool use; the sub-1× speedup on HR-Bench 8K with Thyme shows the overhead of running M_S can exceed savings in low-bypass regimes. - Phase I (tool-use judgment) is executed by the expensive large model M_L, adding a non-trivial fixed cost per query that partially offsets gains; using M_L as the judge rather than a cheaper classifier is a design choice not ablated. - Evaluation is limited to three benchmarks and two agentic backbones, all on a single A100; there is no analysis of how speedups scale with larger agentic depths D or with more capable/larger speculative models, and no multi-GPU or production-serving evaluation.
Recommended 2603.23483 PDF
#69 — Innovative (68)

DA-Flow: Degradation-Aware Optical Flow Estimation with Diffusion Models (35 ▲ )

Jaewon Min, Jaeeun Lee, Yeji Choi, Paul Hyunbin Cho, Jin Hyeon Kim, Tae-Young Lee, Jongsik Ahn, Hwayeong Lee, Seonghyun Park, Seungryong Kim · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Mar 2026
Why this rank: DA-Flow defines degradation-aware optical flow as a new task and offers a technically well-motivated solution: lifting a pretrained image restoration DiT to video via spatio-temporal attention rather than adopting a monolithic video diffusion backbone. The 32% EPE reduction on Sintel over strong baselines is substantial, and the principled layer-selection analysis validates the design. However, the reliance on synthetic degradations and pseudo ground-truth from existing flow models limits the demonstrated scope.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Optical flow models trained on clean synthetic data fail catastrophically when applied to real-world videos corrupted by blur, noise, compression artifacts, or low resolution. The problem is fundamentally ill-posed: corruptions destroy fine textures and attenuate motion boundaries, leaving insufficient visual evidence for reliable matching. Prior work (RobustSpring) benchmarked this brittleness but offered no solution. Simply augmenting training data with synthetic corruptions is insufficient because what is needed are representations that simultaneously encode degradation patterns and preserve spatial structure for dense matching. This paper introduces Degradation-Aware Optical Flow as a new task and proposes DA-Flow to address it. Methodology: The core insight is that intermediate features of image restoration diffusion models are inherently degradation-aware but lack temporal reasoning. The authors start from DiT4SR, a pretrained DiT-based image restoration model (MM-DiT architecture), and "lift" it to video by replacing per-frame spatial attention with full spatio-temporal MM-Attention across all layers. This reshapes the frame-batch tensor so every token attends to all spatial tokens across all frames, enabling inter-frame correspondence without collapsing per-frame spatial structure (which video diffusion models do problematically via 3D convolutions). The lifted model is finetuned on YouHQ (38,576 YouTube videos) using paired LQ/HQ frames generated by the Real-ESRGAN degradation pipeline plus video compression. Feature analysis reveals that query/key features from selected spatio-temporal attention layers (layers 3, 13, 16, 17) exhibit strong zero-shot geometric correspondence under corruption. DA-Flow then builds on RAFT's iterative refinement framework: the lifted model's coarse (1/16 resolution) query/key features are upsampled via DPT heads and concatenated channel-wise with conventional RAFT CNN encoder features, forming a hybrid representation for the correlation volume and context conditioning. Training uses pseudo ground-truth flow generated by applying SEA-RAFT to HQ frame pairs, with LQ frames as input. Two-stage training: stage 1 finetunes the lifted diffusion model; stage 2 freezes it and trains the flow head. Key Results: On Sintel, DA-Flow achieves EPE of 6.912 vs. 10.185 (SEA-RAFT) and 10.241 (FlowSeek) — a ~32% reduction. On Spring, EPE drops from 2.703 (SEA-RAFT) to 2.207, with 1px outlier rate falling from 41.51% to 30.95%. On TartanAir, DA-Flow achieves the best outlier rates at all thresholds (72.35% at 1px vs. 75.17% for RAFT), though EPE is slightly higher than FlowSeek (8.866 vs. 7.694), attributed to a small number of large-displacement outlier pixels. Ablation confirms that finetuning (lifting) is critical — unfinetuned full-attention features are substantially worse, especially on Sintel (EPE 7.41 vs. 7.02 at step 0). DPT upsampling and CNN encoder are both necessary; removing either degrades performance. DA-Flow also outperforms a finetuned RAFT baseline (RAFT*) trained on identical data (EPE 6.912 vs. 7.033), demonstrating the diffusion priors add value beyond domain adaptation alone. Applied to video restoration as a temporal consistency aid (combined with MGLD), DA-Flow achieves best warping error (3.483×10⁻³ vs. 4.532 for MGLD alone) alongside improved PSNR (23.47 vs. 22.50). Strengths: - The lifting strategy — extending a pretrained image restoration DiT with spatio-temporal attention rather than adopting a monolithic video diffusion backbone — is technically well-motivated: it avoids temporal latent collapse that makes video diffusion features ill-suited for pairwise frame matching, while being more compute-efficient. - The empirical feature analysis is thorough and principled: layer-wise and timestep-wise EPE comparisons justify feature selection choices, and the lifted features are shown to be stable across denoising steps while the baseline is highly sensitive, providing reliable features regardless of extraction timing. - The pseudo ground-truth training strategy cleanly sidesteps the impossibility of obtaining GT optical flow for real degraded videos, and the application to video restoration temporal consistency (Section C.4) demonstrates practical downstream value beyond the core benchmark results. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Inference requires 10 denoising steps through a large DiT backbone, making DA-Flow substantially slower than RAFT/SEA-RAFT/FlowSeek; no runtime numbers are reported, making the computational cost impossible to assess quantitatively. - Evaluation is entirely on synthetically degraded benchmarks (applying Real-ESRGAN + video compression to clean datasets); no evaluation on genuinely in-the-wild corrupted footage is provided, leaving open whether performance transfers to real sensor noise, motion blur, or codec artifacts with different statistics than the training degradation pipeline. - The pseudo ground-truth training signal (SEA-RAFT applied to HQ frames) introduces label noise for fast or occluded motions where SEA-RAFT itself fails; this ceiling effect is not quantified and likely limits accuracy in the hardest motion regimes.
Recommended 2603.23499 PDF
#70 — Innovative (68)

Are Language Models Any Good at Density Modeling?

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 68 for an elegant experimental design that fills a genuine gap: creating a synthetic language with known ground-truth distributions to directly measure LLM density modeling fidelity. The syntactic-vs-semantic decomposition of learnability and the substring bias discovery are clean conceptual contributions. However, the vast simplicity gap between the 6.3M-parameter model on 2M numbers and real LLMs on natural language limits the generalizability of conclusions.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large Language Models are increasingly used as proxies for the probability distribution of natural language — e.g., for corpus-level linguistic analyses, simulating human writers, or estimating sentence frequencies. This rests on the untested assumption that pre-trained LLMs are accurate density models. The problem is that no ground truth distribution exists for natural language, making it impossible to directly measure how well a model captures the true distribution. Prior work (Meister & Cotterell 2021; Ilia & Aziz 2024) has raised doubts using indirect corpus metrics or human comparisons, but none have directly measured distribution fidelity against a known target. Methodology: The authors create a controlled synthetic language consisting of all integers from 0 to 2,000,000 written out in English words (e.g., "forty six thousand seven hundred and seven"). This language has genuine syntactic and semantic complexity analogous to natural language, while being orderable on a single numeric axis, enabling well-defined probability mass functions. A GPT-2-style transformer (6.3M parameters, 8 decoder layers, 4 attention heads, embedding dim 256) is trained from scratch on 10% of this language (i.i.d. sampled) under various target distributions: uniform, linear, binomial, sinusoidal with period 3, and sinusoidal with period 10. The learned distribution P_model is compared to the true distribution P_true via KL divergence (using a low-variance unbiased estimator) and visual inspection of PMF/CMF curves. A secondary experiment tests "passage mode" where numbers are grouped into multi-element sequences, probing whether sentence-position context affects learned probabilities. Key Results: Models learn syntactically correct numbers with <1% error rate quickly, regardless of training data fraction. For distributions learnable from syntactic patterns (uniform, linear, binomial, sinusoidal-period-10), the model achieves near-perfect alignment: KL divergence drops from 0.23 to 0.004 for uniform (98.33% improvement), from 0.39 to 0.029 for linear (92.51% improvement), and from 0.43 to 0.007 for sinusoidal-period-10 (98.36% improvement). However, for sinusoidal-period-3 — whose phase cannot be read off any syntactic suffix — the model completely fails, with KL divergence actually increasing from 0.688 to 0.732 (−6.43%). The model collapses to approximately uniform rather than learning divisibility-by-3 semantics. A strong "substring bias" is identified: numbers like "one" and multiples of ten receive disproportionately high probabilities because they appear as substrings in large fractions of the corpus (e.g., "hundred" appears in >99.99% of all numbers), and the model fails to discount these co-occurrences from contextually isolated appearances. Passage-mode experiments confirm that first-position probabilities in a passage closely mirror the standalone trained distribution, supporting prior work's assumption that sentence-level probabilities can be treated as context-independent. Strengths: - Elegant experimental design: the synthetic number language is a principled controlled testbed that allows ground-truth distribution comparison — a gap no prior work filled directly. - The syntactic vs. semantic decomposition of distribution learnability is a clean and insightful conceptual contribution that explains why models succeed or fail. - The substring bias discovery is a concrete, well-evidenced failure mode with direct implications for downstream NLP tasks (e.g., answer-preference bias in QA models). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The synthetic language (2M numbers, 37-word vocabulary, 6.3M parameter model) is vastly simpler than real LLMs trained on natural language; the generalizability of findings — particularly the degree of substring bias — to billion-parameter models on diverse corpora is asserted but not demonstrated. - The paper only tests one-dimensional, numeric distributions; natural language has high-dimensional, compositional structure that may exhibit qualitatively different density modeling failure modes not captured here. - The "passage mode" experiment that validates prior work's assumption is done on the same synthetic language, so it provides only weak indirect evidence for the original natural-language claim.
Recommended
#71 — Innovative (67)

Predictive Regularization Against Visual Representation Degradation in Multimodal Large Language Models

Enguang Wang, Qiang Wang, Yuanchen Wu, Ke Yan, Xinbin Yuan, Shouhong Ding, Xialei Liu, Ming-Ming Cheng · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scores 67 for its rigorous multi-level diagnostic establishing that language-centric training systematically degrades visual representations in MLLMs — a phenomenon not previously characterized at this depth. The four-pronged analysis (linear probing, patch cohesion/coupling, statistical geometry, training dynamics) across multiple architectures is methodologically thorough. The proposed fix is elegantly lightweight (zero inference overhead, +0.045% training FLOPs), though gains are modest and inconsistent across configurations.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) fine-tune almost exclusively through a language-centric next-token prediction objective. This creates a fundamental asymmetry: visual representations are not optimized for their own fidelity but are progressively transformed to serve text generation. Prior work analyzing MLLM internals focused on how well visual features serve language tasks, leaving the intrinsic visual quality of intermediate representations unexplored. This paper asks: what is the cost of purely language-driven training on visual representations? The question matters because a robust MLLM needs both strong cross-modal reasoning and fine-grained visual perception. Methodology: The paper first conducts a multi-level diagnostic to establish the degradation phenomenon. (1) Global functional degradation: linear probing experiments on frozen intermediate-layer features across four MLLM architectures (Qwen2.5-VL-7B, LLaVA-1.5-7B, LLaVA-1.6-7B, InternVL-3.5-4B) and two datasets (CUB-200, ImageNet-100) reveal a consistent classification accuracy drop in mid-layers relative to the initial projected visual features. (2) Patch structure degradation: using COCO-Stuff segmentation masks, the paper measures Intra-Object Cohesion and Inter-Object Coupling across layers; the Semantic Contrast Ratio (cohesion/coupling) consistently declines, meaning semantic boundaries between objects blur. (3) Statistical confirmation: PCA effective dimension peaks and mean off-diagonal feature correlation drops in mid-layers, indicating the LLM deliberately builds a high-capacity, statistically independent representation workspace for language—at the cost of local visual structure. (4) Causal evidence: tracking linear probe accuracy and GQA accuracy through pre-training shows a clear inverse correlation, confirming degradation is a systematic byproduct of language optimization. To counteract this, the authors propose PRe (Predictive Regularization): a lightweight 2-layer MLP prediction head applied to degraded mid-layer visual patch tokens, trained to predict the stop-gradient anchor features at the LLM input. The total loss is L_total = L_LM + λ·L_PRe with λ=0.5. The PRe head is discarded at inference, incurring zero inference overhead. Key Results: PRe consistently improves downstream vision-language performance across diverse architectural combinations. For Vicuna-7B + frozen CLIP, PRe yields: GQA +0.7, AI2D +1.7, MMStar +4.3, OCRbench +11, TextVQA +1.1. For Qwen2.5-7B + SigLIP2 (trainable), PRe yields: GQA +0.9, MMMU +0.4, RealWorldQA +1.6, OCRbench +15. Benefits generalize to 3B LLMs and to stronger encoders and higher resolutions (672²). Ablations confirm that mid-layer regularization outperforms last-layer (which causes regressions because deep layers deliberately collapse visual tokens to semantically inert high-frequency tokens), and patch-level regularization outperforms global feature regularization due to richer spatial supervision. Using internal Pre-LLM features as anchors outperforms external foundation models (DINOv2, SAM) due to representational alignment. Training overhead is negligible at +0.045% PFLOPs and -0.85% throughput. Strengths: - Thorough multi-level diagnostic (global linear probe, patch cohesion/coupling, statistical geometry, training dynamics) that rigorously establishes the degradation phenomenon across multiple architectures and datasets before proposing a fix. - Exceptionally lightweight solution: a 2-layer MLP regularizer with zero inference overhead and negligible training cost (+0.045% PFLOPs), making it trivially applicable to any LLaVA-style MLLM. - Principled design choices validated by ablation: mid-layer vs. last-layer targeting is explained mechanistically via logit-lens analysis revealing visual token silencing in deep layers. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Gains are modest and inconsistent across configurations: several settings show regressions (e.g., Qwen+CLIP frozen on MMMU −0.7, Qwen+SigLIP on MMStar −0.2, MMVP −0.7), suggesting the method is not universally reliable and the effect size may not consistently justify the added complexity. - The causal framing of degradation as a 'visual sacrifice' rests primarily on correlational evidence (inverse training dynamics); the paper does not rule out that the degradation is simply an emergent property of layer specialization unrelated to a deliberate trade-off, and does not test interventions that would disentangle the two. - Evaluation is confined to LLaVA-1.5/1.6-style two-stage training; the method has not been validated on more modern architectures with dynamic resolution (e.g., InternVL2, Qwen2.5-VL with NaViT-style processing) where patch merging and variable token counts complicate the anchor alignment scheme.
Citations 2603.20808 PDF
#72 — Innovative (67)

Leveraging LLM-GNN Integration for Open-World Question Answering over Knowledge Graphs

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: GLOW makes a meaningful contribution through its training-free decoupled GNN-LLM architecture and the new GLOW-Bench benchmark that rigorously tests open-world KGQA with answers explicitly removed from the KG. The fallback mechanism providing robustness when GNNs are weak is a practical design insight. However, the per-pattern GNN training requirement limits scalability, and the substantial 1-hop vs. 2-hop performance gap (83% vs. 42%) indicates the core multi-hop challenge remains largely unsolved.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Traditional Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA) assumes a closed-world setting where answers must exist as explicit paths in the KG. This fails in real-world domains like biomedicine, finance, and scientific research where KGs are incomplete, evolving, or have missing links. Retrieval-based methods (GNN-RAG, G-Retriever, RoG, GCR) fail under open-world conditions because they require complete answer paths. The closest prior work, AskGNN, addresses open-world QA via GNN-guided in-context learning but relies on structural embeddings without semantic grounding, struggles with multi-hop questions, and requires joint GNN–LLM training that scales poorly. Methodology: GLOW (Graph-LLM for Open-World QA) is a training-free hybrid system combining a pre-trained GNN with an LLM through a concept the authors call "In-Structure Learning." The pipeline has four stages: (1) Question Understanding & Linking — entity extraction followed by schema-aware LLM-based entity-relation linking via SPARQL to map question entities and predicates to KG URIs; (2) KG Retrieval — SPARQL-generated 1-hop neighborhood triples (RC) around the question entity, excluding the gold answer; (3) Augmentation — GNN-predicted top-k candidate answers (using per-pattern RGCN models trained with GraphSAINT on task-oriented subgraphs via KGTOSA sampling) serialized into the prompt alongside RC triples and candidate label lists; (4) Generation — an LLM selects the answer from the structured prompt, evaluated via an LLM-as-a-Judge module supporting exact match and hierarchical match. Three variants are studied: GLOW-G (KG context only), GLOW-N (GNN predictions only), and GLOW-GN (combined). The GNN trains independently from the LLM in ~1.7 hours, compared to AskGNN's ~23.4 hours of joint training. The approach requires no fine-tuning of the LLM and generalizes across LLM architectures. The authors also introduce GLOW-Bench, a new benchmark of 1,000 questions spanning 25 templates across four KGs (YAGO4, BioKG, LinkedMDB, CrunchBase), covering 1- and 2-hop reasoning with answers explicitly removed. Key Results: GLOW-GN consistently outperforms all baselines across five LLMs (Qwen3-8B, DeepSeek-R1-7B, Granite-3.3-8B, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-V3) and all datasets. On AskGNN's existing benchmark datasets (arxiv2023, ogbn-arxiv, ogbn-products), GLOW-GN with Qwen3-8B scores 81%, 77%, and 57% vs. AskGNN's 62%, 70%, and 29% respectively — an average 18% gain. On GLOW-Bench 1-hop questions, GLOW-GN reaches 83.3% vs. AskGNN's 79.4%; on 2-hop questions, 42.4% vs. 34.3%. Against GCR, GLOW-GN improves by 64%, 49%, and 27% on the three existing datasets. Against GoG, improvements are 20%, 35%, and 13%. GLOW-GN also demonstrates robustness when GNNs are weak: on questions where GNN accuracy is below 50%, GLOW-GN recovers up to 12% over the raw GNN, while AskGNN often matches or underperforms the GNN alone. Exact match accuracy of 66.6% and hierarchical match of 78.5% align closely with manual verification at 82.4%, with hierarchical matching recovering 95.2% of manually verified correct answers. Optimal performance occurs at top-K=3 GNN candidates; increasing K degrades LLM accuracy. GLOW-GN uses only 0.65K tokens per prompt vs. GoG's 1.5K, and trains in 1.7 hours vs. AskGNN's 23.4 hours. Strengths: - The decoupled GNN–LLM design avoids joint training, dramatically reducing training time (1.7h vs. 23.4h) while remaining plug-and-play across any LLM without fine-tuning. - The fallback mechanism is elegant: if the GNN underperforms, the LLM can still rely on verbalized KG context (RC), making GLOW-GN robust to weak GNN components in a way AskGNN is not. - GLOW-Bench is a genuine contribution — a carefully constructed open-world benchmark with answers explicitly removed from the KG, covering multi-hop reasoning and diverse domains that expose the failure modes of closed-world and retrieval-based methods. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The approach requires training a separate GNN model per question pattern (e.g., Protein→Species), which creates a proliferation of specialized models and complicates deployment at scale across many relation types or new KGs. - All questions are framed as node classification (multiple-choice) tasks, which constrains the problem formulation — open-ended or generative KGQA scenarios are not addressed, and the authors acknowledge some questions may be better suited to link prediction. - Performance on 2-hop questions remains substantially weaker than 1-hop (42% vs. 83% on GLOW-Bench with Qwen3-8B), indicating the approach does not fully solve multi-hop open-world reasoning — the challenge is acknowledged but not resolved.
Recommended
#73 — Innovative (67)

HopChain: Multi-Hop Data Synthesis for Generalizable Vision-Language Reasoning (48 ▲ )

Shenzhi Wang, Shixuan Liu, Jing Zhou, Chang Gao, Xiong-Hui Chen, Binghai Wang, An Yang, Shiji Song, Bowen Yu, Gao Huang, Junyang Lin · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Mar 2026
Why this rank: HopChain introduces a principled two-dimensional hop taxonomy (perception-level and instance-chain hops) that structurally forces repeated visual re-grounding during reasoning, addressing a genuine gap in RLVR training data. The breadth of evaluation (24 benchmarks, 2 model scales) and the clean ablation showing monotonic improvement with hop depth are convincing, though the core contribution is ultimately a data synthesis pipeline built atop existing models and RL algorithms rather than a methodological breakthrough.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-language models (VLMs) trained with reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) still fail on tasks requiring fine-grained, multi-step visual reasoning. The authors diagnose the root cause: long chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning exposes diverse, compounding failure modes — perception errors, reasoning errors, knowledge gaps, and hallucinations — where one faulty intermediate visual judgment propagates through subsequent steps. Critically, most existing RLVR training data lacks complex reasoning chains that demand repeated visual re-grounding at each step, so these failure modes remain unexposed during training. Simply scaling existing data does not address this structural deficit. Methodology: HopChain is a four-stage scalable pipeline for synthesizing multi-hop vision-language queries suited to RLVR. Stage 1 uses Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking to enumerate semantic categories in each image. Stage 2 applies SAM3 to segment and localize individual instances per category. Stage 3 prompts the same large VLM to generate multi-hop queries over combinations of 3–6 instances, forming logically dependent chains where each hop either (a) switches perception level between single-object (Level 1) and multi-object relational reasoning (Level 2) — a "perception-level hop" — or (b) follows an explicit instance dependency chain A→B→C where each next instance is identifiable only from results of prior hops — an "instance-chain hop." All queries terminate in a specific, unambiguous numerical answer for verifiable reward. Stage 4 applies human-in-the-loop annotation (four independent annotators must agree on the final answer) and difficulty calibration (queries on which a weaker model achieves 100% accuracy are discarded). An upstream image-filtering step uses a teacher-student pipeline (Qwen3-VL-235B as teacher, fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B as student) to retain perceptually challenging images. Approximately 6k–8k multi-hop RLVR samples are produced per model. RLVR training uses the SAPO algorithm, which replaces hard clipping of GRPO/GSPO with a temperature-controlled soft gate for stability. Key Results: HopChain data is added as a complement to the original RLVR data for two Qwen3.5 MoE models: Qwen3.5-35B-A3B and Qwen3.5-397B-A17B. Across 24 benchmarks spanning STEM/Puzzle, General VQA, Text/Document Understanding, and Video Understanding, adding multi-hop data improves 20 of 24 benchmarks for both model scales. Notable gains include: EMMA(mini) +5.0 pts (35B) and +2.75 pts (397B), MathVision +2.34 / +2.03, CharXiv +4.1 / +2.6, InfoVQA +2.73 / +1.37, ZEROBench 1→3 and 4→8. Ablations on hop depth are decisive: reducing full chained queries to half-chain or single-hop drops the five-benchmark average on Qwen3.5-35B-A3B from 70.4 → 66.7 → 64.3. The benefit scales with CoT length, peaking at >50 accuracy-point improvement in the ultra-long-CoT regime on Qwen3.5-397B-A17B. The distribution of error types corrected by multi-hop training closely mirrors the overall baseline error distribution (dominated by perception and reasoning errors), confirming broad generalization rather than narrow patch-up. Video understanding improves on 5 of 6 benchmarks despite training data being image-only. Strengths: - The two-dimensional hop taxonomy (perception-level hops + instance-chain hops) provides a principled, non-trivial formalization that structurally blocks language-only shortcuts and forces repeated visual re-grounding. - Human-in-the-loop verification combined with model-based difficulty calibration yields a dataset with verified correctness and a well-spread difficulty distribution (>50% of queries partially solved), making it suitable for RLVR across model scales. - Evaluation breadth (24 benchmarks, 2 model scales, ablations on hop depth, response-length analysis, error-type breakdown) is thorough and supports the central claims about generalizability rather than benchmark overfitting. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The synthesis pipeline is gated on successful SAM3 instance segmentation: images without detectable objects are excluded, which may systematically bias the training distribution toward object-dense, scene-type images and away from abstract diagrams, charts, or text-heavy documents — the very modalities where VLMs commonly fail. - The dataset size (6k–8k queries per model) is modest and the paper does not report scaling curves; it is unclear whether further scaling of multi-hop data continues to improve performance or saturates quickly, nor whether diminishing returns are reached at the current scale.
Recommended 2603.17024 PDF
#74 — Innovative (67)

Voxtral TTS (23 ▲ )

Alexander H. Liu, Alexis Tacnet, Andy Ehrenberg, Andy Lo, Chen-Yo Sun, Guillaume Lample, Henry Lagarde, Jean-Malo Delignon, Jaeyoung Kim, John Harvill, Khyathi Raghavi Chandu, Lorenzo Signoretti, Margaret Jennings, Patrick von Platen, Pavankumar Reddy Muddireddy, Rohin Arora, Sanchit Gandhi, Samuel Humeau, Soham Ghosh, Srijan Mishra, Van Phung, Abdelaziz Bounhar, Abhinav Rastogi, Adrien Sadé, Alan Jeffares, Albert Jiang, Alexandre Cahill, Alexandre Gavaudan, Alexandre Sablayrolles, Amélie Héliou, Amos You, Andrew Bai, Andrew Zhao, Angele Lenglemetz, Anmol Agarwal, Anton Eliseev, Antonia Calvi, Arjun Majumdar, Arthur Fournier, Artjom Joosen, Avi Sooriyarachchi, Aysenur Karaduman Utkur, Baptiste Bout, Baptiste Rozière, Baudouin De Monicault, Benjamin Tibi, Bowen Yang, Charlotte Cronjäger, Clémence Lanfranchi, Connor Chen, Corentin Barreau, Corentin Sautier, Cyprien Courtot, Darius Dabert, Diego de las Casas, Elizaveta Demyanenko, Elliot Chane-Sane, Emmanuel Gottlob, Enguerrand Paquin, Etienne Goffinet, Fabien Niel, Faruk Ahmed, Federico Baldassarre, Gabrielle Berrada, Gaëtan Ecrepont, Gauthier Guinet, Genevieve Hayes, Georgii Novikov, Giada Pistilli, Guillaume Kunsch, Guillaume Martin, Guillaume Raille, Gunjan Dhanuka, Gunshi Gupta, Han Zhou, Harshil Shah, Hope McGovern, Hugo Thimonier, Indraneel Mukherjee, Irene Zhang, Jacques Sun, Jan Ludziejewski, Jason Rute, Jérémie Dentan, Joachim Studnia, Jonas Amar, Joséphine Delas, Josselin Somerville Roberts, Julien Tauran, Karmesh Yadav, Kartik Khandelwal, Kilian Tep, Kush Jain, Laurence Aitchison, Laurent Fainsin, Léonard Blier, Lingxiao Zhao, Louis Martin, Lucile Saulnier, Luyu Gao, Maarten Buyl, Manan Sharma, Marie Pellat, Mark Prins, Martin Alexandre, Mathieu Poirée, Mathieu Schmitt, Mathilde Guillaumin, Matthieu Dinot, Matthieu Futeral, Maxime Darrin, Maximilian Augustin, Mert Unsal, Mia Chiquier, Mikhail Biriuchinskii, Minh-Quang Pham, Mircea Lica, Morgane Rivière, Nathan Grinsztajn, Neha Gupta, Olivier Bousquet, Olivier Duchenne, Patricia Wang, Paul Jacob, Paul Wambergue, Paula Kurylowicz, Philippe Pinel, Philomène Chagniot, Pierre Stock, Piotr Miłoś, Prateek Gupta, Pravesh Agrawal, Quentin Torroba, Ram Ramrakhya, Randall Isenhour, Rishi Shah, Romain Sauvestre, Roman Soletskyi, Rosalie Millner, Rupert Menneer, Sagar Vaze, Samuel Barry, Samuel Belkadi, Sandeep Subramanian, Sean Cha, Shashwat Verma, Siddhant Waghjale, Siddharth Gandhi, Simon Lepage, Sumukh Aithal, Szymon Antoniak, Tarun Kumar Vangani, Teven Le Scao, Théo Cachet, Theo Simon Sorg, Thibaut Lavril, Thomas Chabal, Thomas Foubert, Thomas Robert, Thomas Wang, Tim Lawson, Tom Bewley, Tom Edwards, Tyler Wang, Umar Jamil, Umberto Tomasini, Valeriia Nemychnikova, Vedant Nanda, Victor Jouault, Vincent Maladière, Vincent Pfister, Virgile Richard, Vladislav Bataev, Wassim Bouaziz, Wen-Ding Li, William Havard, William Marshall, Xinghui Li, Xingran Guo, Xinyu Yang, Yannic Neuhaus, Yassine El Ouahidi, Yassir Bendou, Yihan Wang, Yimu Pan, Zaccharie Ramzi, Zhenlin Xu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The hybrid AR+flow-matching decomposition — semantic coherence via autoregression, acoustic richness via continuous flow matching — is a well-motivated and cleanly ablated design that reduces per-frame AR steps from 36 to 8 NFEs. The ASR distillation loss using Whisper cross-attention for soft alignment is an elegant technique that avoids forced aligners entirely. However, the TTS landscape is mature and competitive, and the model underperforms Gemini on emotion steering, limiting the novelty claim to architectural efficiency rather than capability frontier.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Expressive, natural-sounding text-to-speech (TTS) in zero-shot settings — where the model must clone an unseen speaker's voice from a short reference clip — remains an open challenge. Existing neural TTS systems achieve reasonable intelligibility but struggle with naturalness and expressivity, particularly across multiple languages. Prior hierarchical codec-based generators (e.g., Moshi) model acoustic tokens depth-wise autoregressively, which is slow and suboptimal for capturing fine-grained acoustic variation. This paper asks whether the dense acoustic modeling component can be replaced by a faster, higher-quality continuous generative model while retaining autoregressive coherence for semantics. Methodology: Voxtral TTS is a 4B-parameter multilingual zero-shot TTS system with a hybrid architecture. It is built around Voxtral Codec, a novel convolutional-transformer autoencoder that compresses 24 kHz mono audio into 12.5 Hz frames of 37 discrete tokens: one semantic token (VQ codebook of size 8192) and 36 acoustic tokens (FSQ with 21 uniform levels each), at 2.14 kbps. The semantic token is learned via an ASR distillation loss against frozen Whisper decoder hidden states, using soft alignment derived from Whisper cross-attention weights via DTW — avoiding the need for forced aligners or paired transcripts. A multi-resolution STFT discriminator with adversarial and feature-matching losses trains the codec end-to-end. The TTS model uses a Ministral 3B decoder-only transformer as the autoregressive backbone. Given a voice reference and text tokens, the backbone autoregressively generates semantic tokens until an End-of-Audio token. At each decoding step, a lightweight bidirectional 3-layer flow-matching (FM) transformer takes the backbone hidden state and generates the 36 acoustic tokens in continuous space using 8 Euler integration steps with classifier-free guidance (CFG, α=1.2). The FM outputs are discretized back to FSQ levels before the next AR step. Compared to alternatives (MaskGIT, Depth Transformer), FM reduces per-frame sequence length from 38 to 3 inputs and from 36 AR steps to 8 NFEs, with better quality and lower latency. Post-training uses DPO adapted to the hybrid discrete-continuous setting: standard DPO on semantic tokens combined with a flow-DPO objective on acoustic tokens. Winner/loser pairs are constructed via rejection sampling using WER, speaker similarity, loudness consistency, and UTMOS-v2. The model is served via vLLM-Omni with CUDA graph acceleration of the FM transformer and asynchronous chunked streaming, achieving 70 ms first-audio latency and RTF of 0.103 on a single H200. Key Results: On Voxtral Codec benchmarked against Mimi at comparable bitrate (2.1 vs 2.2 kbps, 16 codebooks), Voxtral Codec outperforms on all metrics: Mel distance (0.545 vs 0.618), STFT distance (0.982 vs 1.100), PESQ (3.05 vs 2.67), ESTOI (0.882 vs 0.865), ASR-WER (10.66% vs 11.01%), and speaker similarity (0.843 vs 0.829). On automatic TTS benchmarks (SEED-TTS and MiniMax-TTS across 9 languages), Voxtral TTS significantly outperforms both ElevenLabs v3 and Flash v2.5 on speaker similarity (e.g., English: 0.786 vs 0.484/0.489), while achieving competitive WER (English: 0.63%) and UTMOS scores. In human evaluations for zero-shot voice cloning, Voxtral TTS achieves a 68.4% win rate over ElevenLabs Flash v2.5 across all languages, with particularly strong gains in Spanish (87.8%), Hindi (79.8%), and Portuguese (74.4%). For flagship voices in implicit emotion steering, it outperforms both ElevenLabs models (58.3% vs Flash, 55.4% vs v3), though Gemini 2.5 Flash TTS remains the strongest competitor (35.4–37.1% win rate for Voxtral). DPO reduces WER substantially in several languages (German: 4.08→0.83%, French: 5.01→3.22%) with consistent UTMOS gains. CUDA graph acceleration reduces FM transformer latency by 47% (133→70 ms, RTF 0.258→0.103). At concurrency 32, a single H200 achieves 1,431 char/s/GPU throughput with sub-second latency and zero wait rate. Strengths: - The hybrid AR+flow-matching architecture cleanly separates semantic coherence (handled by AR) from acoustic richness (handled by FM), yielding better quality and lower compute than depth-wise AR alternatives — a principled and well-ablated design choice. - The ASR distillation loss using Whisper cross-attention weights for soft alignment is an elegant way to learn text-aligned semantic tokens without forced aligners or paired transcripts, producing genuinely semantic (not merely phonetic) representations. - The production-readiness emphasis — CUDA graph capture of the ODE solver, asynchronous chunked streaming, and thorough throughput benchmarking — makes this a credible deployed system, not just a research prototype. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Explicit emotion steering requires a reference audio clip embodying the target emotion rather than text instructions, limiting programmatic control compared to ElevenLabs v3 or Gemini 2.5 Flash TTS; the model substantially underperforms Gemini on this axis (35.4% win rate). - Human evaluations compare only against ElevenLabs Flash v2.5 for voice cloning (not v3), and the flagship voice evaluations exclude direct comparisons with state-of-the-art open-source TTS systems, making it hard to assess the model's position within the broader research landscape. - The DPO stage causes a regression on Hindi WER (3.39→4.99%) and the paper acknowledges that longer DPO training leads to more robotic speech, suggesting instability in the preference optimization for certain language/acoustic distributions.
Recommended 2603.25551 PDF
#75 — Innovative (67)

The other mind: How language models exhibit human temporal cognition

ojs.aaai.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The multi-level mechanistic analysis — neuronal, representational, and data-structural — provides unusually rigorous converging evidence for emergent temporal cognition in LLMs, going well beyond typical behavioral probing studies. The reference-log-linear distance metric and the use of independent embedding models to characterize training data structure are methodologically creative. The scope is somewhat narrow (years only) and the philosophical discussion overreaches the empirical evidence.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly display cognitive patterns resembling those of humans despite these not being explicitly specified in training objectives. This paper investigates one specific instance: temporal cognition. Understanding whether and how LLMs develop human-like temporal perception is important both for mechanistic interpretability and for AI alignment — if models develop emergent internal world-models that are not directly observable from behavior, behavioral control alone may be insufficient for safety. The gap this paper fills is the lack of multi-level mechanistic analysis linking behavioral temporal patterns in LLMs to their neural, representational, and data-structural origins. Methodology: The authors apply the similarity judgment task from cognitive science to 12 models spanning Qwen2.5 (1.5B–72B), Llama 3 (1B–70B), GPT-4o, and Gemini-2.0-flash. Models rate pairwise similarity of years from 1525 to 2524 (1,000 years, ~1M pairs). Three theoretical distance metrics are compared: log-linear distance (Weber-Fechner / pure numerical), Levenshtein distance (string edit), and a novel Reference-Log-Linear distance anchored at a subjective reference point R=2025 — the last being designed to capture human-like temporal orientation. Model fit (R²) determines which metric best predicts model behavior. The analysis proceeds at three levels: (1) Neuronal — temporal-preferential neurons in FFN layers are identified via Cohen's d > 2.0, FDR-corrected t-tests (p < 0.0001), and consistency > 0.95 across years; their activations are regressed against log(|2025 − year|). (2) Representational — linear probes trained layer-by-layer on residual states predict the three distance measures, tracking how temporal encoding evolves with depth. (3) Informational — three independent pre-trained embedding models (Qwen3-Embedding-8B, text-embedding-3-large, Gemini-embedding-001) are used to examine the inherent temporal structure of training data via cosine similarity matrices and MDS visualization. Key Results: Larger models exhibit a clear Weber-Fechner-like compression of temporal distance centered near 2025, with the reference-log-linear distance achieving the highest R² on the similarity judgment task for most large models — outperforming both log-linear and Levenshtein distances. Smaller models (1B–3B) show weaker or absent reference-point effects. Temporal-preferential neurons constitute only 0.67%–1.71% of FFN neurons, are concentrated in middle-to-late layers, and their mean activation forms a trough at the reference year, rising logarithmically as years deviate — with R² up to 0.756 for past years in Qwen2.5-72B (layer 71). Layer-wise probing reveals a hierarchical construction: early layers encode log-linear (numerical) distance; deeper layers increasingly encode reference-log-linear (temporal orientation) distance, with the Qwen series additionally suppressing the numerical representation as the temporal one dominates. Pre-trained embedding models confirm that training corpora contain an inherent non-linear temporal structure (R² up to 0.83 between embedding distances and theoretical distances), with future years semantically clustered due to sparse event coverage — mirroring the behavioral asymmetry whereby models assign higher similarity to future year pairs. Estimated subjective reference points via a non-parametric sliding-window method range from 2010 (Llama-3.1-70B) to 2024 (GPT-4o), consistent with training data cutoffs. Strengths: - Rigorous multi-level mechanistic analysis (neuronal, representational, and data-structural) provides converging evidence rather than relying solely on behavioral observations, making the claim of genuine internal temporal structure well-supported. - The experimental design controls carefully for the year-vs-number distinction, disentangling temporal cognition from generic numerical cognition, which is a non-trivial confound given that years are also integers. - The use of three independent embedding models to characterize the informational structure of training data is a methodological strength that prevents circular reasoning about model-internal representations. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The reference point R is fixed at 2025 for cross-model comparability rather than fit as a free parameter, which may systematically bias R² estimates for models with training cutoffs years earlier (e.g., Llama-3.1 with a 2023 cutoff); the non-parametric estimates range from 2010–2024, suggesting this is a meaningful confound. - The study is limited to years as temporal stimuli to avoid periodicity and time-sensitivity in finer-grained formats (dates, months), but this restriction makes it unclear whether findings generalize to the broader temporal cognition that actually governs downstream tasks involving dates, durations, and event sequences. - The experientialist philosophical framework introduced in the discussion, while intellectually interesting, is speculative and not empirically tested; the claim that LLMs may develop "alien cognitive frameworks" is provocative but unsupported by any specific mechanistic evidence in this paper beyond the asymmetry finding.
Citations
#76 — Innovative (67)

CommitMoE: Efficient Fallback-Free MoE Inference with Offloading Under GPU Memory Constraints

ojs.aaai.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: CommitMoE's core insight — that router certainty predicts both prediction accuracy and model robustness to expert substitution — is clean, non-obvious, and well-validated. The fallback-free design addresses the root cause of offloading latency rather than applying heuristic mitigations. Practical impact is clear for edge deployment, though the batch-size-1 evaluation and per-model hyperparameter tuning limit the scope of the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mixture of Experts (MoE) models are too large to fit in GPU memory on resource-constrained hardware (edge devices, consumer GPUs), requiring expert parameters to be offloaded to CPU RAM or NVMe and loaded on demand. CPU-GPU data transfer dominates per-layer latency — up to 95% for models like Mixtral-8x7B. Existing prefetching approaches partially hide this by predicting which experts to load ahead of time, but when predictions are wrong the system must fall back to on-demand loading of the correct experts. Because CUDA's async memory copy cannot be canceled once started, each misprediction adds 13.7%–47.6% extra sequential transfer overhead. No prior work eliminates the fallback without requiring model retraining (Pre-gated MoE requires full retraining). Methodology: CommitMoE introduces a "Commit Router" that unconditionally commits to predicted expert selections — it never falls back to loading the original router's chosen experts, even when the prediction is wrong. The core insight, validated empirically, is that router certainty (measured by KL divergence between the router's softmax distribution and a uniform distribution) strongly predicts both prediction accuracy and model robustness: high-certainty inputs are easy to predict correctly; low-certainty inputs are hard to predict but the model output is largely insensitive to which experts are chosen in those cases. The Commit Router is implemented as lightweight MLPs trained offline — a single-layer MLP for same-token cross-layer predictions (exploiting high cosine similarity between adjacent-layer hidden states) and a two-layer MLP for cross-token predictions. Training minimizes KL divergence between the predictor's output distribution and the true router's distribution. To compensate for quality loss when high-certainty predictions are partially wrong, CommitMoE adds an optional Output Weight Adjustment (OWA) mechanism: it redistributes gating score mass from missed experts to correctly predicted ones (scaled by hyperparameter α1), then rescales to maintain overall magnitude (α2). OWA is selectively triggered only for intermediate prediction accuracy ranges where the model is sensitive to expert choice; fully correct and fully incorrect predictions bypass it. The system is built on PyTorch and MoE-Infinity, using asynchronous CUDA multi-streaming to overlap prefetch with computation. Key Results: Evaluated on three models (Mixtral-8x7B-Instruct, Qwen1.5-MoE-Chat, DeepSeek-V2-Lite-Chat) across four benchmarks (GSM8K, TruthfulQA, BBH, WMT16 de→en) on two hardware environments (RTX 4090 + PCIe Gen4, and RTX 2080Ti + PCIe Gen3). On the higher-end Environment 1, CommitMoE achieves 1.3–1.8× speedup over vLLM. On the bandwidth-constrained Environment 2, speedups reach 4.3× (Mixtral), 9.4× (Qwen), and 7.5× (DeepSeek) over vLLM. CommitMoE consistently outperforms both MoE-Infinity (prefetch with fallback) and a "+Prefetch" ablation (Commit Router predictor with fallback retained), isolating the gains from fallback elimination. Model quality is largely preserved: CommitMoE with OWA matches or slightly exceeds the base model on average for DeepSeek (48.48% vs 47.81%) and nearly matches for Qwen (43.06% vs 43.17%); Mixtral without OWA slightly outperforms the base (51.02% vs 50.85%). Wilcoxon signed-rank tests confirm statistical significance (p < 0.001) with Cohen's d ranging from 0.567 to 7.356. Strengths: - The empirical insight linking router certainty to prediction accuracy and model robustness is clean, well-validated on two models and two datasets, and provides genuine theoretical grounding for the commitment strategy. - Fallback-free design eliminates the worst-case sequential transfer penalty entirely, which is the right fix for the root cause rather than a heuristic mitigation. - No model retraining required; the lightweight MLP predictors are trained offline and the approach is compatible with any pre-trained MoE. Weaknesses / Limitations: - OWA hyperparameters (α1, α2) require per-model grid search on a validation set, adding a tuning burden and potentially limiting generalizability to new models without calibration data. - Evaluation is limited to batch size 1 (single-user, sequential decoding), which is realistic for edge deployment but does not address batched or server-side inference where the throughput-latency tradeoff looks very different. - The commitment strategy permanently substitutes predicted experts for true experts during computation, meaning errors in high-certainty layers — where the model is most sensitive — cannot be corrected, and OWA's redistribution heuristic is a proxy fix rather than an exact recovery.
Search Results
#77 — Innovative (67)

Neuron-Level Emotion Control in Speech-Generative Large Audio-Language Models

Xiutian Zhao, Ismail Rasim Ulgen, Philipp Koehn, Björn Schuller, Berrak Sisman · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper earns 67 for being the first systematic study of emotion-sensitive neurons in speech-generative large audio-language models, producing a mechanistically informative finding that LM-side MLP layers are actionable while downstream synthesis MLPs are inert. The four-stage training-free pipeline with success-filtered activation aggregation is a principled methodological contribution. However, absolute improvements are modest (+4 pp emotion match on the best model) over an extremely weak baseline (sub-25%), and generalization to unseen speakers is inconsistent, limiting practical impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large audio-language models (LALMs) capable of joint speech-text processing can generate expressive speech, but reliable emotion control during inference remains difficult. Instruction-following emotional voice conversion (EVC) — converting neutral speech to a target emotion while preserving linguistic content word-for-word — fails in multiple ways: the model may produce the wrong emotion, refuse the task, hallucinate content, or paraphrase. These content-preservation failures are qualitatively worse than acoustic distortions because they alter the underlying message. Prior EVC approaches rely on learned style representations or fine-tuning, and while neuron-level steering has been explored in text LLMs, no prior work has systematically studied whether compact neuron subsets causally mediate emotional expressiveness in speech-generative LALMs. Methodology: The paper introduces a four-stage, training-free pipeline. (1) Activation sampling: during EVC inference, per-neuron gate activations in decoder-side MLP layers (SwiGLU/SiLU gated units) are logged for all generation steps. (2) Success filtering: only generations that pass both a speech emotion recognition (SER) judge (emotion2vec+large, 97.6% accuracy on ESD) and a WER threshold (≤15%) against the source transcript are retained, forming per-emotion success sets. (3) Emotion-sensitive neuron (ESN) identification: four selector strategies are compared — LAP (raw activation frequency), LAPE (entropy-based selectivity), MAD (mean activation deviation across emotions), and CAS (contrastive margin between top and second-best emotion). A selection rate r controls mask sparsity. (4) Inference-time intervention: identified neuron gates are manipulated via gain scaling, additive shift, floor clamping, or deactivation, with model weights untouched. Self-effects (mask emotion = target emotion) and cross-effects (mask emotion ≠ target emotion) are compared to isolate emotion-specific versus nonspecific perturbations. Experiments use the ESD dataset (10 English speakers, 5 emotions) across three open LALMs: Qwen2.5-Omni-7B, MiniCPM-o 4.5, and Kimi-Audio. Human evaluation (20 raters, 100 pairwise trials) supplements automatic metrics. Key Results: Baseline EVC is surprisingly poor across all three models: average emotion match rates range from 7.88% (MiniCPM-o 4.5) to 25.10% (Qwen2.5-Omni-7B under emotion2vec), with 'surprise' consistently the hardest and a strong neutral bias. After ESN intervention with the best selector (CAS, r=0.5%, α=1.0), Qwen2.5-Omni-7B gains +4.27/+3.60 pp emotion match (emotion2vec/Qwen3-Omni judge) with a self–cross gap of +3.93/+3.65 pp, confirming emotion-specific rather than generic effects. Kimi-Audio shows the largest Qwen3-judge gain (+7.65 pp). CAS and MAD substantially outperform LAP and LAPE; LAPE is particularly poor, with cross-effects rivaling self-effects. Intervention strength sweeps show that moderate α (0.3–0.5) preserves WER near baseline while still improving emotion match, while α=2.0 yields +8.89/+20.67 pp emotion gain but catastrophically inflates WER to 203.65% (+184.65 pp), with naturalness (UTMOS) nearly unchanged throughout — semantic drift, not acoustic collapse, is the primary failure mode. Human evaluation at the anchor setting shows 62% win / 11% tie / 27% loss for the intervened system, with happy achieving 69% win. ESNs concentrate in intermediate-to-late decoder MLP layers (layers 11–15 for Qwen, 19–26 for Kimi-Audio); analogous interventions in downstream speech synthesis MLPs are largely inert across all selectors. Generalization to unseen speakers holds for angry and surprise but degrades for happy and sad. Strengths: - The success-filtered activation aggregation protocol is a principled solution to EVC's multi-objective nature; mixing failed generations into activation statistics would corrupt ESN estimates, and this design decision is validated empirically. - Comprehensive ablation across selector type, mask sparsity, success set size, and intervention method and strength provides actionable design guidance rather than a single point result. - Localization finding (LM-side MLPs actionable, synthesis MLPs inert) is a mechanistically informative and practically useful result that constrains future interpretability work on speech LALMs. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The absolute emotion match improvements are modest (+4 pp self-effect on the best model), and baseline EVC performance is extremely low (sub-25%), raising questions about whether neuron steering is a practical control mechanism or primarily a mechanistic analysis tool at current scale. - Generalization to unseen speakers is emotion-dependent and notably weak for happy, suggesting the identified ESNs partially encode speaker-specific rather than purely emotion-specific signals; the paper acknowledges but does not resolve this. - Only neutral-to-emotion conversion is studied; bidirectional EVC and cross-emotion conversion (e.g., angry→happy) are left unaddressed, limiting conclusions about the generality of the ESN framework.
Recommended 2603.17231 PDF
#78 — Innovative (66)

Talon: Breaking the Synchronization Barrier in Speculative Decoding with Hybrid Model-based and Retrieve-based Drafting

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Talon's asynchronous pipeline design cleanly eliminates both target-idle and draft-idle bubbles, and the multi-layer feature fusion for datastore construction is a principled enrichment over simple n-gram retrieval. Results consistently beat EAGLE-3 across diverse models and tasks. However, the core ideas—async execution, hybrid retrieval+model drafting, tree verification—are individually well-established, and the contribution is primarily in their effective integration rather than fundamental novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by having a lightweight draft model propose tokens that a larger target model verifies in parallel. However, existing implementations suffer from two fundamental bottlenecks: (1) the mutual waiting problem—the target model sits idle during draft generation while the draft model stalls during verification, creating alternating pipeline bubbles; and (2) constrained token acceptance rates—retrieval-based drafters excel in knowledge-intensive domains but underperform in general settings, while model-based drafters exhibit the opposite weakness. Both deficiencies cap achievable speedups well below theoretical limits. Methodology: Talon introduces two complementary innovations. First, an asynchronous execution paradigm with two strategies: (a) pre-retrieve and pre-verify, which uses the target model's idle time during draft generation to simultaneously execute a retrieval query and pre-compute verification probabilities over both draft and retrieved tokens; and (b) pre-retrieve and post-verify, which uses the draft model's idle time during verification to concurrently generate the next batch of draft tokens and retrieved candidates. When the leading draft token is rejected, accepted retrieved tokens plus a resampled token still yield γ_eff = k+1 useful tokens per step rather than falling back to just one. Second, an adaptive hybrid drafting strategy that constructs a knowledge-enriched datastore D using four techniques: tokenizer-based segmentation, target model distribution alignment, layer-specific token injection from transformer layers {2, 16, 29} to capture syntactic, semantic, and discourse representations, and hierarchical feature fusion via concatenation of hidden states from these layers. During decoding, suffix matching retrieves candidate sequences from D; a retrieval tree is built and frequency-pruned, then fused with the model-based draft tree via longest-prefix matching to eliminate redundant attention computations. A single forward pass of the target model verifies the entire combined tree using tree attention with masked positional encodings. Key Results: Evaluated on Vicuna-13B, LLaMA-Instruct-3.1-8B, and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B across MT-Bench, HumanEval, GSM8K, Alpaca, and CNN/DM under greedy decoding (temperature=0). Talon achieves 4.04×–6.52× speedup over vanilla autoregressive decoding. Against the current state-of-the-art EAGLE-3, Talon improves by 4.9% on average for Vicuna-13B (up to 15.1% on GSM8K), 9.0% average for LLaMA-3.1-8B (11.8% on MT-Bench), and strong gains on DeepSeek-R1-8B (13.0% on Alpaca). Representative acceptance lengths: τ = 8.11 for Vicuna-13B on MT-Bench and τ = 8.58 on HumanEval, versus EAGLE-3's 6.65 and 7.54 respectively. Talon outperforms all compared methods (SpS, PLD, Medusa, Lookahead, Hydra, EAGLE, EAGLE-2, EAGLE-3) across all model-task combinations. Strengths: - The asynchronous pipeline design addresses a genuine and underexplored structural inefficiency in speculative decoding; eliminating both target-idle and draft-idle gaps is conceptually clean and practically impactful. - The multi-layer feature fusion for datastore construction (injecting tokens from shallow, mid, and high transformer layers) is a principled approach to enriching retrieval quality beyond simple n-gram matching. - Comprehensive evaluation across 3 diverse model families and 5 benchmarks with reproducible EAGLE-3 comparisons on identical hardware (A800), lending credibility to the reported gains. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper does not quantify memory overhead from the knowledge-enriched datastore D, which could be substantial for multi-domain coverage; this is a critical practical concern for deployment. - Evaluation is restricted to greedy (temperature=0) decoding; lossless guarantees under sampling (temperature>0) require the ratio-based acceptance criterion to hold, but no stochastic decoding experiments are reported. - Some baseline comparisons (Medusa, Lookahead, etc.) are taken from original papers on A100 hardware rather than re-run on the A800 used for Talon and EAGLE-3, introducing potential hardware-induced confounds in relative speedups.
Recommended
#79 — Innovative (66)

PixelSmile: Toward Fine-Grained Facial Expression Editing (98 ▲ )

Jiabin Hua, Hengyuan Xu, Aojie Li, Wei Cheng, Gang Yu, Xingjun Ma, Yu-Gang Jiang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 Mar 2026
Why this rank: PixelSmile tackles the underexplored problem of expression entanglement at category boundaries with a well-integrated system: continuous affective annotations replace brittle one-hot labels, symmetric contrastive training explicitly pushes confusable pairs apart, and textual latent interpolation enables smooth intensity control. The FFE-Bench metrics are thoughtfully designed to expose trade-offs invisible to standard metrics. The circular evaluation concern (synthetic data from a related generative prior) and failure on 6/15 expression blends temper the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Facial expression editing with diffusion models suffers from a fundamental semantic overlap problem: expressions like fear/surprise and anger/disgust share facial muscle activations (wide eyes, furrowed brows), causing generative models trained on discrete one-hot labels to produce entangled latent representations. This manifests as cross-category confusion, where editing one expression inadvertently triggers another, and limits continuous intensity control. Prior work relying on discrete class labels, StyleGAN latent directions, or coarse action-unit priors cannot resolve this boundary ambiguity. The gap is simultaneously a data problem (no dataset with continuous affective annotations and same-identity expression pairs) and a training problem (no mechanism to explicitly push confusing expression pairs apart in representation space). Methodology: The paper introduces three tightly coupled contributions. First, the Flex Facial Expression (FFE) dataset: 60,000 images (30K real, 30K anime) generated from ~6,000 base identities per domain using Nano Banana Pro, annotated with 12-dimensional continuous expression score vectors predicted by Gemini 3 Pro, replacing one-hot labels with soft affective distributions that capture semantic overlap. Second, FFE-Bench: a benchmark with four metrics — Mean Structural Confusion Rate (mSCR, bidirectional confusion between confusable pairs), Harmonic Editing Score (HES, harmonic mean of expression strength and identity similarity), Control Linearity Score (CLS, Pearson correlation between control parameter α and VLM-predicted intensity), and editing accuracy (Acc). Third, PixelSmile: a LoRA fine-tuned MMDiT (Multi-Modal Diffusion Transformer) with two core mechanisms. For continuous control, textual latent interpolation computes e_cond(α) = e_neutral + α·(e_target − e_neutral) in the frozen text encoder's embedding space, with score-supervised flow matching that explicitly couples the interpolation coefficient to ground-truth expression intensity during training. For disentanglement, a fully symmetric joint training framework samples confusable pairs (e.g., fear/surprise) and jointly trains two branches, each treating the other's expression as a hard negative via InfoNCE-style contrastive loss in CLIP feature space; the symmetric design (both orderings trained simultaneously) prevents directional bias. An ArcFace identity loss prevents expression editing from drifting facial biometrics. At inference, α > 1 enables extrapolation beyond training intensity, and barycentric interpolation of multiple target embeddings produces compound expressions. Key Results: Against general editing models, PixelSmile achieves the lowest mSCR (0.0550) — far below GPT-Image-1.5 (0.1107) and Nano Banana Pro (0.1754), with most baselines exceeding 0.20. It achieves the highest 6-class accuracy (0.8627) while maintaining identity similarity in the natural 0.6–0.7 range, avoiding both the identity-rigidity failure mode (Seedream, ID Sim 0.7221 but mSCR 0.3725) and identity collapse (FLUX-Klein, ID Sim 0.4146). Against linear control models, PixelSmile achieves CLS-6 = 0.8078 and CLS-12 = 0.7305, compared to SliderEdit (0.5599, 0.5217) and K-Slider (negative CLS, failing linear control entirely). HES = 0.4723 exceeds all baselines. Ablations confirm each component is necessary: removing contrastive loss raises mSCR to 0.2725; removing the symmetric framework raises it to 0.1350; removing identity loss drops ID Sim from 0.6522 to 0.5749. Training on MEAD instead of FFE degrades all metrics substantially (mSCR 0.2125, CLS-6 0.7047). User study (2,400 images, 10 annotators) scores PixelSmile at (continuity 4.48, identity 3.80) versus SliderEdit (3.16, 1.14) and K-Slider (1.36, 4.06), confirming it uniquely balances both dimensions. Expression blending produces perceptually coherent compound expressions for 9 of 15 zero-shot pairwise combinations. Strengths: - The continuous affective annotation paradigm directly targets the root cause (one-hot label entanglement) rather than working around it, and the FFE-Bench metrics are well-designed to expose trade-offs invisible to SSIM/LPIPS/CLIP. - The fully symmetric contrastive training is technically clean and empirically important: the asymmetric ablation converges faster initially but to a worse minimum, showing the bidirectional constraint is a genuine regularizer, not just doubling compute. - Cross-domain generalization to anime via separate LoRA adapters and domain-specific CLIP (DanbooruCLIP) is practically valuable and rarely addressed in expression editing work.
