Weaknesses / Limitations: - The FFE dataset is entirely synthetic — images are generated by Nano Banana Pro, then re-edited by PixelSmile. Circular evaluation bias is a real concern: PixelSmile is fine-tuned on data from the same generative prior it builds upon (Qwen-Image-Edit), potentially inflating its advantage on a benchmark scored by Gemini 3 Pro. - The identity similarity metric is bounded by an inherent editing/identity trade-off, and the authors' interpretation that ID Sim > 0.8 indicates 'copy-paste' behavior (used to discount strong competitors like Seedream) is ad hoc and not validated independently. - Expression blending fails for 6 of 15 combinations (including fear+surprise, the primary confusable pair the method targets), suggesting the disentanglement in latent space is incomplete for the hardest cases.
Recommended 2603.25728 PDF
#80 — Innovative (66)

2Xplat: Two Experts Are Better Than One Generalist (15 ▲ )

Hwasik Jeong, Seungryong Lee, Gyeongjin Kang, Seungkwon Yang, Xiangyu Sun, Seungtae Nam, Eunbyung Park · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Mar 2026
Why this rank: 2Xplat's core insight — that separating geometry and appearance into specialized pretrained experts outperforms monolithic architectures — is clean and well-validated with consistent 3–6 dB gains. The training efficiency (5K vs. 150K iterations) is notable. However, the contribution is primarily architectural composition rather than algorithmic innovation; it is difficult to disentangle gains from the modular design principle versus simply using stronger pretrained components (DA3, MVP) than prior work had access to.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) methods that work without camera poses (pose-free) have largely adopted monolithic architectures that jointly estimate camera poses and synthesize 3D Gaussian representations within a single shared backbone. The paper argues this entanglement is a fundamental design flaw: geometry estimation and appearance modeling have conflicting objectives, and a shared representation lacks the representational capacity for high-fidelity Gaussian synthesis. Additionally, monolithic designs cannot cleanly exploit the rich ecosystem of pose-conditioned architectural mechanisms (epipolar transformers, PRoPE, RayRoPE, etc.) that power state-of-the-art posed feed-forward methods, because pose information is never cleanly separated from learned features. Prior "geometry-first" pipelines existed but emphasized self-supervised training rather than combining best-of-breed specialists. Methodology: 2Xplat decomposes the pose-free 3DGS task into two dedicated experts connected sequentially. The geometry expert (Depth Anything 3 / DA3) takes uncalibrated multi-view images and predicts camera poses (intrinsics + extrinsics) for all views. The appearance expert (Multi-view Pyramid Transformer / MVP) then receives the context images together with the predicted poses and synthesizes pixel-aligned 3D Gaussians. No new modules are introduced; both experts are fully pretrained and the entire pipeline is fine-tuned end-to-end with a combined loss: a rendering loss (MSE + perceptual) on target views plus a relative pose supervision loss (geodesic rotation loss + Huber translation loss + L2 intrinsics loss). The relative pose loss is used as a regularizer to prevent geometric drift while allowing the appearance expert to tolerate noisy pose estimates. Because both experts are already mature and pretrained, no randomly initialized task-specific heads are added, enabling convergence in fewer than 5K iterations on 8 H200 GPUs—compared to 150K iterations on 16 GH200 GPUs for the primary baseline (YoNoSplat). Key Results: On DL3DV with 6/12/24 views at 224×224, 2Xplat (pose-free, no GT intrinsics) achieves 26.01/26.02/25.89 PSNR vs. YoNoSplat's 22.29/20.38/19.71 PSNR—a 3–6 dB improvement over the best prior pose-free method. Crucially, 2Xplat also outperforms all pose-dependent baselines including MVSplat, DepthSplat, and YoNoSplat with GT poses. On RE10K (6 context views), 2Xplat achieves 26.16/27.24 PSNR (without/with EPA) vs. YoNoSplat's 19.72/24.57. On high-resolution DL3DV (960×540) with 16–128 input views, 2Xplat is the only pose-free method evaluated and comes within ~1–1.5 dB of the posed MVP baseline. Cross-dataset generalization from DL3DV to ScanNet++ shows 2Xplat outperforming YoNoSplat by ~2–3 dB at 64–128 views. Pose estimation AUC@10° of 0.843 is competitive with dedicated geometry models (DA3: 0.826, π³: 0.841) and substantially better than prior pose-free 3DGS methods (NoPoSplat: 0.627). The lighter Ours-L variant (0.5B params, 0.15s inference) already surpasses YoNoSplat (1B params, 0.33s) by ~3.5 dB PSNR at 6× faster speed. Strengths: - The core insight—that separating geometry and appearance into specialized pretrained experts is strictly better than a monolithic shared backbone—is both simple and empirically compelling, with consistent 3–6 dB PSNR gains across diverse benchmarks. - Exceptional training efficiency: convergence in <5K iterations with no newly initialized parameters, compared to 150K iterations for the state-of-the-art baseline, making the approach practically accessible. - The modular design naturally handles the posed/pose-free duality (simply bypass the geometry expert when GT poses are available) and independently benefits from future improvements to either expert. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper conflates two contributions—the modular design principle and the specific choice of high-quality pretrained experts (DA3, MVP)—making it difficult to isolate how much of the gain comes from architecture vs. simply plugging in stronger pretrained models than prior work used. - Cross-dataset generalization to Tanks&Templates and Mip-NeRF360 is notably weaker: 2Xplat trails the posed MVP baseline by 1–3 dB, and the method is compared only against Long-LRM and iLRM rather than the strongest posed baselines (e.g., DepthSplat, YoNoSplat-posed), raising questions about generalization limits. - The sequential design introduces a hard dependency: if the geometry expert produces poor poses (e.g., for textureless or repetitive scenes), errors propagate directly to the appearance expert with no mechanism to recover beyond the pose-robustness gained via joint fine-tuning.
Recommended 2603.21064 PDF
#81 — Innovative (65)

LEXI: Lossless Exponent Coding for Efficient Inter-Chiplet Communication in Hybrid LLMs

Miao Sun, Alish Kanani, Kaushik Shroff, Umit Ogras · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: LEXI exploits a well-validated empirical insight — BF16 exponents carry fewer than 3 bits of entropy — to achieve 3.1× lossless compression of inter-chiplet traffic at negligible hardware cost (0.09% chiplet area). The microarchitectural design is clean engineering with practical line-rate operation. However, evaluation on only sub-2B models on a simulated chiplet architecture limits confidence in generalization, and the interaction with quantized inference remains unvalidated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM inference on multi-chiplet accelerators is dominated by data movement rather than compute, particularly during the autoregressive decode phase where all model weights and KV/state caches must be loaded each token. BF16, the de facto precision for LLM training and inference, allocates 8 bits to the exponent field to match FP32's dynamic range — but empirical profiling reveals that these 8-bit exponent streams carry fewer than 3 bits of Shannon entropy during inference, with observed values concentrated within just 32 distinct symbols. The mantissa, by contrast, saturates its full 7-bit range and is incompressible. This asymmetry creates an untapped lossless compression opportunity that can reduce inter-chiplet traffic without any accuracy degradation or quantization artifacts. Methodology: LEXI applies per-layer Huffman coding exclusively to the exponent field of BF16 tensors (weights, activations, KV caches, and Mamba state caches), leaving sign and mantissa bits unmodified. The system has two paths: weights are compressed offline and stored in HBM in compressed form for just-in-time decompression; activations and hybrid caches are compressed on-the-fly at NoC egress ports and decompressed at ingress. The encoder uses M=10 parallel lanes each with an 8-entry local frequency cache to build a global exponent histogram without throughput stalls. From this histogram, a pipelined hardware Huffman tree builder — comprising a 15-cycle bitonic sorter, 31-cycle priority-queue tree construction, and 32-cycle LUT programming — generates a per-layer codebook in 78 cycles total (~78 ns at 1 GHz), pipelined with the incoming data stream. Encoding is a single-cycle LUT lookup replicated across all lanes. The decoder uses a four-stage cascaded LUT (indexed by 8/16/24/32-bit prefixes, 8 entries per stage, 10 parallel lanes) that resolves high-frequency short codes in one cycle and rare long codes in up to four, achieving 11.6 ns average decode latency for 10 exponents at 98.5 µm² area. Flit-aligned packetization preserves the one-flit-per-cycle link rate. The design is synthesized in GF 22 nm and evaluated on a 6×6 homogeneous chiplet array based on Simba, using cycle-accurate HeteroGarnet simulation. Key Results: LEXI achieves exponent compression ratios of ~3.1× across Jamba, Zamba, and Qwen models, substantially outperforming base-delta-immediate compression (2.40×) and RLE (which actually expands the data at 0.64×). On WikiText-2, inter-chiplet communication latency is reduced by 45.4% for Jamba (86.70 ms → 47.35 ms), 33.5% for Zamba, and 38.3% for Qwen; on C4, reductions range from 34–42%. Since communication accounts for 68–95% of end-to-end latency in the uncompressed baseline, these savings translate to 30–35% end-to-end inference latency reductions across all three models and both datasets. The full LEXI codec (compressor + decompressor) occupies 14,995 µm² in GF 22 nm (scaled to 5,453 µm² for the 16 nm Simba node), corresponding to just 0.09% of a 6 mm² chiplet area, with 45.43 mW total power. The codebook generation overhead of 78 cycles is incurred once per layer and is negligible relative to the millions of activations processed per layer. Strengths: - Strictly lossless: no approximation to weights, activations, or caches; model accuracy is preserved by construction, making LEXI orthogonal to and composable with lossy techniques such as quantization or pruning. - Negligible hardware cost (0.09% chiplet area, 45 mW) with full line-rate operation — a compelling engineering contribution demonstrating that entropy coding can be made chiplet-practical through careful microarchitecture choices (shallow LUT pipelines, parallel lanes, per-lane local caches). - The key empirical insight — that BF16 exponents have <3 bits entropy due to layer normalization keeping activations in a narrow dynamic range — is well-motivated, experimentally validated across Transformer, Mamba, and MoE blocks, and directly drives the design. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to three small models (319M–1.8B parameters) on a simulated chiplet architecture based on Simba; it is unclear whether the entropy and compression ratio results generalize to frontier-scale models (70B+) or heterogeneous chiplet designs where layer distributions may differ significantly. - The paper does not quantify the interaction between LEXI and quantized models (e.g., INT8 or INT4 weights), where the exponent entropy profile would change fundamentally and the claimed composability remains unvalidated.
Search Results 2603.15589 PDF
#82 — Innovative (65)

Chimera: Latency-and Performance -Aware Multi-agent Serving for Heterogeneous LLMs

Kangqi Ni, Wenyue Hua, Xiaoxiang Shi, Jiang Guo, Shiyu Chang, Tianlong Chen · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Chimera's co-design of semantic routing, workflow-level length prediction (STJF across future stages), and real-time load monitoring addresses a genuine gap in heterogeneous multi-agent serving. The workflow-level STJF formulation is a non-obvious and meaningful extension beyond standard SJF. However, evaluation is narrow (two tasks, one workflow template), and the modest semantic router accuracy suggests substantial room for improvement.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern LLM deployments increasingly run multi-agent workflows—sequences of dependent LLM calls where each stage's output feeds the next. Existing serving systems (vLLM, SGLang, Autellix) assume homogeneous clusters of identical model replicas, leaving unexploited the opportunity of routing requests across models of different sizes and capabilities. The gap is not merely a routing problem: in heterogeneous clusters, model selection and queue congestion are tightly coupled—routing to a stronger, slower model can improve task accuracy but amplify contention and degrade end-to-end latency for all in-flight workflows. Prior routing methods treat latency as a fixed model attribute rather than a dynamic outcome of queueing under load, and prior schedulers lack workflow-level awareness. Chimera addresses the joint optimization of end-to-end latency and task performance for multi-agent workflows on heterogeneous LLM clusters. Methodology: Chimera is a middleware layer sitting between agent applications and backend vLLM inference engines. It comprises four co-designed modules. (1) A Semantic Router: a fine-tuned ModernBERT-large encoder with a multi-label sigmoid head trained via binary cross-entropy on offline execution traces, producing per-model confidence scores q[m] estimating the probability that model m solves the request correctly. (2) A Length Predictor: a Quantile Random Forest (QRF) trained to predict the conditional median of total remaining output tokens across all future stages of the workflow (not just the current request), using features including token counts, workflow stage identity, model name, and a TF-IDF + truncated SVD sketch of the user prompt. This workflow-level prediction enables a Shortest Total Job First (STJF) priority policy. (3) An Activity Monitor: tracks in-flight predicted token volumes per engine to estimate per-model time-to-last-token (TTLT) load: L[m] = (sum of in-flight predicted tokens × decode_ms_per_token) / max_batch_size. (4) A Load Balancer: selects the highest-confidence model whose estimated TTLT is within a configurable latency slack τ of the fastest model, subject to a minimum confidence margin Δs to avoid switching for marginal gains and to promote KV-cache reuse across stages. Requests are enqueued with STJF priority and an aging-based anti-starvation mechanism (starvation count threshold S, running quantum Q) to bound worst-case waiting time. All scheduling components run asynchronously with batched inference to keep overhead minimal. Key Results: Evaluated on APPS (code generation) and MATH (competition math) across four heterogeneous model configurations spanning Qwen2.5 (1.5B–14B), Llama3.2-3B, and Ministral-8B at request rates of 8–16 RPS. Chimera consistently dominates the latency–performance Pareto frontier. On APPS (Qwen1.5B+7B), Chimera achieves a 3.4× latency speedup and +16.0 pp accuracy over vLLM, a 1.42× speedup and +19.6 pp over MLFQ, and a 1.31× speedup and +16.0 pp over LTR. On APPS (Qwen1.5B+14B), it achieves 2.9× speedup and +10.5 pp over vLLM. On MATH, gains are consistent though smaller: 2.5× speedup and +2.2 pp over vLLM for Qwen1.5B+7B, up to 2.1× speedup and +5.0 pp for Qwen1.5B+14B. Averaged across all settings, Chimera reduces end-to-end latency by 1.2–2.4× and improves task performance by 8.0–9.5 percentage points over competitive baselines. Scheduling overhead is negligible: ≤2.2% of E2E latency. Ablations confirm the semantic router is responsible for most performance gains, while STJF via the length predictor drives latency reduction (STJF reduces queue time 15–34% beyond SJF, which itself reduces 26–48% beyond FCFS). The QRF predictor achieves Kendall's tau of ~0.155–0.262 vs. ~0.499 for FCFS baseline. Oracle comparisons show Chimera is near-optimal in several settings. Strengths: - The co-design of routing, workflow-level length prediction, and real-time load monitoring is principled and fills a genuine gap—prior work addresses each component in isolation, missing their coupling in heterogeneous serving. - The workflow-level STJF formulation (predicting total remaining output tokens across all future stages, not just the current request) is a meaningful and non-obvious extension of standard SJF to multi-agent settings, with clear empirical payoff. - Practical engineering choices are sound: CPU-based QRF for length prediction avoids GPU contention; asynchronous batched inference for router and predictor keeps scheduling overhead below 2.2%; the system exposes standard OpenAI-compatible APIs atop vLLM, easing adoption. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to two tasks (code generation, math reasoning) and one workflow template (ReAct), with models from only a few families. Generalization to more diverse agentic workflows (e.g., tool-use, retrieval-augmented, open-ended generation) and truly heterogeneous families (mixture of proprietary and open models) is unvalidated. - The semantic router's mAP is modest—0.521 on APPS and 0.740 on MATH—meaning routing decisions are substantially noisy. The oracle router analysis shows meaningful headroom remains, particularly for Qwen1.5B+14B configurations, and the system's robustness in more difficult routing scenarios is unclear. - The model assignment locality heuristic (reusing the same model for all stages of a workflow once assigned) is a practical simplification that may be suboptimal when different stages have very different difficulty profiles, and this design choice is not ablated.

#83 — Innovative (65)

Beyond Local Patterns: Multiscale Inconsistency Learning for Graph Anomaly Detection

ojs.aaai.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: MI-GAD's core insight — that GNN neighborhood smoothing is architecturally misaligned with anomaly detection — is well-argued with both theoretical grounding (Theorem 1) and strong empirical validation. The combination of local affinity and global inconsistency scoring elegantly addresses community camouflage. However, the individual components (MLP encoding, hypersphere projection, one-class center deviation) are well-established techniques recombined rather than fundamentally new, placing this as a meaningful but not paradigm-shifting contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Unsupervised graph anomaly detection (UGAD) aims to identify nodes that deviate significantly from normal structural or attribute patterns, with applications in fraud detection, spam mitigation, and financial security. Existing methods uncritically transplant graph representation learning paradigms—GNN backbones, reconstruction losses, and contrastive pretext tasks—into the anomaly detection setting, despite a fundamental mismatch: GNNs are designed for neighborhood smoothing, which dilutes anomaly signals rather than amplifying them. A second gap is the reliance on purely local inconsistency signals, which fail on "community camouflage" anomalies—anomalous nodes that mimic the local similarity patterns of normal nodes and are therefore indistinguishable from their neighbors. The authors demonstrate empirically that on the T-Finance dataset, abnormal node pairs actually exhibit higher pairwise similarity than normal node pairs, making local affinity useless in isolation. Methodology: The paper proposes MI-GAD (Multiscale Inconsistency learning for Graph Anomaly Detection), a GNN-free framework built on four components. (1) Individual Node Encoding: a plain MLP encodes each node's attributes independently, deliberately avoiding the neighborhood aggregation that corrupts anomaly signals in GNNs. A theoretical result (Theorem 1) formalizes that GNN message passing implicitly minimizes a local affinity objective but does so with reduced flexibility and signal quality compared to an explicit, decoupled formulation. (2) Hypersphere Space Mapping: all node embeddings are L2-normalized onto a unit hypersphere to prevent representation collapse, equalize norms, and provide a uniform geometric space for both local and global scoring. (3) Local Affinity Discrimination (LAD): inspired by group discrimination, a binary cross-entropy loss trains the model to assign high similarity to neighbor pairs (positive) and low similarity to non-neighbor pairs (negative), sampled to match neighbor count for scalability. This produces a local anomaly score equal to the mean neighbor cosine similarity. (4) Global Inconsistency Modeling (GIM): a learnable representation center c is defined as the mean of all hypersphere embeddings; node deviation JND = ||h̃_i − c||² measures each node's distance from the global normal distribution centroid, analogous to one-class classification (SVDD/DeepSVDD). Minimizing the mean deviation pulls normal nodes toward c while anomalies remain distant. Final anomaly scores additively combine the local affinity score and the node deviation, weighted by a scalar λ. Total time complexity is O(ND² + ND + ED), linear in graph size for fixed embedding dimension. Key Results: MI-GAD is evaluated on seven real-world datasets (Facebook, Reddit, Amazon, Yelp, T-Finance, Elliptic, T-Social—the last two with millions of nodes) against eight baselines including DOMINANT, CoLA, PREM, VGOD, TAM, GADAM, HUGE, and SmoothGNN. MI-GAD achieves the best AUROC on all seven datasets: 98.37% on Facebook (+1.94% over HUGE), 61.07% on Reddit, 90.99% on Amazon (+2.34% over HUGE), 63.84% on Yelp (+6.36% over SmoothGNN), 94.65% on T-Finance (+18.80% over SmoothGNN, the largest margin), 78.38% on Elliptic (+12.30% over SmoothGNN), and 69.52% on T-Social (+3.65% over SmoothGNN). AUPRC improvements are equally strong (51.79% on Facebook vs. 30.10% for GADAM; 63.45% on T-Finance vs. 14.09% for SmoothGNN). Several baselines run out of memory (24 GB GPU) on large graphs while MI-GAD scales to 5M-node T-Social. Ablation confirms all three non-trivial components (MLP backbone, hypersphere mapping, both LAD and GIM) are necessary; removing GIM causes catastrophic failure on T-Finance (21.32% AUROC), while removing LAD causes collapse on Facebook (33.91%). Strengths: - The core insight—that GNN smoothing is architecturally misaligned with anomaly detection—is both theoretically grounded (Theorem 1 connecting graph convolution to implicit local affinity optimization) and empirically validated with a clean GNN-vs-MLP ablation before the main method is introduced. - The global inconsistency module (node deviation) directly addresses community camouflage, a well-documented failure mode of local-only methods, and the combination of local and global signals produces complementary gains confirmed by ablation. - The method is simple, scalable (linear complexity in nodes and edges), and memory-efficient; it is one of the few that successfully handles the 5M-node T-Social graph without OOM errors, making it practical for real-world deployment. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The balance parameter λ requires dataset-specific tuning (values range from 0.2 to 0.5 across datasets with no principled selection criterion), and the paper offers no automatic or adaptive strategy for setting it, which limits out-of-the-box usability. - The global inconsistency module assumes a unimodal normal distribution (single centroid c), which may fail when normal nodes form multiple distinct clusters; the paper does not analyze or discuss this structural assumption or its breakdown conditions. - Evaluation is limited to node-level anomaly detection on static graphs; the framework's behavior on dynamic graphs, edge anomalies, or heterogeneous graphs is unexplored, constraining the scope of the claimed generality.
Citations
#84 — Innovative (65)

ExPosST: Explicit Positioning with Adaptive Masking for LLM -Based Simultaneous Machine Translation

Yuzhe Shang, Pengzhi Gao, Yazheng Yang, Jiayao Ma, Wei Liu, Jian Luan, Jingsong Su · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: ExPosST presents a clean engineering solution to the positional mismatch problem in LLM-based simultaneous translation via pre-allocated positional slots, enabling zero-recomputation KV cache reuse. The dual contribution of inference efficiency and policy-consistent training is well-motivated and thoroughly ablated. However, the core idea is an architectural adaptation rather than a conceptual advance, and evaluation is confined to a single small benchmark (IWSLT 2017).
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Simultaneous Machine Translation (SimulMT) requires generating target tokens in real time as source tokens stream in, creating a fundamental tension when using decoder-only LLMs. Modern LLMs rely on KV caching for efficient autoregressive decoding, but SimulMT's streaming nature breaks this: each newly arriving source token shifts positional indices of subsequent target tokens, invalidating cached KV states. Existing remedies either recompute the full KV cache (correct but prohibitively slow) or reuse stale caches (fast but positionally inconsistent). Prior approaches—SimulMask (ALiBi-specific), Group Position Encoding (non-integer positions incompatible with standard pretraining), and conversational prompting (frequent role-switching degrades fluency)—each sacrifice one of efficiency, positional consistency, or broad model compatibility. ExPosST targets all three simultaneously. Methodology: ExPosST introduces two tightly coupled components. First, Pre-allocated Positions Inference: rather than inserting source tokens into the middle of the sequence (which shifts target positions), the framework pre-reserves a fixed-length positional slot S^(i) of size L_slot for incoming source tokens. Target tokens are always generated starting at pos(s^(i)_start) + L_slot, so their positional indices never shift during READ/WRITE cycles—enabling zero-recomputation KV cache reuse. When source tokens overflow a slot, a new slot is allocated immediately after current output, and subsequent output resumes from the next invariant position. Second, Policy-Consistent Fine-tuning: training data is restructured to match the slot-based format used at inference (source sentence segmented into L_slot-sized chunks), and a policy-consistent attention mask forces each target token to attend only to the source prefix visible under the chosen simultaneous policy (wait-k or read-n). This eliminates the train/inference gap that plagues simpler fine-tuning schemes. Both components are required: ablations show removing either causes significant BLEU degradation. The default L_slot = 16 is chosen via sensitivity analysis, balancing translation quality and training sequence length (which follows a U-shaped curve as a function of L_slot). Key Results: On IWSLT 2017 across five English-to-X language pairs (Fr, De, Nl, It, Ro) with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, ExPosST(wait-k) outperforms GPE by 2–3 BLEU points at equivalent latency levels; ExPosST(read-n) surpasses Conversational SimulMT by over 1 BLEU point uniformly across the latency range. Results hold consistently across all five language pairs. On Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (En-Fr, En-De), ExPosST similarly dominates baselines, demonstrating architecture generality. On the ALiBi-based falcon-rw-1b, ExPosST matches SimulMask—the method specifically engineered for ALiBi—confirming encoding-scheme agnosticism. Computationally, ExPosST achieves the lowest median GFLOPs among all methods during inference: Prefix Fine-tuning is costliest (full recomputation), Conversational SimulMT suffers from prompt bloat due to role-switching markers, while ExPosST's slot design eliminates both overheads. COMET scores in the appendix corroborate BLEU findings across all five language pairs. Strengths: - Clean, principled solution to the positional mismatch problem: pre-allocating slots is a structural fix that works without modifying positional encodings, making it compatible with RoPE-based models (Llama, Qwen) out of the box. - Dual contribution: inference efficiency (zero KV recomputation) and training-inference alignment (policy-consistent masking) are both addressed and shown individually necessary via ablation. - Thorough empirical coverage: five language pairs, two mainstream LLMs, one ALiBi model, two decoding policies (wait-k and read-n), and both BLEU and COMET metrics. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to IWSLT 2017, a relatively small and clean benchmark; performance on longer, noisier, or out-of-domain speech (e.g., MuST-C) is untested, leaving generalization to realistic deployment scenarios unclear. - The slot length L_slot introduces a training-inference coupling: using a significantly smaller L_slot at inference than at training time causes substantial BLEU degradation (e.g., L_slot=4 vs. trained at 16 drops En-De wait-7 from 32.15 to 13.23), which could complicate deployment when the source sentence length distribution shifts. - No latency analysis beyond GFLOPs (e.g., wall-clock latency, memory footprint with multiple concurrent slots) is provided, leaving practical real-time deployment costs partially uncharacterized.
Search Results 2603.14903 PDF
#85 — Innovative (65)

Efficient and Effective Table-Centric Table Union Search in Data Lakes

Yongkang Sun, Zhihao Ding, Huiqiang Wang, Reynold Cheng, Jieming Shi · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: TACTUS contributes a well-motivated inversion of the search paradigm from column-centric to table-centric for table union search, backed by strong empirical results and order-of-magnitude efficiency gains. However, the core technical components — contrastive learning with hard negative mining, attention-based aggregation, and reranking — are established techniques applied in a new combination rather than fundamentally new methods. The work is a solid engineering contribution that advances the state of the art but operates within familiar methodological territory.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Data lakes contain information on the same subject fragmented across many tables, making table union search—finding the top-k tables that can be unioned with a query table to add more rows—a critical task. Existing state-of-the-art methods (Starmie, LIFTus) are column-centric: they learn embeddings per column and aggregate column-level unionability scores to rank tables. This approach has two fundamental flaws: (1) it overlooks holistic table-level semantics, leading to misrankings where structurally dissimilar but column-coincident tables outscore truly unionable ones, and (2) column-based filtering generates large, noisy candidate sets because every table sharing any column with any query column gets retrieved. The paper addresses both quality and efficiency gaps simultaneously. Methodology: TACTUS (TAble-Centric method for Table Union Search) inverts the search paradigm from column-to-table to table-to-column. Offline, it trains a table encoder using self-supervised contrastive learning with three novel components: (1) Positive table pair construction—for each table, a positive counterpart is created by jointly sampling rows and columns, with row sampling applied to both sides to simulate realistic unionable tables with overlapping but distinct content; (2) Two-pronged negative sampling—within each batch, tables with cosine similarity above threshold γ=0.9 are excluded as latent positives, and from the remaining tables only the top half by similarity score (hard negatives) are used for training, discarding easy negatives; (3) Attentive table encoder—a BERT backbone with priority TF-weighted serialization produces per-column embeddings, which are aggregated into a single table embedding via multi-head attention followed by an MLP projection, trained with InfoNCE loss. Online, TACTUS performs a single nearest-neighbor search over table embeddings to retrieve an initial set S_init of 3k candidates, then adaptively trims this to a compact pool S by scanning the ranked score distribution and cutting at a sharp drop (τ_drop=0.2) or absolute floor (τ_abs=0.5). Finally, dual-evidence reranking combines the table-level score μ_T with a lightweight FastText-based column alignment score μ_A (frequency-weighted value embeddings, max-alignment per query column) to produce the final score μ = μ_T + μ_A for top-k selection. Key Results: Evaluated on six benchmarks (SANTOS Small/Large, TUS Small/Large, Wiki Union, WDC) against Starmie, LIFTus, SANTOS, D³L, Sherlock, and SATO. TACTUS achieves average rank 1.0 across all metrics and datasets. On TUS Small: MAP@60=99.36%, P@60=98.76%, surpassing LIFTus by 2.27%/4.95%. On TUS Large: P@60=97.28%, exceeding LIFTus by 3.71% and Starmie by 12.20%. On Wiki Union (sparse tables, avg 2.62 cols), TACTUS achieves MAP@40=65.37% vs. SATO's 52.63%, a 12.74% gap—column-centric methods collapse here due to limited column context. Efficiency gains are decisive: offline processing is 12.8× faster than LIFTus on SANTOS Small (66s vs. 842s) and 11.8× faster than SATO on Wiki Union; online query processing is 11.8× faster than LIFTus and 34.95× faster than Starmie on TUS Large (4.18s total). TACTUS retrieves candidate sets 3–5× smaller than baselines with 89–95% truly unionable tables vs. 25–68% for Starmie/LIFTus. Embedding index size is also dramatically smaller: 97.1 MB on TUS Large vs. 3,719.5 MB for LIFTus. In low-data regimes (10% training data), TACTUS outperforms LIFTus by 31.82% MAP on Wiki Union. Strengths: - The table-first paradigm is well-motivated with a concrete failure mode of column-centric methods (Figure 1), and the ablation confirms that table-level embeddings alone (w/o rerank) already beat all baselines, validating the core thesis rather than attributing gains to reranking alone. - The two-pronged negative sampling is principled: distinguishing latent positives from hard negatives from easy negatives addresses a known weakness of in-batch contrastive learning, and the dynamic, batch-local implementation avoids data leakage. - The efficiency gains (order-of-magnitude in both offline and online stages) plus smaller index size make TACTUS practically deployable at data lake scale, not just a quality improvement. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The adaptive candidate retrieval relies on two hand-tuned thresholds (τ_abs=0.5, τ_drop=0.2) that are applied uniformly across all datasets; while the paper shows robustness, the interaction between these thresholds and different score distribution shapes (e.g., highly skewed vs. uniform) is underexplored, and the method could fail silently if score distributions shift at deployment time. - The column alignment score μ_A uses a simple max-alignment (each query column matched to its best candidate column) with equal weighting across query columns, ignoring structural asymmetry and column cardinality differences; this parameter-free sum combination may be suboptimal for tables with highly heterogeneous column counts or importance distributions.
Search Results 2603.17298 PDF
#86 — Innovative (65)

Language Models Don't Know What You Want: Evaluating Personalization in Deep Research Needs Real Users

Nishant Balepur, Malachi Hamada, Varsha Kishore, Sergey Feldman, Amanpreet Singh, Pao Siangliulue, Joseph Chee Chang, Eunsol Choi, Jordan Lee Boyd-Graber, Aakanksha Naik · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The central empirical finding — that four frontier LLM judges fail to predict user satisfaction better than a majority-class baseline on any of nine failure-mode aspects — is well-demonstrated and directly challenges the field's reliance on offline evaluation for personalization. The grounded taxonomy of nine failure modes from 1,044 human judgments provides actionable targets. However, the study is limited to 21 CS researchers on a single system, and the personalization approach itself is relatively straightforward prompt injection.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deep Research (DR) systems synthesize scientific literature into multi-section reports to help researchers cope with explosive publication growth, yet virtually none adapt to the individual researcher asking the query. Personalized DR could tailor content focus, framing, and depth to a user's background — but the field lacks both systems that do this and evaluation methodology that reveals whether personalization actually helps. A survey of 31 ACL'25 personalization papers finds that all evaluate offline (synthetic data and/or LLM judges) and only two run studies with real users, raising the question of whether offline evaluations adequately capture what users value. Methodology: The paper introduces MYSCHOLARQA (MYSQA), a three-stage personalized DR tool built on top of SCHOLARQA. Stage 1 constructs a persistent researcher profile (25 sentence-level inferences across five aspects: knowledge, research style, writing style, positions, audience) from researcher-selected papers using a reasoning LLM. Stage 2, given a user query, generates 16 personalized and generic actions across four categories (content, style, specificity, research ideas) that the user can toggle and edit before submission. Stage 3 runs a modified SCHOLARQA pipeline that injects the approved actions into all execution prompts (retrieval, section planning, generation) and highlights which text spans correspond to each action. The paper then conducts both offline evaluation (synthetic dataset of 281/291 dev/test query-user pairs from ScholarQA-CS2 and CS-PaperSum, 16 LLM-judged metrics, comparisons against OPENSCHOLAR, STORM, Perplexity Sonar, and OpenAI o3 DR) and online evaluation (90-minute interviews with 21 active CS researchers on Upwork, think-aloud protocol, open thematic analysis of 1,044 satisfaction judgments). An additional experiment tests whether four off-the-shelf LLM judges (Gemini-2.5 Flash, GPT-4.1, Claude-4 Sonnet, DeepSeek-V3) can predict the nine discovered failure modes as binary classifiers. Key Results: On offline benchmarks MYSQA performs well: profiles achieve 97% inference accuracy (Gemini-Pro), 99.4% category accuracy, and specificity scores of ~4.0/5; personalized actions win over generic actions at 91–95% win rate; reports beat all baselines on 3/5 quality metrics (answer coverage 91.4, citation precision 91.8, citation recall 81.4) and beat the base SQA system across the board. In the user study, 73% of profiles, actions, and reports are satisfactory. However, 27% reveal nine failure modes entirely invisible to offline metrics: four profile errors (DOMAIN 27.6% — terminology mismatches; OVERCLAIM 17.9% — overgeneralization; CONVENTION 12.8% — field-generic inferences; CONTRAST 12.2% — misrepresented contrasts), two action errors (NARROW 43.8% — overly constraining actions; OFFTOPIC 23.6% — query drift), and three report errors (UNINFORM 38.0% — insufficient depth; PRESENT 25.3% — wrong format/style; IGNORE 22.8% — dropped requirements). Critically, none of the four LLM judges predict user satisfaction better than a majority-class baseline on any of the nine aspects, regardless of the number of few-shot examples or whether metric definitions are provided. Strengths: - The core empirical finding — that LLM judges systematically fail to predict user satisfaction even with full context, few-shot examples, and well-designed prompts — is demonstrated rigorously with statistical significance testing (Bonferroni-corrected binomial tests) across multiple models and prompt variants. - The qualitative taxonomy of nine failure modes is grounded in 1,044 human judgments with 90% inter-annotator agreement, giving the community concrete, actionable targets for future work. - The system itself makes non-trivial design choices (profile attribution with paper citations, action-level highlights, persistent profiles vs. per-query clarification) that are validated against user preferences in both formative and usability studies. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The online study is limited to 21 CS researchers recruited via Upwork, all using MYSQA specifically, limiting generalizability to other user populations, domains, and DR systems; the nine failure modes may not be exhaustive or representative of non-CS users. - The paper evaluates only prompt-based personalization (profile → action → report via Claude-4 Sonnet) and does not compare against fine-tuned personalization approaches or retrieval-augmented user modeling methods, leaving open whether the identified failure modes are fundamental or artifact of the prompt-only design. - Latency is a practical barrier (profile generation ~3 min, report ~5 min), and the paper notes that MYSQA's positive reception may partly reflect novelty effects rather than sustained utility; longitudinal studies are acknowledged but not conducted.
Recommended 2603.16120 PDF
#87 — Innovative (65)

LumosX: Relate Any Identities with Their Attributes for Personalized Video Generation (21 ▲ )

Jiazheng Xing, Fei Du, Hangjie Yuan, Pengwei Liu, Hongbin Xu, Hai Ci, Ruigang Niu, Weihua Chen, Fan Wang, Yong Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Mar 2026
Why this rank: LumosX introduces a technically principled dual-level treatment of face–attribute binding through Relational Rotary Position Embedding and structured attention masking, directly addressing the root cause of subject confusion in multi-subject video generation. Achieving state-of-the-art results with a 1.3B backbone against 5B–14B competitors demonstrates the efficiency of the architectural design, though the framework's tight coupling to face-attribute pairs limits generalization beyond human subjects.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Personalized multi-subject video generation requires precisely binding each person's face with their associated attributes (clothing, accessories, hairstyle) across the generated video. Existing methods either focus narrowly on single-face identity preservation or enable multi-subject personalization but lack explicit mechanisms to enforce face–attribute correspondence. When captions contain ambiguous subject descriptions (e.g., "a man on the left... and a man on the right..."), implicit textual modeling fails to prevent attribute entanglement and cross-subject mismatches. A further gap is the absence of public datasets with annotated face–attribute dependency structures suitable for training such models. Methodology: LumosX addresses this at both the data and model levels, built atop the Wan2.1 (1.3B) Diffusion Transformer text-to-video backbone. On the data side, a collection pipeline processes videos from Panda70M using Qwen2.5-VL (32B) to extract captions, detect human bounding boxes, segment attribute regions via SAM/GroundingDINO, and assign explicit face–attribute correspondences. After quality filtering, this yields 1.57M training videos (1.31M single-subject, 0.23M two-subject, 0.03M three-subject). On the model side, two novel attention modules are introduced. Relational Self-Attention uses (1) Relational Rotary Position Embedding (R2PE), which assigns shared temporal indices to face and attribute tokens within the same subject group while extending them along spatial axes, and (2) a Causal Self-Attention Mask (CSAM) that confines condition token interactions within each subject branch and allows unidirectional attention from denoising video tokens to condition tokens only. Relational Cross-Attention introduces a Multilevel Cross-Attention Mask (MCAM) with three correlation levels—strong (same semantic entity or subject group), weak (cross-subject group), and neutral—injected as a scaled additive bias into the cross-attention scores. Training proceeds in two phases: 15k iterations on single-subject data followed by 16k on mixed multi-subject data, requiring ~883 GPU-days on H20 GPUs. A new benchmark covering 220-video single-face and 500-video multi-subject test sets is also released. Key Results: On single-face identity-consistent generation, LumosX achieves ArcSim 0.542 and CurSim 0.575, substantially outperforming ConsisID (0.458/0.474) and Concat-ID (0.467/0.485). On multi-face identity-consistent generation, LumosX (0.510/0.540) is competitive with Phantom (0.508/0.536) and outperforms SkyReels-A2 (0.382/0.401) while using a much smaller base model (1.3B vs. 14B for SkyReels-A2). On subject-consistent multi-subject generation, LumosX achieves the best scores across nearly all metrics: Dynamic 0.723 (vs. 0.671/0.661), ViCLIP-T 0.260 (vs. 0.251/0.254), ViCLIP-V 0.932 (vs. 0.839/0.865), DINO-I 0.261 (vs. 0.192/0.216), and ArcSim 0.454 (vs. 0.271/0.444). Ablation studies confirm that R2PE improves ArcSim (+0.047), CSAM recovers CLIP-T, and MCAM at r=0.5 yields the largest combined gains (+0.066 ArcSim, +0.004 CLIP-T over R2PE+CSAM baseline). Strengths: - The dual-level treatment of face–attribute binding (positional encoding via R2PE and attention masking via CSAM/MCAM) is technically principled and directly addresses the root cause of subject confusion rather than relying on implicit textual disambiguation. - The data pipeline is self-contained and scalable, enabling construction of a large annotated training set and reproducible benchmark without manual labeling. - Achieves SOTA results with a 1.3B parameter backbone against methods using 5B–14B models, demonstrating efficiency of the architectural design. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The approach is validated primarily on human subjects; generalization to non-human multi-entity scenarios (e.g., multiple animals or objects without faces) is unclear, as the framework explicitly couples the concept of a "subject" to a face-attribute pair. - Training requires ~883 GPU-days, making reproduction or fine-tuning for new domains prohibitively expensive for most researchers. - The benchmark uses automated metrics (ArcFace similarity, CLIP scores) which may not fully capture perceptual quality or temporal coherence of attribute consistency across video frames; no human evaluation is reported.
Recommended 2603.20192 PDF
#88 — Innovative (65)

BISTRO: a general purpose oracle for macroeconomic time series

bis.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: BISTRO represents a meaningful contribution by demonstrating that domain-specific fine-tuning of a general-purpose time series foundation model yields dramatically better macroeconomic forecasts, particularly at longer horizons. The conditional forecasting framework — where covariate paths can be imposed without re-estimation — is operationally clean and practically valuable for central bank scenario analysis. However, the core methodology is fine-tuning an existing architecture (MOIRAI) on curated domain data, which is a well-established transfer learning strategy rather than a fundamentally new approach. The partially compromised out-of-sample evaluation and inability to support causal inference limit its impact for the counterfactual policy exercises that constitute the primary use case.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Traditional macroeconomic forecasting requires building a bespoke model for each task — specifying functional form, estimating parameters, and validating — all for a single variable or country context. This is labor-intensive and inflexible: every change in the variable set, sample length, or frequency demands a new modeling cycle. The success of large language models (LLMs) as general-purpose zero-shot learners motivates asking whether a single foundational model, trained once on a broad macroeconomic corpus, can serve as a flexible forecasting tool for central banks without task-specific re-estimation. Existing general-purpose time series foundational models (e.g., MOIRAI) are trained on heterogeneous data spanning healthcare, energy, and finance, making them poorly calibrated for the specific distributional characteristics of macroeconomic series, especially at longer horizons and with covariates. Methodology: BISTRO (BIS Time-series Regression Oracle) fine-tunes the MOIRAI transformer architecture — itself built on the patch-based adaptation of the attention mechanism from NLP — on the BIS macroeconomic dataset comprising 4,925 time series across 63 economies from 1970–2024 (training window 1984–2024). Two key architectural constraints differentiate BISTRO from MOIRAI: (1) frequency is fixed to daily with a patch size of 32 days, accommodating monthly/quarterly series via last-value-carry-forward to enforce real-time publication lag discipline; (2) fine-tuning targets macroeconomic series exclusively, enabling the attention weights to learn macro-relevant co-movement patterns (e.g., oil-inflation, policy rate-inflation). The model produces probabilistic forecasts over a user-specified prediction window (PDT) given a context window (CTX) of historical data. At inference, weights are frozen — no re-estimation occurs — making it a single-pass foundational forecaster. The workflow supports both unconditional (baseline) and conditional (scenario) forecasts, where future paths for covariates can be fixed to explore counterfactuals. Benchmarks are AR(1) and the unmodified MOIRAI. Key Results: The headline qualitative result is BISTRO's ability to anticipate the persistence of the 2021–22 US inflation surge: starting from June 2021, BISTRO correctly projects a high and sustained inflation trajectory while AR(1) and MOIRAI mechanically revert toward the historical mean. Quantitative evaluation uses relative RMSFE (R-RMSFE) against AR(1) across four held-out windows (1995, 2005, 2015, 2023–24) not in the training sample. For the US, BISTRO achieves R-RMSFE of 0.946 at 3-month, 0.804 at 6-month, and 0.481 at 12-month horizons (vs. MOIRAI's 1.241, 1.335, 1.349 at the same horizons) — a dramatic improvement especially at long horizons. Euro area results are similar directionally (0.898 at 12 months vs. MOIRAI's 1.689). Other advanced economies show mixed results closer to benchmark. EMEs show systematic improvement at medium term. In conditional scenario analysis with oil price shocks (−3%, +3%, +9%), BISTRO correctly captures non-linear, asymmetric pass-through to inflation (larger positive shocks have disproportionate effects; negative shocks have smaller pass-through), while MOIRAI's attention weights on oil prices are effectively zero, producing identical forecasts across all scenarios. The Taylor rule exercise shows BISTRO correctly infers that the inflation-policy rate link depends on the length of historical context provided (12-year low-inflation regime vs. 37-year multi-regime history), while MOIRAI is insensitive to context length. Strengths: - Domain-specific fine-tuning decisively outperforms the general-purpose MOIRAI at longer horizons (12-month R-RMSFE of 0.481 for the US vs. 1.349), validating the hypothesis that macro-specific training is necessary for meaningful covariate integration - No re-estimation at inference: the frozen-weight foundational model generalizes across countries, variables, and frequencies without any user-side parameter fitting, dramatically lowering deployment cost - Conditional forecasting via covariate path conditioning is operationally clean — researchers can impose scenario paths for any variable without modifying model structure, enabling straightforward scenario/stress analysis Weaknesses / Limitations: - The 2021–22 inflation surge evaluation is partially compromised by training sample overlap: the inflation series was in the training data (albeit in month-on-month form without an "inflation" label), making this a weaker out-of-sample test than advertised - BISTRO captures historical correlations only and explicitly cannot support causal/structural inference: conditioning on policy rates yields a spurious positive correlation with inflation (policy tightening appears inflationary), undermining its usefulness for the counterfactual policy exercises central banks most need - Performance for other advanced economies (e.g., UK, New Zealand in 2023–24) is notably weak, with large errors relative to AR(1), and results for EMEs at the 12-month horizon barely beat the benchmark (R-RMSFE 0.969), suggesting uneven generalization across institutional and data-quality contexts
Citations
#89 — Innovative (64)

Self-Purification Mitigates Backdoors in Multimodal Diffusion Language Models

Guangnian Wan, Qi Li, Gongfan Fang, Xinyin Ma, Xinchao Wang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 64 for identifying a genuine security gap in multimodal diffusion language models and designing an architecturally motivated defense that exploits MDLMs' unique masked-input flexibility. The Fisher-Jacobian saliency estimator with Hutchinson approximation is a technically sound contribution. However, the work addresses a niche threat model (MDLMs are not yet widely deployed), evaluates only projector-level fine-tuning attacks, and does not consider adaptive adversaries that could craft low-saliency triggers to evade the defense — limiting both the current scope and demonstrated robustness.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Diffusion Language Models (MDLMs) — which generate text via iterative masked-token denoising rather than autoregressive decoding — have emerged as competitive alternatives to AR-based multimodal LLMs, but their vulnerability to backdoor attacks is entirely unexplored. Existing backdoor defenses are designed for AR models and cannot be directly transferred to the MDLM setting, leaving a critical security gap. Adversaries can exploit this by poisoning fine-tuning datasets, causing models to exhibit attacker-controlled behaviors (content insertion, targeted refusal, semantic misclassification) whenever a visual trigger is present while behaving normally on clean inputs. Methodology: The paper introduces Diffusion Self-Purification (DiSP), a two-stage defense that exploits a property unique to MDLMs: their ability to accept partially masked inputs. The core insight is that backdoor activation is dominated by a small subset of high-saliency visual tokens — masking these tokens at inference time suppresses trigger-induced behavior without substantially degrading clean utility. Saliency scores for each visual token are estimated via the Fisher–Jacobian quadratic form, approximated efficiently using a Hutchinson estimator with Monte Carlo probes (avoiding the prohibitively expensive explicit J⊤FJ product). The top-ρ fraction of visual tokens by saliency are replaced with mask embeddings, and the compromised model is run on these masked inputs to generate purified responses. These purified (image, clean-response) pairs replace the poisoned responses in the training set, which is then used to fine-tune the backdoored model — eliminating the embedded backdoor without requiring auxiliary clean models or external reference data. Key Results: Experiments on LLaDA-V and LaViDa across three backdoor targets (content insertion, targeted refusal, semantic misclassification) show that without defense, backdoored models achieve ASR >90% on triggered inputs. DiSP reduces ASR to ≤1.5% in all settings on LLaDA-V (e.g., content insertion: 92.5% → 0.5%; targeted refusal: 98.5% → 1.0%; misclassification: 94.5% → 0.5%), with clean performance (MMMU accuracy) essentially unchanged (49.56 → 49.22, within noise). Baseline defenses — random data drop, model pruning, and the AR-targeted BYE filter — leave ASR between 15% and 100% on LLaDA-V. DiSP also generalizes across four trigger types (noise patch, multi-trigger, blended trigger), reducing ASR to 0% in three of four cases. Ablation confirms both saliency-guided token selection and the masked-input rewriting step are necessary: removing either causes ASR to remain ≥88%. Strengths: - Identifies and exploits a structural property of MDLMs (masked-input flexibility) that has no analogue in AR models, producing a defense that is architecturally motivated rather than heuristic. - Requires no auxiliary clean models, no external reference data, and no knowledge of which samples are poisoned — matching the realistic threat model where defenders only control the training pipeline. - The Fisher–Jacobian saliency estimator is computationally practical: the Hutchinson approximation avoids forming the full Jacobian, making the method scalable to large vision encoders. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments fine-tune only the projector while keeping the vision encoder and LLM backbone frozen; it is unclear how DiSP performs when full model fine-tuning is used, which is a more powerful (and increasingly common) attack surface. - The defense is evaluated on patch-style visual triggers; adaptive attacks that specifically craft triggers to have diffuse, low-saliency visual signatures could potentially evade the saliency-based masking step, and no such adaptive adversary is considered. - Only two MDLMs (LLaDA-V, LaViDa) and relatively small poisoned datasets (1,000 training samples) are tested; scalability to larger pre-training-scale poisoning scenarios remains undemonstrated.
Recommended 2602.22246 PDF
#90 — Innovative (64)

RouterKGQA: Specialized--General Model Routing for Constraint-Aware Knowledge Graph Question Answering

Bo Yuan, Hexuan Deng, Xuebo Liu, Min Zhang · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: RouterKGQA introduces a practical and well-motivated routing mechanism between specialized and general models using a cheap reachability signal, combined with an explicit Constraint-aware Reasoning Path representation that meaningfully improves precision on comparative and superlative queries. The relation-only beam search and hop-count stopping criterion are clever efficiency contributions. The approach is limited to Freebase with SPARQL annotations and the routing signal is coarse, but the cost-performance tradeoff is compelling.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) sits at the intersection of two unsatisfying extremes: retrieval-based methods using small fine-tuned LLMs are efficient but suffer from relation hallucination and fail to encode implicit temporal/entity/numeric constraints in questions; agent-based methods using large general LLMs handle these failure modes but require 5–27× more LLM calls and cost. No prior work explicitly bridges these paradigms by routing queries selectively between them, nor do existing specialized models generate explicit constraint representations for deterministic answer filtering. Methodology: RouterKGQA is a three-stage pipeline. Stage 1 trains a small specialized model (Llama-2-7B or Llama-3.1-8B via LoRA) to generate Constraint-aware Reasoning Paths (CRPs): a flat relation sequence (main path) plus typed constraints (entity, numeric, string) over intermediate variables, converted deterministically from SPARQL training annotations. A reachability check executes the skeleton query on Freebase; if it returns empty results, the question is routed to Stage 2. Stage 2 runs an efficient general-model (GPT-4o-mini) repair: the LLM first generates a one-shot reasoning blueprint (sequence of natural-language subtasks), then KG-guided beam search over relations (not entity–relation pairs) expands candidate paths hop-by-hop. SBERT cosine similarity performs coarse filtering at each step; the LLM makes one selection call per depth level, capped at d+1 total calls where d is the hop count predicted by the specialized model (accurate in 97–99% of cases). Stage 3 converts the corrected CRP to SPARQL and executes it with progressive constraint relaxation (string → numeric → entity) as fallback. Key Results: On WebQSP and CWQ (Freebase), RouterKGQA (Llama-3.1-8B + GPT-4o-mini) achieves 91.15/86.38 F1 and 89.66/83.78 F1 respectively, outperforming the previous best F1 baseline by +4.30 on WebQSP and +2.84 on CWQ, while matching or exceeding Hits@1. It requires only 1.15 average LLM calls per question—comparable in cost to pure retrieval-based methods ($0.57/10K questions vs. $0.21–$0.89 for retrieval baselines) and 14–34× cheaper than agent-based baselines (ToG: $19.35, PoG: $8.07). Ablation confirms constraints primarily improve precision (F1 drops sharply without them; Hits@1 barely changes), with entity constraints contributing most. The flat CRP format achieves higher skeleton accuracy than S-expressions on both benchmarks (+3.35% WebQSP, +1.62% CWQ) and substantially better exact match (+7.26% WebQSP). On complex CWQ query types, RouterKGQA dominates GCR most on comparative (+32.40 F1) and superlative (+34.65 F1) questions—exactly where constraint-free path generation fails most. Sub-question consistency analysis shows 34.7% of RouterKGQA paths are semantically exact-equal to the question vs. 10.8% for GCR and 5.9% for GNN-RAG. Strengths: - The routing mechanism elegantly combines the efficiency of specialized models with the generalization of large agents; the reachability signal is cheap, interpretable, and requires no learned router. - CRP representation is a principled improvement over both flat relation paths (adds explicit constraints) and nested logical forms (easier for small models to generate), with empirical validation of generation accuracy. - The relation-only beam search in Stage 2 is a practically important efficiency gain over prior entity+relation search; using the specialized model's hop prediction as stopping criterion avoids extra LLM calls while being highly reliable (97–99% depth accuracy). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The routing criterion (main-path reachability) is a binary, coarse signal: a reachable but semantically wrong path is never repaired, which is a real failure mode the paper acknowledges but does not address. - Evaluation is confined to two English Freebase benchmarks; generalization to other KG schemas (Wikidata, domain-specific graphs), other languages, or open-domain settings is untested and non-trivial given the SPARQL-to-CRP conversion relies on Freebase-specific structure. - CRP constraints are limited to flat entity/numeric/string types; nested or compositional constraints (e.g., superlative over a filtered subset) cannot be represented, bounding the approach on harder future benchmarks. - Cost comparison uses pricing as of February 2026 and assumes the specialized model is already fine-tuned; training cost and the need for SPARQL-annotated training data (not always available) are not factored into the efficiency analysis.
Citations 2603.20017 PDF
#91 — Innovative (64)

Tabular LLMs for Interpretable Few-Shot Alzheimer's Disease Prediction with Multimodal Biomedical Data

Sophie Kearney, Shu Yang, Zixuan Wen, Weimin Lyu, Bojian Hou, Duy Duong-Tran, Tianlong Chen, Jason H. Moore, Marylyn D. Ritchie, Chao Chen, Li Shen · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: TAP-GPT is the first systematic application of a tabular-specialized LLM to multimodal AD biomarker prediction, preserving tabular structure through the encoder while enabling language-grounded reasoning—a qualitatively different capability from both TabPFN and serialization approaches. The self-reflection stability analysis is a novel evaluation dimension for multi-agent clinical deployment. Rigorous experimental design with 10 seeds, four modalities, and principled missingness handling strengthens the contribution. Limited to binary classification excluding MCI and small dataset sizes temper the translational claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Accurate Alzheimer's disease (AD) diagnosis depends on tabular biomarker data (AT[N] framework: amyloid, tau, neurodegeneration markers), yet clinical datasets are small, high-dimensional, and frequently incomplete — conditions where deep learning fails to beat classical tree-based methods. Existing tabular foundation models (e.g., TabPFN) are strong predictors but produce no natural-language reasoning, limiting clinical interpretability and integration into agentic workflows. LLM-based serialization approaches (converting tables to text) lose column structure and become token-inefficient at scale. The paper addresses this gap by adapting a tabular-specialized LLM — one that preserves structural table representations — to the multimodal AD biomarker prediction task in a few-shot in-context learning (ICL) paradigm. Methodology: The authors propose TAP-GPT (Tabular Alzheimer's Prediction GPT), built on TableGPT2-7B (encoder-decoder: schema-aware table encoder + Qwen2.5-7B LLM decoder). The tabular encoder is frozen; only the decoder is adapted via QLoRA (4-bit quantization, low-rank adapters), keeping parameter count low. Task formulation is tabular few-shot ICL: a prompt contains k labeled example rows above an unlabeled target patient row, and the model predicts a binary label {AD=1, CN=0} via constrained decoding. Four prompt formats are tested: zero/few-shot × tabular/serialized. Interpretability is induced purely via prompt engineering ('Let's think step by step') requesting structured JSON outputs (label, confidence, reasoning), with no architectural changes. Feature selection (LASSO) is applied upstream for high-dimensional imaging datasets (74 ROIs reduced to top 16). Experiments cover four ADNI-derived datasets: QT-PAD biomarkers (333 subjects, 15 features), and region-level amyloid PET (n=1,031), tau PET (n=610), and structural MRI (n=1,031) datasets. Baselines include vanilla LLMs (Qwen2.5-Instruct, Qwen3-8B, GPT-4.1-mini), TableGPT2/TableGPT-R1 (backbone without AD fine-tuning), TabPFN, and classical ML (LR, RF, SVM, XGBoost). All experiments run across 10 random seeds; hyperparameters tuned via Optuna on validation F1. Key Results: On QT-PAD (few-shot tabular, k=8), TAP-GPT achieves mean F1=0.83 (standard) and F1=0.89 with chain-of-thought interpretable prompting, outperforming TabPFN (F1≈0.71), all classical ML baselines (best ≈0.74 for Random Forest), vanilla Qwen2.5 (F1≈0.28), and TableGPT2 (F1≈0.70). On imaging datasets (k=4, p=16), TAP-GPT shows consistent stepwise improvements over backbone: in tau PET, Qwen2.5→F1=0.43, TableGPT2→0.48, TAP-GPT→0.68 vs. GPT-4.1-mini→0.53. On amyloid PET, Interpretable TAP-GPT reaches F1=0.64, matching GPT-4.1-mini (F1=0.65). MRI is the weakest modality; GPT-4.1-mini leads (F1=0.66) with TAP-GPT's serialized interpretable variant close behind (F1=0.65). Under simulated missingness (QT-PAD), TAP-GPT maintains competitive F1 through 40% missing data before degrading; under natural real-world missingness (541 held-out patients, avg 24.7% missing), performance remains stable across missingness bins without imputation. On self-reflection stability, TAP-GPT shows minimal performance drop when prompted to revise its own predictions, while GPT-4.1-mini degrades substantially across all three imaging modalities — a critical property for multi-agent system deployment. Feature selection (LASSO, top-16) is essential: TAP-GPT degrades sharply with all 72 ROIs; TableGPT-R1 collapses to near-zero F1 at p=72 across modalities. Strengths: - First systematic application of a tabular-specialized LLM to multimodal AD biomarker prediction, demonstrating a principled architecture that preserves tabular structure (encoder) while enabling language-grounded reasoning (decoder) — a qualitatively different capability from both TabPFN and serialization-based LLMs. - Rigorous experimental design: 10 random seeds, four modalities, four prompt formats, hyperparameter tuning via Optuna, strict ICL pool isolation preventing data leakage, and ablations over both k (context size) and p (feature count). - Practical missingness handling without imputation, validated on both synthetic and real-world incomplete ADNI cohorts, is a genuine clinical advantage over classical pipelines. - Self-reflection stability analysis is a novel and practically meaningful evaluation dimension for positioning the model within multi-agent clinical decision systems. Weaknesses / Limitations: - TAP-GPT is architecturally constrained to the frozen TableGPT2 encoder, preventing decoder upgrade to stronger backbones (e.g., Qwen3, GPT-4-class models); GPT-4.1-mini — a much larger proprietary model — outperforms TAP-GPT on MRI and is competitive on amyloid PET, suggesting the backbone is the binding constraint. - Interpretable reasoning outputs contain factual errors (e.g., misinterpreting FDG directionality, hallucinating values from other rows), yet the paper treats these qualitatively rather than quantitatively measuring reasoning accuracy — a significant gap given the clinical safety stakes. - Evaluation is limited to binary AD vs. cognitively normal classification, excluding the clinically important mild cognitive impairment (MCI) intermediate stage and longitudinal progression modeling, which limits practical translational relevance. - Dataset sizes for the key QT-PAD experiment are very small (333 subjects, 96 AD), and the imaging datasets involve extreme class imbalance (e.g., tau PET: 484 CN vs. 126 AD); the few-shot regime (k=4–8 training examples per inference) is atypical compared to standard supervised learning evaluations, making it difficult to assess how TAP-GPT would perform in more data-rich settings.
Citations 2603.17191 PDF
#92 — Innovative (64)

Parallel Node-Breaker Based Transient Stability Simulations Using Multi-Area Thevenin Equivalents

authorea.com · karypis - new results
Why this rank: This paper addresses a genuine and timely gap — no prior parallel TSS framework handled Node-Breaker models — and delivers the non-obvious insight that NB's larger matrices actually improve parallel scalability by delaying the global-link bottleneck. The new G and H mapping matrices exploit NB-specific structural properties to eliminate matrix multiplications, and the weighted METIS partitioning with a priori saturation heuristic is a practical contribution. Speedups of 18–28× on large systems represent a meaningful advance over the BB-MATE baseline's 3–6×. Limited by shared-memory-only implementation and constant-admittance load simplification.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Power system transient stability simulation (TSS) is computationally expensive, and the industry is shifting toward Node-Breaker (NB) models that represent substations at the breaker and switch level rather than collapsing each substation to a single bus (Bus-Branch, BB). NB models are essential for simulating protection relay actions, breaker failures, switching operations, and cascading fault analysis, but they produce system matrices 37–50× larger than their BB counterparts, making real-time or near-real-time simulation intractable with sequential solvers. Existing parallel TSS methods were developed exclusively for BB models; no parallel framework existed that natively handled the Sparse Tableau formulation required for NB representation. Methodology: The paper extends the Multi-Area Thévenin Equivalents (MATE) framework—originally a coarse-grained space-parallel method based on Kron's diakoptics—to NB models represented via the Sparse Tableau Approach (STA). STA encodes KCL, KVL, and element port equations (including circuit breakers modeled by a binary parameter τ) into a single sparse matrix M, avoiding explicit Network Topology Processing. The key contributions are: (1) A distributed STA construction where each substation builds its own local tableau in parallel, and inter-substation series elements contribute cross-terms when substations are assembled into per-subnetwork tableaux. (2) Three new NB-specific mapping matrices—F (boundary station to boundary node), G (local node to boundary node, stored as a list since each row has one nonzero), and H (global link to boundary node, also list-storable because each NB boundary node connects to exactly one global link)—replacing the BB matrices Q, R, D, C. These eliminate a full matrix–vector multiply required in the BB case. (3) A weighted graph partitioning strategy using METIS where each substation's weight equals its tableau order (eq. 12), achieving imbalance factors ≤10% across all tested systems. (4) Hybrid space + task parallelism using OpenMP: network subproblems are solved space-parallel while machine/control DAEs are distributed task-parallel across all processors regardless of subnetwork assignment. Numerical integration of DAEs uses the multi-stage Adomian Decomposition method. The sequential bottleneck is the global link solve Z^l i^l = e^l, which grows with partition count; the paper derives a heuristic (ΔR/ΔN^S vs. N^S) to predict saturation before running exhaustive sweeps. Key Results: Validation on the WECC 3-machine 9-bus system shows exact waveform match (terminal voltages, rotor angles, field excitation, slip) between MATE-NB and a sequential Y-Bus BB solver, confirming correctness. Performance is evaluated on three large systems with Breaker-and-Half (BAH) substation configuration on a 64-core Intel Xeon Platinum 8352Y @ 2.2 GHz / 256 GB RAM node: Polish 2383-bus (expands to 14,302 nodes, STM order 87,592) achieves 2.8–3.3 s for a 10 s simulation run, with peak speedup 18.3× at 32 areas/32 processors; Pegase 9241-bus (68,542 nodes, STM order 460,432) achieves 23.5–28 s with peak speedup 24.86× at 44 areas/44 processors; Pegase 13651-bus (91,334 nodes, STM order 594,078) achieves 30–34.6 s with peak speedup 27.64× at 56 areas/56 processors. By contrast, MATE-BB saturates at only 6–8 areas and ≤15 processors with speedups of 2.66–5.57×, because BB subnetwork matrices are too small to benefit from more parallelism. The NB matrices—37–50× larger—allow far more partitions before the Z^l bottleneck dominates, enabling proportionally greater parallelism. Execution times are consistent across bus faults, line faults, and generator outage scenarios (100 runs each), demonstrating robustness. Strengths: - The insight that NB models are inherently better suited to MATE than BB models—because larger subnetwork matrices delay the global-link bottleneck—is both non-obvious and empirically validated across three large systems - The new G and H mapping matrices exploit a structural property of NB topology (each boundary node connects to exactly one global link) to eliminate matrix multiplications present in the BB case, reducing memory and compute overhead - The weighted graph partitioning scheme with substation-level granularity achieves ≤10% imbalance factors, and the ΔR/ΔN^S heuristic provides a low-cost a priori estimate of the optimal partition count, avoiding full parameter sweeps in practice Weaknesses / Limitations: - The solver is implemented only in a shared-memory (OpenMP) paradigm; distributed-memory scaling (MPI across nodes) is not explored, limiting applicability to single multi-core servers and leaving open how the approach scales beyond 64 cores or to continent-scale grids - Loads are modeled exclusively as constant admittances (I_L = 0), a simplification that avoids dynamic load modeling and may limit fidelity for voltage stability or load-shedding scenarios common in operational planning - The paper does not report comparison against existing sequential NB TSS solvers in terms of wall-clock time overhead per time step (only parallel vs. parallel BB is compared), making it difficult to assess the raw cost of STA relative to Y-Bus-based approaches at equal model fidelity
Search Results
#93 — Innovative (64)

TabGeoFlow: A Geometric Flow Matching Model for Tabular Data Synthesis

ojs.aaai.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The geometric manifold-constrained regularization of flow matching vector fields is a principled and creative approach to the privacy-utility trade-off in tabular generation, more targeted than standard parameter regularization or DP. However, utility and fidelity gains over the best baselines are marginal, and the privacy claims rest on empirical DCR/MIA metrics without formal guarantees, limiting the strength of the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: High-fidelity tabular generative models (GANs, VAEs, diffusion models) achieve strong statistical and utility performance, but their capacity to accurately fit complex distributions also causes them to memorize training instances, making them vulnerable to Membership Inference Attacks (MIA). Existing mitigations either apply indirect regularization (L1/L2 parameter norms, adversarial objectives) or rely on Differential Privacy, which incurs severe utility degradation. This paper hypothesizes that memorization stems from generative trajectories deviating off the intrinsic data manifold to "reach for" specific training points, and proposes addressing this by directly regularizing the geometry of the flow matching vector field. Methodology: TabGeoFlow extends Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) with a Geometric Structure-Aware Module. A shared Transformer backbone encodes the current state and feeds four parallel heads: (1) a Principal Flow Head predicting the tangent-aligned component v‖, (2) a Residual Flow Head predicting the normal component v⊥, (3) a Tangent Basis Head outputting an orthonormal k-dimensional basis Q_t (k=30 fixed) approximating the local tangent space, and (4) an Uncertainty Proxy (Entropy) Head outputting ρ_t ∈ [0,1] to drive a dynamic regularization threshold τ_dyn. The decomposition uses projection: v‖ = Q_t Q_t^T v_θ, v⊥ = v_θ − v‖. The total loss is L_Total = L_Flow + λ_SFR · L_SFR, where the Structural Flow Regularization (SFR) loss penalizes the ratio |v⊥|² / (|v‖|² + ε) exceeding a dynamic threshold τ_dyn = τ_end + (τ_start − τ_end) · ρ_t. This schedule applies weaker regularization near t≈0 (noisy prior region) and stronger regularization near t≈1 (data manifold), analogous to annealing. The Tangent Basis Head is trained implicitly end-to-end with no direct supervision on tangent accuracy; it is incentivized to align its basis with the dominant flow directions to minimize total loss. Key Results: Evaluated on seven public datasets (Adult, Default, Shoppers, Magic, Beijing, News, Diabetes) against eight baselines (CTGAN, TVAE, GOGGLE, GReaT, STaSy, CoDi, TabDDPM, TabSyn, TabDiff). On Trend Similarity Error Rate (lower is better), TabGeoFlow achieves 2.34% average vs. TabDiff's 2.29% and TabSyn's 2.94% — essentially on par with the best. On Shape Similarity Error Rate, TabGeoFlow averages 1.34% vs. TabDiff's 1.24% and TabSyn's 1.29%, again competitive. On TSTR downstream ML utility (average gap from real data), TabGeoFlow achieves 6.75 vs. TabDiff's 6.02 and TabSyn's 6.82 — marginally behind TabDiff but comparable. The primary advantage is in privacy: TabGeoFlow achieves average DCR of 50.45% (ideal = 50%), compared to TabDiff's 52.06% and TabSyn's 51.12%, with near-random MIA accuracy across all datasets. TabDDPM shows severe DCR deviation (59.37% average) indicating significant memorization. Ablation confirms SFR is the key driver: removing it degrades DCR to 51.11% / 48.46% on Adult/Default while utility drops marginally (AUC from .913 to .898 / .761 to .745). Replacing the dynamic threshold with a fixed one also degrades performance, validating the adaptive annealing schedule.
