Strengths: - The geometric inductive bias (manifold-constrained vector field decomposition) is a principled and causally direct approach to privacy-utility trade-off, more targeted than parameter regularization or DP noise injection. - The Uncertainty Proxy Head enabling a dynamic regularization threshold is a clever adaptive mechanism that handles the inhomogeneous density along generative trajectories (noisy early, structured late). - Strong empirical validation across 7 datasets and 8 baselines, with privacy metrics (DCR ≈ 50%, near-random MIA) substantially better than high-fidelity models like TabDiff and TabSyn without meaningful utility loss. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper claims to reduce memorization via off-manifold penalization, but the theoretical connection between suppressing v⊥ and preventing sample memorization is informal — there is no formal privacy guarantee (e.g., DP-style (ε,δ) bound), and the evaluation relies solely on empirical DCR and shadow-model MIA, which have known limitations as privacy metrics (cited but not fully addressed). - The tangent basis dimension k=30 is fixed as a universal hyperparameter across all datasets regardless of actual intrinsic dimensionality, and the tangent basis is learned implicitly without supervision — it is unclear whether it genuinely approximates the data manifold's tangent space or merely learns a convenient decomposition for loss minimization, making the geometric interpretation partly aspirational. - Fidelity and utility gains over the best baselines (TabDiff, TabSyn) are marginal and within noise on several datasets; TabGeoFlow does not decisively outperform on quality metrics, meaning the primary contribution is privacy protection at parity, not a new quality frontier.
Citations
#94 — Innovative (64)

Utterance-level Detection Framework for LLM -Involved Content Detection in Conversational Setting

aclanthology.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The paper delivers a meaningful engineering contribution by combining an LLM tokenizer with a lightweight recurrent transformer and the DFA training strategy, achieving near-SOTA accuracy at dramatically lower compute. However, the core detection paradigm is familiar, and the 98%+ accuracy figures on in-distribution data constructed via the same pipeline raise artifact concerns that the authors do not adequately address. The limited ecological validity of the human-LLM portion (20 participants, narrow age range) further tempers the claimed generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing LLM-generated text detectors are designed for static, document-level content (articles, essays, QA responses) and fail in conversational settings where text is inherently short, dynamic, hybrid (mixed human and LLM), and requires real-time per-utterance judgments. The core challenge is that statistical methods like DetectGPT rely on a single LLM prior (e.g., GPT-neo-2.7B), making them biased toward detecting GPT-family models while poorly detecting LLaMA- or Qwen-generated text. No prior dataset covered multi-turn human-LLM and LLM-LLM dialogues across diverse open-domain topics, leaving a critical gap for deployable real-time detection. Methodology: The paper contributes both a dataset and a model. The dataset contains over 120,000 dialogues across three categories — human-human (sourced from Wizard of Wikipedia, Persona-Chat, MuTual), LLM-LLM (generated by GPT-4o, LLaMA-3.1-70B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct using persona-and-scenario-grounded prompts with alternating turn generation), and human-LLM (20 recruited speakers interacting with LLMs blind to which model they faced). The model uses the LLaMA3 tokenizer for feature extraction (rather than full LLM hidden states or a generic tokenizer), feeding token embeddings into a custom 4-layer transformer with self-attention and cross-attention blocks. A recurrent architecture processes question-answer pairs turn-by-turn, passing an intermediate state matrix across rounds to accumulate conversational context. The key innovation is Detection Focus Augmentation (DFA): during training, first-speaker utterances are randomly dropped and their encoding weights down-weighted, with higher dropout for LLM-LLM dialogues than human-LLM, forcing the model to rely on second-speaker responses (the detection target) while still using first-speaker context. For variable-length dialogues, the model pads shorter inputs and applies a sliding window with majority voting for longer ones. Key Results: The proposed model achieves 98.14% accuracy on GPT-4o-generated human-LLM dialogues, 98.59% on LLaMA3.1, and 98.73% on Qwen2.5 at utterance level — outperforming SeqXGPT (98.11%, 91.73%, 90.51%) and Fast-DetectGPT at utterance level (52.31%, 81.01%, 73.57%). Critically, on unseen LLMs (Gemini-2.0-Flash and DeepSeek-v3, not in training), the model achieves 95.89% and 96.77%, demonstrating strong zero-shot generalization. Inference speed is 1630x faster than DetectGPT and 34x faster than Fast-DetectGPT on GPU, with only 0.15 GB memory versus 15+ GB for full LLM hidden state methods. Ablation confirms DFA improves accuracy from 93.27% to 98.23% on the full dataset, with the gap widening as second-speaker utterances become shorter relative to first-speaker ones (85.60% → 98.80% at 0.70x coverage). Using LLM tokenizer versus a general tokenizer improves accuracy by ~9 percentage points (89% → 98%) with no additional memory cost. Recurrence contributes a further ~11.4 point gain (86.79% → 98.23%) over a flat transformer baseline. Out-of-domain zero-shot accuracy on HC3 ranges from 78.06% (Wikipedia) to 92.83% (Open Q&A). Strengths: - The combination of LLM tokenizer (not full encoder) with a custom recurrent architecture achieves near-LLM-encoder accuracy at a fraction of the inference cost — a genuine engineering contribution with practical deployment value. - Prior-model-free design eliminates the systematic bias of perturbation-based methods, yielding consistently high accuracy across GPT-4o, LLaMA, Qwen, Gemini, and DeepSeek without needing to match the prior to the target LLM. - DFA is a well-motivated and empirically validated training strategy that directly addresses the imbalanced-utterance failure mode inherent to conversational detection. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The dataset's human-LLM portion is small (~2,000 dialogues with only 20 recruited participants) and age-restricted (18–27), raising questions about ecological validity and whether the training signal from real human-LLM interaction is sufficient or representative. - Evaluation is limited to two-party dialogues; multi-party conversations, voice transcripts, and code-switched or non-English settings are entirely unaddressed, limiting claimed generality. - The 98%+ accuracy figures are measured on held-out data constructed via the same pipeline as training data (same topic distributions, same LLM prompting conventions), making it unclear how much of the gain reflects genuine linguistic discrimination versus dataset artifacts.
Search Results
#95 — Innovative (64)

VideoDetective: Clue Hunting via both Extrinsic Query and Intrinsic Relevance for Long Video Understanding (45 ▲ )

Ruoliu Yang, Chu Wu, Caifeng Shan, Ran He, Chaoyou Fu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The spatio-temporal affinity graph with iterative relevance diffusion is a meaningful departure from unidirectional query-to-video matching, and the training-free plug-and-play design with consistent gains across 10+ heterogeneous backbones demonstrates genuine generality. The modality scaling finding — that the bottleneck is visual perception, not language reasoning — is a practically valuable insight that reframes resource allocation for long-video understanding.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Long video understanding with MLLMs is fundamentally constrained by limited context windows, forcing models to identify sparse query-relevant segments rather than processing entire videos. Existing methods rely on a unidirectional query-to-video matching paradigm — keyframe sampling, retrieval-based approaches, or agent-based reasoning — all of which treat each segment in isolation and ignore the video's intrinsic temporal and semantic structure. This leads to brittle clue localization, particularly for questions requiring multi-step reasoning where relevant evidence is scattered across non-contiguous segments. Methodology: VideoDetective frames long-video QA as iterative relevance state estimation over a Spatio-Temporal Affinity Graph. The video is first segmented using SigLIP visual embeddings (cosine similarity boundary detection), and segments become graph nodes connected by edges combining visual affinity (cosine similarity between node features) and temporal proximity (exponential decay kernel). Two state vectors are maintained: a sparse Injection Vector Y recording verified relevance scores at observed nodes, and a dense Belief Field F tracking estimated relevance across all nodes via graph diffusion. The core loop runs Hypothesis–Verification–Refinement iterations: (1) Hypothesis selects anchor segments via query decomposition into semantic facets (keyword sets + event descriptions), with iterative active sampling using either neighbor exploration (when evidence is insufficient) or global gap filling (when all facets are resolved); (2) Verification extracts multi-source evidence per segment — VLM captions, OCR via EasyOCR, and ASR via Whisper — scored using source-aware fusion of IDF-weighted lexical overlap and SigLIP embedding cosine similarity; (3) Refinement propagates scores via iterative graph diffusion F^(t+1) = β·W_norm·F^(t) + (1−β)·Y^(t+1), with symmetric normalized Laplacian ensuring convergence. Final segment selection uses Graph-NMS to enforce diversity while guaranteeing coverage of all query facets. The framework is training-free and plug-and-play. Key Results: On VideoMME-long without subtitles, VideoDetective improves InternVL-2.5 (8B) by 7.5% and Oryx-1.5 (7B) by 7.0%, and consistently improves all tested backbones (8B–32B) without task-specific tuning. In controlled comparison against LVNet, DVD, VideoAgent, and VideoRAG under identical backbone configurations, VideoDetective achieves 55.6% (vs. 50.3% for VideoRAG) with Qwen3-8B and 65.6% (vs. 62.0% for VideoRAG) with Qwen3-30B+SeedVL-1.5 on VideoMME-long. With SeedVL-1.5 (20B), VideoDetective achieves 67.9% on LongVideoBench, surpassing GPT-4o (66.7%), Gemini-1.5-Pro (64.0%), and LLaVA-Video-72B (63.9%) while using only ~10K tokens per video vs. ~100K for proprietary models — roughly 10× more efficient. Ablation shows graph propagation contributes 4.2%, the iterative active loop 4.6%, and multimodal textual evidence 5.7%. Modality scaling analysis reveals a striking asymmetry: upgrading the LLM from 8B to 30B gains only +0.2%, while upgrading the VLM gains +9.5%, indicating the performance bottleneck lies in visual perception rather than language reasoning. Strengths: - The graph diffusion mechanism is theoretically grounded (closed-form solution, convergence guarantees via spectral radius bound) and computationally efficient at O(TKk) with sparse operations, avoiding the O(K³) inversion cost. - The plug-and-play design with demonstrated gains across 10+ heterogeneous backbones (8B–32B, different architectures) is a strong indicator of genuine generality rather than overfitting to a specific model. - The modality scaling ablation provides a practically valuable insight — that the bottleneck is visual, not linguistic — which directly informs future research and compute allocation decisions. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The Verification step depends heavily on VLM self-reflection quality (detecting "missing keywords"); if the VLM fails to correctly identify absent evidence, the active search loop may converge prematurely or explore irrelevant neighborhoods, and the paper does not quantify how often this occurs or its impact on failure cases. - Despite claims of token efficiency, the framework requires multiple sequential LLM/VLM calls per video (up to 10 iterations × 9-frame windows plus preprocessing), adding significant wall-clock latency that is not reported — the token count comparison may understate actual inference cost for latency-sensitive applications. - The graph construction relies on a fixed cosine similarity threshold (θ_sim = 0.82) for segmentation and top-k = 8 neighbors, which are validated only on VideoMME-long; performance on highly dynamic or visually homogeneous long videos (e.g., lectures, sports) where visual similarity is a poor proxy for semantic relatedness is not examined.
Recommended 2603.22285 PDF
#96 — Innovative (64)

Look Where It Matters: High-Resolution Crops Retrieval for Efficient VLMs (56 ▲ )

Nimrod Shabtay, Moshe Kimhi, Artem Spector, Sivan Haray, Ehud Rivlin, Chaim Baskin, Raja Giryes, Eli Schwartz · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Mar 2026
Why this rank: AwaRes presents a clean and practical solution to adaptive resolution in VLMs by framing spatial escalation as a multi-turn tool-calling decision, which is compatible with standard inference stacks and KV-caching — a genuine deployment advantage over prior methods. The fused coupled-decision policy and the SFT-to-GRPO training pipeline are well-engineered, and the 4.4x speedup over VisionThink at equivalent accuracy is a strong practical result. However, the conceptual contribution is moderate: adaptive resolution selection is well-studied, the discrete 10-region crop vocabulary is coarse, and the approach is validated on only a single 7B backbone.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-language models (VLMs) increasingly require high-resolution inputs for detail-sensitive tasks such as document QA, chart understanding, and OCR in natural images. However, visual token count scales quadratically with resolution, making full high-resolution inference a major computational bottleneck. Existing approaches fall into two unsatisfying camps: token pruning methods reduce tokens globally but introduce irregular sequence lengths incompatible with standard inference stacks (e.g., vLLM), while resolution escalation methods like VisionThink retrieve the entire high-resolution image when escalation is triggered—wasting compute on regions irrelevant to the query. The key insight motivating this work is that the need for high fidelity is spatially sparse: most questions require fine detail in only a small portion of an image. Methodology: AwaRes (spatially Aware Resolution) implements a spatial-on-demand inference framework via a multi-turn tool-calling interface. The model first processes a low-resolution global view (height and width halved, RTR=0.25 baseline). If additional detail is required, it emits a structured GET_CROPS tool call selecting from a discrete candidate set C of 10 regions (four quadrants, four half-image strips, a center crop, and full image), after which the selected high-resolution crops are appended to the dialogue context and the model produces its final answer. This fused coupled-decision policy (CDP) jointly decides whether to escalate and where—treated as a single policy π_θ(C|q,I_low) where C=∅ means direct answer and C≠∅ means spatial escalation. The multi-turn structure enables KV-cache reuse: the low-resolution prefill is cached and reused in the second turn. Training proceeds in two stages. First, a cold-start SFT stage: supervision is generated automatically without manual annotation by (i) running a base VLM on both low- and full-resolution inputs and using LLaMA-3.3-70B as an LLM-as-a-Judge (LaaJ) to label whether the low-resolution answer is insufficient, and (ii) using an oracle grounding model (Qwen3-VL-A235B-A22B) to localize the answer region as a bounding box, which is mapped to the discrete crop set via IoU≥0.5. The SFT uses trajectory-level optimization with tool-call turns upweighted (w_t=5). Second, multi-turn GRPO refines the policy using a composite reward R(τ)=R_ans - C_tool, where R_ans is cosine similarity between sentence-transformer embeddings of predicted and ground-truth answers, and C_tool is an asymmetric cost that penalizes missed crop requests more heavily than unnecessary ones, while also penalizing total crop area via a λ‖C‖ term. Training data is 50K samples from ChartQA, DocVQA, TextVQA, LLaVA-Multi, and VisionThink-Smart. Key Results: Evaluated on six benchmarks (ChartQA, DocVQA, OCRBench, POPE, RealWorldQA, V*-Bench) using Qwen2.5-VL-7B as the base model, AwaRes achieves an average score of 80.30% versus the full-resolution baseline of 80.46%—a gap of only 0.16 points—while consuming only 36% of visual tokens (RTR=0.36). On ChartQA and DocVQA, AwaRes slightly exceeds the full-resolution baseline (+0.84 and +0.43 points respectively). Against the closest adaptive baseline VisionThink, AwaRes improves both accuracy (80.30 vs. 79.23) and efficiency (RTR 0.36 vs. 0.61). VisionThink also occasionally exceeds the full-resolution compute budget (RTR=1.15 on ChartQA). Against fixed-budget pruning at 70% retention, VisionZIP trails AwaRes by 3.83 average points. On wall-clock latency, AwaRes averages 0.61s per sample versus VisionThink's 2.71s—a 4.4× speedup—primarily because AwaRes encodes decisions in short structured tool calls (mean 6–16 characters) rather than verbose reasoning traces (mean 46–178 characters). The SFT→GRPO pipeline is ablated extensively: GRPO-only training barely uses the tool, SFT-only over-crops (RTR=0.49), and removing the area cost raises RTR to 0.42. LaaJ-based supervision yields 3.8 points higher average accuracy than ANLS-based labeling. Strengths: - Elegant spatial-on-demand design: fusing the escalation and localization decisions into a single first-turn policy action is conceptually clean and eliminates the need for auxiliary models or external heuristics at inference time. - Fully automatic data curation pipeline that generates multi-turn tool-use trajectories without any manual spatial annotations, with high inter-model agreement (96.88% between LLaMA and DeepSeek judges) indicating robustness. - Practical deployment friendliness: the discrete crop vocabulary produces predictable sequence lengths compatible with KV-caching and standard inference stacks, and response length variance is dramatically lower than VisionThink (std 6 vs. 206 characters on ChartQA). - Thorough ablation study isolating contributions of trajectory-level SFT, tool-call upweighting, GRPO reward components, and labeling strategy. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The crop vocabulary is restricted to 10 coarse, axis-aligned regions. Queries requiring attention to small objects at arbitrary locations (e.g., a detail in the center-right of a quadrant boundary) may require fetching a superset of necessary area, capping achievable efficiency gains; the authors acknowledge this and list continuous bounding-box prediction as future work. - The evaluation base model is a single 7B-parameter architecture (Qwen2.5-VL-7B), and all comparisons are constrained to this backbone. Generalization to other VLM families or larger models is not demonstrated, leaving open questions about whether the learned CDP policy transfers or whether the SFT/GRPO training costs remain tractable at scale. - The oracle grounding model (Qwen3-VL-A235B-A22B, 235B parameters) used for data curation is extremely large, making the data pipeline expensive to replicate and introducing a dependency on a model that is orders of magnitude larger than the student being trained.
Recommended 2603.16932 PDF
#97 — Innovative (64)

FlowScene: Style-Consistent Indoor Scene Generation with Multimodal Graph Rectified Flow (24 ▲ )

Zhifei Yang, Guangyao Zhai, Keyang Lu, YuYang Yin, Chao Zhang, Zhen Xiao, Jieyi Long, Nassir Navab, Yikai Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Scores 64 for filling a meaningful capability gap — unified multimodal-conditioned layout, shape, and texture generation — that prior graph-conditioned methods left open. The InfoExchangeUnit coupling graph message passing at every denoising step is a well-motivated architectural contribution with clean ablation support. However, each component (GCN, rectified flow, VQ-VAE) is well-established, evaluation is confined to synthetic 3D-FRONT, and the 16³ voxel resolution limits practical geometric fidelity.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Generating high-fidelity, style-consistent 3D indoor scenes with fine-grained user control remains an open problem. Language-driven retrieval methods (e.g., Holodeck, LayoutVLM) compose scenes from object databases but lack per-object control and produce style-incoherent results. Graph-conditioned generative models (e.g., CommonScenes, EchoScene, MMGDreamer) improve controllability via explicit relational structure but cannot generate textured scenes end-to-end, yielding low-fidelity outputs unsuitable for downstream tasks. No prior method unifies multimodal inputs (text + images), geometry generation, texture generation, and style coherence in a single framework. Methodology: FlowScene is a tri-branch generative model conditioned on multimodal scene graphs. Each node in the graph can carry text features (CLIP), visual features (DINOv2), or both; missing modalities are zero-padded. The three branches — Layout, Shape, and Texture — each operate via a dedicated Multimodal Graph Rectified Flow module. At the core is an InfoExchangeUnit, a triplet-GCN that performs message passing over the graph at every denoising timestep, incorporating the current noisy data state as additional node features. This tight coupling propagates relational constraints (spatial, size, style) across all objects jointly during generation rather than conditioning on a fixed graph embedding. The Layout branch generates 3D bounding boxes; the Shape branch generates sparse voxel latents via a shape VQ-VAE (Φ) on a 16³ grid; the Texture branch anchors Gaussian noise to the decoded voxel geometry and denoises structured latents via a texture VQ-VAE (Ψ) at 64³ resolution using DINOv2-derived multi-view features. All three branches use Transformer-based flow matching denoisers (rectified flow, 25 inference steps) and are trained independently on SG-FRONT / 3D-FRONT. At inference, layout and shape branches run in parallel; the texture branch runs sequentially after shape decoding. An application interface allows input via natural language (parsed by LLM into a scene graph) or interactive GUI selection. Key Results: On scene-level fidelity (FID/FIDCLIP/KID on top-down renderings), FlowScene outperforms all baselines in retrieval mode on bedrooms (FID 35.01 vs. MMGDreamer 42.38; FIDCLIP 3.14 vs. 4.48) and in pure generation mode across all room types. On object-level metrics (MMD, COV, 1-NNA), FlowScene achieves large gains: for nightstands MMD drops 43.9% and COV increases 45.4% over MMGDreamer; COV reaches 69.54% for beds vs. 42.59% for MMGDreamer. On the FPVScore ranking (1–5, higher is better), FlowScene scores 3.85 on style consistency vs. 3.25 for the next-best method. In the perceptual study (1–10 scale, 25 participants, 20 scenes), FlowScene achieves 8.72 on style consistency and 8.57 overall preference vs. 7.31 and 7.42 for MMGDreamer. For inference speed, FlowScene without the texture branch runs in 6.83 seconds — 84.9% faster than MMGDreamer (45.34s) — because rectified flow allows parallel layout+shape generation with fewer steps than diffusion. Inter-shape consistency (Chamfer Distance) shows order-of-magnitude improvements for lamps (0.19 vs. 2.29 for MMGDreamer). Ablations confirm all three exchange units contribute, with the shape exchange unit providing the largest quality gain when switching from diffusion to flow backbone. Strengths: - The tight-coupling of graph message passing at every denoising step (InfoExchangeUnit) is a principled and effective mechanism for enforcing holistic style consistency, clearly validated by ablations showing each exchange unit contributes independently. - The end-to-end textured scene generation — layout + shape + texture in a single unified framework — is a meaningful capability gap over prior graph-conditioned methods that stop at untextured geometry. - The inference speed improvement (84.9% faster than diffusion-based baselines) is practically significant and stems from a well-motivated architectural choice (rectified flow with near-linear ODE paths), not just engineering. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The model is trained and evaluated exclusively on the synthetic 3D-FRONT/SG-FRONT dataset (~5,754 scenes across three room types), leaving generalization to real-world scan data, outdoor scenes, or larger/more diverse environments entirely unvalidated. - Quality is bottlenecked by the upstream graph constructor: errors or ambiguities from the LLM/VLM that parses text or images into the scene graph directly propagate to the final scene, and the system has no mechanism to detect or correct such errors — a significant fragility in the full pipeline. - The texture branch runs sequentially after shape generation (not in parallel with layout), partially negating the speed advantage when textures are required (37.38s total), and the voxelization-based shape representation at 16³ resolution limits geometric detail for objects with fine topology.
Recommended 2603.19598 PDF
#98 — Innovative (64)

The Avengers: A Routing Recipe for Collective Intelligence in Language Models

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The Avengers demonstrates that a training-free, cluster-based routing framework over small open-source models can match or exceed frontier proprietary models on diverse benchmarks, which is a practically useful and well-validated finding. The thorough ablation coverage across embedding models, clustering algorithms, and data efficiency substantially de-risks the design. However, the compute budget mismatch (10-sample Self-Consistency vs. greedy decoding for GPT-4.1/4.5) weakens the headline comparison, and the core technical components — embedding, K-Means clustering, majority voting — are individually well-established.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Proprietary large language models (GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, GPT-4.5) dominate performance benchmarks, while open-source small language models (SLMs, ~7B parameters) remain competitive only on narrow tasks. The central question is whether a coordinated ensemble of small open-source models can match or exceed frontier proprietary models across a broad range of tasks. Prior routing approaches either require neural network training (making them expensive to retrain for new tasks/models) or mixture-based aggregation (which degrades for small models that lack strong instruction-following and long-context capabilities). Methodology: The Avengers is a two-stage, training-free routing framework built on four lightweight operations. In offline calibration: (1) queries from a validation set are embedded using a text embedding model (gte-qwen2-7B-instruct by default); (2) embeddings are clustered into k=64 groups via K-Means, defining semantic query types; (3) each candidate model is evaluated on the validation set and assigned a cluster-wise capability profile vector p = [p1, ..., pk]. At online inference: each test query is embedded and assigned to its nearest cluster; the top-performing model(s) for that cluster are selected; responses are generated via repeated sampling (10 rounds) with Self-Consistency (majority voting) for single-model selection or Model-Switch for multi-model. Automatic model selection from a pool of 22 candidates uses a rank-aggregation score s(m') = Σ 1/r^c_{m'} to select complementary models without human curation. The framework requires no neural network training, no prompt engineering, and no retraining when new datasets or models are introduced—only incremental re-evaluation and optional re-clustering. Key Results: Using 10 automatically selected ~7B models, Avengers achieves an average score of 70.54 across 15 diverse datasets, surpassing GPT-4.1 (69.20), GPT-4.5 (68.18), and GPT-4o (60.22) by 1.95%, 3.46%, and 17.14% respectively. It outperforms GPT-4.1 on 9 of 15 datasets, with large gains on AIME (+37.04%), MBPP (+31.86%), LiveMathBench (+26.03%), and KORBench (+15.41%). GPT-4.1 retains an advantage on knowledge-intensive tasks (GPQA, MedQA). On out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization across 5 held-out datasets, Avengers scores 74.42, surpassing the best trained router baseline EmbedLLM by 8.14%. Ablations confirm robustness: performance varies only 70.28–70.70 across five embedding models, 70.04–70.71 across five clustering algorithms, and remains above GPT-4.1/4.5 for cluster counts K ≈ 14–140. With only 3 auto-selected models, Avengers already matches GPT-4.1/4.5. Data efficiency experiments show that using only 30% of validation data still surpasses GPT-4.1/4.5. Strengths: - Training-free design with a single hyperparameter (k) that is broadly robust, enabling plug-and-play integration of new models or datasets at minimal incremental cost. - Demonstrates convincingly that a no-training router can outperform neural router baselines (RouterDC, EmbedLLM, MODEL-SAT) even when those baselines are given their peak test-set scores—an unusually fair and conservative comparison. - Thorough ablation coverage (embedding model, clustering algorithm, ensemble strategy, model count, data split ratio, cluster count) substantially de-risks the core design choices. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The comparison is inherently favorable: baselines are given access to the same 10 auto-selected models and enhanced with Self-Consistency (not their native setup), so reported gains partly reflect these non-standard enhancements rather than the routing mechanism alone. - GPT-4.1/4.5 are evaluated with greedy/default decoding while Avengers uses 10 samples per query via Self-Consistency; the compute budget is therefore not matched, making the head-to-head comparison misleading on inference cost grounds. - Performance relies on access to labeled validation queries per task for cluster scoring; the system cannot generalize to fully zero-shot settings where no labeled examples exist for a new domain.
Recommended
#99 — Innovative (64)

REAL: Regression-Aware Reinforcement Learning for LLM-as-a-Judge

Yasi Zhang, Tianyu Chen, Mingyuan Zhou, Oscar Leong, Ying Nian Wu, Michal Lukasik · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: REAL scores 64 for its clean theoretical contribution — proving MSE minimization implies optimal Pearson correlation — and the principled resolution of the policy-dependent reward problem via generalized policy gradients. The two-term gradient decomposition separating reasoning exploration from score prediction is conceptually appealing. The work is technically solid but operates within a well-explored design space (RL for LLM scoring), and the restriction to pointwise scoring over a single training dataset narrows impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-as-a-Judge systems assign numeric scores to model outputs, but this is fundamentally a regression task. Standard RL post-training (e.g., GRPO, REINFORCE) uses binary 0/1 accuracy rewards, treating a prediction of 1 and a prediction of 4 equivalently when the ground truth is 5 — ignoring ordinal structure entirely. Regression-aware SFT methods like RAFT and TRACT address the ordinal issue but remain confined to static, ground-truth trajectory supervision without active exploration of reasoning pathways. No prior work combined regression-aware objectives with RL exploration. Empirically, standard RL applied to LLM-as-a-Judge causes correlation metric collapse during training. Methodology: REAL (REgression-Aware Reinforcement Learning) introduces a policy-dependent regression reward: r_REAL(θ, x, c) = -(ŷ_θ(x,c) - y)² + λ log π_θ(y|c,x), where ŷ_θ is the RAIL expected-value predictor (weighted sum over digit token probabilities). Because this reward explicitly depends on policy parameters θ, the standard policy gradient — which assumes ∇_θr = 0 — is invalid. The authors invoke the generalized policy gradient (Schulman et al., 2015), deriving a gradient that decomposes into two terms: (1) a CoT policy-gradient term that explores reasoning trajectories weighted by the regression reward, and (2) a prediction refinement term that backpropagates directly through the differentiable reward. Training uses RLOO variance reduction with K sampled CoT trajectories per prompt and a dynamic sampling strategy that filters to only partially-correct prompts (0 < acc < 1). The authors also prove (Lemma 3.1) that minimizing squared error loss is a sufficient condition for maximizing Pearson correlation — closing a theoretical gap in prior regression-aware LLM work. Experiments use Mistral2-7B, Qwen3-8B, and Qwen3-32B on the Feedback Collection training set, evaluated on Feedback Bench (in-domain), FLASK, Vicuna Bench, and MT Bench (out-of-domain). Key Results: REAL consistently outperforms zero-shot, RAFT, TRACT, standard binary-reward RL, and JEPO across all three model scales and all four benchmarks. On Qwen3-32B, REAL achieves average Pearson/Spearman of 71.0/70.0 versus RAFT's 62.6/62.8 — gains of +8.4/+7.2 over the SFT baseline and +18.3/+11.2 over the base model. On Mistral2-7B, REAL reaches average Pearson 67.9 vs. TRACT's 63.2 and standard RL's 64.1. The out-of-domain improvement is particularly pronounced: on MT Bench (Mistral2-7B), REAL achieves Pearson 59.3 vs. TRACT 52.1 and standard RL 52.9. Performance at N=1 generation is nearly identical to averaging N=10 generations, indicating a robust learned policy. Ablations confirm: larger group size K improves performance (K=16 > K=8 > K=4); regression-only reward (λ=0) already substantially beats TRACT; and initializing from a strong SFT checkpoint further boosts REAL's gains. Strengths: - The theoretical grounding is non-trivial and closes a real gap: the proof that MSE minimization implies optimal Pearson correlation provides principled justification for the regression reward, not just empirical motivation. - The generalized policy gradient derivation cleanly resolves the policy-dependent reward problem, producing an interpretable two-term decomposition that distinguishes reasoning exploration (CoT update) from score prediction (backprop update) — a conceptually meaningful separation. - Out-of-domain generalization gains are substantially larger than in-domain gains, which is the harder and more practically important regime for a judge model; this validates that REAL learns transferable evaluation skills rather than dataset-specific patterns. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method is restricted to pointwise scalar scoring and does not address pairwise preference learning, which is a dominant paradigm in RLHF alignment and LLM evaluation. - The theoretical optimality result (Lemma 3.1) relies on the conditional independence assumption c ⊥ y | x — i.e., that the chain of thought carries no additional information about the label beyond the input. This is questionable: a good CoT that correctly analyzes the response should be informative about y, potentially undermining the clean theoretical story. - Evaluation is limited to a single training dataset (Feedback Collection) and a narrow benchmark suite focused on instruction-following quality scoring; generalization to other judge tasks (e.g., mathematical correctness, safety) is undemonstrated.
Recommended 2603.17145 PDF
#100 — Innovative (63)

Mitigating Low-Quality Reasoning in MLLMs: Self-Driven Refined Multimodal CoT with Selective Thinking and Step-wise Visual Enhancement

ojs.aaai.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scores 63 for the clever relative attention mechanism that exploits the dichotomy between multiple-choice and open-ended attention patterns to cancel edge artifacts — a technically elegant observation. The training-free, architecture-agnostic design with simultaneous efficiency and accuracy gains is practically valuable. However, it combines known ingredients (entropy-based routing, attention manipulation) in a new configuration rather than introducing fundamentally new concepts.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Chain-of-Thought (MCoT) methods for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) suffer from two interrelated problems: (1) overthinking, where models apply expensive step-by-step reasoning to trivially easy queries, wasting tokens without accuracy gain; and (2) inefficient visual grounding, where reasoning chains fail to attend to task-relevant image regions at each step. Existing approaches either ignore visual content during reasoning (prompt-based), inject coarse visual supplements at the input stage incurring large token overhead (visual supplementation), or insert cropped/generated image patches at each step requiring external tools or specialized training (interleaved-modal). None simultaneously address both problems in a training-free, architecture-agnostic way. Methodology: The paper proposes Self-Driven Refined Multimodal CoT (SDR-MCoT), a training-free framework with two modules. The selective thinking module uses entropy of the first generated token's logit distribution as a confidence proxy: low entropy (confident) queries are answered directly; high entropy queries trigger full chain-of-thought reasoning. The threshold θ is set adaptively by averaging entropy over a small sample subset, requiring no labeled calibration data. The step-wise visual enhancement module operates during each CoT reasoning step without inserting new tokens. It computes a "relative attention" map by dividing attention weights from open-ended prompting (question-focused but edge-noisy) by attention from multiple-choice prompting (dispersed but edge-clean), effectively isolating semantically relevant visual regions. At each reasoning step, this relative attention is multiplied element-wise with the current step's attention map (Region Amplification), morphological erosion is applied to suppress attention sink artifacts, adaptive token selection determines how many visual tokens to boost based on remaining high-attention patch count, and finally selected tokens' post-softmax attention weights are scaled by factor α and renormalized. The entire pipeline adds only a single extra forward pass (for confidence estimation) and in-place attention manipulation during decoding. Key Results: Experiments span 8 benchmarks (M³CoT, CoMT, ScienceQA, MMStar, A-OKVQA, V*, HallusionBench, MathVista) across 4 base models (Qwen2-VL-7B, InternVL3-8B, LLaVA-1.5-7B/13B). On Qwen2-VL-7B, SDR-MCoT achieves 63.69% average accuracy vs. 61.75% for the best baseline (MMCoT), while using only 67.94 tokens on average vs. 172-920 tokens for competing MCoT methods — approximately 60% reduction relative to zero-shot CoT (172 tokens). On InternVL3-8B, SDR-MCoT reaches 72.87% average, outperforming MMCoT (70.89%) with 100 tokens vs. 407. On LLaVA-1.5-7B/13B, gains are more modest but consistent. Ablation confirms both modules contribute: selective thinking alone cuts tokens by ~65% (212→74) with moderate accuracy gain; visual enhancement adds consistent accuracy improvement with negligible token cost; combined performance is best across all three ablation benchmarks. Strengths: - Genuinely training-free and plug-and-play across architecturally diverse MLLMs (decoder-only Qwen2-VL, InternVL, LLaVA), requiring no fine-tuning, RL, or external tools. - The relative attention mechanism is technically elegant — exploiting an existing attention dichotomy (multiple-choice vs. open-ended prompting) to cancel edge-attention artifacts, computed once per sample with negligible cost. - Addresses both efficiency and effectiveness simultaneously, unlike prior methods that trade one for the other; token savings are substantial (60%+) while accuracy consistently improves. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The entropy threshold θ is set by sampling a small subset at inference time, introducing a dataset-level calibration dependency; it is unclear how sensitive results are to subset size or distribution shift. - The relative attention trick requires the question to have discrete answer options for the "dispersed" attention reference pattern; open-ended questions fall back to using raw active attention, losing the edge-cancellation benefit. - Evaluation is limited to classification/VQA-style tasks; applicability to generation-heavy tasks (captioning, visual dialogue, grounded generation) where correct answer entropy is harder to define is not examined. - The enhancement factor α and morphological erosion parameters are not ablated, leaving open questions about robustness to hyperparameter choice across different image resolutions and model architectures.
Citations
#101 — Innovative (63)

SMIDT: High- Performance Inference Framework for MoE Models with Dynamic Top-K Routing

ojs.aaai.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: SMIDT earns 63 for its technically elegant co-design where fine-grained module-level partitioning creates memory slack that DPMoE then monetizes to eliminate all-to-all communication — a mutually reinforcing pair of optimizations not previously combined. The automated parallelism search with clean O(log D × NM) complexity and sub-second solve times for 94-layer models is practically valuable. However, the gains are concentrated in prefill-dominant regimes where PP already excels, and the profiling-based cost model assumes representative Top-K distributions, limiting robustness.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Serving large Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models efficiently at scale is expensive — DeepSeek alone spends ~$87k/day for DeepSeek-V3/R1 serving. Two promising orthogonal optimizations exist: (1) pipeline parallelism (PP), which partitions a model into sequential stages for high-throughput inference, and (2) dynamic Top-K routing, which reduces average FLOPs by activating fewer experts per token depending on input complexity (e.g., Ada-K cuts FLOPs >25% without accuracy loss). The gap this paper fills is that naively combining the two creates serious pipeline imbalance: dynamic Top-K produces highly variable per-layer computation costs, so the standard uniform-layer PP partitioning leaves some stages idle while others are bottlenecked. Simultaneously, time-based uneven partitioning wastes >30% of GPU memory in early stages. No prior system (DistServe, Apex, Seesaw) handles both PP and dynamic Top-K jointly for MoE models. Methodology: SMIDT (Strategy for MoE Inference with Dynamic Top-K) introduces three tightly integrated components. First, fine-grained module-level PP partitioning breaks from the standard whole-layer granularity by treating attention and MoE sub-modules as independent partition units (2N boundaries for N layers instead of N). This enables finer load balancing across PP stages. Second, DPMoE (Data-Parallel MoE), analogous to SGLang's DPAttention, exploits the spare GPU memory freed by fine-grained partitioning by replicating selected MoE expert weights along the DP dimension, eliminating all-to-all communication for those layers — trading memory for reduced communication. Third, an automated parallelism search algorithm navigates the exponentially large joint space of partition boundaries, TP/EP/DP degrees per module, and DPMoE application decisions. The search reformulates the problem as a min-max optimization (minimize the slowest stage duration subject to per-GPU memory constraints), then solves it via binary search over duration thresholds combined with a dynamic-programming knapsack algorithm that uses a greedy sliding-window heuristic to reduce complexity from O(N²) to O(NM) per binary search iteration. Overall complexity is O(log D × NM), enabling sub-second search for 94-layer models on 32 GPUs. The system also adds asynchronous PP communication to SGLang to overlap activation transfers with computation. Dynamic Top-K kernel support required reimplementing SGLang's permutation kernels (moe_align_block_size, pre_reorder_triton_kernel) in Triton to accept per-token variable Top-K rather than a global fixed value. Key Results: Evaluated on Qwen2-57B-A14B, Qwen3-235B-A22B, and DeepSeek-V2 using 32 A40 GPUs and 16 A100 GPUs, across 7 tasks under both PD colocation and PD disaggregation modes. Under PD colocation (prefill-only tasks): SMIDT achieves 1.78–3.08× throughput improvement over no-PP baselines and 1.17–1.31× over hand-tuned PP baselines; for summarization tasks with both prefill and decoding, gains are 1.71–1.89× and 1.07–1.16× respectively. At large scale (Qwen3-235B-A22B, 32 A40 GPUs), SMIDT achieves 1.28–1.62× average speedup over the best manual baselines. On NVLink-connected A100s, gains are 1.14–1.31× and 1.18–1.37× for DeepSeek-V2 and Qwen3-235B-A22B. Under PD disaggregation, SMIDT improves mean TTFT by 1.10–1.17× and P99 TTFT by 1.21–1.26×. Ablation shows fine-grained partitioning contributes ~6–10% speedup, DPMoE 4–14%, and async PP communication 2–10%, with the async component becoming more valuable at higher PP degrees. Strengths: - The combination of module-level partitioning and DPMoE is technically elegant: fine-grained partitioning creates memory slack that DPMoE then monetizes, turning a resource waste into a communication reduction — these two components are mutually reinforcing. - The search algorithm is practically efficient (< 0.2s for 94-layer model vs. 23.7 hours for exhaustive search at 14 layers), with a clean theoretical complexity bound and a well-motivated monotonicity observation that enables the sliding-window optimization. - Evaluation is thorough: three models, two dynamic Top-K schemes (Top-P and Ada-K), two GPU clusters (PCIe A40 and NVLink A100), two deployment modes (colocation and disaggregation), and an ablation that cleanly separates each component's contribution. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The profiling-based cost model (using Nsight Systems/PyTorch at runtime) assumes that layer execution times during profiling are representative of production workloads; distributional shift in Top-K patterns across different inference batches could degrade the quality of the derived parallelism plan. - The framework is evaluated only in prefill-dominant regimes where PP is known to excel; gains in decode-heavy workloads are modest (PP is acknowledged to be disadvantageous in the decoding phase), and the forced single-plan constraint under PD colocation limits the system's flexibility compared to adaptive approaches like Seesaw. - Ada-K routing was reproduced by the authors rather than using the official implementation, introducing potential fidelity concerns in results attributed to that routing scheme.
Search Results
#102 — Innovative (63)

Navigating the Infinite Dynamic Web Space: Effective In-Context Exploration via Cognitive Multi-Agent Collaboration

aclanthology.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The ACT-R-grounded decomposition of history into declarative and procedural streams is a principled framing, and the In-Context Exploration mechanism addresses the well-documented backtracking deficit in web agents without requiring state snapshots. However, multi-agent web navigation is an increasingly crowded space, and the three-LLM-calls-per-step overhead without cost analysis weakens the practical case. The core contribution is more of a smart engineering combination than a paradigm shift.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-driven web navigation agents fail in two compounding ways: they operate greedily (selecting locally optimal actions at each step with no backtracking), and they rely on proprietary models (GPT-4o, Claude-3.5 Sonnet) that limit reproducibility and accessibility. Tree search alternatives are theoretically sound but computationally prohibitive on the infinite web graph, and irreversible web actions make state restoration frequently impractical. Empirically, existing reactive agents backtrack on only 8–16% of trajectories, far below the human baseline of 30–50%. This myopia means that once an agent enters an incorrect navigation branch, it tends to delve deeper indefinitely rather than recovering. Methodology: HintNavigator is a three-agent cognitive framework grounded in the ACT-R cognitive architecture's distinction between declarative knowledge (factual observations) and procedural knowledge (action sequences and reasoning). History H is partitioned into Declarative History D (HTML snippets, environmental facts extracted per step) and Procedural History P (the full action-thought trajectory). At each step: (1) a Declarative Agent φ compresses D iteratively to maintain candidate answers within context limits; (2) a Procedural Agent ψ reads P and generates a natural-language Hint that diagnoses whether the current path is suboptimal and, if so, recommends a corrective navigation action (go back, goto); (3) the Policy Agent (a ReAct-style LLM) receives the current accessibility tree plus Working Memory W = φ(D) + Hint and selects the next action. The key innovation is In-Context Exploration: rather than maintaining an explicit search tree or computing value functions, the agent uses LLM-generated hints as lightweight, dynamic guidance signals that trigger self-directed backtracking within the context window. The action space is kept minimal (click, fill, goto, go_back, send_msg_to_user, wait) to reduce the gap with pretraining distributions. Backbone models are Qwen-2.5 72B Instruct and Llama-3.1 70B Instruct. Key Results: On WebArena (812 tasks across Shopping, CMS, Reddit, GitLab, Maps, and multi-site), HintNavigator with Qwen-2.5 72B achieves 36.5% task success rate (SR), outperforming the prior open-source SOTA AgentOccam-J on the same model (28.6%) and surpassing Claude-3.5 Sonnet via AgentLab (36.2%). With Llama-3.1 70B it achieves 27.2%, a 47.8% relative improvement over AgentLab on the same backbone (18.4%). On Online-Mind2Web (300 live-web tasks), HintNavigator (Qwen 72B) reaches 28.7% SR, above Browser Use + GPT-4o (24.3%) and Claude Computer Use (26.0%). On WorkArena, Qwen-72B HintNavigator achieves 51.5%, above GPT-4o (42.7%). Ablations confirm the full W = φ(D) + Hint configuration is best; Hint alone (30.1%) substantially outperforms D alone (27.6%), P alone (26.4%), and D+P concatenated (28.5%), validating that undifferentiated history is worse than structured, agent-processed history. HintNavigator's backtracking rate reaches 40.76% of trajectories, versus 24.80% for AgentOccam-J and 6.90% for AgentLab, much closer to the human 30–50% range. Bootstrap significance tests (1,000 iterations) confirm p < 0.005 for both backbone results. Strengths: - The dual-history decomposition is principled (grounded in ACT-R) and practically effective: the ablation cleanly isolates the contribution of each component, and the Hint mechanism specifically addresses the backtracking deficit documented quantitatively in Table 1. - Achieves open-source SOTA and matches proprietary models without any task-specific fine-tuning, broadening accessibility and reproducibility. - The In-Context Exploration approach sidesteps the irreversible-action problem that breaks tree search methods: hints guide logical backtracking (via goto/go_back actions) without requiring state snapshots. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Multi-site task performance is poor (8.3%), and the error analysis attributes 53.2% of multi-site failures to completion hallucination — the hint mechanism does not resolve the agent's difficulty in knowing when a task is truly done across heterogeneous sites. - Running three LLM calls per step (declarative agent, procedural agent, policy agent) multiplies inference cost relative to single-agent baselines; the paper does not report latency, token counts, or cost comparisons, making practical deployment trade-offs unclear. - The framework is evaluated only on pre-trained, static LLMs; the authors acknowledge that fine-tuning on web navigation trajectories could substantially improve results, leaving the ceiling performance unknown and the comparison with fine-tuned open-source agents (e.g., WebRL) incomplete.
Citations
#103 — Innovative (63)

Calibri: Enhancing Diffusion Transformers via Parameter-Efficient Calibration (36 ▲ )

Danil Tokhchukov, Aysel Mirzoeva, Andrey Kuznetsov, Konstantin Sobolev · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Calibri's core finding—that 57–482 learned scalar multipliers can match alignment methods updating millions of parameters—is surprising and practically valuable, achieving a 10^5× parameter reduction versus Flow-GRPO while preserving generation diversity. The inference speedup as a byproduct (2–3× fewer steps) adds real deployment value. The contribution is conceptually simple, though, and bounded by reward model fidelity, with no theoretical explanation for why post-hoc block re-weighting works so well.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) are built from nominally identical blocks, yet prior work (Stable Flow) showed their functional contributions are highly uneven. This paper asks whether the standard assignment of equal unit weight to every block's output is optimal, and whether post-hoc re-weighting—without any gradient-based fine-tuning—can meaningfully improve generation quality. Existing alignment approaches (RLHF, DPO, DDPO, GRPO) require full or near-full model fine-tuning, updating tens of millions of parameters at significant compute cost. The gap is a lightweight, training-free-style calibration method that can sit on top of any pretrained DiT. Methodology: Calibri frames block calibration as a black-box reward maximization problem over a small vector c ∈ ℝ^(L+1) of scalar multipliers, one per DiT block (and optionally per layer or per gate). The calibrated model output is ωf^s_θ(x,t,p), where each block's residual contribution is multiplied by a learned scalar s before being added back. Three granularity levels are offered: Block Scaling (one scalar per block, ~57 parameters for FLUX), Layer Scaling (separate scalars for attention and MLP within each block, ~76 parameters), and Gate Scaling (separate scalars for visual and textual token gates in MM-DiT, ~114–482 parameters). The optimization uses CMA-ES, a gradient-free evolutionary strategy that iteratively refines a multivariate Gaussian sampling distribution. At each CMA-ES iteration, a population of ~20 candidate coefficient vectors is sampled, each used to generate a batch of 16 images from T2I-Compbench++ training prompts, scored by a reward model (HPSv3 or PickScore), and used to update the distribution mean and covariance. Calibri Ensemble extends the approach to N jointly calibrated model copies whose outputs are averaged; for N=2 this generalizes Skip Layer / Spatiotemporal Guidance. The entire optimization is a one-time offline procedure; inference adds zero overhead. Key Results: On FLUX.1-dev, layer scaling improves HPSv3 from 11.41 to 13.41, ImageReward from 1.15 to 1.24, and Q-Align from 4.85 to 4.90, while halving inference steps from 30 to 15. On SD-3.5M, HPSv3 jumps from 11.15 to 14.10 with NFE reduced from 80 to 30. On Qwen-Image, HPSv3 rises from 11.26 to 12.95 with NFE cut from 100 to 30. A large-scale human study (200 users, 5,600 assessments) confirms that Calibri is preferred over baselines in overall quality (51.9% vs 40.8% for FLUX; 54.6% vs 37.5% for Qwen-Image) and text alignment. Calibri with only 216 parameters matches Flow-GRPO PickScore alignment (PickScore 23.13 vs 23.78) which updates 18.78M parameters—a 10^5× parameter reduction. Calibri can also stack on top of already-aligned Flow-GRPO checkpoints for additional gains. Generation diversity (DINO diversity score) is preserved at 0.20 after Calibri, whereas Flow-GRPO degrades it to 0.15. Calibration cost ranges from 32 to 356 H100 GPU-hours depending on model and granularity. Strengths: - Extreme parameter efficiency: 57–482 scalars achieve quality gains competitive with methods updating millions of parameters, making deployment practical and the method applicable as a plug-in to any pretrained DiT. - Inference acceleration as a byproduct: the optimal step count shifts from 30–100 to 15–30 steps, delivering a 2–3.3× wall-clock speedup at no extra cost per step. - Composability: Calibri stacks additively on top of existing alignment methods (Flow-GRPO), boosting quality further without degrading diversity, and generalizes known inference-time techniques (Skip Layer Guidance) as special cases. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Reward model ceiling: calibration quality is bounded by the fidelity of the reward model used as the objective. Current reward models are insensitive to anatomical artifacts (extra limbs, distorted fingers), so Calibri can converge to coefficients that score well yet produce visually unrealistic images in those failure modes. - Non-trivial calibration compute: 32–356 H100 GPU-hours is one-time but not negligible, and the cost scales poorly with search-space size (gate scaling on SD-3.5M requires ~2,280 CMA-ES iterations vs ~200 for block scaling on FLUX), limiting practical granularity choices. - Limited theoretical grounding: the paper is empirically motivated—there is no analysis of why specific blocks are over- or under-weighted after pretraining, nor why CMA-ES finds consistent optima rather than reward-hacking local minima. The generalization of calibrated coefficients to out-of-distribution prompts is not studied.
Recommended 2603.24800 PDF
#104 — Innovative (63)

Let's Think with Images Efficiently! An Interleaved-Modal Chain-of-Thought Reasoning Framework with Dynamic and Precise Visual Thoughts

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: DAP-ICoT offers an elegant, training-free solution combining confidence-based gating with SAM2 object segmentation to address two well-identified failure modes in interleaved-modal CoT. The 72.6% token reduction with consistent accuracy gains is practically significant and well-validated across five MLLMs.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Interleaved-modal Chain-of-Thought (ICoT) reasoning extends standard multimodal chain-of-thought by producing both text and image tokens during the reasoning process, allowing visual thoughts to be interleaved with textual rationale. However, existing ICoT methods suffer from two critical flaws: (1) Static Visual Thought Positioning — visual information is inserted after every reasoning step regardless of whether it is needed, producing redundant computation and inflexible reasoning; and (2) Broken Visual Thought Representation — the selected image tokens are discontinuous patches lacking semantic coherence, which impairs understanding and causes the model to miss critical visual content. Together these issues inflate token costs and degrade reasoning accuracy. Methodology: The paper proposes DAP-ICoT, comprising two complementary modules. Dynamic Visual Thought Integration (DVTI) uses confidence-aware gating: at each reasoning step t, a step-level confidence score C_t is computed as the mean logit margin (top-1 minus top-2 predicted logit) across all decoded tokens in that step's rationale. If C_t falls below a tuned threshold τ, a visual thought is injected for the next step; otherwise the model proceeds on text alone. This eliminates unnecessary image insertions. Precise Visual Thought Guidance (PVTG) replaces raw image patch tokens with semantically whole object-level sub-images. SAM2 is applied to the original image to segment it into N distinct object regions. When DVTI triggers a visual insertion, cross-modal attention similarity is computed between the current textual rationale and each candidate object image; the highest-scoring object sub-image is selected and embedded into the reasoning sequence. The threshold τ is tuned on the M³CoT validation set by grid search over [0,1]; the best value was τ=0.2. The approach is training-free and model-agnostic — it wraps any MLLM at inference time. Key Results: Experiments are conducted on three benchmarks (M³CoT, ScienceQA, MME) across five MLLMs (Chameleon-7B, LLaVA-V1.5-7B, LLaVA-V1.5-13B, Qwen2-VL-2B, Qwen2-VL-7B) in 0-shot and 1-shot settings, compared against Direct, MMCoT, DDCoT, SCAFFOLD, CCoT, and the ICoT baseline. DAP-ICoT achieves best performance in virtually all settings. On M³CoT with Qwen2-VL-7B (0-shot), DAP-ICoT reaches 57.2% vs. the next-best SCAFFOLD at 49.9%. On ScienceQA with Qwen2-VL-7B (1-shot), it achieves 78.5% vs. SCAFFOLD at 75.0%. On MME with Qwen2-VL-7B (0-shot), it scores 2012.2 vs. CCoT at 1866.3. Token efficiency is dramatic: DAP-ICoT averages 314 tokens per sample vs. 1,146 for ICoT — a 72.6% reduction. Image insertion frequency drops from 2.6 per sample (ICoT) to 1.2, and average inserted image tokens fall from ~156–240 to ~16–41 depending on the benchmark. Ablation shows removing DVTI causes a 14.4% accuracy drop on M³CoT and 20.8% on ScienceQA; removing PVTG causes 13.8% and 20.4% drops respectively. Confidence analysis shows DAP-ICoT increases model confidence after image insertion in 80.7% of samples vs. 46.4% for ICoT, with a positive average confidence delta of +0.13 to +0.17 for DAP-ICoT vs. negative deltas for ICoT. Strengths: - The confidence-based gating mechanism is elegant and computationally lightweight — it requires no additional network components, just monitoring the logit margin during standard decoding. - Using SAM2 object-level segmentation to provide semantically coherent visual thoughts is a principled and effective solution to the broken-token problem, and directly addresses a well-identified failure mode. - The 72.6% token reduction is practically significant and well-documented through multiple complementary metrics (insertion count, token count, total consumption), making the efficiency claim credible rather than cherry-picked. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The confidence threshold τ is tuned on M³CoT and applied universally, but the paper does not show whether the same τ=0.2 generalizes across all models and benchmarks or whether each model/benchmark benefits from independent tuning — a potential source of hidden overfitting. - The method depends on SAM2 at inference time, adding non-trivial latency from object segmentation that is not accounted for in the token-efficiency analysis; wall-clock runtime comparisons are absent. - Evaluation is limited to classification/VQA tasks (M³CoT, ScienceQA, MME); performance on open-ended generation or tasks requiring spatial/relational reasoning where object-level segmentation may be insufficient is not explored.
Search Results
#105 — Innovative (62)

Disentangling Geometry, Performance, and Training in Language Models

Atharva Kulkarni, Jacob Mitchell Springer, Arjun Subramonian, Swabha Swayamdipta · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 62 for a methodologically rigorous falsification of the widely cited claim that unembedding effective rank causally determines LLM performance. The 108-model ablation study with partial Spearman and predictive ΔR² controls is exemplary experimental design, and the OLMo-14M vs. Pythia-14M comparison cleanly refutes the saturation-causation hypothesis. The contribution is primarily corrective rather than constructive, and confinement to small models (≤75M) leaves open whether conclusions hold at frontier scale.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: A growing body of interpretability research uses geometric properties of the Transformer unembedding matrix—particularly its effective rank—as proxies for model quality. Prior work on Pythia models claimed that low effective rank (concentrated singular value spectrum) causally degrades downstream performance and drives late-stage saturation in small language models. However, these findings rest almost exclusively on the Pythia family, and no prior study systematically varies training conditions to isolate whether low effective rank is a cause or merely a correlate of poor performance. This paper asks three questions: (RQ1) Does effective rank reliably predict performance across tasks and training setups? (RQ2) Why does it sometimes fail to predict performance? (RQ3) Do alternative geometric metrics—cosine similarity, isotropy—do better, and do these findings extend to final-layer representations? Methodology: The authors pre-train a controlled suite of 108 OLMo-style language models ranging from 4M to 75M non-embedding parameters on the Pile dataset (8B–128B tokens). They systematically ablate batch size over {32, 64, 128, 256, 512}, weight decay over {0.01, 0.05, 0.1}, and learning rate scale factors {0.1, 1, 10, 100}, while also varying learning rate annealing schedules (0%, 1%, 10% of peak LR). For each model, they compute three geometric metrics on the unembedding matrix W: effective rank R(W) (exponential of spectral entropy), cosine similarity A(W) (mean pairwise angular alignment of rows), and isotropy I(W) (partition-function-based uniformity). Analogous metrics are computed on the final-token last-layer representations H. Performance is evaluated across five settings: in-distribution loss (Pile-10K), out-of-distribution (Paloma/Dolma benchmark), fine-tuning (StarCoder-Python, OpenWebMath), post-training quantization (GPTQ), and catastrophic forgetting. Correlation is quantified via raw Spearman, Chinchilla-adjusted residual Spearman, partial Spearman (controlling all hyperparameters simultaneously), and predictive ΔR² (k-fold cross-validation). Key Results: Effective rank shows a strong raw Spearman correlation with in-distribution loss (−0.699 for R(W)), but this collapses to −0.261 as partial Spearman after controlling for hyperparameters. Predictive ΔR² is only 0.003—well under 0.5% additional variance explained beyond scaling laws alone. Crucially, batch size monotonically increases R(W) but shows a U-shaped relationship with performance, so many high-rank models are poor performers. Weight decay strongly raises R(W) yet has inconsistent performance effects across model sizes. Learning rate annealing (milder decay → higher LR at end) raises R(W) but degrades performance. In the saturation analysis, an OLMo-14M model trained identically to the saturating Pythia-14M shows no loss degradation or rank collapse under extended training, falsifying the hypothesis that low rank causes saturation. Alternative metrics offer marginal improvement: cosine similarity A(W) shows a non-monotone 'moon-shaped' correlation with rank, isotropy I(W) is tightly clustered around 0.5 and largely invariant to performance. Final-layer representation geometry (H) has higher rank but less variation than W and similarly fails to predict downstream performance. Across fine-tuning and catastrophic forgetting evaluations, no geometric metric consistently predicts outcomes; partial Spearman values remain near zero across all five evaluation regimes. Strengths: - Unusually rigorous experimental design: 108 models with systematic hyperparameter ablation and five distinct evaluation regimes provide much stronger causal evidence than previous single-family observational studies. - The use of partial Spearman and predictive ΔR² as stringent controls for confounding by model size and compute is methodologically sound and reveals that raw correlations are largely artifacts of these confounders. - The falsification of the saturation-causation hypothesis via the OLMo-14M vs. Pythia-14M comparison is a clean adversarial experiment that substantially narrows the scope of prior claims. - Practical takeaways are actionable: the paper correctly identifies effective rank as a symptom of hyperparameter choices rather than an independent diagnostic, redirecting practitioners toward standard performance metrics. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The study is confined to small models (4M–75M parameters), making it unclear whether the same decoupling between geometry and performance holds at the scale of modern LLMs (1B–70B+), where the mechanisms driving rank collapse may differ. - Only the unembedding matrix and last-token final-layer representations are analyzed; intermediate-layer geometry, which several concurrent works show exhibits distinct behavior, is explicitly left out of scope. - Because it is impossible to directly manipulate unembedding geometry while holding all other factors constant, the results remain observational. The paper calls for interventional studies but does not perform them, leaving causal claims about why geometry and performance decouple somewhat underspecified.
Recommended 2602.20433 PDF
#106 — Innovative (62)

Compositional Chain-of-Thought Prompting for Large Multimodal Models

Chancharik Mitra, Brandon Huang, Trevor Darrell, Roei Herzig · academia.edu · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scores 62 for demonstrating that structured scene graph prompting — without any training — surpasses a supervised SG fine-tuning baseline on compositional benchmarks. The training-free, architecture-agnostic design is practically appealing, and the ablations provide genuine mechanistic insight into why JSON-structured SGs outperform captions and free-form CoT.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) such as LLaVA, InstructBLIP, and GPT-4V have demonstrated strong general VQA performance, but empirical studies show they treat images as a "bag of objects" — recognizing individual objects well but failing to capture inter-object relationships and attributes needed for compositional reasoning. Scene graphs (SGs) are a natural structured representation to address this, but annotated SG data is scarce (Visual Genome has only ~100K pairs), and fine-tuning on SG data risks catastrophic forgetting of the pretrained objective. The paper targets this gap: improving compositional visual understanding without SG annotations or any fine-tuning. Methodology: The proposed Compositional Chain-of-Thought (CCoT) is a zero-shot, two-step inference-time prompting method. In Step 1, the LMM is prompted with the input image and task prompt to generate a scene graph in JSON format encoding objects, their attributes, and inter-object relationships. Conditioning the SG generation on the task prompt (not just the image) keeps the graph focused and token-efficient. In Step 2, the generated SG is inserted into a second prompt alongside the original image and task, instructing the LMM to use both the image and SG as context for its final response. The JSON format is intentional: ablations show it improves over free-form SG text. The method requires no additional parameters, no annotated data, and no model weight updates — it is purely prompting-based and architecture-agnostic, demonstrated on InstructBLIP-13B, LLaVA-1.5-13B, SPHINX, and GPT-4V. Key Results: CCoT consistently improves over both the base model and a standard zero-shot CoT baseline (ZS-CoT) across all four LMMs on all tested benchmarks. On compositional benchmarks: LLaVA-1.5-13B-CCoT gains +6.3/+2.3/+5.0 points on Winoground Text/Image/Group and +13.9 on WHOOPS! VQA; InstructBLIP-13B-CCoT gains +13.2/+13.7/+7.0 on Winoground and +14.6 on WHOOPS!. GPT-4V-CCoT achieves Winoground Group score of 43.3, surpassing the supervised SGVL baseline (23.3) that was fine-tuned on ground-truth SG annotations. On general multimodal benchmarks, CCoT achieves 1–5 point gains on SEEDBench-Image, MMBench, and LLaVA-Bench-in-the-Wild. On the SEEDBench multimodal CoT comparison, LLaVA-1.5-13B-CCoT (69.7 overall) outperforms VidIL (68.9) and substantially outperforms DDCoT (58.0) and MMCoT (34.4), the latter two requiring fine-tuning or caption-based reasoning. Importantly, ZS-CoT ("Let's think step-by-step") often degrades performance on compositional benchmarks, underscoring that the SG structure — not just multi-step prompting — drives the gains. Ablations establish that all three SG components (objects, attributes, relationships) contribute, that JSON structure is essential (−2.0% without it), that replacing SGs with captions degrades performance (−1.4%), and that removing the image in Step 2 collapses accuracy by 26.1%. The method is less effective for 7B-scale models and shows no benefit on benchmarks that heavily overlap with COCO/Visual Genome fine-tuning data (GQA: −0.8%, VL Checklist: −0.6%). Strengths: - Clean, training-free design: the entire method is prompting-only at inference time, adding no parameters and avoiding catastrophic forgetting while surpassing a supervised SG fine-tuning baseline (SGVL) on Winoground. - Architecture-agnostic: demonstrated on four qualitatively different LMM families (Q-Former, linear-projection, mixed-weight, proprietary), suggesting broad applicability. - Thorough ablation study isolating the contributions of JSON format, SG component types, SG token length, model size, image removal, and random SG regularization, providing genuine mechanistic insight. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method requires two separate LMM forward passes (SG generation + response generation), doubling inference cost; this is not analyzed or benchmarked in terms of latency/compute overhead. - Performance gains are model-size dependent: negligible improvement on 7B models, raising questions about usefulness as smaller, more efficient models become preferred in practice. The mechanism by which larger models leverage SGs better is not explained. - On benchmarks heavily overlapping with COCO/Visual Genome fine-tuning data, CCoT marginally hurts performance, suggesting the benefit is tied to generalization scenarios and may not transfer broadly across all real-world deployments.
Search Results 2311.17076 PDF
#107 — Innovative (61)

WWW. Serve: Interconnecting Global LLM Services through Decentralization

Huanyu Wang, Ziyu Xia, Zhuoming Chen, Beidi Chen · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: WWW.Serve creatively combines PoS routing, blockchain credit ledgers, and a duel-and-judge quality mechanism into a self-consistent incentive framework for decentralized LLM serving, with a formal convergence guarantee. The problem framing—treating GPU providers as profit-maximizing competitors rather than cooperative agents—is well-motivated. However, experiments at 4–8 nodes with a simplified shared ledger leave the core scalability and Sybil resistance claims unvalidated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large language model serving is overwhelmingly centralized, creating scalability bottlenecks and leaving idle GPU resources underutilized globally. Prior decentralized approaches fail in practice because they model GPU providers as cooperative participants subject to centralized oversight, ignoring the reality that providers are profit-maximizing competitors with heterogeneous hardware, varying software stacks, and fluctuating resource availability. This creates a gap: no existing framework simultaneously provides trustless request delegation, incentive-compatible quality enforcement, and dynamic participation without relying on a trusted central coordinator. Methodology: WWW.Serve introduces a peer-to-peer LLM serving layer structured around three core mechanisms. First, a blockchain-inspired credit transaction system assigns transferable credits as reputation proxies; providers earn credits by serving delegated requests and spend them to offload their own. A local Credit Block Chain per node records all transactions in cryptographically linked, majority-validated blocks, preventing double-spending and manipulation. Second, a Proof-of-Stake (PoS) routing mechanism selects executors with probability proportional to their staked credits, creating a market where nodes self-regulate participation via stake adjustment. Third, a duel-and-judge mechanism randomly designates a fraction of delegated requests as duels: two executors each produce a response, k PoS-sampled judges perform pairwise comparison, and the winner earns additional credits while the loser is penalized. This aligns individual incentives with quality. Dynamic network membership is handled by a gossip protocol in which nodes periodically exchange peer state (online/offline status, addresses) in lightweight pairwise rounds, enabling rapid integration of new nodes and detection of departures without centralized coordination. A game-theoretic analysis (Theorem 5.8) proves the system converges to a high-quality equilibrium: high-quality nodes accumulate stake share while low-quality nodes are gradually excluded. Key Results: Across four heterogeneous experimental settings (varying models from Qwen3-0.6B to 32B, GPUs from RTX3090 to A100, backends vLLM and SGLang), WWW.Serve improves global SLO attainment by up to 1.5× over single-node deployment and matches or exceeds centralized scheduling in three of four settings. Average request latency is reduced by up to 27.6% versus single-node (e.g., Setting 3: 237.9s → 198.3s). Quality incentivization experiments confirm credit accumulation tracks quality: in model-capacity experiments, Qwen3-8B, 4B, and 0.6B achieve duel win rates of 0.57, 0.53, and 0.39 respectively, with credit trajectories diverging accordingly. More aggressive quantization (int4-wo-32 vs. fp8) yields lower win rates (0.47 vs. 0.54) and slower credit accumulation. Efficient attention backends (FlashInfer/Triton) earn more credits than SDPA purely through throughput advantage (788 vs. 426 requests served). Ablation shows duel rates of 5%, 10%, and 25% produce nearly identical latency CDFs, confirming low overhead. Strengths: - The combination of PoS routing, blockchain ledger, and duel-and-judge creates a self-consistent incentive structure with a formal convergence guarantee, not just an engineering claim. - Backend-agnostic design (unified OpenAI-compatible API layer over SGLang/vLLM) enables heterogeneous nodes to participate without code changes, making the system practically deployable. - The duel-and-judge mechanism leverages established evidence that pairwise LLM evaluation is more reliable than absolute scoring, grounding the quality signal in principled methodology. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are conducted at small scale (4–8 nodes) using a simplified shared ledger rather than a full distributed blockchain, raising significant questions about consensus latency, fork resolution, and Sybil resistance at internet scale. - The game-theoretic equilibrium proof rests on a continuous-time ODE model with strong assumptions (rational stake adjustment proportional to realized payoffs, fixed quality parameters), which may not hold under strategic manipulation, collusion among judges, or adversarial Sybil attacks. - The duel-and-judge mechanism relies on LLMs judging other LLMs; for tasks with objective correct answers this is reasonable, but for open-ended generation the judge quality becomes entangled with the executor quality, creating a circular dependency not addressed in the evaluation.
Search Results 2603.20661 PDF
#108 — Innovative (61)

TDMM-LM: Bridging Facial Understanding and Animation via Language Models

Luchuan Song, Pinxin Liu, Haiyang Liu, Zhenchao Jin, Yolo Yunlong Tang, Zichong Xu, Susan Liang, Jing Bi, Jason J Corso, Chenliang Xu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: TDMM-LM is the first to unify 3D facial motion understanding and generation within a single LLM framework, and the geometry tokenization achieving 300-500x compression over image tokens is an elegant efficiency win.
