The Open3DFaceVid dataset with 187 balanced emotion labels fills a real gap. However, evaluation is largely on synthetic test clips sharing the same T2V generation distribution as training, and Gemini-2.5 outperforms on the more deployment-relevant natural-image setting, limiting the demonstrated practical advantage.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Text-guided facial animation lags significantly behind full-body motion generation due to two compounding problems: (1) existing video-language models process frames as hundreds of image tokens, causing temporal sparsity that destroys subtle facial dynamics (micro-expressions, lip twitches, brow raises) when frames are downsampled; and (2) real-world training corpora are overwhelmingly neutral-expression-dominated (YouTube, TikTok, VoxCeleb), leaving models with a narrow, low-variance facial prior. Existing labeled datasets like MEAD offer only 8 coarse one-hot emotion labels and studio-controlled subjects, lacking the open-form text annotations needed for language-driven modeling. This creates a fundamental gap: no large-scale, class-balanced, richly text-annotated corpus of 3D facial motion exists. Methodology: The paper addresses the data gap by introducing Open3DFaceVid, a synthetic corpus of ~80 hours (57.2K clips, 4–6s each) generated by prompting multiple T2V models (Wan2.1/2.2, HuMo, Open-Sora, Veo2/3) with a curated lexicon of ~200 emotion and facial-action descriptors (grin, pout, smirk, squint, etc.), ensuring class balance across 187 labels. Per-frame FLAME 3DMM parameters (expression codes, head yaw/roll/pitch) are extracted via monocular regression, and ~10K wild clips annotated by Gemini are appended to narrow the synthetic-to-real gap. Building on this data, the authors train a Geometry VQ-VAE that discretizes 3DMM sequences into a compact codebook by operating in mesh space (vertex positions) rather than raw coefficient space, avoiding many-to-one mapping ambiguities where similar expressions map to different coefficient patterns. Each video frame is encoded as a single geometry token—replacing 300–500 image tokens—drastically reducing context length while preserving temporal continuity. Two complementary tasks are then trained on the same LLM: (1) Motion2Language fine-tunes a Qwen3 backbone to consume geometry-token sequences and produce free-form natural-language descriptions of emotion, intensity, and head dynamics; (2) Language2Motion uses an autoregressive transformer conditioned on word-level T5 embeddings to predict geometry token sequences from text prompts, enabling single-keyword control over emotional style and intensity. Key Results: For Motion2Language on geometry inputs, the model outperforms HumanOmni and Gemini-2.5 VLM across all GPT-4-judged and human-rated metrics: emotion correctness 4.02 vs. 2.45 (Gemini), motion correctness 3.35 vs. 2.91, intensity 3.63 vs. 3.51, human emotion rating 4.29 vs. 2.88. On natural image inputs (a fairer comparison for VLMs), Gemini-2.5 leads on emotion (4.21 vs. 4.02), but the proposed method uses only 1 token per frame versus hundreds for Gemini, a major efficiency advantage. For Language2Motion, the model outperforms T2M-GPT on all metrics: L2 0.219 vs. 0.226, Fréchet Distance 31.75 vs. 37.04, token accuracy 0.920 vs. 0.895, expression correctness 4.13 vs. 3.57, and human rating 3.95 vs. 3.91. Dataset ablations confirm that Open3DFaceVid (187 labels, well-separated t-SNE clusters) significantly outperforms MEAD (8 labels) and Gemini-annotated YouTube (37 labels, heavy neutral bias) in both tasks. Scaling experiments show Motion2Language saturates at 4B–8B parameters, while Language2Motion continues benefiting from larger backbones. Strengths: - First unified framework to cast 3D facial parameter modeling as a language problem, enabling both semantic understanding and controllable generation with a single LLM backbone. - Geometry tokenization achieves ~300–500× token compression versus image-based VLMs while preserving subtle temporal facial dynamics, making it both efficient and effective for long sequences. - Open3DFaceVid provides by far the richest facial-motion dataset available (187 emotion labels, class-balanced, with both text and 3DMM ground truth), which is likely to have standalone value for the community. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method is evaluated primarily on synthetic test clips that share the same T2V generation distribution as training data; generalization to diverse real-world video (e.g., extreme occlusions, non-frontal poses, in-the-wild lighting) is not demonstrated. - On natural image inputs—the setting most relevant to real deployment—Gemini-2.5 outperforms the model on emotion understanding (4.21 vs. 4.02 CorE), and the paper uses a circular evaluation for Language2Motion correctness (the trained Motion2Language model scores its own sibling task). - Camera constraints during dataset synthesis ("frame remains steady, head and shoulders centered") limit head-motion diversity; the ablation acknowledges this results in narrower CorM scores compared to YouTube, potentially limiting utility for dynamic talking-head applications.
Recommended 2603.16936 PDF
#109 — Innovative (61)

Omni-WorldBench: Towards a Comprehensive Interaction-Centric Evaluation for World Models (41 ▲ )

Meiqi Wu, Zhixin Cai, Fufangchen Zhao, Xiaokun Feng, Rujing Dang, Bingze Song, Ruitian Tian, Jiashu Zhu, Jiachen Lei, Hao Dou, Jing Tang, Lei Sun, Jiahong Wu, Xiangxiang Chu, Zeming Liu, Kaiqi Huang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Omni-WorldBench fills a genuine gap by systematically evaluating interactive causal consistency rather than visual fidelity alone, and the three-level interaction hierarchy provides a principled stress-testing axis absent from prior benchmarks. The InterStab-L dynamics gating and AgenticScore adaptive weighting are thoughtful design choices. As a benchmark paper, the contribution is primarily organizational rather than algorithmic, and the incomplete human alignment study leaves the core validity claim unresolved.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing evaluation benchmarks for video-based world models are inadequate for assessing their core capability: generating causally consistent, physically plausible responses to interaction conditions. Metrics like FID, FVD, and general benchmarks (VBench, WorldScore) focus on visual fidelity, text-video alignment, or narrow forms of interaction (camera motion only), completely ignoring whether a model correctly simulates how actions drive state transitions across space and time. The field is converging toward 4D generation — jointly modeling spatial structure and temporal evolution — yet no benchmark systematically evaluates interactive response in this paradigm. This gap means model rankings on existing benchmarks bear little relation to actual world-modeling capability. Methodology: The paper introduces Omni-WorldBench, composed of two components. First, Omni-WorldSuite is a prompt suite of 1,068 evaluation cases spanning a three-level interaction hierarchy: Level 1 (effects confined to the acting object), Level 2 (one object directly affects another), and Level 3 (multi-object cascading environmental changes). Prompts cover both general daily-life scenes (indoor/outdoor, physics, causality, common sense) and task-oriented domains (autonomous driving via DriveLM, embodied robotics via InternData-A1, gaming via Sekai). Two construction pipelines are used: dataset-grounded generation (real first frames + camera trajectories + VLM-generated captions verified by humans) and concept-driven generation (LLM/VLM-synthesized prompts cross-checked by multiple models and human curators, with FLUX.1-generated initial frames). Each prompt includes auxiliary metadata: affected/unaffected entity sets with expected motion annotations, temporally ordered key event lists, and camera motion annotations. Second, Omni-Metric is an agent-based evaluation framework measuring three dimensions: (1) Generated Video Quality (imaging quality, temporal flickering, motion smoothness, content alignment, dynamic degree); (2) Camera-Object Controllability (camera control error, VQA-based object control, scene transition detection); and (3) Interaction Effect Fidelity, the core contribution, comprising four novel metrics — InterStab-L (loop-closure consistency via SSIM+CLIP similarity at revisit frames with static-sequence gating), InterStab-N (optical-flow-based stability of non-target regions), InterCov (VLM-verified semantic recall of causal entity behaviors), and InterOrder (fraction of temporally consistent event pairs verified by VLM). These dimension scores are aggregated into an AgenticScore by an MLLM that adaptively weights each dimension based on the semantic content of the prompt. Key Results: 18 models spanning T2V (Director3D, OpenSoraPlan, T2V-Turbo, HunyuanVideo), IT2V (Matrix-Game2.0, Wan2.1, Wan2.2, CogVideo, OpenSora, Cosmos, LargeVideoPlanner), and camera-conditioned generation (HunyuanWorld, HunyuanGameCraft, ViewCrafter, Gen3C, Lingbot, FantasyWorld, WonderWorld) were evaluated. Key findings: (1) IT2V models dominate — Wan2.2 achieves the highest AgenticScore at 75.92%, followed by Cosmos (75.42%); (2) Best T2V model is HunyuanVideo at 73.96%; (3) Camera-conditioned models are led by HunyuanWorld (74.36%) and WonderWorld (74.02%); (4) Nearly all models saturate Temporal Flickering (>95%) and Motion Smoothness (>98%), meaning these metrics no longer differentiate models; (5) Interaction Effect Fidelity scores are substantially lower — Wan2.2 leads at 67.34% average, revealing that causal/physical interaction consistency is the primary failure mode; (6) Camera-conditioned models exhibit sharp intra-category trade-offs (e.g., WonderWorld scores 84.96% on InterStab-L but only 24.89% on InterStab-N, revealing that camera scheduling destabilizes non-target regions); (7) Matrix-Game2.0 scores only 42.76% on interaction fidelity and shows catastrophic temporal collapse in qualitative examples (human figure disappears mid-sequence). Human alignment studies are mentioned but not yet fully reported. Strengths: - The three-level interaction hierarchy is principled and well-motivated, providing a systematic axis for stress-testing world models beyond the camera-motion-only paradigm of prior benchmarks. - InterStab-L's dynamics gating mechanism (penalizing trivially static videos) is a thoughtful design choice that prevents degenerate high-similarity scores from static sequences, a real failure mode of naive loop-closure evaluation. - The AgenticScore aggregation via MLLM weighting is well-suited to a heterogeneous prompt suite where some prompts emphasize camera control while others test physical causality — flat averaging would be inappropriate. - Scale (1,068 prompts) and diversity (11 coverage axes including physics, causality, common sense, loop-closure, spatial constraints) make this the most comprehensive world-model benchmark to date per the comparison table. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The human alignment study is incomplete — only mentioned as forthcoming. Without it, the validity of AgenticScore as a proxy for human judgment of interaction quality remains unverified, particularly for the novel InterCov and InterOrder metrics which rely on VLM reasoning that may itself be unreliable. - The benchmark cannot cover long-horizon or highly dynamic open-world settings by the authors' own admission. With a maximum of 1,068 prompts and relatively simple structured scenarios, generalization to complex multi-step planning horizons is untested. - The AgenticScore aggregation depends on an MLLM's ability to correctly rank evaluation dimensions from prompt text, introducing a non-transparent and potentially inconsistent source of variance — prompts near category boundaries may receive unstable weight assignments across runs.
Recommended 2603.22212 PDF
#110 — Innovative (61)

A foundation model for electrodermal activity data

Leonardo Alchieri, Matteo Garzon, Lidia Alecci, Francesco Bombassei De Bona, Martin Gjoreski, Giovanni De Felice, Silvia Santini · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scores 61 primarily for the EDAMAME dataset contribution, which unifies 24 fragmented public EDA datasets into a standardized archive addressing the field's most critical bottleneck. The efficiency-performance tradeoff is impressive (matching models 8-385x larger), but the core methodology applies standard contrastive self-supervised learning to a new modality without methodological novelty, and performance gains over well-engineered handcrafted features are statistically non-significant (Nemenyi p≈0.9).
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Electrodermal activity (EDA) measures sympathetic nervous system arousal through skin conductance changes and is widely used to infer stress, cognitive load, and engagement from wearable devices. Despite growing interest in physiological foundation models (for PPG, ECG, EEG), no EDA-specific foundation model exists. The core obstacle is data: the only large-scale EDA archive is proprietary (Fitbit Sense 2), and the broader landscape of public EDA datasets is fragmented across incompatible formats and protocols. Without a curated, open collection, training a foundation model for EDA has been infeasible. Methodology: The paper makes two coupled contributions. First, EDAMAME (EDA Multi-dataset Archive for Model training and Evaluation) aggregates 24 public datasets totaling ~25,000 hours of EDA data from 634 users, all collected via Empatica E4 wristbands at 4 Hz. The collection spans six physiological scenarios (sleep, stress induction, engagement, real-world stress, workplace analysis, daily living) and balances longitudinal depth with population diversity. Second, UME (foUndation Model for Electrodermal activity data) is trained on ~19,000 hours (~275 million 60-second windows) of this data using self-supervised contrastive learning with InfoNCE loss. The backbone is a 1D EfficientNet (~1M parameters) operating on three-channel inputs: the raw EDA signal plus its phasic and tonic components (decomposed via cvxEDA). Positive pairs are generated by applying EDA-specific augmentations (loose sensor simulation, thermoregulation artifacts, band-pass filtering) alongside standard time-series augmentations. No per-user normalization is applied, to support cold-start deployment. Downstream evaluation uses linear probing (logistic regression on frozen embeddings) under two cross-validation protocols: Leave-One-Participant-Out (LOPO, testing inter-personal generalization) and Time-Aware (TA, testing intra-personal temporal generalization). Masked autoencoder pretraining was explored but failed to yield useful representations. Key Results: UME outperforms both generic (d=12) and EDA-specific (d=45) handcrafted feature baselines in 8 out of 10 downstream classification tasks. Against the generic baseline it wins 9/10, establishing that learned embeddings capture signal dynamics that simple statistics miss. UME matches generalist time-series foundation models—Mantis (8M params), Chronos (~200M), and MOMENT (~385M)—with no statistically significant difference (Friedman-Nemenyi, p≈0.9 vs. Mantis and EDA-specific HC), while requiring 20× fewer FLOPs than Mantis (0.04 vs. 0.85 GFLOPs/sample) and running in 184 ms vs. 350 ms (CPU). Balanced accuracy scores across tasks range roughly from 0.55 to 0.89, with high variance—the authors attribute the ceiling to intrinsic EDA noise and weak label-signal alignment rather than model deficiencies. The Friedman test confirms statistically significant differences exist overall (p≈0.0001), driven by the gap between all feature-based methods and the dummy classifier. Strengths: - Concrete community resource: EDAMAME unifies 24 heterogeneous public datasets under a single data-sharing agreement with standardized preprocessing, directly addressing the most critical bottleneck in EDA modeling research. - Excellent efficiency-performance tradeoff: UME achieves parity with models 8–385× larger in parameter count and 21–1500× more computationally expensive, making it viable for on-device deployment on resource-constrained wearables. - Rigorous and honest evaluation: The paper uses two complementary cross-validation protocols (LOPO and TA), reports balanced accuracy for imbalanced tasks, applies Friedman-Nemenyi post-hoc correction, and explicitly flags that performance ceilings may reflect intrinsic signal limitations rather than model failures—an unusually candid assessment. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Device monoculture limits generalizability: restricting EDAMAME to Empatica E4 data eliminates hardware-induced domain shift but also makes it unclear whether UME representations transfer to other EDA sensors (e.g., Empatica Embrace Plus, Shimmer), limiting practical deployment scope. - Performance gains are statistically marginal: while UME nominally outperforms EDA-specific handcrafted features on most tasks, the confidence intervals overlap and the Nemenyi test finds no statistically significant difference (p≈0.9), raising doubts about whether training a domain-specific foundation model confers meaningful benefit beyond well-engineered features for this signal modality at current data scales.
Citations 2603.16878 PDF
#111 — Moderately Innovative (59)

A Pipelined Collaborative Speculative Decoding Framework for Efficient Edge-Cloud LLM Inference

Yida Zhang, Zhiyong Gao, Shuaibing Yue, Jie Li, Rui Wang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: PicoSpec's sparse compression for rejection sampling achieves a striking 3-order-of-magnitude bandwidth reduction through a mathematically correct decomposition, and the training-free, plug-and-play design is practically valuable. The formal throughput model with graceful degradation guarantees is rigorous. However, pipelining and async execution are well-known systems techniques, gains are heavily acceptance-rate-dependent, and the evaluation scope is narrow.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying large language models on resource-constrained edge devices is infeasible due to memory and compute requirements, yet pure cloud inference is expensive. Edge-cloud collaborative speculative decoding—where a small draft model runs on the edge and a large verifier runs in the cloud—is a natural fit, but existing approaches suffer from a fundamental "communication-computation mismatch": the edge must idle during every round-trip to the cloud (stop-and-wait), so WAN latency (RTT) completely negates speculative gains. Empirically, vanilla speculative decoding in WAN settings performs worse than plain cloud autoregressive decoding (0.44× on one benchmark). Alternative approaches using early-exit or hidden-state sharing require model retraining, sacrificing generality. Methodology: PicoSpec is a training-free, plug-and-play framework built around three mechanisms. First, Parallel Drafting: the edge device does not wait for cloud verification of batch S_i before starting batch S_{i+1}; it speculatively assumes full acceptance and immediately begins drafting the next batch. The amortized latency thus becomes max(T_draft, T_RTT + T_verify) rather than their sum, effectively hiding RTT when drafting time dominates. On a rejection, the edge receives an interrupt signal, rolls back the KV cache, and redrafts. Second, Fast Verification: rather than waiting for the complete draft batch to arrive before starting cloud verification, the cloud begins a "Pre-Verify" pass as soon as partial tokens are transmitted, using token-streaming overlap to further shrink pipeline bubbles. Third, Separate Rejection Sampling with Sparse Compression: instead of transmitting full vocabulary distributions (O(V), up to ~500 KB for Llama-3.1 70B with V=128,256), the edge sends only the scalar draft-token probabilities q_1…q_γ (<50 bytes uplink). The cloud transmits a correction only on rejection, compressed to Top-K (index, probability) pairs—reducing downlink from ~500 KB to <100 bytes per rejected token (>3 orders of magnitude). The edge reconstructs the corrected token locally via norm(max(0, P_j(x) − Q_j(x))). A latency-aware truncation mechanism dynamically shortens drafts when the edge falls behind, preventing cloud idle time. The system is evaluated on NVIDIA Jetson AGX (edge) paired with A100 GPUs (cloud) over WAN, using Qwen3 0.6B/32B and Llama 3.2 1B / 3.1 70B model pairs, tested on GSM8K and HumanEval. Key Results: PicoSpec achieves up to 2.90× speedup over cloud autoregressive decoding. For Llama 1B/70B on HumanEval, throughput rises from 6.86 to 19.88 tokens/s (+189.8%); on GSM8K from 6.87 to 17.22 tokens/s (+150.6%). For Qwen 0.6B/32B, gains are more modest: +45.3% on GSM8K (13.89→20.19 tokens/s) and +13.1% on HumanEval. Vanilla speculative decoding degrades below AR baseline in some settings (0.44× for Qwen/HumanEval), confirming the stop-and-wait pathology. Ablation studies quantify individual contributions: removing parallel drafting drops Llama/GSM8K throughput from 17.22 to 12.51 tokens/s; removing split rejection sampling inflates T_verify from 166 ms to 284 ms; removing fast verification reduces throughput from 20.19 to 17.87 tokens/s on Qwen/GSM8K. Draft length n=4 is optimal, with performance stable across n=3–5 (all above 18 tokens/s), indicating robustness to parameter choice. Strengths: - The asynchronous pipeline design is rigorous: the paper provides a formal throughput model with closed-form speedup bounds (Eq. 9–10), including a graceful degradation guarantee (S→1 as α→0) that other async schemes lack. - The sparse compression for rejection sampling is practically impactful—a 3-order-of-magnitude reduction in per-rejection downlink payload (500 KB → <100 bytes) without altering output distribution, which is the correct mathematical decomposition of standard rejection sampling. - Completely training-free and model-agnostic; works with any SLM/LLM pair, unlike early-exit or hidden-state-exchange approaches that require architectural modifications or fine-tuning. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The performance gains are heavily acceptance-rate-dependent: Qwen 0.6B/32B achieves only 1.13× on HumanEval (avg. acceptance length 1.88), suggesting that for poorly aligned model pairs the framework offers marginal benefit despite its machinery. - The evaluation is narrow: only two model pairs, two datasets, and a single edge device (Jetson AGX). No results on varying RTT conditions, bandwidth constraints, or multi-user serving scenarios, making it difficult to characterize when PicoSpec is and is not beneficial. - The rollback-on-rejection mechanism discards all pre-drafted tokens when even a single token is rejected, which can be costly at low acceptance rates. Tree-based speculative decoding (used in EAGLE, Medusa) partially recovers failed drafts; PicoSpec's sequential drafting misses this opportunity. - The Top-K sparse approximation for the correction distribution introduces a subtle approximation error (truncated probability mass is renormalized), but the paper does not report any measurement of output quality degradation relative to exact rejection sampling.
Search Results 2603.19133 PDF
#112 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Transforming FHIR into an OWL Knowledge Graph for Schema-Grounded Natural-Language Querying and Exploratory Data Analysis

preprints.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: This paper delivers a concrete, reproducible five-stage ETL pipeline that achieves an 80% node reduction and >95% NL2SPARQL accuracy, demonstrating genuine engineering value. The ontologization strategy—particularly promoting ICD codes to first-class OWL individuals and the two-stage schema-grounded query architecture—represents meaningful practical novelty. However, evaluation on only 100 patients with 34 curated queries limits generalizability claims, and incomplete terminology coverage (no SNOMED CT, LOINC, or RxNorm axiomatization) constrains the clinical utility.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) is optimized for transactional interoperability at the point of care, but its resource-centric, deeply nested architecture is ill-suited for secondary research use cases such as exploratory data analysis, cohort-level aggregation, and machine learning feature engineering. Clinical meaning is distributed across networks of interlinked resources requiring multi-hop traversals through blank nodes in RDF. The HL7-sanctioned RDF/OWL serialization (fhir.ttl) preserves round-trip fidelity with JSON/XML but retains the nesting that makes analytic querying burdensome. A further barrier is that querying OWL knowledge graphs requires dual expertise in both SPARQL syntax and ontology schema—prior benchmarks show GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 produce poor SPARQL without explicit schema grounding. Methodology: The authors apply a five-stage ETL pipeline to the MIMIC-IV Demo FHIR dataset (914,373 resources, 100 ICU patients) to produce two complementary representations. First, FHIR NDJSON resources are ingested into MongoDB and converted to a FHIR-faithful RDF graph following HL7 guidelines. Second, an ontologized OWL graph is derived by applying five structural transformations: (1) flattening nested backbone elements into single-hop OWL datatype properties (e.g., Condition.code.coding.code → flat:icd10_code); (2) resolving polymorphic choice[x] fields into explicitly typed properties; (3) normalizing variable-length arrays via repeated datatype assertions or auxiliary intermediate classes (LocationEncounter, MedicationMix, DosageInstruction); (4) converting MIMIC's shifted dates into derived age-at-encounter values; and (5) introducing auxiliary structural classes. ICD-9-CM and ICD-10-CM codes are promoted from opaque string literals to first-class OWL individuals linked via object properties. Structural validation uses SHACL constraints in GraphDB; schema-level consistency is verified using ELK, HermiT, and Pellet reasoners in Protégé. A schema-grounded NL2SPARQL interface—implemented via a two-stage GPT-4o pipeline (ontology-guided query planning followed by constrained SPARQL generation with BFS-derived class paths)—is evaluated on a curated set of 34 queries spanning 1–7 resource classes, run 10 times each at three temperatures. Key Results: Ontologization reduces graph nodes by ~80% (from 20.8M to 4.25M) and edges by ~53% (from 33.1M to 15.6M) while introducing 103 OWL datatype properties and 16 object properties versus zero in the FHIR-faithful RDF. SPARQL queries that required 8 predicate hops in the native RDF reduce to 3-line single-hop queries in the OWL graph. All SHACL shapes passed after correction of detected inconsistencies, and no logical unsatisfiability was detected under three reasoners. The NL2SPARQL interface achieved a mean accuracy of 95.0% (range 94.1%–97.1%) across 10 runs at temperature 0 against gold-standard expert-authored SPARQL queries, with low sensitivity to temperature variation, indicating that accuracy is primarily driven by schema grounding rather than stochastic sampling. Strengths: - The pragmatic ontologization strategy is concrete and reproducible: five clearly defined structural transformations are applied to standard FHIR NDJSON, making the pipeline portable to other FHIR repositories beyond MIMIC-IV. - Promoting ICD codes from string literals to first-class OWL individuals enables terminology-grounded querying and internal code–label consistency validation—a meaningful advance over purely tabular FHIR flattening tools. - The two-stage NL2SPARQL architecture (semantic planning separated from syntactic SPARQL generation) is well-motivated by prior benchmarks showing LLM hallucination of SPARQL predicates, and the >95% accuracy result is strong for this class of system. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is conducted on a toy dataset (100 patients, demo corpus) and a modest benchmark of only 34 curated queries under controlled API conditions; generalizability to production-scale EHR repositories and diverse query distributions is unestablished. - Terminology coverage is incomplete: SNOMED CT, LOINC, RxNorm, and NDC are absent or minimally represented; ICD codes are modeled as flat individuals rather than axiomatized class hierarchies, precluding subsumption-based reasoning (e.g., querying all hypertensive disorders under a parent concept). - The round-trip fidelity trade-off is explicit but one-directional—the OWL graph cannot be reliably converted back to the original FHIR resources, limiting use cases that require exchange-format compatibility alongside analytic querying.
Search Results
#113 — Moderately Innovative (58)

mSFT: Addressing Dataset Mixtures Overfiting Heterogeneously in Multi-task SFT (26 ▲ )

Woosung Koh, Jeyoung Jeon, Youngjin Song, Yujin Cheon, Soowon Oh, Jaehyeong Choi, Se-Young Yun · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Mar 2026
Why this rank: MSFT contributes a useful insight that single-pass search for per-task stopping points is fundamentally flawed because excluding datasets shifts the optimization landscape of remaining tasks. The iterative roll-out/roll-back algorithm is practical and well-motivated, but the core idea of heterogeneous overfitting across sub-tasks is well-known, and the method is validated only on small-scale mixtures far from frontier SFT regimes.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-task Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) of language models universally applies a single global compute budget across all sub-datasets in a mixture. This is sub-optimal because different tasks have heterogeneous learning dynamics — some converge and overfit early while others remain under-fitted at the same global stopping point. The paper empirically confirms this gap: across 10 sub-datasets and 6 model families, individual sub-datasets reach peak generalization at training epochs that differ by an average of 1.93 epochs from the overall mixture's optimum. Despite this known problem, all major frontier models (Qwen, DeepSeek, OLMo, Magistral) still use homogeneous compute schedules. Methodology: The paper proposes MSFT (Multi-task mixture SFT), an iterative roll-out/roll-back algorithm. Each iteration trains the model on the current active mixture for a fixed compute budget C, identifies which sub-dataset peaked earliest (lowest optimal compute c_i), and rolls the model back to that peak checkpoint while permanently excluding the overfit sub-dataset. This repeats until all sub-datasets are exhausted. The key insight motivating iterative rather than single-pass search is that excluding even one sub-dataset (1/10 of the mixture) shifts the optimal compute for remaining tasks by an average of 0.91 epochs, because the optimization trajectory changes when gradient contributions are removed. A naïve single roll-out search (SRO SFT) that pre-computes all exclusion points and re-trains from scratch is therefore provably approximate, since the computed c_i values become increasingly inaccurate as the exclusion set grows. MSFT avoids this by keeping search and training aligned: at each stage, the search rollout and the final training rollout are identical. The only new hyperparameter is C (the per-stage compute budget), and the method operates purely at the data-scheduling level with no per-step gradient manipulation overhead. Key Results: MSFT achieves an average accuracy of 63.7% across 10 benchmarks and 6 base models (OLMo 2 1B, Qwen2.5 0.5–7B, Qwen3 8B), versus 61.9% for standard SFT (+1.8% average gain). It outperforms all four baselines — standard SFT, continual SFT (−2.2% vs SFT due to catastrophic forgetting), DynamixSFT (+0.2%), and IES (+0.6%) — and is the only method to improve on all three task categories: Science & Knowledge (+0.7%), Commonsense & Language (+2.4%), and Mathematics (+3.0%). MSFT achieves 1st place on individual benchmarks 26 times across model configurations, double the next best baseline (IES, 13 times), and achieves the lowest standard deviation across benchmarks, confirming that gains are broadly distributed rather than driven by outliers. Gains are robust across dataset sizes (9K–27K) and task counts (5–15), yielding a consistent +5.4% average improvement over SFT. At compute budget C=1, MSFT simultaneously improves performance (+3.4%) while reducing net training FLOPs by 120.3 PFLOPs relative to SFT, because zero roll-out overhead is incurred and sub-dataset exclusion reduces active tokens. Statistical significance is confirmed (p=0.023 on three seeds). Disk overhead averages 4.44× a single model checkpoint, managed via a dynamic pruning algorithm. Strengths: - The key theoretical contribution — that single-pass search is fundamentally flawed because dataset exclusion shifts the optimization landscape of remaining tasks — is rigorously motivated with gradient-level analysis and empirically validated across multiple models and scales. - The method is practical and simple: one new hyperparameter (C), no per-step overhead, no architecture changes, and operates at the data-scheduling level, making it a drop-in replacement for standard SFT. - Evaluation breadth is strong: 6 model families spanning 0.5B–8B parameters, 10 benchmarks, 3 task categories, and ablations over dataset size, task granularity, and compute budget sensitivity all support the claims. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All experiments use a fixed equal-weight mixture of 10 small sub-datasets (1,800 samples each, 18K total), which is far smaller than real-world SFT mixtures used in frontier models (hundreds of thousands to millions of samples, dozens of sub-tasks). Whether heterogeneous overfitting dynamics persist and whether MSFT scales to this regime is not demonstrated. - The method requires saving multiple intermediate checkpoints (average 4.44× model size), which becomes non-trivial at the scale of 70B+ parameter models where even a single checkpoint is hundreds of gigabytes. The authors acknowledge this but do not validate the checkpoint pruning algorithm at realistic frontier scales. - The evaluation benchmarks (CommonsenseQA, GSM8K, ARC, HellaSwag, etc.) are well-studied public benchmarks where data contamination is a concern, and the 5-shot greedy decoding protocol may not reflect instruction-following quality relevant to real SFT use cases.
Recommended 2603.21606 PDF
#114 — Moderately Innovative (58)

DuoKD: Dual Knowledge Distillation from Large Language Models for Robust Graph Neural Networks

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: DuoKD introduces a well-motivated dual positive-negative knowledge distillation paradigm that fills a genuine gap in LLM-to-GNN distillation by exploiting contrastive inter-class structure from LLM confidence distributions. The hallucination filtering gate via Levenshtein distance and perplexity is a practical contribution. However, the framework fundamentally extends existing KD and LLM-augmented GNN pipelines rather than introducing a new paradigm, and evaluation is limited to citation network node classification.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Prior LLM-augmented GNN methods extract only semantically similar (positive) signals from LLMs to enhance node representations on text-attributed graphs. This single-polarity strategy ignores the discriminative value of semantically dissimilar (negative) signals that sharpen inter-class decision boundaries. Worse, on noisy or ambiguous graphs, positive-only augmentation can amplify LLM hallucinations, degrading GNN robustness. The paper targets this gap: how to extract, filter, and distill both positive and negative LLM knowledge into a lightweight GNN in a reliable, principled way. Methodology: DuoKD is a three-module knowledge distillation framework operating on text-attributed graphs (TAGs). (1) Positive-Negative Knowledge Extraction: a three-step prompting pipeline queries a frozen LLM (GPT-4o-mini) per node. Step 1 elicits soft label distributions over all classes; the highest-confidence class becomes the positive opinion and the lowest-confidence class becomes the negative opinion. Steps 2–3 extract semantic rationales (natural language explanations) and structural rationales (neighbor importance scores) for both the positive and counterfactual negative labels, drawing on counterfactual explanation paradigms to make the LLM rationalize incorrect labels and thus expose inter-class inconsistencies. (2) Language-based Knowledge Evaluation: each semantic rationale is scored by a weighted combination of Textual Distance (Levenshtein distance from a counterfactual reference) and Perplexity (GPT-2 fluency). Rationales exceeding a threshold τ are gated out before distillation to suppress hallucinations. (3) Adaptive Dual Knowledge Distillation: opinion-level distillation uses KL divergence to align the student GNN with positive soft labels and a margin-based cosine penalty to push the GNN away from negative label distributions. Rationale-level distillation aligns GNN embeddings (via a projection head) to SBERT-encoded positive semantic rationales (MSE loss) and repels them from negative rationale embeddings (margin loss). Structural rationales modulate GNN message passing edge weights. The unified loss balances supervised cross-entropy with positive and negative distillation terms via three λ hyperparameters. Key Results: On three benchmark datasets (Cora, PubMed, ogbn-arxiv) with 60/20/20 splits, DuoKD consistently outperforms classical GNNs, LLM-augmented models (GIANT, GLEM), and the most recent LLM-to-GNN distillation baseline LinguGKD. Best accuracy: Cora 92.30% (DuoKD+GAT) vs. 90.91% (LinguGKD+SAGE); PubMed 90.93% (DuoKD+GCN) vs. 88.93% (LinguGKD+GAT); ogbn-arxiv 80.13% (DuoKD+GAT) vs. 77.62% (LinguGKD+GAT). Robustness experiments under DICE structural attacks and random text replacement show DuoKD degrades most gracefully: at 40% structural perturbation on Cora, DuoKD achieves 84.21% vs. GLEM's 80.78% and GCN's 71.62%. Ablation confirms positive supervision contributes most (~5–6% accuracy drop when removed), followed by negative supervision (~2–3% drop), with semantic and structural rationales each providing additional smaller gains. Strengths: - The dual positive-negative paradigm is a well-motivated extension of standard KD, filling a genuine gap: prior methods ignore that the LLM's full confidence distribution encodes not just the predicted class but also contrastive inter-class structure. - The language-based quality gate (TD + PPL filtering) is a concrete, lightweight mechanism to suppress hallucination propagation — a practical necessity when LLM inputs come from noisy graphs, and one that prior distillation approaches lack. - Architecture-agnostic design (works with GCN, GAT, SAGE) and the frozen-teacher paradigm avoid costly LLM fine-tuning while still delivering strong gains on a large-scale OGB benchmark. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The per-node LLM querying strategy (three prompt calls per node for opinions, positive rationales, and negative rationales) incurs substantial offline inference cost that is not analyzed or reported, making scalability to very large graphs unclear. - The quality filter relies on Levenshtein distance against a counterfactual reference and GPT-2 perplexity — both shallow proxies for semantic faithfulness. High-confidence but factually hallucinated rationales with normal fluency and edit distance will pass through undetected, limiting the filter's actual reliability guarantee. - Experiments are restricted to citation network node classification tasks; it is unclear whether the positive/negative opinion framework generalizes to graphs with non-textual or heterogeneous attributes, or to tasks like link prediction and graph classification.
Recommended
#115 — Moderately Innovative (58)

RSPlace: Rotation Sensing Macro Placement via Bidirectional Tree Expansion

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: RSPlace identifies a real overlooked gap — no RL-based placer incorporated macro rotation — and the fix is well-engineered with lightweight rotation sensing reusing existing mask computations. However, the contribution is fundamentally an extension of the existing EfficientPlace framework with an additional degree of freedom, and the greedy per-macro rotation selection is acknowledged as myopic. Consistent HPWL improvements across all 8 ISPD2005 benchmarks are solid but expected given the expanded solution space.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Macro placement — determining the positions of large circuit modules (macros) on a chip layout — is a critical subproblem in VLSI chip design, with half-perimeter wirelength (HPWL), density, and congestion as key optimization objectives. Reinforcement learning (RL)-based placers have shown promise, but all prior methods restrict the state space to two-dimensional discrete coordinates, ignoring macro orientation entirely. This omission fundamentally limits the solution space: a macro that cannot fit or connect well in one orientation might be optimal when rotated. No existing RL-based method incorporates rotation, leaving the explored solution space inherently sub-optimal. Methodology: RSPlace builds on the bi-level framework of EfficientPlace (MCTS-guided global search tree + RL agent for local policy learning) and extends it with two rotation-aware mechanisms. First, a rotation sensing module in top-down tree expansion: for each macro to be placed, RSPlace computes position masks and wire masks under all four 90°-increment rotation angles, then selects the orientation minimizing the current HPWL increment via argmin over the combined mask matrices. Pin positions are adjusted using a 2D rotation matrix. The RL agent (trained with PPO) then places the macro at the resulting optimal position/orientation. Second, a bottom-up tree expansion with genotype perturbation: after completing several RL rollouts, RSPlace selects the best leaf node (highest Q-value), randomly samples a subset of macros, perturbs their orientations by ±90°/180°/270°, then greedily re-adjusts each macro's position to the nearest feasible grid that minimizes HPWL increment — preserving zero overlap. This creates a new leaf node representing a phenotype evaluation, which is propagated back up as a bottom-up tree expansion. The solution representation is extended from (x, y) pairs to (x, y, θ) triples. Frontier-based node selection using UCT balances exploration vs. exploitation across the bidirectional tree. Key Results: Evaluated on the ISPD2005 benchmark suite (adaptec1–4, bigblue1–4) across 5 random seeds, RSPlace achieves the lowest HPWL on all 8 circuits compared to 7 baselines (Graph Placement, DeepPR, MaskPlace, ChiPFormer, SA, WireMask-EA, EfficientPlace). On adaptec1, RSPlace achieves 4.72×10⁵ vs. EfficientPlace's 5.90×10⁵ — a 20% reduction. On adaptec2, it achieves 39.79×10⁵ vs. 42.52×10⁵ (~6%). On the largest benchmark bigblue4 (1024 macros), RSPlace achieves 82.29×10⁵ vs. WireMask-EA's 86.88×10⁵ (~5%). All improvements are achieved with the same 1k run-step budget as EfficientPlace and WireMask-EA. Congestion results track HPWL improvements. Mixed-size placement (RSPlace + DREAMPlace) achieves best results on 7 of 8 circuits. Ablation confirms that both top-down (rotation sensing) and bottom-up (perturbation) components are necessary; random rotation without guidance degrades performance significantly. Strengths: - Identifies and addresses a genuine, overlooked gap: prior RL-based placers all ignore macro rotation, and the paper provides theoretical and empirical analysis showing why this matters (more feasible positions, lower HPWL increments). - The rotation sensing mechanism is computationally lightweight — it reuses existing position/wire mask computations across 4 orientations rather than requiring separate RL rollouts per angle. - Consistent improvements across all 8 benchmarks of varying scale (up to 1024 macros), with strong ablation validating the contribution of each component. t-SNE trajectory analysis provides compelling evidence that RSPlace explores qualitatively different (and superior) solution regions. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The rotation sensing greedily minimizes the immediate HPWL increment per macro, which is a myopic criterion — the paper acknowledges this is why bottom-up perturbation is needed, but the interaction between greedy rotation choices and long-horizon placement quality is not deeply analyzed. - Evaluation is limited to HPWL and RUDY congestion as proxy metrics; actual post-routing wirelength, timing, and power results — the true PPA objectives — are not reported, making it unclear whether HPWL gains translate to real chip quality improvements. - The method still requires an external analytical placer (DREAMPlace) for mixed-size placement (standard cell legalization), limiting its standalone applicability and introducing a dependency on a separate tool not trained with rotation awareness.
Search Results
#116 — Moderately Innovative (58)

MSR-Rec: Multi-Step Reasoning-Enhanced LLM for Sequential Recommendation

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: MSR-Rec presents a creative three-stage pipeline combining reasoning chain distillation from a 671B model into an 8B model with a novel bidirectional perplexity-based fusion mechanism. The decomposed reasoning chain design and the insight that conflicting predictions should favor higher-perplexity outputs are methodologically interesting. However, the evaluation on only 1,500 users with 6-item candidate sets significantly inflates metrics and does not reflect realistic ranking, and the circular evaluation bias from using DeepSeek-R1 outputs as both training data and reference ground truth undermines the reasoning quality claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Sequential recommendation systems based on LLMs typically formulate prediction as a single-step instruction-following task, relying on implicit pattern matching over user interaction histories. This approach fails to model the complex cognitive logic behind user decision-making, leading to cognitive biases and limited interpretability. Two critical gaps motivate this work: (1) recommendation datasets contain only implicit interaction records with no annotated multi-step reasoning chains to supervise LLM reasoning, and (2) the tension between reasoning generation (which prioritizes logical coherence) and recommendation prediction (which prioritizes item accuracy) creates optimization conflicts that prior work has not resolved. Methodology: MSR-Rec introduces a three-stage pipeline to activate and leverage multi-step reasoning in a fine-tuned small LLM (Llama3-8B). First, a task-decomposed reasoning chain is designed with six steps — user profile understanding, item feature analysis, matching logic reasoning, comparative differentiation, recommendation decision, and concise summary — to simulate the human decision-making process. This chain is used as a CoT prompt to elicit high-quality reasoning paths from DeepSeek-R1 (671B), followed by data pruning that filters paths for both correctness (predicted item matches ground truth) and step completeness. Second, the pruned reasoning data and behavior data are jointly used to fine-tune Llama3-8B via LoRA, with a weighted loss combining a reasoning loss (α=0.7) and a recommendation loss (β=0.3). Special delimiter tokens separate reasoning and recommendation outputs. Third, at inference, a bidirectional reasoning strategy generates recommendations independently from user-side prompts (preference-candidate matching) and item-side prompts (feature-user alignment), then adaptively fuses outputs using perplexity: when predictions agree, the lower-perplexity output is chosen; when they conflict, the higher-perplexity output (signaling more distinctive reasoning) is selected. Key Results: On three Amazon Review datasets (Electronics, Home & Kitchen, Health & Personal Care, each with 1,500 users), MSR-Rec achieves HitRatio@1 of 0.4453, 0.3453, and 0.4513 respectively, outperforming the strongest baselines (LLaRA and TALLRec) by 30.97%, 25.11%, and 6.94%. Gains are largest on the sparser Electronics and Home datasets, suggesting reasoning is especially beneficial when interaction signals are weak. On reasoning quality metrics (METEOR, BERTScore, GPTScore), MSR-Rec outperforms TALLRec and LLaRA substantially, but scores slightly below RecSAVER (which uses the full 671B DeepSeek-R1 at inference), confirming that the quality gap between a fine-tuned 8B model and a 671B model in reasoning generation persists. Ablation confirms that removing reasoning supervision drops HitRatio@1 by ~2–5 points and GPTScore by ~10–20 points, while using the full training-time reasoning chain at inference (MSR-Rec full) improves reasoning metrics but hurts recommendation accuracy — validating the design choice to use simplified bidirectional prompts at inference. Strengths: - The use of a large frontier model (DeepSeek-R1 671B) purely for offline reasoning data synthesis, then distilling into a small 8B model, is a practical and cost-effective design that avoids inference-time reliance on expensive models. - The bidirectional reasoning with perplexity-based adaptive fusion is a lightweight and principled way to reconcile conflicting user-side and item-side predictions without additional learned components. - The ablation study is thorough, isolating the contributions of reasoning supervision during fine-tuning vs. reasoning prompts during inference, and showing both are necessary. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation setup is narrow: only 1,500 users per dataset with a 6-item candidate set (1 ground truth + 5 negatives), which does not reflect realistic full-catalog ranking and inflates HitRatio@1 compared to standard evaluation protocols used in most RecSys work. - The reasoning quality comparison uses DeepSeek-R1's own outputs as the reference ground truth, which introduces circular evaluation bias — MSR-Rec was trained on these same paths, so the metrics partially reward memorization rather than genuine reasoning quality. - There is no analysis of computational cost or latency at inference time: bidirectional reasoning requires two separate LLM forward passes plus perplexity computation, which may be prohibitive for real-time recommendation compared to single-pass baselines.
Recommended
#117 — Moderately Innovative (58)

An Ontology -Driven Knowledge Graph for Basketball Box Scores: Semantic Filtering and LLM-Based Querying

mdpi.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: This paper scores 58 for building a complete ontology-driven knowledge graph pipeline for basketball analytics with a concrete participation-centered modeling pattern and an LLM-based text-to-SPARQL system that triples baseline accuracy. The prompting strategy using structured path-and-literal patterns is a practical contribution, and the 25-season statistical analysis demonstrates real analytical value. However, the core components (ontology design, KG construction, text-to-SPARQL) are well-established techniques applied to a new but narrow domain, and the lack of user evaluation limits impact claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Sports analytics lacks structured, semantically interconnected data representations. Existing solutions store basketball statistics in HTML tables, relational databases, or JSON files that make multi-entity analytical queries (e.g., "find all EuroLeague games where a Greek player under 2m scored 15+ points against a Russian team") cumbersome and error-prone. Major knowledge graphs (Wikidata, DBpedia) do not capture fine-grained sports statistics, a gap explicitly noted by G. Weikum. The paper addresses this by building an end-to-end semantic framework for basketball box scores, enabling advanced analytics, interactive browsing, and natural-language querying without SPARQL expertise. Methodology: The authors design the BBall ontology (16 classes, 25 object properties, 63 data properties) with a participation-centered modeling pattern that separates persistent identity entities (Player, Team, Coach) from contextualized performance entities (PlayerParticipation, TeamBoxscore, Statline). This handles dual-level statistics, temporal evolution across seasons, and multi-role persons cleanly. Data from the public EuroLeague API (JSON) are transformed to RDF and loaded into a Virtuoso SPARQL triplestore, yielding 5.45 million triples covering 25 seasons (2000–01 through 2024–25): 6,370 games, 3,330 players, 240 coaches, 307 referees. Three prototype applications are built on this KG: (1) EuroLens, a faceted-search tool that maps UI filter combinations to SPARQL templates; (2) HoopProphets, a text-to-SPARQL system using a structured path-and-literal-pattern prompt fed to GPT-4, enabling editable generated SPARQL and multilingual input; (3) EuroLens-Chat, a dialogue-based extension retaining conversational context across turns and formatting results in natural language. A benchmark of 100 questions (20 each for SELECT, FILTER, ORDER BY, GROUP BY, NEGATION) measures text-to-SPARQL accuracy. Historical trend analysis uses linear regression, Welch's t-tests, and Pearson correlation on 25 seasons of statistics. Key Results: GPT-4 with the path-and-literal-pattern method achieves 79% accuracy on the 100-question benchmark, versus 25% for a naive baseline that provides only class/property lists. Performance is strongest on SELECT (≈90%) and ORDER BY/GROUP BY queries, and weakest on FILTER (≈65%) and NEGATION (≈50%). Average SPARQL endpoint response time is 0.31 s; text-to-SPARQL generation takes 1.8 s; natural-language result formatting adds 2.0 s, for ~4.1 s end-to-end latency per chat turn. KG updates for a full EuroLeague round complete in under 30 s. Error analysis of the 21 failed queries shows: 47.6% filtering/logical errors (particularly FILTER NOT EXISTS), 28.6% aggregation/calculation errors, 14.3% schema/property errors, 9.5% semantic misinterpretation. Historical analysis confirms a statistically significant 44% increase in three-point attempts from 2000 to 2025 (r=0.948, R²=0.900); the 2012 assist-rule change produced a jump from 25.5 to 34.2 assists/game (Cohen's d=4.74, p<0.0001); crowd attendance has no meaningful effect on home-court advantage outside of the COVID season. Strengths: - The participation-centered ontology design cleanly handles dual-level (team vs. player) statistics, role transitions (player-to-coach), and temporal segmentation, addressing real modeling challenges rather than trivially mapping a relational schema to RDF. - The LLM prompting strategy (structured path-and-literal patterns with explicit datatype annotations) is a concrete, reproducible improvement over naive property-list baselines, tripling accuracy from 25% to 79%. - The 25-season historical analysis with proper statistical validation (regression, t-tests, effect sizes) demonstrates tangible analytical value of the KG beyond a proof-of-concept, surfacing quantified trends and rule-change impacts. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The 79% text-to-SPARQL accuracy means roughly 1 in 5 queries fails silently—there is no automated error detection or query repair, so users must independently verify results or read generated SPARQL, which undermines usability for non-technical audiences. - The paper lacks any task-based user evaluation with actual analysts, journalists, or fans; all accuracy figures come from a benchmark the authors constructed, making it impossible to assess whether the applications meaningfully improve real workflows or whether the benchmark captures representative real-world query distributions.
Search Results
#118 — Moderately Innovative (58)

DiffBench Meets DiffAgent: End-to-End LLM -Driven Diffusion Acceleration Code Generation

ojs.aaai.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: DiffBench provides the first structured benchmark for LLM-generated diffusion acceleration code, and DiffAgent's GA-based closed-loop optimization is empirically critical for hard performance-constrained tasks. However, the approach is narrowly scoped to four specific acceleration techniques within the diffusers ecosystem, and the agentic framework is a relatively standard combination of existing components (Reflexion debugging, genetic algorithms, plan-and-code agents).
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Diffusion models have become dominant for high-fidelity image and video generation, but their multi-step iterative inference incurs substantial computational overhead. While many acceleration techniques exist (fast samplers, feature caching, token merging, kernel compilation), combining them correctly for a given model architecture and deployment target requires deep expert knowledge and bespoke engineering. Existing LLMs can do general code generation but lack the domain knowledge and feedback mechanisms needed to autonomously produce correct, optimized diffusion acceleration code. No benchmark existed to rigorously measure this capability. Methodology: The paper introduces two components. DiffBench is a benchmark of 604 tasks organized across five difficulty levels: (L1) basic pipeline generation, (L2) single-method acceleration, (L3) compositional multi-technique acceleration, (L4) acceleration meeting an explicit speedup target, and (L5) acceleration under a hard latency constraint. Tasks cover six diffusion architectures (SD1.5/2.1/XL, DiT, PixArt-α/Σ), three samplers (DDIM, DPM-Solver, UniPC), and four acceleration techniques (ToMe, DeepCache, T-Gate, FP16). Evaluation is a three-stage automated pipeline: static parameter matching, absolute quality assessment via CLIP-Score on COCO samples, and relative speedup/latency measurement against a baseline. DiffAgent is the proposed LLM-based agentic framework with four components: (1) a Planning Agent that decomposes the user prompt into a structured code plan and, during optimization loops, augments plans with feedback and prior domain knowledge; (2) a Coding Agent that generates code conditioned on the plan using reference acceleration code templates as in-context examples; (3) a Debugging Agent based on the Reflexion architecture that iteratively identifies and corrects errors, with fallback to plan regeneration after Tcode×Tdebug failures; and (4) a Genetic Algorithm (GA) Selector that maintains a population of P=7 candidate implementations, scores them on quality loss (L) and speedup (U), selects M=4 promising offspring, and drives iterative plan refinement for up to Tsel=4 rounds. The GA introduces diversity by generating P−M fresh plans each round to avoid local optima. Key Results: Baseline LLMs (GPT-4.1, Claude Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5 Flash, o3-mini) achieve pass rates below 35% on DiffBench's acceleration tasks, highlighting the benchmark's difficulty. DiffAgent consistently and substantially improves all four models. The best combination, Claude Sonnet 4 + DiffAgent, achieves an average pass rate of 81.59% versus 54.30% for Claude Sonnet 4 alone (+27.29 pp), and reaches 99.23% on Level 3 and 63.44% on Level 5. For o3-mini the gain is +44.54 pp (11.92% → 56.46%). Error analysis shows DiffAgent nearly eliminates compile errors (e.g., from 31.18% to 2.15% at Level 5), removes absolute quality failures entirely in several settings, and dramatically reduces relative quality/speed errors at Levels 4–5. Ablation studies confirm that removing the GA collapses Level 4/5 pass rates to ~4.3% (from 33/63%), removing the knowledge base drops average Sp by 16.69 pp, and removing the debugging agent reduces Level 5 pass rate by ~30 pp. Strengths: - DiffBench provides the first structured, multi-level, automatically evaluated benchmark for LLM-generated diffusion acceleration code, covering diverse architectures, conditioning types, samplers, and optimization strategies — a genuine infrastructure contribution to the community. - The GA-based closed-loop optimization loop is well-motivated and empirically critical: ablation shows it is responsible for nearly all gains on the hardest performance-constrained tasks (Levels 4–5), where pass rates collapse from 33/63% to 4.3% without it. - The three-stage evaluation protocol is rigorous and practically meaningful, combining syntactic correctness, absolute output quality, and relative efficiency measurement rather than relying on any single proxy metric. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is constructed entirely around four specific acceleration techniques (ToMe, DeepCache, T-Gate, FP16) and six specific model families, all from the Hugging Face/diffusers ecosystem. Generalization to other frameworks (e.g., JAX-based, custom CUDA), video diffusion models, or emerging techniques is unproven. - Even the best result (Claude Sonnet 4 + DiffAgent) achieves only 33.3% pass rate on Level 4 and 63.4% on Level 5, meaning roughly one-third to two-thirds of quantitatively constrained tasks still fail, limiting practical deployment readiness for hard optimization scenarios. - The computational cost of running DiffAgent — up to Tcode×Tdebug×Tsel LLM calls plus actual GPU execution per task — is not analyzed or reported, making it unclear whether the approach is practical for rapid development iteration or only for offline batch optimization.
Search Results
#119 — Moderately Innovative (58)

BEAVER: A Training-Free Hierarchical Prompt Compression Method via Structure-Aware Page Selection (10 ▲ )

Zhengpei Hu, Kai Li, Dapeng Fu, Chang Zeng, Yue Li, Yuanhao Tang, Jianqiang Huang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Mar 2026
Why this rank: BEAVER is a well-engineered system that combines known techniques — page-level segmentation, hybrid lexical-semantic scoring, and structural priors — into an effective training-free compression framework with impressive speed. The triple structural prior (Anchor/Flow/Flash) is intuitive but not conceptually surprising. While results are strong, especially on RULER, the approach is a careful integration of existing ideas rather than a departure from prior paradigms.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As LLM context windows scale to millions of tokens, two critical bottlenecks emerge: (1) the O(L²) self-attention complexity causes prefill latency to surge with context length, and (2) extending context does not proportionally improve information utilization — models suffer from the "Lost in the Middle" effect. Existing prompt compression methods address this but fall into two flawed paradigms: unsupervised statistical methods (e.g., LongLLMLingua) that use perplexity-based token pruning produce syntactically fragmented outputs, while supervised methods (e.g., LLMLingua-2) incur high training costs and generalize poorly across model families. Neither paradigm preserves discourse integrity at scale. Methodology: BEAVER is a training-free framework that reframes prompt compression as hierarchical page selection rather than token-level pruning. The pipeline has three components. The Segmenter partitions the context into logical segments using natural language delimiters (newlines, headings) and greedily packs them into fixed-capacity 2D page tensors (default page size M=64), enabling efficient batch matrix operations and preserving local semantic boundaries. The PageEncoder produces dense page-level representations via dual-path pooling: weighted average pooling (with in-context Inverse Term Frequency weighting to suppress high-frequency uninformative tokens) captures global semantics, while max pooling captures salient local features (e.g., rare keywords); the two paths are linearly fused. For short queries a single dense vector is produced; for longer queries a late-interaction ColBERT-style multi-vector representation is used. The QueryPlanner scores each page via a hybrid semantic-lexical relevance function: cosine similarity between page and query embeddings (semantic branch) combined with ITF-weighted token overlap (lexical branch), normalized and mixed with hyperparameter λ. Page selection then enforces three structural priors: fixed Anchor pages (first k_anc pages, preserving document metadata), Flow pages (a contiguous window preceding the query, preserving local context), and Flash pages (top-k_flash by hybrid score, capturing distant evidence). Finally, a sentence-level smoothing step extends selected token spans outward to sentence boundaries to ensure syntactic completeness before concatenation. Key Results: On LongBench (2k token budget), BEAVER achieves an average of 42.2, outperforming LLMLingua-2 (39.1) and approaching LongLLMLingua (48.0) at 5.2× speedup vs. LongLLMLingua's 2.6×. The single-document QA score of 40.7 sets a new SOTA over all baselines. On ZeroSCROLLS, BEAVER scores 32.0 average, comparable to LongLLMLingua (32.7) while running nearly 2× faster. The most striking results appear on RULER (16k context, 3k budget): BEAVER achieves an overall average of 83.7 vs. LLMLingua-2's 47.9 and LongLLMLingua's 28.8. Single-needle retrieval is 100%, multi-needle (Key-1/Key-2) reaches 99%/88%, and all baselines collapse to single-digit scores on multi-needle tasks. On the out-of-domain L-Eval benchmark BEAVER scores 57.6, beating LLMLingua-2 (54.6) and LongLLMLingua (51.5). Latency at 128k context is 1.20s vs. 31.7s for LongLLMLingua (26.4× speedup) and 7.1s for LLMLingua-2 (5.9× speedup), with sub-linear scaling in context length. Cross-model scalability tests on Qwen3 0.6B–32B show BEAVER retains 84–98% of dense performance, versus 28–48% for LLMLingua-2. Strengths: - The page-level abstraction is an elegant solution to the fragmentation problem: by treating semantically coherent segments as atomic units, BEAVER avoids the garbled outputs that plague token-level pruning methods, as demonstrated compellingly in qualitative comparisons on financial QA, code, and few-shot tasks. - The triple structural prior (Anchor + Flow + Flash) mimics a cognitively motivated reading strategy; ablations confirm each component is load-bearing, with Flash-only dropping 2.8 points and Flow-only dropping 8.1 points from the full model. - Truly training-free and model-agnostic: the 26.4× latency speedup over LongLLMLingua and near-perfect RULER scores across 0.6B–32B models make BEAVER practically deployable as a plug-and-play module without dataset collection or fine-tuning. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The page-granularity design sacrifices fine-grained precision for speed; the paper acknowledges it may retain minor redundancies within selected pages that token-level pruning would eliminate, which likely explains why BEAVER underperforms LongLLMLingua on some summarization and few-shot tasks where uniform coverage of the original document matters more than precision retrieval. - The hybrid scoring mechanism relies on lexical and semantic similarity, making it structurally ill-suited for multi-hop reasoning where intermediate supporting evidence is lexically distant from the query — the authors concede this limitation explicitly, but it is non-trivial since many real-world QA tasks require exactly this kind of chain reasoning. - Several key hyperparameters (λ, γ, k_anc, w_flow, M) are fixed globally and may require manual tuning for new domains; unlike end-to-end learned methods that adapt automatically, BEAVER lacks a principled way to set these without held-out validation data.

#120 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Rethinking Token-Level Policy Optimization for Multimodal Chain-of-Thought (17 ▲ )

Yunheng Li, Hangyi Kuang, Hengrui Zhang, Jiangxia Cao, Zhaojie Liu, Qibin Hou, Ming-Ming Cheng · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Mar 2026
Why this rank: PEPO's token-level analysis rigorously establishing the complementarity of visual similarity and entropy signals is well-executed, and the resulting lightweight weighting scheme delivers consistent gains across diverse tasks. However, the method fundamentally layers a reweighting heuristic atop existing GRPO/DAPO frameworks rather than rethinking the optimization paradigm, and the task-specific hyperparameter sensitivity limits its practical novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) methods for training Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) apply sequence-level supervision uniformly across all tokens in a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) response. This coarse-grained approach ignores the heterogeneous roles of individual tokens: some tokens are visually grounded (perceptual anchors) while others represent uncertain decision points (exploratory transitions). Prior entropy-based token-level methods borrowed from text-only LLMs lack visual awareness, and perception-aware alternatives introduce auxiliary masking branches or attention-based measures incompatible with efficient training frameworks like FlashAttention. The gap is a token-level policy optimization framework that jointly captures both perceptual grounding and exploratory reasoning dynamics in a lightweight, plug-in manner. Methodology: The paper first conducts a token-level analysis on Geometry3K using Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct, computing per-token visual similarity (VS) as the mean cosine similarity between a response token's hidden state and all vision token hidden states across all transformer layers. They show that correct responses concentrate significantly higher VS scores in their top-K tokens than incorrect responses, while the bottom-K tokens show negligible separation. Complementarily, high-entropy tokens cluster around reasoning-transition expressions (verification, correction), whereas high-VS tokens cluster around perceptual concepts (geometric entities, spatial attributes), establishing that the two signals are complementary and non-redundant. Building on this analysis, the paper proposes Perception-Exploration Policy Optimization (PEPO). For each response token, PEPO computes: (1) a perception score VSt via layer-averaged cosine similarity to vision tokens, and (2) an exploration score Ht via token-level Shannon entropy of the output logits. Both are min-max normalized per response. A smooth gating operator computes a mean-centered joint score and applies a tanh activation, multiplied by the raw VSt to keep perception dominant and prevent entropy from amplifying visually irrelevant tokens. A softmax rescaled to unit mean produces token weights wt. The final token-level advantage is A_t = [(1-λ) + λ·wt]·A, where λ is linearly annealed from 0 to 1 during training to ensure stable early optimization. PEPO adds negligible overhead (overhead ratio ρ < 1%) and integrates directly into GRPO (PEPOG) or DAPO (PEPOD) without auxiliary branches or extra supervision. Key Results: On geometry and math/logic reasoning (Geometry3K, MathVista, MathVerse, LogicVista), PEPOG outperforms GRPO by +3.67 points on Qwen2.5-VL-3B and +3.51 points on InternVL3-2B (avg@8 across four benchmarks). PEPOD outperforms DAPO by +0.45 (Qwen2.5-VL-3B) and +5.15 (InternVL3-2B). On visual puzzles (PuzzleVQA + AlgoPuzzleVQA), PEPOG gains +1.65 and PEPOD gains +1.71 over respective baselines. For visual grounding (RefCOCO + LISA), PEPOG achieves +0.86 average IoU@50 gain, while High-Entropy RL collapses entirely in 3 of 3 runs. For few-shot classification (FGVC Aircraft, Flower102), PEPOG improves over GRPO by +5.32 and +1.46 points respectively. On the large-scale ViRL39K scalability experiment, PEPOG achieves 49.84 average versus GRPO's 45.97 and PAPO's 48.27 across 7 diverse benchmarks (+3.87 over GRPO). Ablations confirm that neither perception-only nor exploration-only achieves the full gain, and that removing gradual scheduling, min-max normalization, or gated fusion each independently degrades performance. Strengths: - Principled, empirically grounded motivation: the token-level analysis rigorously establishes the complementarity of visual similarity and entropy before proposing the method, providing interpretable justification rather than heuristic design. - Lightweight and modular: PEPO introduces <1% computational overhead and integrates seamlessly into any GRPO-style framework without auxiliary networks, extra supervision, or modifications to model architecture. - Broad empirical validation across five diverse task categories (geometry reasoning, visual grounding, few-shot classification, visual puzzles, and large-scale scaling) and two independent model families, demonstrating consistent and robust gains. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All experiments are limited to 2B–3B parameter models due to GPU memory constraints; it is unclear whether the perception–exploration complementarity and resulting gains hold for 7B+ models where representational dynamics may differ substantially. - The hyperparameter α requires per-task tuning (different values for geometry, grounding, classification), and the analysis shows task-specific sensitivity, raising questions about practical deployment in mixed-task or continual learning settings. - The visual similarity metric averages cosine similarity across all layers equally, which may be suboptimal; the paper shows all-layer aggregation beats any single depth range but does not explore learned or attention-weighted layer aggregation as a potentially stronger alternative.
Recommended 2603.22847 PDF
#121 — Moderately Innovative (58)

CUA-Suite: Massive Human-annotated Video Demonstrations for Computer-Use Agents (69 ▲ )

Xiangru Jian, Shravan Nayak, Kevin Qinghong Lin, Aarash Feizi, Kaixin Li, Patrice Bechard, Spandana Gella, Sai Rajeswar · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Mar 2026
Why this rank: CUA-SUITE is a valuable large-scale data contribution — 55 hours of continuous 30 fps expert video with rich annotations across 87 professional desktop applications is a genuine step up from prior screenshot-based datasets. The universal-format design ensuring forward compatibility with future training paradigms is thoughtful. However, innovation is primarily in data engineering and scale rather than methodology, and the core claim that continuous video improves over screenshot-based training is not empirically validated in this work.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Computer-use agents (CUAs) that can autonomously operate desktop software represent a transformative capability, yet progress is bottlenecked by the scarcity of high-quality training data. Existing datasets rely on sparse screenshots rather than continuous video, discarding the temporal dynamics of human interaction. The largest prior open dataset (ScaleCUA) contains only 2 million screenshots amounting to less than 20 hours of video. Furthermore, datasets overwhelmingly target web and mobile platforms while professional desktop applications — IDEs, 3D modeling tools, scientific software — remain poorly covered. This gap forces agents trained on existing data to fail at precisely the specialized desktop tasks where automation would be most valuable (~60% task failure rate on professional apps). Methodology: CUA-SUITE is a three-component ecosystem built from a unified data engine of human expert recordings. The core component, VIDEOCUA, captures continuous 30 fps screen video of human annotators performing ~10,000 tasks across 87 open-source professional desktop applications, yielding ~55 hours and 6 million frames. Recordings also include millisecond-precise kinematic cursor traces (clicks, drags, scrolls, keystrokes). Keyframes preceding state-changing actions are manually annotated with bounding boxes for every visible UI element, textual labels, and 50% functional categorization into eight semantic types. Dense multi-layered reasoning annotations are synthesized via Claude-Sonnet-4.5, generating four layers per trajectory step: observation (~157 words), reasoning chain (~194 words), action description (~18 words), and reflection (~127 words), averaging 497 words per step total. The second component, GROUNDCUA, derives a 700K instruction-tuning dataset from 56K annotated screenshots with 3.6 million element annotations, used to train GROUNDNEXT (3B and 7B VLMs) via SFT then RL. The third component, UI-VISION, is a 450-task benchmark testing element grounding, layout grounding, and action prediction. The dataset is designed as a universal superset: continuous video can be losslessly downsampled into any format required by existing agent frameworks (screenshot-action pairs, state-action-next-state triplets, kinematic traces). Key Results: On the UI-VISION element grounding benchmark, the current best model (MAI-UI-32B) achieves 47.7% average accuracy — roughly double the performance of UI-TARS-72B one year prior, yet still far from practical reliability. Spatial grounding remains the hardest sub-task across all models (best: 26.9%), while Basic and Functional categories approach 60% for top models. For action prediction on VIDEOCUA's 256 sampled tasks, OpenCUA-32B achieves only 37.7% @50px and 22.0% @20px, while OpenCUA-7B reaches 16.5% @50px — confirming severe struggles with professional desktop applications. Per-application accuracy ranges from 3.6% to 73.3% @50px; canvas-based and specialized creative tools are hardest. Human evaluation of the 32B model on 576 steps reveals 57.6% combined stepwise accuracy: action type correctness is 85.9%, but grounding accuracy on coordinate-based steps is the dominant bottleneck. GROUNDNEXT-3B, trained on GROUNDCUA, achieves 50.6 on OS-World Verified when paired with an o3 planner, demonstrating practical downstream utility. Strengths: - Scale and quality: 55 hours of continuous 30 fps expert video with kinematic cursor traces and 497-word multi-layered reasoning annotations per step is a qualitative leap over prior datasets — no existing open dataset provides continuous video for professional desktop CUAs at this scale. - Universal format: Capturing full video as a superset means CUA-SUITE remains applicable as new training paradigms emerge (video world models, continuous spatial control, offline RL), without requiring re-collection. - Holistic ecosystem: Tightly coupling training data (VIDEOCUA, GROUNDCUA) with evaluation (UI-VISION) on the same data distribution enables rigorous diagnosis of model failures and controlled training/evaluation comparisons. - Honest failure analysis: The preliminary evaluation candidly quantifies how badly current foundation models fail on professional desktop tasks, providing clear targets for future work rather than cherry-picking favorable results. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Desktop-only, open-source software coverage: Focusing exclusively on 87 open-source applications may limit transferability to closed-source commercial software (Microsoft Office, Adobe suite) where real-world automation demand is highest; it is unclear how well agents trained on this data generalize. - Non-coordinate action evaluation gap: Automated metrics cover only coordinate-based actions; keyboard shortcuts, text entry, and menu navigation are excluded from quantitative evaluation, leaving a significant portion of real agent behavior unassessed beyond the small human study (576 steps, 49 tasks). - No training experiments on VIDEOCUA itself: Despite being the primary contribution, no fine-tuning results on VIDEOCUA's continuous video data are presented — the paper evaluates pre-trained models zero-shot, so the core claim that continuous video improves over screenshot-based training is not empirically validated in this work.
Recommended 2603.24440 PDF
#122 — Moderately Innovative (58)
