Skillens: recognising and mapping novel skills from millions of job ads across Europe using language models

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: SkiLLens addresses a real operational need — detecting emerging skills before taxonomies update — and delivers at genuine European scale (18M ads, 28 countries, 23 languages), which no prior system has achieved. However, the methodology is a well-engineered pipeline of individually standard components (FastText embeddings, GPT-4 classification, embedding retrieval) rather than a technically novel approach. The human-in-the-loop design is pragmatic and the empirical improvements over prior baselines are solid, but the contribution is primarily one of scale and systems integration rather than methodological innovation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Static labor market taxonomies like ESCO (13,000+ skills) cannot keep pace with emerging competencies such as "prompt engineering" or "MLOps monitoring" that appear in job advertisements before any official taxonomy update. Manually reviewing millions of job postings to detect and validate novel skills is infeasible at scale. Eurostat has collected 450M+ OJAs from 27 EU countries and the UK, but exploiting this resource for real-time skill monitoring requires both extracting skill mentions from unstructured multilingual text and solving the entity-linking problem of mapping them to a standardized taxonomy — neither of which existing systems handle at this scale or across this many languages. Methodology: SkiLLens is a three-step human-in-the-loop pipeline applied to ~18M job ads across 28 countries and 23 languages. Step 1 extracts candidate novel skills by training country-specific FastText Skip-Gram embeddings (100d, 5 epochs, lr=0.01, selected via grid search over 160 configs using the HSS metric) on each national OJA corpus, then comparing token embeddings to all ~98,000 ESCO preferred and alternative labels via cosine similarity. Candidates with fuzzy match score ≤70 (normalized Levenshtein) to any existing ESCO entry are retained as lexically distinct. Step 2 scores candidates by a weighted combination of semantic similarity and corpus frequency (α=0.85 for similarity), retaining terms covering 95% of cumulative frequency. GPT-4 then performs binary skill/non-skill classification with few-shot prompting (recall-favoring, ~70% precision, >98% recall in spot checks). National labor market experts from the European Network of Regional Labour Market Monitoring then validate the filtered list for contextual relevance and novelty — each expert reviewing up to 400 candidates. Step 3 maps validated candidates to ESCO: texts are translated to English via DeepSeek v3 (translation-then-encode proved superior to native multilingual embeddings), encoded with nlper/gte-large (335M params, top MTEB performer), and the top-3 most similar ESCO preferred labels retrieved via ChromaDB cosine search. GPT-4 then selects the best match from the three candidates given ESCO descriptions. Key Results: Out of 4,941 proposed novel skills, 3,552 (71.9%) were validated by national experts as relevant and genuinely novel. On the ESCO mapping task, SkiLLens outperforms both prior English-only baselines (Decorte et al. 2022 and Clavié & Soulié 2023) on tech and house skill datasets across all metrics: RP@1 0.633 vs. 0.465 (tech), MRR 0.731 vs. 0.537 (tech), RP@10 0.901 vs. 0.689 (tech). In the multilingual baseline evaluation mapping ESCO alternative labels to preferred labels across 22 languages, Acc@3 ranges from 59.9% (Estonian) to 90.5% (Croatian), with English at 89.0% Acc@3 / 84.5% LLM accuracy. LLM refinement over embedding-only retrieval provides consistent but modest gains (e.g., Italian: 65.5% → 70.7%; Romanian: 46.8% → 54.5%). Strengths: - Operates at genuine European scale (18M ads, 28 countries, 23 languages), filling a gap that all prior work leaves unaddressed — no existing system has extracted and assessed novelty of skills across this linguistic and geographic breadth. - The HITL design is well-engineered: GPT-4 filtering with recall-favoring prompts minimizes expert burden (≤400 candidates per expert) while preserving valid skills (>98% recall), and national experts provide ground-truth novelty judgments that pure automated systems cannot. - Solid empirical comparison against the two most relevant prior methods (Decorte et al., Clavié & Soulié) on their own benchmark datasets, with substantial margin improvements across all reported metrics. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The pivot-translation approach (everything translated to English before embedding) loses language-specific nuance and is a practical workaround rather than a principled multilingual solution; performance on morphologically rich low-resource languages (Estonian Acc@1=46.5%, Romanian=46.8%) remains substantially below high-resource languages, but this gap is acknowledged without quantified analysis of root causes. - The ESCO mapping benchmark uses alternative-label-to-preferred-label matching as a proxy for novel skill placement, which is not the same task — alternative labels are already in-taxonomy, so the benchmark may overestimate performance on genuinely out-of-taxonomy emerging skills. - No inter-annotator agreement metric is reported across the expert validation step, making it impossible to assess consistency of the "novelty" judgments that underpin the core claim; country-level precision varies substantially due to differing expert interpretations of what constitutes a "skill" vs. an occupation or experience.
Recommended
#123 — Moderately Innovative (58)

GameplayQA: A Benchmarking Framework for Decision-Dense POV-Synced Multi-Video Understanding of 3D Virtual Agents (15 ▲ )

Yunzhe Wang, Runhui Xu, Kexin Zheng, Tianyi Zhang, Jayavibhav Niranjan Kogundi, Soham Hans, Volkan Ustun · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Mar 2026
Why this rank: GAMEPLAYQA introduces a well-structured evaluation framework with a principled Self-Other-World entity decomposition and a structured distractor taxonomy that converts accuracy into diagnostic information. The combinatorial template pipeline is reproducible. However, as a benchmark paper, the core methodological contribution is in evaluation design rather than new capabilities, the scale is modest (2,365 QA pairs), and the findings largely confirm expected model limitations rather than revealing surprises.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal LLMs are increasingly used as perceptual backbones for autonomous agents in 3D environments, yet existing video understanding benchmarks fail to evaluate three capabilities critical to agentic perception: dense state-action tracking, other-agent modeling, and environment grounding. Current benchmarks are slow-paced and passive, provide only coarse global metrics that cannot diagnose whether failures stem from temporal misinterpretation, object hallucination, or role confusion, and focus almost exclusively on single-viewpoint perception. The absence of multi-POV, decision-dense evaluation leaves a critical gap for embodied AI research. Methodology: GAMEPLAYQA is an end-to-end benchmarking framework built around synchronized multiplayer 3D gameplay videos from 9 commercial games (Counter-Strike 2, Battlefield 6, Arc Raiders, Minecraft, Apex Legends, etc.). The core annotation schema uses a Self–Other–World entity decomposition with six primitive label types (Self-Action, Self-State, Other-Action, Other-State, World-Object, World-Event), densely captioned at ρ ≈ 1.22 labels/second across 2,219 seconds of footage. Annotation follows a two-stage human-in-the-loop workflow: Gemini-3-Pro generates candidate labels and distractors, then four graduate student annotators verify and refine them (31.1% deleted, 42.7% edited, 26.2% accepted as-is). QA pairs are generated through a combinatorial template-based algorithm instantiating questions across five orthogonal dimensions (number of videos, context target, entity type, distractor type, question form), producing 399K candidate pairs downsampled to 4K for balance, then reduced to 2,365 gold pairs after quality assurance. A structured distractor taxonomy—Lexical, Scene, Temporal, Role, and Cross-Video—enables fine-grained hallucination diagnosis. Language prior filtering queries a model text-only to remove questions solvable without visual grounding. The benchmark organizes tasks into three cognitive levels: L1 (single-reference perception), L2 (temporal reasoning), and L3 (cross-video understanding across synchronized multi-POV footage). Key Results: Sixteen frontier MLLMs were evaluated zero-shot. The best model, Gemini 2.5 Pro, achieves 71.3% overall accuracy versus 80.5% for humans—a 9-point gap that widens substantially on harder sub-tasks. Accuracy degrades monotonically across cognitive levels: L1 averages 61.2%, L2 drops to 56.0%, and L3 falls to 49.4% across all models. The hardest tasks are Occurrence Count (36.5% average) and Cross-Video Ordering (38.8%). Error analysis by entity type reveals Other-Action (54.0%) and Other-State (55.4%) are roughly 8 points harder than World-Object recognition (62.0%), confirming that models struggle with other-agent attribution. Cross-video and temporal distractors cause the most errors (39.7% and 35.0% error rates), while scene distractors are easiest (6.5%). Fast-paced shooters (Counter-Strike 2: 49.7% error, Battlefield 6: 47.1%) are substantially harder than exploration games (Cyberpunk 2077: 30.5%). Ablation studies confirm that removing video entirely drops GPT-5-mini from 62.7% to 29.4%, and shuffling frames degrades L2/L3 performance but not L1, isolating temporal ordering as the key bottleneck. Cross-domain transfer to dashcam collision and ego-centric Lego assembly videos (213 questions) preserves relative model rankings and task difficulty ordering with minimal pipeline adjustment. Strengths: - The Self–Other–World triadic decomposition is well-motivated and cleanly maps to multi-agent reinforcement learning frameworks, making the benchmark directly relevant to embodied AI evaluation. - The structured distractor taxonomy is a genuine methodological contribution: it converts a black-box accuracy metric into a diagnostic tool that pinpoints whether failures stem from temporal confusion, role misattribution, or semantic error. - The combinatorial template-based generation pipeline is reproducible and scalable to new games and domains, as demonstrated by the cross-domain transfer experiment requiring only actor label renaming. - The language prior filtering procedure is a principled safeguard against question-phrasing shortcuts, and the human evaluation of 120 sampled questions provides credible validity evidence. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark covers only 100 videos and 2,365 QA pairs, which is small relative to general video QA benchmarks (Ego4D: 74K questions, TVQA: 152K), limiting statistical power for fine-grained sub-category analysis, especially for rarer question types with fewer than 15 examples. - Annotation error propagates combinatorially: a single mislabeled timestamp or entity type can corrupt multiple generated questions, and the admitted 8% faulty question rate in human evaluation—combined with the inherent subjectivity of intent categories—means benchmark noise is non-trivial for evaluating closely matched models. - The frame-sampling strategy (1 FPS, max 32 frames) used for non-video-native models is a severe approximation for the very fast-paced decision-dense gameplay the benchmark is designed to stress-test, potentially underestimating model capabilities rather than isolating temporal modeling failures. - The benchmark does not include decision-reasoning tasks ("What is the best action to take?"), which are arguably the most important capability for planning agents; the current scope stops at perception and intent recognition, leaving the hardest agentic evaluation to future work.
Recommended 2603.24329 PDF
#124 — Moderately Innovative (58)

GRAFF: GRaph-Augmented Fine-grained Fusion for Large Language Models

aclanthology.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: GRAFF presents a clean engineering solution to graph-LLM integration by reusing the LLM's own embeddings to avoid encoder misalignment, and the counterfactual diagnostic is a well-designed evaluation contribution. However, the core components (GAT injection, residual adapters, dual-channel input) are standard building blocks recombined rather than fundamentally new ideas, and the gains on the most challenging benchmark (WebQSP) are marginal at best.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs struggle to effectively leverage structured graph data for question answering. Existing approaches either verbalize graphs as flat text (losing structural relationships) or use graph pooling to compress the entire graph into a single token prepended to the LLM input (losing fine-grained node-level signals and misaligning graph embeddings from external encoders with the LLM's embedding space). Both paradigms limit the model's ability to perform multi-hop reasoning over complex graph structures, particularly in specialized domains where high-quality structured knowledge is available. Methodology: GRAFF (GRaph-Augmented Fine-grained Fusion) introduces a lightweight plug-in adapter that injects node-level graph information into an intermediate decoder layer of a frozen LLM. The key innovations are: (1) a dual-channel graph input mechanism that separates the text-attributed graph into a structural channel (single average-pooled token per node/edge using the LLM's own embeddings) and a textual channel (full subword token sequences), concatenated as input; (2) a 2-layer Graph Attention Network (GAT) module inserted at a tunable intermediate decoder layer D, which processes the structural channel's hidden states to propagate multi-hop neighborhood information; and (3) a residual integration step that projects GAT outputs back to the LLM's hidden dimension via learned up/down projection matrices, adding them to the corresponding node token representations before passing through the remaining decoder layers. Only the adapter parameters (GAT + projections) are trained via standard supervised fine-tuning on (graph, question, answer) triples; the base LLM is frozen. The LLM's own token embeddings are reused for graph node encoding, eliminating embedding space misalignment from external encoders like BERT. Key Results: Evaluated on the GraphQA benchmark (ExplaGraphs, SceneGraphs, WebQSP) plus a synthetic counterfactual dataset using LLaMA-3.2-3B as the backbone. GRAFF achieves: WebQSP 72.2 (vs. LoRA 71.1, G-Retriever 67.4), ExplaGraphs 92.5 (vs. LoRA/GRAG 88.9, G-Retriever 83.7), SceneGraphs 90.2 (vs. LoRA 85.3, G-Retriever 82.3), and Synthetic 79.4 (vs. LoRA 58.3, G-Retriever 56.4). Average improvement over the strongest baseline is 10.14% across four datasets; on the counterfactual synthetic set the gain is +36.2%, indicating that GRAFF grounds reasoning in the provided graph rather than in memorized pretraining knowledge. Results are statistically significant (Welch's t-test, p < 0.05 on all datasets). On an alternative backbone (Qwen-2.5-3B), GRAFF similarly outperforms LoRA and G-Retriever by 5.3% and 10.1% relative on WebQSP and ExplaGraphs respectively. Ablations confirm that: (a) single-layer insertion at an intermediate layer (layers 6–8 optimal) outperforms inserting at all layers, and (b) the dual-channel input is essential — removing either channel degrades accuracy by 20–25+ points. Strengths: - The dual-channel input design is technically clean: it avoids external encoders entirely by reusing the LLM's own embeddings for graph node representations, directly addressing the embedding misalignment problem of prior work like G-Retriever. - The counterfactual synthetic dataset is a well-motivated diagnostic that isolates whether gains come from genuine graph utilization vs. LLM memorization — the 36.2% gap there is a strong indicator of structural grounding. - Comprehensive ablations (placement depth, GAT depth, single- vs. dual-channel, one- vs. all-layer fusion) provide actionable insights and validate each design choice independently. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The dual-channel mechanism doubles or more the number of input tokens (structural + textual channels), significantly increasing memory and compute costs as graph size grows; the paper only demonstrates effectiveness on small retrieved subgraphs and does not address scalability to larger graphs. - Experiments are confined to a single small base model (LLaMA-3.2-3B; Qwen-2.5-3B in appendix), leaving open whether the gains hold for larger LLMs where pretraining knowledge is much richer and harder to override with a lightweight adapter. - The improvements on WebQSP over the best non-GNN baseline (LoRA, 71.1→72.2) are modest and barely significant (p=0.044), which somewhat undercuts the narrative for knowledge-graph QA where multi-hop reasoning is most critical.
Search Results
#125 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Externalizing Tacit Craft Knowledge Through Semantic Graphs and Real-Time VR Simulation

mdpi.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The Apprentice Studio system achieves a genuinely novel integration of executable semantic knowledge graphs, offline FEM simulation, and real-time VR rendering in a single craft-education pipeline — no prior system combines all six evaluated capabilities. However, the individual technical components (CIDOC-CRM ontologies, Abaqus FEM, PhysX, Unity PBR, Moodle LMS) are all well-established, making this primarily a systems-integration contribution. The N=12 user study without a control group leaves the central learning-effectiveness claim unvalidated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Traditional craft education depends on prolonged apprenticeship to transmit tacit knowledge — the implicit, embodied understanding of tool forces, material behavior, and procedural decision-making that experts develop over years of practice. Novice learners frequently make errors that waste materials, reduce confidence, and are hard to self-diagnose without expert guidance. Existing digital platforms (YouTube tutorials, Craftsy, Moodle courses) provide passive video instruction but lack physically grounded feedback, error-aware branching, or formal representation of expert knowledge. No prior system combined all of: executable semantic process modelling, physics-based simulation, perceptually accurate rendering, LMS deployment, and quantitative learner assessment in a single architecture targeting traditional crafts. Methodology: The paper proposes a three-layer framework implemented as the "Apprentice Studio" system. The Semantic Layer models craft processes as CIDOC-CRM-aligned Knowledge Graphs (KGs) encoded in RDF/Turtle. Each action node stores typed execution parameters (force ranges in N, tool angle in degrees, contact duration in ms) as RDF data properties queried via SPARQL. Error branches encode recoverable versus irreversible failure conditions (e.g., angular deviation >20° triggers irreversible failure; von Mises strain ≥ ε_max triggers material fracture). The Offline Simulation Layer uses Abaqus/Standard with a quasi-static implicit FEM formulation (J2 isotropic hardening plasticity for metals, orthotropic elasticity for wood) on C3D10 quadratic tetrahedral meshes (element size ≈0.05 mm near contact zone). The full parameter library for the Pointillé use case required ~1800 FEM runs over ~12 hours. Four scalar descriptors (contact stiffness, restitution, effective friction, yield onset displacement) are extracted from FEM output to configure the real-time NVIDIA PhysX layer. The Visualization and Feedback Layer runs in Unity (HDRP/URP), using precomputed FEM deformation proxies looked up by nearest-neighbor interpolation at runtime via a GPU compute shader, adding only 0.4–0.8 ms/frame overhead and maintaining ≥90 FPS on an RTX 3080. PBR material parameters (complex refractive indices from Palik 1985, surface roughness from profilometry, subsurface scattering from Jensen et al. 2001) drive microfacet BRDF shaders. Craft content is delivered through a Moodle-based eLearning portal ("Craeft") that maps KG semantic entities to Moodle instructional components following Cognitive Load Theory principles. The primary use case is Pointillé, a 17th-century metal displacement technique applied to silver, copper, and brass using punches of varying tip geometry. Expert knowledge was elicited through three ethnographic sessions with two professional silversmiths (15 and 22 years of experience). Key Results: A preliminary user study (N=12 graduate students, no prior metalworking experience) evaluated usability, perceived realism, and error awareness. In Study Mode, 74.2% of stippling actions were classified as correct, improving across sessions from 66.3% to 78.7% (+12.4 pp), indicating progressive procedural learning. In Examination Mode (no scaffolding), correct action rate fell to 61.8%, consistent with guidance-fading effects in motor skill literature. Quantitatively, Study Mode angular deviation (5.9°±2.7°) was near the expert threshold (≤5°), while Examination Mode degraded to 8.7°±4.3°; expert reference was 2.1°±0.8°. Force coefficient of variation was 18.3% in Study Mode (below the 25% threshold) vs. 33.1% in Examination Mode. The System Usability Scale score was 81.3 ("Good" category, threshold ≥70). Perceived realism averaged 4.1/5.0 Likert, with 9/12 participants rating material response as realistic or very realistic; correlation between perceived realism and correct surface-deformation recognition was r=0.71 (p<0.05). For error awareness, 10/12 participants correctly identified the cause of ≥80% of their flagged errors in post-session interviews. Expert validators described FEM simulation results as "visually convincing" and "representative of real silver behavior." Comparative analysis against five published systems (Zabulis et al. 2024, OPVSim, Studer et al. CHI 2024, Sobocinski et al. 2023, SimLab VR Studio) shows this is the only system combining all six evaluated capabilities: executable KG semantics, physics simulation, PBR rendering, LMS integration, quantitative multi-metric assessment, and error branching with recovery logic. Strengths: - The semantic-to-simulation pipeline is technically rigorous: action parameters stored as typed RDF properties are automatically translated via SPARQL to Abaqus FEM configurations, creating a reproducible, expert-auditable chain from ethnographic knowledge capture to physically grounded simulation. - The hybrid FEM-offline/PhysX-realtime architecture is a well-engineered solution to the computational cost problem: precomputed deformation libraries with barycentric interpolation and GPU compute shaders achieve ≥90 FPS while maintaining physical fidelity, with a clear path to MOR or GNN surrogate upgrades. - The framework genuinely advances heritage preservation beyond archival documentation toward operational knowledge — executable process schemas can drive simulation, assessment, and adaptive instruction simultaneously, which no prior system achieves in combination. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The user study is far too small (N=12, single session ~45 min, no control group, no pre/post test) to support any claim about learning effectiveness; the paper is transparent about this but the limitation is fundamental — the central claim that the system teaches tacit craft knowledge remains unvalidated against the apprenticeship baseline it is designed to replace. - The framework is explicitly best suited to crafts with well-defined procedural sequences and observable material outcomes; crafts involving high stylistic variability, subjective aesthetic judgment, or complex whole-body motion (weaving, pottery throwing, glass-blowing rhythm) cannot be faithfully captured by the current deterministic KG branching model, limiting generalizability to a narrow subset of heritage crafts. - Haptic feedback is absent, which is a significant fidelity gap for a force-sensitive technique like Pointillé where learners must internalize force magnitude through proprioception; the paper acknowledges this and proposes a theoretical integration architecture but it remains unimplemented and untested.
Search Results
#126 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Customer Segmentation Framework for Enterprise Weibo (Postprint)

L. Turban, J. -Y. Fortin · chinarxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The paper delivers a rare full exact analytical solution for the A+A→A process on the complete graph, with the discovery of a sharp crossover between Gaussian and non-Gaussian extreme-value statistics being a genuinely interesting structural finding. The connection to Jacobi theta functions and KPZ-like distributions adds unexpected depth. However, the fully-connected lattice is the simplest possible geometry, and the results, while mathematically elegant, have limited direct relevance to physically realistic low-dimensional reaction-diffusion systems.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: This paper studies the diffusion-coalescence process (A + A → A) on the fully-connected lattice (complete graph with N sites), an infinite-dimensional system that exhibits mean-field behavior in the thermodynamic limit. While the mean particle density decay x(t) ~ t^{-1} is well-known from mean-field theory, the full statistical distributions — especially for rare, extreme events — have not been characterized exactly. The gap being filled is the derivation of exact probability distributions beyond mean values, particularly for the survival time needed to reach a very small number of remaining particles, where fluctuations are expected to be anomalously large and non-Gaussian. Methodology: The authors formulate the stochastic dynamics via a master equation for S_N(s,k), the probability of s particles surviving after k random sequential updates. They solve the associated eigenvalue problem for the tridiagonal transition matrix analytically, obtaining exact eigenvectors in terms of binomial coefficients and an explicit closed-form expression for S_N(s,k) as a sum over eigenmodes. Moments are extracted from a generating function related to Legendre polynomials. The scaling limit (N,k,s → ∞ at fixed t=k/N and x=s/N) is treated via Euler-Maclaurin summation, yielding Gaussian probability densities for the particle count fluctuations. For the first-passage time T_N(v,k) to reach v survivors, a generating function is constructed as a product of geometric series for sequential coalescence steps, yielding exact means and variances. Two scaling regimes are distinguished: v=O(N) (finite density), where fluctuations are Gaussian and scale as N^{-1/2}, and v=O(1) (vanishing density), where fluctuations are non-Gaussian, scale as N, and the probability density is expressed in closed form via Jacobi theta functions. Asymptotic behaviors in both limits (large and small scaled time θ') are derived using Jacobi's imaginary transformation. Key Results: For particle count statistics: the mean decays as s_N(t)/N → 1/(t+1), recovering the mean-field result exactly, while the variance scales as N·κ(t) with κ(t) = [1-(3t+1)/(t+1)^3] / [3(t+1)], and the distribution is Gaussian in the scaling limit. For first-passage times at finite density x=v/N: the mean is t̄_N(x) = 1/x - 1 (inverse of mean-field), the variance scales as N^{-1}·χ(x) with χ(x) = 2/3 - 1/x + 1/(3x^3), and fluctuations are again Gaussian. For first-passage times to a finite number v=O(1) of survivors: the variance diverges as N^2 (non-self-averaging), and the probability density T'(v,θ') is given by an infinite series involving exponentials e^{-n(n-1)θ'} that can be expressed through derivatives of the Jacobi theta function ϑ_1(z,q). The distribution vanishes at θ'=0 with an essential singularity ~(π/θ')^{2v+3/2} exp(-π^2/4θ') and decays exponentially for large θ'. Numerical simulations with N up to 1024 confirm data collapse onto all analytical predictions. Strengths: - Rare combination of full exact analytical solution (eigenvalues, eigenvectors, generating functions, probability densities) confirmed by clean numerical verification across all regimes. - Discovery of a sharp crossover between Gaussian (v=O(N)) and non-Gaussian extreme-value (v=O(1)) statistics within a single model, with both regimes treated by different but rigorous scaling arguments. - Elegant representation of the v=O(1) first-passage distribution via Jacobi theta functions and differential operator products, revealing an unexpected structural similarity to interface width distributions in Edwards-Wilkinson and KPZ models. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The fully-connected lattice is a mean-field geometry with no spatial structure; the non-Gaussian fluctuations found here arise from combinatorial path multiplicity rather than genuine spatial fluctuation phenomena, limiting direct relevance to low-dimensional reaction-diffusion systems where fluctuations are physically richer. - The paper focuses entirely on single-species coalescence (A+A→A) with unit coalescence probability and a specific fully-occupied initial condition; generalization to partial reaction rates, two-species systems, or other initial conditions is not addressed and would require substantial new analysis. - Density-density correlation functions and aging behavior, which the authors themselves identify as natural extensions, are left entirely for future work, leaving the dynamical picture incomplete.
Search Results 1711.01248 PDF
#127 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Diffusion Model Based on Reverse Guidance of Regional Samples

jair.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The paper's core insight — that intra-class distributional heterogeneity (safe, border, rare, outlier regions) should explicitly guide diffusion-based minority oversampling — is well-motivated and represents a creative synthesis of SMOTE-era region characterization with modern generative modeling. The dynamic guidance schedule and formal proof that the forward process is preserved add methodological rigor. However, the approach heavily combines existing components (DDPM, k-NN region partitioning, classifier-guided diffusion) rather than introducing fundamentally new machinery, and the evaluation scope is narrow.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Class imbalance in tabular datasets causes classifiers to be biased toward majority classes, producing unacceptably high error rates on minority classes — a critical problem in domains like medical diagnosis where minority misclassification carries high cost. Traditional oversampling methods (SMOTE, ADASYN) generate synthetic samples through interpolation constrained by the original data distribution, which becomes problematic in high dimensions and fails to account for the heterogeneous internal structure of the minority class. Most diffusion model work on imbalanced data focuses on image data or treats the minority class as monolithic, ignoring the fact that minority samples can themselves be distributed across qualitatively different subregions (safe, borderline, rare, outlier). This paper addresses the gap by explicitly modeling intra-class distributional heterogeneity to guide minority sample synthesis. Methodology: The paper proposes LReDDPM (Local Regional Samples Guidance Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model), a conditional DDPM for tabular oversampling. The approach proceeds in two stages. First, minority class samples are partitioned into four region types — safe (region 1: 5:0 or 4:1 same-class neighbors among k=5 nearest), border (3:2 or 2:3), rare (1:4), and outlier (0:5) — using the Heterogeneous Value Difference Metric (HVDM) for distance. Second, a classifier trained on the selected regional samples provides gradient signals that steer the reverse diffusion process: at each denoising step, the reverse mean is shifted by τΣ∇log p(y|x_t, s), where s denotes the regional sample set. The gradient scaling factor τ is set dynamically as τ = 60 · 1/√β_t, so guidance strengthens as t→0 (early steps preserve sample quality; later steps inject regional specificity). At the final denoising step, the mean of the regional samples is added to anchor generated samples within the target region. The forward process is unchanged from vanilla DDPM. Categorical variables are embedded following FinDiff; numerical variables are left unembedded for efficiency. The method is evaluated on 10 binary classification datasets (UCI/KEEL) with imbalance ratios from 1.34 to 39.31, comparing AUC, F1-score, and G-mean under decision tree and logistic regression classifiers against ADASYN, BorderlineSMOTE, FinDiff, CTGAN, and TVAE. Key Results: LReDDPM achieves the best AUC on 9/10 datasets under the decision tree classifier (only page0 is suboptimal) and best AUC on most datasets under logistic regression. F1-score is best across all 10 datasets (DT). Qualitatively, generated samples are more concentrated within the guided region and exhibit less overlap with the majority class than competing methods. On distribution fidelity (JS, WD, MMD average ranks across all datasets), LReDDPM ranks best on JS (2.3 vs. next-best FinDiff at 2.7) and WD (3.05 vs. TVAE at 3.25), though TVAE dominates MMD (1.7 vs. LReDDPM's 3.1). Concrete AUC improvements with the DT classifier on the most challenging datasets include: turkish 94.24 vs. baseline 90.70, yeast3 83.0 vs. 75.8, parkin 60.6 vs. 47.3, abalone17 65.7 vs. 61.8. Ablation experiments confirm that: (i) the simplified regional gradient q(y|s) outperforms both q(y|s,x_t) and q(y|x_t) on most datasets; (ii) safety and border region guidance yields the largest classification gains; (iii) static τ values (τ=1 or τ=60) are inferior to the dynamic schedule; (iv) training DDPM directly on regional samples without gradient guidance underperforms LReDDPM on 8/10 datasets. Strengths: - The core insight — that minority class intra-distributional structure matters for synthesis quality — is well-motivated and supported empirically; the four-region taxonomy provides a principled, interpretable partition. - The guidance mechanism is theoretically grounded: the paper formally derives that the proposed conditional distribution q̂ leaves the forward process unchanged (Appendix A), validating that only the reverse process is modified. - The dynamic τ schedule is a practical design that balances sample fidelity (early steps) against regional specificity (late steps), and the ablation over τ settings substantiates its superiority. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The region partitioning relies on k-NN with HVDM, which scales poorly with feature dimensionality; the authors acknowledge O(n·d) cost causes slowdowns on the 752-feature parkin dataset but offer no empirical runtime comparison against baselines. - The choice of which region to use for guidance is dataset-dependent and determined heuristically based on observed region distributions — there is no principled automated selection criterion, limiting practical usability without manual inspection. - Evaluation is restricted to binary classification with only two downstream classifiers (decision tree and logistic regression); generalizability to multi-class settings, more powerful classifiers (gradient boosting, neural networks), and larger-scale datasets remains undemonstrated.
Citations
#128 — Moderately Innovative (57)

EVA: Efficient Reinforcement Learning for End-to-End Video Agent (32 ▲ )

Yaolun Zhang, Ruohui Wang, Jiahao Wang, Yepeng Tang, Xuanyu Zheng, Haonan Duan, Hao Lu, Hanming Deng, Lewei Lu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The planning-before-perception MDP formulation with a 4D action space is a clean generalization over prior video agents, and the three-stage training pipeline (SFT→KTO→GRPO) is well-motivated engineering. However, the empirical gains are modest (1-3% over baselines) and the core components — RL fine-tuning, tool-use agents, iterative reasoning loops — are established techniques assembled in a competent but not surprising configuration.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Long-video understanding with multimodal large language models (MLLMs) faces two compounding problems: (1) uniform frame sampling is token-inefficient and often misses key moments in long videos, and (2) existing agent-based methods still operate perception-first—they feed uniformly sampled frames to the model before any reasoning occurs, then allow only rigid, fixed-parameter tool calls (e.g., select a time range but not resolution). This results in wasted visual tokens, anchoring to irrelevant frames, and failure on videos with sparse or temporally localized relevant content. The gap is an end-to-end trainable agent that decides what, when, and how to watch before committing visual tokens. Methodology: EVA introduces a planning-before-perception paradigm formulated as an MDP. At each step, the agent receives only the text query (no initial frames) and must generate a tool call with four free parameters: start_time, end_time, nframes, and resize (spatial downsampling ratio). This four-dimensional action space subsumes prior agents, which typically only controlled temporal range. The agent reasons in an iterative Summary–Plan–Action–Reflection loop: it summarizes retrieved frames, plans the next action, issues a tool call, then reflects on whether sufficient evidence exists to answer or whether another round is needed. Training uses a three-stage pipeline. Stage 1 (SFT cold-start): a teacher model (Qwen2.5-VL-72B) with a multi-agent pipeline (Executor + Reflective Thinker + Experience Bank) generates ~10k high-quality trajectories covering diverse workflows. Stage 2 (KTO): ~11k trajectories are labeled chosen/rejected to correct systematic failure modes—guessing without evidence, under-sampling long windows, over-sampling short ones—using Kahneman–Tversky Optimization, which requires only single-sample labels (avoiding DPO's pairwise assumption that is misaligned with multi-turn interaction). Stage 3 (GRPO): online RL on ~9.6k open-ended + ~1.1k MCQ pairs using a composite reward: ROUGE for open-ended, Completeness Self-Verification (CSV) for MCQ (both the agent and a judge must agree on the correct answer), plus a small format reward. A data-enhanced GRPO loop collects failure cases and uses the teacher to generate new QA pairs for unseen videos (HD-VILA), addressing static dataset limitations. The base model is Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, trained on 32 H100 GPUs. Key Results: On LSDBench (Sampling Dilemma Bench), EVA achieves 51.8% accuracy using only ~6.2K visual tokens, vs. Qwen2.5-VL at 50.1% with 166K tokens—a 2.6% gain at roughly 27× fewer tokens. On four long-video benchmarks, EVA-GRPO scores: LongVideoBench 55.0%, MLVU 68.3%, VideoMME-Long 48.4%/Overall 60.2%, LVBench 43.3%, outperforming the best adaptive agent baseline (FrameThinker) by ~1–3% while using fewer estimated frames (~22–26 vs. 21–24). On zero-shot Video-Holmes reasoning, EVA-GRPO achieves 37.2%, exceeding Video-R1 (36.5%) and VideoChat-R1 (33.0%). On ELV-Halluc, EVA (7B) achieves 26.2% overall accuracy—comparable to InternVL3-38B (26.1%)—and reduces SAH-ratio from 8.8% to 5.0% vs. the Qwen2.5-VL-7B baseline. Ablations confirm the progressive value of each stage: SFT alone is the weakest, KTO significantly improves strategy selection, and GRPO achieves the highest scores by learning deliberate multi-round reasoning with fewer but better-targeted tokens. Mixed open-ended + MCQ data in GRPO is critical for preventing reward hacking. Strengths: - The planning-before-perception MDP formulation is clean and general: by exposing spatial resize as a first-class action parameter alongside temporal controls, EVA's action space strictly subsumes all prior video agents, making it a genuine generalization rather than an incremental variant. - The KTO stage is a well-motivated design choice for multi-turn agents: unlike DPO, it handles single-sample labels and avoids the shared-dialogue-round assumption that would truncate multi-turn strategies; this is a concrete engineering insight, not just borrowing an off-the-shelf method. - The data-enhanced GRPO loop (collecting failures → generating new QA pairs via teacher for unseen videos) addresses a real limitation of static RL training and provides curriculum-like difficulty growth without manual curation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The EVA frame count in Table 2 is estimated by assuming 650 tokens/frame for fair comparison, but in practice EVA uses adaptive resolutions, making token-efficiency comparisons imprecise—the paper acknowledges this but it undermines the headline efficiency claims. - The reward signals are weak proxies for video understanding quality: ROUGE is a poor metric for open-ended factual QA and the CSV reward depends on having a separate judge model agree, which creates a circular dependency and may propagate systematic errors of the judge. - Generalization to truly novel query distributions or unseen tool interfaces is untested; the conclusion acknowledges the system may fail on noisy or out-of-distribution queries, and the tool schema is pre-defined and fixed during inference.
Recommended 2603.22918 PDF
#129 — Moderately Innovative (56)

SAR: A Structure-Aligned Reasoning Framework for Temporal Knowledge Graph Question Answering

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: SAR correctly identifies structural misalignment as a concrete failure mode in temporal KGQA and addresses it with a sensible four-step retrieval pipeline preserving quadruple schema throughout. The gains on multi-constraint questions are substantial. However, the individual components (ReAct reasoning, SBERT retrieval, LLM verification) are largely assembled from existing techniques, and evaluation on only 560 sampled questions limits confidence.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Temporal Knowledge Graph Question Answering (TKGQA) requires answering questions grounded in time-stamped quadruples (head, relation, tail, timestamp). Existing LLM-augmented RAG approaches treat KG facts as unstructured text, causing structural misalignment: free-text queries retrieve semantically similar but structurally incorrect facts — e.g., retrieving a nomination fact instead of a win fact when asking who won an award. This problem is especially acute for multi-hop queries with multiple temporal constraints, where the LLM reasoning chain fails to enforce strict temporal logic. Methodology: SAR (Structure-Aligned Reasoning) is an iterative agent-based framework with three modules. First, a ReAct-inspired Reasoning and Answer Generation module decomposes the query through thought–action–observation cycles, issuing structured SEARCH sub-queries with explicit temporal constraint types (BEFORE, AFTER, IN, ANYTIME). Second, the Structure-Aligned Evidence Retrieval module processes each sub-query through four steps: (1) temporal filtering to narrow the KG subgraph by timestamp, (2) LLM-based structured query decomposition into (head, relation, tail) triples mirroring the KG schema, (3) SBERT embedding-based retrieval scoring facts via component-wise cosine similarity across all three triple components, and (4) chronological re-ranking of the top-K retrieved facts. Third, an Iterative Answer Verification module uses an LLM critic to check answer type alignment and temporal consistency, triggering up to N=2 refinement iterations when the candidate fails. The key departure from prior work is that queries are never flattened to unstructured text — the quadruple schema is preserved throughout. Key Results: On MultiTQ (560 sampled questions), SAR with GPT-4.1 achieves 78.2% Hits@1, surpassing TempAgent (60.7%) by 17.5 points and ReAct RAG (43.4%) by 34.8 points. The advantage is most pronounced on multiple-constraint questions: SAR reaches 50.0% vs. TempAgent's 28.6% — a 21-point gap. On CronQuestions, SAR achieves 91.7% overall (vs. TempAgent's 87.5%). Even with the open-source Llama3-70B backbone, SAR (65.0%) outperforms TempAgent with the same backbone (54.3%). Ablations show structured query decomposition accounts for the largest gain (+7.1 points overall, +12 on complex queries), while iterative verification contributes an additional +1.2 points. Optimal K=15 retrieved facts; beyond K=30 performance degrades slightly. Strengths: - Identifies and directly targets a concrete, well-motivated failure mode (structural misalignment) rather than adding generic reasoning capacity, which explains the large performance gains on hard multi-constraint questions. - The four-step retrieval pipeline (filter → decompose → embed → rerank) is modular and each step has clear ablation justification; the component-wise SBERT scoring over (h,r,t) is a simple but effective design choice that outperforms flat-text search. - Evaluation is thorough: multiple backbone LLMs, two benchmarks, breakdown by question complexity and answer type, ablations of both main modules, and hyperparameter sensitivity analysis. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The test set for MultiTQ is only 560 questions randomly sampled, not the full benchmark, which limits statistical confidence and reproducibility; no evaluation on other TKGQA datasets (e.g., TimeQuestions, ICEWS) is provided. - The structured query decomposition step itself relies on an LLM call to parse sub-queries into (h, r, t) triples — errors here propagate downstream, but the paper provides no analysis of decomposition failure rates or robustness under noisy sub-queries. - The framework is evaluated only in a closed KG setting where all relevant facts are assumed present; applicability to open-domain temporal QA where the KG may be incomplete is not addressed.
Search Results
#130 — Moderately Innovative (56)

CasMoE: A Cascaded Framework for Efficient MoE Inference on Resource-constrained Devices

ojs.aaai.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: CasMoE's prompt-level, all-layers-at-once expert prediction is a meaningful departure from prior layer-wise offloading approaches, eliminating pipeline bubbles by pre-loading experts before decoding begins. The cascaded EAM+EAP design with confidence-based gating is principled. However, evaluation on only Mixtral-8×7B and the substantial MMLU accuracy drop (57.2%) weaken the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs such as Mixtral-8×7B require ~87GB VRAM at inference time, yet only ~24GB of weights (dense components plus activated experts) are actually used per token due to sparse gating. This mismatch makes MoE inference intractable on consumer or even high-end GPU hardware. Existing remedies are unsatisfying: model compression sacrifices accuracy; on-demand expert offloading (CPU→GPU per layer) incurs 40.8% of total inference time in transfer overhead; SE-MoE's eager prefetch of all next-layer experts wastes memory and bandwidth; and prior cross-layer prediction methods (Pregated-MoE, AdapMoE, MoE-Infinity) struggle to balance prediction accuracy against retrieval speed, yielding suboptimal downstream task performance. The key gap is the absence of a mechanism that can predict expert activations for all MoE layers simultaneously before decoding begins, thereby enabling full pipeline overlap between data transfer and computation. Methodology: CasMoE introduces a two-stage offline–online cascaded framework centered on a standalone Cascade Planner that operates independently of the MoE model. In the offline stage, a calibration dataset is run through the frozen MoE to collect expert activation patterns. The pattern label EP is defined as the sum of router scores α weighted by an indicator of each expert's participation across all tokens in a prompt, encoding both selection frequency and token-decoding significance. Two complementary components are built from these patterns: (1) an Expert Activation Matcher (EAM), a non-parametric HNSW index mapping sentence-transformer prompt embeddings to stored EP matrices, enabling sub-millisecond approximate nearest-neighbor retrieval with a fused average over top-k′ retrieved patterns; and (2) an Expert Activation Predictor (EAP), a six-layer TinyBERT encoder with L per-layer MLP regression heads trained end-to-end via a combined SimCSE contrastive loss (for robust embeddings) and Huber regression loss against the EP labels. In the online stage, for each incoming prompt the planner computes its embedding, consults the EAM, and uses the resulting cosine similarity as a confidence score: if it exceeds threshold ε the EAM prediction is used directly (1.22 ms); otherwise the EAP is invoked (10.73 ms). Crucially, this single-pass prediction covers all MoE layers before any decoding begins, allowing the system to pre-load exactly the selected k experts per layer and eliminate layer-wise pipeline stalls. A buffer of size C=30 continuously refreshes the EAM pattern database with new prompt–pattern pairs. Key Results: On GLUE (8 subtasks), CasMoE achieves an average accuracy of 0.71 vs. 0.73 for the all-GPU upper bound, outperforming Mixtral-Offloading (0.59) and AdapMoE (0.64). Throughput improvements over MoE-OnDemand are 2× and over SE-MoE are 10×, exceeding 3.0 tokens/s across nearly all GLUE subsets. On WMT16 translation (a long-sequence task), CasMoE scores 34.15 SacreBLEU (80% of the 43.37 upper bound), substantially better than Mixtral-Offloading (27.72, 64%) and AdapMoE (25.02, 58%). On MMLU, CasMoE reaches 0.572 accuracy (72% preservation) versus 0.626 for Mixtral-Offloading. The headline claim is 65.13% throughput improvement with >96.6% performance preservation relative to the on-demand offloading baseline. Ablation confirms that the custom EP label design outperforms a simple count-based softmax baseline (0.72 vs. 0.62 on CoLA), and combining EAP+EAM achieves higher throughput (3.074) than EAP alone (2.971) or all-in-GPU (2.963) by bypassing BERT computation on high-confidence prompts. Strengths: - Prompt-level, all-layers-at-once prediction is a meaningful architectural advance over layer-wise approaches; it eliminates pipeline bubbles and enables full pre-loading before decoding. - The cascaded EAM+EAP design with confidence-based gating is principled: fast retrieval for seen prompt types, parametric prediction as fallback, with a transparent hyperparameter ε controlling the tradeoff. - The novel EP label (router-score-weighted frequency) is empirically justified and smoother than count-based alternatives, making regression tractable. - Online EAM updates via a rolling buffer enable continual adaptation to new prompt distributions without retraining. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to a single MoE model (Mixtral-8×7B); it is unclear whether the approach generalizes to architectures with different expert counts, layer structures, or routing mechanisms (e.g., DeepSeek-V3, Switch Transformer). - MMLU accuracy drops to 57.2% of the full-GPU baseline, which is a substantial degradation for knowledge-intensive tasks; the paper does not adequately diagnose whether this stems from expert mis-prediction or quantization artifacts. - The offline calibration stage introduces a distribution dependency: expert patterns learned on calibration data may generalize poorly to out-of-distribution prompts, and no analysis of calibration set size, composition, or transfer is provided. - The gating threshold ε is a free hyperparameter whose sensitivity and optimal setting are not systematically analyzed across tasks or domains.
Search Results
#131 — Moderately Innovative (56)

Adaptive and Context-rich Generative Self-supervised Learning on Graphs

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: ACE-GSL systematically identifies four complementary limitations in graph masked autoencoders and addresses each with a dedicated module. The breadth of evaluation across 20 datasets and 28 baselines is impressive. However, the framework is a kitchen-sink composition of known techniques (REINFORCE masking, momentum encoders, self-distillation, ranking losses) rather than a single unifying insight, and the added complexity is substantial.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Generative self-supervised learning on graphs (via masked autoencoders) has become a competitive alternative to contrastive methods, but existing approaches suffer from four concrete limitations: (1) random masking treats all nodes as equally informative, ignoring heterogeneous node significance; (2) reconstruction objectives target either edges or features in isolation, missing holistic graph knowledge; (3) training operates purely in the output space via reconstruction loss, ignoring high-level semantic structure in the representation space; and (4) masking large fractions of the input introduces variance that destabilizes reconstructions. These gaps leave state-of-the-art methods such as GraphMAE2 and S2GAE underperforming their potential. Methodology: ACE-GSL is a generative graph SSL framework built around four components. First, an adaptive feature mask generator uses a multi-head attention network followed by a feed-forward layer to compute per-node probability scores, then samples masked nodes preferentially from those with high reconstruction error. Because sampling is non-differentiable, a REINFORCE-based policy gradient optimizes the generator by maximizing expected reconstruction error. Second, a ranking-based structure reconstruction module randomly masks edges (Bernoulli sampling) and defines a margin-based ranking loss that encourages the inner-product similarity between connected node pairs to exceed that of disconnected pairs — replacing binary edge classification with a softer relational objective. Third, a bootstrapping-based similarity module maintains a momentum encoder (EMA of the online encoder) to produce stable target representations; the online encoder is trained to predict these momentum targets across the two masking views (feature-masked and structure-masked), introducing high-level semantic supervision complementary to the output-space reconstruction. Fourth, a consistency assurance module employs a momentum decoder (teacher) whose EMA reconstructions serve as pseudo-targets for the online decoder (student), minimizing a scaled cosine error; this self-distillation stabilizes learning when large mask rates create noisy reconstruction targets. The total loss combines feature reconstruction, structure reconstruction, bootstrapping similarity, and consistency assurance losses. Encoder/decoder backbones are task-specific: GAT for node classification, GIN for graph classification. Key Results: ACE-GSL is evaluated on 20 datasets across node classification, graph classification, and transfer learning, against 28 baselines. On node classification: Cora 85.4% (+1.0 over GraphMAE2), CiteSeer 74.6% (+1.2), PubMed 82.6% (+1.2), Ogbn-arxiv 72.43% (+1.72 over GraphMAE2), PPI 75.43% (competitive with supervised GAT 97.3% on PPI is a Micro-F1 task where DGI scored 63.8%). On graph classification: IMDB-M 53.13%, IMDB-B 76.82%, PROTEINS 77.26%, COLLAB 82.04%, REDDIT-B 89.92% — best or tied-best across 6 of 7 datasets. On transfer learning (ROC-AUC averaged over 8 molecular datasets): ACE-GSL scores 76.6 vs. best baseline S2GAE at 74.1, with consistent per-dataset improvements across all 8 benchmarks. Ablation confirms each of the four components contributes positively; removing any single component degrades performance on both Cora and PubMed. Strengths: - Identifies and addresses a coherent set of complementary limitations in graph masked autoencoders, with each component targeting a distinct failure mode rather than stacking redundant inductive biases. - REINFORCE-based adaptive masking is a principled solution to the non-differentiability of discrete node sampling, and the empirical gains validate that importance-weighted masking is beneficial over uniform random masking. - Comprehensive evaluation (20 datasets, 3 task types, 28 baselines) with consistent SOTA or near-SOTA results demonstrates broad applicability across transductive, inductive, and transfer learning settings. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework introduces substantial complexity — four additional modules, two momentum networks (encoder and decoder), and multiple loss terms — making the method harder to tune and significantly increasing computational cost relative to GraphMAE or S2GAE; the paper does not report training time or parameter count comparisons. - The REINFORCE estimator for the mask generator is known to have high variance; the paper does not analyze variance reduction techniques or convergence behavior of the policy, leaving open whether this is robust across graph types with different feature distributions. - Results on MUTAG (small dataset, ~188 graphs) show the method does not achieve the best performance, attributed to data insufficiency, but this reveals a potential sensitivity to graph dataset size that is not systematically characterized.
Search Results
#132 — Moderately Innovative (56)

Representation Alignment for Just Image Transformers is not Easier than You Think (5 ▲ )

Jaeyo Shin, Jiwook Kim, Hyunjung Shim · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The paper delivers a sharp diagnosis of why REPA fails in pixel space (information asymmetry between pixel-level representations and compressed semantic targets) and proposes a clean fix via masked transformer adapter that acts as an information bottleneck on the alignment branch only. Results are consistent across model scales. However, the contribution is narrow—limited to class-conditional ImageNet 256×256—and pixel-space diffusion still meaningfully trails leading latent-space methods despite the improved training efficiency.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Representation Alignment (REPA) has proven highly effective for accelerating training of latent-space Diffusion Transformers (DiT/SiT) by aligning intermediate activations to features from frozen semantic encoders like DINOv2. Pixel-space diffusion models such as Just image Transformers (JiT) are attractive because they avoid the reconstruction bottleneck imposed by pretrained tokenizers, but are expensive to train. Applying REPA to JiT is a natural next step — yet this paper shows it backfires: JiT+REPA yields worse FID than vanilla JiT as training progresses at 256×256 resolution. The paper diagnoses why and proposes a fix. Methodology: The authors identify the root cause as an information asymmetry: in latent diffusion both the denoising space and the semantic encoder have undergone perceptual compression, so their degrees of freedom are roughly matched and direct feature regression is well-posed. In pixel space the denoiser operates over O(H×W) dimensions while the semantic encoder produces a compact bottleneck representation, causing many pixel-distinct images to collapse to the same encoder region — a failure mode the authors call feature hacking. They confirm this empirically by showing REPA hurts JiT at 256×256 but helps at 32×32, and that degradation is concentrated on images tightly clustered in semantic feature space yet visually diverse in pixel space. To address this, the authors propose PixelREPA, which replaces REPA's pointwise MLP projection head with a Masked Transformer Adapter (MTA). The MTA consists of (1) a 2-block shallow Transformer adapter that performs contextual aggregation via self-attention before projecting JiT intermediate features toward the semantic target, and (2) a partial token masking strategy (default ratio r=0.2) applied to the adapter input. Masking serves two roles: it breaks trivial per-token regression shortcuts, and acts as an information bottleneck on the pixel side, narrowing the dimensionality gap between pixel representations and the compressed semantic target. Critically, MTA is attached only to the alignment branch and is discarded at inference, adding no sampling cost. The final training objective is L_JiT + λ·L_PixelREPA where the alignment loss is cosine similarity between frozen DINOv2 features and adapter outputs over masked token subsets. Key Results: On ImageNet 256×256 class-conditional generation, PixelREPA consistently outperforms vanilla JiT across all model scales: B/16 FID 3.66→3.17 (−13.4%), IS 275.1→284.6; L/16 FID 2.36→2.11 (−10.6%), IS 298.5→309.5; H/16 FID 1.86→1.81 (−2.7%), IS 303.4→317.2. Notably, PixelREPA-H/16 (953M params) surpasses JiT-G/16 (2B params, FID 1.82) with roughly half the parameters. Convergence is over 2× faster than vanilla JiT-B/16. In comparison, naive JiT+REPA at 200 epochs degrades FID to 5.14 versus JiT's 4.37 — a 17.6% regression — while PixelREPA achieves 4.00 at the same checkpoint. Ablations confirm both components are necessary: adapter-only without masking gives FID 4.68 (better than REPA but worse than baseline); adding masking at r=0.2 yields 4.00. Mask ratios above 0.3–0.5 degrade performance due to excessive gradient blocking. Strengths: - Sharp, well-motivated diagnosis of REPA's failure mode in pixel space, backed by controlled experiments (resolution sweep, most/least similar subset analysis with t-SNE visualization) that cleanly isolate the information asymmetry hypothesis. - The MTA design is elegant: it inserts an information bottleneck selectively on the alignment branch without touching the main denoising pathway, preserving full pixel-level fidelity at inference with zero additional cost. - Results are consistent and scalable across B/L/H model sizes, and PixelREPA-H/16 achieves competitive FID against recent pixel-space models (SiD2 UViT/2 at 1.73, PixelFlow at 1.98) while being architecturally simpler. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are limited to class-conditional ImageNet 256×256; generalization to text-to-image, higher resolutions (512×512+), or other pixel-space backbones is not demonstrated. - The ablation of mask ratio is only evaluated at 200 epochs on B/16; sensitivity at other model scales or longer training is unexplored, and the interaction between mask ratio and model capacity remains unclear. - PixelREPA-H/16 still trails the best pixel-space methods (SiD2 UViT/1 FID 1.38) by a meaningful margin, and the gap to leading latent-space models (DDT-XL/2 FID 1.26, RAE FID 1.13) suggests pixel-space diffusion has not yet closed the quality gap despite the improved training efficiency.
Recommended 2603.14366 PDF
#133 — Moderately Innovative (56)

I/O for LLM Inference: A Survey of Storage and Memory Bottlenecks

researchsquare.com · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The unified I/O decomposition into W/K/A flows with roofline grounding is a genuinely useful organizational lens, and the composability waterfall analysis showing predictable bottleneck oscillation is the paper's strongest contribution. However, as a survey with entirely theoretical estimates and no empirical validation, it contributes a framework for reasoning rather than new techniques or surprising findings.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The dominant bottleneck for large language model (LLM) inference has shifted from computation to data movement. Autoregressive decode runs at arithmetic intensity I ≈ 1 FLOP/byte—approximately 300× below the H100's ridge point of I ≈ 295—meaning GPU compute is almost entirely idle waiting for bytes from HBM. GPU compute has scaled ~18× from V100 to B200, while HBM bandwidth has improved only ~9×, widening the structural imbalance. At scale, inference cost dwarfs training cost, and a 32-bit DRAM read costs ~640 pJ versus ~3.7 pJ for a multiply-accumulate, so data movement also dominates energy. Existing surveys organize optimizations by algorithmic category (quantization, attention, KV cache) without a unifying lens explaining why each technique helps or when* it stops helping. This paper fills that gap. Methodology: The paper proposes a unified I/O-centric framework decomposing every decode step into three flows: weight I/O (W, ~141 GB per step for Llama-3 70B FP16), KV cache I/O (K, grows as O(B·s)), and activation I/O (A, typically small at decode). Each flow is mapped against the roofline model to identify its binding memory-hierarchy tier. The framework then systematically covers every major optimization class—weight quantization (GPTQ, AWQ, INT4, FP8), structured sparsity (2:4), speculative decoding, MoE, FlashAttention, PagedAttention, KV quantization (KIVI, KVQuant), eviction (H2O, StreamingLLM), KV offloading, disaggregated prefill-decode, continuous batching, prefix caching, and hardware trends (HBM evolution, CXL, PIM, UMA)—mapping each to the specific flow and hierarchy level it targets. A composability analysis (waterfall table) traces the dominant bottleneck as optimizations are stacked on Llama-3 70B at B=32, s=4096. A crossover batch size B* = P·b_w / (2·L·s·n_kv·d_h·b_kv) is derived analytically, pinpointing when to prioritize weight vs. KV compression. A companion toolkit computing I/O profiles from HuggingFace configs is released. Key Results: The composability analysis reveals that stacking INT4 weights (AWQ), INT4 KV (KIVI), 2:4 sparsity, and speculative decoding (γ=5, α=0.8) yields ~12× cumulative I/O reduction: total per-step data movement drops from 183 GB to ~15 GB, and estimated TPOT improves from 68 ms to 5.6 ms at B=32 on H100. Strikingly, the dominant bottleneck oscillates—FP16 baseline is W-dominated; adding INT4 weights flips dominance to K; adding INT4 KV cache flips it back to W; 2:4 sparsity keeps it W with less margin; speculative decoding flips it back to K. With the full stack, the verification pass achieves I ≈ 403, exceeding the H100's FP16 ridge point and transitioning from memory-bound to compute-bound—a validation that aggressive I/O optimization does close the bandwidth gap. For UMA systems (Apple M4 Max, I ≈ 49), hardware utilization at B=8 is ~16% vs. ~2.7% on H100 for the same batch—lower absolute performance but far better matched to decode's arithmetic intensity. The paper also quantifies the crossover batch size under various quantization regimes: for Llama-3 70B, B ≈ 108 (FP16/FP16), drops to B ≈ 27 with INT4 weights only, rises back to B ≈ 106 with symmetric INT4/INT4, and falls to B* ≈ 53 when adding 2:4 sparsity. Strengths: - The unified I/O decomposition into three flows (W, K, A) with roofline grounding provides a genuinely novel and explanatory taxonomy that prior surveys lack; it clarifies why superficially different techniques (quantization, speculative decoding, batching) share a common roofline mechanism. - The composability / waterfall analysis is the most concrete contribution: it shows that bottleneck oscillation is predictable and quantifiable, enabling deployment-time reasoning about which optimization to apply next, rather than applying all techniques blindly. - The crossover batch size B* derivation is clean, analytically tractable, and directly actionable for practitioners—a rare combination in survey papers. - Coverage is comprehensive and technically precise, with detailed numerical tables (I/O profiles for 20+ models, hardware specs across four GPU generations, offloading latency comparisons) that can serve as reference. - The treatment of UMA architectures (Apple M-series) as a distinct inference frontier with different optimization targets is insightful and underrepresented in prior work. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The analysis is almost entirely theoretical / back-of-envelope; no end-to-end inference benchmarks are reported, so it is unclear how closely the TPOT estimates in Table 7 match wall-clock measurements on actual frameworks (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM). The paper acknowledges roofline estimates are within 10–15% for memory-bound decode kernels but provides no empirical validation of the composability table itself. - The speculative decoding row in Table 7 uses an optimistic acceptance rate (α=0.8) that the paper itself notes is task-dependent and degrades for larger γ or diverse workloads; the resulting TPOT estimate (5.6 ms) may be significantly optimistic in practice, yet it is presented as the headline result of the composability section. - The survey omits training from its scope by design, but several inference-relevant topics receive shallow treatment: multi-modal KV I/O (only raised as an open problem at the end), structured generation constraints on KV management, and the interaction between chunked prefill chunk size and quality are noted but not analyzed. - Open challenges (Section 9) are framed qualitatively without quantitative targets or clear evaluation criteria, making them aspirational rather than actionable research questions with falsifiable hypotheses.
Citations
#134 — Moderately Innovative (56)

MARS: Multimodal Adaptive Reasoning Model for Avoiding Overthinking

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: MARS contributes a principled three-stage curriculum and the IB-GRPO algorithm for difficulty-adaptive reasoning compression, achieving a striking 90% token reduction. However, the accuracy improvements are marginal (+0.54%), making this primarily an efficiency contribution rather than a reasoning advance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) that use Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning suffer from "overthinking" — generating excessively long, redundant reasoning chains regardless of question difficulty, leading to high computational costs and inference latency. Prior work on CoT compression focuses mainly on unimodal (text-only) tasks, ignores question difficulty as a signal for adaptive reasoning, and often degrades accuracy when compressing CoT. The gap is the absence of a multimodal model that can dynamically calibrate reasoning depth — skipping it for easy questions and applying full CoT only when warranted. Methodology: MARS introduces a three-stage training framework built on top of Mimo-VL-7B-RL as the backbone. Stage 1 (CoT Masking Learning) randomly masks one reasoning step from a CoT chain and trains the model to reconstruct it from context, improving logical coherence and reducing errors that trigger overthinking loops. Stage 2 (Adaptive Reasoning Instruction Learning) fine-tunes the model to first predict question difficulty (easy/medium/hard) and then conditionally generate CoT: null for easy, step-by-step for medium/hard. Stage 3 (CoT Lightweight Reinforcement Learning) applies a novel IB-GRPO algorithm — GRPO augmented with an Information Bottleneck regularizer that penalizes token-level output entropy and rewards peaked distributions, encouraging concise outputs. The reward combines relaxed accuracy with a difficulty-conditioned ReLU length penalty (different λ and threshold T per difficulty tier). Training data comes from the newly constructed MART-Dataset: 38,148 human-annotated samples across 18 domains and 105 sub-domains drawn from 21 public multimodal QA datasets, with difficulty labels determined by majority vote from two annotators plus a tie-breaker. Key Results: On the out-of-domain SciChart benchmark, MARS (7B) achieves R-Acc of 42.31%, surpassing the backbone Mimo-VL-7B-RL (41.77%), GPT-4o (41.79%), and Claude-3.7-Sonnet-Thinking (38.81%), while reducing average reasoning tokens from 980.09 to 95.81 — a 90.22% reduction. Per-question-type breakdown shows the model correctly skips thinking for easy questions (Peak Number: +1.87% accuracy, −87.27% tokens; Shape: +1.94% accuracy, −67.47% tokens), maintains accuracy on medium questions (Peak Value: +0.71% accuracy, −92.72% tokens), and incurs only a modest accuracy drop on the hardest questions (FWHM: −1.56% accuracy, −79.67% tokens). On the in-domain M3CoT dataset, MARS-7B achieves 62.55% accuracy, outperforming LLaVA-V1.5-13B (59.50%) and all smaller specialized models. Ablation confirms each stage contributes: removing Stage 1 drops R-Acc to 38.23%; replacing IB-GRPO with standard GRPO keeps average tokens at 256.68 vs. 95.81 with IB-GRPO. Under max-thinking-length constraints (0.5k tokens), MARS degrades by only 1.35% versus 8.34% for Mimo-VL-RL. Strengths: - The three-stage curriculum (masking → instruction tuning → RL) is well-motivated and each stage is empirically validated via ablation, with clear additive contributions. - IB-GRPO provides a principled mechanism — not just a length penalty — for compressing CoT by minimizing distributional entropy, achieving a further 2.6× token reduction over standard GRPO at comparable or better accuracy. - MART-Dataset is a meaningful contribution: 38k samples with human-annotated difficulty labels across 18 domains is non-trivial and directly enables the difficulty-conditioned training. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The accuracy improvements are marginal (0.54% overall R-Acc gain over baseline), making the primary contribution efficiency rather than task performance; the practical value depends heavily on deployment contexts where token cost matters. - Evaluation is limited to two datasets (M3CoT and SciChart), both chart/science-focused; generalizability to broader multimodal reasoning tasks (visual math, embodied QA, open-ended generation) is undemonstrated. - Difficulty labels are human-annotated on the training set, but difficulty classification at inference is done by the model itself — there is no analysis of how often the model miscategorizes difficulty and what the downstream accuracy/efficiency impact of such errors is.
Search Results
#135 — Moderately Innovative (56)

FDC-Ground: Improving GRPO for GUI Grounding via Exponential Rewards and Fact-Aligned Pruning

ojs.aaai.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: FDC-Ground makes a clean technical contribution by identifying and fixing a genuine failure mode of continuous rewards within GRPO — out-of-box predictions receiving positive advantages due to group normalization — and the resolution-invariant EDDR reward is a practical improvement over prior squared-decay designs. Data efficiency (3.2K samples, 1 epoch) is impressive. However, the scope is narrow (GUI grounding only), the semi-binary +0.5 bonus reintroduces manual tuning, and the reward saturation issue remains unresolved.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: GUI grounding — localizing screen elements from natural language instructions — is a core capability for building GUI agents across mobile, desktop, and web platforms. Existing supervised fine-tuning (SFT) approaches require large-scale annotated datasets and generalize poorly. Reinforcement learning alternatives using GRPO are promising but suffer from two compounding problems: (1) binary reward functions produce sparse or vanishing gradients when all sampled predictions are incorrect, and (2) GRPO's requirement for multiple full forward passes per prompt makes broad exploration computationally prohibitive at high resolutions. Prior continuous reward designs (squared decay) are also resolution-sensitive, producing wildly different reward values for the same absolute prediction error depending on screen size. Methodology: FDC-Ground combines two components atop a GRPO training loop using Qwen2.5-VL (3B and 7B) backbones. First, the Exponentially Decayed Distance Reward (EDDR) replaces binary and squared-decay rewards with R(p,g) = (1 − d/d_max) · e^(−k·d) + 0.5 if predicted point p falls inside bounding box g, and without the +0.5 bonus otherwise. Here d is Euclidean distance to the box center and d_max is the image diagonal, making the reward scale-invariant across resolutions. A bonus of +0.5 for in-box predictions provides a discrete signal on top of the continuous spatial gradient. Second, Fact-Aligned Dynamic Completions Pruning (FDC-Pruning) addresses a pathology introduced by continuous rewards: incorrect predictions (outside the box) can still receive positive advantages due to group-relative normalization, producing misguided gradient updates. FDC-Pruning filters the candidate completions to retain only those whose advantage sign aligns with factual correctness — positive advantages for in-box predictions and negative advantages for out-of-box predictions — then selects the top-m by absolute advantage magnitude. Candidate generation is dynamic, continuing until at least one correct answer is found or the maximum is reached, enabling exploration space expansion without proportional compute cost. Training uses token-level DAPO-style loss, DeepSpeed ZeRO-3, FlashAttention-2, and only 3.2K samples for a single epoch on 8×A800 GPUs. Key Results: FDC-Ground-7B achieves 88.3% on ScreenSpot and 91.0% on ScreenSpot-v2, outperforming UIShift-7B (87.8%/90.3%, 8 epochs) and SE-GUI-7B (88.2%/90.3%, 10 epochs) with only 1 epoch of training. The 3B model reaches 82.1%/85.5%, surpassing SFT models up to 18B parameters (CogAgent: 47.4%, SeeClick-9.6B: 53.4%) and approaching Aguvis-7B (84.4%). The 3B model improves +26.6% over the Qwen2.5-VL-3B base on ScreenSpot and +38.6% on ScreenSpot-v2. Ablations show EDDR alone over binary reward improves from 74.4% to 85.9%; adding FDC-Pruning pushes to 88.1% (+2.6% over standard GRPO). FDC-Pruning with g=4, m=2 yields 1.18× training speedup and +5.9% accuracy over standard GRPO; expanding to g=16 gives an additional +10.5%. On AndroidControl (500 steps), FDC-Ground-7B outperforms UGround-V1-7B in both high-level (50.6 vs. 49.8) and low-level (67.0 vs. 66.2) scenarios. Strengths: - The EDDR reward is resolution-invariant by construction (normalizing by image diagonal), directly solving a practical problem that prior squared-decay rewards have, with empirical validation showing reward stability across d_max ∈ {1000, 2000, 3000}. - FDC-Pruning addresses a genuine and non-obvious failure mode of applying continuous rewards within GRPO: out-of-box predictions receiving positive advantages due to group normalization, which would produce misguided gradient updates without the fact-alignment filter. - Remarkable data efficiency: competitive or superior results to models trained on 32K samples (InfGUI-R1) using only 3.2K samples and 1 epoch, with clear ablations isolating the contribution of each component. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The +0.5 in-box bonus is a manually tuned hyperparameter that effectively re-introduces a semi-binary component; its interaction with group normalization and sensitivity to tuning is not analyzed. - Reward saturation after the first half of an epoch is acknowledged but unresolved — entropy collapse is cited as a likely cause, but no fix is demonstrated, limiting the method's utility for longer training regimes or harder tasks. - Experiments are confined to moderate-resolution screens due to hardware constraints, and the paper acknowledges high-resolution GUI environments remain an open problem despite resolution-robustness being a primary motivation.
Citations
#136 — Moderately Innovative (55)

DREAM: Dynamic Prompts and GuidedMix for Efficient Continual Adaptation of Visual-Language Models

openaccess.thecvf.com · Recommended articles
Why this rank: DREAM's dual use of GuidedMix as augmentation, text-alignment vehicle, and OOD threshold calibrator is an elegant design choice, and the input-conditioned dynamic prompts are a meaningful step beyond static task-level prompts. However, the gains over MoE-Adapter4CL are modest (1-2.6 pp), evaluation is limited to a single architecture and task-incremental protocol, and dataset-specific hyperparameter tuning suggests some brittleness.
Show full summary & analysis
