Problem & Motivation: Vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP exhibit strong zero-shot transfer but are static — they cannot incrementally adapt when presented with new tasks without catastrophic forgetting. Conventional continual learning methods address forgetting but ignore the equally critical need to preserve zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks. Prior VLM-specific methods either rely on large external reference datasets throughout training (ZSCL) or require training separate task-specific autoencoders for task identification (MoE-Adapter4CL), making them data-hungry and parameter-heavy. The gap is a lightweight, data-efficient framework that jointly achieves task adaptation, forgetting mitigation, and zero-shot preservation — including under limited annotation. Methodology: DREAM builds on a frozen CLIP (ViT-B/16) backbone and introduces two main components. First, a dynamic prompt system consisting of (a) a prompt composition module that learns R=20 prompt embeddings per task per MSA layer and computes attention-weighted combinations conditioned on each input's encoder representation, enabling instance-specific prefix prompts rather than a single static prompt per task; and (b) a query-key module that learns task-specific token weights to form discriminative queries from the visual encoder's token sequence, which are matched against learned task keys via cosine similarity for task routing at inference. Task parameters are frozen after training to prevent forgetting. Second, GuidedMix augmentation selects semantically similar image pairs using Grad-CAM activation map similarity, blends them via a smooth sigmoid mask guided by class-discriminative regions, and pairs the mixed image with a mixture-aware text embedding (e.g., "a photo of {c1} mixed with {c2}") for image-text alignment training. The full training loss combines cross-entropy classification loss, a mixing alignment loss weighted by mixing ratio, and a contrastive separation loss that drives GuidedMix queries to have lower key similarity than original samples. At inference, per-task thresholds computed from GuidedMix similarity scores serve as OOD detectors: inputs scoring below threshold bypass prompts and fall back to the zero-shot VLM backbone. The framework is entirely replay-free and requires no external data. Key Results: Evaluated on an 11-task multi-domain benchmark (Aircraft, Caltech101, CIFAR100, DTD, EuroSAT, Flowers, Food, MNIST, OxfordPet, StanfordCars, SUN397) under two task orderings. Under full supervision, DREAM achieves Transfer/Average/Last of 70.0/77.8/86.6 (Order-I) and 65.3/75.8/86.7 (Order-II), outperforming the prior best MoE-Adapter4CL by 1.1/1.1/1.6 and 1.0/1.1/2.6 percentage points respectively. Transfer score matches or exceeds the original zero-shot CLIP baseline (69.4), demonstrating preserved generalization. Under partial supervision (5 labeled samples per class), DREAM achieves 69.6/73.0/78.7 (Order-I), beating MoE-Adapter4CL+FixMatch (69.4/72.0/76.7). Parameter count is 30.4M versus 59.8M for MoE-Adapter4CL (49.2% reduction). Ablations confirm that both Lmix and Lsep are necessary — removing Lsep causes the Average score to collapse from 73.0 to 64.3. GuidedMix-based OOD thresholding achieves 92.21% precision and 74.23% recall, dominating all fixed-percentile strategies. Dynamic prompts outperform static prompts (Last: 78.7 vs. 77.1), and learned token weighting outperforms both [class]-token and uniform averaging (Last: 78.7 vs. 74.5 vs. 71.9). Strengths: - The dual use of GuidedMix — simultaneously as training augmentation, as a vehicle for mixture-aware text alignment, and as OOD proxies for threshold calibration — is elegant and internally consistent, avoiding any need for external data. - The dynamic, input-conditioned prompt composition is a meaningful upgrade over task-level static prompts, and the learned token weighting for query construction is well-motivated and empirically validated with interpretable visualizations. - The framework is genuinely parameter-efficient (30.4M trainable parameters across 11 tasks) and replay-free, making it practically appealing for annotation-scarce and privacy-sensitive settings. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The gains over MoE-Adapter4CL are modest (1–2.6 pp), and the benchmark is limited to a single architecture (ViT-B/16 CLIP) and one continual learning protocol (task-incremental with known task boundaries at inference for threshold selection); generalization to class-incremental or task-agnostic settings is undemonstrated. - The GuidedMix procedure requires Grad-CAM computation over the training set at the start of each task, which adds preprocessing overhead and depends on gradient quality — potentially unreliable for fine-grained or domain-shifted datasets where Grad-CAM localizations are noisy. The dataset-specific hyperparameter tuning (e.g., different α, ζ for Caltech101) also hints at brittleness.
Recommended
#137 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Neural Structure Embedding for Symbolic Regression via Continuous Structure Search and Coefficient Optimization

Fateme Memar, Tao Zhe, Dongjie Wang · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: SRCO provides a clean engineering decomposition of symbolic regression into structure search and coefficient optimization, with the COF abstraction enabling coefficient-agnostic Transformer training. However, bootstrapping the structure corpus from gplearn inherently limits discovery to recombinations of what GP already found, and evaluation is confined to a single benchmark family (Feynman equations) with low dimensionality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Symbolic regression (SR) — discovering interpretable mathematical equations from data — has long relied on discrete search methods like genetic programming (GP). These suffer from three interrelated problems: (1) small edits to expression trees cause large, non-smooth functional changes, yielding unstable search dynamics; (2) joint optimization of symbolic structure and numeric coefficients inflates search complexity; and (3) discrete representations prevent reuse of structural knowledge across tasks. Neural SR approaches (DSO, NeSymReS) mitigate heuristic quality but preserve fundamentally discrete, token-by-token generation. No prior method learns a continuous embedding explicitly capturing whole-equation structural similarity and uses it to drive the SR pipeline end-to-end. Methodology: SRCO (Symbolic Regression via Continuous structure search and cOefficient optimization) decomposes SR into three decoupled stages. First, structure embedding: a GP tool (gplearn) generates ~1000 candidate equations per dataset; all numeric constants are abstracted to a shared COF placeholder token, and expressions are converted to postfix (RPN) notation. A Transformer is trained autoregressively on these sequences to learn a probabilistic prior p_θ(S) over symbolic structures. Second, continuous structure search: new templates are sampled from the Transformer using temperature-controlled top-k decoding, producing M diverse candidates. Each candidate passes a syntactic stack-parser (arity check), semantic filter (e.g., division-by-zero elimination), and complexity budget constraint C(S) ≤ C_max. A lightweight proxy scorer based on the accumulated token log-probabilities ranks candidates cheaply. Third, coefficient optimization: COF placeholders are replaced by distinct learnable parameters w and optimized via gradient descent (MSE loss) on training data, with early stopping. Final equations are selected by held-out performance under complexity constraints. The key architectural novelty is treating the Transformer-learned latent space as a structural prior that guides sampling rather than as a black-box predictor. Key Results: On both Feynman-synthetic and Feynman-real-world benchmarks (30/40/50 equations across easy/medium/hard tiers with 2/3/4 input variables), SRCO substantially outperforms four baselines: DSO, FFX, EFS, and gplearn. On Feynman-synthetic, SRCO achieves R² ≈ 0.998/0.996/0.937 across easy/medium/hard tiers; by contrast, DSO reaches negative R² on medium/hard tiers. On Feynman-real-world, SRCO attains R² = 0.950/0.999/0.950 vs. all baselines below 0.60 on medium/hard. Ablation replacing gradient-based coefficient fitting with stochastic hill-climbing on a representative equation drops R² from 0.999 to 0.928. Noise robustness experiments show R² ≥ 0.95 even under 70% feature corruption. On inference efficiency, SRCO evaluates at 0.00649 s/equation — matching EFS (0.00651 s) and outperforming DSO (2.6×), FFX (6.1×), and gplearn (38.5×). Hyperparameter sweeps show diminishing returns past max_term ≈ 18–22 and max_trig_vars ≈ 3–6. Strengths: - Clean decoupling of structure discovery from coefficient fitting is conceptually principled and empirically validated — the ablation shows gradient-based coefficients alone contribute meaningfully even when structure is held fixed. - Competitive inference speed (≈ EFS, 38× faster than gplearn) demonstrates that the complexity budgeting mechanism produces genuinely compact expressions. - The COF abstraction enabling coefficient-agnostic structure learning is a simple but effective mechanism that generalizes across datasets without per-equation retraining of the Transformer. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The approach is data-hungry in a circular way: it uses gplearn to bootstrap the structure corpus, meaning SRCO inherits gplearn's structural biases and cannot discover equation patterns that gplearn never generates. The method is partly constrained to recombine what GP already found. - Evaluation is limited to two variants of a single benchmark family (Feynman physics equations with up to 4 variables and 6 operators), all low-dimensional. Scalability to higher-dimensional spaces, richer operator libraries, or non-physics domains is undemonstrated. - The paper presents no ablation on the Transformer itself (architecture size, training corpus size, or whether the structural prior actually captures generalizable structure vs. dataset-specific memorization), making it hard to understand where the gains originate relative to simply running gplearn longer.
Search Results 2603.22429 PDF
#138 — Moderately Innovative (55)

ThinkJEPA: Empowering Latent World Models with Large Vision-Language Reasoning Model (9 ▲ )

Haichao Zhang, Yijiang Li, Shwai He, Tushar Nagarajan, Mingfei Chen, Jianglin Lu, Ang Li, Yun Fu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Mar 2026
Why this rank: ThinkJEPA's architectural insight — using a frozen VLM as a cached semantic guidance provider rather than a dense predictor — is clean and well-motivated, elegantly sidestepping compute and bottleneck issues. The hierarchical pyramid extraction across intermediate layers is well-ablated. However, evaluation is confined to two egocentric hand-manipulation benchmarks with modest gains on one (EgoExo4D), and the offline caching requirement limits practical applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Latent world models like V-JEPA2 forecast future states in representation space, avoiding costly pixel generation. However, they suffer from two key limitations: (1) dense sampling from short observation windows biases predictors toward local, low-level dynamics and misses long-horizon semantic context; and (2) self-supervised visual pretraining yields motion-sensitive features with little alignment to open-vocabulary concepts or compositional knowledge. Vision-language models (VLMs) offer strong semantic grounding but are ill-suited as standalone dense predictors due to compute-driven sparse frame sampling, a language-output bottleneck that compresses fine-grained interaction states into text-oriented representations, and data-regime mismatch when adapting to small action-conditioned datasets. ThinkJEPA addresses this gap by using a VLM not as a predictor but as a semantic guidance provider injected into a JEPA-style latent world model. Methodology: ThinkJEPA employs a dual-temporal pathway architecture. A dense JEPA branch processes densely sampled frames within a short observation window to capture fine-grained motion and contact cues; a VLM-thinker branch (Qwen3-VL Thinking) processes uniformly sampled frames spanning the full video clip to capture long-horizon semantics and world knowledge. To transfer useful guidance from the VLM, the paper introduces a hierarchical pyramid representation extraction module that aggregates features from multiple intermediate LLM layers (L={0,4,8,12,16,20,24,27}) plus ViT visual encoder tokens, rather than relying on the final layer alone (which is increasingly shaped toward language generation). These multi-depth VLM features are pooled, projected into the predictor space, and injected into each block of the V-JEPA predictor via Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM): FiLM(z; γ, β) = γ⊙z + β. VLM features are cached offline, so the VLM does not participate in the training forward pass. For long-horizon rollout, predicted future latents are fed recursively as past tokens into subsequent steps. The downstream task is 3D hand trajectory regression via a lightweight temporal MLP head. Key Results: On EgoDex (egocentric dexterous manipulation), ThinkJEPA achieves ADE=0.061, FDE=0.056, Acc=0.596, compared to V-JEPA Predictor (ADE=0.071, FDE=0.066, Acc=0.471) and Qwen3-VL Thinking standalone (ADE=0.142, FDE=0.144, Acc=0.084). On EgoExo4D, ThinkJEPA attains ADE=0.622, FDE=0.597, Acc=0.171, vs. V-JEPA Predictor (0.659/0.636/0.074) and Qwen3-VL (0.661/0.690/0.038). ThinkJEPA also outperforms all six EgoDex trajectory baselines (BC, DDPM, Flow Matching with decoder-only and encoder-decoder heads), reducing ADE from 0.0767 (best BC baseline) to 0.0610. In recursive rollout at horizon H=32, ThinkJEPA achieves ADE=0.111 vs. V-JEPA Predictor=0.142; Qwen3-VL degrades catastrophically to ADE=1.026, confirming VLMs are unsuitable as standalone dense predictors. Ablations show that combining both encoder tokens and autoregressive tokens with the JEPA branch is critical—each token source alone provides minimal gain—and that using all pyramid layers substantially outperforms using only the last or a single mid layer (ADE drops from ~0.128 to 0.061). A pure zero-shot prompt-only VLM baseline achieves ADE=10.855, confirming task-specific training is necessary. Strengths: - Clean architectural insight: VLM as a cached, frozen guidance provider rather than a dense predictor elegantly sidesteps compute cost, language-output bottleneck, and catastrophic forgetting—the three stated failure modes of direct VLM use. - The hierarchical pyramid extraction is well-motivated and ablated: using all intermediate layers gives a dramatic jump (ADE 0.128 → 0.061) that neither last-layer nor mid-layer alone achieves, validating the design. - Thorough ablation suite covering token sources, layer selection, conditioning mechanism (FiLM vs. cross-attention vs. AdaLN), temporal stride, prompt conditioning, and deepstack-token removal provides strong evidence that gains are attributable to the guidance pathway rather than confounds. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is narrow: both datasets (EgoDex, EgoExo4D) are egocentric hand-manipulation benchmarks. There is no demonstration that ThinkJEPA generalizes to other embodied or video prediction tasks, and the paper acknowledges this as future work. - The improvement on EgoExo4D is modest (ADE 0.659 → 0.622 over the JEPA baseline), suggesting the gains may be domain-specific and may not hold uniformly across task types; the authors do not analyze why EgoExo4D shows weaker gains. - Caching VLM features offline limits applicability to online or streaming settings. The paper does not report inference latency or memory overhead, making it difficult to assess practical deployability of the full dual-branch system.

#139 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Understanding the Challenges in Iterative Generative Optimization with LLMs (13 ▲ )

Allen Nie, Xavier Daull, Zhiyi Kuang, Abhinav Akkiraju, Anish Chaudhuri, Max Piasevoli, Ryan Rong, YuCheng Yuan, Prerit Choudhary, Shannon Xiao, Rasool Fakoor, Adith Swaminathan, Ching-An Cheng · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Mar 2026
Why this rank: This paper provides a useful diagnostic framework by identifying three concrete, previously unnamed design dimensions (starting artifact, credit horizon, experience batching) in LLM-based generative optimization and drawing apt parallels to classical ML. However, it is primarily descriptive—it names the problems and demonstrates their difficulty across diverse domains but offers no algorithmic solutions or principled heuristics, limiting its contribution to a well-structured empirical survey rather than a methodological advance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Despite active research interest, LLM-based generative optimization — using LLMs to iteratively improve artifacts (code, prompts, workflows) via execution feedback — remains poorly adopted in practice: only 9% of surveyed production agentic systems use any automated optimization. The paper argues this is not due to insufficient tooling or infrastructure (rich frameworks like DSPy, Trace, and LangGraph exist), but rather due to hidden engineering complexity in setting up the learning loop correctly. The authors identify three under-studied design decisions that critically determine success or failure: the choice of starting artifact, the credit horizon for execution traces, and the batching of trials into learning context. Methodology: The paper is structured as a position/empirical analysis rather than proposing a new algorithm. The authors formalize a "learning loop" using the OPTO framework (Trace Oracle), decomposing the design space into: (1) Starting Artifact — what code structure and documentation is provided initially; (2) Credit Horizon — how many steps of an execution trace to include before each optimizer update; and (3) Experience Batching — how many independent trials to aggregate into a single update. Three case studies isolate each factor. For the starting artifact problem, they compare one-function vs. many-function ML pipeline initializations on MLAgentBench (Kaggle tasks), using OptoPrime as the optimizer with Claude Sonnet-3.5-v2. For credit horizon, they optimize Python game-playing agents on 8 Atari games using OCAtari object-centric representations, comparing one-step (immediate reward) vs. multi-step (full rollout) update strategies. For experience batching, they optimize prompted LLM agents on BigBench Extra Hard (8 tasks) with batch sizes of 1, 3, and 5 examples per update. All experiments use OptoPrime as the generative optimizer backend. Key Results: Starting artifact: the many-function modular initialization surpasses 86.6% of Kaggle leaderboard submissions for Spaceship Titanic (vs. 72.7% for single-function), but the ordering reverses for Housing Price (75.6% one-function vs. 54.6% many-function), demonstrating strong task-dependence. Both outperform the ResearchAgent baseline by 11.5–22.4% on average. Credit horizon: multi-step optimization outperforms one-step in 4 of 8 Atari games (Pong, Breakout, Space Invaders, Asterix); one-step performs better in the other 4 (Freeway, Enduro, Q*bert, Seaquest), confirming that optimal horizon depends on whether immediate rewards align with long-term success. As a side finding, LLM-based code optimization reaches competitive Atari scores ~26x faster in wall-clock time than DQN/PPO baselines. Experience batching: optimal batch size is task-dependent and non-monotonic — batch=1 is best for Disambiguation QA (0.537) and Movie Recommendation (0.889); batch=3 for Geometric Shapes and Boolean Expressions; batch=5 for Causal Understanding (0.531). For Boardgame QA, all optimization configurations degrade below the unoptimized baseline (meta-overfitting). The paper draws explicit parallels to classical ML: starting artifact ↔ network initialization, credit horizon ↔ truncated BPTT/RL horizon, experience batching ↔ SGD batch size. Strengths: - Clearly identifies and names three concrete, previously underspecified design dimensions in generative optimization, providing a useful vocabulary and framework for practitioners. - Experiments span diverse domains (tabular ML, game playing, NLP), making the task-dependence conclusion credible rather than cherry-picked. - The analogy to classical ML optimization (initialization, credit horizon, batch size) is apt and provides a constructive research agenda for future theoretical and empirical work. - The Atari efficiency comparison (26x wall-clock speedup vs. deep RL) is a compelling, concrete datapoint for LLM-based optimization advocates. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is primarily descriptive/diagnostic: it identifies the problems and shows they are hard, but provides no algorithmic solution, principled heuristics, or automated methods for choosing any of the three factors. The "practical guidance" offered is largely qualitative. - Small experimental scale limits statistical conclusions: 5 runs per MLAgentBench condition, 3 trials per BBEH condition, and limited ablation over intermediate configurations (e.g., only 3 batch sizes tested). The variance in some conditions (e.g., Causal Understanding batch=1: ±0.146) is very high relative to differences between configurations. - The Atari one-step vs. multi-step comparison conflates multiple confounds: one-step condition also updates far more frequently, so the credit horizon and update frequency are not independently controlled.
Recommended 2603.23994 PDF
#140 — Moderately Innovative (54)

Syllogism-Inspired TableQA: Evidentialization Makes Decomposition Reasoning and Answer Verification More Reliable

ojs.aaai.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: SIRV's framing of TableQA as syllogistic reasoning is a reasonable conceptual lens, but the underlying mechanism — extracting evidence from the table before decomposing questions — is a natural and relatively expected improvement over purely semantic decomposition. The gains are meaningful but modest (~2-5%), and the reliance on a single backbone model (GPT-3.5) and only two benchmarks limits the strength of generalization claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Table Question Answering (TableQA) with LLMs typically relies on two processes: decomposition reasoning (breaking questions into sub-questions) and answer verification (checking outputs for errors). Existing methods decompose questions purely at the semantic level, ignoring factual evidence embedded in tables — leaving LLMs to infer implicit relationships like "country codes are in parentheses in the Cyclist column" without being told. Separately, LLM self-correction without grounding in factual evidence is well-documented to be unreliable and can actually degrade accuracy. There is a gap: no prior method systematically incorporates table-grounded factual evidence into both decomposition and verification. Methodology: SIRV (Syllogism-Inspired Reasoning and Verification) frames TableQA as syllogistic reasoning: the table provides a factual background from which major and minor premises are extracted, and conclusions (answers) must be strictly derived from those premises. The pipeline has three steps. (1) Decomposition Reasoning: SIRV first calls an LLM to extract question-relevant factual evidence from the table as structured premises (e.g., "country codes are extracted from parentheses in the Cyclist column," "rows included only if Rank ≤ 10"). These premises are then used to generate sub-questions that explicitly cite the relevant evidence rather than splitting semantically. Both Direct Prompting (DP) mode (step-by-step sub-question answering) and Agent mode (code-generating interaction with the table) are supported. (2) Answer Verification: SIRV critically examines consistency between the constructed premises and actual table content; if discrepancies are found, premises are revised before verification proceeds. Each sub-answer or interaction result is then verified against the validated premise-grounded knowledge background. (3) Joint Reasoning: Five candidate answers are generated in each mode and combined via majority voting across configurable DP/Agent ratios to select the final answer. The backbone model is GPT-3.5 throughout. Key Results: SIRV is evaluated on WikiTableQuestions (WTQ, 4,344 test samples) and TabFact (2,024 test samples) against 11 baselines including CHAIN-OF-TABLE, BINDER, DATER, ReAcTable, and the prior SOTA TIDE. In joint DP+Agent mode, SIRV achieves 77.89% on WTQ and 91.85% on TabFact, outperforming TIDE by 2.89% and 2.03% respectively (mean +2.46%). In single-mode comparisons, SIRV-Agent achieves +5.24% on WTQ over TIDE-Agent (73.96% vs. 68.72%), and SIRV-DP achieves +4.65% on TabFact over TIDE-DP (85.97% vs. 81.32%). The evidence-grounded answer verification (SIRV-AV) corrects 39.25%/72.17% of erroneous answers on WTQ/TabFact, compared to 29.53%/65.25% for plain self-correction — an 8.32% mean improvement in correction rate. Ablation confirms both decomposition and verification components are independently necessary; removing either causes 2–6% accuracy drops across modes. SIRV also demonstrates efficiency: mean interaction count is 2.05 (WTQ Agent) vs. TIDE's 2.57, achieving better results with fewer steps. Strengths: - The premise construction mechanism is technically well-motivated: grounding sub-question generation in extracted table evidence directly addresses a known failure mode of semantic-only decomposition, with a clear qualitative example demonstrating the contrast. - Evidence-grounded verification sidesteps the documented pitfall of LLM self-correction without external grounding, and the correction-rate comparison (Table 4) quantitatively isolates this contribution cleanly. - The framework is mode-agnostic: it applies to both DP and Agent paradigms, and the joint voting over both further improves results, giving practical flexibility. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Premise construction is itself an LLM call on the full table, which can fail or hallucinate premises for tables with implicit or ambiguous schemas — the paper does not analyze premise construction error rates or failure modes. - The method relies on GPT-3.5 as the sole backbone; no experiments with stronger models (GPT-4, open-source LLMs) make it unclear whether the gains are specific to GPT-3.5's weaknesses in semantic decomposition or generalize broadly. - Evaluation is restricted to two established benchmarks (WTQ, TabFact) with well-structured tables; performance on noisier, semi-structured, or multi-table settings is not assessed, limiting claims about generalization.
Citations
#141 — Moderately Innovative (54)

PEARL: Personalized Streaming Video Understanding Model (11 ▲ )

Yuanhong Zheng, Ruichuan An, Xiaopeng Lin, Yuxing Liu, Sihan Yang, Huanyu Zhang, Haodong Li, Qintong Zhang, Renrui Zhang, Guopeng Li, Yifan Zhang, Yuheng Li, Wentao Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Mar 2026
Why this rank: PEARL formulates a new and well-motivated task (personalized streaming video understanding) and introduces a clean benchmark decomposition into three query types. However, the proposed method — dual memory with embedding-based retrieval and query rewriting — assembles relatively standard retrieval-augmented generation components without fundamental methodological novelty. The training-free plug-and-play property is practical but the large gap to human performance (52% vs 97%) suggests the approach is a reasonable baseline rather than a solution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing multimodal personalization methods for vision-language models (VLMs) are confined to static images or offline, pre-recorded video. They cannot handle the continuous, real-time nature of streaming video, nor do they support on-the-fly concept registration during an ongoing stream. This gap is critical for next-generation AI assistants (wearable devices, personalized robotics, fitness coaching) that must continuously recognize user-specific entities and actions while delivering instant, interactive responses. No benchmark existed to measure this capability. Methodology: The paper makes two principal contributions. First, it proposes the Personalized Streaming Video Understanding (PSVU) task and introduces PEARL-Bench, the first benchmark for this setting. The benchmark covers 132 videos (average duration 1,458 seconds) and 2,173 timestamped annotations across two granularities: Frame-level (recognizing specific persons or objects across discrete frames) and Video-level (recognizing personalized action sequences spanning continuous clips). Three query types are defined — Concept-Definition QA, Real-Time QA, and Past-Time QA — to evaluate concept registration, current-scene grounding, and long-range temporal retrieval respectively. Annotation quality is enforced via ablation-based automated filtering (removing questions solvable without the concept or historical evidence) plus multi-round human review. Concept names are anonymized using 10k SSA names to prevent exploitation of prior model knowledge. Second, the paper proposes PEARL, a training-free plug-and-play framework. Its Dual-grained Memory System separates two concerns: a Streaming Memory that segments the video using PySceneDetect and stores each clip with a Qwen3-VL-Embedding-2B embedding, and a Concept Memory that, upon a definition event, stores a concept's name, a reference visual (last frame for frame-level; the clip itself for video-level), and a compact VLM-generated textual description focusing on stable features (face/hair/body for persons; core kinematics for actions). At query time, the Concept-aware Retrieval Algorithm rewrites the query by substituting concept names with their textual descriptions, encodes the rewritten query with the same embedding model, and performs cosine-similarity retrieval to fetch top-K clips (K=4) with N=1 adjacent clips expanded for temporal context. The retrieved concept entries, retrieved clips, and current clip are then passed to any off-the-shelf VLM for response generation. Key Results: Evaluated against 8 offline and online baselines on PEARL-Bench, Qwen3-VL-8B+PEARL achieves 52.24% average frame-level accuracy vs. 28.77% for the base model (a +23.47% gain) and 48.39% on video-level vs. 25.51% baseline (+22.88%). It surpasses the best online baseline StreamForest-7B by 17.22% on frame-level and 24.28% on video-level, and outperforms Gemini3-pro-preview (proprietary, 64-frame context) by over 4% on frame-level and ~24% on video-level. The framework is architecture-agnostic: average gains across LLaVA-OV-7B, Qwen2-VL-7B, and Qwen3-VL-8B are 13.79% frame-level and 12.80% video-level. End-to-end latency for LLaVA-OV-7B+PEARL is 775ms — faster than all online baselines — with retrieval and rewriting contributing only 5–154ms overhead; LLM inference dominates at 582–1909ms. Ablation confirms that Concept Memory alone drives a +35% Real-Time accuracy jump, Streaming Memory adds +20% Past-Time accuracy, and Query Rewriting adds a further +4.28% average. Strengths: - The task formulation is well-motivated and cleanly decomposed into three query types that isolate distinct capabilities (current-scene grounding, long-range retrieval, concept registration), enabling targeted diagnostics. - The training-free, plug-and-play design is genuinely practical: PEARL improves three distinct architectures with no fine-tuning and adds negligible latency overhead relative to LLM inference, making it immediately deployable. - The dual-grained memory decoupling (concept-centric vs. stream-centric) is principled and the ablation study validates each component's contribution independently, providing clear mechanistic insight. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The video-level split contains only 20 synthesized videos using Mixamo 3D assets, creating a significant domain gap from real-world action recognition; it is unclear how well results generalize to natural video with complex, partially-occluded actions or person-specific style variations. - All models — including the best PEARL variant — remain far below human performance (52.24% vs. 97.03% frame-level average), indicating that the benchmark exposes a fundamental ceiling, yet the paper offers limited analysis of failure modes or where the retrieval mechanism breaks down (e.g., concept drift over very long streams, ambiguous concept boundaries). - The evaluation protocol (4/4 cyclic rotation correctness) is extremely strict and may artificially suppress absolute scores, making cross-benchmark comparisons difficult and potentially masking qualitative differences between methods.
Recommended 2603.20422 PDF
#142 — Moderately Innovative (53)

SFGA: Similarity-Constrained Fusion Learning for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection in Multiplex Graphs

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: SFGA makes a reasonable contribution by extending graph anomaly detection to multiplex graphs with cross-attention fusion and similarity-constrained scoring. The hierarchical scoring separating structural from feature anomalies is well-motivated. However, the methodology is largely a natural combination of existing components — GCN autoencoders, cross-attention, and homophily-based regularization — applied to the multiplex setting, representing solid engineering rather than surprising novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Real-world systems like e-commerce platforms and financial networks are naturally modeled as multiplex graphs — graphs with shared nodes but multiple relation types (e.g., review, browse, purchase). Detecting anomalies in such graphs poses two challenges not addressed by existing homogeneous graph anomaly detection (GAD) methods: (1) relation-specific anomalies, where fraudulent behavior manifests in only one or a few relation types while appearing normal in others, and (2) camouflaged anomalies, where malicious actors mimic normal behavior patterns across relations, causing their latent representations to overlap with benign nodes. Collapsing multiplex graphs into homogeneous graphs — the standard workaround — destroys relational distinctions that are key to detecting both anomaly types. Methodology: The proposed method, SFGA (Similarity-constrained Fusion Graph Autoencoder), is a fully unsupervised end-to-end framework with three main components. First, a relation-separated autoencoder applies independent GCN encoder channels per relational subgraph to learn view-specific node embeddings, with MLP-based decoders reconstructing node features. This captures relation-specific patterns while incurring larger reconstruction errors for anomalies. Second, an attentive inter-relation fusion module at the bottleneck uses a cross-attention mechanism to aggregate representations across relational views: for each node in a focal graph, attention weights are computed against all nodes in contextual graphs, enabling selective integration of cross-relation signals to detect inconsistency-based anomalies. Third, a similarity-constrained detection module enforces a homophily-aware loss: it maximizes cosine similarity between a node's latent representation and its 1-hop neighbors (local coherence) while minimizing similarity to non-neighbors (global discrimination). The combined training objective is L = αL_rec + (1−α)L_con. At inference, a hierarchical anomaly score combines the per-view reconstruction error and the negated local neighbor similarity, weighted by β. Key Results: SFGA is evaluated on four datasets — IMDB and Freebase (injected synthetic anomalies) and Amazon-fraud and YelpChi (real fraud labels) — against seven baselines spanning contrastive learning (CoLA, PREM, AD-GCL), graph autoencoders (DOMINANT, ADA-GAD, GADAM), and message-passing methods (TAM). SFGA achieves the best AUROC and AUPRC across all four datasets. On Freebase, SFGA reaches 92.98% AUROC vs. the next best 84.89% (TAM), an ~8-point gain. On IMDB, AUPRC improves from 24.38% (DOMINANT) to 43.07% — over 16 points. On Amazon, AUROC is 78.48% vs. 71.13% for the next best (TAM). On YelpChi, where TAM runs out of memory on a 96GB GPU, SFGA achieves 70.06% AUROC vs. 54.25% for CoLA. Ablation confirms each component contributes meaningfully: removing the cross-attention (ATT) drops AUROC on YelpChi by ~10 points; removing the similarity constraint (SC) reduces both metrics; and removing the hierarchical detector (HD) further degrades performance. SFGA ranks 3rd in training efficiency across the two tested datasets but achieves the best detection performance. Strengths: - The cross-plex attention at the bottleneck is a well-motivated design choice that enables cross-relation anomaly detection without collapsing multi-relational structure, directly addressing the paper's core problem. - The similarity-constrained loss has a principled homophily-based justification: camouflaged anomalies disrupt local graph smoothness in latent space even when features appear normal, and the local/global split makes this tractable. - The hierarchical scoring naturally separates structural anomalies (caught by similarity scores) from feature anomalies (caught by reconstruction errors), with visualization evidence showing clean score stratification between anomaly types. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The time complexity of O(r·N²·h) due to the global attention over all node pairs in cross-plex fusion makes scalability to very large graphs (beyond YelpChi scale) a concern; the paper acknowledges this and defers it to future work. - The approach reconstructs only node features, not graph structure, which may miss structural anomalies that are better captured through adjacency reconstruction — a deliberate simplification to reduce computation that may limit detection on purely structural anomaly types. - Hyperparameter sensitivity is significant: both α (loss balance) and β (score balance) have narrow optimal ranges, and performance degrades noticeably at extreme values, suggesting the method requires careful tuning per dataset.
Search Results
#143 — Moderately Innovative (53)

McK: ontology for mapping semantic conflict in plot summary

cambridge.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Scores 53 for a creative cross-disciplinary formulation that operationalizes McKee's narrative conflict theory into a formal OWL 2 DL ontology and demonstrates how knowledge graph injection reshapes LLM interpretive behavior. The problem framing connecting ontology engineering to computational narratology and LLM alignment narrowing is genuinely interesting. However, the single case study with no quantitative metrics and only five runs makes this a preliminary existence proof rather than a validated contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Computational analysis of abstract narrative concepts such as "conflict" is stymied by two interacting problems: the structural polysemy of conflict (it resists stable definitional grounding even for human annotators), and the alignment-induced conceptual narrowing of LLMs, which tend to reproduce stereotypical frames (violence, power hierarchies) rather than structurally rich interpretations. Prior computational narratology work—AI planning models (Ware & Young 2010), character relationship networks (Yavuz et al. 2025)—is theoretically sophisticated but too complex for applied creative industry workflows such as screenplay coverage. The paper asks whether injecting a domain ontology via prompt can steer an LLM toward more structured, typologically diverse analysis of conflict without the overhead of full narrative planning systems. Methodology: The authors formalize Robert McKee's three-level conflict schema—inner conflict (body/mind/emotions), personal conflict (interpersonal relationships), and extra-personal conflict (society, institutions, physical environment)—into the McK ontology, expressed in OWL 2 DL. The ontology is event-centric: each conflict instance links subjects via mck:affects, internal origins via mck:hasOriginIn (with sub-properties for Body, Mind, Emotions), and external forces via mck:isAgainst. Structural disjointness axioms enforce mutual exclusivity across the three conflict types; a cardinality constraint requires inner conflicts to have at least one internal origin. The ontology was verified with the HermiT 1.4.3.456 reasoner (300ms, confirming tractability). It was then populated with entities from A Clockwork Orange (e.g., mck:AlexVsState_LudovicoTechnique typed as ExtraPersonalConflict, isAgainst mck:TheState). Experimental evaluation compared two prompt conditions over 5 identical runs each using Claude Sonnet 4: (1) synopsis + knowledge graph injected as Turtle triples, (2) synopsis only. Evaluation is qualitative-comparative, examining differences in the type, structure, and diversity of insights produced. Key Results: Under Condition 1 (KG+synopsis), the model produced typed, structured analyses anchored to McKee's triad. It traced conflict transformations across categories (e.g., personal → extra-personal → inner as a narrative arc), identified structural patterns not explicit in the synopsis—"Origin Inversion" (institutionally implanted inner conflicts subverting the authenticity of body/mind/emotions) and "Recursive Recognition" (past personal conflicts recycled as extra-personal ones through victim-institution alignment)—and derived implicit inferences from ontological axioms (class definitions, property restrictions). Under Condition 2 (synopsis only), outputs collapsed to two dominant frames: state violence as institutional monopoly and cyclical retribution as social order. The KG condition distributed interpretive attention more broadly across individual-level conflict metamorphoses; the no-KG condition privileged macro-political readings driven by LLM pretraining patterns. No quantitative metrics are reported; the contribution is framed as existence proof and ontological scaffolding. Strengths: - Formally principled operationalization of a practitioner heuristic (McKee) into OWL 2 DL with explicit axioms, cardinality constraints, and structural disjointness—making the theoretical commitments inspectable and queryable - Clear two-condition comparison that concretely demonstrates how knowledge injection reshapes LLM interpretive behavior, directly addressing the known alignment-narrowing problem - All artifacts (ontology, populated KG, execution transcripts) are publicly released, enabling replication Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is a single case study with no quantitative metrics, no human annotation baseline, and no inter-annotator agreement—making it impossible to determine whether KG-augmented outputs are more accurate or merely differently biased - McKee's conflict schema is a practitioner framework, not an empirically validated narratological theory; the analysis findings are intrinsically conditional on this theoretical choice, a limitation the paper acknowledges but does not quantitatively bound - The experiment uses a Wikipedia synopsis rather than the screenplay itself (due to copyright), dramatically limiting the granularity of conflict evidence available; the ontology's potential for fine-grained scene-level analysis is untested - Five runs with identical parameters and a single LLM provide no basis for assessing model-invariance or prompt sensitivity; the paper explicitly disclaims generalizability but offers no sensitivity analysis

#144 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Insight-V++: Towards Advanced Long-Chain Visual Reasoning with Multimodal Large Language Models

Yuhao Dong, Zuyan Liu, Shulin Tian, Yongming Rao, Ziwei Liu · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scores 52 because while the dual-agent decomposition and role-specific RL objectives (ST-GRPO, J-GRPO) are well-engineered, the system heavily combines existing techniques (GRPO, DPO, multi-agent collaboration) rather than introducing fundamentally new ideas. The self-evolving loop's modest gains (+1.3% video) relative to its substantial complexity suggest diminishing returns from architectural elaboration.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) lag significantly behind LLMs in long-chain reasoning despite LLM advances (e.g., OpenAI o1, DeepSeek-R1). Two bottlenecks are responsible: (1) the scarcity of high-quality, structured visual reasoning data—harder and more expensive to collect than text data—and (2) the absence of training pipelines that can stably optimize MLLMs for complex multi-step visual inference. Existing approaches either apply chain-of-thought prompting naively (limited gains), use offline DPO (degrades as models evolve), or cannot extend from static images to temporally dynamic video requiring spatial-temporal coherence. Methodology: The paper presents Insight-V and its extension Insight-V++, a unified multi-agent framework for visual reasoning. The architecture has three interlocking components. First, a scalable data generation pipeline uses a progressive strategy: a reasoning generator iteratively produces structured JSON reasoning chains (each step outputs a brief summary, a detailed response, and a continue/summary action), followed by a multi-granularity assessment that filters by answer correctness and scores remaining paths 1–100 using a strong VLM judge (Qwen2-VL). For video, the pipeline incorporates in-context golden examples curated with Gemini-2.5-Pro to guide quality scoring of temporal trajectories. Second, a dual-agent architecture separates reasoning (generates step-by-step chains) from summarization (critically evaluates the chain and produces the final answer, robust to flawed reasoning). The summary agent trains on both high-quality and intentionally flawed reasoning paths to develop critical robustness. Third, the training pipeline for Insight-V uses supervised fine-tuning followed by iterative DPO; Insight-V++ replaces DPO with two novel on-policy GRPO variants: ST-GRPO for the reasoning agent, with a composite reward combining binary QA accuracy, temporal IoU for grounding, and a video-jigsaw clip-order restoration task; and J-GRPO for the summary agent, using a two-stage curriculum that first trains on high-quality paths with equal judge/answer reward weighting, then shifts to 3:7 in favor of the final answer on harder, lower-quality paths. Finally, a self-evolving training loop has the two trained agents collaborate iteratively (up to 3 rounds): the reasoning agent generates a path, the summary agent critiques and provides feedback, the reasoning agent refines, and the resulting high-quality trajectories (filtered by the data pipeline) are used to jointly re-fine-tune both agents before another RL round—enabling annotation-free data self-generation. Key Results: On general image reasoning with LLaVA-NeXT-LLaMA3, Insight-V lifts average reasoning from 46.8% to 54.9% (+8.1%) and perception from 65.5% to 72.3%. Applied to Qwen2.5-VL (a much stronger baseline at 66.3% reasoning), Insight-V++ raises reasoning to 71.1% (+4.8%) while preserving perception. On advanced image benchmarks (MathVision, MathVerse, WeMath, LogicVista, DynaMath, CharXiv), Insight-V++ achieves an average of 53.9, surpassing OpenMMReasoner (52.9) and Open-Vision-Reasoner (50.7) despite all being 7B-scale RL-based models. On six video benchmarks starting from Qwen2.5-VL's 47.3% average, RL multi-agent training reaches 52.9% (+5.6%) and self-evolving adds a further +1.3% to 54.2%. Notable video gains: VideoMMLU +10.9% (37.5→48.4%, nearly matching GPT-4o's 49.4%), VideoMMMU +10.2% (exceeding Video-R1 and VideoChat-R1), VideoHolmes +5.5%. Ablations confirm each component contributes: multi-agent over vanilla SFT (+1.5% avg on four benchmarks), iterative DPO over single DPO (+0.6%), ST-GRPO as the largest single RL contributor, J-GRPO adding robustness, and self-evolving outperforming collaborative inference at fixed weights. Strengths: - The dual-agent decomposition (reasoning vs. summarization) is well-motivated and empirically validated: the summary agent trained on mixed-quality reasoning paths learns genuine critical evaluation rather than copying outputs, providing meaningful robustness against flawed chains. - The two novel RL objectives are thoughtfully role-specific: ST-GRPO introduces a video-jigsaw auxiliary task with a principled per-clip accuracy reward to force temporal alignment, while J-GRPO's two-stage curriculum with adaptive reward weighting (judge:answer = 1:1 then 3:7) reflects a sound insight that severely degraded reasoning is better overcome by a strong answer signal than by attempting to fix the chain. - The closed-loop self-evolving strategy is annotation-free and achieves genuine co-optimization, not just inference-time collaboration: the feedback loop generates superior data that re-trains both agents, driving continued improvement without additional human labeling. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The system's compute footprint is substantial and underreported: two separate 7B MLLMs at inference (reasoning + summary agent), plus multi-round self-evolving training with SFT→RL→SFT→RL cycles, makes deployment and training cost significantly higher than a single-model baseline—yet no latency, memory, or wall-clock comparisons are provided. - The self-evolving gains, while real, are modest (+1.3% video, +1.6% general image reasoning) relative to the complexity introduced, and the evaluation relies heavily on Qwen2.5-VL as the base model; it is unclear whether the framework's self-evolving component transfers as effectively to weaker or architecturally different base models. - Quality evaluation for video reasoning paths depends on Qwen2.5-VL with Gemini-2.5-Pro in-context examples as the judge, introducing a potential ceiling where the system cannot surpass the implicit quality standard set by the judge model, and the in-context golden set is human-curated (partially undermining the annotation-free claim for video).
Citations 2603.18118 PDF
#145 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Thermally-Aware System-Technology Co- Optimization for AI Systems

nanocad.ee.ucla.edu · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The closed-loop coupling of LLM workload simulation with thermal analysis is a useful engineering contribution, and the material sensitivity hierarchy (TIM dominates, underfill negligible) is an actionable design guideline. However, the methodology fundamentally integrates two existing simulators with an iterative feedback loop, and the scope limitations (single GPU, steady-state, lumped thermal model) constrain the practical applicability of the quantitative findings.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As AI workloads drive chiplet-based integration (2.5D and 3D stacking) to overcome monolithic die scaling limits, thermal throttling becomes a first-order performance bottleneck. High-performance GPU+HBM packages face temperature-induced DVFS on the GPU and elevated refresh rates on HBM, directly degrading end-to-end LLM training and inference throughput. Prior work treats thermal simulation and performance modeling as separate, decoupled analyses, making it impossible to accurately predict how packaging material choices (TIMs, underfill, infill) or stacking architecture decisions (2.5D vs. 3D, memory-on-compute vs. compute-on-memory) translate into real workload performance. No prior unified framework existed for this co-optimization at the LLM workload level. Methodology: The authors develop a closed-loop System-Technology Co-Optimization (STCO) framework that iterates between a performance simulator (Rapid-LLM, which models operator-level LLM execution on GPU clusters) and a thermal simulator (Anemoi, a fast commercial steady-state solver). Given a chiplet stackup and workload, Rapid-LLM estimates per-chiplet power, which feeds into Anemoi to determine GPU and HBM peak temperatures. A throttling model then adjusts GPU clock frequency via quadratic DVFS scaling (threshold at 95°C) and HBM refresh rates via JEDEC-specified trip points at 85°C and 95°C (causing 2× and 4× refresh, reducing bandwidth to 0.91× and 0.73× respectively). Updated throttling parameters feed back into Rapid-LLM for the next iteration, converging when peak HBM temperature changes by less than 0.1°C. To accelerate design-space sweeps, the authors precompute a power-to-temperature calibration table, replacing repeated full thermal simulations with interpolation. They evaluate 2.5D (GPU adjacent to HBM) and two 3D configurations (memory-on-compute, compute-on-memory) modeled after the NVIDIA A100, using Llama2-7B and Llama3.1-8B as workloads for both training and inference. Key Results: Under baseline cooling (HTC = 7 kW/m²K), 2.5D incurs only 1–2% thermal performance degradation, while 3D stacking suffers 10–18% degradation under the same cooling solution due to higher power density and more complex thermal paths. Among packaging materials, improving TIM conductivity from 5 W/mK to 50 W/mK recovers up to 6.8% runtime performance for 3D systems; improving underfill and infill from 1.6 W/mK to 19 W/mK yields negligible benefit because Cu pillars (400 W/mK) dominate lateral heat conduction and infill is off the primary thermal path. Improving the system-level HTC from 7 to 10 kW/m²K yields up to 12.5% performance improvement, nearly eliminating 3D throttling. For 3D stacking order, compute-on-memory outperforms memory-on-compute by up to 3.7% because the GPU, being the dominant heat source, is directly coupled to the heatsink. Increasing HBM stack height from 8 (HBM3E) to 16 dies (HBM4) increases thermal throttling, with 3D configurations degrading further. Memory-bound workloads (Llama2-7B, long decode) benefit more from 3D's higher bandwidth but also suffer more throttling; GQA-equipped Llama3.1-8B is less memory-bound and shows a narrower 2.5D vs. 3D gap. Strengths: - The closed-loop co-design framework is a genuine contribution: it is the first to couple full LLM workload performance (training and inference) with steady-state thermal simulation and a detailed throttling model, enabling packaging material trade-off analysis at application level rather than synthetic microbenchmarks. - The calibration table optimization (precomputing power-to-temperature maps and using interpolation during iterations) is a practically important engineering decision that makes design-space exploration tractable without sacrificing fidelity. - The material sensitivity hierarchy finding — TIM dominates, underfill/infill are negligible — is a concrete, actionable design guideline that contradicts naive intuition and is well-explained by the heat path geometry. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework assumes uniform, steady-state temperatures across all HBMs and uses a single-GPU configuration with a lumped HTC boundary condition; transient effects, spatial non-uniformity across chiplets, and multi-GPU systems are explicitly left as future work, limiting applicability to real datacenter nodes. - The 3D bandwidth advantage is modeled with a conservative 4× multiplier over 2.5D (against a projected 25× from UCIe-3D), and several key parameters (HBM trip temperatures, GPU TDP, die dimensions) are assumed from public specs rather than measured, introducing uncertainty that could shift the quantitative conclusions substantially.
Search Results
#146 — Moderately Innovative (52)

LLM-Enhanced Semantic Data Integration of Electronic Component Qualifications in the Aerospace Domain

Antonio De Santis, Marco Balduini, Matteo Belcao, Andrea Proia, Marco Brambilla, Emanuele Della Valle · arxiv.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Scores 52 for its pragmatic hybrid architecture combining Virtual Knowledge Graphs with LLM-driven data cleaning and vector similarity search, each matched to the appropriate qualification type rather than forcing a single mechanism. The production deployment at Thales Alenia Space with ~50 users and the grounded cost-benefit crossover analysis add real-world credibility rare in applied KG papers. However, the core components (Ontop VKGs, embedding-based retrieval, LLM text extraction) are all established techniques composed rather than invented, and the circular evaluation methodology weakens the empirical claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large aerospace manufacturers like Thales Alenia Space maintain qualification records for electronic components (capacitors, resistors, ICs) across siloed, heterogeneous databases. Determining whether a component is already qualified under ESA/ECSS standards requires manually cross-referencing two internal systems: a Product Lifecycle Management Database (PLM-DB) with tens of thousands of entries and a Qualification Catalog (QC) in the thousands. The QC is manually maintained, error-prone, lacks a dedicated Part Number column (PNs are buried in free-text notes), and uses inconsistent manufacturer naming conventions. Before this work, designers spent ~40 person-minutes per component on manual lookup, risking missed qualifications and redundant re-qualification efforts costing significant resources. Methodology: The paper proposes a three-phase pipeline combining Virtual Knowledge Graphs (VKGs) and LLMs. Phase 1 (Data Cleaning) uses GPT-OSS-120B on-premise to semi-automatically normalize manufacturer name variants (466 unique names → 50 normalization rules, 18% requiring expert refinement) and extract Part Numbers from free-text QC notes, with PLM-DB cross-checks and human-in-the-loop validation for failures (~2% of cases). Phase 2 (Semantic Data Integration) builds a VKG using the Ontop framework over two relational databases, with an ontology defining PLMDB_COMPONENT and QUALIFICATION_CARD entities linked by PN, manufacturer, package, and subpackage attributes. Ontop Lenses apply the LLM-generated cleaning rules as virtual views without modifying source data. Phase 3 (Data Access) serves direct and by-similarity qualifications via exact SPARQL queries rewritten to SQL by Ontop. For alternative qualifications—where criteria are component-type-specific and involve unstructured attributes like assembly process descriptions—components and qualification records are embedded as JSON using multilingual-e5-large-instruct, filtered by SPARQL, and ranked by cosine similarity for expert review. As a baseline comparison, a RAG pipeline was also implemented: both databases embedded, top-200 most similar QC records retrieved per component, and an LLM asked to classify qualification matches in-context. Key Results: On a benchmark of 675 PLM-DB components (using the VKG+LLM pipeline output as ground truth), RAG with GPT-OSS-120B achieves F1 of 92.1% for direct, 93.7% for by-similarity, but only 61.9% (IoU 59.6%) for alternative qualifications. Mistral 3 24B collapses to an overall F1 of 13.1%, suggesting severe sensitivity to context window size. The VKG+LLM pipeline produces exact, complete results for direct and by-similarity types (by construction), with only alternative qualifications requiring expert review. Cost modeling over dataset scale shows: manual AS-IS is cheapest below ~200 components; RAG (10 person-days setup, ~15 min/component) is optimal from 200–5,000 components; VKG+LLM (60 person-days setup, ~5 min/component) dominates beyond ~5,000 components, delivering >80% effort savings at 10,000 components. The system is deployed in production at Thales Alenia Space with ~50 active users across five engineering departments. Strengths: - The hybrid symbolic+vector architecture is well-matched to the problem: exact SPARQL queries handle the two qualification types that admit deterministic rules, while vector search handles the fuzzy alternative qualification type—rather than trying to use one mechanism for everything. - The LLM integration is pragmatic and controlled: LLMs generate cleaning rules and extract structured fields, but outputs are cross-validated against the PLM-DB and refined by domain experts, avoiding hallucination propagation into the knowledge graph. - The cost-benefit analysis is grounded in real deployment data (actual setup times, measured per-component effort) and provides actionable guidance on when each approach is appropriate—a rare and valuable contribution in applied KG papers. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The RAG baseline uses the VKG+LLM pipeline's output as ground truth, creating circular evaluation: since alternative qualifications in the ground truth are themselves based on vector similarity + expert review, the RAG comparison for that category is methodologically questionable. - The cost model omits LLM inference costs (hardware, API fees, energy), which are non-trivial for on-premise GPT-OSS-120B at scale and could materially change the RAG vs. VKG+LLM crossover point—especially as LLMs improve and RAG becomes more accurate. - The ontology and pipeline are bespoke to Thales Alenia Space's two specific databases; generalizability claims are aspirational (mechanical qualifications are mentioned as future work) with no evidence yet that the approach transfers without comparable knowledge engineering effort at each new site.
Search Results 2603.20094 PDF
#147 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Structure-Enhanced Adapter for Self-Supervised Heterogeneous Graph Learning

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: HSADP's plug-and-play design that decouples structural learning from model architecture is a clean engineering contribution, and simultaneously addressing edge deficiency and semantic redundancy is well-motivated. The consistent improvements across four self-supervised backbones and five datasets validate generality. The core techniques — cosine similarity expansion, co-occurrence edge inference, MLP-based edge gating — are standard building blocks assembled competently rather than novel methods.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Heterogeneous information networks (HINs) model real-world data with multiple node and edge types, yet existing heterogeneous graph neural networks (HGNNs) — both semi-supervised and self-supervised — assume fixed graph topologies and ignore structural noise inherent in raw graphs. The paper identifies two specific failure modes in HINs: (1) edge deficiency, where latent beneficial connections are absent (e.g., cold-start users with sparse interactions), and (2) semantic redundancy, where multi-relational edges dilute discriminative signal (e.g., purchases of popular items obscuring user preferences). Prior structure learning methods address only homogeneous (single-relation) graphs or, for heterogeneous graphs, remain tightly coupled to specific downstream tasks and models, preventing reuse. Methodology: The paper proposes HSADP (Heterogeneous graph Structure ADaPter), a plug-and-play dual-path module that can be inserted into any heterogeneous graph pre-training framework without architectural changes. The first path, the Homogeneous Subgraph Enhancer (HoSE), decomposes the heterogeneous graph into semantic homogeneous subgraphs via predefined meta-paths and then applies two complementary homophily-boosting mechanisms: (a) self-feature similarity neighborhood expansion — connecting same-type node pairs whose cosine similarity exceeds threshold η, and (b) multi-relational propagation — inferring new target-node edges by identifying target nodes that share similar non-target neighbors with sufficient co-occurrence frequency (threshold γ). The two expanded structures are fused with the original via learned attention weights and top-K sparsification. The second path, the Heterogeneous Edge Discriminator (HeED), processes each existing edge by concatenating the embeddings of its two endpoint nodes with a learnable relation embedding, passing them through a relation-specific MLP to produce an edge necessity probability, which dynamically gates the original edges. Both modules are trained end-to-end jointly with the pre-training objective of the host model. Feature spaces are aligned across node types via per-type MLPs before either path. The iterative update allows refined heterogeneous edge sets to feed back into the homogeneous subgraph construction. Key Results: HSADP is evaluated on five heterogeneous graph benchmarks (ACM, DBLP, Freebase, Yelp, AMiner) under 10-shot and 20-shot node classification. When plugged into four self-supervised baselines (HeCo, HGCML, HGMAE, HERO), it yields consistent improvements across nearly all dataset–metric combinations. Representative gains: on ACM 20-shot, HERO improves from 87.68 Ma-F1 to 91.23 (+3.55); on DBLP 20-shot, HERO improves from 91.35 to 93.48 (+2.13 Ma-F1) and Mi-F1 from 92.38 to 94.38; on Freebase 10-shot, HGMAE improves Ma-F1 from 47.33 to 52.64 (+5.31). Improvements are larger in the 10-shot regime, indicating the adapter is especially helpful under label scarcity. The adapter also improves semi-supervised HAN and HGT baselines (Table 3), demonstrating generality beyond self-supervised settings. HSADP consistently outperforms the specialized heterogeneous structure learning baseline HGSL, which requires label supervision. Ablations confirm both HoSE and HeED contribute independently, with their combination yielding the best performance. Strengths: - Clean plug-and-play design that decouples structural learning from model architecture, enabling application across contrastive, generative, and semi-supervised HGNNs without model-specific redesign. - Simultaneously addresses both structural pathologies (edge deficiency and semantic redundancy) with complementary mechanisms, whereas all prior heterogeneous GSL methods handle at most one. - Empirical validation is thorough: five datasets spanning different domains, 10- and 20-shot regimes, three metrics (Ma-F1, Mi-F1, AUC), four self-supervised backbones, and ablations for each component plus sensitivity analysis for three key hyperparameters. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method requires pre-specified meta-paths, which depend on domain knowledge and may be non-trivial to define for unfamiliar HINs; meta-path quality likely has significant impact on performance but is not systematically analyzed. - Hyperparameter sensitivity is non-trivial: similarity thresholds η and γ require careful tuning and the paper shows performance degrades noticeably for suboptimal values, raising questions about robustness across new datasets without extensive search. - Scalability is not analyzed — the pairwise cosine similarity computation and multi-relational propagation are potentially O(|V|²) in the worst case, and no runtime or memory measurements are reported for the large Freebase graph (180K nodes, 1M+ edges, 36 relation types).
Recommended
#148 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Accelerating personalization signal learning via synthetic data

amazon.science · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The surprising empirical finding that taxonomy-guided synthetic data strictly outperforms real de-identified data for personal knowledge inference is a meaningful contribution with clear practical value for privacy-constrained production systems. However, the extremely short format omits critical reproducibility details, and the absence of ablations makes it impossible to identify which components drive the gains.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Personalized AI assistants (e.g., Alexa) require models that can infer user interests and attributes from interaction histories — a task called Personal Knowledge Inference (PKI). Large-scale supervised training for this task is bottlenecked by privacy constraints that limit access to real user data, data sparsity, and poor topical diversity in production logs. The paper addresses this by replacing real training data with synthetic data generated entirely from structured personas, enabling privacy-safe, scalable training without relying on de-identified customer interactions. Methodology: The framework constructs Comprehensive Synthetic Personas (CSPs) — structured user profiles with 130 attributes organized into a seven-tier hierarchical ontology (interests, background, behavior, etc.). Persona generation uses taxonomy-guided knowledge enrichment: an interest taxonomy of 1,800+ nodes across 25+ categories is sampled via hybrid random/depth-stratified leaf selection to produce semantic anchor paths, which then ground an LLM's persona generation. From each persona, multi-turn dialogues are synthesized using a role-playing LLM conditioned on the persona and few-shot exemplars of real de-identified assistant interactions, with controlled variation across affective dimensions (intensity and polarity) calibrated to production statistics. A separate LLM extractor then annotates PKIs from each dialogue, producing type, polarity, confidence, and reasoning traces. The full dataset (dialogues + PKIs + CoT traces) is used for Chain-of-Thought instruction fine-tuning of a <10B-parameter LLM, tested with both LoRA and full supervised fine-tuning (SFT). Evaluation uses LLM-as-a-Judge (LLMaaJ) for precision (relevance + sensibility) and a QA-based recall metric (Sentence-BERT cosine similarity against gold answers), validated against human annotation (Cohen's κ = 0.62). Key Results: All models are evaluated on a real de-identified test set relative to a >20B zero-shot baseline. Synthetic Full SFT achieves +8.0% precision and +13.0% recall (Table 1), versus +3.0% / +4.9% for the real-data Full SFT counterpart. Averaged across fine-tuning strategies, synthetic data yields +5% precision and +8% recall gains over real-data training (Table 2). Synthetic data is also structurally richer: +2.2 avg. turns per dialog, +4.6K vocabulary diversity, +0.9 PKIs per dialog, and +31% interest category coverage relative to real data (Table 3). Human validation on 115 users confirms >85% acceptance of PKIs generated by the synthetic-trained model. Strengths: - Synthetic data strictly outperforms real de-identified data on a real test set across both LoRA and full SFT, which is a strong and surprising empirical result that challenges the default assumption that real data is always preferable. - The taxonomy-guided enrichment over 1,800 nodes is a principled mechanism for diversity control, addressing long-tail topic coverage that real logs chronically underrepresent. - The framework is fully privacy-preserving with no real user data required at training time, which is a genuine practical contribution for production deployment at scale. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is extremely short (5 content pages) and omits critical experimental details: the base LLM identity, dataset sizes (number of personas, dialogues, PKIs), and the specific LLMaaJ model used are all undisclosed, making replication or fair comparison impossible. - The recall metric relies on QA-based proxy measurement with Sentence-BERT similarity thresholds, and the paper acknowledges that 'correlation with human judgment is non-trivial' — the ground-truth user interest distribution is unobservable, so the recall numbers carry significant measurement uncertainty. - There is no ablation isolating the contribution of individual components (taxonomy enrichment vs. CoT distillation vs. affective dimension control), making it unclear which design choices are load-bearing for the observed gains.
Search Results
#149 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Generative AI for Interpretable Satisficing Solution Design in Manufacturing

asmedigitalcollection.asme.org · genai manufacturing - new results
Why this rank: The framework integrates multiple existing GenAI components (GPT-5, fine-tuned LLaMA, knowledge graphs) into a coherent pipeline for multi-objective manufacturing design, and the quantitative validation gate on LLM-generated knowledge statements is a genuinely useful safeguard. However, the 'generative' framing is misleading—the system re-queries a fixed 169-scenario library rather than generating new solutions, and the core methodology is an application-level assembly of established LLM prompting, LoRA fine-tuning, and knowledge graph techniques to a known formalism (cDSP).
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-objective manufacturing design problems require navigating high-dimensional trade-off spaces with conflicting goals, yet existing tools fail along three axes: scalability (visualization and search break down beyond 3 objectives), interpretability (Pareto-front methods produce black-box solution sets without narrative explanation), and adaptivity (static one-shot optimization cannot respond to evolving designer preferences in real time). The paper targets this triple gap by proposing a GenAI-augmented decision-support framework grounded in the Compromise Decision Support Problem (cDSP) formalism, specifically its robust coupled variant (rc-cDSP), to find satisficing—rather than Pareto-optimal—solutions that meet all goals to an acceptable level simultaneously. Methodology: The framework is structured as a three-step pipeline applied to a pre-computed scenario library. Step 1 (Knowledge Creation): 169 rc-cDSP scenarios—each encoding two-level weight allocations, decision variables, and goal outcomes—are serialized as structured JSON and fed to GPT-5 via few-shot prompting to extract declarative knowledge statements (synergies, conflicts, thresholds). Candidate statements are retained only if they pass quantitative validation (Pearson correlation for relationship claims, range comparison for sensitivity claims, conditional frequency for threshold claims). Validated statements are then converted into a schema-constrained knowledge graph using a LoRA fine-tuned LLaMA-3.1-8B model (rank r=32, AdamW, lr=2×10⁻⁴, temperature=0), producing typed subject–relation–object triples with quantitative attributes. Step 2 (Knowledge Exploration): Designers query the knowledge graph and underlying scenario database in natural language; the KG first resolves intent semantically before triggering numerical retrieval. Concurrently, embedding-based clustering groups the 169 scenarios into archetypes with LLM-generated narrative labels. Step 3 (Adaptable Solution-Space Generation): Designers iteratively adjust satisficing thresholds; the LLM re-queries the fixed scenario library, reports feasibility status, explains conflict drivers, and proposes targeted threshold relaxations to restore a non-empty feasible set. The test problem is the Hot Rod Rolling (HRR) steel manufacturing process, with 6 goals across 2 hierarchical design levels (materials and cooling process). Key Results: Applied to the 169-scenario HRR dataset, the LLM extracted 19 validated knowledge statements capturing global trade-off structure—e.g., strong inverse relationship between EMI_YS and DCI_TS (r=−0.956), strong synergy between DCI_TS and DCI_HV (r=+0.987), and a threshold effect where EMI_YS falls below 1.0 in 61.5% of scenarios with w_YS<0.20. One candidate statement (DCI_HV strongly correlated with EMI_YS) was correctly rejected by the quantitative filter (r=−0.971, directionality inconsistent with claim). Interactive queries correctly identified Scenario #55 (DCI_TS=6.82, EMI_YS=1.078) as the best compromise and Scenario #93 (DCI_Dα=3.17, DCI_S0=11.54) for joint microstructure optimization. Under strict six-goal thresholds the system returned an empty feasible set, diagnosed the EMI_YS vs. DCI_TS/HV conflict, and recommended relaxing DCI_TS from 6.0 to 3.5 and DCI_HV from 6.0 to 5.0, after which scenarios 40, 42, and 93 became feasible. The clustering step yielded two archetypes: a 'High Performance' cluster (high DCI_TS/HV, low EMI_YS) and a 'Microstructure-Focused' cluster (extreme Level-2 variation). The framework's feasibility assessments and scenario rankings were found to be consistent with those produced by the iSOM baseline used in the source study. Strengths: - The quantitative validation gate on LLM-generated knowledge statements is a concrete, well-designed safeguard against hallucination: only statements passing correlation, range, or conditional-frequency tests enter the knowledge graph, with unsupported statements explicitly discarded. - The two-phase offline/online architecture is practically sound: expensive knowledge extraction runs once over the scenario library, while interactive queries operate on the pre-built graph at low latency, making the approach tractable for iterative design sessions. - The comparison against the iSOM baseline on an identical dataset provides a meaningful consistency check, showing the GenAI framework reproduces established visualization findings while adding dynamic, natural-language interaction that iSOM cannot provide. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The 'adaptivity' demonstrated is narrowly scoped: the system re-queries a fixed 169-scenario library rather than generating new rc-cDSP evaluations, so it cannot discover solutions outside the pre-computed design space; the authors acknowledge extending to genuine generative sampling is future work. - The fine-tuning dataset for the knowledge-graph builder consists of only 100 synthetic input-output pairs manually constructed within the HRR vocabulary—too small and too domain-specific to credibly claim that the schema-constrained extraction generalizes to other manufacturing problems without re-tuning. - There are no user studies or quantitative metrics assessing whether the LLM narratives accurately represent the statistical structure of the clustered scenarios; interpretability claims rest entirely on qualitative consistency with iSOM, not on human-centered evaluation of designer outcomes or decision quality.
Search Results
#150 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Learning to Parse and Reconstruct: Bidirectional Modeling of Question-to-Program Mapping

ojs.aaai.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: BiPaR applies the well-established cycle-consistency principle (familiar from back-translation and CycleGAN) to neuro-symbolic program parsing, yielding strong low-data improvements. The idea is sound but not surprising, and the narrow baseline comparison (vanilla Transformer only) and lack of end-to-end VQA evaluation limit the demonstrated contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: In neuro-symbolic visual question answering, the core task is translating natural language questions into executable programs that a symbolic reasoning engine can run over visual scene representations. Existing methods treat this exclusively as a forward (question→program) translation problem, ignoring the reverse direction. This one-sided view wastes a potentially rich training signal: if a generated program is correct, it should allow faithful reconstruction of the original question; if it is wrong, reconstruction will fail noisily. The paper exploits this asymmetry to improve program generation quality and reduce annotation cost. Methodology: The paper proposes BiPaR (Bidirectional Parsing and Reconstruction), a Transformer-based framework that jointly trains a forward program parser g: Q→P and a backward question reconstructor h: P→Q̂, with the composition h∘g approximating the identity on the question space. Two architectural variants are offered: BiPaR-Full uses encoder-decoder Transformers for both modules; BiPaR-DOnly replaces the reconstruction module's encoder with a lightweight decoder-only Transformer. The joint training objective is a convex combination of cross-entropy losses for program parsing (L_PP) and question reconstruction (L_QR), with weight λ=0.7 found optimal.
