For semi-supervised learning, unlabeled questions are processed by a pre-trained parser to generate pseudo-programs, which are then held fixed while training the reconstruction head on those unlabeled inputs. A theoretical analysis using a first-order Taylor expansion of h around the ground-truth program P* shows that reconstruction variance is proportional to prediction variance scaled by ‖J_h‖²_F, meaning early-training errors are amplified into stronger gradient signals, explaining the observed faster convergence. Key Results: On CLEVR with 10K labeled examples, BiPaR-Full reaches sequence-level accuracy of 0.676 vs. 0.093 for the Transformer baseline—a 7× improvement. At 30K samples, BiPaR achieves ~0.9 accuracy within 30 epochs while the baseline requires the full 100 epochs to reach that level. At 700K samples all methods converge to ~1.0. On a 700K-sample GQA subset, BiPaR-Full scores 0.832 vs. 0.818 for the Transformer at convergence, with again substantially faster convergence at smaller sizes (e.g., 0.579 vs. 0.095 at 10K). In the semi-supervised setting with 10K labeled + 50K unlabeled questions, BiPaR-Full improves over the supervised-only baseline by +37.9% relative on CLEVR (0.676→0.932) and +15.9% on GQA (0.579→0.671). BiPaR-DOnly nearly matches BiPaR-Full across all conditions, validating the lightweight variant. Strengths: - The bidirectional training signal is conceptually clean and practically impactful: the reconstruction loss provides amplified error feedback early in training when predictions are poor, yielding dramatic convergence speedups at low data regimes. - The semi-supervised extension is natural and effective—unlabeled questions (no program annotations required) contribute meaningfully, with 50K unlabeled examples nearly closing the gap to much larger supervised baselines. - The theoretical analysis via Taylor expansion of the reconstructor's Jacobian gives principled intuition for why the method works, grounding the empirical observations. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The baseline comparison is narrow: only a vanilla Transformer seq2seq model is used. No comparison to more competitive recent methods (e.g., TMN, NeSyCoCo, ProTo, or LLM-based approaches like VISPROG) is provided, making it hard to assess where BiPaR stands in the broader field. - Experiments are restricted to program parsing accuracy (sequence- and token-level), not end-to-end VQA accuracy. Since program accuracy and answer accuracy can diverge (e.g., due to executor robustness), the practical downstream impact is unverified. - The GQA experiments use a sampled subset and are not reported on the full benchmark, limiting comparability with published results. The paper also does not test on harder out-of-distribution or compositional generalization splits, where neuro-symbolic methods are most relevant.
Search Results
#151 — Moderately Innovative (52)

WildWorld: A Large-Scale Dataset for Dynamic World Modeling with Actions and Explicit State toward Generative ARPG (65 ▲ )

Zhen Li, Zian Meng, Shuwei Shi, Wenshuo Peng, Yuwei Wu, Bo Zheng, Chuanhao Li, Kaipeng Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Mar 2026
Why this rank: WildWorld's explicit per-frame state annotations at 108M-frame scale are a genuine differentiator from prior game-based datasets, and the automated collection pipeline is impressively scalable. However, the contribution is primarily a dataset locked to a single commercial game with unclear transferability, the state alignment scores remain low across all baselines, and the long-tailed action distribution limits practical utility for rare but important interactions.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing video world model datasets fail to support learning structured, action-conditioned dynamics because (1) their action spaces are small and semantically shallow (e.g., camera rotations, simple movements), and (2) actions are directly coupled to pixel-level visual changes rather than mediated by underlying latent states. This entanglement prevents models from learning stable, interpretable world dynamics and leads to error accumulation in long-horizon generation. The gap is the absence of large-scale data with rich, semantically meaningful actions and explicit per-frame state annotations that go beyond what can be inferred from pixels alone. Methodology: WildWorld is a dataset automatically collected from the photorealistic AAA action RPG Monster Hunter: Wilds using a custom data acquisition platform. The platform hooks into the game engine to record per-frame ground-truth state (character/monster position, rotation, velocity, animation IDs, health, stamina), skeletal poses for all entities, camera intrinsics/extrinsics, and depth maps alongside RGB. An automated gameplay pipeline (behavior-tree NPC AI + programmatic quest selection) drives data collection with minimal human input, yielding diverse combat scenarios across 5 maps, 29 monster species, and 4 weapon types. After timestamp-aligned multi-stream recording, a multi-dimensional filtering pipeline removes short clips (<81 frames), temporal discontinuities (frame gap >1.5× target interval), extreme luminance, camera/character occlusions, and ambiguous first-frame overlaps. Hierarchical captions are generated at two levels: action-sequence level using Qwen3-VL-235B with action GT as context, and sample level using Gemini 3 Flash summarization. The resulting WildWorld contains 108M frames with 119 annotation columns per frame and 5,960 unique character action triplets. The companion benchmark WildBench evaluates models on four axes: Video Quality (VBench metrics: Motion Smoothness, Dynamic Degree, Aesthetic Quality, Image Quality), Camera Control (ATE/RPE against GT camera trajectories estimated via ViPE), Action Following (Gemini 3 Flash binary agreement between generated and GT action segments, 85% human agreement), and State Alignment (TAPNext keypoint tracking vs. GT skeleton projections at 4/8/16/32-pixel thresholds). Three baseline model families are fine-tuned on WildWorld: CamCtrl (camera-conditioned, fine-tuned Wan2.2-Fun-5B-Control-Camera), SkelCtrl (skeleton video-conditioned), and StateCtrl/StateCtrl-AR (structured state embeddings via transformer injected into DiT intermediate layers, with an autoregressive state predictor for inference-time rollout). Key Results: All fine-tuned models improve over the Wan2.2-TI2V-5B baseline on interaction metrics. CamCtrl reduces ATE from 4.63 to 2.02 and RPE from 0.18 to 0.13. SkelCtrl achieves the highest Action Following (92.81 vs. 53.77 baseline) and State Alignment (22.03 vs. 11.29), roughly doubling both metrics, but at the cost of lower Aesthetic Quality (47.92 vs. 50.81) and Image Quality (62.43 vs. 65.62). StateCtrl achieves the best camera control (ATE 0.94, RPE 0.07) and competitive Action Following (85.66) with preserved image quality (67.78 IQ), while StateCtrl-AR degrades Action Following to 74.66 due to autoregressive error accumulation. VBench Motion Smoothness and Dynamic Degree are saturated (>95%) across all methods, confirming these metrics are insufficient to differentiate interactive world model quality—the key contribution of WildBench's action/state metrics. Strengths: - The explicit state annotation (not implicit latent) is a genuine differentiator from all prior game-based datasets; it enables direct, quantitative evaluation of state alignment that pixel-only datasets cannot support. - The automated data collection pipeline is scalable and reproducible, achieving 108M frames with 119 annotation columns with minimal human labeling cost. - WildBench's Action Following metric achieves 85% human agreement, providing a credible automated evaluation signal that exposes model failure modes invisible to standard VBench metrics. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The dataset is locked to a single commercial game (Monster Hunter: Wilds), raising domain specificity concerns: action distributions, physics, and visual style are game-engine artifacts that may not transfer to real-world or other game domains. - The action distribution is highly long-tailed (top-150 action IDs cover only 58.49% of samples), meaning rare but potentially important actions are severely underrepresented; models trained on this data will inherit this imbalance. - State Alignment scores remain low across all methods (best: 22.03 on SkelCtrl out of 100), and the metric conflates tracking failures with genuine generation failures—the 43.23% coordinate accuracy on GT videos sets a ceiling that reveals fundamental tracking limitations rather than purely model shortcomings.
Recommended 2603.23497 PDF
#152 — Moderately Innovative (52)

MACRO: Advancing Multi-Reference Image Generation with Structured Long-Context Data (26 ▲ )

Zhekai Chen, Yuqing Wang, Manyuan Zhang, Xihui Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 Mar 2026
Why this rank: MACRO is primarily a well-engineered dataset and benchmark contribution rather than a methodological advance — the core insight is that structured multi-reference training data across four complementary task dimensions improves multi-reference generation. The data construction pipelines are carefully designed with multi-stage VLM filtering, but the approach relies on knowledge distillation from closed-source models and standard fine-tuning without architectural innovation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-reference image generation — producing a coherent output conditioned on many input images simultaneously — is critical for real-world applications such as multi-subject composition, narrative illustration, novel view synthesis, and video keyframe prediction. Current open-source models (e.g., Bagel, OmniGen2) suffer severe performance degradation beyond 3–5 input references, while closed-source systems (e.g., GPT-Image-1.5, Nano Banana Pro) handle up to 10 references far more capably. The root cause identified is a fundamental data bottleneck: existing training datasets are dominated by single- or few-reference pairs and provide no structured supervision for learning dense inter-reference dependencies such as spatial consistency, temporal coherence, or multi-identity composition. A secondary gap is the lack of standardized evaluation protocols that test models at varying input scales and task types. Methodology: The paper introduces MacroData, a 400K-sample training dataset structured across four complementary dimensions: (1) Customization — composing multiple referents (humans, objects, scenes, clothing, styles) into coherent scenes, with target images generated via Nano Banana Pro and filtered via bidirectional VLM consistency checks; (2) Illustration — generating illustrative images for interleaved narrative contexts, with anchor image selection and quality scoring via Gemini-3-Flash and Gemini-3-Pro; (3) Spatial — synthesizing novel viewpoints from multi-view captures of 3D objects (Objaverse) and indoor/outdoor panoramas; (4) Temporal — predicting future keyframes from video sequences segmented via shot-boundary detection and DINOv2 similarity. Each subset contains 100K samples with 1–10 input images, averaging 5.44 references per sample. The paper also proposes MacroBench, a 4,000-sample evaluation benchmark with task-specific LLM-as-Judge metrics (Gemini-3-Flash as judge, validated against GPT-4.1 and human annotators at Pearson r=0.821). Experiments fine-tune three open-source models — Bagel, OmniGen2, and Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 — on MacroData. Ablation studies analyze cross-task co-training synergies, data ratio strategies for progressive vs. non-progressive tasks, and data scaling behavior. Additional exploration covers three token selection strategies (block-wise, image-wise, text-aligned) for managing long-context token sequences, and two alternative strategies (think-before-generation and collage preprocessing) that prove ineffective. Key Results: Fine-tuning Bagel on MacroData improves its MacroBench average score from 3.03 (base) to 5.71, surpassing all open-source baselines and ranking third overall behind only GPT-Image-1.5 (7.50) and Nano Banana Pro (7.12). MacroData consistently outperforms alternative fine-tuning datasets (Echo4o, MICo, OpenSubject) across all tasks under identical architectures. For Customization with 6–10 inputs, fine-tuned Bagel scores 7.12 vs. 2.53 for base Bagel and approaches Nano Banana Pro (7.60); for Spatial tasks it surpasses Nano Banana Pro (3.48 vs. 3.20). Cross-task co-training provides consistent synergistic benefits: the full multi-task model uniformly outperforms single-task-trained models. Upweighting long-context samples (ratio 2:2:3:3 across input-count groups) significantly improves high-input performance on progressive tasks without degrading low-input performance. Data scaling shows consistent gains up to 20K samples with diminishing returns beyond 10K. Among token selection strategies, text-aligned selection at 30–50% retention achieves the best efficiency-performance tradeoff. Collage preprocessing and think-before-generation both underperform baselines. Strengths: - The dataset construction pipelines are carefully engineered: they combine knowledge distillation from strong closed-source models with real-world corpora and apply multi-stage VLM filtering, yielding high-quality structured data well beyond existing alternatives in both diversity and input scale. - The four-task design is principled: Customization, Illustration, Spatial, and Temporal collectively span the key inter-reference dependency types (identity, narrative, geometric, temporal), and cross-task co-training is empirically validated to provide synergistic benefits. - MacroBench provides a substantially more rigorous evaluation protocol than prior benchmarks, covering all four task types and systematically varying input scale from 1–10 images, with a carefully validated judge model. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The gains on OmniContext (a customization-only benchmark) are less impressive than on MacroBench: Bagel + Ours All (8.00) actually underperforms Bagel + Ours Customization (8.26) and even some prior methods on standard customization tasks, suggesting that multi-task training may dilute single-task customization quality. - The Spatial task remains the lowest-scoring dimension for all models including closed-source ones (GPT-Image-1.5: 3.32/4.24 for 1–5/6–10 inputs), revealing that the underlying generative architectures — not just data — are fundamentally limited for 3D-aware view synthesis, and no architectural innovations are proposed to address this. - The token selection strategies evaluated are relatively heuristic (block-wise retention, image-wise pruning, text-aligned attention weighting) and provide only modest gains over the full-context baseline; the paper acknowledges this as exploratory rather than a solved efficiency solution.
Recommended 2603.25319 PDF
#153 — Moderately Innovative (52)

How Confident Is the First Token? An Uncertainty-Calibrated Prompt Optimization Framework for Large Language Model Classification and Understanding

Wei Chen, Guoyang Ju, Yuanyuan Qi · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The focal-loss-inspired prior modulation of first-token uncertainty is a clean and well-motivated idea, and the adaptive RAG gating delivers practical compute savings. However, the core contribution—adjusting entropy by label frequency—is a relatively straightforward calibration fix rather than a fundamental methodological advance, and the restriction to logit-accessible models limits real-world applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: In-context learning (ICL) for LLM classification tasks is highly sensitive to prompt design, yet existing uncertainty measures (primarily entropy-based) provide poor guidance for prompt optimization. Standard entropy ignores label prior distributions from pretraining, making it unable to distinguish "spurious confidence" on high-frequency classes (dominated by pretraining statistics) from "true certainty" driven by contextual understanding. Additionally, always-on retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) introduces semantic noise for easy samples and incurs unnecessary compute. The gap is a calibrated, prior-aware uncertainty metric that can simultaneously guide exemplar selection and adaptively gate RAG retrieval. Methodology: The paper proposes two contributions: (1) Log-Scale Focal Uncertainty (LSFU), a first-token-based uncertainty metric defined as LSFU(x,y) = log10(H(P_top-k) · (1 − P_prior(y))² + ε), where Shannon entropy over the top-50 first-token logits is modulated by a focal-loss-inspired prior term. High-frequency classes (high P_prior) have their uncertainty suppressed toward zero, while low-frequency classes retain full uncertainty signal, analogous to how focal loss down-weights easy examples in object detection. (2) UCPOF (Uncertainty-Calibrated Prompt Optimization Framework), a two-stage system: an offline phase selects "gold shot" exemplars (lowest LSFU, correctly predicted) for a static prompt and calibrates a gating threshold T at 90% error-coverage on a held-out set; an online phase first runs static-prompt inference, computes LSFU on the first token, and only triggers RAG retrieval and a reflective prompt (containing similar retrieved examples plus an explicit re-evaluation instruction) when LSFU exceeds T. Retrieval uses all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings with cosine similarity. Experiments use Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct as the primary model, with Llama-3-8B and Qwen2.5-14B for cross-model validation, across six datasets (ACE, CASIE, AgNews, SST5, CB, R8). Key Results: UCPOF achieves 78.09% average accuracy versus 72.06% for the few-shot baseline (+6.03%) and 72.34% for always-on Full RAG (+5.75%), while reducing the average RAG trigger rate by 50.66% (49.34% vs. 100%). The largest gain is on ACE (+11.01% over baseline), a long-tail event extraction task. UCPOF also avoids the catastrophic failure of Full RAG on CB (48.21% for Full RAG vs. 82.14% for UCPOF). Gold Shot selection alone improves average accuracy by +1.75% over random selection (73.81% vs. 72.06%). Ablations confirm that removing P_prior degrades average AUC from 0.7555 to 0.7429, and removing Gold Shot lowers UCPOF accuracy from 78.09% to 75.89%. ECE and Brier score both decrease when P_prior is included. Cross-model results show consistent UCPOF gains of 2.17–3.56% over baselines across Llama-3-8B and Qwen2.5-14B. Strengths: - The focal-loss analogy for prior modulation is conceptually clean and well-motivated: suppressing high-frequency pseudo-uncertainty while amplifying long-tail risk is precisely the calibration failure that standard entropy exhibits, and the fix is computationally free. - Adaptive RAG gating is practically valuable: the 90% error-coverage threshold selection via a cost–benefit elbow analysis is principled and avoids both over-triggering (noise injection) and under-triggering (missed corrections), demonstrated concretely by the CB dataset failure of Full RAG. - Comprehensive ablations isolate contributions of each component (P_prior, Gold Shot, dynamic correction) and the cross-model evaluation on three architectures/sizes provides meaningful generalization evidence. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The prior probability P_prior is estimated from the training set label frequencies, which conflates the model's pretraining priors with the dataset's empirical class distribution—these can diverge substantially, and the paper provides no analysis of this mismatch or how to handle it when labeled data is scarce. - The gating threshold T is derived under an IID assumption (same domain distribution between calibration and test sets), but this is rarely satisfied in deployment; the paper acknowledges this limitation but offers no mechanism for threshold adaptation under distribution shift beyond noting the 90% coverage provides a safety margin. - LSFU requires access to first-token logits, restricting applicability to white-box or logit-accessible models; no discussion of how to extend to black-box APIs is provided despite this being a practical constraint for many real deployments.
Search Results 2603.18009 PDF
#154 — Moderately Innovative (52)

DA-DFGAS: Differentiable Federated Graph Neural Architecture Search with Distribution-Aware Attentive Aggregation

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: DA-DFGAS competently unifies two active research threads — differentiable NAS and distribution-aware federated aggregation — into a single framework, and the multiplicative aggregation weight design is principled. The contribution is primarily integrative rather than conceptually novel, as each component (DARTS-style search, attention-based client weighting, KL-divergence alignment, proximal regularization) is drawn from existing work, and the resulting accuracy gains are modest (0.5–2.5%).
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Real-world graph data is distributed across edge devices under strict privacy regulations, preventing centralized training. Federated Graph Neural Networks (FedGNNs) must contend with Non-IID data — clients holding subgraphs from different domains with divergent statistical distributions. Existing FedGNN frameworks have two core failure modes: (1) naive aggregation strategies (e.g., FedAvg) that treat all client updates equally despite distributional heterogeneity, and (2) enforcing a single global architecture across all clients, which cannot adapt to the distinct structural and feature characteristics of individual local graphs. Prior NAS-for-GNN methods assume centralized data and are inapplicable in federated settings. Methodology: DA-DFGAS combines differentiable Neural Architecture Search (NAS) with a distribution-aware federated aggregation mechanism. The search space is organized as a directed tree topology with three functional layers — feature filtering (coarse-grained node-wise scaling Fs and fine-grained feature-wise scaling Fd), neighborhood aggregation (sum/max/mean), and activation functions — plus Zero and Identity skip-connection operators. Architecture parameters are continuous-relaxed via Softmax and jointly optimized with weights via gradient descent (DARTS-style). At discretization, a hierarchical pruning strategy retains top edges per layer, with an adaptive path preservation rule for the middle layer that keeps two paths when their probability gap is below threshold δ, promoting feature diversity. For aggregation, each client computes a self-attention matrix over its local features, compressed via Frobenius norm and multiplied by its local validation accuracy to form a reliability weight Wi. Distribution alignment is measured via KL divergence between each client's predicted class distribution Pi and an estimated global reference Pglobal; clients closer to the global distribution receive higher aggregation weight via a bounded similarity score. A temporal smoothing coefficient γt (monotonically increasing toward 1) gradually freezes the global architecture to ensure convergence. System heterogeneity is addressed via FedNova-style gradient normalization to correct for varying numbers of local update steps. Local optimization uses a proximal bi-level objective: architecture parameters updated on validation loss, weights updated on training loss with an L2 proximity term to the global model to suppress client drift. Key Results: Evaluated on eight datasets (Cora, CiteSeer, DBLP, ACM, SBM-PATTERN, SBM-CLUSTER, CIFAR10, MNIST), DA-DFGAS outperforms 12+ baselines. Against centralized GNNs: 98.15% vs. GatedGCN's 97.34% on MNIST; 81.21% vs. GatedGCN's 80.13% on Cora; 61.37% vs. GatedGCN's 60.40% on SBM-CLUSTER. Against FedGNN baselines: 0.5–2.5% gains across all five datasets in Table 5, with the largest gains on heterogeneous datasets (DBLP: 59.36% vs. FGSSL's 58.64%; SBM-CLUSTER: 60.56% vs. FedSSP's 59.81%). Against FedGNAS baselines (Table 4), DA-DFGAS is both more accurate and 30–40% faster in search time (1.2h vs. FL-BlockQNN's 1.8h on SBM-PATTERN) with fewer parameters (0.37M vs. 0.43M). Ablation confirms all three components contribute: removing distribution-aware aggregation drops DBLP accuracy from 59.36% to 58.67%; removing proximal regularization increases required communication rounds from 97 to 172 on DBLP. Strengths: - The combination of differentiable NAS with distribution-aware aggregation is well-motivated and addresses two distinct failure modes of existing FedGNNs simultaneously rather than patching one with the other. - The aggregation weight design is principled: it multiplicatively combines a self-attention-based feature quality measure, a validation accuracy correction factor, and a KL-divergence-based distribution alignment score, giving richer client importance signals than similarity-only or volume-only baselines. - The temporal smoothing mechanism for architecture convergence (Eq. 11–12) is an elegant solution to the tension between early exploration and late-stage stability, and is specifically adapted to the federated NAS setting. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The Transformer projection parameters {Qi, Ki, Vi} used for local distribution alignment are kept local as a privacy safeguard, but the predicted class distributions Pi themselves are uploaded to the server — this leaks label-distribution information and may violate privacy in sensitive settings (e.g., medical records). The paper does not quantify this privacy risk formally (e.g., no differential privacy analysis). - The evaluation uses Metis-based graph partitioning to simulate Non-IID splits, which produces a structured, controlled form of heterogeneity. Real federated graph scenarios (e.g., separate institutional knowledge graphs) may exhibit more severe or qualitatively different distribution shifts, and generalization to those settings is untested.
Search Results
#155 — Moderately Innovative (52)

CMiner: An Algorithm to Discover Frequent Structures in Conceptual Models

openproceedings.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: CMiner fills a genuine tooling gap by combining directed multigraph support, multi-hot labeling, within-graph frequency, and template-guided search—features no prior FSM algorithm offers together. The bitset-based NET/EET structures are a clean engineering contribution. However, the core pattern-growth approach closely follows established FSM paradigms (gSpan, Gaston), and the paper defers the critical question of whether discovered patterns are actually useful to modelers, limiting its conceptual contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Conceptual models (UML, BPMN, OntoUML, ArchiMate) are widely used to design information systems, and a significant research effort targets the automated discovery of recurring structural patterns within them. Existing Frequent Subgraph Mining (FSM) algorithms fall short in this domain for four concrete reasons: (1) they typically handle only undirected, single-labeled graphs, whereas conceptual models are directed multigraphs with multiple labels per node and edge; (2) they compute only cross-graph support, not within-graph frequency, which is needed to detect domain-specific modeling habits; (3) none support user-defined template constraints to guide and narrow the search; and (4) they often crash or exhaust memory on moderately large inputs, making them impractical for non-expert users on commodity hardware. Methodology: CMiner is a pattern-growth FSM algorithm that operates on directed multigraphs with multi-hot labeled nodes and edges. Starting from frequent single-node subgraphs, it iteratively extends candidates by first adding a new node (node extension) and then adding edges between existing nodes (edge extension). The key data structures are the Node Extension Table (NET) and Edge Extension Table (EET), which compactly encode, via bitset multi-hot encoding, all possible ways to extend a current subgraph based on its occurrences in the database. Bitwise AND operations efficiently check label subset relationships, avoiding redundant work. To prevent duplicate candidate generation, CMiner computes a canonical code for each subgraph using the BLISS graph automorphism tool, combined with a lexicographic ordering of node and edge labels. Edge extensions that produce subgraphs occurring in identical sets of database graphs are grouped and applied simultaneously, ensuring generated extensions are induced subgraphs. Optional parameters allow: seeding the search with user-defined template subgraphs (located via the companion MultiGraphMatch algorithm); bounding subgraph size; excluding specific labels; and enforcing a minimum within-graph frequency threshold. CMiner also supports retrieval of closed and maximal frequent subgraphs. Theoretically, the time complexity is O(|V|(MΔ)^{M−1} · (|E| + M² + T_can(M))) in the sparse case, where the exponential factor depends on maximum degree Δ rather than graph size, and space is dominated by the canonical code store at O(|V|(MΔ)^{M−1} · M² · ⌈(|T|+|L|)/w⌉). Key Results: Four experiments validate the four stated requirements. Test 1 confirms correctness: on synthetic directed multigraphs (20 graphs, up to 1,350 nodes and 2,250 edges), CMiner recovers a complex multi-labeled UML pattern with exact support=20 and within-graph frequency=200 in 10–14 seconds on a MacBook M2/8 GB. Test 2 demonstrates template-guided mining on OntoUML (94 graphs) and ArchiMate (100 graphs): the blinded run yields 1,378 and 2,750 solutions respectively, whereas fully-specified templates reduce this to 2 and 120 solutions, cutting runtime from 303 s to 0.28–25 s—a strong reduction in irrelevant output. Test 3 compares against gSpan (Python) and Gaston (C++) on reified versions of both datasets across support thresholds 10–50. Gaston (C++) is fastest at low graph counts, but both gSpan and Gaston overflow memory at support ≤ 20–25 on OntoUML; CMiner never crashes, remains within 214–3,728 MB, and at support=10 finds 163 solutions (non-reified) vs. 993 (reified) on OntoUML in 88 s and 8,675 s respectively. CMiner additionally avoids the 2× graph-size inflation of edge reification. Test 4 evaluates scalability on large synthetic databases derived from the LSQB social network benchmark (|DB| ∈ {10,50,100}, average edges ∈ {1k,3k,5k}): all 9 experiments hit a 30-minute timeout, but CMiner extracts hundreds of patterns before timeout and exhibits sub-linear memory scaling—a 10× increase in |DB| (10→100) yields only a 1.6× RAM increase (5.0→8.1 GB). Strengths: - Unique combination of features absent in any prior FSM tool: directed multigraphs with multi-labeled nodes/edges, simultaneous cross-graph support and within-graph frequency, and user-defined template seeding—directly addressing the practical needs of conceptual modeling researchers. - Compact bitset-based NET/EET structures with bitwise-AND label containment checks are an elegant engineering contribution that reduces redundant subgraph matching work. - Robust in practice: CMiner never crashes across all experiments (unlike gSpan and Gaston which overflow at lower support thresholds), and sub-linear memory scaling in database size makes it viable on standard laptops. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Execution time is substantially worse than Gaston (C++) in comparable settings, and on large dense graphs (Test 4) CMiner consistently fails to complete within 30 minutes; the template mechanism partially mitigates this but does not eliminate the fundamental scalability gap. - The comparison is weakened by an uneven playing field: Gaston operates on reified, numerically relabeled undirected graphs and is C++ vs. CMiner's Python, so the benchmarking does not isolate algorithmic efficiency from implementation quality. - The paper evaluates only structural frequency and explicitly defers the question of whether discovered frequent subgraphs constitute meaningful conceptual modeling patterns to future work; there is no end-to-end user study demonstrating that CMiner's output actually helps modelers find useful patterns.
Citations
#156 — Moderately Innovative (51)

HINPool: A Unified Heterogeneous Graph Pooling Framework for Accurate Molecular and Protein Property Prediction

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: HINPool's reframing of standard graph datasets as heterogeneous information networks by mapping existing node/edge attributes to explicit types is conceptually clean and requires no domain-specific metapath engineering. However, gains over strong homogeneous baselines like GIUNet are modest or absent on molecular datasets, and evaluation is restricted to five small benchmarks with no large-scale validation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Graph-level property prediction (e.g., molecular mutagenicity, protein function) is typically addressed with homogeneous graph pooling methods that treat all nodes as the same type. This discards structurally meaningful distinctions — atom types in molecules, secondary structural elements (SSEs) in proteins — that are encoded as node features rather than as explicit graph heterogeneity. Heterogeneous Graph Neural Networks (HGNNs) have been studied for node classification and link prediction, but their application to whole-graph classification via heterogeneous pooling remains largely unexplored. Existing heterogeneous graph pooling methods (UaG, MPMol) are either domain-specific or require extensive hand-crafted metapaths, limiting generalizability. Methodology: HINPool introduces a three-stage pipeline. First, a systematic HIN construction procedure converts standard homogeneous graph datasets into Heterogeneous Information Networks by mapping node attributes to explicit node types (atom types for molecules; helix/sheet/turn SSEs for proteins) and edge attributes to explicit edge types (bond types). Second, a Type-aware Heterogeneous Graph Pooling (THeGP) layer stacks three components: (i) a shared-feature-space HGNN encoder (SimpleHGN) that produces node embeddings while respecting type-specific linear projections and learnable edge-type embeddings; (ii) a Type-Aware Selector (TAS) that independently applies type-specific MLPs to score nodes, selects the top-K fraction within each type, and scales retained embeddings by their scores; and (iii) a Readout Aggregator (RA) that combines type-aware mean readouts (fused via concatenation, element-wise ops, or attention) with a global mixed-type mean readout into a layerwise graph embedding. Third, a cross-layer fusion function (attention over per-layer embeddings) merges representations across all N THeGP layers into a final graph embedding passed to a linear classifier. Overall complexity is O(n(log n + e)), dominated by Top-K selection and message passing. Key Results: Evaluated on five standard benchmarks (MUTAG, NCI1, NCI109, PROTEINS, ENZYMES) using a rigorous train/val/test (80/10/10) split with 5 runs — avoiding the overly optimistic 10-fold CV protocol common in prior work. HINPool achieves: MUTAG 100.0 AUROC / 97.7% Acc (perfect score); NCI1 87.5 AUROC / 80.3% Acc (best among all baselines, statistically significant at p<0.05 vs. second-best GIUNet at 85.4); PROTEINS 89.8 AUROC / 81.2% Acc (best); ENZYMES 92.8 AUROC / 79.3% Acc (best, p<0.001, +7 AUROC points over second-best GIUNet at 86.2). NCI109 is the one dataset where HINPool (86.2 AUROC) slightly trails GIUNet (86.5), attributed to sparse per-type node samples. Ablations confirm cross-layer fusion is the single most impactful component (removal causes -39% AUROC on MUTAG, -23% on ENZYMES), while TAS and type-aware readout each contribute meaningfully. The optimal configuration uses 3 THeGP layers with a constant pooling ratio of 0.9 per type. Strengths: - The reframing of standard homogeneous graph datasets as HINs is conceptually clean and practically impactful, requiring no domain-specific metapath engineering beyond mapping existing node/edge attributes to types. - Ablation study is thorough and isolates each component's contribution, including the nuanced comparison between type-aware Top-K and mixed Top-K selection. - Evaluation protocol is more rigorous than the dominant 10-fold CV approach in graph classification literature, reducing optimistic bias and enabling fair comparison via fixed splits. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Gains on molecular datasets (NCI1, NCI109, MUTAG) are modest or absent relative to strong homogeneous baselines like GIUNet, which already captures rich local structure; the heterogeneous view adds limited signal when node types correlate tightly with features already exploited by homogeneous models. - The framework is only validated on five small-to-medium datasets (max 4,127 graphs, avg. ~30 nodes); scalability and effectiveness on large-scale or structurally diverse graph corpora (e.g., OGB benchmarks) is not demonstrated. - The HGNN backbone is fixed to SimpleHGN in all reported experiments despite claims of modularity; the generalizability of the TAS/RA design across other HGNN encoders is asserted but not empirically validated.
Search Results
#157 — Moderately Innovative (51)

DRIVINGVQA: A Dataset for Interleaved Visual Chain-of-Thought in Real-World Driving Scenarios

aclanthology.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: DRIVINGVQA fills a real gap with expert-annotated multi-entity reasoning traces and the RIV-CoT retrieval mechanism is well-motivated. However, the dataset is small (3,931 samples), domain-narrow, and the accuracy gains from the full method are modest (+3.1 pts), with reasoning correctness remaining low at 25.4%.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-language models (VLMs) applying chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning tend to over-rely on textual cues and memorized knowledge rather than genuinely grounding their reasoning in visual content, leading to hallucinations and poor spatial reasoning. Existing visual reasoning datasets either use synthetic imagery, lack multi-entity grounding annotations, rely on LLM-generated or template-based explanations, or cover only single regions of interest per image. Real-world driving scenarios demand multi-entity perception, spatial reasoning, and rule-based logic simultaneously — a gap no existing dataset adequately fills. Methodology: The paper introduces DRIVINGVQA, a 3,931-sample visual QA dataset derived from French driving theory exams, each sample comprising a real driving scene image, multiple-choice questions, expert-written step-by-step explanations, and manually annotated bounding boxes for 5,657 relevant entities (256 unique labels, avg. 1.4 per image). Dataset construction follows a three-step pipeline: (1) scraping and filtering real exam problems, retaining only visually-grounded samples (GPT-4o filter with ~0.95 inter-annotator agreement), then translating from French to English; (2) semi-automated entity extraction using GPT-4o-mini for label identification and GroundingDINO for localization, followed by human expert correction; (3) generating interleaved explanations where entity bounding-box coordinates are embedded inline in the reasoning text via GPT-4o few-shot prompting. The core training approach, Retrieval-based Interleaved Visual CoT (RIV-CoT), fine-tunes LLaVA-OneVision-7B (SigLIP encoder + 2-layer MLP adapter + Qwen2-7B) using a multi-turn autoregressive objective. At inference time, generation pauses each time the model predicts a bounding box, retrieves the corresponding 50%-expanded image crop from the input, encodes it via the vision encoder, and injects the crop tokens into context before continuing. This iterates until the final answer is produced. Evaluation uses both an exam score (exact match over all correct answers) and an LLM-as-a-judge reasoning correctness metric (GPT-4o-mini, validated at F1=0.83 against human annotations). Key Results: Zero-shot evaluation shows state-of-the-art models struggle: LLaVA-OV-7B achieves 33.5%, GPT-4o 59.1%, and OpenAI o3 67.0%, all well below the human baseline of 79.6%. Removing image input from GPT-4o drops performance by 26 pts (to 33.1%), confirming visual grounding is necessary. In supervised fine-tuning experiments on LLaVA-OV-7B, the progressive addition of entity information yields monotonic gains: standard CoT reaches 56.2% exam score; adding entity labels gives +0.8 pts; adding bounding boxes gives +1.5 pts over CoT; adding visual crops gives 58.4%; and the full RIV-CoT (interleaved entities + crops) achieves 59.3% — a +3.1 pt gain over vanilla CoT. Reasoning correctness improves from 20.8% (CoT) to 25.4% (RIV-CoT), a +4.6 pt gain. An oracle experiment providing ground-truth entities at inference yields +3.3 pts over predicted-entity RIV-CoT, identifying the grounding bottleneck. RIV-CoT also outperforms a multi-scale AnyRes baseline (60.7% with 4 image splits vs. 62.0% with entity crops), confirming that targeted crop retrieval is superior to uniform image splitting. Scaling to A-OKVQA with auto-extracted pseudo-labels, RIV-CoT reaches 84.2% MC accuracy vs. 80.6% for CoT and 78.2% for DirectAnswer. Strengths: - The dataset uniquely combines real-world images, expert-written (not LLM-generated) multi-entity reasoning traces, and precise bounding-box annotations, filling a genuine gap in visually-grounded VQA benchmarks. - RIV-CoT's iterative retrieve-and-reason loop is technically clean and well-motivated: it conditions text generation on locally retrieved visual evidence, directly addressing the known VLM failure mode of ignoring fine-grained image content during reasoning. - The generalization demonstration on A-OKVQA with auto-extracted pseudo-labels is practically significant, showing the method does not require gold-standard annotations and can scale to general-domain datasets. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The dataset is limited to 3,931 samples from French driving exams, introducing geographic and regulatory bias; while the authors argue the reasoning reflects universal EU/UN conventions, the domain coverage is narrow compared to the complexity of real-world autonomous driving perception. - Experiments are conducted exclusively on LLaVA-OV-7B, with no results on larger or architecturally distinct VLMs; the oracle gap (+3.3 pts from perfect entity grounding) reveals that the 7B model's entity detection is a significant bottleneck, and it remains unclear whether stronger grounding models would close this gap or shift the ceiling entirely. - Reasoning correctness scores remain surprisingly low even for the best method (25.4%), and the LLM-as-a-judge evaluation, while validated, adds a layer of noise; the paper does not deeply analyze failure modes in reasoning quality or disentangle errors due to incorrect entity detection vs. incorrect reasoning given correct entities.
Search Results
#158 — Moderately Innovative (49)

C-GNN-PRUNE: A Unified Graph-Based Framework for Structure-Aware Pruning of Mixture-of-Experts Models

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: C-GNN-PRUNE combines graph modeling of expert interactions with GNN-based embeddings and entropy-guided layer-wise budget allocation in a principled four-stage pipeline. The ablation evidence is solid. Yet the core components — GNN autoencoders, community detection, entropy-based allocation — are established techniques reassembled for MoE pruning rather than fundamentally new ideas, and the lack of any fine-tuning recovery step limits practical utility at aggressive pruning rates.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs achieve computational efficiency through sparse expert activation, but still require all parameters to reside in memory during inference, creating a severe memory bottleneck. Existing pruning methods treat experts as isolated units, ignoring inter-expert structural dependencies and the heterogeneous importance of different MoE layers. Uniform pruning ratios applied across all layers further degrade performance by over-pruning structurally critical layers. The paper addresses these two gaps: lack of structural modeling and lack of layer-adaptive budget allocation. Methodology: C-GNN-PRUNE is a four-stage pipeline. (1) Entropy-Guided Allocation: Layer-wise pruning budgets are computed by formulating a constrained optimization that minimizes discrepancy between each layer's retention ratio and its normalized activation entropy S(l) = H_l / log(N_l). Layers with higher entropy (more uniform expert usage, hence more structurally important) receive smaller pruning ratios; the global budget constraint is enforced via projected gradient descent. (2) Expert Graph Construction: An expert interaction graph is built with edge weights combining functional output similarity and routing probability divergence, weighted by a hyperparameter ω (empirically set to 0.3 via ablation). (3) GNN-Based Embedding Learning: A graph autoencoder with a two-layer GCN encoder learns structure-aware expert embeddings by minimizing adjacency reconstruction loss, capturing higher-order inter-expert dependencies beyond pairwise statistics. (4) Community Detection and Selection: Expert communities are identified using the Girvan-Newman algorithm on a revised similarity graph that fuses GNN embeddings with co-activation frequency. Within each community, the most frequently activated expert is retained. The number of communities per layer is derived from the entropy-based budget, tightly coupling allocation and clustering. Key Results: Experiments span three MoE models—DeepSeek-V2-Lite (15.7B), Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B (14.3B), and Mixtral 8×7B—at pruning rates of 20%, 25%, and 50%. On DeepSeek-V2-Lite at 20% pruning, C-GNN-PRUNE achieves an average of 24.84 across MMLU/GSM8K/HumanEval, outperforming the strongest baseline C-PRUNE (21.78). On Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B, it achieves 43.38 average vs. C-PRUNE's 37.65, with notable gains on reasoning tasks (GSM8K: 43.54 vs. 39.40). On Mixtral 8×7B at 50% pruning, it achieves the highest average (62.49) over EEMoE (61.10) and Wanda (59.72). At 25% pruning on Mixtral, it leads on BoolQ (84.49), RTE (69.31), and OBQA (32.60) while trailing EEMoE by ≤1.5 points on ARC-e and HellaSwag. Ablation confirms GNN embeddings contribute ~3.4 average MMLU points and entropy allocation contributes ~1.6 points on Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B. Girvan-Newman community detection outperforms KMeans, Spectral, and Agglomerative clustering by up to 4.33 points on individual benchmarks. Strengths: - The integration of both functional output similarity and routing divergence into a single expert graph, refined by a GNN autoencoder, is a principled and well-motivated approach that demonstrably improves over raw activation statistics. - The entropy-based layer-wise budget allocation elegantly couples pruning aggressiveness to structural sensitivity without requiring manual per-layer tuning, and is backed by solid ablation evidence. - Evaluation is thorough: three architecturally distinct MoE models, multiple pruning ratios, and diverse benchmarks (reasoning, language understanding, coding), with consistent top average performance across all settings. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The Girvan-Newman algorithm has O(m²n) complexity in the number of edges and nodes, which may become prohibitive for very large MoE models with hundreds of experts per layer (e.g., DeepSeek-V3 with 256 experts); scalability is not analyzed. - The method does not recover any of the accuracy lost to pruning—there is no fine-tuning or knowledge distillation step—so at high pruning rates (50%) all methods including C-GNN-PRUNE show meaningful degradation relative to the base model, which limits practical utility for aggressive compression. - The closest competitor, C-PRUNE (Guo et al. 2025a), is a concurrent preprint (arXiv:2504.07807), and both references cited as 2025a and 2025b in the paper point to the same arXiv entry, raising concerns about baseline availability and fair comparison.
Recommended
#159 — Moderately Innovative (49)

AVControl: Efficient Framework for Training Audio-Visual Controls (10 ▲ )

Matan Ben-Yosef, Tavi Halperin, Naomi Ken Korem, Mohammad Salama, Harel Cain, Asaf Joseph, Anthony Chen, Urska Jelercic, Ofir Bibi · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 Mar 2026
Why this rank: AVControl's parallel canvas insight—leveraging LTX-2's per-token timestep to distinguish reference from generation tokens without new positional embeddings—is technically clean and enables genuine modular extensibility across 13 control modalities via independent LoRAs. However, the approach largely assembles existing components (LoRA, parallel canvas, attention modulation) rather than introducing fundamentally new ideas, and audio quality substantially lags dedicated methods.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Controlling video and audio generation requires support for diverse input modalities — depth maps, pose skeletons, camera trajectories, audio intensities — yet existing approaches either train a single monolithic model for a fixed control set (e.g., VACE) or introduce costly architectural changes per new modality (e.g., ControlNet extensions, RoPE-augmented backbones). Neither approach scales gracefully: monolithic systems cannot be extended without full retraining, while per-modality architectural additions impose heavy compute and data requirements. The paper addresses how to make control-modality addition cheap, modular, and extensible across both video and audio in a joint generation system. Methodology: AVControl builds on LTX-2, a joint audio-visual DiT, and trains each control modality as a separate low-rank adapter (LoRA) with no architectural changes to the frozen backbone. The core mechanism is a parallel canvas: the reference signal (e.g., depth map, pose, camera parameters) is encoded at diffusion timestep=0 (clean) and appended as additional tokens in self-attention alongside the noisy generation tokens at timestep=t. Because LTX-2 assigns a unique per-token timestep, the model inherently distinguishes reference tokens from generation targets without needing new positional embeddings — the key differentiator from Flux Kontext, which requires learning new RoPE offsets from large-scale paired data. The paper also shows that naive spatial concatenation (IC-LoRA style) fails for structural controls like depth because semantically corresponding positions are spatially distant in the concatenated layout, weakening their attention interaction. Parallel canvas resolves this by co-locating reference and target tokens in the same attention context. A small-to-large control grid reduces reference canvas resolution for sparse modalities (camera parameters) relative to dense ones (depth maps), lowering inference latency. Thirteen modalities are trained independently in ~55K total steps across all LoRAs, compared to VACE's 200K-step joint training. Key Results: On the VACE Benchmark (20 samples per task), AVControl outperforms all evaluated baselines on average VBench score across depth (+2.9 pts over VACE), pose (+2.3 pts), inpainting (+3.8 pts), and outpainting (+2.3 pts). For camera control on the ReCamMaster Benchmark (200 videos), AVControl achieves the highest CLIP-F score (99.13% vs. ReCamMaster's 98.74%), though COLMAP-based rotation error (6.00°) is higher than ReCamMaster (1.22°), in part because SfM fails on 27% of generated videos. For audio intensity control on VGGSound (254 samples), AVControl achieves the highest Inception Score (34.51 vs. MMAudio's 17.18) but substantially worse FAD (57.25 vs. 5.32) and KL (7.74 vs. 1.64) than MMAudio — attributed to joint video+audio generation and 20-25× less training data (7.8K vs. 160-200K samples). On the HDTF talking-head benchmark, AVControl achieves best FID (12.31) and expression FID (0.18) but weaker lip-sync scores (Sync-C=4.50, Sync-D=10.31) compared to MultiTalk (8.54, 6.69), which uses a dedicated architecture with audio cross-attention layers. Strengths: - The parallel canvas + per-token timestep insight is technically clean and leverages an existing model property (LTX-2's per-token timestep) to distinguish reference from target without any new positional embeddings, making the approach substantially cheaper than Flux Kontext-style extensions. - Genuine modular extensibility: 13 independently trained LoRAs totaling ~55K steps, each trained on a few hundred to a few thousand steps on a single H100, covering an unusually diverse modality set (spatially-aligned controls, camera trajectory with intrinsics, sparse motion, video editing, and audio-visual controls including a novel who-is-talking modality). - The inference-time attention strength modulation (global and local) is a real capability advantage over channel-concatenation methods, enabling smooth trade-offs between structural fidelity and creative freedom that cannot be achieved by input-level fusion. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Audio quality on the intensity control task is substantially behind dedicated video-to-audio methods (FAD 57.25 vs. 5.32 for MMAudio), and the paper attributes this partly to the LTX-2 vocoder ceiling — a dependency on an external component limits reproducibility of improvement. - Lip-sync quality in the who-is-talking modality is noticeably weaker than MultiTalk (Sync-C 4.50 vs. 8.54), which uses dedicated audio cross-attention layers, suggesting that a single abstract bounding-box reference signal is insufficient for precise temporal audio-visual synchronization. - Evaluation on the VACE Benchmark uses only 20 samples per task, which is a thin basis for statistical claims of outperformance, especially given that the gains (2.3–3.8 average VBench points) may not be robust to larger test sets or diverse real-world conditions.
Recommended 2603.24793 PDF
#160 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Characterizing CPU-Induced Slowdowns in Multi-GPU LLM Inference

Euijun Chung, Yuxiao Jia, Aaron Jezghani, Hyesoon Kim · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: A well-executed empirical characterization that systematically quantifies CPU-induced bottlenecks in multi-GPU LLM serving — the attacker-victim methodology and 4.65M Slurm job log analysis are solid contributions. However, the core finding (CPU underprovisioning hurts performance) is intuitive rather than surprising, and the work is diagnostic rather than methodologically novel, offering 'add more cores' as the primary remedy without evaluating software mitigations.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-GPU LLM inference systems are commonly assumed to be GPU-bound, yet this paper argues the CPU is frequently the actual performance bottleneck. In shared multi-tenant HPC clusters and cloud environments, users manually request CPU allocations that are grossly insufficient relative to their GPU count — analysis of 4.65 million Slurm job records from two university clusters reveals median CPU-to-GPU ratios of 1–2 on instructional clusters, well below the 4–8 cores per GPU needed to avoid slowdowns. The paper fills a gap: while practitioners informally acknowledge CPU importance, no prior work systematically characterizes how CPU underprovisioning degrades end-to-end LLM serving performance across modern, fully-optimized stacks. Methodology: The authors conduct controlled experiments using vLLM v0.11.1 (V1 engine with CUDA Graphs, chunked prefill, torch.compile, prefix caching all enabled) on H100, H200, and RTX Pro 6000 Blackwell systems spanning 4- and 8-GPU configurations. An attacker–victim experimental design isolates CPU bottleneck effects: concurrent "attacker" requests impose controllable tokenization load that competes with a "victim" request's kernel dispatch path. CPU core counts are swept from (#GPUs+1) — deliberately below vLLM's minimum process count to reflect realistic cluster under-provisioning — up to 8×#GPUs. Two LLMs are evaluated: Llama 3.1 8B and Qwen 2.5 14B. Beyond end-to-end latency, the authors instrument CPU/GPU utilization traces and profile NCCL AllReduce barriers and vLLM's shared-memory broadcast queue (shm_broadcast.py) with PyTorch Profiler to pinpoint root causes. Cluster allocation logs are analyzed to establish real-world prevalence. Key Results: Tokenization alone accounts for up to 50% of TTFT in long-sequence configurations (sequence length 102k, batch size 1–16) on a 4×H200 system, and this fraction does not shrink with modern chunked-prefill stacks. Under concurrent attacker load, CPU-starved configurations (5 cores for 4 GPUs) exhibit sustained CPU saturation for up to 100 seconds, with GPU utilization dropping to near zero during this period. Providing adequate CPU resources reduces TTFT by 1.36–5.40× across all model/GPU/RPS combinations without adding any GPUs. At 16 RPS with 114k-token attacker sequences, minimal-CPU configurations time out entirely (>200s), while 32-core configurations remain responsive. Profiling reveals two structural bottlenecks: (1) NCCL barrier synchronization amplifies any per-rank CPU delay across all GPUs — a 1 ms OS scheduling delay on one core causes all other GPUs to busy-wait; (2) vLLM's shared-memory broadcast queue (1-writer-N-reader) experiences up to 19× slowdown under load (12 ms → 228 ms), exceeding the 44 ms GPU decode step by 5×, with contention scaling linearly with tensor parallelism degree. Adding 16 vCPUs to an AWS p5.48xlarge instance costs ~$0.80/hr (~1.5% overhead), making CPU provisioning a highly cost-effective fix. Strengths: - The attacker–victim experimental methodology cleanly isolates CPU-bound versus GPU-bound regimes without requiring invasive instrumentation, making findings generalizable beyond the specific stack tested. - Root-cause analysis goes beyond surface-level observations: identifying the shared-memory broadcast queue as a structural, tensor-parallelism-scaling bottleneck that additional cores alone cannot fully resolve is a non-obvious and actionable insight. - The cluster log analysis (4.65M job records) grounds the experimental findings in real deployment data, establishing that the studied failure modes are not synthetic edge cases but common operational conditions. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All quantitative experiments use a single serving framework (vLLM) on Intel CPUs with SMT disabled; the paper explicitly acknowledges it does not validate on SGLang or TensorRT-LLM, and CPU-GPU coupled architectures (Grace Hopper, AMD EPYC) may exhibit different profiles. - The paper prescribes "add more CPU cores" as the primary remedy but does not evaluate complementary software mitigations (CPU pinning via taskset/isolcpus, priority scheduling, or tokenizer offloading approaches like NetTokenizer/BlockBPE) under varying core budgets, leaving the practitioner without a clear decision procedure for when each remedy applies. - Experiments disable SMT, which underestimates available parallelism in typical cloud deployments where vCPUs are hyperthreads; the reported speedups may not directly translate to cloud instance sizing recommendations without SMT-enabled baselines.
Search Results 2603.22774 PDF
#161 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Cooperative Graph Transformer with Structural Consensus for Multi-View Learning

ojs.aaai.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: CoGFormer's differentiable Shrinkage Enhancement function for graph denoising is a reasonable contribution, and injecting METIS community structure as attention bias is a creative design choice. Consistent gains across six datasets are noted. The approach is fundamentally a combination of well-known components (shared autoencoders, GCNs, community detection, attention) without a strong unifying theoretical insight, and the O(N²) complexity limits practical applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-view learning must integrate data from heterogeneous sources by exploiting consistency and complementarity across views. GCN-based multi-view methods are limited to local neighborhoods and fail to capture global dependencies. More critically, multi-view data lacks explicit graph structure, so topology is typically constructed via node similarity — a process prone to noise. Simple weighted fusion of view-specific graphs is insufficient to suppress these noisy edges or uncover latent structural consensus. The gap is the absence of a unified framework that jointly denoises the fused graph structure and captures both local and global cross-view representations. Methodology: CoGFormer consists of three tightly coupled modules. (1) Shared Representation Learning: Multi-view features are projected into a common latent space via a shared encoder with per-view decoders, trained with a Frobenius-norm reconstruction loss to enforce cross-view consistency. (2) Denoised Structural Consensus GCN: View-specific adjacency matrices are combined via softmax-weighted fusion into a consensus graph Â. A differentiable Shrinkage Enhancement (DSE) function ρ(·) is then applied element-wise: edges below a data-driven threshold T are exponentially attenuated (shrinkage), while edges above it are linearly boosted (enhancement). T itself is computed from node features via a low-rank projection, making it data-dependent. The denoised graph feeds a standard 2-layer GCN to produce local representation Z_L. (3) Structure-Guided Attention Mechanism: The union of all view adjacency matrices is partitioned into C balanced communities via METIS. Shortest-path distances between nodes and communities in the community adjacency graph form a distance matrix D ∈ R^{N×C}. These distances are encoded into attention bias terms injected into a modified multi-head attention mechanism. To reduce the O(N²) attention cost, queries project from full nodes N while keys and values project from the C community centroids (low-rank projection Z_p). The resulting global representation Z_G is concatenated with Z_L and projected to yield the final classification representation Z_f, trained end-to-end with cross-entropy loss. Key Results: CoGFormer is evaluated on six multi-view image classification benchmarks (100leaves, ALOI, Animals, Caltech20, MNIST, NoisyMNIST) against seven baselines (Co-GCN, TMC, LGCN-FF, IMvGCN, ECML, GEGCN, ECMGD) using 10% labeled data. CoGFormer achieves best accuracy on all six datasets: 96.93% on 100leaves (+6.54 over GEGCN), 98.04% on ALOI (+2.53 over LGCN-FF), 85.76% on Animals (+1.13 over IMvGCN), 90.24% on Caltech20 (+2.26 over ECMGD), 93.78% on MNIST (+0.17 over GEGCN), and 97.06% on NoisyMNIST (+1.62 over GEGCN). Ablation confirms that both DSE denoising and structure-guided attention contribute independently, with removing either component degrading performance by 0.5–5% depending on the dataset. The model converges in ~150 epochs. Runtime complexity remains O(N²) despite the community-projection optimization. Strengths: - The DSE function provides a principled, differentiable, data-driven mechanism for graph denoising — simultaneously suppressing weak noisy edges and amplifying strong consensus edges — which is more expressive than hard thresholding or simple fusion. - Using METIS-derived community structure to inject high-order topological bias into attention is a creative way to inject structural priors without relying on the raw (noisy) adjacency, and community projection reduces attention complexity meaningfully. - Empirical gains are consistent and large across diverse datasets (small-scale 1K to large-scale 15K, 2 to 6 views, 10 to 100 classes), with low variance, suggesting the method is robust rather than tuned to one regime. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Despite the community-projection trick, the overall time complexity remains O(N²) due to the GCN and pairwise operations; the paper acknowledges this but does not quantify the practical gap relative to baselines at scale, and no datasets exceed 15K nodes. - The method depends on METIS partitioning, which is a non-differentiable preprocessing step run once before training. This means community structure does not adapt during learning, potentially locking in a suboptimal partition if the initial kNN graphs are noisy. - Evaluation is restricted to semi-supervised classification on image datasets with relatively clean view correspondence; performance on truly incomplete or severely misaligned views, or on non-image domains, is not examined.
Search Results
#162 — Moderately Innovative (48)

AP2O-Coder: Adaptively Progressive Preference Optimization for Reducing Compilation and Runtime Errors in LLM-Generated Code

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: AP2O-Coder offers a principled refinement of DPO by introducing error-type-aware curriculum learning and adaptive replay, with broad empirical coverage across 12+ model sizes. However, the core idea — curriculum-based training ordered by error frequency — is a natural extension of existing curriculum learning and DPO methods, and absolute gains remain modest at ~3% pass@1.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-generated code frequently produces compilation and runtime errors (SyntaxError, TypeError, etc.), and existing offline preference optimization methods like DPO treat all failures identically via binary pass/fail signals. This creates three concrete problems: (1) unawareness of error types, since no fine-grained signal distinguishes a KeyError from a TypeError; (2) inability to focus on specific error types, since data is uniformly shuffled across batches; and (3) neglect of the LLM's changing weaknesses as training progresses, since the preference dataset is static. The paper targets these shortcomings in the context of offline DPO-based post-training for code generation. Methodology: AP2O-Coder introduces a two-module framework on top of DPO. First, the progressive preference optimization module runs each LLM on M coding problems ("exam"), collects failed answers, and uses a language-specific analyzer (e.g., Python interpreter) to categorize each failure by error type and frequency ("analysis"). Failed answers are then sorted by error frequency and processed via a sliding window that exposes the LLM to one error type at a time — either low-to-high (L2H) or high-to-low (H2L) frequency ordering ("correction"). Second, the adaptive error replay module periodically evaluates the model on a small validation set ("quiz"), identifies the current distribution of errors, and injects proportional samples of those error types into the active sliding window to prevent forgetting and re-target the LLM's current weaknesses. The combined loss adds a DPO term over replayed validation failures to the progressive DPO term over training failures. No additional models, reward models, or oracle LLMs are needed; the language interpreter acts as the error analyzer. Key Results: Evaluated on EvalPlus (HumanEval and MBPP) and LiveCodeBench v6 across 12+ model sizes (0.5B–34B) spanning CodeLlama, DeepSeek-Coder, Qwen2.5-Coder, Llama3, Qwen2.5, and Qwen3. AP2O-Coder outperforms five baselines (Init, SFT-Coder, DPO-Coder, Curri-DPO-Coder, Dyn-DPO-Coder) by up to 3.1% in pass@1 and improves over pretrained baselines by up to 8.5%. On EvalPlus HumanEval, AP2O-Coder (H2L) scores 49.6 on CodeLlama-34B vs. 46.2 (Init) and 45.2 (DPO-Coder). On LiveCodeBench v6, AP2O-Coder (H2L) reaches 19.0 on Qwen2.5-Coder-7B vs. 18.3 (Init). Data efficiency is substantially better: AP2O-Coder requires only 4–60% of the preference data pairs used by DPO to reach optimal performance. Unlike other baselines, AP2O-Coder never introduces new error types and consistently reduces error counts across all error categories. For large models (≥7B), H2L outperforms L2H; for small/weaker models (0.5B), L2H is better. Pass@5 and pass@10 also improve, unlike Curri-DPO which degrades at higher k. Strengths: - Principled motivation: the three identified DPO shortcomings are specific and verifiable, not just rhetorical framing, and the two proposed modules address each directly. - Broad empirical coverage: 6 model families, 12+ sizes from 0.5B to 34B, two training sets (MBPP, TACO), two benchmarks, and ablations — making the results credible and unlikely to cherry-pick a favorable regime. - Data efficiency: achieving better performance with 4–60% of DPO's data is a practically important advantage, especially for large models where retraining is expensive. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Python-only evaluation: all experiments use Python; it is unclear whether error-type-specific progression generalizes to other languages where interpreter-based diagnosis may be less structured (e.g., C++ compilation errors are noisier). - Modest absolute gains: the 3% improvement in pass@1 is statistically consistent but modest in absolute terms, and the approach adds training complexity (sliding windows, periodic quiz evaluations, error-type bookkeeping) that may be non-trivial to engineer in practice. - L2H vs. H2L choice requires knowing model capability a priori: the paper provides post-hoc explanations (weaker models need breadth first), but no automatic mechanism is given to select the right progression direction without running both variants.
Recommended
#163 — Moderately Innovative (48)

RealRestorer: Towards Generalizable Real-World Image Restoration with Large-Scale Image Editing Models (37 ▲ )

Yufeng Yang, Xianfang Zeng, Zhangqi Jiang, Fukun Yin, Jianzhuang Liu, Wei Cheng, jinghong lan, Shiyu Liu, Yuqi Peng, Gang YU, Shifeng Chen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 Mar 2026
Why this rank: RealRestorer applies a straightforward two-stage fine-tuning curriculum to a large pre-existing image editing model, and while the degradation synthesis pipeline is comprehensive and RealIR-Bench is a useful community resource, the core methodology — synthetic pretraining followed by real fine-tuning with progressive mixing — is a standard transfer learning recipe. The proposed FS metric shows only moderate correlation with human judgment (SRCC=0.30), and the undisclosed base model architecture limits reproducibility.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Real-world image restoration is a critical preprocessing step for downstream tasks like autonomous driving, object detection, and remote sensing. Images in the wild suffer from diverse and co-existing degradations — blur, rain, noise, low-light, moiré patterns, haze, compression artifacts, reflections, and lens flare. Existing all-in-one restoration models are trained on narrow synthetic distributions and generalize poorly to real degradations. Closed-source large image editing models (e.g., Nano Banana Pro, GPT-Image-1.5) show strong generalization but are inaccessible and non-reproducible. This work aims to narrow the open-/closed-source performance gap for real-world image restoration. Methodology: RealRestorer fine-tunes an open-source large image editing model using a two-stage training strategy over nine degradation types. The first stage (Transfer Training) trains on ~1M synthetic degradation pairs built with a comprehensive synthesis pipeline: motion blur from video temporal averaging, compression via JPEG simulation, moiré patterns following UniDemoiré, low-light via brightness/gamma attenuation plus a trained Retinexformer inversion, noise with granular and segment-aware modeling, flare via collected glare pattern blending, reflection via SynNet, haze via atmospheric scattering with depth priors, and rain via collected streak/splash patterns. VLMs and quality-assessment models filter unsuitable samples. The second stage (Supervised Fine-tuning) introduces ~100K real-world degradation pairs with cosine annealing LR decay to improve generalization. A Progressively-Mixed training strategy retains a small portion of synthetic data during the second stage to prevent overfitting to specific real patterns. A new benchmark, RealIR-Bench, is introduced with 464 non-reference real-world degraded images across nine tasks. Novel evaluation metrics are proposed: Restoration Score (RS, VLM-based degradation severity improvement) and Fidelity Score (FS, combining RS with LPIPS content consistency), since reference-based PSNR/SSIM are unavailable for no-reference real data. Key Results: On RealIR-Bench, RealRestorer ranks first among all open-source image editing models and third overall (behind Nano Banana Pro and GPT-Image-1.5). It achieves the best FS on deblurring and low-light enhancement, ranks second on moiré removal, and ranks first among open-source on five of nine tasks. Its average FS of 0.146 is only 0.007 points behind the top closed-source model (Nano Banana Pro at 0.153) and 0.019 points ahead of the second-best open-source model (Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 at 0.127). On the FoundIR benchmark with paired references, RealRestorer achieves the best PSNR/SSIM on 5 of 7 degradation types with an average PSNR of 21.45 and SSIM of 0.70, surpassing Step1X-Edit (19.37/0.66) and Nano Banana Pro (20.37/0.67). The Progressively-Mixed training strategy contributes +0.004 FS improvement. Zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks (snow removal, old photo restoration) is qualitatively demonstrated. Strengths: - Comprehensive degradation synthesis pipeline covering nine distinct degradation types with realistic simulation (depth-based haze, video-derived blur, segment-aware noise) substantially closes the synthetic-to-real gap. - Two-stage curriculum (synthetic pretraining → real fine-tuning with progressive mixing) is well-motivated with clear ablation evidence of each component's contribution. - RealIR-Bench is a valuable community resource: 464 real internet-sourced images with no synthetic contamination, and the FS metric shows moderate but statistically significant correlation with human judgments (Kendall's τ=0.25, SRCC=0.30). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The proposed FS/RS metrics exhibit only moderate correlation with human perception (SRCC=0.30), indicating the automatic evaluation remains a rough proxy and results may not fully align with real perceptual quality. - The evaluation relies on comparing against closed-source models (Nano Banana Pro, GPT-Image-1.5) that are non-reproducible, making fair benchmarking difficult and potentially subject to versioning inconsistencies. - No ablation of the base image editing model's identity or architecture details are disclosed, limiting reproducibility and the ability to isolate contributions of the data pipeline from the backbone's pre-existing priors.

#164 — Moderately Innovative (48)

From Static Templates to Dynamic Runtime Graphs: A Survey of Workflow Optimization for LLM Agents (41 ▲ )

Ling Yue, Kushal Raj Bhandari, Ching-Yun Ko, Dhaval Patel, Shuxin Lin, Nianjun Zhou, Jianxi Gao, Pin-Yu Chen, Shaowu Pan · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The ACG abstraction and GDT/GPM taxonomy provide genuinely clarifying vocabulary for a fragmented literature, and the minimum reporting protocol addresses a real gap in workflow evaluation standards. As a survey, however, it synthesizes rather than creates, and the framework's utility remains empirically unvalidated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As LLM-based systems move beyond single-prompt interactions into multi-step workflows interleaving LLM calls, tool use, retrieval, code execution, and verification, there is a growing need to optimize these workflows systematically. Existing surveys treat workflow structure as a given—covering adjacent topics like planning, tool learning, or multi-agent collaboration—rather than treating workflow structure itself as the primary optimization object. This survey fills that gap by introducing a unified framework and taxonomy for workflow optimization, recognizing that what structure to use, when to determine it, and how to optimize it under quality-cost trade-offs are first-class research questions. Methodology: The paper introduces the Agentic Computation Graph (ACG) as a unifying abstraction for LLM-centered workflows. An ACG template is formally defined as a tuple (V, E, Φ, Σ, A) covering nodes, edges, node parameters, scheduling policy, and admissible edit actions. The paper distinguishes three related objects: the reusable ACG template, the run-specific realized graph, and the execution trace. The primary taxonomy axis is when workflow structure is determined, formalized via two descriptors: Graph Determination Time (GDT: offline / pre-execution / in-execution) and Graph Plasticity Mode (GPM: none / select / generate / edit). Methods are further classified along three orthogonal axes—optimization target (node, graph, or joint), feedback/evidence type (metric, verifier, preference, or trace-derived text), and update mechanism (search, RL, supervised learning, repair/edit, or hybrid). A comprehensive survey of 77 in-scope works (39 core, 7 adjacent, 31 background) is organized under this framework, with uniform comparison cards applied to all methods. The paper also proposes a minimum reporting protocol requiring workflow papers to report not just task outcomes but the workflow used, its variation across instances, graph-level properties, and cost. Key Results: The paper synthesizes cross-paper empirical patterns rather than reporting new experimental results. Key findings include: (1) Static optimization is often sufficient when the operator space is constrained, the evaluator is trustworthy, and the workload is repetitive—well-searched static templates can dominate ad hoc dynamic generation in stable API environments (e.g., code generation with unit tests). (2) Runtime selection/pruning captures a large fraction of cost savings without the validity overhead of graph generation, but cannot introduce missing structural capabilities absent from the super-graph. (3) Pre-execution generation is preferable when task heterogeneity is structural rather than scalar—different queries requiring different decompositions or operator sequences—as demonstrated by methods like AFlow (MCTS over typed operator graphs), FlowReasoner (RL-trained meta-controller), and G-Designer (VGAE-based topology generation). (4) In-execution editing is most compelling in interactive environments where partial execution reveals information a one-shot plan cannot anticipate. (5) Graph-level optimization cannot be replaced by prompt tuning when failures stem from missing verification, incorrect control flow, or redundant communication. (6) Verifiers deliver the largest returns when they are both cheap and semantically meaningful (unit tests, schema checks, executability checks), and their placement—not just presence—is a key design variable. Strengths: - The template/realized-graph/trace trichotomy is a genuinely clarifying conceptual contribution that resolves ambiguities (e.g., offline-trained workflow generators are dynamic by this taxonomy), and the GDT/GPM descriptors provide precise vocabulary for comparing gray-area methods. - The survey achieves consistent cross-paper comparability through uniform classification cards applied to all 39 core methods, enabling structured comparison that paper-specific framing obscures. - The proposed minimum reporting protocol is practically valuable: it identifies a real gap (workflow papers reporting only final accuracy while hiding structural costs, edit counts, and fallback behavior) and specifies concrete remedies. - Coverage is genuinely comprehensive, spanning static template search, node-level prompt optimization, dynamic selection/pruning, pre-execution generation, in-execution editing, and evaluation assets, with clear scope tags distinguishing core/adjacent/background contributions.
