They compare five conditions: (1) JA-PARAPHRASE-63B — paraphrasing JA-WEB-9B into four styles (easy, medium/Wikipedia-style, hard/abstruse, QA) using Qwen3-14B; (2) JA-INSTRUCT-63B — augmenting JA-WEB-9B documents with synthetic document-grounded QA pairs in four formats (free-form, multiple-choice, each with/without chain-of-thought); (3) JA-TRANSLATE-63B — translating the 63B-token English Web corpus into Japanese; (4) EN-WEB-63B — using English Web text directly as a baseline; (5) JA-WEB-9B repeated ×8 as a multi-epoch baseline; plus an oracle condition (6) JA-WEB-63B filling the budget with additional organic Japanese text. Synthesis uses Qwen3-14B with vLLM on H200 GPU clusters (~80 node-days for synthesis). Models are trained at 1.8B, 3.8B, and 7.2B parameters and evaluated on JSQuAD (reading comprehension), NIILC (knowledge QA), JEMHopQA (multi-hop QA), and ALT (En→Ja translation) using the llm-jp-eval framework. Post-training experiments with SFT and contamination analyses are also conducted. Key Results: JA-PARAPHRASE-63B and JA-INSTRUCT-63B consistently outperform all baselines across all three model sizes, and match or exceed the oracle JA-WEB-63B setting. For the 7.2B model, JA-PARAPHRASE achieves an average score of 0.584 vs. 0.520 for the oracle JA-WEB-63B and 0.442 for the EN-WEB-63B baseline. JA-INSTRUCT achieves 0.543 at 7.2B. Multi-epoch repetition of JA-WEB-9B performs worst among Japanese-language strategies. JA-TRANSLATE-63B beats EN-WEB-63B for the 1.8B model but the gap closes at larger scales, suggesting larger models leverage cross-lingual transfer more effectively. After supervised fine-tuning, synthetic corpora still outperform baselines, though the margin narrows, indicating synthetic pre-training improves genuine language modeling beyond mere imitation of instruction format. Contamination analysis shows negligible benchmark leakage across all conditions. A cost-efficiency analysis finds that synthesizing only 9B or 27B tokens (with multi-epoch padding) largely matches the performance of full 63B synthetic budgets. Strengths: - Rigorous controlled experimental design: synthesizer, decoding parameters, and tokenizer are fixed across conditions, isolating the effect of data construction strategy from model or decoding variation. - The finding that paraphrase/instruction-style synthesis matches or beats the oracle organic Japanese upper bound is a strong positive result with practical significance for low-resource language LLM development. - The partial synthesis analysis is practically valuable: it shows that 9B–27B tokens of synthetic data combined with multi-epoch training can nearly match full 63B synthetic budgets, significantly reducing generation compute costs. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The entire study uses a single synthesizer (Qwen3-14B); robustness to synthesizer choice is unverified, and the pipeline assumes availability of a target-language–fluent LLM, which may not hold for truly low-resource languages. - Experiments are confined to Japanese and to relatively small model scales (up to 7.2B parameters), limiting generalizability — the authors themselves acknowledge these findings need validation in other languages and at larger scales before being taken as general guidance.
Recommended
#180 — Moderately Innovative (44)

Do diacritics matter? evaluating the impact of arabic diacritics on tokenization and llm benchmarks

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: A thorough empirical evaluation filling a documented gap in Arabic LLM research, with rigorous statistical testing and multi-level analysis spanning tokenization metrics to internal representations. However, no new methods or models are proposed — the contribution is entirely diagnostic — and the findings (full diacritization hurts, wild diacritics are mostly harmless) are relatively unsurprising given known tokenizer behavior with out-of-vocabulary characters.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Arabic diacritics are orthographic marks that convey phonological, lexical, and morphosyntactic information, but their prevalence varies widely across domains — dense in children's books, poetry, and religious texts, but nearly absent in news and user-generated content. While many Arabic NLP pipelines strip diacritics during preprocessing to sidestep orthographic variation, this practice has unknown consequences for modern LLMs. The paper addresses a critical gap: no prior work had systematically studied how varying degrees of diacritization affect subword tokenization and downstream benchmark performance across a broad set of Arabic LLMs. Methodology: The authors evaluate nine LLMs — five Arabic-centric (AceGPT-v2-8B, ALLaM-7B, Fanar-9B, Jais-13B, Jais-2-8B), three multilingual (Aya-Expanse-8B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen3-8B), and GPT-4o — under three controlled diacritization conditions: (i) all diacritics removed (undiac), (ii) naturally occurring diacritics preserved (wild), and (iii) fully diacritized text (full*) produced by a BERT-based morphological disambiguation system with 88.9% word-level accuracy. Evaluation uses three Arabic benchmarks: ArabicMMLU (14,455 samples), ArabCulture (3,482 samples), and AraTrust (522 samples), all run zero-shot. Tokenization analysis measures fertility (subwords per word), token density (characters per subword), tokenization consistency (% equal subword counts, Jaccard similarity), and layer-wise cosine similarities of word representations across diacritization conditions using the Wild2MaxDiacs dataset. Key Results: Fertility increases monotonically from undiac to wild to full across all tokenizers. The Jais tokenizers are worst-affected under full diacritization, reaching fertilities of 9.3–10.1 subwords/word vs. 1.9 undiacritized, approaching character-level tokenization because individual diacritic Unicode characters are absent from their vocabularies. ALLaM (57.5% Arabic vocabulary) and Fanar show the best tokenization robustness to wild diacritics (24.6% and 22.3% equal subword counts undiac-wild). Jaccard similarities confirm near-zero overlap under undiac-full for all models. Layer-wise cosine similarity analysis shows representations converge across layers due to anisotropy, but undiac-full divergence persists at lower layers. On benchmarks, removing wild diacritics has minimal and mostly statistically insignificant impact on performance. Full diacritization, however, consistently and significantly degrades performance across models: macro-average drops from 62.8 (wild) to 56.2 (full*) across benchmarks. Jais Chat (13B) suffers the most — 14.4 points on ArabicMMLU, 9.7 on ArabCulture, 26.2 on AraTrust under full vs. wild. GPT-4o is the most robust, with only a 0.7-point macro drop. Counterintuitively, retaining wild diacritics often yields equal or slightly better performance than stripping them, challenging the conventional preprocessing practice. Strengths: - First systematic, multi-model study connecting tokenization behavior to benchmark performance under controlled diacritization conditions, filling a documented gap in Arabic LLM evaluation. - Rigorous statistical testing (McNemar's test) to distinguish meaningful performance differences from noise, avoiding overclaiming. - Analysis spans both surface-level tokenization metrics and internal representation similarity, providing mechanistic grounding for observed performance degradation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is strictly zero-shot multiple-choice; it is unknown whether findings generalize to generation tasks, few-shot settings, or fine-tuned models where the model has seen diacritized text in context. - The full* condition relies on an automatic diacritizer with 88.9% word-level accuracy, introducing noise that conflates true diacritization effects with diacritization errors, potentially inflating the measured performance drop. - All three benchmarks are Modern Standard Arabic; the impact of diacritics on dialectal Arabic — where diacritization tools are far less mature and diacritic conventions differ — is entirely unexplored.
Recommended
#181 — Moderately Innovative (44)

Ontology -and temporal-logic-based knowledge representation in structural health monitoring

smarsly.wordpress.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: SHMO's comprehensive scope—unifying BIM, sensors, damage, structural analysis, and hazards in one federated ontology—fills a genuine gap, and the Metric Temporal Logic integration is a conceptually interesting addition for time-bounded reactive rules. The approach is methodologically sound but follows established ontology engineering patterns (NeOn, OWL, SPARQL), and the MTL component remains entirely theoretical with no runtime implementation or evaluation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Structural health monitoring (SHM) of civil infrastructure requires integrating heterogeneous information — 3D building design (BIM), sensor networks, damage records, structural analysis results, and hazard data — into a unified, queryable knowledge base. Existing ontologies for SHM are either too narrow (e.g., bridge-specific or dam-specific) or too broadly defined to support the full range of SHM concerns. No prior representation simultaneously addresses all five domains (3D design, sensor networks, damage, structural analysis, hazards) in a formally grounded, interoperable way. This gap limits semantic interoperability, automated querying, and real-time decision-making — increasingly critical as digital twins proliferate in civil engineering. Methodology: The paper proposes SHMO (Structural Health Monitoring Ontology), a modular federated ontology developed using the NeOn methodology (scope definition → taxonomy definition → OWL development in Protégé → verification → validation against competency questions). SHMO integrates seven sub-ontologies: ifcOWL (BIM/3D geometry), SOSA (sensor observations and actuators), SSN (sensor networks), DOT (damage topology), DASB (inspection assessment), and two novel ontologies introduced in this work — SAO (Structural Analysis Ontology, covering finite element models, loads, and results) and CHO (Construction Hazard Ontology, covering hazard types and impacts). Key semantic relationships link facilities to sensor networks (sno:hosts), elements to damage areas (shmo:hasDamageArea), facilities to FEM models (sao:hasFEM_Representation), and structures to hazards (cho:affectedBy). A novel addition is the integration of Metric Temporal Logic (MTL) operators (Always ◻, Eventually ◇) alongside the ontology to express time-bounded reactive rules — e.g., that an actuator must be triggered within 2 time units of a detected impact. Data is stored in GraphDB and queried via SPARQL. Validation is conducted on the 45.5 m Citadel swing bridge in Hamburg, instrumenting it with wireless accelerometer nodes, converting an IFC model to ifcOWL, and ingesting sensor data from Excel files via a Python mapping script. Key Results: Validation demonstrates that SHMO successfully answers the five competency questions (CQs) defined during ontology design, including querying acceleration measurements by sensor location (CQ1), identifying sensor networks on a bridge (CQ2), identifying actuator responses to hazards (CQ3), querying damage area dimensions (CQ4), and querying structural analysis loads (CQ5). A SPARQL query for CQ1 returns timestamped vertical acceleration values (ranging approximately ±2 m/s²) from the bridge slab sensors, confirming correct data mapping and queryability. No quantitative benchmarks against alternative representations are reported; validation is demonstrative rather than comparative. Strengths: - Comprehensive scope: SHMO is the first SHM ontology to simultaneously integrate 3D BIM geometry, sensor networks, damage, structural analysis models, and hazard/safety knowledge in a single modular framework. - Methodological rigor: Use of the NeOn methodology with explicit competency questions, OWL verification in Protégé, and staged validation provides a reproducible ontology engineering process. - Novel temporal reasoning: Integration of Metric Temporal Logic with ontology classes enables time-bounded reactive rules (e.g., actuator response deadlines after hazard detection), which static ontologies cannot express. Weaknesses / Limitations: - No comparative evaluation: The paper does not benchmark SHMO against existing SHM ontologies (BSHM, OntoDSMS) on any quantitative metric — expressivity, query performance, scalability, or coverage — making it impossible to assess how much improvement SHMO actually provides. - Shallow validation: Only one competency question (CQ1) is demonstrated end-to-end with real data; CQ2–CQ5 covering hazards, actuators, damage, and structural analysis are not validated with actual sensor or damage data on the bridge, leaving the ontology's coverage of those domains unconfirmed in practice. - MTL integration is conceptual only: The temporal logic formulas are presented as notation but no runtime enforcement mechanism, reasoner, or monitoring engine is implemented or tested; the MTL component remains theoretical.
Search Results
#182 — Moderately Innovative (44)

Instructional Agents: Reducing Teaching Faculty Workload through Multi-Agent Instructional Design

aclanthology.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The multi-agent instructional design framework is a competent engineering contribution that systematically applies the ADDIE model, but the core architecture is prompt-engineered role-playing within a single LLM rather than genuine multi-agent collaboration, limiting its conceptual novelty. The finding that LLM auto-evaluators lack discriminative power is a useful methodological insight, but the system itself represents a well-executed integration of known techniques (multi-agent prompting, structured pedagogical frameworks) rather than a departure from existing approaches.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Preparing university-level course materials — syllabi, slides, lecture scripts, assessments — is labor-intensive and typically requires coordination among faculty, instructional designers, and teaching assistants. Existing AI tools address only isolated tasks (tutoring, grading) without end-to-end instructional design workflows, leading to fragmented alignment between learning objectives, content, and assessments. The gap is an automated pipeline grounded in established pedagogical frameworks that preserves coherence across all course artifacts. Methodology: The paper presents Instructional Agents, a multi-agent LLM framework organized around the first three phases of the ADDIE instructional design model (Analyze, Design, Develop). Five role-specialized agents — Teaching Faculty, Instructional Designer, Teaching Assistant, Course Coordinator, and Program Chair — collaborate through structured prompt exchanges across nine subtasks: objectives definition, audience analysis, resource assessment, syllabus design, slide planning, assessment planning, materials generation (LaTeX Beamer slides, slide scripts, assessments), validation, and simulated pilot testing. The system supports four operational modes: Autonomous (no human input beyond a course name), Catalog-Guided (pre-loaded institutional guidelines), Feedback-Guided (post-hoc human corrections per subtask), and Full Co-Pilot (human-in-the-loop approval at each subtask plus catalog). All agents use the same underlying LLM backend; role behavior is entirely prompt-engineered. Evaluation used an adapted Quality Matters (QM) rubric across six output types on five CS/AI graduate courses, rated by five expert human instructors per course on a 1–5 Likert scale measuring estimated revision effort. Key Results: Full Co-Pilot mode consistently achieved the highest quality (avg ~3.74 on 5-point scale), outperforming Autonomous mode by 0.5–0.9 points across all material types. Even Autonomous mode produced average scores above 3.0 (avg ~3.01), indicating usable baseline quality. Among model backends, gpt-4o-mini matched gpt-4o and o1-preview in quality (all ~3.52–3.54 avg) while costing 17× less ($0.15/1M tokens vs. $2.50 for gpt-4o). A Friedman test confirmed no statistically significant quality difference among backends (Q=0.473, p=0.789). Compute costs are remarkably low: Full Co-Pilot requires only $0.36 per course with ~4.73 hrs inference time and 30–45 minutes of human time. Ablation studies confirm that role specialization is essential — removing any single agent degrades quality, with the single-agent baseline scoring only 2.33 avg vs. 3.01 for the full multi-agent Autonomous system. LLM-based automated evaluators produce tightly clustered scores (2.9–3.1) with low variance, rendering them unreliable for distinguishing material quality; human evaluators showed far greater discriminative range. Bias evaluation via CEAT found no statistically significant bias across racial, gender, or national categories (all scores <0.2, all p-values >0.05). Strengths: - End-to-end pipeline grounded in ADDIE, a well-established instructional design framework, giving the system principled pedagogical structure rather than ad hoc prompting. - Four operational modes with a genuine spectrum of human involvement allow deployment in resource-constrained settings (Autonomous) through to high-quality co-creation (Full Co-Pilot), with transparent cost/quality trade-offs quantified. - Rigorous human evaluation using an adapted Quality Matters rubric with five domain-expert raters per course across five distinct CS/AI courses; the important finding that LLM auto-evaluators are unreliable is a methodologically valuable contribution beyond the main system. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework covers only the Analyze, Design, and Develop phases of ADDIE; the Implement and Evaluate phases — which require real classroom deployment and measurement of actual student learning outcomes — are entirely absent, leaving the core claim of pedagogical effectiveness unvalidated. - All agent "collaboration" is prompt-engineered turn-taking within a single LLM; there is no genuine inter-agent state, memory, or negotiation, making the claimed role specialization structurally superficial and raising questions about whether the multi-agent framing adds anything beyond careful prompt decomposition.

#183 — Moderately Innovative (44)

MuRF: Unlocking the Multi-Scale Potential of Vision Foundation Models (5 ▲ )

Bocheng Zou, Mu Cai, Mark Stanley, Dingfu Lu, Yong Jae Lee · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 Mar 2026
Why this rank: MuRF applies a straightforward idea — process images at multiple resolutions through a frozen VFM and concatenate the features — with solid engineering but limited conceptual novelty. The training-free nature and broad empirical validation across four task families are practical contributions, but multi-scale feature fusion is a well-established technique, and the 2.6× inference slowdown tempers the practical appeal.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) like DINOv2 support flexible input resolutions during training, yet standard inference protocols still process images at a single fixed scale. This is wasteful because different scales carry complementary information: low-resolution views capture globally coherent semantics (recognition), while high-resolution views resolve fine-grained boundaries and local detail (refinement). Forcing a single-scale choice at inference thus sacrifices either global context or local precision. Prior multi-scale approaches either require costly task-specific training (FPN), break spatial continuity (tiling in LLaVA-NeXT), or merely upsample within a single forward pass without adding new information (FeatUp, JAFAR). Methodology: MuRF (Multi-Resolution Fusion) is a training-free inference-time strategy that processes the same input image through a frozen VFM at a pyramid of resolutions, then fuses the resulting feature maps into a single unified representation. Concretely, for a set of scaling factors S_res = {s1, ..., sk}, each resized image is passed through the frozen encoder to yield patch-level feature maps F_s. These are bilinearly upsampled to a common spatial resolution and concatenated channel-wise, producing F_MuRF ∈ R^{H'×W'×D} where D = |S_res| × d. Channel-wise concatenation is chosen over mean pooling or addition to preserve the orthogonality of scale-specific signals, allowing lightweight downstream heads to adaptively route recognition vs. refinement information. Task adaptation uses: (1) a linear/convolutional head for dense prediction (segmentation, depth); (2) per-resolution nearest-neighbor memory banks with averaged score maps for unsupervised anomaly detection; and (3) channel-concatenation of low- and high-resolution features (keeping visual token count fixed at 576) for MLLM integration. Experiments use DINOv2-ViT-B/14 as the primary frozen backbone, with additional validation on SigLIP2. Key Results: On semantic segmentation (linear probing), MuRF achieves 47.4 mIoU on ADE20K and 83.1 on PASCAL VOC, versus the best single-scale DINOv2 baseline of 46.1 and 81.8 respectively (+4.2% and +5.9% relative improvement over medium-resolution baseline). On depth estimation (NYU Depth V2, Lin.1 protocol), MuRF reaches RMSE 0.368 versus 0.394 for the single-scale baseline (6.6% relative improvement). In the MLLM setting (LLaVA-1.5 with DINOv2), MuRF improves MME from 1291.6 to 1357.1 (+65.5) and the VLMsAreBiased metric from 278.6 to 366.4 (+87.8), with negligible training overhead (71 vs. 72 minutes pretrain, 270 vs. 274 minutes finetune). For unsupervised anomaly detection on MVTec AD 2, MuRF achieves 62.3 AU-PRO on the TESTpriv,mix subset, surpassing the previous best training-free method SuperAD (59.3) by +2.6 points and even outperforming the trained RoBiS method (59.7). Ablations confirm monotonically improving performance as more resolution scales are fused, with diminishing returns, and show MuRF and multi-layer feature concatenation provide orthogonal benefits that combine to achieve the best overall results. Strengths: - Genuinely training-free for the backbone — no retraining of VFMs required, making it a drop-in enhancement applicable to any frozen ViT-based VFM family (DINOv2, SigLIP2). - Breadth of empirical validation is unusual: the same core idea is validated across four qualitatively different task families (dense prediction, depth estimation, MLLM VQA, and training-free anomaly detection), strengthening the universality claim. - Token-count-preserving design for MLLMs (channel-concat instead of sequence append) is practically important and avoids quadratic attention cost blowup, enabling zero-overhead LLM integration. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The inference computational cost scales linearly with the number of resolutions (MuRF at {0.5, 1.0, 1.5}× costs 58.35 ms/iter vs. 22.61 ms for single-scale 1.0×, a 2.6× slowdown), which may be prohibitive for real-time applications despite the modest VRAM increase. - The VQA gains over the DINOv2 baseline are moderate and inconsistent: MuRF with DINOv2 still substantially underperforms CLIP-based LLaVA-1.5 on several benchmarks (e.g., MME Perceptual 1357.1 vs. 1511.4), suggesting that VFM choice and pretraining data matter more than multi-scale fusion for multimodal understanding tasks. - Experiments are confined to DINOv2-ViT-B/14; no experiments with larger ViT-L or ViT-g variants are reported, leaving open whether gains persist or diminish as backbone capacity increases and potentially learns scale-robust features on its own.
