#197 — Incremental (37)

DS2-Instruct: Domain-Specific Data Synthesis for Large Language Models Instruction Tuning

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: DS2-INSTRUCT assembles known components (keyword expansion, Bloom's Taxonomy prompting, self-consistency filtering, retrieval augmentation) into a coherent zero-shot pipeline for domain-specific instruction generation. The engineering is sound and the ablations confirm each component's contribution, but the individual techniques are established and the combination, while useful, represents incremental progress over prior synthetic data generation methods.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Adapting LLMs to specialized domains (finance, medicine, law, etc.) requires high-quality instruction-tuning data, which is prohibitively expensive to create via human annotation. Existing synthetic data methods target general-purpose tasks and fail to capture domain-specific terminology and reasoning patterns. Prior approaches either require proprietary/hard-to-obtain domain corpora or human-written seed examples for in-context learning — both demanding significant domain expertise and limiting scalability. There is no scalable, fully automatic, zero-shot method for generating domain-specific instruction data without any human supervision. Methodology: DS2-INSTRUCT is a three-stage zero-shot framework requiring only a task definition as input. Stage 1 (keyword generation) starts with 50 LLM-generated seed keywords from the task description, then iteratively expands them via bi-directional expansion (generating both prerequisite foundational concepts and advanced specialized topics per iteration, 100 iterations total, 5 new keywords per direction). A retrieval-augmented step uses BM25 over the Pile corpus (ArXiv, FreeLaw, StackExchange, Wikipedia, GitHub) to extract additional domain-grounded keywords and reduce hallucination. Stage 2 (instruction generation) pairs each keyword with all six levels of Bloom's Taxonomy (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate, Create) to generate cognitively diverse instructions; paired-keyword instructions further explore relational reasoning. Stage 3 (quality filtering) applies self-consistency filtering: for each candidate instruction, N=5 independent responses are generated at temperature 0.7, and instructions are retained only if the majority-vote answer exceeds threshold τ=3/5. The majority-vote answer becomes the final response. Datasets of 6,000 instruction-response pairs per domain are generated using Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct, then used to fine-tune Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B-v0.1, and Qwen2.5-7B via LoRA (r=8, α=16). Key Results: Evaluated across seven benchmarks (CFA, PubMedQA, MedQA, GPQA, LogiQA, GSM8K, MATH) against four baselines (Zero-Shot, domain-aware Self-Instruct, InstructMix, ExploreInstruct), DS2-INSTRUCT achieves the best average performance for all three model families. For Qwen2.5-7B, average accuracy improves from 48.92% (zero-shot) to 54.95%, versus 53.07% for the next-best InstructMix. Gains are most pronounced for weaker models: Llama3 improves from 14.34% (zero-shot) to 42.83% vs. 25.94% for InstructMix; Mistral improves from 9.70% to 37.83% vs. 30.29% for InstructMix. Ablation studies confirm all three components are necessary: removing bi-directional expansion drops GSM8K accuracy from 42.75% to 37.56%; removing retrieval augmentation drops it further to 31.36%; removing cognitive-level guidance drops GSM8K from 42.75% to 39.18%; removing self-consistency filtering drops it to 35.64%. Data quality evaluation shows 92–97% valid instructions and 91–95% valid responses. Scaling experiments show no saturation at 6,000 samples and consistent gains across Qwen model sizes (3B–14B), with diminishing returns at larger scales. Strengths: - Genuinely zero-shot and unsupervised: requires only a natural language task description, eliminating the need for human-written seeds, domain corpora, or annotation — a practical advantage for fast domain adaptation. - Bloom's Taxonomy integration is principled and shown to be effective: ablation confirms cognitive diversity is not cosmetic but meaningfully improves downstream reasoning performance. - Self-consistency filtering provides an automatic, annotation-free quality signal that is both cheap and effective, outperforming naïve inclusion of all generated data. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Keyword expansion relies on static retrieval from the Pile corpus, which may miss emerging, niche, or highly proprietary domains not well-represented in public corpora — the coverage bias is acknowledged but not quantified. - Quality evaluation (Table 3) is performed by a paper author rather than independent domain experts, creating potential confirmation bias; inter-annotator agreement is not reported. - Evaluation is English-only and confined to multiple-choice and math problem-solving formats; generalization to open-ended generation tasks, non-English domains, or tasks without automatic correctness signals is untested.
Recommended
#198 — Incremental (37)

Supporting Medicines Manufacturing through Semantic Technologies: Oligonucleotide Synthesis Use Case

scitepress.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: A competent application of well-established semantic web standards (SOSA/SSN, PROV-O, QUDT, BFO) to oligonucleotide manufacturing, with a clean hub-and-spoke modular design. The ontology engineering is principled and the domain grounding with CPI experts is solid, but the methodology is standard ontology development with no novel techniques, and evaluation on a single batch severely limits generalizability claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Pharmaceutical manufacturing generates heterogeneous, siloed data across instruments and process steps, making interoperability difficult and regulatory compliance burdensome. In oligonucleotide synthesis specifically, linking synthesis parameters to downstream analytical results requires tedious manual cross-referencing across spreadsheets — a process that is error-prone and low-trust in regulated environments. Existing ontology efforts (Allotrope Foundation Ontology, OBI, IOF) each cover partial aspects of the problem but none provide end-to-end semantic coverage connecting synthesis platforms with analytical instruments and time-series sensor data in a FAIR-compliant manner. Methodology: The authors develop a proof-of-concept ontology and knowledge graph for oligonucleotide manufacturing using a five-phase framework: scope definition, competency question elicitation (14 questions from CPI domain experts), ontology selection, mapping/modelling, and ETL implementation with validation. The ontology follows a modular hub-and-spoke architecture: a shared core TBox (90 classes, 68 subclass axioms, 56 properties) aligned to BFO, plus platform-specific spoke modules for the AKTA OligoSynt synthesizer and Thermofisher Orbitrap LC-MS. W3C standards are used throughout — SOSA/SSN for sensors and observations, PROV-O for provenance, QUDT for units, DCAT for dataset descriptions — alongside domain ontologies IOF and ChEBI. An ETL pipeline transforms raw CSV instrument exports into RDF triples via RML mappings executed by the morph-kgc engine, loaded into GraphDB for SPARQL querying. Two modelling patterns are central: collection-level provenance (consolidating observation sets under a shared prov:wasGeneratedBy) and the SOSA result pattern (sosa:hasResult pointing to a separate result node for explicit QUDT unit assignment). Static knowledge (instrument models, terminology) is authored in Excel and serialised to OWL/Turtle; dynamic experimental data flows through the automated ETL path. Key Results: The resulting knowledge graph contains 2,420,028 RDF triples — 1,050,195 explicit and 1,369,833 inferred through reasoning — with synthesis observations alone contributing 881,735 triples. All 14 competency questions were answered correctly with no false positives or missed results, validated by manual cross-checking against source spreadsheets. Simple traceability queries completed in under 1 second; cross-workflow queries over nested observation collections finished within a few seconds. A self-assessment against the Pistoia Alliance FAIR Maturity Matrix placed the system at Level 3 ("Pretty FAIR") out of 4, falling short of Level 4 due to lack of public persistent identifiers and organisational governance policies. The system replaces manual spreadsheet reconciliation for cross-platform queries such as linking synthesis molecular weight targets to LC-MS fractional abundance measurements. Strengths: - Practically grounded: developed collaboratively with domain experts at CPI who defined the competency questions, ensuring the ontology addresses real operational needs rather than hypothetical ones. - Principled reuse of established standards (SOSA/SSN, PROV-O, QUDT, IOF, ChEBI) rather than building from scratch, improving interoperability and long-term maintainability. - Modular hub-and-spoke design cleanly separates stable shared concepts from platform-specific and batch-specific data, making the architecture extensible to additional instruments without disrupting the core. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluated on a single batch run only; no demonstration of robustness across multiple batches or different synthesis campaigns, which severely limits confidence in the ontology's generality and the ETL pipeline's stability. - No SHACL shapes implemented, meaning automated data integrity and constraint checking are absent — a significant gap for a system intended for regulated pharmaceutical environments where data quality assurance is mandatory. - Scalability of the full RDF materialisation approach is unresolved: time-series sensor data dominates triple count, and the authors only note Ontology-Based Data Access (OBDA) as a future alternative without demonstrating it; there is no performance benchmarking beyond simple query response times.
Search Results
#199 — Incremental (37)

Aerial Agentic AI: Synergizing LLM and SLM for Low-Altitude Wireless Networks

Li Dong, Feibo Jiang, Kezhi Wang, Cunhua Pan, Dong In Kim, Ekram Hossain · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The hierarchical SLM/LLM decomposition maps sensibly onto the UAV resource asymmetry, and the semantic summary synchronization mechanism is a reasonable design choice. However, this is primarily a framework paper: the central technical claim — that distilled SLMs retain meaningful LLM-level task performance — is never evaluated, the experimental scope is a single simulated task with four UAVs, and no comparison against non-LLM baselines (classical planners, DRL) is provided to isolate the contribution of the agentic architecture.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Low-Altitude Wireless Networks (LAWNs), built on UAVs and mobile terminals, are positioned as a key 6G extension for inspection, monitoring, and emergency response. Deploying Large Language Models directly in this context faces three hard constraints: UAVs lack the compute and energy budget for LLM inference; low-altitude links are bandwidth-limited and intermittent, making continuous context transmission impractical; and LLM generation latency is fundamentally incompatible with the tight perception–decision–control loops aerial platforms require. Existing edge intelligence work addresses individual constraints but lacks a unified architecture that handles all three simultaneously. Methodology: The paper proposes Aerial Agentic AI, a hierarchical two-tier agent framework. UAVs run fast-thinking Small Language Models (SLMs) for on-board perception, real-time decision-making, and local tool execution, while a Base Station (BS) runs slow-thinking LLMs for global planning, long-term memory, and complex tool orchestration. The architecture has three main components: (1) a six-stage LLM-to-SLM capability transfer pipeline (data collection → curation → task clustering → SLM selection → distillation → continual fine-tuning via LoRA/QLoRA); (2) a two-tier memory system where UAVs hold lightweight short-term episodic caches and the BS maintains a Milvus-backed long-term knowledge base, with synchronization via semantic summaries rather than raw context backhaul; and (3) a hierarchical tool framework where UAVs invoke deterministic on-board APIs (sensors, flight control) and the BS orchestrates high-compute tool chains (multi-UAV path planning, global resource scheduling). SLMs are optimized for aerial deployment via AWQ quantization (INT8/INT4), structured pruning, KV cache management, operator fusion, and Flash Attention. The experimental setup uses TinyLlama-1.1B on a Jetson Orin NX (UAV) and LLaMA2-7B on dual A100 GPUs (BS), evaluated on a simulated urban monitoring task with four UAVs. Key Results: On the SLM optimization side, the combined TinyLlama + AWQ + Operator Fusion + Flash Attention configuration achieves deployable inference energy and latency on the Jetson Orin NX, while LLaMA2-7B with AWQ alone still exceeds the platform's operational capacity — confirming that the SLM is necessary, not just preferable. On the trajectory planning task, the collaborative SLM-LLM scheme (UAVs make 9 local decisions between BS synchronizations) achieves near-optimal trajectory length comparable to the pure G-LLM baseline while keeping decision latency close to the local L-SLM baseline. Pure L-SLM achieves lowest latency but converges to local optima on complex paths; pure G-LLM produces shortest trajectories but at the highest latency. Specific numeric values for trajectory lengths and latencies are visible only in figures and not stated precisely in the text. Strengths: - The hierarchical SLM/LLM decomposition is a principled fit to the actual resource asymmetry of UAV vs. BS platforms, and the six-stage capability transfer pipeline is more operationally concrete than typical framework papers in this space. - The two-tier memory architecture (semantic summary synchronization rather than raw context backhaul) directly addresses the bandwidth bottleneck with a practical mechanism rather than a conceptual gesture. - The experiments, though limited in scope, use real hardware (Jetson Orin NX, A100) and compare meaningful ablations of optimization techniques, lending credibility to the feasibility claims. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The experimental evaluation is narrow: a single simulated urban monitoring task with four UAVs, no ablation of the distillation pipeline, and no comparison against non-LLM baselines (e.g., classical planners or DRL agents), making it difficult to assess how much of the gain is attributable to the agentic AI architecture versus the underlying path-planning algorithms. - The paper does not quantify the communication overhead of semantic summary synchronization, the memory footprint of the Milvus knowledge base, or the latency of the BS-side LLM reasoning loop under realistic link conditions — all critical for assessing whether the scheme is viable in actual LAWN deployments. - The capability transfer pipeline (stages S1–S6) is described procedurally but never evaluated: there are no results showing whether distilled SLMs actually retain meaningful LLM-level task performance, which is the central technical claim enabling the entire architecture.
Search Results 2603.22866 PDF
#200 — Incremental (37)

Extracting Non-Taxonomic and Ternary Relations from Patient-Generated Texts for Semantic Interoperability

researchsquare.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The delayed fusion strategy and ternary relation formulation for patient-generated text show thoughtful design, and using BioBERT as a zero-shot validation sieve is a pragmatic choice. However, the overall pipeline (BiLSTM + transformer validation + dictionary features) is a standard combination of known components, the 66.7% precision is concerning, and the unpublished preprint has significant reporting gaps that undermine confidence in the results.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Patient-generated texts from online health forums contain clinically valuable information but lack the structure required for interoperable health databases. Existing relation extraction work overwhelmingly focuses on hierarchical (taxonomic) relations such as broader/narrower, neglecting non-taxonomic relations (associative, causal, functional, risk-factor, statistical) and ternary (N-ary) relations that capture multi-concept clinical contexts. This gap leads to incomplete knowledge graphs that cannot resolve ambiguity in isolated clinical facts — for example, knowing that mirtazapine both relieves anxiety and causes fatigue requires contextual qualification that binary relations cannot provide. The paper targets semantic interoperability for mental health data (anxiety and depression) specifically. Methodology: The authors propose a four-layer knowledge-infused neural framework combining a BiLSTM with a delayed fusion strategy and BioBERT validation. The architecture has two input streams: a sequential text input (seq_input) processed through bidirectional LSTM layers with layer normalization, and a dictionary feature input (dict_feat) derived from a locally constructed clinical ontology seeded with SNOMED CT terms and expanded via WordNet synonyms (capped at 5 per variant). The two streams are fused via an attention mechanism that weights dictionary features against neural representations, balancing contextual learning with interpretable rule-based knowledge. The compact BiLSTM (26.78 MB total, 15.33 MB trainable) first extracts candidate entity-relation triples; then BioBERT performs zero-shot validation at a confidence threshold of 0.4, acting as a precision sieve to filter semantically unsound relations. The corpus consists of 38,115 documents (27,183 key phrases) from anxiety/depression online forums (patient.info, HealthUnlocked, Mental Health Forum, Kaggle Depression Dataset), split 80/20 for training and testing. Two concept extraction strategies are compared: a hybrid per-class adaptive strategy (113 unique concepts, conservative) and a hybrid union strategy (222 unique concepts, broader coverage). Key Results: The full framework achieved 98.91% accuracy and 77.6% F1 score on the test set, representing a 10% F1 improvement and 17.2% recall improvement over a standalone BiLSTM baseline (F1=67.7%, recall=75.6%, precision=61.3%). The framework produced 414 candidate relations after deduplication from an initial 5,089 extractions; BioBERT validated 384 of these (92.7% validation rate). Of the validated relations, 240 were ternary (expanded from 55 initially extracted by BiLSTM, as BioBERT's pre-trained biomedical knowledge identified additional valid contexts) and 144 were non-taxonomic (40.1% of the 359 initially proposed). Confidence scores by category ranged from 75% (statistical) to 96% (associative), with an overall average of 87.4%. Jaccard similarity between extracted and ground-truth predicates was 0.667, and Hamming loss was 0.029. Per-class F1 scores were 0.789 (treatment), 0.768 (symptoms), and 0.657 (disease), confirming difficulty with the minority class (disease, 18 concepts vs. symptoms at 53). Strengths: - The delayed fusion strategy is a principled design choice that avoids early fusion pitfalls, letting neural and symbolic components each develop independent representations before integration via attention — this is substantiated by the measurable gain over standalone BiLSTM. - The use of BioBERT as a zero-shot validation layer (rather than a fine-tuned classifier) is computationally lightweight and leverages pre-trained biomedical knowledge without requiring additional labeled data for relation validation. - The ternary relation structure (concept, relation, concept | {clinical context}) concretely addresses the ambiguity-of-isolated-facts problem, demonstrated with a worked clinical example (mirtazapine relieving and causing harm simultaneously depending on temporal context). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The precision of 66.7% is low and the BioBERT validation rate for non-taxonomic relations was only 40.1% (144/359), suggesting significant noise in the initial BiLSTM extraction; the paper frames this positively but does not deeply analyze what makes non-taxonomic relations so difficult to validate. - The dataset is limited to English-language online forums for anxiety and depression only; generalizability to other conditions, languages, clinical note styles, or structured EHR text is entirely undemonstrated. - The paper is a Research Square preprint that has not undergone peer review, and key experimental details are missing or inconsistently reported (e.g., the abstract claims 240 ternary relations validated but Table 3 shows 240 emerging from BioBERT expanding on 55 BiLSTM extractions, which is methodologically unclear and not well explained; figures referenced in the text as 'Fig. 6' and 'Table 10' do not appear in the document).

#201 — Incremental (36)

Dual-Distillation Vision-Language Model for Multimodal Emotion Recognition in Conversation with Quantized Edge Deployment

mdpi.com · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 36 as an applied combination of established techniques — EMA self-distillation, cross-modal KD, residual gating, weight-only quantization — assembled for a specific use case. The VLM-to-text-space visual encoding is a reasonable design choice but not novel, and evaluation on a single dataset (MELD) with the audio modality omitted limits the strength of the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Emotion Recognition in Conversation (ERC) must handle a fundamental reliability gap: text achieves ~67% WA-F1 on MELD while vision achieves only ~34%, meaning naive fusion can actually degrade performance by injecting visual noise. Existing multimodal ERC methods also rely on purely vision-based (CNN/Transformer) encoders whose representations are not semantically aligned with text, and they are built on large pretrained backbones (RoBERTa, LLMs) that are impractical on resource-constrained edge devices like NVIDIA Jetson due to memory and latency constraints. No prior ERC work has systematically addressed both the modality-imbalance problem and practical edge deployability together. Methodology: The paper proposes DDVLM, a four-component system. (1) Feature extraction: text uses RoBERTa with speaker tokens and an emotion prompt ("Now <si> feels <mask>"), yielding a 768-d embedding from the [mask] token. Visual features are extracted by prompting Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct to generate 2–3 objective, emotion-word-free sentences describing facial/body state per utterance clip, then embedded via Sentence-BERT (all-mpnet-base-v2) into a 768-d vector semantically aligned with the text space. (2) EMA-based self-distillation on the text modality: an EMA teacher (decay λ=0.99) generates smoothed soft targets; the text student is trained with a combined cross-entropy + KL loss (α=0.7 hard/soft balance, temperature τ=2), with KL only activated from epoch 2 onward to allow the EMA teacher to stabilize. (3) Cross-modal knowledge distillation from text (teacher) to visual (student): two losses are combined — response-based distillation using Pearson correlation across both inter-class and intra-class dimensions (temperature τ=4), and feature-based relational distillation using KL divergence on pairwise similarity matrices augmented with a JSD stability term (λ_JSD=0.2). (4) Residual fusion: visual embeddings are projected into text space via a two-layer MLP, and a gated residual correction δ — modulated by a gate value (clamped 0.05–0.5), cosine similarity between modalities, and visual prediction confidence — is added to the original text logits. This preserves textual dominance while selectively injecting aligned visual cues. For edge deployment, a Weight-Only Quantization (WOQ) pipeline converts linear-layer weights to INT8 via per-channel max-based scaling (AWQ-lite), while retaining activations and LayerNorm in FP16/FP32, structured as three decoupled modules (text encoder, VLM encoder, fusion) for independent quantization. Key Results: On MELD (7-way classification), DDVLM achieves 68.28% accuracy and 67.80% WA-F1, surpassing the prior best (CoTCL: 68.00% accuracy, CBERL: 66.89% WA-F1). Critically, DDVLM is evaluated on all 7 emotion classes including fear and disgust, which several competing methods exclude; it achieves 31.25% F1 on fear and 30.91% on disgust, 5–20% above those that do report these classes. Ablation confirms all components contribute: removing cross-modal KD drops 0.92%, removing self-distillation drops 0.38%, removing residual fusion drops 0.56%. Visual-only without cross-modal KD drops 2.19 points. On NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin, the full WOQ pipeline (Experiment 3) reduces the text encoder from ~1.32 GB to 409.2 MB, cuts end-to-end inference latency from 200.35 ms to 42.51 ms/sample, and reaches 23.52 samples/s throughput, while preserving 66.27% WA-F1 (vs. 67.80% FP32 baseline — only 1.53% drop). Competing quantization strategies either introduce larger accuracy drops (TensorRT INT8: 65.81%), prohibitive latency (Dynamic INT8: 616 ms/sample), or structural incompatibilities with the dynamic-length text encoder. Strengths: - The VLM-to-text-space visual representation strategy (Qwen2.5-VL → Sentence-BERT) is a principled solution to the cross-modal alignment problem, converting visual signals into a shared semantic space rather than forcing a heterogeneous fusion. - The dual-distillation design (EMA self-distillation to stabilize the teacher before cross-modal KD) is a coherent hierarchical approach that addresses a real failure mode: a noisy teacher corrupts student learning. - The edge deployment evaluation is rare in ERC literature and practically valuable; the paper honestly characterizes the trade-offs across four competing quantization strategies on real hardware. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The VLM visual pipeline (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct generating text descriptions) is preprocessing-intensive and not end-to-end trainable; subtle visual cues like micro-expressions or facial dynamics may be lost or distorted during the visual-to-text conversion step, fundamentally limiting what the visual modality can contribute. - The audio modality is entirely absent despite MELD providing it, and several competing baselines use text+audio+visual. The paper frames this as text+visual only, which makes SoTA claims across the board misleading since the model has less input than some competitors. - All experiments use a single dataset (MELD, derived from the TV show Friends), limiting generalizability; the heavy reliance on conversational domain specificity of the VLM prompting may not transfer to naturalistic or cross-cultural settings.

#202 — Incremental (35)

A Stream Reasoning Framework for Thermal Image-Based Anomaly Detection in Lithium-Ion Batteries

scitepress.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The framework applies established knowledge representation tools (OWL ontologies, C-SPARQL stream reasoning) to battery thermal monitoring. While the interpretability advantage is real and valuable for safety-critical applications, the methodology assembles existing components with domain-specific thresholds rather than introducing novel detection techniques. The evaluation on a single controlled test bench with fixed thresholds further limits the generalizability claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Lithium-ion battery degradation poses safety and longevity risks in electric vehicles and energy storage systems. Existing anomaly detection approaches—statistical methods, deep learning (autoencoders, LSTMs), and thermal imaging with CNNs—either lack interpretability due to their black-box nature or require large labeled datasets. There is also a gap in the literature: no ontology exists that models the nuances of thermal images specifically for battery degradation. This paper addresses both the interpretability deficit and the data-scarcity problem by proposing a knowledge-based, rule-driven framework. Methodology: The framework integrates three components: (1) thermal image segmentation, (2) a domain ontology in OWL, and (3) stream reasoning using C-SPARQL. Images are first cropped to isolate the battery cell, then converted to HSV color space for segmentation via predefined thresholds plus morphological refinement. A small neural network (RGB→temperature, 3-32-16-1 layers, MAE=0.0086°C) maps pixel colors to temperature values. Each detected hotspot segment (centroid, area, temperature) is serialized as RDF quadruples forming a knowledge graph. The ontology, which extends the BattINFO battery ontology, defines classes (Battery, BatteryCell, Image, Segment, Pixel, Anomaly, HighTemperatureAnomaly, ThermalImbalance) and object properties linking spatial/temporal relationships. The stream reasoner executes sliding-window C-SPARQL queries that detect: localized overheating (segment temperature > 32°C), thermal imbalance (left/right average temperature difference > 0.8°C), hotspot growth (segment area increase > 20 units in same spatial region between consecutive frames), and temperature propagation from center to extremes (>0.05°C rise in adjacent region). Static SWRL rules handle instantaneous threshold violations; C-SPARQL handles temporal evolution. Detected anomalies are instantiated as new OWL individuals and linked to source images and segments. Key Results: Evaluated on 1,300 thermal images from a FLIR T560 camera during controlled cycling experiments, the framework is compared against six unsupervised baselines (STFPM, DRAEM, PaDiM, FastFlow, PatchCore, FAUAD) using AUROC under clean and 10%-contaminated (noisy) conditions. The proposed method achieves AUROC = 1.000 on clean data (tied with FAUAD and FastFlow) and AUROC = 0.920 under 10% contamination, outperforming STFPM (0.871), PaDiM (0.863), FastFlow (0.793), and PatchCore (0.774). Only DRAEM (0.922) and FAUAD (0.990) match or slightly exceed it under contamination. Critically, all baselines require high data, offer no symbolic reasoning, and are non-interpretable; the proposed framework requires only low data, high domain knowledge, and is fully interpretable and traceable. Strengths: - Interpretability by design: every anomaly detection can be traced to an explicit expert rule (e.g., "segment A exceeded 2°C rise within 30s"), which is valuable in safety-critical battery monitoring where black-box decisions are unacceptable. - Low data dependency: the framework requires no large labeled training corpus; expert rules substitute for learned representations, addressing the chronic data scarcity in battery failure datasets. - Temporal reasoning capability: C-SPARQL sliding windows capture evolving patterns (hotspot growth, thermal propagation) that per-frame static analysis cannot detect, representing a genuine advance over threshold-only approaches. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The AUROC comparison is methodologically uneven: the knowledge-based system operates on pre-extracted semantic features (centroid, area, temperature) while the data-driven baselines operate on raw images—the comparison partially evaluates different input representations rather than detection paradigms on equal footing. - Fixed thresholds (32°C overheating, 0.8°C imbalance, 0.05°C propagation) are brittle: they are validated on a single controlled test bench with one battery type and chemistry, and it is unclear how these values generalize across different cell geometries, cooling architectures, or operating profiles. - The segmentation component, which is the entry point to the entire pipeline, is not evaluated quantitatively; segmentation errors propagate silently into the reasoning stage and no ablation isolates their impact on end-to-end detection performance.
Search Results
#203 — Incremental (35)

A Comparative Study of Large Language Models for Gesture Selection in Virtual Agents

researchsquare.com · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: This is primarily a comparative study applying off-the-shelf LLMs to gesture selection rather than proposing a new method. The counterintuitive finding that Llama3 outperforms GPT-5.2 due to less over-generation is interesting but undermined by an extremely small evaluation corpus (20 utterances, one speaker, two non-blind co-author annotators).
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Co-speech gestures significantly impact face-to-face interaction, influencing comprehension, recall, engagement, and trust. Embodied virtual agents benefit from appropriate gestures, yet automating gesture selection remains unsolved. Rule-based systems offer designer control but lack flexibility; data-driven (end-to-end) models generate fluid motion but struggle with semantically meaningful gestures such as metaphorics. The paper asks whether LLMs can fill this gap — selecting contextually appropriate, role-consistent gestures from utterance text — while also operating fast enough for real-time virtual human interaction. Methodology: The authors formulate gesture selection as two sub-tasks: when to gesture (utterance segment identification via rheme/theme discourse analysis) and what gesture to use (selecting from a predefined vocabulary of seven image-schema-grounded gestural intents: Progress, Regress, Cycle, Collect, Container, Oscillation, Temporal). Ground truth annotations are drawn from 6 minutes of video of labor activist Elizabeth Shuler (21 training utterances, 20 test utterances). Four prompting strategies are evaluated on GPT-4 in a 2×2 factorial design (gestural intent descriptions ± / annotated examples ±). Expert evaluation (two gesture researchers) judges appropriateness at both gesture and utterance levels. For real-time feasibility, three additional models are compared: GPT-4 (baseline), Llama3 (local, RTX 3090), and GPT-5.2 instant. Inference is reported as network/local latency. The full pipeline converts LLM JSON output to Behavior Markup Language (BML) for SmartBody animation in the SIMA virtual agent architecture. Key Results: On GPT-4 prompting approaches, enriching the prompt with gestural intent descriptions and speaker-annotated examples (Approach 3) yields the highest appropriateness and alignment; the unconstrained baseline (Approach 0) produces the most inappropriate gestures and worst timing alignment. For the rheme/theme analysis (when-to-gesture), GPT-4 correctly locates 43 of 49 speaker gestures within the predicted rheme (87.8% recall). For model comparison using Approach 3, gesture-level appropriateness is: Llama3 80.8% (21/26), GPT-4 75.9% (22/29), GPT-5.2 72.2% (26/36). At the stricter utterance level (all gestures in an utterance must be appropriate), Llama3 leads at 77.3% (17/22), followed by GPT-4 72.7% (16/22) and GPT-5.2 59.1% (13/22). GPT-5.2 produces 36 gestures across 22 utterances vs. Llama3's 26, demonstrating over-generation. Llama3 has the highest match rate (34%) to gold-standard gestures and the lowest false-positive rate (~36%) on utterances where the speaker does not gesture. Inference times: Llama3 avg 0.61 s, GPT-5.2 avg 1.12 s, GPT-4 avg 6.51–9.29 s — only Llama3 meets the sub-one-second target for real-time use. Strengths: - Grounded evaluation against annotated human gestural performance with expert judging, providing a meaningful gold standard rather than crowd-sourced ratings. - The counterintuitive and practically important finding that smaller/weaker models (Llama3) outperform larger ones (GPT-5.2) on gesture selection quality, specifically because they over-generate less — a concrete and actionable insight for deployment. - End-to-end pipeline demonstrated in a real virtual agent system (SIMA + SmartBody), bridging the gap between LLM output and animatable BML, which many gesture-selection papers omit. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation corpus is extremely small: 20 test utterances from a single speaker in one domain (labor activism), making it impossible to assess generalization across roles, topics, cultures, or individual gestural styles. - Expert appropriateness judgments are made by two annotators (who are co-authors), without independent inter-rater reliability statistics or blind evaluation — the near-zero reported disagreement is implausible and raises validity concerns. - The gestural intent vocabulary (7 classes) is hand-crafted from training data of one speaker, severely limiting scope; the paper does not address how the approach scales to other speakers or broader gesture taxonomies.
Search Results
#204 — Incremental (34)

Wikontic: A Tool for Building Knowledge Graphs from Text Aligned with the Wikidata Ontology

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Wikontic combines entity canonicalization with Wikidata ontology enforcement in a clean three-stage pipeline, but the contribution is primarily systems integration. As a 2-page demo paper with no quantitative evaluation on standard benchmarks, it is impossible to assess whether the ontology alignment actually improves extraction quality over simpler baselines.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Open information extraction (oIE) pipelines that use LLMs to build knowledge graphs from raw text suffer from noisy, redundant, and ambiguous graphs. Surface form variation (e.g., "NYC" vs. "New York City"), synonymy, overlapping predicates, and the absence of schema constraints yield incoherent triples that undermine the structural advantages of KGs. Existing frameworks (LlamaIndex, LangChain, Neo4j, GraphRAG, HippoRAG, AriGraph, KGGen) either lack ontology grounding entirely or perform only partial deduplication, leaving a gap for a system that combines flexible LLM extraction with rigorous schema enforcement and full entity canonicalization. Methodology: Wikontic is a three-stage pipeline. Stage 1 (Candidate Triplet Extraction) uses an LLM to extract subject–relation–object triples enriched with entity types and contextual qualifiers (time, location, condition). Stage 2 (Ontology-aware Triplet Refinement) maps extracted entity types to Wikidata entries via dense vector retrieval over a curated ontology database of 2,414 unique relation types, expands them through the type hierarchy (P31 instance-of, P279 subclass-of), retrieves legal relations between candidate types from Wikidata's schema, ranks them by embedding similarity to the original relation, and has the LLM rewrite the triple with the ontology-compliant type–relation pair. Stage 3 (Subject/Object Name Refinement) links each entity mention to existing same-type KG nodes via embedding similarity and LLM verification; matched entities adopt the canonical label while the original surface form is stored as an alias, and unmatched mentions become new nodes. For multi-hop QA, entity mentions in queries are linked to KG entries and their 5-hop neighborhoods are retrieved as structured context for the LLM. A Streamlit web interface ties the components together. Key Results: The paper is a demo/system paper presented at AAAI-26 and does not report quantitative benchmarks on extraction quality or QA accuracy. The primary contribution is a qualitative comparison (Table 1) showing that Wikontic is the only system among seven evaluated frameworks to combine full entity deduplication/canonicalization with automatic Wikidata ontology enforcement. The running example (text about Christopher Nolan directing Inception) illustrates how the pipeline corrects an extracted "movie" type to "film" and a "directed" relation to "director" in compliance with Wikidata schemas. Strengths: - Unique combination of full entity canonicalization with alias tracking and automatic Wikidata ontology enforcement — no competing system in the comparison achieves both simultaneously. - Ontology database construction is principled: 2,414 relation types with recursive type hierarchy expansion, enabling broad domain coverage without manual schema design per use case. - Contextual qualifiers (time, location, condition) are extracted alongside core triples, preserving nuance that flat triple representations typically discard. Weaknesses / Limitations: - No quantitative evaluation: there are no precision/recall/F1 numbers on standard KG construction benchmarks (e.g., REBEL, WebNLG, NYT), making it impossible to assess actual extraction quality or ontology alignment accuracy. - Wikidata coupling is both a strength and a constraint — entities or relations outside Wikidata's 100M+ entity coverage will be forced into the closest ontology entry, potentially introducing systematic type errors for specialized or emerging domains. - The paper is two pages (demo track), so the description of the deduplication and refinement steps is high-level; key design choices such as embedding model selection, thresholds for entity merging, and behavior under ontology mismatches are not discussed.
Recommended
#205 — Incremental (34)

A Survey on LLM-based Conversational User Simulation

aclanthology.org · Nesreen K. Ahmed - new articles
Why this rank: This survey provides a well-structured Who/What/How taxonomy that usefully organizes the fragmented conversational user simulation literature. However, as a purely organizational contribution with no original experiments or benchmarking, its novelty is limited to the taxonomic framework itself, which follows a standard survey methodology.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Conversational user simulation—generating synthetic human-like dialogue—is critical for training dialogue systems, evaluating conversational AI, and augmenting scarce labeled data. Prior to LLMs, user simulators were domain-specific, data-hungry, and unable to capture the complexity and variability of human behavior. While LLMs have catalyzed rapid progress, the field lacked a unified taxonomy and systematic analysis. This survey fills that gap by organizing the fragmented literature into a coherent framework. Methodology: The authors introduce a three-axis taxonomy structured around three questions: (1) Who is being simulated — ranging from general/default users (§3.1), persona-level users grounded in demographics and psychometrics (§3.2), role-play simulation of real or fictional characters (§3.3), to individual user simulation grounded in personal history (§3.4), plus hybrid combinations; (2) What interaction paradigm is being simulated — Human–AI (most common, used for instruction tuning), Human–Human, AI–AI (emergent behavior studies, CAMEL, AutoGen), Many-Human–AI (collaborative multi-party), and hybrids; (3) How the simulation is technically realized — via prompt-based methods (zero-shot, few-shot, CoT, persona/task-specific prompting), RAG (always-on, adaptive, goal/state-driven), supervised fine-tuning (full-model, parameter-efficient LoRA, interactive self-play), RL/DPO (personalization, memory, long-horizon planning, hindsight learning), and hybrid pipelines combining multiple techniques. The survey also reviews evaluation methodology (BLEU/ROUGE, human judges, LLM-as-Judge), datasets (30+ categorized), and applications (recommendation, education, HCI, video understanding). Key Results: The survey covers 100+ representative works. Key empirical findings reported from role-playing benchmarks: on RoleLLM, prompt-based large models (RoleGPT: avg 47.7) outperform fine-tuned smaller models overall but fine-tuned models show stronger role-specific knowledge (SPE: 48.8 for RoleLLaMA2-13B vs. 32.3 for RoleGPT). On WikiRole, GPT-4 achieves 80% accuracy but Qwen-72B+Chat reaches 90%. Across benchmarks, fine-tuning consistently improves role-specific knowledge over prompting, while RAG-based approaches (e.g., ChatPLUG: avg 28.2) underperform both, suggesting fine-tuning is more effective for character elicitation than retrieval. The survey identifies three open challenges: (1) long-conversation persona drift, (2) cultural/behavioral homogeneity in simulated populations, and (3) toxicity and bias propagation when simulating demographic or public-figure personas. Strengths: - Comprehensive and well-structured taxonomy (Who/What/How) that unifies previously fragmented work under a principled framework with formal definitions - Broad coverage: 100+ papers across simulation targets, interaction paradigms, techniques, evaluation methods, datasets, and applications, with a detailed comparison table (Table 1) mapping each work to all taxonomy dimensions - Identifies non-obvious gaps, particularly the under-theorized Many-Human–AI and hybrid simulation paradigms, and the absence of standardized benchmarks for multi-party conversational settings Weaknesses / Limitations: - No original empirical benchmarking: all quantitative comparisons are drawn from individual papers with different experimental setups, making cross-method conclusions unreliable — the survey acknowledges this explicitly - The taxonomy, while thorough, has boundary cases (hybrid or domain-specific methods) that do not map cleanly, and the "Hybrid" catch-all categories in both Who and What/How axes reduce taxonomic precision - Coverage is heavily skewed toward English-language systems and Western benchmark datasets, with limited discussion of multilingual or culturally diverse simulation
Recommended
#206 — Incremental (34)

Multi-GPU accelerated hydrodynamic model for rapid and high-resolution urban flood simulation

skxjz.nhri.cn · karypis - new results
Why this rank: This is a competent engineering integration of well-established components — METIS partitioning, MPI domain decomposition, OpenACC GPU kernels, SWMM sewer coupling — into a production multi-GPU flood simulation pipeline. The 26× speedup and sub-10-minute city-scale simulation are practically valuable, but the methodology applies standard HPC parallelization techniques without algorithmic novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Urban flood forecasting at city scale requires fine spatial resolution (meter-level grids) to capture complex urban terrain, yet explicit hydrodynamic solvers are constrained by CFL time-step conditions that scale inversely with grid refinement. Simulating an 800 km² urban area at 4 m resolution produces ~50 million computational cells, making real-time or near-real-time flood prediction with CPU-based models infeasible. Single-GPU approaches hit physical memory and compute ceilings. The gap addressed is the lack of production-ready multi-GPU hydrodynamic models capable of city-scale, high-resolution flood nowcasting within operationally useful time windows. Methodology: The authors develop a three-module coupled hydrodynamic model: (1) a 2D surface runoff module solving the shallow water equations (SWE) via Godunov-type finite volume method on unstructured mixed triangular/quadrilateral grids, using the HLLC Riemann solver, SRM slope-source discretization, and semi-implicit friction treatment; (2) a surface–sewer interaction module computing inlet drainage and manhole overflow via weir and orifice formulas; and (3) a 1D underground sewer module based on the SWMM Saint-Venant solver with implicit Euler time integration. For multi-GPU parallelization, the computational domain is partitioned using the METIS multilevel k-way graph partitioning library, with each partition assigned to one GPU. Ghost/boundary/interior cell classification enables asynchronous non-blocking MPI communication (MPI_Isend/Irecv) overlapped with interior cell computation, minimizing communication latency. GPU kernels are implemented via OpenACC directives (compiled with NVIDIA Nvfortran 24.3), with explicit GPU memory management and cache-aware mesh reordering (ghost → boundary → interior) to maximize GPU cache hit rates. I/O is parallelized using distributed binary formats. The model is validated against the May 2024 "24·5" extreme rainstorm in the Zhongshundawei region of Guangdong Province (~811 km²), using high-resolution radar nowcasts from Hong Kong Observatory's SWIRLS system (2 km resolution) as boundary forcing. Key Results: Against 13 waterlogging observation points, the model achieves R²=0.91 and mean absolute error of 0.11 m in simulated inundation depth; at three representative points the error is below 0.1 m. Computational benchmarks on 8× NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada (48 GB VRAM each) vs. 64-core AMD EPYC 9534 CPU show: at 4 m resolution (50.68M cells), 8-GPU parallel achieves 579.2 s vs. CPU's 15,280.8 s — a 26.38× speedup. At 10 m resolution (8.11M cells), 8-GPU completes in 71.8 s vs. CPU's 1,609.7 s (19.68×). Notably, the 4 m / 4-GPU case achieves 11× speedup vs. CPU, which is super-linear relative to single-GPU (2.81×), attributed to parallel I/O and improved GPU cache utilization from finer per-partition mesh sizes. The full 6-hour flood process for 811 km² at 4 m resolution completes in under 10 minutes — operationally suitable for flood nowcasting with 1–2 hour lead times. Strengths: - Demonstrates a practically deployable end-to-end multi-GPU pipeline: Metis partitioning → MPI domain decomposition → OpenACC GPU kernels → asynchronous overlap of communication and computation, validated at real city scale with 50 million cells. - Super-linear scaling at high resolution (26.38× with 8 GPUs vs. 64-core CPU) is a strong engineering result, with mechanistic explanation (parallel I/O, cache locality from reordering) rather than mere empirical observation. - Tight physical coupling of 2D surface runoff, 1D sewer (SWMM), and surface–sewer exchange modules enables realistic urban dual-drainage simulation, with partial sewer network data compensated by equivalent infiltration where detailed pipe data are unavailable. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Static mesh partitioning generates "idle" computation in the early simulation phase when only a fraction of the domain is wet — dry cells still participate in the compute loop, wasting GPU cycles. The authors acknowledge this and flag dynamic partitioning as future work, but the magnitude of this inefficiency is not quantified. - Validation is limited to 13 point observations of inundation depth extracted from news images and disaster reports, not systematic gauging or remote sensing; the uncertainty in these reference values is acknowledged but not characterized, weakening the R²=0.91 claim. - The 1D sewer module runs only on a single CPU process (OpenMP within MPI rank 0); for large-scale sewer networks or future scenarios requiring GPU acceleration of the pipe network, the current architecture would bottleneck at the sewer solver, an issue recognized but not resolved.
Search Results
#207 — Incremental (33)

GLUScope: A Tool for Analyzing GLU Neurons in Transformer Language Models

Sebastian Gerstner, Hinrich Schütze · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 33 as a useful but incremental engineering contribution. The quadrant decomposition for GLU neurons is a sensible architectural observation, and the gate−/in− finding on neuron 31.9634 is a nice demonstration. However, this is a system paper with no new theoretical results, no quantitative evaluation of interpretability quality, coverage of only a single model, and explicit inability to handle MoE or non-Transformer architectures increasingly dominant at the frontier.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mechanistic interpretability research on transformer language models has historically relied on tools designed for vanilla activation functions (ReLU, GELU, Swish). However, modern LLMs — including OLMo, Llama, and Gemma — widely use gated activation functions (GLU variants) such as SwiGLU and GEGLU. In these architectures, each MLP neuron's output is the product of two scalars: Swish(x_gate) · x_in, where both x_gate and x_in can independently be positive or negative, yielding four distinct sign combinations with potentially different functional roles. Prior tools like Neuroscope implicitly assume that large positive activations are the primary signal worth examining, which is insufficient for gated neurons: a highly interpretable activation pattern may only appear in the gate−/in− quadrant at small absolute magnitude, and would be missed entirely by any tool that ranks by overall activation magnitude. Methodology: GLUScope is an open-source interpretability tool comprising three released artifacts: (1) a curated 20M-token Dolma subset (45,734 texts, ≤1024 tokens each) used as the analysis corpus; (2) a precomputed activation dataset for OLMo-7B-0424, recording — for every neuron across all layers — the frequency of each of the four sign combinations (gate+/in+, gate+/in−, gate−/in+, gate−/in−) plus the top-16 and bottom-16 activation examples within each quadrant for the four intermediate values (x_gate, Swish(x_gate), x_in, and the final product); and (3) a web interface that renders per-neuron pages with summary statistics tables and token-highlighted text examples for each sign combination and intermediate activation. The activation dataset was computed by running OLMo-7B-0424 through TransformerLens (with a custom fork for OLMo support) over the corpus, requiring approximately five days on a single A100-80GB GPU. Generating a single neuron page from the precomputed dataset takes 30–60 seconds. The tool is built on TransformerLens and CircuitsVis. Key Results: The paper demonstrates utility through two usage examples on neuron 31.9634 of OLMo-7B-0424. First, a model-wide correlation analysis using the activation dataset reveals a strong negative correlation between cos(w_in, w_out) and the frequency of x_gate > 0 across neurons — a structural relationship not discoverable without the per-quadrant frequency data. Second, detailed inspection of neuron 31.9634 reveals that 67.7% of its activations fall in the gate+/in− quadrant (a heavily negative final output), contrary to what weight-similarity analysis would predict. More strikingly, the gate−/in− quadrant — occurring only 17.34% of the time and producing final activations at most 1.58 in magnitude — is the only quadrant with a clear semantic pattern: these activations consistently occur before the token "again" (e.g., on "once" in "once again"), suggesting the neuron promotes the "again" direction in the residual stream specifically in this sign regime. This pattern would be entirely invisible to Neuroscope-style tools, which would surface gate+/in+ examples (extrema up to 3.48) and miss the interpretable but smaller gate−/in− activations. Strengths: - Identifies a genuine and previously unaddressed gap: existing neuron analysis tools are architecturally mismatched to GLU-based models, and GLUScope's quadrant-decomposition is a principled response to this structural reality. - Follows through on the software imperative — all artifacts (code, activation dataset, Dolma subset, demo website) are publicly released under permissive licenses, enabling reuse for other models without re-running the expensive GPU computation. - The demonstration in section 5.2 is concrete and non-trivial: the gate−/in− finding is a genuinely novel insight that the authors credibly could not have obtained with prior tools, strengthening the tool's motivation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is a system demonstration, not a research contribution in the traditional sense — it presents no new theoretical results, no quantitative comparison of interpretability quality against baselines, and no user study validating that the tool actually accelerates researcher insight. - Coverage is extremely narrow: the activation dataset is precomputed for a single model (OLMo-7B-0424) on a single corpus (20M tokens of Dolma). The five-day A100 runtime per model creates a significant barrier to community extension, and the demo website currently covers only a handful of manually selected neurons out of millions. - The tool explicitly cannot handle MoE architectures (e.g., DeepSeek) or non-Transformer models (e.g., Mamba), limiting applicability to a shrinking fraction of the frontier model landscape as mixture-of-experts designs become standard.
Recommended 2602.23826 PDF
#208 — Incremental (33)

Shifting Models Left: End-to-End Traceability as a Foundation for Knowledge Graphs as Data Products

openreview.net · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The five formal lineage properties (Completeness, Correctness, Minimality, Source Minimality, Graph Shape) provide a useful testable specification for KGC pipelines, and the vertical/horizontal mapping distinction is a practical conceptual contribution. Yet this is a pure vision paper with no implementation or comparison to existing provenance systems like W3C PROV, and the category theory framing is invoked suggestively rather than rigorously.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge Graph Construction (KGC) pipelines that integrate heterogeneous data sources into RDF knowledge graphs currently require deep tacit knowledge from engineers who must manually assemble mapping logic bottom-up. There is no principled, reproducible framework for verifying that source data characteristics are correctly and completely reflected in the resulting graph. This "semantic gap" — the disconnect between how data is structured (schemas) and what it means in context (semantics) — is exacerbated in multi-domain settings where multiple subgraphs must be stitched together (e.g., an IT Network Digital Twin spanning IP backbone, data centers, and optical networks). Existing declarative KGC tools like RML lack a formal connection between data source descriptions and mapping rules, disrupting provenance calculus and making data lineage incomplete. The paper argues this must be solved before knowledge graphs can be treated as trustworthy, auditable data products. Methodology: This is a vision/position paper that proposes a conceptual framework grounded in Semantic Web technologies, category theory, and formal proof systems rather than presenting a new algorithm or implementation. The central contribution is the hypothesis of end-to-end traceability as a design-time requirement for KGC pipelines. The authors formalize five objects in the KGC process — Data Sources, Use Cases, Data Mappings, Application Domains, and Knowledge Graphs — and model their relationships using a category theory perspective with functional edges (a, b, c, c⁻¹). From this they derive three hypotheses: (Hyp. 1) End-to-End Traceability as a fundamental pipeline requirement, decomposed into three formal properties — Lineage Completeness (all source attributes contributing to an RDF triple are traced), Lineage Correctness (no spurious dependencies in the lineage graph), and Lineage Minimality (no unnecessary dependencies). (Hyp. 2) End-to-End Semantic, requiring semantically analyzable source descriptions and formal end-user requirement descriptions, enabling Source Minimality (identifying mandatory source attributes per use case) and Graph Shape determination. (Hyp. 3) Two Axes Mapping, distinguishing Vertical mapping (traversing abstraction levels within a domain, e.g., RML rules lifting tabular records into OWL class hierarchies) from Horizontal mapping (cross-domain alignment at equivalent abstraction levels, e.g., SSSOM mappings). The roadmap proposes algebraic/functional/category-theoretic formalization of RML and SPARQL operations to enable design-time formal proof of lineage properties. Key Results: As a vision paper, there are no experimental results, benchmarks, or quantitative evaluations. The contribution is purely conceptual: a five-property specification framework (Props. 1–5) that, if implemented, would enable auditable multi-domain data lineage, reduced data collection footprints through source minimality, and automated mapping rule inference. The authors illustrate the framework with a running example from the NORIA-O IT network ontology and identify three concrete future research directions: (1) algebraic/category-theoretic dataset compatibility frameworks; (2) formal proof of RML+SPARQL pipelines using dependent types; (3) bridging to non-KG tooling such as the ODCS datacontract-cli. Strengths: - Clearly articulates an important and under-addressed gap in the KGC literature: the absence of a principled, verifiable connection between source schemas, mapping rules, and graph output. - The five formal properties (Lineage Completeness, Correctness, Minimality, Source Minimality, Graph Shape) provide a concrete, testable specification that future implementations can target. - The vertical/horizontal mapping distinction (Hyp. 3) is a useful conceptual contribution that has practical implications for mapping reusability and transferability across domains. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is a pure vision/position paper with no implementation, prototype, or empirical validation; all five properties remain unproven and their feasibility is undemonstrated. - The category theory framing is invoked suggestively rather than rigorously — the paper explicitly notes that c⁻¹ is "an analytical device for formal verification rather than a claim of strict invertibility," which weakens the mathematical grounding. - No comparison to related work on data lineage systems (e.g., W3C PROV, OpenLineage) or existing provenance-tracking RML extensions is performed, making it unclear how much of the proposed framework is genuinely novel versus a reorganization of existing ideas.
Search Results
#209 — Incremental (33)

KV Cache Optimization Strategies for Scalable and Efficient LLM Inference

Yichun Xu, Navjot K. Khaira, Tejinder Singh · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: A well-organized survey with a clean five-category taxonomy and a practically useful scenario-to-technique mapping. However, as a pure literature review with no original experiments, all quantitative comparisons are drawn from heterogeneous sources with incompatible baselines, limiting the reliability of cross-method conclusions. The contribution is organizational rather than technical.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As production LLMs extend context windows from thousands to millions of tokens, the key-value (KV) cache — which stores past token representations to avoid redundant recomputation during autoregressive generation — becomes a primary deployment bottleneck. Its memory footprint scales linearly with context length: a 7B LLaMA-2 model's KV cache reaches ~64 GB at 128K tokens, exceeding an A100 GPU's 80 GB VRAM when model weights are also included. Existing surveys either focus on a single technique in isolation or provide broad but shallow coverage. This paper targets a middle-ground: a systematic, mechanism-depth review that also offers actionable practitioner guidance. Methodology: The paper organizes ~25 recent KV cache optimization methods into a five-category taxonomy: (1) Cache Eviction — selectively discarding low-importance tokens based on attention scores (H2O, SnapKV, NACL, InfiniPot, HASHEVICT, MorphKV, RocketKV, KVzip, Ada-KV); (2) Cache Compression — reducing bit-width or exploiting structural redundancy via quantization or low-rank projection (KIVI, KVQuant, MiniCache, PALU); (3) Hybrid Memory Solutions — offloading portions of the KV cache to CPU, disk, or near-storage accelerators (PagedAttention/vLLM, InfiniGen, LayerKV, INF2, KVPR, Oneiros, CLO); (4) Novel Attention Mechanisms — replacing quadratic softmax attention with linear, log-linear, or hybrid architectures that eliminate or bound KV cache growth (Transformers-are-RNNs, Log-Linear Attention, Local Linear Attention, KIMI Linear); and (5) Combination Methods — integrating eviction, compression, and offloading (FlexGen, Q-Hitter, ShadowKV, TailorKV). For each method the paper explains the core mechanism in detail, provides complexity analysis, and summarizes empirical numbers from the original papers. It then maps categories to seven deployment scenarios: long-context single requests, minimal model modification, high-throughput serving, edge devices, multi-turn conversations, prefill-heavy workloads, and accuracy-critical reasoning. Key Results: The comparative table (Table 6) aggregates quantitative results across methods. Highlights include: H2O achieves 5–10× memory reduction and up to 29× throughput improvement with accuracy comparable to baseline; SnapKV delivers 8.2× memory efficiency and 3.6× generation speedup; KIVI achieves 2.6× peak memory reduction and 2.35–3.47× throughput with <2% accuracy drop; KVQuant enables 3.7–6.9× memory savings with <0.1 perplexity degradation at 3-bit quantization, supporting up to 10M token contexts; PagedAttention (vLLM) delivers 2–4× throughput improvement losslessly; LayerKV improves time-to-first-token (TTFT) by up to 69× losslessly; Oneiros achieves 44.8–82.5% reduction in token latency and up to 86.7% higher throughput over vLLM; FlexGen enables 40–100× higher maximum throughput than DeepSpeed Zero-Inference on a single GPU with negligible accuracy loss; KIMI Linear achieves 75% KV reduction and 6× throughput at 1M context while outperforming full attention. The central finding is that no single technique dominates: eviction and compression excel for ultra-long-context single requests, hybrid memory systems are strongest for high-throughput multi-tenant serving, and new attention mechanisms offer asymptotically better scaling but require retraining and still lag on reasoning tasks. Strengths: - The five-category taxonomy is clean and well-motivated, with each category covering a distinct system-level objective (memory footprint vs. bandwidth vs. storage tier vs. computational complexity). - The scenario-to-technique mapping (Section 5) is practically valuable and more actionable than a pure literature listing — it gives concrete recommendations with supporting numbers for each of seven realistic deployment profiles. - The mechanism descriptions go beyond summary-level: KIVI's per-channel vs. per-token asymmetric quantization, PALU's SVD-based low-rank projection with matrix fusion, InfiniGen's partial-Q speculative prefetching, and KIMI Linear's delta-attention gating formulas are all explained with sufficient mathematical detail to understand design trade-offs. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper performs no original experiments; all quantitative numbers are taken from the original papers, which used different models, benchmarks, hardware, and baselines. Direct cross-method comparison in Table 6 is therefore misleading — e.g., H2O's "29× throughput" and ShadowKV's "3.04×" are measured against entirely different reference points. - Coverage of cache eviction methods is noticeably heavier than other categories (9 methods vs. 4 for compression), and several important recent techniques (e.g., Quest, PyramidKV, CLA) are absent, raising questions about selection criteria. - The taxonomy treats "combination methods" as a fifth peer category rather than recognizing that most real deployments already combine techniques; this creates some forced classification (e.g., ShadowKV uses SVD compression + CPU offloading + sparse attention, yet is placed only in combination methods).
Search Results 2603.20397 PDF
#210 — Incremental (33)

Типология региональных рынков государственных закупок для субъектов МСП: подход на основе комбинированной кластеризации

rusjel.ru · 7 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The study addresses a genuine policy-relevant gap by clustering Russian regions on SME-specific procurement indicators rather than generic socioeconomic metrics. The finding that macroeconomic structure constrains but does not determine procurement outcomes is useful for policy differentiation. However, the methodology is a straightforward application of standard clustering techniques with modest rule-based modifications, the main analytical payoff (the full K×E cross-tabulation) is deferred to a follow-up paper, and the single cross-section design limits generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Russia's Federal Law No. 44-FZ mandates a uniform 25% procurement quota from SMEs for contracts under 20 million rubles — a one-size-fits-all rule applied to an economy where the actual SME share in regional procurement varies from 19% to 93%. This extreme heterogeneity renders uniform policy ineffective. Prior clustering studies of Russian regions either focused on general macroeconomic indicators or on broad SME development metrics, but none targeted the specific structure of SME participation in public procurement as the unit of analysis. The paper's central hypothesis is that Russian regions exhibit deep structural heterogeneity both in SME procurement patterns and in macroeconomic conditions, and that these two dimensions are partially independent — necessitating differentiated rather than uniform policy. Methodology: The study builds two independent typologies from 2023 data covering 81 of 85 Russian regions (four newly-integrated territories excluded). The procurement data come from the Unified Information System of Public Procurement (1,636,569 contract records), and macroeconomic data from Rosstat. For the procurement-activity typology, four relative indicators are constructed: SME coverage index (Icov — unique SME suppliers / total registered SMEs), SME share of procurement volume (Dp), share of contracts below 20M rubles (D<20), and regional concentration of local SME suppliers (Rloc). For the macroeconomic typology, three structural indicators are used: Herfindahl–Hirschman Index on GRP structure (HHI), Pearson correlation of regional vs. national economic structure (rxy), and pre-subsidy budget sufficiency index (BOi). Standard algorithms (k-means, hierarchical, DBSCAN) produced economically uninterpretable clusters on the procurement data due to weak internal structure and high overlap, motivating a novel combined approach: rule-based clustering using empirical quartile thresholds to create 81 initial 'bins,' followed by CHAMELEON-style agglomerative hierarchical clustering to merge them. This ensures economic interpretability alongside mathematical rigor. The macroeconomic typology uses k-means (optimal k=4 by elbow/silhouette), followed by expert splitting of one heterogeneous cluster on the budget sufficiency threshold, yielding five final clusters. Procurement clusters are validated with ANOVA; macroeconomic clusters with Silhouette (0.38), Calinski–Harabasz (59.26), and Davies–Bouldin (0.86) indices. Key Results: Five stable procurement-activity clusters emerge: K1 — balanced SME participation (18 regions, median Dp=0.60); K2 — high SME involvement, open/inclusive market (19 regions, Dp=0.72, D<20=0.54, the best-performing policy model); K3 — transitional, heterogeneous model with high local concentration (30 regions); K4 — high market concentration dominated by large lots and local incumbents inaccessible to most SMEs (14 regions, including Moscow, St. Petersburg, Tatarstan); K5 — systemically low SME participation (4 regions, Dp=0.34, D<20=0.19 — resource-extraction regions). All four indicators differ significantly across clusters by ANOVA (p<0.01; F=21.9 for D<20, F=15.6 for Rloc). Five macroeconomic clusters are also identified: E1 diversified donors (12 regions, BOi=1.28), E2 industrial mono-specialized (10 regions), E3 diversified subsidized (41 regions, the largest group), E4 depressed (16 regions, BOi=0.39, rxy=0.32), and E5 resource-export (6 regions, HHI=0.40, BOi=1.31). Cross-analysis reveals the key finding: macroeconomic structure sets constraints but does not determine procurement outcomes. High-budget diversified regions (E1) fall into K4 (high concentration, low SME access) because their large infrastructure contracts exceed the 20M ruble threshold. Subsidized diversified regions (E3 overlap with K2) achieve high SME involvement precisely because limited budgets force smaller lot sizes. Resource-export regions (E5) map to K5 despite high budget sufficiency, as procurement is dominated by extractive sector contracts. The SME coverage index is paradoxically highest in K5 (1.47× average) while the value share is lowest (0.34× average), explained by a small total supplier pool where many micro-SMEs share trivial contracts while large federal firms capture the bulk. Strengths: - First Russian study to cluster regions on SME-specific procurement participation indicators rather than general SME development or socioeconomic metrics, directly addressing a documented gap in the literature. - Methodological innovation: the rule-based + CHAMELEON-style combined clustering explicitly overcomes the well-documented failure of standard algorithms on this feature space, with a transparent, data-driven threshold construction grounded in empirical quartiles. - The dual-typology design that deliberately keeps procurement and macroeconomic dimensions separate is analytically superior to integrated approaches — it cleanly identifies where institutional/policy factors override structural economic endowments, which integrated models would conflate. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Single cross-section (2023 only): the study cannot establish causality, assess temporal stability of clusters, or detect which regions are moving between clusters — all explicitly deferred to future work. - The critical cross-tabulation of the two typologies (the full K×E matrix of 25 combinations that would yield concrete policy recommendations) is explicitly deferred to a follow-up paper, leaving the main analytical payoff undelivered in this article. - The expert correction step in the macroeconomic clustering — iteratively splitting one k-means cluster on a manually chosen budget sufficiency threshold — introduces subjectivity that partially undermines the claim of algorithmic rigor, and the chosen threshold is not independently validated. - K5 contains only four highly heterogeneous regions (Chechnya, Tyumen, Chukotka, Yamalo-Nenets), driven by structurally opposite mechanisms; the cluster is too small and internally incoherent to support robust policy prescriptions, and its high indicator variance is acknowledged but not fully resolved.
Citations
#211 — Incremental (32)

Neural - Symbolic Logic Query Answering in Non-Euclidean Space

Lihui Liu · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: HYQNET is a straightforward single-component swap of GNN-QE's Euclidean GNN with a hyperbolic variant, with all other architectural and experimental choices inherited unchanged. The empirical improvements are consistently below 1 MRR point across all datasets and metrics, falling within noise range and undermining the practical significance of the geometric motivation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Answering complex First-Order Logic (FOL) queries over knowledge graphs is a core reasoning challenge. Existing approaches fall into two camps: pure neural methods (GQE, Q2B, BetaE) that learn query embeddings but are opaque, and symbolic methods that are interpretable but fail on incomplete graphs. Recent neural-symbolic hybrids like GNN-QE combine both via fuzzy-set logic operations and GNN-based relation projection, but they embed the recursive query tree—which grows exponentially in depth—in Euclidean space, which poorly captures hierarchical structure. This paper argues that hyperbolic geometry, which naturally models exponential branching, is a more appropriate space for this task. Methodology: HYQNET builds directly on GNN-QE [Zhu et al., ICML 2022], replacing its Euclidean GNN with a hyperbolic GNN that uses a learned curvature per layer in the Poincaré ball model. FOL queries are decomposed into four primitive operations over fuzzy sets: relation projection, conjunction, disjunction, and negation. Relation projection is the only learned component; it is implemented as a multi-layer hyperbolic GNN where node embeddings are updated via the Möbius addition–based exponential and logarithmic maps at curvature c (learned per layer). After T propagation steps the final-layer embeddings are fed through an MLP + sigmoid to produce membership probabilities over all entities. Logical operations use closed-form product fuzzy logic (conjunction = elementwise product, disjunction = inclusion-exclusion, negation = complement), which provably satisfies commutativity, associativity, and non-contradiction. Training minimizes binary cross-entropy over correct vs. incorrect answers. A theoretical claim ("Theorem 1") states HYQNET dominates Euclidean GNN-QE because Euclidean space is a degenerate case (curvature → 0). Key Results: HYQNET is evaluated on FB15k, FB15k-237, and NELL995 using the BetaE benchmark query set (9 EPFO + 5 negation query types). Metrics are MRR and Hits@1 on hard answers. On FB15k, HYQNET achieves average MRR of 74.2 (EPFO) / 38.6 (negation) vs. GNN-QE's 73.7 / 38.3, and average Hits@1 of 69.5 / 21.9 vs. GNN-QE's 68.6 / 21.7. On FB15k-237, HYQNET scores 26.5 / 8.9 MRR vs. GNN-QE's 26.1 / 8.4. On NELL995, 28.9 / 8.9 vs. GNN-QE's 28.7 / 8.6. Margins over the primary baseline (GNN-QE) are consistently positive but uniformly small (0.3–0.5 MRR points on average). On cardinality prediction (Spearman correlation with ground-truth answer count), HYQNET achieves 0.958 average on FB15k vs. GNN-QE's 0.952—again a minor improvement. Strengths: - Principled geometric motivation: hyperbolic space is well-suited to the exponential recursive tree structure of GNN-based relation projection, making the architectural choice theoretically grounded. - Inherited interpretability: by retaining product fuzzy logic for all logical operations, the symbolic layer of the model remains exactly interpretable and obeys logical laws, unlike purely learned geometric operators. - Learned per-layer curvature adds flexibility without architectural complexity, and the "Theorem 1" argument provides a formal lower-bound guarantee over Euclidean baselines. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Empirical gains are marginal: improvements over GNN-QE are consistently below 1 MRR point across all three datasets and both metrics, which is within the range of noise for these benchmarks and raises questions about practical significance. - The paper is essentially an engineering extension of GNN-QE with a single component swap (Euclidean GNN → hyperbolic GNN); the fuzzy logic operations, training procedure, evaluation protocol, and datasets are unchanged, limiting the novelty of the contribution. - "Theorem 1" is not a rigorous proof—it is a capacity argument that curvature → 0 recovers Euclidean embeddings, which says nothing about optimization or generalization, and does not constitute a guarantee of empirical improvement. - No ablation is provided on curvature learning (fixed vs. learned, shared vs. per-layer), making it impossible to isolate where the benefit comes from. - Scalability is not discussed: hyperbolic GNNs involve more expensive operations (Möbius addition, exp/log maps) than standard GNNs, yet runtime comparisons are absent.
Search Results 2603.15633 PDF
#212 — Incremental (31)

Improving Diabetes Prediction Using a Hybrid Approach Based on Community Detection and Ontological Reasoning

ceai.srait.ro · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Combining Louvain community detection with ontological reasoning is a reasonable idea, but both components are well-known techniques applied in a straightforward manner.
